segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Stomach Ailments: Wekhedu, Constipation, Bloating, and Stomach Pain (Columns 36–42)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 36-42: Remedies for diseases of the stomach — wekhedu (rot/putrefaction), constipation, bloating, stomach pain. Includes aloe, figs, frankincense, castor oil preparations.
segments.translation: Columns 36 through 42 of the Ebers Papyrus present a series of compounded remedies directed at diseases of the stomach, principally the pathological condition known as 'wekhedu' [wḫdw] — a humoral concept of internal rot or putrefaction believed to originate in the bowel and spread corruption throughout the body — as well as constipation [ḥmw or related terms], bloating [distension of the belly], and generalised stomach pain. The prescriptions recorded in these columns employ the following materia medica and methods: (1) ALOE [qmy.t or related designations]: used in decoction or as a paste applied to the abdomen; believed to purge wekhedu and relieve constipation by stimulating evacuation of the bowel. (2) FIGS [dqr]: frequently administered as a sweetened decoction or consumed whole, either alone or combined with other substances; used to soften the intestines, counter constipation, and soothe stomach pain. (3) FRANKINCENSE [snṯr]: incorporated into both oral preparations and fumigations directed at the belly; considered to counteract internal putrefaction (wekhedu) and reduce abdominal bloating. (4) CASTOR OIL [degem / ḏgm]: expressed from the castor plant (Ricinus communis) and administered orally, often warmed or blended with beer or water as a vehicle; primary use is purgative — to drive out wekhedu and relieve constipation; also applied externally in some formulations. General preparation methods indicated in these columns include: boiling ingredients in water or beer to produce a decoction [pḥ]; grinding dried plant materials into powder and mixing with a liquid vehicle (beer, water, milk, or honey); applying warm compresses or poultices of plant pastes to the abdomen; and fumigation [sdt] of the patient over heated aromatic preparations, particularly in cases of bloating and wekhedu. Dosage instructions typically specify the volume to be drunk over one, three, or four days. Several remedies are prefaced with the injunction that the patient is to be examined first, and the physician should determine whether the belly is 'heated' or 'cold' before selecting the appropriate remedy.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Intestinal Worms and Parasites (Cols. 43–49)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 43–49: Remedies for intestinal worms and parasites — prescriptions using pomegranate root bark, wormwood, male fern, and date wine. Also recipes for expelling tapeworms.
segments.translation:
Columns 43–49 of the Ebers Papyrus present a series of prescriptions directed against intestinal worms [Egyptian: fnḏ.t or ꜥfꜣ.t, terms covering various helminth and parasite species] and related parasitic conditions affecting the bowel. The remedies described are as follows:

[Col. 43] — A decoction of the root bark of the pomegranate [Egyptian: inḥmn; Punica granatum L.] is to be prepared by boiling the outer and inner bark of the root in water until the liquid is reduced. The patient is to drink this preparation on an empty stomach over the course of one or two days. The active principle causes paralysis and expulsion of intestinal worms, particularly tapeworm segments [proglottids], which are then purged from the body.

[Col. 44] — Wormwood [Egyptian: ṯm or sꜣm; Artemisia absinthium L., or possibly Artemisia judaica L.] is combined with sweet beer or date wine [Egyptian: ḥnq.t n bnr.t] and administered as a drink. The bitter volatile oils present in wormwood were understood to be inimical to worm infestation. This preparation may also be applied as a warm compress to the abdomen to drive worms outward.

[Col. 45] — Male fern rhizome [Egyptian: identity debated; possibly Dryopteris filix-mas (L.) Schott, or an equivalent local fern species] is ground and mixed with fat or oil, then administered orally. The oleoresin extracted from the rhizome acts as a taenicide [tapeworm-killing agent]. Physicians are cautioned that excessive doses may cause harm to the patient's vision or liver, and that the preparation should be followed swiftly by a purgative to clear the dead worms from the intestine.

[Col. 46] — Date wine [Egyptian: ḥnq.t n bnr.t or irp n bnr.t] alone, or infused with pomegranate rind, is prescribed as a vehicle for delivering antiparasitic agents and as a mild laxative to assist in the mechanical expulsion of worms following treatment.

[Col. 47] — A compound recipe for expelling tapeworms [Egyptian: ḥfꜣ.t, lit. 'the great worm' or 'the long worm'] prescribes a multi-ingredient preparation: root bark of pomegranate, wormwood, and a resinous substance [possibly terebinth resin or natron] are ground together, mixed into date wine, and taken in measured doses over two consecutive mornings. A purgative — castor oil [Egyptian: dꜣr.t; Ricinus communis L.] — is administered on the evening of the second day to flush the expelled parasites from the bowel.

[Col. 48] — Fumigation of the anal region using smoke from burning aromatic herbs [species not precisely specified; possibly kyphi-type aromatics] is described as a supplementary measure to draw worms downward and outward. The patient is directed to squat over a vessel containing smoldering material so that the smoke reaches the affected area.

[Col. 49] — A closing prescription recommends the repeated administration of wormwood decoction over several days for chronic or recurrent worm infestation, with particular emphasis on maintaining an empty stomach during treatment to maximize efficacy. The physician is also instructed to examine the patient's stool to confirm expulsion of the parasites before ceasing treatment.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Liver and Spleen Disorders (Cols. 50–56)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 50-56: Remedies for liver and spleen disorders — preparations using kyphi (compound resin), myrrh, sycamore figs, and heated mineral preparations.
segments.translation:
Columns 50–56 of the Ebers Papyrus prescribe a series of remedies targeting disorders of the liver and spleen [Egyptian: 'ḥ3ty' and 'mḥt', terms variously interpreted as referring to the liver, heart-region, and spleen-adjacent viscera]. The following therapeutic preparations are recorded:

1. KYPHI COMPOUND RESIN PREPARATION (cols. 50–51): A composite aromatic resin known as 'kyphi' [Egyptian: 'kp.t' or 'kp.y'], prepared from multiple resins, aromatic woods, and binding agents including honey and wine, is administered orally or applied topically to the region of the liver and spleen. Kyphi was among the most complex pharmaceutical formulations in ancient Egyptian medicine, containing up to sixteen ingredients in later classical descriptions. It is prescribed here to alleviate swelling and hardness of the visceral organs, likely indicating hepatomegaly or splenomegaly.

2. MYRRH PREPARATION (cols. 51–52): Myrrh ['antyw'], harvested from Commiphora species, is prepared as a decoction by heating in water or beer and administered as a drink, or mixed with fat and applied as a poultice directly over the afflicted visceral region. It is indicated for pain and inflammation associated with the liver, and for 'eating away' [i.e., ulceration or necrotic conditions] of internal organs.

3. SYCAMORE FIG PREPARATION (cols. 53–54): The fruit of the sycamore fig ['nehet', Ficus sycomorus] is prescribed mashed, mixed with water or honey, and taken as a drink or paste. It is indicated for conditions of 'fullness' and 'heaviness' in the abdominal region, consistent with hepatic or splenic enlargement, constipation secondary to liver disease, or biliary obstruction.

4. HEATED MINERAL PREPARATIONS (cols. 55–56): Certain unspecified mineral substances [possibly including natron, malachite, or ochre] are heated, powdered, and either dissolved in liquid for ingestion or mixed with oils/fats for topical application over the liver and spleen regions. These are indicated for hardness [likely fibrotic or cirrhotic presentations] and pain in the right and left hypochondriac regions respectively. The heating process may serve both ritual purification and practical pharmaceutical transformation (calcination).

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Cough, Asthma, and Chest Diseases (Cols. 57–62)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 57-62: Recipes for cough, asthma, and chest diseases — steam inhalations, honey preparations, milk with carob, fig decoctions.
segments.translation:
Columns 57–62 of the Ebers Papyrus present a systematic collection of prescriptions directed against disorders of the chest and respiratory tract, including persistent cough [Egyptian: šnḥ or cognate terms], laboured breathing [Egyptian: ꜣšꜣ, often rendered 'asthma'], and generalised chest ailments [Egyptian: mr.t n ḥꜣty, 'disease of the chest/heart region']. The remedies recorded fall into several therapeutic categories:

1. STEAM INHALATIONS: The patient is instructed to crouch over a vessel of heated liquid — typically water in which aromatic or medicinal plant material has been steeped or boiled — and to inhale the rising vapour. This technique (Egyptian: snṯr, broadly 'fumigation/incensing') was regarded as a direct means of conveying medicinal vapour into the airways. Ingredients specified for inhalation preparations in this section include resinous botanicals and aromatic woods.

2. HONEY PREPARATIONS: Honey [Egyptian: bit] functions both as a base vehicle and as a therapeutic agent in its own right. It is combined with powdered or macerated plant material and administered orally, either swallowed directly or dissolved in warm liquid. Honey's demulcent and antimicrobial properties, today well-attested pharmacologically, would have soothed inflamed mucosal surfaces of the throat and bronchi.

3. MILK WITH CAROB: Fresh or warm milk [Egyptian: irṯt] is prescribed as a decoction base, to which ground carob pod [Egyptian: nḏm, Ceratonia siliqua] is added. The patient drinks this preparation. Carob contains tannins, sugars, and mucilaginous compounds that may reduce irritation of the respiratory mucosa; the milk base adds further soothing demulcent action.

4. FIG DECOCTIONS: Dried or fresh figs [Egyptian: dꜣb, Ficus carica] are boiled in water or another liquid medium to produce a decoction [Egyptian: psi, 'to cook/boil']. The resulting liquid is strained and consumed. Figs contain mucilage, sugars, and flavonoids; their softening and expectorant properties have been recognised in traditional medicine across cultures. In this context they are used to loosen chest congestion and ease breathing.

General prescriptive notes: Several recipes specify quantities in terms of the heqat or hin measure. Some preparations are to be taken over multiple days ('for four days thou shalt apply this'). The practitioner [Egyptian: swnw, 'physician'] may be instructed to assess the patient's response before continuing. In some entries an incantation [Egyptian: rꜣ, 'spell/utterance'] accompanies the physical preparation, reflecting the dual magico-medical character of Egyptian therapeutic practice.

segments.title_inferred: The Heart Treatise: Cardiac Vessels, Pulse Doctrine, and Remedies (Cols. 99–102)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 99-102: The heart treatise — Egyptian physicians' understanding of cardiac disease, blood vessels, and pulse. 46 vessels described. Remedies: garlic, onion, natron, resins.
segments.translation: Columns 99–102 of the Ebers Papyrus contain the so-called 'Heart Treatise' (Egyptian: [metu] doctrine), the most systematic surviving ancient Egyptian account of cardiovascular anatomy and pathology. The text asserts that the heart [ib] is the center of a network of 46 vessels [metu] that carry blood, air, water, mucus, urine, semen, and other vital substances to all parts of the body. Physicians are instructed to assess the condition of the heart and its vessels by palpating the pulse at various points on the body — the temples, wrists, and other sites — since 'the heart speaks through the vessels of every limb.' Disease of the heart and vessels is attributed to accumulation of morbid matter [wekhedu], stagnation of the vessel contents, or invasion by external harmful agents. The treatise prescribes the following remedies for cardiac and vascular conditions: (1) Garlic [Egyptian: khesau; Allium sativum]: to be administered internally, either raw or as a decoction, to clear obstructions in the vessels and strengthen the heart. (2) Onion [Egyptian: hedshu; Allium cepa]: prescribed similarly to garlic, often combined with it, for purging stagnant matter from the vessels and alleviating chest pain. (3) Natron [Egyptian: netjeri; sodium carbonate/bicarbonate mixture]: used both internally as a purging and cleansing agent and externally as a wash or fumigant, intended to purify the blood and the metu of harmful accumulations. (4) Resins [Egyptian: senefer / antiu, likely including frankincense and myrrh]: burned as fumigants or dissolved in preparations to be applied to the chest or administered as drinks, to drive away malevolent influences and reduce inflammation of the vessels. The text explicitly connects irregular or weakened pulse with advanced vessel disease and poor prognosis. This section is considered among the earliest documented attempts at a systematic physiology of circulation, though its model diverges fundamentally from the modern understanding of blood circulation.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Eye Diseases: Cataracts, Night Blindness, and Ocular Infections (Ebers Papyrus cols. 336–356)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 336–356: Eye diseases — remedies for clouding of the eye (cataracts), night blindness, infections. Ingredients: malachite (green eye paint), kohl, honey, human milk.
segments.translation:
Columns 336–356 of the Ebers Papyrus contain a substantial body of prescriptions directed at diseases and conditions of the eye. These include: remedies for 'clouding of the eye' [Egyptian: likely ḫꜣwt or a cognate term describing opacity of the visual field, interpreted by scholars as cataracts or corneal opacity], treatments for 'night blindness' [Egyptian: šnt or cognate, a condition of impaired vision in low light, possibly corresponding to nyctalopia or vitamin A deficiency], and preparations for ocular infections [conjunctivitis, trachoma, or related conditions of inflammation and discharge].

Prescribed ingredients and their applications include:

1. MALACHITE [Egyptian: wꜣḏ — 'green [mineral]']: Ground malachite (basic copper carbonate, Cu₂(CO₃)(OH)₂), used widely as a green eye cosmetic and medicinal pigment. Applied directly to the eye or eyelid as a powder or paste. Modern pharmacology confirms that copper compounds possess antimicrobial properties, lending partial validity to this use in treating ocular infections. Malachite was among the most consistently employed ophthalmological substances in ancient Egyptian medicine.

2. KOHL [Egyptian: msdmt — 'black eye paint']: A dark mineral preparation, most commonly galena (lead sulfide, PbS) or stibnite (antimony trisulfide, Sb₂S₃), ground finely and applied to the eye and eyelid margins. Used for both cosmetic and therapeutic purposes. Some modern analyses suggest that lead chloride compounds forming secondarily on skin contact may stimulate nitric oxide production, providing a limited antimicrobial effect. However, chronic lead exposure poses known toxicological risks not recognised in antiquity.

3. HONEY [Egyptian: bit]: Applied topically to the eye, either alone or as a vehicle for other medicinal substances. Honey's high osmolarity, low pH, hydrogen peroxide content, and presence of defensin-1 (bee defensin) are now recognised as conferring genuine broad-spectrum antimicrobial and wound-healing properties. Its use in ophthalmology represents one of the more pharmacologically validated remedies in the Ebers corpus.

4. HUMAN MILK [Egyptian: irṯt n mwt — 'milk of a [nursing] woman/mother']: Prescribed for instillation into the affected eye. Human breast milk contains immunoglobulins (particularly IgA), lactoferrin, lysozyme, and growth factors. Modern clinical literature documents its use as a treatment for neonatal conjunctivitis and corneal ulcers, offering genuine antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory benefit. The Ebers Papyrus thus anticipates a remedy that retains limited modern clinical application.

Preparation methods across these columns include: direct topical application of powdered minerals to the eye; combination of ingredients into pastes or liquid instillations [eye drops]; and, in some prescriptions, incorporation into fatty bases [goose fat, cattle fat] as ointment vehicles for prolonged contact with ocular tissue. Fumigation is not prominently attested in this section but may appear in adjacent columns. Prescriptions are typically introduced with the formula 'Another remedy for…' [Egyptian: kꜣ n…], indicating a catalogue or formulary structure.

segments.title_inferred: Further Eye Remedies: Inflammation, Weeping Eyes, and Eyelid Growths
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 357-383: Further eye remedies — inflammation, weeping eyes, growths on the eyelid. Preparations using blood of lizards, bile of tortoise, goose fat.
segments.translation: Columns 357–383 of the Ebers Papyrus present a continuing series of prescriptions directed at diseases and conditions of the eye, encompassing inflammatory states of the ocular tissues, persistent lacrimation [weeping or watering of the eyes], and abnormal growths or lesions upon the eyelid margin. The therapeutic agents prescribed include: the blood of lizards [likely various species of small Egyptian lizard, possibly Agama or Acanthodactylus spp.], applied topically to the affected eye or eyelid; the bile [gall] of the tortoise [possibly the Nile soft-shelled turtle, Trionyx triunguis, or a land tortoise], used as a medicinal liquid preparation instilled into or daubed upon the eye; and goose fat [rendered lard from domestic or Nile geese, Anser anser or Alopochen aegyptiaca], employed as a vehicle or base for topical application, likely to soften tissue, reduce irritation, or carry other medicinal substances into the affected area. These substances were to be prepared and administered according to the specific formulae described, with application methods including direct smearing [anointing] onto the eyelid or conjunctival surface, and possibly fumigation or warm poulticing in select cases, consistent with Egyptian medical practice documented elsewhere in the papyrus.

segments.title_inferred: Comprehensive Eye Treatments: 96 Prescriptions for Diseases and Conditions of the Eye (Including Night Blindness Remedy)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 384–431: Comprehensive eye treatment — 96 prescriptions for every condition of the eye. Includes the famous remedy for night blindness (liver of ox = vitamin A).
segments.translation:
Columns 384–431 of the Ebers Papyrus constitute the most extensive ophthalmological section of the ancient Egyptian medical corpus, comprising 96 discrete prescriptions [Egyptian: sḫr, 'treatment plan' or 'remedy'] addressing virtually every known ailment of the eye [Egyptian: irt, 'eye']. The section opens with formulas for general inflammation and redness of the eye and proceeds systematically through conditions including clouding of vision, discharge, pain, itching, granular eyelid disease [consistent with trachoma], pterygium [growth over the cornea], trauma, and loss of sight. Remedies are drawn from the mineral, plant, and animal kingdoms and are administered as topical collyria [eye-drops/washes], ointments, and compresses.

NOTABLE PRESCRIPTIONS WITHIN THIS SECTION:

1. REMEDY FOR NIGHT BLINDNESS [Egyptian: šnt-irt, loosely 'failure/weakness of the eye at night']: The patient is instructed to have the liver [Egyptian: mỉsỉ] of an ox [Egyptian: kꜣ] cooked and pressed, with the expressed fluid [juices] allowed to drip into the affected eye or the organ itself held close to the eye as a poultice in the evening hours. This prescription, remarkable for its clinical effectiveness, is attributable to the exceptionally high concentration of retinol (vitamin A) in bovine hepatic tissue. Night blindness [nyctalopia] is a classic presentation of vitamin A deficiency [hypovitaminosis A], and the administration of ox liver would have constituted a direct and pharmacologically valid treatment. This represents one of the earliest documented instances of empirically successful nutritional therapy in the historical record.

2. REMEDIES FOR INFLAMMATION AND DISCHARGE [consistent with bacterial conjunctivitis / blepharitis]: Multiple prescriptions call for the application of compresses or washes incorporating honey [Egyptian: bit], malachite [Egyptian: wꜣḏ; copper carbonate], galena [Egyptian: msdmt; lead sulfide], and various plant-based decoctions including castor oil [Egyptian: dꜣrw], acacia leaves [Egyptian: šnḏt], and natron [Egyptian: nṯr, sodium bicarbonate/carbonate]. Honey and copper compounds demonstrate measurable antimicrobial properties in modern pharmacological studies. Galena (kohl) also possesses mild antiseptic action and was ubiquitous in Egyptian personal and medical use.

3. REMEDIES FOR PAIN AND CLOUDING OF THE EYE: Decoctions of willow [Egyptian: ṯrt; Salix sp.] and other astringent plant materials are applied as eye-washes. Willow contains salicin, a precursor to salicylic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties. Frankincense resin [Egyptian: snṯr; Boswellia sp.] appears in ointment preparations for corneal opacities.

4. REMEDIES FOR PTERYGIUM AND GROWTHS: Plant-based caustic or astringent preparations—including sycamore fig juice [Egyptian: nbt; Ficus sycomorus] and substances described as 'sharp' or 'cutting' [Egyptian: spt]—are applied to fibrous growths on the corneal surface, consistent with pterygium or pinguecula. These likely functioned as chemical cauterizing agents.

5. REMEDIES FOR EYELID CONDITIONS [consistent with trachoma or trichiasis]: Formulations including copper-based mineral pigments applied to the inner eyelid margin, alongside plant-oil carriers. The antimicrobial and astringent properties of copper minerals would have had genuine therapeutic benefit against Chlamydia trachomatis infection, the causative agent of trachoma.

GENERAL PREPARATION METHODS ACROSS THIS SECTION:
— Collyrium [eye-wash/drop]: Active substances dissolved or decocted in water, beer [Egyptian: ḥqt], or milk [Egyptian: irṯt], strained, and instilled directly into the eye.
— Ointment [Egyptian: mrḥt]: Active ingredients ground finely and suspended in goose fat [Egyptian: ꜣpd-n-smn], castor oil, or another fatty vehicle, then applied with a probe or fingertip to the lid margin or conjunctival surface.
— Compress/Poultice: Fresh or cooked organic material (e.g., liver, plant leaves) held against the closed eyelid.
— Fumigation [rare in this section]: Aromatic smoke directed toward the open eye for certain chronic conditions.

CONDITIONS DOCUMENTED IN COLS 384–431:
— Night blindness (nyctalopia)
— Acute and chronic conjunctivitis
— Blepharitis
— Corneal opacity / cataract [partially]
— Pterygium / pinguecula
— Trachoma / granular eyelid disease
— Ocular trauma
— Eyelid swelling and abscess
— Excessive lacrimation
— Itching and burning of the eye
— General loss of vision

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Skin Diseases, Burns, Itching, Crusts, and Scaling Skin (Cols. 432–452)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 432-452: Skin diseases and burns — remedies for itching, crusts, scaling skin. Ingredients: honey, wax, natron, red ochre, carob, aloe.
segments.translation:
Columns 432–452 of the Ebers Papyrus concern the treatment of various skin afflictions, including persistent itching [Ancient Egyptian: ḥnw or equivalent dermatological terminology], crusted lesions, and scaling or flaking of the skin [conditions likely corresponding to eczema, psoriasis, fungal infections, or post-burn desquamation]. The following remedies are prescribed:

[General therapeutic principle]: Conditions of the skin arising from heat, corruption of the flesh, or insect irritation are to be treated by application of compounded preparations, laid upon the affected area, and in some cases administered as fumigations to drive out causative agents.

[Remedy class — cooling and sealing preparations]: Honey [bj.t] is applied directly to burns and crusted wounds as a primary vehicle, its adhesive and preserving qualities [now understood as antibacterial via hydrogen peroxide release and low water activity] serving to protect raw flesh and suppress putrefaction. Wax [mnj or mni.t, likely beeswax] is combined with honey to form a semi-solid ointment base, creating an occlusive layer over scaling or burned skin to retain moisture and exclude contaminants.

[Remedy class — cleansing and caustic agents]: Natron [ḥsmn; hydrated sodium carbonate/bicarbonate, sourced from Wadi Natrun] is employed as a cleansing and desiccating agent applied to crusted or weeping lesions. Its alkaline nature would disrupt microbial cell walls and loosen adherent crusts. It may be dissolved in water and used as a wash, or applied dry as a powder to scaling skin.

[Remedy class — astringents and coloring agents]: Red ochre [msdmt or šʿ; iron oxide, Fe₂O₃] is incorporated into topical compounds for its astringent and desiccating properties, likely intended to staunch weeping wounds and reduce inflammation. It may also carry a ritual-protective function given its solar and blood associations in Egyptian cosmology.

[Remedy class — binding and soothing agents]: Carob [nbs or dqr; pods of Ceratonia siliqua] provides a mucilaginous, tannin-rich binding medium when ground or boiled. It would form a soothing poultice base and its tannins carry genuine astringent and antimicrobial activity against surface pathogens on broken skin.

[Remedy class — anti-inflammatory and purgative topicals]: Aloe [ṯḥnw or equivalent; Aloe vera or a related species] is prescribed for application to burns and inflamed, scaling skin. The gel derived from the inner leaf pulp contains polysaccharides (acemannan), anthraquinones, and salicylate-like compounds, supporting its documented use as an anti-inflammatory, moisturizing, and wound-healing agent — effects now extensively validated in modern pharmacology.

[Preparation methods noted]: (1) Direct topical application of raw or minimally processed ingredients. (2) Compounding of wax and honey into ointments [Egyptian: mrḥ.t] over low heat. (3) Dissolution of natron in water for wound washing. (4) Grinding of carob pods into paste, possibly mixed with honey or oil as a carrier. (5) Expression of aloe leaf gel for direct application or incorporation into compound preparations. (6) Fumigation over smoldering aromatic or medicated materials may be implied for itching of uncertain origin.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Burns, Scalding, and Wound Infections Using Cooling Botanical Preparations
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 453-465: Remedies for burns and scalding — cooling preparations using water of carob pods, cucumber juice, oil of Ben tree (Moringa). Also wound infections.
segments.translation:
Columns 453–465 of the Ebers Papyrus prescribe a series of remedies for burns [Egyptian: *srf*, lit. 'heat-inflammation'] and scalding injuries, as well as secondary wound infections arising therefrom. The therapeutic approach centres on the topical application of cooling and emollient substances drawn from plant sources.

(1) CAROB POD WATER [Egyptian: *nḏm*, 'sweet/carob']: The liquid extracted by soaking or decocting the pods of the carob tree is applied directly to the burned surface. This preparation is understood to draw heat [*srf*] from the wound and to reduce the pain associated with thermal injury.

(2) CUCUMBER JUICE [Egyptian: *šsp.t* or related term for gourd-family plants]: The expressed juice of the cucumber is applied as a cooling wash or compress to scalded skin. Its high moisture content and cooling sensation are invoked as the therapeutic rationale. This preparation may also be combined with other substances to form a poultice.

(3) OIL OF THE BEN TREE [Egyptian: *bḳ*, 'Moringa oil']: The expressed oil of the Moringa tree [*bḳ*-tree, identified as *Moringa peregrina* or *Moringa oleifera*] is applied as an emollient dressing to burns and infected wounds. It is noted for its lubricating, soothing, and putrefaction-inhibiting properties. In some formulations it serves as a carrier oil for additional medicinal ingredients.

For wound infections secondary to burns, one or more of the above preparations may be used in combination, applied as a bandage-dressing [*idr.t*] renewed on successive days. The physician [*swnw*] is directed to observe the wound for signs of festering [*wḥꜣ*] and to adjust the preparation accordingly.

segments.title_inferred: Hair and Scalp Remedies: Baldness, Grey Hair, and Dandruff Treatments
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 461-471: Hair and scalp remedies — treatment for baldness, grey hair, dandruff. Includes fat of hippopotamus, crocodile, serpent, ibex mixed with plant extracts.
segments.translation: Remedies for the scalp and hair, as prescribed for conditions of hair loss [alopecia], greying of the hair, and flaking of the scalp [dandruff]. The physician is instructed to prepare compound unguents and ointments incorporating the rendered fat [merhet] of the hippopotamus [deb], the crocodile [meseh], the serpent [djet], and the ibex [nwt], blended together with extracts derived from medicinal plants. These animal fats serve as both active therapeutic agents and as emollient carriers [base media] for the botanical constituents. The combined preparation is to be applied topically to the affected areas of the scalp to stimulate hair growth, arrest greying, and eliminate scaling of the skin of the head. Such formulations represent a class of compound remedies [pefet] in which animal-derived substances of perceived sympathetic or apotropaic power are combined with plant matter to produce a synergistic therapeutic effect. The specific animal fats are selected according to the doctrine of signatures and ritual association: the hippopotamus, being a creature of vigorous growth and great vitality, was believed to impart regenerative force to failing hair roots; the crocodile, associated with power and renewal, was similarly employed; fat of the serpent, a creature that periodically sheds and renews its skin, was considered especially apt for conditions of scalp flaking and renewal of the hair; and ibex fat, from an animal of notable agility and robust constitution, contributed restorative properties. The plant extracts, though not fully specified in the transmitted fragment, likely included preparations derived from castor [kiki], fenugreek [fenu], moringa [bak], and onion [hedet], all of which appear elsewhere in the Ebers Papyrus in hair-related contexts. The ointment is rubbed into the scalp with the fingers, and treatment is to be repeated over multiple days.

segments.title_inferred: Gynecological Remedies: Menstrual Disorders, Uterine Prolapse, and Vaginal Infections
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 783-801: Gynecological remedies — treatments for menstrual disorders, uterine prolapse, vaginal infections. Ingredients: dates, beer, cedar oil, acacia.
segments.translation: Columns 783–801 of the Ebers Papyrus contain a series of gynecological prescriptions addressing a range of conditions affecting women, including irregularities of menstruation [Egyptian: likely described as disruption of the monthly blood flow], descent or prolapse of the uterus [Egyptian: 'falling of the womb'], and infections or discharges of the vaginal region. The remedies prescribed draw upon the following materia medica: dates [bnr], beer [ḥnqt] (likely including both ordinary beer and medicinal-grade preparations), cedar oil [mrḥt nt šndt-cedar or similar resinous conifer oil], and acacia [šndt, Acacia nilotica]. Specific formulations described include: (1) Preparations for menstrual irregularity — dates and beer are combined, possibly macerated or decocted, and administered orally to encourage or regulate menstrual flow; (2) Treatments for uterine prolapse — topical or fumigatory applications using cedar oil, applied externally to the lower abdomen or introduced via fumigation directed at the uterus, intended to reduce swelling and encourage the organ to return to proper position; (3) Remedies for vaginal infection or discharge — acacia preparations, possibly ground pods or bark decocted in liquid, applied locally as a wash or pessary [Egyptian: a substance introduced internally], exploiting acacia's astringent and antimicrobial properties to reduce infection and discharge. The prescribed quantities and exact ratios of ingredients are not fully specified in this summary segment but would have been detailed in the original hieratic columns.

segments.title_inferred: Contraception, Conception Promotion, and Pregnancy Testing (Cols. 802–820)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 802-820: Contraception and fertility — contraceptive pessaries using acacia tips ground with honey; remedies to promote conception; pregnancy tests.
segments.translation: Columns 802–820 of the Ebers Papyrus address reproductive medicine, encompassing three principal domains: (1) Contraceptive pessaries [wḥm.t / 'that which causes a woman not to conceive']: A preparation is described in which the tips [pods/shoots] of acacia (Egyptian: šnd.t) are ground finely and mixed with honey to form a paste, which is then introduced intravaginally as a pessary. Additional contraceptive formulations mention the inclusion of dates, carob pod pulp, and natron. The mixture is applied on moistened plant-fibre lint [ḥbs] to form a suppository placed in the vagina. (2) Remedies to promote conception [s.t mwt.f / 'to cause a woman to conceive']: Prescriptions are given to enhance female fertility, including the application of warmed oils, fumigation of the womb with aromatic substances, and the internal administration of concoctions incorporating sweet beer [ḥnq.t], herbs, and minerals. Instructions advise the woman to sit over fumigating vapours so that the restorative warmth penetrates the uterus. (3) Pregnancy testing [rḫ.t ỉn.t / 'knowing whether a woman will give birth']: A diagnostic test is described wherein emmer wheat [bdt] and barley [ỉt] are moistened with the urine of the woman in question and allowed to germinate. If barley sprouts first, the child will be male; if emmer wheat sprouts first, the child will be female; if neither germinates, the woman will not give birth [i.e., is not pregnant or will not carry to term]. A further test involves the woman vomiting into a mixture or observing reactions of her body to certain substances to confirm or deny pregnancy.

segments.title_inferred: Childbirth Remedies: Accelerating Labor, Relieving Pain, and Treating Retained Placenta
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 821-839: Childbirth remedies — to accelerate labor: saffron in beer; to relieve pain: plant oil applications; for retained placenta: onion bulb preparations.
segments.translation: Columns 821–839 of the Ebers Papyrus document a series of obstetric remedies addressed to three principal concerns of childbirth. (1) To accelerate [hasten] labor: saffron [kꜣ-nfr or cognate term] is administered dissolved or steeped in beer [ḥnqt], the fermented grain beverage commonly employed as a medicinal vehicle. (2) To relieve pain [during labor]: oils derived from plant sources are applied topically to the body of the parturient woman, likely by massage or anointing of the abdomen and lower back. (3) For retained placenta [failure of the afterbirth to be expelled]: preparations made from onion bulbs [ḥḏ.w or iḥw] are prescribed, presumably administered either as a suppository, a vaginal application, or an oral decoction, following established Egyptian pharmaceutical convention for uterine complaints.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Animal Bites: Crocodile, Hippopotamus, and Dog Wounds
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 840-854: Remedies for bites of animals — crocodile bites, hippo bites, dog bites. Antibacterial preparations: honey, fat, resins, incantations combined.
segments.translation: Columns 840–854: Prescriptions for the treatment of wounds inflicted by the bites of animals, including those of the crocodile [sobek-beast], the hippopotamus [deba], and the dog [iw]. These remedies are compounded from honey [bit], animal fat [merhet], and aromatic resins, applied directly to the wound. The recitation of protective incantations [heka] is prescribed alongside physical application, indicating a dual therapeutic and magical framework. Honey is applied as a wound dressing to cleanse and seal the wound, fat serves as a vehicle and emollient to reduce inflammation and promote tissue adherence, and resins provide antiseptic and occlusive properties. The incantations invoke divine protection against infection and malevolent forces believed to enter through open wounds. These preparations are to be bound upon the wound [with linen], refreshed daily, and accompanied by spoken words of power directed toward the wound.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Scorpion Stings and Insect Bites: Cooling Preparations, Poultices, and Incantations (Cols. 870–877)
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus cols 870-877: Remedies for scorpion stings and insect bites — cooling preparations, plant poultices, incantations. Includes the use of the djed plant.
segments.translation: Columns 870–877: Prescriptions [sekheret] against the bite of the scorpion and the wounds inflicted by stinging insects. To cool [seqeb] the burning [heat] thereof: prepare a poultice [tjemet] of the djed plant, ground fine and mixed with water; apply cold to the sting site. Certain preparations are to be accompanied by spoken words of power [heka — incantation], to be recited over the remedy four times, so that the venom [metwet] be driven out. The djed plant is to be bruised [crushed between stones] and laid upon the wound while fresh, so as to draw out the poison [wekhedet]. Further: the cooling preparation may be combined with fat of goose or oil of moringa [bak-oil] to ease the pain of the flesh and reduce the swelling [aat]. One may also prepare a decoction [pesesh] of the djed plant in beer [henqet], and this is to be administered by mouth if the patient's throat [aat en kherp] be affected by the venom. The incantation [rw] to be spoken: 'O poison, come forth! Fall to the ground! The words of Isis are spoken against thee; Ra speaks and the venom has no power [sekheret].' These words are spoken over the remedy before it is applied to the patient [iri medjat]. Note: the djed plant is known to cool heat and dispel poison when used fresh.

segments.title_inferred: Kyphi (Kapet): Sacred Egyptian Compound Incense and Medicinal Preparation
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Kyphi (kapet): the sacred Egyptian compound incense and medicine. 16 ingredients including raisins, wine, honey, myrrh, juniper, calamus, lemongrass. Used for respiratory diseases and as a sedative.
segments.translation: Kyphi [Egyptian: kapet] — the sacred compound incense and medicinal preparation of ancient Egypt, composed of sixteen ingredients including raisins, wine, honey, myrrh, juniper berries, calamus [sweet flag], and lemongrass. Prescribed for diseases of the respiratory tract and administered as a sedative agent.

segments.title_inferred: Materia Medica: Complete Catalogue of Drugs in the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Materia medica: catalogued drugs. Complete list of plant, animal, and mineral drugs used across all prescriptions — over 700 substances identified. Key plants: castor oil plant, lotus, mandrake, sycamore fig, pomegranate, acacia.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus contains a comprehensive materia medica — a catalogued inventory of drugs drawn from plant, animal, and mineral sources employed across all recorded prescriptions. Over 700 distinct substances have been identified within the text. Among the key botanical entries are: the castor oil plant, the lotus, mandrake, the sycamore fig, pomegranate, and acacia. These plants appear repeatedly across therapeutic prescriptions addressing a wide range of conditions, and are prepared by various methods including decoction, direct topical application, fumigation, suppository, and oral ingestion in admixture with carriers such as beer, honey, milk, and oil.

segments.title_inferred: Plant Oils and Animal Fats in Egyptian Pharmacy: Castor, Olive, Moringa, Linseed, and Animal-Derived Fats
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Plant oils and fats: medicinal uses of castor oil (kiki), olive oil, moringa oil (balanites), linseed oil, goose fat, beef fat, and crocodile fat in Egyptian pharmacy.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the medicinal application of a range of plant-derived oils and animal fats within the Egyptian pharmacopoeia. These substances served as both active therapeutic agents and as vehicles [carriers] for the delivery of other medicinal ingredients. The principal oils and fats documented include: (1) Castor oil [Egyptian: kiki] — expressed from the seeds of Ricinus communis, used extensively as a purgative [laxative], topical emollient, and vehicle for compound remedies; applied internally to evacuate the bowels and externally to treat scalp and skin conditions, including alopecia and parasitic infections of the hair. (2) Olive oil [Egyptian: nehet or merhet] — pressed from the fruit of Olea europaea, used as an emollient base for ointments, a vehicle for medicinal compounds, a soothing application for wounds and burns, and a dietary supplement in preparations for systemic ailments. (3) Moringa oil [Egyptian: bak, derived from Balanites aegyptiaca or Moringa peregrina] — extracted from the seeds of the moringa or desert date tree; used as a perfumed ointment base, a wound dressing, a topical treatment for skin lesions, and a vehicle for medicinal compounds; highly valued for its stability and resistance to rancidity in the Egyptian climate. (4) Linseed oil [from Linum usitatissimum] — derived from pressed flaxseed; used internally as an emollient laxative and externally as a soothing application for inflamed or irritated tissues. (5) Goose fat [Egyptian: merit or adj-fat of goose] — rendered fat of Anser anser or related waterfowl; employed as an emollient and penetrating vehicle in topical ointments, particularly for joint pain, muscular ailments, alopecia treatments, and wound management; considered to have superior penetrating properties. (6) Beef fat [rendered tallow from cattle] — used as an ointment base and emollient, particularly in preparations for skin diseases, burns, and joint conditions; also incorporated into compound suppositories and pessaries. (7) Crocodile fat [from Crocodylus niloticus] — a prestige and magico-medical ingredient; applied topically, particularly to the scalp in hair-growth and hair-darkening preparations, and used in ointments for specific ailments; its use may reflect both empirical and ritual/symbolic dimensions of Egyptian medicine.

segments.title_inferred: Honey and Bee Products as Medicinal Agents in Ancient Egyptian Practice
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Honey and bee products: honey as the primary wound dressing in ancient Egyptian medicine. Also beeswax (for ointments), bee larvae (protein), propolis (antibacterial).
segments.translation: Honey and its associated bee products occupy a central role in ancient Egyptian medical practice as documented in the Ebers Papyrus. Honey served as the primary topical wound dressing, applied directly to lacerations, burns, and infected tissue to promote healing and prevent putrefaction. Beeswax was employed as a binding and emollient agent in the formulation of ointments and salves. Bee larvae, rich in protein, appear as a nutritive or therapeutic ingredient in certain preparations. Propolis, the resinous substance collected by bees from plant sources, functioned as an antibacterial and sealing agent in medical compounds.

segments.title_inferred: Mineral Drugs in the Ebers Papyrus: Natron, Malachite, Ochre, Galena, Alum, Calcite, and Salt
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Mineral drugs: natron (sodium carbonate), malachite (copper carbonate), red ochre (iron oxide), lead sulfide (galena/kohl), alum, calcite, salt. All with specific therapeutic applications.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the use of several mineral substances as therapeutic agents, each assigned to specific medical conditions and preparation methods: (1) Natron [Egyptian: nṯry / netjery] — hydrated sodium carbonate/bicarbonate, sourced from desert lake deposits (notably Wadi Natrun); used as a cleansing, preservative, and antiseptic agent, applied topically and internally. (2) Malachite [Egyptian: wꜣḏ / wadj, 'the green one'] — copper carbonate hydroxide, ground and applied as an ophthalmic remedy for eye diseases including conjunctivitis and trachoma; its copper ions confer genuine antimicrobial efficacy. (3) Red Ochre [Egyptian: mnšt or similar] — iron oxide (hematite), used in wound dressings, skin treatments, and as a styptic/haemostatic agent; also carried ritual-protective valence. (4) Lead Sulfide / Galena [Egyptian: msdmt / mesdemet] — the primary component of kohl eyeliner; applied periocularly to treat and prevent eye infections and reduce glare; modern research suggests lead(II) ions may stimulate nitric oxide production, conferring a degree of antimicrobial protection, though chronic toxicity is a significant concern. (5) Alum [Egyptian: approximately ḥmt or referenced by function] — potassium aluminium sulfate; used as an astringent, styptic, and antiseptic in wound care and gynecological preparations. (6) Calcite [Egyptian: šsp or inr HD, 'white stone'] — calcium carbonate; ground and incorporated into medicinal pastes and suppositories; used as a base/filler and possibly as an antacid. (7) Salt [Egyptian: ḥmꜣt / hemat] — sodium chloride; widely employed as a preservative, antiseptic wound wash, and component of compound remedies; used both internally and externally.

segments.title_inferred: Garlic (Allium sativum) in the Ebers Papyrus: Therapeutic Applications and Cultural Context
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Garlic (Allium sativum) in Egyptian medicine: extensive use across the papyrus — 22 separate prescriptions. For heart disease, tumours, infestations, infections. Workers at Giza paid in garlic.
segments.translation: Garlic [Ancient Egyptian: likely 'ḥdw' or 'ipt', though orthography varies by column] appears in twenty-two distinct prescriptions throughout the Ebers Papyrus, making it one of the most frequently cited medicinal substances in the entire text. Its therapeutic applications span a broad range of conditions, including: diseases of the heart [Egyptian conceptualisation of cardiac and circulatory disorders], tumorous or abnormal growths [swellings, cysts, or neoplastic lesions as understood in Egyptian medical theory], parasitic and microbial infestations [including intestinal worms and putrefactive infections], and generalised infections [both internal and topical]. Garlic was administered via multiple preparation methods including oral decoction, direct topical application, and incorporation into compound remedies. Of additional historical and economic significance, documentary evidence records that labourers engaged in construction at the Giza plateau were remunerated in part with garlic rations, attesting to its perceived value as both a nutritive and prophylactic substance in ancient Egyptian society.

segments.title_inferred: Onion (Allium cepa) in Egyptian Medical Practice: Prescriptions, Therapeutic Uses, and Archaeological Context
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Onion (Allium cepa) in Egyptian medicine: 14 prescriptions. For coughs, fevers, skin conditions. Found in the tombs of pharaohs.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus records fourteen distinct prescriptions involving the onion (Allium cepa) within the corpus of Egyptian medical practice. These prescriptions address a range of conditions including coughs, febrile states [fever], and disorders of the skin. Onions have additionally been attested archaeologically through their recovery from the funerary contexts of pharaonic tombs, indicating both their medicinal and symbolic or ritual significance in ancient Egyptian culture.

segments.title_inferred: Sycamore Fig (Ficus sycomorus) as Medicinal Vehicle and Therapeutic Agent in the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Fig (Ficus sycomorus, sycamore fig) in Egyptian medicine: the primary sweet vehicle for medicines. Used in 59 prescriptions. Also specific therapeutic actions.
segments.translation: The sycamore fig (Ficus sycomorus), known in ancient Egyptian as 'nehet' (nḥt) for the tree and 'dab' (dꜣb) for the fruit, functions in the Ebers Papyrus chiefly as the primary sweet vehicle [pharmaceutical excipient] for compounding medicines. It appears in approximately 59 distinct prescriptions throughout the papyrus, making it one of the most frequently cited botanical ingredients in the entire corpus. Beyond its role as a carrier or masking agent for bitter or unpalatable drugs, the sycamore fig is also attributed with specific independent therapeutic actions, including laxative properties, wound-healing applications, and use in treating gastrointestinal complaints. The fruit's natural sweetness and soft, pulpy texture made it ideal for combining with powdered minerals, dried herbs, and other medicinal substances to produce palatable oral preparations. It was employed in decoctions, pastes, and direct topical applications. The fig tree itself — its fruit, sap, and bark — held both practical and ritual significance in Egyptian culture, further reinforcing its prominent role in medical texts.

segments.title_inferred: Acacia (Acacia nilotica, shont) in Egyptian Medicine: Uses, Preparations, and Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Acacia (Acacia nilotica, shont) in Egyptian medicine: pods, bark, gum, seeds used across 47 prescriptions. Antibacterial, astringent. Key ingredient in contraceptive pessaries.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the use of Acacia (known in ancient Egyptian as 'shont'), identified as Acacia nilotica, across 47 distinct medical prescriptions. Multiple parts of the plant were employed therapeutically, including the pods, bark, gum, and seeds. The plant was recognised for its antibacterial and astringent properties. Notably, it served as a principal active ingredient in contraceptive pessaries [vaginal suppositories used to prevent conception].

segments.title_inferred: Castor Oil Plant (Ricinus communis / degem) in Egyptian Medicine: 30 Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Castor oil plant (Ricinus communis, degem) in Egyptian medicine: 30 prescriptions. Laxative, skin conditions, head complaints, hair growth.
segments.translation: The castor oil plant, known in ancient Egyptian as 'degem' (Ricinus communis), is documented across 30 separate prescriptions within the Ebers Papyrus. Its recorded therapeutic applications include use as a laxative [purgative agent], treatment of various skin conditions, remedies for complaints of the head, and promotion of hair growth.

segments.title_inferred: Pomegranate (Punica granatum) as Anthelmintic and Digestive Remedy
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Pomegranate (Punica granatum, inhemen) in Egyptian medicine: bark used as anthelmintic (tapeworm). Fruit for digestive issues. One of the most scientifically validated Egyptian remedies.
segments.translation: The pomegranate, known in ancient Egyptian as 'inhemen' (inḥmn), is documented in the Ebers Papyrus as a medicinal plant of considerable importance. The bark of the pomegranate was employed as an anthelmintic agent, specifically prescribed for the expulsion of tapeworms [internal parasites]. The fruit was additionally administered for the treatment of digestive complaints. This remedy is regarded as one of the most robustly scientifically validated treatments recorded in the Egyptian medical corpus.

segments.title_inferred: Frankincense (Boswellia sacra) in Egyptian Medicine: Therapeutic Applications Across 43 Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Frankincense (Boswellia sacra, sonter) in Egyptian medicine: 43 prescriptions. For respiratory disease, skin problems, wounds, and ritual purification. Anti-inflammatory (modern validation).
segments.translation: Frankincense, known in ancient Egyptian as 'sonter' (sntṛ), appears across 43 discrete prescriptions within the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), making it one of the most frequently cited therapeutic and ritual substances in the text. Its applications span four primary domains: (1) Respiratory disease — employed in fumigation preparations and inhalation therapies to treat conditions of the chest, throat, and airways; (2) Skin problems — applied topically, often in combination with fats or oils, to address dermatological complaints including lesions, pustules, and inflammatory skin conditions; (3) Wound care — used as a wound dressing agent, likely exploiting its known antimicrobial and resinous sealing properties; (4) Ritual purification — burned as incense in contexts where the boundary between medicinal and sacred practice was deliberately maintained, consistent with Egyptian therapeutic ideology. Modern pharmacological investigation has validated the anti-inflammatory activity of frankincense resin, attributing this action primarily to boswellic acids (notably acetyl-11-keto-β-boswellic acid, AKBA), which inhibit 5-lipoxygenase, a key enzyme in the leukotriene inflammatory pathway. This modern validation lends pharmacological credibility to its pervasive use in ancient Egyptian wound and respiratory therapies.

segments.title_inferred: Myrrh (antyw) in Egyptian Medicine: Therapeutic Applications and Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Myrrh (Commiphora myrrha, antyw) in Egyptian medicine: 31 prescriptions. Antiseptic, analgesic, for wounds and infections. One of the most exported Egyptian drugs.
segments.translation: Myrrh — known in ancient Egyptian as 'antyw' — appears in 31 distinct prescriptions throughout the Ebers Papyrus, making it one of the most frequently cited medicinal substances in the text. It was employed primarily for its antiseptic and analgesic properties, applied to wounds, abscesses, and various infectious conditions of the skin and body. Myrrh was among the most widely traded and exported pharmaceutical commodities of ancient Egypt, prized across the ancient Near East and Mediterranean world for its therapeutic and ritual value.

segments.title_inferred: Blue Lotus (Nymphaea caerulea) in Egyptian Medicine: Psychoactive and Medicinal Properties
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Blue lotus (Nymphaea caerulea) in Egyptian medicine: psychoactive and medicinal. Contains apomorphine and nuciferine. Used for relaxation, cardiac conditions, reproductive issues.
segments.translation: The blue lotus (Nymphaea caerulea), known in ancient Egyptian medical tradition, is documented in the Ebers Papyrus for its psychoactive and medicinal properties. The plant contains the alkaloids apomorphine and nuciferine. It was employed therapeutically for the induction of relaxation and sedation, the treatment of cardiac [heart] conditions, and the management of reproductive ailments.

segments.title_inferred: Moringa Oil in Ancient Egyptian Medicine: Anti-Inflammatory, Wound Healing, and Cosmetic Uses
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Moringa (Moringa peregrina, balanites) in Egyptian medicine: oil used extensively. Anti-inflammatory, wound healing, cosmetic. Found in ancient Egyptian tombs.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the extensive use of Moringa (identified with Moringa peregrina and/or Balanites aegyptiaca) in ancient Egyptian medical practice. The oil extracted from this plant was employed broadly for its anti-inflammatory properties, its capacity to promote wound healing, and for cosmetic applications. Physical specimens and residues associated with this plant have been recovered from ancient Egyptian tomb contexts, corroborating its documented importance.

segments.title_inferred: Cumin (Cuminum cyminum / tepnen) in the Ebers Papyrus: Medicinal Uses and Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Cumin (Cuminum cyminum, tepnen) in Egyptian medicine: 19 prescriptions. Digestive, expectorant, externally for pain. Combined with goose fat as ointment base.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus records cumin — known in ancient Egyptian as 'tepnen' — across 19 distinct prescriptions. It was employed internally as a digestive remedy and expectorant, and applied externally for the relief of pain. A common preparation involved combining cumin with goose fat to form an ointment base [for topical application].

segments.title_inferred: Coriander (Coriandrum sativum) in Egyptian Medical Prescriptions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Coriander (Coriandrum sativum, shaw) in Egyptian medicine: seeds in prescriptions for digestive disorders, infections, pain relief.
segments.translation: Coriander, known in ancient Egyptian as 'shaw' (šꜣw), appears in the Ebers Papyrus as a medicinal seed ingredient incorporated into compound prescriptions addressing digestive complaints, infectious conditions, and the alleviation of pain. The seeds were employed in formulations that likely included decoctions and topical preparations consistent with broader Egyptian pharmaceutical practice.

segments.title_inferred: Fenugreek (Hemayt) in the Ebers Papyrus: Therapeutic Applications and Modern Validation
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum, hemayt) in Egyptian medicine: 18 prescriptions. Digestive, anti-inflammatory, wound healing. Validated by modern research.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents fenugreek — known in ancient Egyptian as 'hemayt' (ḥmꜣyt) — across 18 distinct prescriptions, making it one of the more frequently cited botanicals in the corpus. Its recorded therapeutic applications encompass digestive complaints, inflammatory conditions, and wound healing. These uses have received corroboration from modern pharmacological research.

segments.title_inferred: Thyme (Thymus species) as Antimicrobial, Respiratory, and Wound-Care Agent in Egyptian Medicine
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Thyme (Thymus species) in Egyptian medicine: as antimicrobial agent, for chest complaints, wound dressing. Used in mummification process.
segments.translation: Thyme (Thymus species) is documented in Egyptian medical practice as an antimicrobial agent, employed in the treatment of chest complaints [respiratory ailments], used as a wound dressing [topical application to injuries or lesions], and incorporated into the mummification process [funerary/preservative rites].

segments.title_inferred: Mandrake (rerery) in Egyptian Medicine: Soporific, Analgesic, and Anesthetic Uses
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Mandrake (Mandragora officinarum, rerery) in Egyptian medicine: soporific, pain relief, anesthesia during procedures. Associated with fertility.
segments.translation: The plant known in ancient Egyptian as 'rerery' (mandrake; Mandragora officinarum) was employed in Egyptian medical practice for several therapeutic purposes: as a soporific agent to induce sleep, as an analgesic for the relief of pain, and as an anesthetic to render patients insensible during medical or surgical procedures. The plant was further associated with fertility and reproductive concerns in the Egyptian medical and ritual tradition.

segments.title_inferred: Aloe vera in Egyptian Medicine: Wound Healing, Burns, and Skin Conditions
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Aloe vera (Aloe succotrina) in Egyptian medicine: wound healing, burns, skin conditions. Cleopatra reportedly used aloe for skin care.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the use of aloe [ancient Egyptian name uncertain; possibly referenced as a bitter, gel-bearing plant] in the treatment of wounds, burns, and various skin conditions. The plant identified with modern Aloe vera or the closely related Aloe succotrina was applied topically, its gel or expressed juice used as a soothing and wound-closing agent [Egyptian: likely a direct topical application or poultice]. Classical and later tradition holds that Cleopatra VII incorporated aloe preparations into her personal skin care regimen, suggesting its cosmetic as well as medicinal esteem in the ancient Egyptian world.

segments.title_inferred: Carob (djaret) in Egyptian Medicine: Digestive and Anti-Diarrheal Applications
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Carob (Ceratonia siliqua, djaret) in Egyptian medicine: pods in digestive remedies, as sweetener base, anti-diarrheal. 14 prescriptions.
segments.translation: The carob — known in ancient Egyptian as 'djaret' (Ceratonia siliqua) — appears across 14 distinct prescriptions within the Ebers Papyrus. The pods of this plant were employed in digestive remedies, serving both as a functional therapeutic agent and as a sweetening base to render medicinal preparations more palatable. Its primary documented therapeutic application is as an anti-diarrheal agent [Egyptian medical text employs carob as a binding, astringent agent in gastrointestinal complaints]. The pods were likely prepared as decoctions or incorporated as a paste or powder into compound remedies, consistent with Egyptian pharmaceutical practice of the period.

segments.title_inferred: Cedar (Cedrus libanotica) in Egyptian Medicine: Therapeutic Uses and Preparations
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Cedar (Cedrus libanotica) in Egyptian medicine: cedar oil imported from Lebanon. Used in 14 prescriptions for skin diseases, wounds, ear complaints, as insect repellent.
segments.translation: Cedar (Cedrus libanotica) as documented in the Ebers Papyrus: Cedar oil, imported from Lebanon, appears in at least 14 distinct prescriptions within the papyrus. Its recorded therapeutic applications include treatment of skin diseases, wound care, remedies for ear complaints, and use as an insect repellent.

segments.title_inferred: Calamus (Sweet Flag) as Aromatic Stimulant and Carminative in Egyptian Medicine
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Calamus/sweet flag (Acorus calamus) in Egyptian medicine: aromatic stimulant in compound medicines, especially kyphi. Carminative, anti-spasmodic.
segments.translation: Calamus (sweet flag; Acorus calamus) appears in Egyptian medicine as an aromatic stimulant incorporated into compound medicinal preparations, most notably kyphi — the sacred incense-medicine used in temple ritual and therapeutic contexts. It was employed for its carminative properties (relieving gas and digestive discomfort) and its anti-spasmodic action (reducing involuntary muscular contractions and cramps).

segments.title_inferred: Poppy (Papaver species) in Egyptian Medicine: Pain Relief and Children's Colic Remedies
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Poppy (Papaver species, sepen) in Egyptian medicine: opium-containing prescriptions for pain relief — especially notable in children's colic remedies (poppy seeds + fly excrement).
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the use of the poppy plant, known in ancient Egyptian as 'sepen,' in a range of medicinal preparations containing opium-active constituents, prescribed principally for the relief of pain. Of particular note is a remedy formulated for infant colic, in which poppy seeds are combined with fly excrement [likely dried insect frass used as a binding or activating agent in compound prescriptions] and administered to young children to quiet excessive crying and alleviate abdominal discomfort. This represents one of the earliest recorded uses of an opiate-containing substance in pediatric medicine.

segments.title_inferred: Dill (Anethum graveolens) in Egyptian Medicine: Digestive, Carminative, and Antiparasitic Uses
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Dill (Anethum graveolens) in Egyptian medicine: digestive, carminative, for stomach complaints and intestinal worms.
segments.translation: The Ebers Papyrus documents the medicinal use of dill (Anethum graveolens) in ancient Egyptian medical practice. Dill was employed as a digestive aid and carminative agent, prescribed for stomach complaints and to expel intestinal worms [parasitic infestation]. The plant appears in multiple formularies within the papyrus as a component of compound remedies addressing gastrointestinal disturbances, including bloating, flatulence, and helminthic [worm] infestations. Preparations likely included decoctions of the seeds or aerial parts, administered orally. Dill's aromatic volatile oils — principally carvone and limonene — are recognized in modern pharmacology as having antispasmodic, carminative, and mild antimicrobial properties, lending scientific credibility to its ancient therapeutic applications.

segments.title_inferred: Lettuce (abu) in Egyptian Medicine: Aphrodisiac, Fertility, and Cooling Applications
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Lettuce (Lactuca sativa, abu) in Egyptian medicine: sacred to Min (fertility god). Used in aphrodisiac and fertility preparations. Also cooling properties.
segments.translation: Lettuce, known in ancient Egyptian as 'abu' (𓇑 / transliterated: ꜣbw), was a plant of significant ritual and medicinal importance in Egyptian medicine. It was sacred to Min, the god of fertility and virility, and featured prominently in aphrodisiac and fertility-enhancing preparations. The plant was also recognised for its cooling properties, likely employed in treatments for febrile conditions, inflammation, or heat-related ailments. As documented in the Ebers Papyrus, lettuce was administered in preparations intended to stimulate sexual potency and promote reproductive capacity, consistent with its ritual association with the cult of Min. Its cooling humoral character further positioned it as a therapeutic agent in contexts requiring reduction of bodily heat.

segments.title_inferred: Willow (Salix spp.) in Egyptian Medicine: Bark Decoctions for Pain and Fever
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Willow (Salix species, tjereret) in Egyptian medicine: bark decoctions for pain and fever — the ancient salicylic acid. Anti-inflammatory validated by modern pharmacology.
segments.translation: The willow tree, known in ancient Egyptian as 'tjereret' (Salix species), is documented in the Ebers Papyrus as a medicinal plant of significant therapeutic value. Decoctions prepared from its bark were prescribed for the relief of pain and the reduction of fever. This application represents one of the earliest recorded uses of what modern pharmacology identifies as salicylic acid — the active principle from which aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) is derived. The anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties of willow bark have been extensively validated by contemporary pharmacological research, confirming the empirical efficacy observed and recorded by ancient Egyptian physicians.

segments.title_inferred: Juniper (wan) in Egyptian Medicine: Therapeutic Uses of Berries and Oil
segments.original_text: Ebers Papyrus — Juniper (Juniperus species, wan) in Egyptian medicine: berries and oil in 14 prescriptions. For urinary complaints, digestive disorders, joint pain.
segments.translation: Juniper [Egyptian: wan], identified with Juniperus species, appears across 14 prescriptions in the Ebers Papyrus. Its berries and extracted oil were employed therapeutically for urinary complaints [likely including dysuria and difficulty in urination], digestive disorders [including bloating, constipation, and gastrointestinal pain], and joint pain [possibly rheumatic or arthritic conditions]. Preparations included decoctions of the berries for internal administration and oil-based formulations for topical or internal use. Juniper was valued as a diuretic, carminative, and anti-inflammatory agent within the Egyptian pharmacopoeia.

PAGE 173 173 175 175 175 177 179 179 179 INTRODUCTION It is often assumed even by the most learned historians that the history of medicine began with the Greeks, and that before the time of Hippocrates there was little or nothing that can be called a science of medicine. Yet for more than thirty centuries before the emancipation of human reason in Ionia, numerous practitioners had been attempting to diagnose and treat disease in Egypt, Mesopotamia, and elsewhere. The Papyrus Ebers is the longest and the most famous of the documents relating to this more ancient practice of medicine. The Papyrus, although written about 1500 B.C., is really a collection of bits and pieces of folk-lore, much of it five and some of it probably twenty centuries older. It is expressed in archaic phraseology, so that many of the terms relating to the diseases and drugs are impossible to identify. In the face of these insuperable difficulties, it is not surprising that most scholars have refrained from risking their reputation for scholarship by pretending to translate a technical document in which many of the technical terms are at present untranslatable. An heroic attempt to provide a translation in German was made, however, many years ago by Dr. H. Joachim. This attempt naturally provoked many criticisms, especially from those scholars who had the philological knowledge to recognize the solecisms. But if the time is not yet ripe for an adequate translation, the Papyrus is so interesting that no excuse should be necessary for providing a rendering in English of the only version at present available for those who cannot read the scripts of Ancient Egypt. Hence Dr. Cyril Bryan is to be commended for giving us in English an interpretation based upon Dr. Joachim's translation, though not slavishly following it.
