Whom God loves, he quickens. I am one whom God loves, therefore he quickens me. WORDS TO BE SPOKEN IN THE PREPARING OF MEDICINES FOR ALL PARTS OF THE BODY OF A PERSON WHO IS ILL. As it is to be, a thousand times. This is the book for the healing of all diseases. May Isis heal me even as she healed Horus of all the pains which his brother Set had inflicted on him when he killed his brother Osiris! Oh Isis, thou great enchantress, heal me, deliver me from all evil, bad, typhonic things, from demoniacal and deadly diseases and pollutions of all sorts that rush upon me, as thou didst deliver and release thy son Horus!
segments.translation:
THE BEGINNING OF THE BOOK ON THE PREPARATION OF MEDICINES FOR ALL PARTS OF THE BODY OF A PERSON.

I came forth from Heliopolis with the priests of het-aat [the temple 'het-aat'], the Lords of Defence, the Kings of Eternity and of Protection. I came forth from Sais with the Maternal Goddesses who grant me protection. Words were given to me by the Lord of the Universe [i.e., the supreme deity] wherewith to drive away the afflictions sent by all the gods and deadly diseases of every kind.

SO MANY CHAPTERS ARE upon this my head, this my neck, these my arms, this my flesh, and these my limbs — to punish the mockeries of the High Ones [i.e., powerful divine beings] who cause this disease to enter my flesh by working magic upon these my limbs — so that as often as the disease penetrates into this my flesh, this my head, these my arms, my body, and these my limbs, Ra takes compassion and says: I will protect him from his enemies. His guide, Hermes [Thoth, here equated with Hermes Trismegistus], it is who has given him the words, who furnishes the books, and who bestows upon the learned and upon the physicians who follow him the glory of unravelling that which is obscure. Whom God loves, He quickens. I am one whom God loves; therefore He quickens me.

WORDS TO BE SPOKEN IN THE PREPARING OF MEDICINES FOR ALL PARTS OF THE BODY OF A PERSON WHO IS ILL.

As it is to be — a thousand times. This is the book for the healing of all diseases.

May Isis heal me even as she healed Horus of all the pains which his brother Set had inflicted upon him when Set killed his brother Osiris! Oh Isis, thou great enchantress, heal me, deliver me from all evil, harmful, typhonic [i.e., of or pertaining to the god Set/Typhon] things, from demoniacal and deadly diseases and pollutions of all sorts that rush upon me, as thou didst deliver and release thy son Horus!

segments.title_inferred: Ebers Papyrus: Ritual Incantations Before Taking Medicine and Chapter on Diseases of the Alimentary System (Constipation)
segments.original_text: Little am I and piteous! Oh Ra, thou who hast spoken for thy body! Oh Osiris, thou who prayest for thy manifestation! It is Ra speaketh for his body; it is Osiris prayeth for his manifestation. Deliver me then from all possible evil, from bad, wicked, typhonic things, from demoniacal and deadly fevers of every sort. SO MANY CHAPTERS THERE ARE TO SAY. AS IT IS TO BE, A THOUSAND TIMES! CHAPTER ON THE DRINKING OF MEDICINES. Here follow the medicines. They come to drive away all sorts of things in this my heart, in these my limbs. The charms have great powers over the remedy. Once again. Do I not remember that Horus and Set were led to the great forecourt of Heliopolis to consult over the testicles of Set, and Horus became fresh as he was on earth? He does everything as he wills, as do those of the gods who are yonder. Words to be said when one drinks the medicines. As it is to be, a thousand times. CHAPTER IX DISEASES OF THE ALIMENTARY SYSTEM: CONSTIPATION, ETC. A glance at the Papyrus Ebers dispels once for all any idea that Constipation is a scourge of the modern world brought about by modern conditions of living and eating. For page after page from the very beginning of the Papyrus the Scribe sets down purgative after purgative, all remedies designed to cure constipation or as he more crudely puts it 'to drive out the excrement in the body of a person.' Some of the preparations are to be drunk or eaten or chewed, some are to be made into pills and swallowed, others are to be formed into suppositories and inserted in the rectum. Castor Oil figures in not a few of them; unfortunately for the patients, in only one prescription is it the sole ingredient: REMEDY TO CLEAR OUT THE BODY AND TO GET RID OF THE EXCREMENT IN THE BODY OF A PERSON Berries-of-the-Castor-oil-tree I Chew and swallow down with Beer in order to clear out all that is in the body.
segments.translation: Little am I and piteous! Oh Ra, thou who hast spoken for thy body! Oh Osiris, thou who prayest for thy manifestation! It is Ra who speaketh for his body; it is Osiris who prayeth for his manifestation. Deliver me then from all possible evil, from bad, wicked, typhonic [Set-related, chaotic] things, from demoniacal and deadly fevers of every sort. SO MANY CHAPTERS THERE ARE TO SAY. AS IT IS TO BE, A THOUSAND TIMES! CHAPTER ON THE DRINKING OF MEDICINES. Here follow the medicines. They come to drive away all sorts of ailments in this my heart, in these my limbs. The charms [magical incantations] have great powers over the remedy. Once again: Do I not remember that Horus and Set were led to the great forecourt of Heliopolis to consult over the testicles of Set, and Horus became fresh [restored to health/vigor] as he was on earth? He does everything as he wills, as do those of the gods who are yonder. Words to be said when one drinks the medicines. As it is to be, a thousand times. CHAPTER IX: DISEASES OF THE ALIMENTARY SYSTEM: CONSTIPATION, ETC. A glance at the Papyrus Ebers dispels once and for all any idea that constipation is a scourge of the modern world brought about by modern conditions of living and eating. For page after page, from the very beginning of the Papyrus, the Scribe sets down purgative after purgative — all remedies designed to cure constipation, or as he more crudely puts it, 'to drive out the excrement in the body of a person.' Some of the preparations are to be drunk, eaten, or chewed; some are to be made into pills and swallowed; others are to be formed into suppositories and inserted in the rectum. Castor oil figures in not a few of them; unfortunately for the patients, in only one prescription is it the sole ingredient: REMEDY TO CLEAR OUT THE BODY AND TO GET RID OF THE EXCREMENT IN THE BODY OF A PERSON — Berries of the castor-oil tree, 1 [measure]. Chew and swallow down with beer in order to clear out all that is in the body.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Constipation and Evacuation from the Papyrus Ebers
segments.original_text: Dates | Sea-salt | sebbet-juice | Mix in water, place in an earthenware receptacle, and put therein: Crushed gengent-beans. Cook together, cover, and let the patient drink warm. Thereafter let him drink Sweet Beer. REMEDY TO REGULATE EVACUATION: Honey | sasa-seeds | Wormwood | Elderberry | Berries-of-the-uan-tree | Kernel-of-the-ut'ait-fruit | Caraway | aaam-seeds | Xam-seeds | Sea-salt | Form into a Suppository and put into the Rectum. ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT THE DISEASED EXCREMENT IN THE BODY OF A PERSON: White-cake | Red tit-corn | Milk-of-a-Woman | Mix into one and let the Person drink. ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT THE STERCORAL MASSES IN THE BODY: Figs 4 | sebesten 1 | Grapes | Caraway | Resin-of-Acanthus | Writing-fluid | Peppermint | gengent-beans 2 | Sweet Beer | Keep moist and take for four days. Mass action may account for the efficacy of the foregoing remedies—if they were efficacious—but even this cannot be urged in favour of other of the remedies. Verdigris, even when ground up with Bread, made into three Pills, and swallowed down with Sweet Beer, does not sound very promising; neither does a mixture of Milk, Dough, and Honey, though it be taken for four days; nor Elderberries and Honey cooked in Yeast-of-Beer-which-has-been-whipped-up; nor a draught composed of Goose-grease and Lead-vitriol, flavoured though it be with Wine. And not even the emphasis of the Scribe convinces the impartial critic of the efficacy of another. PREPARE THOU WELL THIS PURGATIVE. HEREAFTER IT IS USED AGAINST THE CONSTIPATED BODY. Herbs-of-the-Field | gengent-beans | aneb-plant | Bread-dough | Pound, make into one, make Four Cakes thereof, and let him eat. Another remedy for Constipation which not only claims to act as a purgative but drives out all the diseases in the body, smacks more of a dawn-of-history cocktail than a purgative. It consists of Half-an-Onion mixed in Froth-of-Beer, and is further described as 'A Delightful Remedy Against...'
segments.translation:
Dates, sea-salt, and sebbet-juice [an unidentified plant juice]: mix in water, place in an earthenware vessel, and add crushed gengent-beans [an unidentified legume]. Cook together, cover, and let the patient drink while warm. Afterward, let him drink sweet beer.

REMEDY TO REGULATE EVACUATION: Honey, sasa-seeds [unidentified], wormwood, elderberry, berries of the uan-tree [unidentified], kernel of the ut'ait-fruit [unidentified], caraway, aaam-seeds [unidentified], xam-seeds [unidentified], and sea-salt. Form into a suppository and insert into the rectum.

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT DISEASED EXCREMENT FROM THE BODY OF A PERSON: White-cake [a prepared flour cake], red tit-corn [an unidentified grain preparation], and milk of a woman [human breast milk]. Mix together and let the person drink.

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT STERCORAL [fecal] MASSES FROM THE BODY: Figs (4 parts), sebesten [Cordia myxa, a mucilaginous fruit] (1 part), grapes, caraway, resin of acanthus [acacia resin], writing-fluid [ink, possibly carbon-based], peppermint, gengent-beans (2 parts), and sweet beer. Keep moist and take over four days.

Mass action [i.e., the combined bulk or laxative effect of multiple ingredients] may account for the efficacy of the foregoing remedies—if they were indeed efficacious—but even this explanation cannot be applied in favour of certain other remedies. Verdigris [copper acetate], even when ground with bread, formed into three pills, and swallowed with sweet beer, does not sound very promising; nor does a mixture of milk, dough, and honey, even if taken for four days; nor elderberries and honey cooked in yeast of beer that has been whipped; nor a draught of goose-grease and lead vitriol [lead sulfate], however flavoured with wine. Not even the scribal emphasis convinces the impartial critic of the efficacy of the following:

PREPARE THOU WELL THIS PURGATIVE. HEREAFTER IT IS USED AGAINST THE CONSTIPATED BODY. Herbs of the field, gengent-beans, aneb-plant [unidentified], and bread-dough. Pound and combine into one mass; form into four cakes and let the patient eat them.

Another remedy for constipation, which claims not only to act as a purgative but to expel all diseases from the body, resembles more a dawn-of-history cocktail than a medicinal preparation. It consists of half an onion mixed in the froth of beer, and is further described as 'A Delightful Remedy Against...' [text continues beyond segment].

segments.title_inferred: Ebers Papyrus: Remedies for Constipation, Diarrhoea, and Indigestion (Alimentary Diseases)
segments.original_text: Wax Water Cook and take for four days. ANOTHER Breadmeal White-of-Egg Onions Water Drink for four days. ANOTHER Breadmeal Fresh Lead-earth White-of-Egg Water 4 dena. Take for four days. ANOTHER Figs Grapes CONSTIPATION, ETC. Bread-dough Pit-corn Fresh Lead-earth Onions Elderberry O, Heru! Again: O, Heru! CHAPTER X DISEASES OF THE ALIMENTARY SYSTEM: INDIGESTION, ETC. THE BEGINNING OF THE REMEDIES TO DRIVE AWAY INDIGESTION Green Onions Put in water in a new hunnu-glass and take for four days. ANOTHER Onions Cook in Sweet Beer and drink the third part thereof for four days. ANOTHER Take a Casserole, half-filled with Water, half with Onions. Let it stand for four days. See that it does not become dry. After it has stood moist, beat to a froth one-fourth of the third-part of the contents of this vessel, and let him who suffers from the vomiting drink it for four days so that he may become well immediately. ANOTHER Date-meal Make into a dough in a hennu-vessel, put into two earthenware jugs, and set on the fire so that the dough cracks. When this has happened, put the pap in Fat and Olive Oil. To be eaten hot by the patient so that he may become well immediately.
segments.translation:
REMEDIES FOR CONSTIPATION AND RELATED CONDITIONS:

[Recipe 1] Wax; Water. Cook and take for four days.

[Recipe 2 — ANOTHER] Breadmeal; White of Egg; Onions; Water. Drink for four days.

[Recipe 3 — ANOTHER] Breadmeal; Fresh Lead-earth [galena or ochre]; White of Egg; Water — 4 dena [a measure]. Take for four days.

[Recipe 4 — ANOTHER] Figs; Grapes; Bread-dough; Pit-corn [emmer wheat or similar grain]; Fresh Lead-earth; Onions; Elderberry. [Accompanied by the ritual exclamation:] 'O, Heru! Again: O, Heru!'

CHAPTER X: DISEASES OF THE ALIMENTARY SYSTEM — INDIGESTION, ETC.

THE BEGINNING OF THE REMEDIES TO DRIVE AWAY INDIGESTION:

[Recipe 1] Green Onions. Place in water in a new hunnu-vessel [a standard Egyptian liquid measure vessel] and take for four days.

[Recipe 2 — ANOTHER] Onions. Cook in Sweet Beer and drink one-third of it for four days.

[Recipe 3 — ANOTHER] Take a casserole, fill it half with Water and half with Onions. Let it stand for four days; ensure it does not dry out. After it has stood moist, beat one-quarter of one-third of the contents to a froth, and let the patient who suffers from vomiting drink it for four days, that he may recover immediately.

[Recipe 4 — ANOTHER] Date-meal. Form into a dough in a hennu-vessel [standard measure vessel]; place into two earthenware jugs and set upon the fire until the dough cracks. When this has occurred, mix the resulting paste with Fat and Olive Oil. To be eaten hot by the patient, that he may recover immediately.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Indigestion from the Papyrus Ebers
segments.original_text:
by crushing Wild Lettuce in a sauce composed of Oil, Beer and Sour Milk, cooked together in a basin. While much ink has been used up throughout the ages in setting down remedies upon remedies for Indigestion, it was only in Ancient Egypt that the ink-bottle itself was spilt into the remedy:

ANOTHER READY REMEDY TO DRIVE INDIGESTION OUT OF THE BODY
Figs 4, sebesten 4, Grapes, Caraway, Resin-of-Acanthus, Writing-fluid, Peppermint, gengent-beans 3, Sweet Beer. Keep moist and take for four days.

Another remedy was probably intended for those victims of indigestion who complain of a stone-like feeling in the pit of the stomach:

ANOTHER
fut-ab-grain I, meni-grain (an aromatic resin) I, aam-plant I. Crush into one. Take Seven Stones and make them hot in the fire. Take one of them and put therein the above-mentioned ingredients. Cover it up in a new vessel, bore a hole in its bottom (? lid), and thrust a calmus-reed through this hole. Put thy mouth to this reed that thou mayst inhale the steam therefrom. Likewise with the remaining six Stones. Afterwards eat something fatty, either Fat Meat or Oil.

ANOTHER REMEDY FOR INDIGESTION
A Hog's Tooth. Crush to powder, put inside four Sugar-cakes, and eat for four days.

A Hog's Tooth! What an inspiration! What an illustration of the habit of observation practised by these old-time medicos; for what is there on this earth that the tooth of a hog halts at on the score of indigestibility? Certainly this particular remedy is no longer in use among the best physicians, but it is still to the hog that the medical world looks to alleviate the pangs of Indigestion. All that we have done to improve on these pioneer organo-therapeutists is to powder his pancreas gland instead of his tooth, and to put it up in sugar-coated pills instead of the more clumsy sugar-cakes. Sometimes the indigestion went further and resu[lted in...]
segments.translation:
Remedies for indigestion as recorded in the Papyrus Ebers:

[First remedy, context from preceding chunk:] Wild Lettuce is crushed into a preparation composed of Oil, Beer, and Sour Milk, cooked together in a basin.

ANOTHER READY REMEDY TO EXPEL INDIGESTION FROM THE BODY:
Figs — 4 [parts]; Sebesten [fruit] — 4 [parts]; Grapes; Caraway; Resin of Acanthus; Writing-fluid [ink]; Peppermint; gengent-beans — 3 [parts]; Sweet Beer. Keep moist and take [the preparation] over four days.

ANOTHER REMEDY [likely for the sensation of heaviness or a stone-like feeling in the stomach]:
fut-ab-grain — 1 [part]; meni-grain (an aromatic resin) — 1 [part]; aam-plant — 1 [part]. Crush all together into one [mixture]. Take seven stones and heat them in the fire. Take one stone and place therein the above-mentioned ingredients. Cover it in a new vessel; bore a hole in its bottom [or possibly: lid], and pass a calamus-reed through this hole. Place thy mouth upon this reed so that thou mayest inhale the steam therefrom. Repeat likewise with the remaining six stones. Afterwards, eat something fatty — either fat meat or oil.

ANOTHER REMEDY FOR INDIGESTION:
A Hog's Tooth. Crush to powder; place inside four sugar-cakes; and eat over four days.

[Editorial commentary by the modern author:] A Hog's Tooth! What an inspiration — and what an illustration of the observational habits practised by these ancient physicians. For what substance on earth is more indigestible than the tooth of a hog? Admittedly, this remedy is no longer in use among the best physicians, yet it is still to the pig that the medical world turns for relief from the pangs of indigestion. The only improvement modern organo-therapeutists have made upon their ancient predecessors is to powder the animal's pancreas gland rather than its tooth, and to present it in sugar-coated pills rather than the cruder sugar-cakes. Sometimes the indigestion progressed further and result[ed in further complications...]

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Abdominal Complaints, Indigestion, Worms, and Parasites from the Papyrus Ebers
segments.original_text: Figs a little Sycamore-dough or Onions-from-the-Oasis. Crush and grind in Sweet Beer to which has been added the strength of the required herbs (also add Wheat and Barley Beer). Keep moist. Do not let it dry or a scum form thereover. Put thereto: Honey, Goose-fat. Make into one and let the man or woman drink it. ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT THE HARDENING IN THE ABDOMEN: Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus, Cat's dung, Red-lead, Watermelon, Sweet Beer, Wine. Make into one and apply as a plaster. ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT INFLAMMATION IN THE ABDOMEN: A-piece-of-the-Stem-of-the-Flax-Plant, Fresh Milk. Apply to the abdomen of the Sick Person. ANOTHER: Oil-from-the-Top-of-the-des-jar. Put on the Patient's abdomen. TO KILL THE ROUNDWORM: Bark-of-the-Pomegranate-root, Water. Keep moist, strain, and take for a day. ANOTHER: Inner-of-the-Fruit-of-the-Castor-oil-tree, Yeast, Water. Keep moist, strain, and take for a day. The Tapeworm proved more obstinate. Worm-cakes were recommended first. One cake was composed of Herbs-of-the-Field and Natron, baked in [continued].
segments.translation:
Take figs, a little sycamore-dough, or onions from the Oasis. Crush and grind these in sweet beer to which the potency of the required herbs has been added (also add wheat and barley beer). Keep the mixture moist; do not allow it to dry out or a scum to form over it. Add to it: honey and goose fat. Combine into one preparation and have the man or woman drink it.

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT HARDENING IN THE ABDOMEN:
Bread of the Zizyphus-Lotus [jujube bread], cat's dung, red-lead [minium], watermelon, sweet beer, wine. Combine into one and apply as a plaster.

A plaster combining cat's dung with watermelon might be counted upon to remove 'hardenings' anywhere and in anyone. The inflamed abdomen was not required to endure such indignities, however. The remedies were many, but two will suffice:

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT INFLAMMATION IN THE ABDOMEN:
A piece of the stem of the flax plant, fresh milk. Apply to the abdomen of the sick person.

ANOTHER [REMEDY]:
Oil from the top of the des-jar [a type of storage vessel]. Place on the patient's abdomen.

None of the eight hundred remedies found in the Papyrus appear to have actually killed any of those whom they were intended to benefit. This is remarkable. But what is stranger still is that they did not prove fatal to every kind of parasite lurking within the alimentary canal of the ancient Egyptian. That they did not do so is evident from the remedies given to expel these parasites — both roundworms and tapeworms.

The roundworm was expelled by means of mixtures and draughts, two of the latter meriting particular commendation:

TO KILL THE ROUNDWORM:
Bark of the pomegranate root, water. Keep moist, strain, and take [the draught] for one day.

ANOTHER [REMEDY]:
The inner part of the fruit of the castor-oil tree [castor bean], yeast, water. Keep moist, strain, and take for one day.

The tapeworm proved more obstinate. Worm-cakes were recommended first. One such cake was composed of herbs of the field and natron, baked in [text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Anal Complaints and Minor Ailments from the Papyrus Ebers
segments.original_text: Lettuce t'as-plant === & Stir, mix into one, and apply as a poultice to the Body of the Man or Woman. That some of the patients after a course of treatment on the lines indicated in the foregoing (and succeeding) pages should complain of anal discomfiture is not a thing for wonder. Consequently it occasions no surprise to find a large section devoted to relieving this sequela. As usual the thing was done thoroughly. Internal remedies were first prescribed: REMEDY TO COOL THE ANUS Onion-meal aby Tail-of-a-Mouse ds 1 Honey 2 Water 4 Strain and take for four days. A suppository was next recommended: ANOTHER TO REDUCE THE SMARTING IN THE ANUS Fat-of-the-Antelope 1 Caraway 1 Roll into a Pill and put in the Anus. THE PAPYRUS EBERS ANOTHER TO HEAL THE DISEASED ANUS Cow-horn 1 Pieces-of-dried-Oil 1 Yeast-of-Wine Make a Peg (suppository) for the Man or Woman. Next, bathing was advocated: ANOTHER TO COOL AFTER THE OTHER MEDICINES Ox-bile 4 Boiled Milk 5 Honey 4 Wonderfruit 4 Strain and pour on the anus for a day. Finally if the anus refused to be cooled, and the smarting went a step further, a poultice was prescribed: ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST SORES IN THE ANUS Egg-of-the-Goose 1 Guts-of-the-Goose 1 Clap on the anus. CHAPTER XI MINOR MEDICINE Quite an appreciable amount of comfort may be derived from the fact that the Ancients of Egypt, wonderful as they were, yet groaned under the same petty annoyances that befall so many of us in this enlightened twentieth century, headaches, backaches, toe-aches, even corns, to mention a few. We find prescriptions for pains in the Head, the Bones, and the Trunk; in the Backbone and the Joints; in all the Limbs together and each of the Limbs separately; in the Thigh, the Leg, even the Front-of-the-Shin, the Foot, and the Toes. But that is not all, for the Scribe, ever thorough, included a remedy for Corns, for Trembling-of-the-Fingers, for Tremors-of-all-the-Limbs, for a Squashed Leg, and for 'Tiredness in the Legs.'
segments.translation:
Lettuce [and] the t'as-plant [quantities unclear due to OCR damage]: stir, mix into one, and apply as a poultice to the body of the man or woman.

That some patients, after a course of treatment along the lines indicated in the foregoing (and succeeding) pages, should complain of anal discomfort is no cause for wonder. Consequently it occasions no surprise to find a large section of the papyrus devoted to relieving this sequela [aftereffect]. As usual, the matter was handled thoroughly.

Internal remedies were first prescribed:

REMEDY TO COOL THE ANUS
Onion-meal [quantity: aby, meaning unclear / possibly a measure]
Tail-of-a-Mouse: 1 part
Honey: 2 parts
Water: 4 parts
Strain and take for four days.

A suppository was next recommended:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO REDUCE THE SMARTING IN THE ANUS
Fat-of-the-Antelope: 1 part
Caraway: 1 part
Roll into a pill and insert into the anus.

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO HEAL THE DISEASED ANUS
Cow-horn: 1 part
Pieces-of-dried-Oil: 1 part
Yeast-of-Wine [quantity unspecified]
Form into a peg [suppository] for the man or woman.

Next, bathing was advocated:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO COOL [THE ANUS] AFTER THE OTHER MEDICINES
Ox-bile: 4 parts
Boiled Milk: 5 parts
Honey: 4 parts
Wonderfruit [unidentified plant/fruit]: 4 parts
Strain and pour onto the anus for one day.

Finally, if the anus refused to be cooled and the smarting progressed further, a poultice was prescribed:

ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST SORES IN THE ANUS
Egg-of-the-Goose: 1 part
Guts-of-the-Goose: 1 part
Apply directly to the anus.

CHAPTER XI — MINOR MEDICINE

Considerable comfort may be derived from the fact that the ancient Egyptians, remarkable as they were, nonetheless suffered under the same petty ailments that afflict so many of us in this enlightened twentieth century: headaches, backaches, toe-aches, and even corns, to mention but a few. We find prescriptions for pains in the head, the bones, and the trunk; in the backbone and the joints; in all the limbs together and in each limb separately; in the thigh, the leg, the front of the shin, the foot, and the toes. But that is not all, for the Scribe, ever thorough, also included a remedy for corns, for trembling of the fingers, for tremors of all the limbs, for a crushed [squashed] leg, and for 'tiredness in the legs.'

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Head Pain, Limb Pain, and Bone Pain from the Papyrus Ebers
segments.original_text: be taken internally but rubbed in externally: REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT THE PAIN IN THE HEAD: Inner-of-Onions, Fruit-of-the-am-tree, Natron, setseft-seeds, Bone-of-the-Sword-fish (cooked), Redfish (cooked), Skull-of-the-Crayfish (cooked), Honey, abra-ointment. Smear the Head therewith for four days. For the stubborn headache there was a wide choice of other messy masses recommended for inunction. For instance, one could smear the aching head with an unguent composed of Hide-of-the-Hippopotamus and Poppy-seeds; or with another of Poppy-berries beaten up in Ass's Fat. Or there were various poultices and lotions. One consisted of Crocodile-earth beaten up with Egg-of-the-Ostrich; another of Mint-of-the-Mountains dissolved in Hippopotamus Oil along with Copper and Lead-vitriol; and still another of Ostrich Oil whipped up with Bile-of-the-Black-abdu-fish. But Aspirin has now displaced most, if not all, of these remedies in the public favour. 'For Pains in All the Limbs' the Scribe made a good start by instructing the Patient to mix up Castor Oil with Honey; but in the next few lines he spoilt everything by telling him to smear this all over the outside of his body instead of swallowing it and allowing it to do its work internally. Of course having got off the track at the start he found it impossible to right himself and accordingly went from bad to worse, his next effort being a poultice of Locusts which have been ground up in a mortar with Butter and Spices. For 'Pains in the Bones in Every Limb according to the Truth,' he recommended a poultice of Black Knife-stone with Fat, Honey, and Saltpetre as adjuvants. The same can be said of the special poultice for the Thigh: 'ANOTHER FOR THE THIGH: Bean-meal, Meal-of-the-p[…]'
segments.translation:
This segment, drawn from a modern commentary and translation of the Papyrus Ebers, describes several ancient Egyptian remedies prescribed for headache, general limb pain, and bone pain, noting that they were applied externally rather than taken internally.

REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT PAIN IN THE HEAD: The ingredients — the inner part of onions, fruit of the 'am'-tree, natron [a naturally occurring salt], setseft-seeds [unidentified], cooked bone of the swordfish, cooked redfish, cooked skull of the crayfish, honey, and 'abra'-ointment [unidentified] — are to be mixed and applied by smearing on the head for four days.

For persistent headache, additional external applications are described: an unguent of hippopotamus hide and poppy seeds; another of poppy berries beaten together with ass's fat; a poultice of 'crocodile-earth' [possibly Nile mud or clay] beaten with ostrich egg; a lotion of mountain mint dissolved in hippopotamus oil with copper and lead vitriol [copper sulfate and lead sulfate]; and a preparation of ostrich oil whipped together with bile of the black 'abdu'-fish [unidentified species]. The modern commentator notes, with irony, that aspirin has now supplanted all such remedies.

For 'Pains in All the Limbs': the scribe began promisingly by prescribing castor oil mixed with honey, but then directed that this be rubbed over the exterior of the body rather than ingested — which the commentator considers an error. The scribe further recommended a poultice of locusts ground in a mortar with butter and spices.

For 'Pains in the Bones in Every Limb according to the Truth': a poultice of black knife-stone [possibly black flint or basalt] with fat, honey, and saltpetre [potassium nitrate] was prescribed. The commentator regards this as entirely without therapeutic merit.

A further remedy 'For the Thigh' follows, beginning: 'Bean-meal, Meal-of-the-p[…]' — text breaks off.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Injuries and Diseases of the Limbs and Digits (Ebers Papyrus)
segments.original_text: ANOTHER FOR A SQUASHED LEG: Chopped-up Chaff soaked in Water — Lay thereon as a Poultice in order to heal him at once. It is also to be used for any Limb that thou wishest. TO HEAL THE DISEASED TOES: Fennel, Wax, Incense, Cyperus, Wormwood, Dried Myrrh, Poppy-plant, Poppy-grain, Elderberries, Berries-of-the-uan-tree, Resin-of-Acanthus, Dough-of-Acanthus, Resin-of-the-mafet-tree, Grain-of-Aloes, Fat-of-the-Cedar-tree, Fat-of-the-uan-tree, Fresh Olive Oil, Water-from-the-Rain-of-the-Heavens — Make into one and Poultice for four days. [For Corns:] A poultice of various berries cooked in Cow-fat. TO DRIVE AWAY TREMBLINGS IN THE FINGERS: Incense, Caraway, Wax, Red-lead, Excrement-of-the-Gods, Honey, Figs, Fresh Lead-earth — Cook into one and apply as a Poultice. TO DRIVE AWAY TREMBLINGS IN ALL THE LIMBS OF A PERSON: Fruit-of-the-Dompalm, Garlic, Honey, Copper Verdigris — Put a Dog's Hide on him! Do not press the Hand on him! [For Suppleness of the Limbs:] Natron, Beans, Honey, Incense, and Sweet Myrrh, beaten up with Hippopotamus [fat — applied as a liniment].
segments.translation:
ANOTHER REMEDY FOR A CRUSHED LEG: Take chopped chaff soaked in water and lay it upon the injury as a poultice so as to heal the patient immediately. This preparation may also be applied to any limb as desired.

TO HEAL DISEASED TOES: Take fennel, wax, incense [olibanum/frankincense], cyperus [sedge/galingale], wormwood, dried myrrh, poppy plant, poppy seed, elderberries, berries of the uan-tree [unidentified Egyptian plant], resin of acanthus, dough of acanthus, resin of the mafet-tree [unidentified], grain of aloes, fat of the cedar tree, fat of the uan-tree, fresh olive oil, and rainwater. Combine all into one preparation and apply as a poultice for four days.

[The editorial commentary notes that a simpler remedy — washing with rainwater and applying fresh olive oil — would likely have sufficed, rendering the remaining ingredients superfluous.]

FOR CORNS: A poultice made of various berries cooked in cow fat was applied to disperse them.

TO DRIVE AWAY TREMBLING IN THE FINGERS: Take incense, caraway, wax, red lead [minium], excrement-of-the-Gods [unidentified ritual substance], honey, figs, and fresh lead-earth [raw lead ore]. Cook all together into one preparation and apply as a poultice.

TO DRIVE AWAY TREMBLING IN ALL THE LIMBS OF A PERSON: Take fruit of the doum palm, garlic, honey, and copper verdigris. Place a dog's hide upon the patient. Do not press the hand upon him.

FOR SUPPLENESS OF THE LIMBS: A liniment composed of natron, beans, honey, incense, and sweet myrrh, beaten together with hippopotamus [fat, applied by rubbing onto the limbs].

segments.title_inferred: Minor Medicine and Minor Surgery: Salves for Suppleness, Hardenings, and Crocodile Bites (Papyrus Ebers)
segments.original_text: the Shadfish and Honey beaten up in Yeast-of-Sweet-Beer. Another salve with surely more claim to colour than cure combined Writing-fluid, Vermilion, and Goat's Fat with Honey and several other ingredients, which were certainly only thrown in to make weight. In another salve Ass's dung reappears; it is combined with Yeast-of-the-Opium-drink, Goat's Fat, Lettuce, Onions, Beans, and White Oil. Sometimes it was a pathological cause such as 'Hardenings' which prevented the suppleness. In these cases the 'Hardenings' had first to be got rid of: SALVE TO MAKE SUPPLE THE HARDENINGS Hog's Fat Oil-of-Worms Oil-of-the-abxersu-animal Mouse Oil Cat's Oil Gather into one and anoint therewith. ANOTHER TO SOFTEN THE HARDENINGS Fruit-of-the-Dompalm Beans Edelkraut Fresh Milk Sebesten CRUSH IT IN THE BIRD-OF-PASSAGE! CRUSH IT IN ITS FEATHERS! Apply as a Poultice. Then once again, with that meticulous care which he displayed so often in his compilation, the Scribe rounds off the remedies with one by which the limbs 'could be protected against everything.' After mixing the Scrapings-from-a-Statue with Mint-of-the-Mountains and adding sasa-pieces, Oil, and Wax, one cooked the mixture, strained it (a not unnecessary precaution), and swallowed it for four days. CHAPTER XII MINOR SURGERY Surgery, judging by the Papyrus Ebers, was not a strong point with the Ancient Egyptians, and it is only when we wander through the domain of Major Surgery that we find the knife and the cautery advocated. Minor Surgery, what there is of it, is treated on 'bloodless' lines. A remedy 'to use against the Bite of a Crocodile' is hardly the sort of entry which one would expect to see under the heading of Minor Surgery, but then times have changed. Three thousand years ago, we are forced to assume, bites from crocodiles were about as common as black eyes in these days; and to carry the simile still further, both were treated in the same way: WHAT TO DO AGAINST TH[E BITE OF A CROCODILE]
segments.translation:
The shad-fish and honey beaten together in yeast of sweet beer [were used as a salve ingredient]. Another salve, with surely more claim to colour than to cure, combined writing-fluid [ink], vermilion, and goat's fat with honey and several other ingredients that were certainly included only to add bulk. In yet another salve, ass's dung reappears; it is combined with yeast of the opium-drink, goat's fat, lettuce, onions, beans, and white oil. Sometimes it was a pathological cause such as 'Hardenings' [indurations or contractures] that prevented suppleness of the limbs. In such cases the 'Hardenings' had first to be removed:

SALVE TO MAKE SUPPLE THE HARDENINGS
Hog's fat
Oil of worms
Oil of the abxersu-animal [unidentified animal]
Mouse oil
Cat's oil
Gather all into one and anoint therewith.

ANOTHER [SALVE] TO SOFTEN THE HARDENINGS
Fruit of the doum-palm
Beans
Edelkraut [noble herb; identity uncertain]
Fresh milk
Sebesten [sebesten plum / Cordia myxa]
CRUSH IT IN THE BIRD-OF-PASSAGE [i.e., using a migratory bird as mortar vessel or ritualistic instrument]! CRUSH IT IN ITS FEATHERS! Apply as a poultice.

Then once again, with that meticulous care which the Scribe displayed so often in his compilation, he rounds off the remedies with one by which the limbs 'could be protected against everything.' After mixing the scrapings from a statue [magical/ritual ingredient] with mint-of-the-mountains and adding sasa-pieces [unidentified plant or material], oil, and wax, one cooked the mixture, strained it—a not unnecessary precaution—and swallowed it over four days.

CHAPTER XII: MINOR SURGERY

Surgery, judging by the Papyrus Ebers, was not a strong point with the ancient Egyptians, and it is only when we enter the domain of Major Surgery that we find the knife and the cautery [hot-iron burning instrument] advocated. Minor Surgery, such as it is, is treated along 'bloodless' lines. A remedy 'to use against the bite of a crocodile' is hardly the sort of entry one would expect to see under the heading of Minor Surgery, but times have changed. Three thousand years ago, we are compelled to assume, bites from crocodiles were about as common as black eyes in our own day; and to carry the analogy further, both were treated in the same manner: WHAT TO DO AGAINST THE [BITE OF A CROCODILE—text continues in following segment].

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Human Bites, Burns, and Related Wound Treatments from the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: the hands, or rather the teeth, of a lady or gentleman friend required more intensive treatment than that demanded by the bite of a crocodile: REMEDY AGAINST THE BITE OR STING OF PEOPLE Make thou for him a poultice of Raw Flesh on the first day. Afterwards treat him with Oil and Honey in order to do him good. Then put thou Oil in Wax in order to make him completely well at once. Next, Burns. Burns are troublesome lesions at the best of times and much needless suffering is saved if one can have at hand a quick and easy remedy to apply. The Ancients recognized this, although we might not be able to see any particular merit in the preparations they employed: TO PREVENT BURN WOUNDS A Frog Warm in Oil and rub therewith. ANOTHER The Head-of-the-Electric-Eel Warm in Oil and apply to the part of the body. If one had more time there were other remedies that could be employed; for example, Elderberries and Papyrus-plant mixed in Gum-water and applied thereto. Or, if one preferred a dry remedy: ANOTHER A-portion-of-Cake Hair-of-a-Cat Crush into one and apply thereto. A more elaborate remedy, or series of remedies, is set forth for the use of those who wish to leave nothing to chance: AGAINST BURNS: TO USE ON THE FIRST DAY Black amat-juice Put thereto. ANOTHER ON THE FIRST DAY Elderberries uah-corn Cat's dung Mix into one in Cake-water and apply thereto. TO USE ON THE SECOND DAY Goat's dung Burn, crush, rub in Yeast-that-is-fermenting, and put thereto. TO USE ON THE THIRD DAY Thorns-of-Acanthus, dried Crush them in cooked durra-corn and Onions, put in Oil, and apply as a plaster. TO USE ON THE FOURTH DAY Wax Roasted Cow's Fat Palm-fibres Make into one in uah-corn, and apply as a plaster. TO USE ON THE FIFTH DAY Onions Red-lead Fruit-of-the-am-tree Crush, rub in Copper-splinters, make into one and apply as a plaster. It is to their credit that these practitioners of a former day provided for emergencies, and in the unlikely event of
segments.translation:
The bite of a human — whether from the hands or, more precisely, the teeth of a lady or gentleman acquaintance — was considered to require more intensive treatment than even the bite of a crocodile.

REMEDY AGAINST THE BITE OR STING OF PEOPLE
Prepare for the patient a poultice of Raw Flesh on the first day. Afterwards, treat him with Oil and Honey to promote healing. Then apply Oil in Wax in order to effect a complete cure at once.

On Burns:
Burns are troublesome wounds under the best of circumstances, and much unnecessary suffering can be avoided if a quick and easy remedy is ready to hand. The ancients recognized this, though we may not readily appreciate the virtue of the preparations they employed:

TO PREVENT BURN WOUNDS
A Frog — warm in Oil and rub [the affected area] therewith.

ANOTHER [REMEDY]
The Head of the Electric Eel — warm in Oil and apply to the affected part of the body.

If more time was available, other remedies might also be employed; for example, Elderberries and Papyrus-plant mixed in Gum-water and applied to the wound. Or, if a dry remedy was preferred:

ANOTHER [REMEDY]
A portion of Cake [and] Hair of a Cat — crush together into one [preparation] and apply thereto.

A more elaborate remedy, or series of remedies, is prescribed for those who wish to leave nothing to chance:

AGAINST BURNS — TO BE USED ON THE FIRST DAY
Black amat-juice — apply thereto.

ANOTHER [REMEDY] FOR THE FIRST DAY
Elderberries, uah-corn [a variety of grain, identity uncertain], Cat's dung — mix together in Cake-water and apply thereto.

TO BE USED ON THE SECOND DAY
Goat's dung — burn [it], crush, rub in Yeast-that-is-fermenting, and apply thereto.

TO BE USED ON THE THIRD DAY
Thorns of Acanthus, dried — crush them together with cooked durra-corn [sorghum] and Onions; combine with Oil and apply as a plaster.

TO BE USED ON THE FOURTH DAY
Wax, Roasted Cow's Fat, Palm-fibres — combine in uah-corn and apply as a plaster.

TO BE USED ON THE FIFTH DAY
Onions, Red-lead [minium], Fruit of the am-tree — crush together, rub in Copper-splinters, combine into one preparation and apply as a plaster.

It is to the credit of these ancient practitioners that they provided for emergencies, and in the unlikely event of [text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Minor Surgery: Treatments for Burns and Open Wounds from the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: from-the-Shore, Crocodile-incense, Fat-of-the-Egyptian-Goat, Onions, and Wax could be used. Or, again, Stone-from-the-Parting-of-the-Waters could be used instead of Stone-from-the-Shore in the last-mentioned prescription. If the burn still persisted in its downward course and began to 'turn white,' one is advised to use a remedy that, applied earlier in the day, one cannot help remarking, would have saved a deal of trouble; namely, a Linen-shirt steeped in Oil. Another simple remedy, although not so simple nor so effective as the foregoing must have proved, is, however, backed up by an unsolicited testimonial that is quite convincing in its brevity: ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE AWAY THE TURNING-WHITE OF A BURN Durra-Bread-in-Oil-and-Salt Mix into one and apply as a plaster in order that he may become well at once. IT IS QUITE TRUE. I HAVE SEEN IT. IT HAS OFTEN HAPPENED TO ME. But, as every medical man knows, Burns can be stubborn affairs. Hence it is not surprising that in spite of the above unsolicited testimonial the Ancients found some burns beyond the ingenuity of mortal man, and were forced to invoke the aid of the gods to reinforce the therapeutic effect of the remedies: INCANTATION FOR A BURN 'O son, Horus! There is Fire in the Land! Water is not there and thou art not there! Bring Water over the River-bank to quench the Fire.' To be spoken over Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-Borne-a-Son. ANOTHER INCANTATION FOR A BURN 'O thou son of god, Horus! There is Fire in the Land! Though there be water there or not: now, the Water is in thy Mouth, the Nile is at thy Feet when thou comest to quench the Fire.' To be spoken over Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-Borne-a-Son. Cake Ram's Hair Apply to the Burn. The victim of a Burn had little to thank the Healing Profession for, it will be admitted; but the person unfortunate enough to contract an open wound had still less. One remedy directs that Cow's Fat or Cow's Flesh be placed on the wound in order [to heal it].
segments.translation: From the shore [mineral/stone ingredient], Crocodile-incense, Fat of the Egyptian Goat, Onions, and Wax could be used [in treatment of burns]. Alternatively, Stone-from-the-Parting-of-the-Waters could be substituted for Stone-from-the-Shore in the aforementioned prescription. If the burn continued to progress downward and began to 'turn white' [i.e., show signs of necrosis or eschar formation], the recommended remedy was a linen shirt steeped in oil — a treatment which, the author observes, would have saved considerable trouble had it been applied earlier. Another simple remedy — though not as simple nor as effective as the foregoing — is nonetheless supported by an unsolicited testimonial remarkable for its brevity: ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE AWAY THE WHITENING OF A BURN: Durra [sorghum] bread in oil and salt. Mix together and apply as a plaster, so that he may recover at once. IT IS QUITE TRUE. I HAVE SEEN IT. IT HAS OFTEN HAPPENED TO ME. But, as every medical man knows, burns can be stubborn. It is therefore unsurprising that, despite the above testimonial, the Ancients sometimes found burns beyond mortal remedy and were compelled to invoke divine assistance to reinforce the therapeutic effect: INCANTATION FOR A BURN: 'O son, Horus! There is Fire in the Land! Water is not there and thou art not there! Bring Water over the River-bank to quench the Fire.' To be spoken over the milk of a woman who has borne a son. ANOTHER INCANTATION FOR A BURN: 'O thou son of god, Horus! There is Fire in the Land! Though there be water there or not: now, the Water is in thy Mouth, the Nile is at thy Feet when thou comest to quench the Fire.' To be spoken over the milk of a woman who has borne a son. [Also:] Ram's hair, formed into a cake, to be applied to the burn. The victim of a burn had little cause to thank the healing profession, it must be admitted; but the person unfortunate enough to suffer an open wound fared even worse. One remedy directs that cow's fat or cow's flesh be placed upon the wound [to promote healing].

segments.title_inferred: Minor Surgery: Remedies for Splinters, Abscesses, and Ulcers from the Papyrus Ebers
segments.original_text: complain of the slowness of medical progress, let him be thankful that in the extraction of splinters, to mention another department, — MINOR SURGERY we have advanced since the days of Tut-Ankh-Amen. It does not seem much to boast of, and yet.... WHAT TO DO TO DRAW OUT SPLINTERS IN THE FLESH The per-baibait-bird-with-Honey Apply thereto. ANOTHER Worms' blood, cook and crush in Oil; Mole, kill, cook, and drain in Oil; Ass's dung, mix in Fresh Milk Apply to the opening. ANOTHER Male-and-Female-Semen Apply thereto. ANOTHER Skull-of-the-Shadfish, cooked in Oil Apply to the point of the splinter. Thereby it comes out. ANOTHER Incense Dough Sea-salt Wasp's dung Fat Red-lead Wax Apply thereto. It draws the matter thereout. It is unfortunate that the Scribe did not think it worth while to describe the 'uases' abscess so that we might make an effort to identify it. That it was a stubborn eruption, the remedies show. One that cures the abscess by 'making the blood fall' is composed of Man's Milk, Granite, Tortoise-shell, and Trash-from-the-Knifestone. Just as picturesque, and assuredly as useless, is the second remedy: ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST THE UASES ABSCESS Blood-of-a-Dove Blood-of-a-Goose Blood-of-a-Swallow Blood-of-a-Vulture Anoint therewith. 'Stinking ulcers'—their number must have been legion—were dealt with at length, but why the tag of advice was attached only to the following remedy is somewhat puzzling. It could with advantage have been appended to many others in the Papyrus: ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT A STINKING ULCER IN THE BODY OF A MAN OR WOMAN Egg-of-an-Ostrich Tortoise-shell Thorns-of-the-am-tree Warm and anoint therewith. But DON'T GET TIRED DOING IT! To dry a wound it was plastered with Incense, Onions, and Cow Fat; or if the only animal available was a goat, then some Goat's Fat was beaten up with Onions and Wax and used as a poultice. For those who had any objection to Cows or Goats there remained the H[orse/other animal — ILLEGIBLE: text truncated]
segments.translation:
Those who complain of the slowness of medical progress should be thankful that in the extraction of splinters — to mention one department of minor surgery — we have advanced since the days of Tutankhamun. It does not seem much to boast of, and yet…

WHAT TO DO TO DRAW OUT SPLINTERS FROM THE FLESH
[Remedy 1:] The per-baibait bird [an unidentified Egyptian bird] with Honey. Apply thereto.

[Remedy 2:] Worms' blood, cooked and crushed in Oil; Mole, killed, cooked, and drained in Oil; Ass's dung, mixed in Fresh Milk. Apply to the opening.

[Remedy 3:] Male-and-Female-Semen [i.e., combined human semen]. Apply thereto.

[Remedy 4:] Skull of the Shadfish [a species of fish], cooked in Oil. Apply to the point of the splinter. Thereby it comes out.

[Remedy 5:] Incense, Dough, Sea-salt, Wasp's dung, Fat, Red-lead, Wax. Apply thereto. It draws the matter out.

It is unfortunate that the Scribe did not think it worthwhile to describe the 'uases' [Egyptian term of uncertain meaning] abscess, so that we might make an effort to identify it. That it was a stubborn eruption the remedies make clear. One remedy that cures the abscess by 'making the blood fall' is composed of Man's Milk, Granite, Tortoise-shell, and Trash-from-the-Knifestone [grinding residue from a whetstone]. Equally picturesque, and assuredly as useless, is the second remedy:

ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST THE UASES ABSCESS
Blood of a Dove, Blood of a Goose, Blood of a Swallow, Blood of a Vulture. Anoint therewith.

'Stinking ulcers' — their number must have been legion — were dealt with at length, but why the following note of practical advice was attached only to the remedy below is somewhat puzzling. It could with advantage have been appended to many others in the Papyrus:

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT A STINKING ULCER IN THE BODY OF A MAN OR WOMAN
Egg of an Ostrich, Tortoise-shell, Thorns of the am-tree [unidentified Egyptian tree species]. Warm and anoint therewith. But DO NOT GROW WEARY DOING IT!

To dry a wound it was plastered with Incense, Onions, and Cow Fat; or, if the only animal available was a goat, then Goat's Fat was beaten up with Onions and Wax and used as a poultice. For those who had any objection to Cows or Goats, there remained the H[— ILLEGIBLE: text truncated]

segments.title_inferred: Treatments for Wheals, Wound Healing, and the Urinary System (Ebers Papyrus)
segments.original_text: DRIVE AWAY THE WHEALS FROM BLOWS Honey Cow's Brain Mason's Clay Linseed-water Date-juice Cook, and apply as a plaster. ANOTHER Dust-of-Alabaster Dust-of-a-Statue Granite Fresh Milk Anoint therewith. ANOTHER A portion-of-hemit, dried Crush, powder, and put in a Sugar-cake. One part thereof soaked in Honey to be eaten by the person. Skin-grafting, it may be shortly said, formed no part of the accomplishments of the medical practitioner in Ancient Egypt. Nevertheless he did his best to stimulate granulation: TO MAKE THE FLESH GROW Collyrium Cow-fat Chips-of-Verdigris Honey Crush, and apply as poultice. Curiously enough this remedy appeared to have succeeded at times; in fact to have over-succeeded on occasions, for it is followed by another remedy to check excessive granulation. Since this, however, was composed of an unguent concocted of Ostrich-egg ground down with Tortoise-shell and Thorns-of-the-am-tree, its efficacy may be seriously doubted. CHAPTER XIII THE URINARY SYSTEM The Urinary System received nothing like the detailed attention paid to the Alimentary System. But it made up for that in variety. Urinary troubles in the adult were corrected in three ways: a rectal injection of Olive Oil, Honey, Sweet Beer, Sea-salt, and Seeds-of-the-Wonderfruit was one way. Anointing the Phallus was another: TO REGULATE THE URINE Xet-plant Mix in Fresh Milk and apply to the Phallus. ANOTHER Crocus-from-the-South, Beans roasted. Put in Oil and anoint the Phallus therewith. ANOTHER Wood-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus Mix in Yeast-of-the-mesta-drink, and anoint the Phallus therewith. A third method was by means of mixtures: REMEDY TO REGULATE THE URINE A measuring-glass filled with Water-from-the-Bird-pond, Elderberry, Fibres-of-the-asit-plant, Fresh Milk, Beer-Swill, Flowers-of-the-Cucumber, Green Dates. Make into one, strain, and take for four days. The less said about the remedies for the treatment of Polyuria and frequency...
segments.translation: DRIVE AWAY THE WHEALS FROM BLOWS: [Ingredients:] Honey; Cow's Brain; Mason's Clay [mortar clay]; Linseed-water; Date-juice. Cook together and apply as a plaster. ANOTHER [remedy]: Dust of Alabaster; Dust scraped from a Statue; Granite [powder]; Fresh Milk. Anoint the affected area therewith. ANOTHER [remedy]: A portion of hemit [unidentified plant or substance], dried. Crush, reduce to powder, and incorporate into a sugar-cake [confection]. One part thereof, soaked in Honey, to be eaten by the patient. Skin-grafting, it may be briefly stated, formed no part of the accomplishments of the medical practitioner in Ancient Egypt. Nevertheless he did his best to stimulate granulation tissue: TO MAKE THE FLESH GROW: [Ingredients:] Collyrium [eye-paint, i.e., kohl]; Cow-fat; Chips of Verdigris [copper acetate]; Honey. Crush together and apply as a poultice. Curiously enough, this remedy appears to have succeeded at times — indeed, to have over-succeeded on occasions — for it is followed by another remedy intended to check excessive granulation. Since this latter remedy, however, was composed of an unguent concocted of Ostrich-egg ground down with Tortoise-shell and Thorns-of-the-am-tree [unidentified], its efficacy may be seriously doubted. CHAPTER XIII: THE URINARY SYSTEM. The Urinary System received nothing like the detailed attention paid to the Alimentary System, but it made up for that in variety. Urinary troubles in the adult were corrected in three ways: a rectal injection of Olive Oil, Honey, Sweet Beer, Sea-salt, and Seeds-of-the-Wonderfruit [probably Ricinus communis, castor plant] was one way. Anointing the Phallus was another: TO REGULATE THE URINE: Xet-plant [unidentified]. Mix in Fresh Milk and apply to the Phallus. ANOTHER [remedy]: Crocus-from-the-South [possibly saffron or a southern variety of crocus]; Beans, roasted. Put in Oil and anoint the Phallus therewith. ANOTHER [remedy]: Wood of the Zizyphus-Lotus [Ziziphus lotus, jujube]. Mix in the yeast of the mesta-drink [an Egyptian fermented beverage], and anoint the Phallus therewith. A third method was by means of internal mixtures: REMEDY TO REGULATE THE URINE: A measuring-glass filled with Water-from-the-Bird-pond [pond water, possibly a specific sacred or medicinal source]; Elderberry; Fibres of the asit-plant [unidentified]; Fresh Milk; Beer-Swill [dregs of beer]; Flowers of the Cucumber; Green Dates. Combine into one preparation, strain, and take [orally] for four days. The less said about the remedies for the treatment of Polyuria [excessive urination] and urinary frequency [the text breaks off here]...

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Children's Urinary Complaints and Diseases of Women (Ebers Papyrus)
segments.original_text: drink the mixture out of a cool flask. If a boy, he had to drink it out of a hennu-vessel. A quaint remedy relieved retention in a child: REMEDY FOR A CHILD FOR CLEARING OUT THE ACCUMULATION OF URINE IN HIS BODY An-old-Book-cooked-in-Oil Smear on his Body. An old Book means, of course, a discarded papyrus and in this case the virtue of the papyrus would be enhanced by the writing-fluid, both of which 'drugs' —paper and ink—are as much a part of the pharmacopoea of the day as Red-lead or Honey, or a host of others, and are frequently prescribed for many ailments. Another remedy proposed for a child who is having trouble with his urine must be noticed here. Xent-grains are made into a pill, which is given to the child in his food if he is old enough to manage it. But, if it is a suckling that is to be treated, the wet-nurse is directed to warm it in her mouth first, and then to spit it into the mouth of the babe for four days. This interpretation of the passage, however, is open to question and is opposed by several writers who have quoted the prescription in their works. Their contention is that the Scribe intended the mother to swallow the Pill, the child receiving the benefit of it in the maternal milk. CHAPTER XIV DISEASES OF WOMEN The women's section covers some four or five pages of the Papyrus and includes remedies for errors of menstruation and lactation, diseases of the breasts and genitalia, aids to abortion and parturition, and various valuable hints to nursing mothers. Two of these hints enable them to distinguish the quality of their milk: thus, if the milk be good, it smells like the pollen of uah-grain; if, on the other hand, the milk be bad, it smells like the entrails of the mehit-fish. Other hints enable them to prognosticate the fate of the new-born child: PROGNOSIS FOR A CHILD ON THE DAY IT IS BORN If it cries ni, it will live. If it cries ba, it will die. ANOTHER If it lets a loud lamentation be heard, it will die.
segments.translation: The mixture was to be drunk from a cool flask. If the patient was a boy, he had to drink it from a hennu-vessel [a specific Egyptian liquid measure or vessel]. A curious remedy was prescribed for urinary retention in a child: REMEDY FOR A CHILD TO CLEAR THE ACCUMULATION OF URINE IN HIS BODY — An Old Book [discarded papyrus] cooked in Oil; smear on his Body. Here, 'An Old Book' refers to a discarded papyrus scroll, the medicinal virtue of which was considered to be enhanced by the writing fluid absorbed into it. Both 'drugs'—the paper and the ink—were as standard a part of the pharmacopoeia of the time as red lead or honey or numerous other substances, and were frequently prescribed for a variety of ailments. Another remedy for a child experiencing difficulty urinating deserves mention here. Xent-grains [unidentified grain or seed] were formed into a pill and given to the child in his food, if he was old enough to take it in that manner. However, if the patient was still a nursing infant, the wet-nurse was instructed to warm the pill in her own mouth first, and then to spit it into the mouth of the infant for four days. This interpretation of the passage is, however, disputed and has been challenged by several scholars who have cited the prescription in their works. Their argument is that the Scribe intended for the mother herself to swallow the pill, with the child receiving its benefit through the mother's milk. CHAPTER XIV — DISEASES OF WOMEN. The section devoted to women's complaints spans some four or five pages of the Papyrus and includes remedies for disorders of menstruation and lactation, diseases of the breasts and genitalia, aids to abortion and to childbirth [parturition], and various practical instructions for nursing mothers. Two of these instructions allow nursing mothers to assess the quality of their milk: if the milk is good, it smells like the pollen of uah-grain [unidentified grain]; if the milk is bad, it smells like the entrails of the mehit-fish [unidentified fish species]. Other instructions allow them to foretell the fate of a newborn child: PROGNOSIS FOR A CHILD ON THE DAY OF ITS BIRTH — If it cries 'ni,' it will live. If it cries 'ba,' it will die. ANOTHER [PROGNOSIS] — If a loud lamentation is heard from it, it will die.

segments.title_inferred: Diseases of Women: Gynecological Remedies, Obstetric Treatments, and Breast Ailments from the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: failed, a douche composed of Fennel, Wonderfruit, Honey and Sweet Beer was substituted and persisted in for four days. To protect a virgin from Leucorrhoea it was necessary to anoint her breast, body, and all the limbs with a lotion prepared by crushing the dried liver of a Swallow in Sour Milk. To produce Milk in the breast of a woman about to suckle a child, Bones-of-the-Swordfish were warmed in Oil and her backbone smeared therewith. The same result could be brought about by mixing Fragrant Bread (which had been made from Soured Durra) with the Poppy-plant, and making her eat it while she sat cross-legged. To bring about abortion, only one remedy is given: Dates, Onions, and the Fruit-of-the-Acanthus, were crushed in a vessel with Honey, sprinkled on a cloth, and applied to the Vulva. It ensured abortion either in the first, second or third period. To hurry on labour, however, there was no lack of remedies: REMEDY TO CAUSE A WOMAN TO BE DELIVERED Peppermint — Let the woman apply it to her bare posterior. ANOTHER TO CAUSE ALL AND EVERYTHING THAT IS IN THE BODY OF A WOMAN TO FALL Pot-of-a-new-hennu-vessel — Crush in Warm Oil and pour on her genitals. ANOTHER TO LOOSEN A CHILD IN THE BODY OF A WOMAN Sea-salt, Clean Grain-of-Wheat, Female Reed — Plaster the abdomen therewith. ANOTHER Fresh Salt, Honey — Strain and take for one day. ANOTHER Fennel, Incense, Garlic, sert-juice, Fresh Salt, Wasp's dung — Make into a ball and put in the Vagina. ANOTHER Tail-of-a-Tortoise, Shell-of-a-Beetle, sefet-oil, sert-juice, Oil — Crush into one and poultice therewith. To remedy a diseased Breast one could plaster it for four days with a compound of Calamine, Cow's Brain, and Wasp's dung; or one could invoke the goddess Isis by means of a magic formula: MAGIC FORMULA FOR THE BREAST — The Breast is the same diseased Breast of Isis who in the city of Xebt bore the gods Su and Tefnut! She has prepared for it this Magic Formula to be said over [text continues].
segments.translation: Where prior treatment had failed, a douche [vaginal irrigation] composed of Fennel, Wonderfruit [unidentified Egyptian plant ingredient], Honey, and Sweet Beer was substituted and continued for four days. To protect a virgin from Leucorrhoea [vaginal discharge], it was necessary to anoint her breast, body, and all the limbs with a lotion prepared by crushing the dried liver of a Swallow in Sour Milk. To produce Milk in the breast of a woman about to nurse a child, Bones-of-the-Swordfish were warmed in Oil and her backbone smeared therewith. The same result could also be brought about by mixing Fragrant Bread (made from Soured Durra [sorghum]) with the Poppy-plant, and having her eat it while she sat cross-legged. To bring about abortion, only one remedy is recorded: Dates, Onions, and the Fruit-of-the-Acanthus were crushed together in a vessel with Honey, sprinkled on a cloth, and applied to the Vulva. This was said to ensure abortion in the first, second, or third period [trimester or stage of pregnancy]. To hasten labour, however, there was no shortage of remedies: REMEDY TO CAUSE A WOMAN TO BE DELIVERED — Peppermint: Let the woman apply it to her bare posterior. ANOTHER, TO CAUSE ALL AND EVERYTHING THAT IS IN THE BODY OF A WOMAN TO FALL — [Substance from] a Pot-of-a-new-hennu-vessel: Crush in Warm Oil and pour upon her genitals. ANOTHER, TO LOOSEN A CHILD IN THE BODY OF A WOMAN — Sea-salt, Clean Grain-of-Wheat, Female Reed: Plaster the abdomen therewith. ANOTHER — Fresh Salt, Honey: Strain and take internally for one day. ANOTHER — Fennel, Incense, Garlic, sert-juice [unidentified plant juice], Fresh Salt, Wasp's dung: Form into a suppository ball and insert into the Vagina. ANOTHER — Tail-of-a-Tortoise, Shell-of-a-Beetle, sefet-oil [unidentified oil], sert-juice, Oil: Crush all together and apply as a poultice. To treat a diseased Breast, one could plaster it for four days with a compound of Calamine [zinc carbonate mineral], Cow's Brain, and Wasp's dung; or alternatively one could invoke the goddess Isis by means of a magic formula: MAGIC FORMULA FOR THE BREAST — The Breast [in question] is the same diseased Breast of Isis who, in the city of Xebt [Coptos/Qift], bore the gods Shu and Tefnut! She has prepared for it this Magic Formula, to be spoken over [the afflicted breast — text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Gynecological Remedies: Vaginal Douches, Uterine Displacement, and Menstrual Disorders (Ebers Papyrus)
segments.original_text:
dryness of the Eyes which ensues. SPEAK OVER AAT-PLANT, OVER HEALTH-GRAINS, OVER THE-FRUITFUL-PART-OF-REEDS, OVER HAIR-OF-THE-HEAD-OF-THE-ABT-PLANT. LET IT POUR OUT ON THE LEFT SIDE. MAKE SEVEN PORTIONS AND GIVE THEM. The Vulva could be protected against the entry of disease by injecting a douche in which Garlic and Horn-of-a-Cow figure as ingredients. Should this prophylactic fail and inflammation ensue, the douche was changed to one of Bile-of-the-Cow, Cassia, and Oil. If the disease progressed and corrosion occurred, the Cow was discarded for the Hog, and a douche of Fresh Dates and Hog's Bile administered. If, in spite of these vigorous measures, pustules appear in the vagina, recourse is now had, as so often before, to the Ass, and the douche is composed of Fresh Dates and Ass's Milk.

REMEDY TO ALLOW THE WOMB OF A WOMAN TO SLIP INTO ITS PLACE
The-Film-of-Dampness-which-is-found-on-the-Wood-of-Ships (mould): Rub in Yeast-of-Fermented-Beer and let her drink it.

ANOTHER: Put an Ibis-of-Wax on the coals and allow the fumes thereof to penetrate into her sex-organs.

ANOTHER: Dried Human excrement — Put in Incense; the woman bends herself over the same and lets the fumes thereof penetrate into her sex-organs.

ANOTHER: Oil-of-the-Earth (? Petroleum) with peddu (manure) — Keep in Honey and rub the body of a woman therewith.

ANOTHER: Dried Excrement, Froth-of-Beer — Rub the finger of the woman therewith and put it to all her limbs against her sufferings. (Joachim suggests that she was to smear the prolapsed uterus.)

WHEN THOU EXAMINEST A WOMAN WHO HAS LIVED many years without her Menstruation having appeared; she vomits something like foam and her body is as though a fire were under it, but she recovers after the vomiting; then say thou to her: This is a rising of Blood to
segments.translation:
...dryness of the eyes which follows. [Ritual incantation:] SPEAK OVER THE AAT-PLANT, OVER HEALTH-GRAINS [kyllestis-grain], OVER THE FRUITFUL PART OF REEDS, OVER THE HAIR OF THE HEAD OF THE ABT-PLANT. LET IT POUR OUT ON THE LEFT SIDE. MAKE SEVEN PORTIONS AND GIVE THEM.

The vulva could be protected against the entry of disease by injecting a douche [vaginal irrigation] in which garlic and horn-of-a-cow figured as ingredients. Should this prophylactic fail and inflammation ensue, the douche was changed to one of bile-of-the-cow, cassia, and oil. If the disease progressed and corrosion [ulceration] occurred, the cow was discarded in favour of the hog, and a douche of fresh dates and hog's bile was administered. If, in spite of these vigorous measures, pustules appeared in the vagina, recourse was had, as so often elsewhere, to the ass, and the douche was composed of fresh dates and ass's milk.

REMEDY TO CAUSE THE WOMB OF A WOMAN TO RETURN TO ITS PROPER PLACE:
The film of moisture found on the timber of ships [ship's mould / fungal growth]: rub together with yeast of fermented beer and let her drink it.

ANOTHER: Place a wax figure of an ibis upon the coals and allow the fumes thereof to penetrate into her genital organs.

ANOTHER: Dried human excrement — place in incense [upon burning coals]; the woman bends over it and allows the fumes thereof to penetrate into her genital organs.

ANOTHER: Oil-of-the-earth [? petroleum / bitumen] with peddu [manure] — keep in honey and rub the body of the woman therewith.

ANOTHER: Dried excrement, froth of beer — rub onto the finger of the woman and apply it to all her limbs against her suffering. (Joachim interprets this as instruction to smear the prolapsed uterus directly.)

WHEN THOU EXAMINEST A WOMAN WHO HAS LIVED MANY YEARS WITHOUT HER MENSTRUATION HAVING APPEARED; she vomits something resembling foam and her body is as though a fire burned beneath it, but she recovers after the vomiting; then say thou to her: This is a rising of blood to [the head — text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Ancient Egyptian Skin Remedies: Itch, Scurf, and Poultices from the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: A mixture composed of Onion, crushed in Honey and taken in Beer, was prescribed. If the Itch was confined to the Neck, a Chopped-up Bat applied to it as a poultice healed it 'at once.' For the Thigh: REMEDY FOR THE THIGH — Onions, Dried Beans, Red Natron, Sea-salt, Sour Milk — Make into a poultice. IT HEALS AT ONCE. If the itching spread to all the limbs and became generalized, various remedies were available. Among them a Poultice of Corn, Natron, Clay-from-the-Gate, Onions, Incense, and Refuse-of-Dates. This was calculated to soothe it, but there was also a certain sure remedy for it: ANOTHER TO ALLAY ITCHING — Cyperus-from-the-Meadow, Onion-meal, Incense, Wild Date-juice — Make into one and apply to the scurvy place. LOOK TO IT BECAUSE THIS IS THE TRUE REMEDY. IT WAS FOUND AMONG THE PROVEN REMEDIES IN THE TEMPLE OF THE GOD, OSIRIS. IT IS A REMEDY WHICH DRIVES AWAY THE SCURF IN EVERY LIMB OF A PERSON. YES, IT HEALS AT ONCE. YOU SEE. If the itching were accompanied by 'blood corrosion' the trouble became involved, and mild or simple remedies were out of the question. A concoction of Elderberries, Linseed, Wormwood, Natron, and various native plants, rubbed in Semen-of-a-Man, Yeast-of-Wine, and Juice-of-the-Wild-Date, was warmed and applied as a poultice. In the event of this and similar remedies not bringing relief, rectal injections were employed. One such enema consisted of Fresh Milk and Sea-salt; another of Fresh Milk, Sea-salt, and Incense; another of Urine, Onions, and Oil; and still another of Honey, Fresh Milk, Olive Oil, Copper-rust, and Collyrium. This line of treatment is that known as counter-irritation and persists to this day, though not in this form, nor with these (nor similar) remedies, nor indeed for this particular disease. For common scurf the ketket-plant and the Berry-of-the-uan-tree were ground into one with the Milk-of-a-Woman, strained, and drunk for four days. A simpler but hardly less pleasant [text breaks off].
segments.translation: A mixture composed of onion crushed in honey and taken in beer was prescribed [for itch]. If the itch was confined to the neck, a chopped-up bat applied as a poultice was said to heal it 'at once.' For the thigh, the prescribed remedy consisted of onions, dried beans, red natron [sodium carbonate/bicarbonate], sea-salt, and sour milk, made into a poultice — declared to heal at once. If the itching spread to all the limbs and became generalized, several remedies were available, among them a poultice of corn, natron, clay-from-the-gate [a locally sourced clay], onions, incense, and refuse-of-dates [date-palm waste]. This was calculated to soothe the condition. There was also a certain and sure remedy: another preparation to allay itching, composed of cyperus-from-the-meadow [a sedge, possibly Cyperus esculentus or related species], onion-meal, incense, and wild date-juice, to be combined and applied to the affected [scurfy] place. The text declares emphatically: 'Look to it, because this is the true remedy. It was found among the proven remedies in the Temple of the God Osiris. It is a remedy which drives away the scurf in every limb of a person. Yes, it heals at once.' If the itching were accompanied by 'blood corrosion' [a term likely indicating an inflammatory or ulcerative condition], the case became more complex and simple remedies were insufficient. A concoction of elderberries, linseed, wormwood, natron, and various native plants, mixed with human semen, yeast-of-wine, and juice-of-the-wild-date, was warmed and applied as a poultice. Should this and similar remedies fail to bring relief, rectal injections [enemas] were employed. One such enema consisted of fresh milk and sea-salt; another of fresh milk, sea-salt, and incense; another of urine, onions, and oil; yet another of honey, fresh milk, olive oil, copper-rust [verdigris], and collyrium [an eye/topical preparation, here used internally]. This therapeutic approach corresponds to what is known as counter-irritation and continues in principle to the present day, though not in this form, nor with these or similar remedies, nor for this particular disease. For common scurf, the ketket-plant and the berry-of-the-uan-tree were ground together with the milk of a woman, strained, and drunk for four days. A simpler but hardly less [pleasant — text breaks off].

segments.title_inferred: Treatment of Scalp Conditions and Diseases of the Eyes (Ebers Papyrus)
segments.original_text: On the second day the head was anointed with Fish-oil. On the third day with Hippopotamus Oil. On the fourth day with abra-oil. After this course of intensive treatment the offending head, if the Eczema still persisted, was smeared daily with Bread-meal and dressed with 'Rotted Cereals.' CHAPTER XVI DISEASES OF THE EYES Nine pages of the original papyrus are devoted to eye-conditions, a fact which need occasion no surprise to anyone acquainted with that country even after the thousands of years that separate us from that far-distant period. In a separate volume published in 1889 Ebers gave a detailed translation of this interesting chapter, differentiating the following conditions: Blepharitis, Blindness, Carcinoma, Cataract, Chalazion, Chemosis, Ectropion, Entropion, Granulation, Hemorrhage, Hydrophthalmus, Inflammation, Iritis, Leucoma, Ophthalmoplegia, Pinguecula, Pterygium, Staphyloma, Trichiasis. To improve the sight a 'lotion' prepared by warming Chips-of-a-new-hennu-pot in Fresh Milk was recommended. Another lotion could be prepared by combining Cream with the Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son. Apply to the eyes frequently, is added. A number of salves are also given in each of which Collyrium figures. Thus in one the Collyrium is simply combined with Honey; in another with Honey and Sap-from-Fresh-Onions; in another with the Marrow-of-an-Ox; in another with Goose-grease and Water; in another with Incense and Real Lapis lazuli; in another with Antimony, Copper-vitriol, Writing-fluid, and Onions; and in still another with Writing-fluid, Myrrh, Opal-resin, Arabian-wood-powder, and Saltpetre-from-Upper-Egypt. For Bleary Eyes one beat up Myrrh, Onions, Verdigris, and Cyperus-from-the-North, with Antilope-dung, Clear Oil, and Entrails-of-the-qadit-animal. This could be painted on with a Vulture's feather. Fortunately for Bleary-eyed patients of that day, and for those whose means did not run to Antilopes and Vultures (not to mention qadit animals)...
segments.translation:
On the second day the head was anointed with Fish-oil. On the third day with Hippopotamus Oil. On the fourth day with abra-oil [an unidentified oil of Egyptian origin]. After this course of intensive treatment, if the Eczema still persisted, the affected scalp was smeared daily with Bread-meal and dressed with 'Rotted Cereals.'

CHAPTER XVI: DISEASES OF THE EYES

Nine pages of the original papyrus are devoted to eye conditions, a fact which should surprise no one acquainted with that country, even across the thousands of years separating us from that remote period. In a separate volume published in 1889, Ebers provided a detailed translation of this interesting chapter, distinguishing the following conditions: Blepharitis [inflammation of the eyelids], Blindness, Carcinoma, Cataract, Chalazion [eyelid cyst], Chemosis [conjunctival edema], Ectropion [outward-turning eyelid], Entropion [inward-turning eyelid], Granulation [trachoma-type granulation tissue], Hemorrhage, Hydrophthalmus [abnormal enlargement of the eye], Inflammation, Iritis [inflammation of the iris], Leucoma [corneal opacity], Ophthalmoplegia [paralysis of eye muscles], Pinguecula [conjunctival deposit], Pterygium [conjunctival overgrowth], Staphyloma [abnormal protrusion of uvea], and Trichiasis [inward-turning eyelashes].

To improve the sight, a lotion prepared by warming chips of a new hennu-pot [a type of ceramic vessel] in Fresh Milk was recommended. Another lotion could be prepared by combining Cream with the Milk of a Woman who has Borne a Son. The instruction adds: apply to the eyes frequently.

A number of salves are also given, in each of which Collyrium [a medicinal eye-wash or eye-salve] figures as an ingredient. In one preparation, Collyrium is simply combined with Honey; in another, with Honey and Sap from Fresh Onions; in another, with the Marrow of an Ox; in another, with Goose-grease and Water; in another, with Incense and Real Lapis lazuli; in another, with Antimony, Copper-vitriol [copper sulfate], Writing-fluid [ink], and Onions; and in yet another, with Writing-fluid, Myrrh, Opal-resin, Arabian-wood-powder, and Saltpetre from Upper Egypt [natron or a related salt].

For Bleary Eyes, one was to beat together Myrrh, Onions, Verdigris [copper acetate patina], and Cyperus from the North, along with Antelope-dung, Clear Oil, and the Entrails of the qadit-animal [unidentified animal]. This compound could be applied with a Vulture's feather used as a brush. Fortunately for bleary-eyed patients of that era, and for those whose means did not extend to Antelopes and Vultures — not to mention qadit animals...

segments.title_inferred: Ancient Egyptian Remedies for Eye Diseases: Bloodshot Eyes, Xanthelasma, Film on the Eyes, and Related Conditions (Ebers Papyrus)
segments.original_text: equal parts, often sufficed to clear up Bloodshot Eyes. If it did not a more complicated poultice composed of Onions, Verdigris, Collyrium, and Arabian-wood powder, crushed 'to a powder' with Ink and Water was recommended. Then there was another: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT BLOOD IN THE EYES Two Clay vessels: in one put powdered Fruit-of-the-Dompalm and Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son. In the other Cow's Milk. Keep moist. In the morning bathe both eyes from the Fruit-of-the-Dompalm. Next wash the Eyes with the Cow's Milk four times for six days. Xanthelasma seemingly proved a troublesome condition, for after laying down various remedies the Scribe thought it advisable to impart some ginger into the treatment: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT FAT-IN-THE-EYES Red-lead I Goose-grease I Smear the eyes therewith. SEE TO IT! Another remedy approximated more to the modern treatment of the disease: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT FAT-IN-THE-EYES Knife-stone Mix in Fresh Milk and apply thereto very often. To-day it is the Knife instead of a Knife-stone that we apply to the eye, while the patient is instructed to drink the Fresh Milk. 'To him whose two eyes suffer from a flow of matter' a poultice of Clay-from-a-Statue, Leaves-of-the-Castor-oil-tree, and Honey, is advised. 'To restore the lustre of the eyes one could apply a poultice of Onions, Resin, and Verdigris, crushed in the Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son. If a film covered the eyes the remedies were many, but two will suffice: TO DISPEL THE FILM IN THE EYES ON THE FIRST DAY Water-from-the-Bird-pond I Whether this was drunk or merely applied to the eyes does not appear; but fortunately in the next remedy there was no room for doubt: ANOTHER REMEDY FOR THE FILMING-OVER WHICH RISES IN THE EYE Dried-excrement-from-the-Body-of-a-Child 1 Honey I Put in Fresh Milk and then apply to the Eyes. Leucoma was a complaint that set these far-off Oculists on their mettle. They tried to coax it away with
segments.translation:
In equal parts, [the preceding preparation] often sufficed to clear up bloodshot eyes [conjunctival hyperemia]. If it did not, a more complicated poultice composed of onions, verdigris [copper acetate], collyrium [kohl/antimony-based eye cosmetic], and Arabian-wood powder, crushed to a powder with ink and water, was recommended. There was then another prescription:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT BLOOD IN THE EYES
Two clay vessels: in one put powdered Fruit-of-the-Dompalm [doum palm fruit, Hyphaene thebaica] and Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son. In the other, Cow's Milk. Keep moist. In the morning bathe both eyes with the Fruit-of-the-Dompalm [preparation]. Next wash the eyes with the Cow's Milk four times [daily] for six days.

Xanthelasma seemingly proved a troublesome condition, for after laying down various remedies the scribe thought it advisable to inject some urgency into the treatment:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT FAT-IN-THE-EYES
Red-lead [minium, lead tetroxide], 1 [part]
Goose-grease, 1 [part]
Smear the eyes therewith. SEE TO IT!

Another remedy approximated more closely to the modern treatment of the disease:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT FAT-IN-THE-EYES
Knife-stone [whetstone or obsidian scraper]
Mix in Fresh Milk and apply thereto very often.
