Today it is the knife rather than a knife-stone that we apply to the eye, while the patient is instructed to drink the fresh milk.

'To him whose two eyes suffer from a flow of matter [purulent discharge/conjunctivitis],' a poultice of Clay-from-a-Statue, Leaves-of-the-Castor-oil-tree [Ricinus communis], and Honey is advised.

'To restore the lustre of the eyes,' one could apply a poultice of Onions, Resin, and Verdigris [copper acetate], crushed in Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son.

If a film [opacity] covered the eyes, the remedies were many, but two will suffice:

TO DISPEL THE FILM IN THE EYES ON THE FIRST DAY
Water-from-the-Bird-pond, 1 [part]

Whether this was drunk or merely applied to the eyes does not appear; but fortunately in the next remedy there was no room for doubt:

ANOTHER REMEDY FOR THE FILMING-OVER WHICH RISES IN THE EYE
Dried-excrement-from-the-Body-of-a-Child, 1 [part]
Honey, 1 [part]
Put in Fresh Milk and then apply to the Eyes.

Leucoma [corneal opacity/white scar on the cornea] was a complaint that set these early oculists on their mettle. They tried to coax it away with [the text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Egyptian Eye Disease Treatments from the Papyrus Ebers: Cataract, Pterygium, and Ritual Formulae
segments.original_text: EYES There is a Shouting in the Southern Sky in the Darkness. There is an Uproar in the Northern Sky. The Hall of Pillars falls into the Waters. The Ship-folk of the Sun-god beat their oars so that the heads at his side fall into the water. Who leads hither what he finds? DISEASES OF THE EYES I lead forth what I find. I lead forth your heads. I lift up your necks. I fasten what has been cut from you in its place. I lead you forth to drive away the God of Fevers and all possible Deadly Arts. SO MANY THERE ARE. A formula to repeat over the Brain-of-a-Tortoise that is mixed in Honey, and then laid on the Eyes. Cataract was another condition which had the Ancients at their wits' end. Goose-grease and Honey was tried as a poultice; Isinglass and Verdigris; Lapis lazuli, Milk, Incense, and Crocodile-earth. But how intractable this disease must have proved itself is revealed in the call made to the gods to reinforce the remedy: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT CATARACT IN THE EYES Come, Verdigris! Come, Verdigris! Come, Thou Fresh One! Come, Efflux from the Eye of the god Horus! It comes, That which issues forth from the Eye of Tum! Come, Juice that gushes from Osiris! He comes to him, he drives away from him Water, Matter, Blood, Inflammation of the Eyes, Mattery-discharge, Blindness, Dripping Eyes. This the God of Fever works all Deadly Arts, the uwedu of every kind, and all things evil of these eyes. SO MANY THERE ARE OF THEM. Words to be spoken over Verdigris mixed with Beetle-wax. Add Cyperus thereto and carefully apply to the Eye. Another growth in the eye, Pterygium, was not looked on kindly, judging by the remedies which these people appeared ready to submit to. Certainly the Scribe leads off with an inoffensive poultice of Honeycomb, following it with another of Beetle-wax, but these must have proved useless for he soon passes on to more complicated and stronger remedies. Excrement-of-the-henut-bird, is recommended, mixed up with Sea-salt and [TEXT CONTINUES]
segments.translation:
DISEASES OF THE EYES

[Ritual incantation:] There is a Shouting in the Southern Sky in the Darkness. There is an Uproar in the Northern Sky. The Hall of Pillars falls into the Waters. The Ship-folk of the Sun-god beat their oars so that the heads at his side fall into the water. Who leads hither what he finds? I lead forth what I find. I lead forth your heads. I lift up your necks. I fasten what has been cut from you in its place. I lead you forth to drive away the God of Fevers and all possible Deadly Arts. SO MANY THERE ARE.

[Prescription:] A formula [incantation] to be recited over Brain-of-a-Tortoise mixed with Honey, then laid upon the Eyes.

Cataract [opacity of the lens] was another condition which had the Ancients at their wits' end. Goose-grease and Honey were tried as a poultice; Isinglass [fish-derived gelatin] and Verdigris [copper acetate]; Lapis lazuli, Milk, Incense, and Crocodile-earth [dried crocodile dung or mud associated with crocodiles]. How intractable this disease must have proved is revealed in the call made to the gods to reinforce the remedy:

ANOTHER [INCANTATION] TO DRIVE OUT CATARACT IN THE EYES

Come, Verdigris! Come, Verdigris! Come, Thou Fresh One! Come, Efflux from the Eye of the god Horus! It comes—that which issues forth from the Eye of Atum [Tum]! Come, Juice that gushes from Osiris! He comes to him; he drives away from him: Water, Discharge [Matter], Blood, Inflammation of the Eyes, Purulent discharge [Mattery-discharge], Blindness, Weeping Eyes [Dripping Eyes]. These the God of Fever works through all Deadly Arts—the uwedu [a class of evil supernatural agency or disease-demon] of every kind—and all things evil afflicting these eyes. SO MANY THERE ARE OF THEM.

[Prescription:] Words to be spoken over Verdigris mixed with Beetle-wax [beeswax or insect-derived wax]. Add Cyperus [rush or sedge, likely Cyperus rotundus] thereto and carefully apply to the Eye.

Another growth in the eye—Pterygium [a fibrovascular conjunctival overgrowth]—was evidently regarded with great concern, judging by the remedies to which these people were prepared to submit. The Scribe begins with an inoffensive poultice of Honeycomb, followed by another of Beetle-wax, but these must have proved useless, for he soon passes on to more complicated and stronger remedies. Excrement of the henut-bird is recommended, mixed with Sea-salt and [TEXT CONTINUES — segment ends abruptly].

segments.title_inferred: Ancient Egyptian Ophthalmic Remedies: Pterygium, Ocular Discharge, Trichiasis, and Ingrowing Eyelashes
segments.original_text:
Crushed some Collyrium in the Egg-of-a-Vulture; but what the colour of the following turned out to be only a brave man would say.

DISEASES OF THE EYES

ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT PTERYGIUM
Red-lead
Powdered-wood-from-Arabia
Iron-from-Apollonopolis-parva
Calamine
Egg-of-an-Ostrich
Saltpetre-from-Upper-Egypt
Sulphur
Honey
Make into one and apply to the Eyes.

Eyes which suffered from a 'flow of matter' were dismissed with a mere couple of remedies. Clay-from-a-Statue was ground up in Honey along with some Leaves-from-the-Castor-Oil-tree, and applied to the eyes. The second remedy was not so easily put up. Some Real Collyrium was soaked in Water for four days; then for another four days it was soaked in Goose-grease. It was now washed in the Milk-of-a-Woman-who-had-borne-a-Son, and allowed to dry for nine days. Thereafter it was crushed with some Myrrh, made up into a ball, and rubbed on the eye.

The victims of ingrowing eyelashes must have been a veritable godsend to the rising ophthalmologist in Ancient Egypt. At the same time they must have proved a very severe drain on the live-stock of the country, as may be seen from a perusal of some of the remedies prescribed for that irritating affliction:

TO DRIVE OUT TRICHIASIS
Myrrh 1
Lizard's Blood 1
Bat's Blood 1
Tear out the Hairs and put thereon in order to make him well.

TO PREVENT THE HAIR GROWING INTO THE EYE AFTER IT HAS BEEN PULLED OUT
Incense-ground-in-Lizard's-dung
Cow's Blood
Ass's Blood
Pig's Blood
Dog's Blood
Stag's Blood
Collyrium
Incense
Crush, rub into one in the different kinds of Blood, and place on the part whence the Hair has been pulled out so that it may not grow again.

ANOTHER
Bat's Blood 1
Rim-of-a-new-hennu-vessel 1
Honey 1
Powder and place where the Hair has been pulled out.

ANOTHER
Fat-of-an-Ox 1
Olive Oil 1
Entrails-of-Moles 1
Crush into one, put on the fire, and put in the place of the Hair.

ANOTHER
Brain-of-[text breaks off]
segments.translation:
Crushed some collyrium [eye-paint/medicinal eye preparation] in the egg of a vulture; but what colour the resulting mixture turned out to be, only a brave man would venture to say.

DISEASES OF THE EYES

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT PTERYGIUM [pterygium: abnormal conjunctival growth]
Red-lead
Powdered wood from Arabia
Iron ore from Apollonopolis Parva [a town in Upper Egypt]
Calamine
Egg of an ostrich
Saltpetre from Upper Egypt
Sulphur
Honey
Combine into one preparation and apply to the eyes.

Eyes suffering from a 'flow of matter' [ocular discharge / conjunctival suppuration] were treated with only a couple of remedies. Clay from a statue was ground up in honey together with leaves from the castor-oil tree, and applied to the eyes. The second remedy required more elaborate preparation. Some genuine collyrium was soaked in water for four days; then for a further four days it was soaked in goose-grease. It was then washed in the milk of a woman who had borne a son, and allowed to dry for nine days. It was thereafter crushed with some myrrh, formed into a ball, and rubbed on the eye.

Sufferers from ingrowing eyelashes must have been a veritable godsend to the aspiring ophthalmologist in ancient Egypt, though at the same time they must have placed a very severe drain on the livestock of the country, as may be judged from a perusal of some of the remedies prescribed for that irritating affliction:

TO DRIVE OUT TRICHIASIS [ingrowing eyelashes]
Myrrh — 1 part
Lizard's blood — 1 part
Bat's blood — 1 part
Pull out the hairs and apply this preparation thereto in order to cure him.

TO PREVENT THE HAIR GROWING BACK INTO THE EYE AFTER IT HAS BEEN PULLED OUT
Incense ground in lizard's dung
Cow's blood
Ass's blood
Pig's blood
Dog's blood
Stag's blood
Collyrium
Incense
Crush together, mix thoroughly in the various kinds of blood, and place upon the site from which the hair was pulled out, so that it may not grow again.

ANOTHER REMEDY
Bat's blood — 1 part
Rim of a new hennu-vessel [a ritual/measuring vessel] — 1 part
Honey — 1 part
Grind to a powder and place where the hair was pulled out.

ANOTHER REMEDY
Fat of an ox — 1 part
Olive oil — 1 part
Entrails of moles — 1 part
Crush together, heat over the fire, and apply to the site from which the hair was removed.

ANOTHER REMEDY
Brain of — [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Ebers Papyrus: Eye Remedies and Related Treatments
segments.original_text: calamity. The first, a 'lotion' made by crushing Dried Myrrh in Sour Milk, we may be sure accomplished nothing. The second, however, a poultice of Powdered Onions, most assuredly made the blind man see stars. The third remedy spared the patient somewhat, the disease being attacked through that less-sensitive organ, the ear: ANOTHER AGAINST BLINDNESS The Two Eyes of a Pig; remove the water therefrom True Collyrium Red-lead Wild Honey Crush, powder, make into one, and inject into the Ear of the Patient. Thereby he will at once recover. When thou hast seen properly to this mixing, repeat this Magic Formula: I HAVE BROUGHT THIS THING AND PUT IT IN ITS PLACE. THE CROCODILE IS WEAK AND POWERLESS. (Twice). 'For the Drawing-together of the Pupil of the Eye,' whatever that may mean, one bathed the eye frequently with a lotion made by crushing Shavings-from-Ebony-Wood with Soda-from-Upper-Egypt in Water. For a swelling in the Nose caused by inflammation of the tear-sac a salve composed of Collyrium, Dried Myrrh, Arabian-wood powder, and Honey, was rubbed in for four days. 'MARX TUTS WELL (the Scribe adds), FOR IT IS THE RIGHT THING TO DO.' Another eye-salve is prescribed for 'Driving out Tumours in-the-Head,' while the Scribe also finds room to quote two salves for general use: one 'AS PRESCRIBED BY THE PRIESTLY PHARMACIST XUI,' the other 'AS TOLD US BY A JEW FROM BYBLOS.' As well he provides against any obscure ailment of the eye not specifically dealt with by him: REMEDY FOR THE EYE WHEN SOMETHING EVIL HAS HAPPENED TO IT: A Human Brain. Divide it in halves. To one half add Honey and anoint the Eye therewith in the Evening. Dry the other half, crush, powder, and anoint the Eye therewith in the Morning. To correct Squint, Collyrium, Red-lead, and Soda were beaten into a paste and applied to the wandering eye. When the futility of this became apparent, a poultice of Onions, Granite, and Resin, was tried. This in turn failed.
segments.translation:
The text describes a series of remedies drawn from the Papyrus Ebers, a collection of ancient Egyptian medical prescriptions, here presented with editorial commentary. The first remedy for blindness — a lotion made by crushing Dried Myrrh in Sour Milk — is judged by the modern commentator to have been entirely ineffective. The second remedy, a poultice of Powdered Onions applied to the eyes, would certainly have caused intense irritation ('made the blind man see stars'). The third remedy takes a different approach, treating the eye disease via the ear, presumably on the theory that this route would be less painful:

ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST BLINDNESS:
Ingredients: The two eyes of a pig (the fluid/water removed); True Collyrium [antimony or kohl]; Red-lead [lead oxide/minium]; Wild Honey. Method: Crush, powder, combine into one preparation, and inject [instill] into the ear of the patient. He will thereby recover at once. After correctly mixing the preparation, recite the following magical formula: 'I HAVE BROUGHT THIS THING AND PUT IT IN ITS PLACE. THE CROCODILE IS WEAK AND POWERLESS.' (To be repeated twice.)

For a condition described as 'Drawing-together of the Pupil of the Eye' [possibly miosis or pupil contraction of uncertain etiology], the eye was frequently bathed with a lotion made by crushing ebony-wood shavings together with natron [soda/sodium carbonate] from Upper Egypt in water.

For a swelling of the nose caused by inflammation of the tear-sac [dacryocystitis], a salve composed of Collyrium, Dried Myrrh, Arabian-wood powder [possibly a resinous wood from Arabia], and Honey was rubbed in for four consecutive days. The scribe appends the note: 'THIS WORKS WELL, FOR IT IS THE RIGHT THING TO DO.'

A further eye-salve is prescribed for 'Driving out Tumours in the Head.' The scribe also records two salves for general ophthalmic use: one as prescribed by the priestly pharmacist Xui, the other as communicated by a Jew from Byblos [ancient Phoenician city, modern Jubayl, Lebanon].

The scribe also provides a catch-all remedy for unspecified eye ailments:

REMEDY FOR THE EYE WHEN SOMETHING EVIL HAS HAPPENED TO IT:
Ingredients: A human brain. Method: Divide in half. Mix one half with Honey and anoint the eye with this in the evening. Dry the other half, crush to a powder, and anoint the eye with this in the morning.

For the correction of a squint [strabismus], Collyrium, Red-lead, and Soda were beaten into a paste and applied to the affected eye. When the inefficacy of this treatment became evident, a poultice of Onions, Granite [powdered granite or stone], and Resin was attempted. This too ultimately failed.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Eye, Ear, Nose and Mouth Diseases from the Papyrus Ebers
segments.original_text: Several remedies for 'Hotness in the Eyes,' a condition which without doubt must have been visited rather frequently on those driven for various reasons to place themselves under the care and treatment of an Oculist of the Pharaohonic period. As an eye-cooler, 'Baked Ox-liver, placed carefully thereon' does not sound very hopeful, but Soda-from-Upper-Egypt (even when dissolved in Spring-water) sounds positively alarming in spite of the Scribe's special note appended to the prescription: 'It Heats It!' The third remedy, however, shows more promise, and reveals once again a belief on the part of these ancient physicians in the homœopathic doctrine of like-curing-like: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT HOTNESS IN THE EYES: Tallow-from-the-Jawbone-of-an-Ass. Mix in Cool Water and let the Patient put on his Temples in order that he may be healed forthwith. Chapter XVII: DISEASES OF THE EAR, NOSE AND MOUTH. In contradistinction to eye-troubles, affections of the Ears receive scant treatment. As might be expected, the chapter opens with a remedy for the Ear-that-Hears-Badly, but it is extremely doubtful if the sufferer experienced any relief from having his ears plugged with Red-lead and Resin-from-the-am-tree, even when rubbed up beforehand with Fresh Olive Oil. For an Ear-that-Discharges-foul-smelling-Matter, an application of Incense-in-Goose-Grease ground up with Cream-from-the-Milk-of-a-Cow in various grains is recommended, but whether as poultice or injection is not altogether clear. Not that it mattered much, one is safe in asserting. Failing the above, an injection composed of Ass's Ear, Red-lead, Caraway and Olive Oil, could be squirted into the Ear. But discharging ears were as resistant to treatment in those days as in these, and detailed instructions were sometimes necessary: ANOTHER TO TREAT THE EAR: Treat it with cooling remedies, not warm ones. When the met trembles then make for him [an amulet] out of Greenstone. Crush an [remainder of text cut off].
segments.translation: Several remedies are given for 'Hotness in the Eyes' [ocular inflammation], a condition that must have been quite common among those who sought the care of an oculist during the Pharaonic period. As an eye-cooler, 'Baked Ox-liver, placed carefully thereon' does not sound very hopeful; and Soda-from-Upper-Egypt [natron], even when dissolved in spring-water, sounds positively alarming, despite the scribe's special marginal note appended to the prescription reading: 'It Heats It!' The third remedy, however, shows more promise and reveals once again the ancient physicians' belief in the homœopathic doctrine of like curing like: ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT HOTNESS IN THE EYES: Tallow from the jawbone of an ass. Mix in cool water and let the patient apply it to his temples so that he may be healed forthwith. Chapter XVII: DISEASES OF THE EAR, NOSE, AND MOUTH. In contrast to eye complaints, affections of the ears receive comparatively little treatment. As might be expected, the chapter opens with a remedy for the Ear-that-Hears-Badly [deafness or impaired hearing], though it is extremely doubtful whether the sufferer gained any relief from having his ears plugged with red lead [minium] and resin from the 'am'-tree, even when first rubbed up with fresh olive oil. For an Ear-that-Discharges-Foul-Smelling-Matter [otorrhea], an application of incense in goose-grease, ground together with cream from cow's milk, combined with various grain ingredients, is recommended—though it is not entirely clear whether this was intended as a poultice or an injection. Failing that remedy, an injection composed of ass's ear [a plant ingredient], red lead, caraway, and olive oil could be instilled into the ear. Discharging ears were, however, as resistant to treatment then as now, and detailed instructions were sometimes necessary: ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO TREAT THE EAR: Treat it with cooling remedies, not warm ones. When the 'met' [possibly a vessel or tendon; Egyptian anatomical term] trembles, then make for him [an amulet or preparation] out of greenstone [malachite or similar green mineral]. Crush an [text incomplete].

segments.title_inferred: Treatments for Tumours, Ear Ulcers, and Coryza (Ebers Papyrus)
segments.original_text: It clears up through the shooting-forth of the god Su. If it does not fall from him onto the earth, then make for him Slime-from-the-Greatness-of-that-which-presents-itself (i.e. the tumour), and enclose it in the Milky-Juice-of-the-Sycamore-Tree, that it may bind up his blood; neither Oil nor Honey put thereto; DISEASES OF EAR, NOSE, AND MOUTH cut off a half, as one would not wish that his Blood fall away from the other half... (uncertain). 4. WHEN AFTERWARDS THOU RECOGNIZEST THAT IT HAS BOUND ITSELF, SO MAKE THOU FOR HIM: Oil Wax Cook and plaster therewith. Don't use too much. Treat in the same way every swelling which breaks. If it goes off in abundance, then have a Linen band tied around the back of his head. Considered in the light of more recent research it seems a pity for the sake of the patient that the Linen-band was not thought of in the first place and the remainder of the remedy conveniently forgotten. Lastly, in the event of ulceration forming and extending into the Ear: WHAT TO DO TO TREAT ULCERS WHICH EXTEND INTO THE EAR There is a swelling internally. There is matter from the ulcers and from the dirt in his Ears, with fluid like Water-of-the-fermented-meswxa-drink. Go round the Ulcers with the Knife as far as there is Disease therein. Prepare for him Oil and Honey; put inside of it Charpie of Flax-of-a-cloth. Plaster it therewith so that it becomes well. THE PAPYRUS EBERS The nose is dealt with even more summarily than are the Ears, Coryza being the only condition mentioned. It could be treated by pouring Date-juice into the nostrils, or by rubbing the Nose with Peppermint-in-Dates; or much more vigorously, not to mention picturesquely, by a combination of Magic, Medicine and Surgery: TO CHARM AWAY CORYZA Spit it out, thou Slime, Son of Slime: Grasp the bones, touch the skull, smear with tallow, give the patient seven openings in the head, serve the god Ra, thank the god Thoth. Then I brought thy remedy for thee, thy drink for the
segments.translation:
It clears up through the shooting-forth [divine expulsion] of the god Su. If it [the tumour or swelling] does not fall from him onto the earth, then prepare for him the Slime-from-the-Greatness-of-that-which-presents-itself (i.e., the tumour), and enclose it in the Milky-Juice-of-the-Sycamore-Tree, so that it may bind up [staunch] his blood; add neither Oil nor Honey to it. [Section heading:] DISEASES OF EAR, NOSE, AND MOUTH. Cut away a half [of the swelling], as one would not wish his Blood to flow away from the other half... (meaning uncertain). 

4. WHEN AFTERWARDS THOU RECOGNIZEST THAT IT HAS BOUND ITSELF [the wound has consolidated], THEN PREPARE FOR HIM: Oil [and] Wax — cook [together] and apply as a plaster. Do not use too much. Treat in the same manner every swelling that breaks open. If [the discharge] flows out in abundance, have a linen bandage tied around the back of his head. Considered in the light of more recent research, it seems a pity for the sake of the patient that the linen bandage was not thought of in the first instance, with the remainder of the remedy conveniently forgotten.

Lastly, in the event of ulceration forming and extending into the Ear:

WHAT TO DO TO TREAT ULCERS WHICH EXTEND INTO THE EAR
There is an internal swelling. There is discharge [pus and matter] from the ulcers and from the accumulated dirt in his Ears, with fluid resembling the Water-of-the-fermented-meswxa-drink [a fermented beverage used as a comparator for the appearance or odour of the discharge]. Go around the Ulcers with the Knife [i.e., excise] as far as the Disease extends within. Prepare for him Oil and Honey; place inside it Charpie [lint/tow] of Flax taken from a cloth. Apply it as a plaster so that he recovers.

THE PAPYRUS EBERS
The nose is treated even more briefly than the ears, Coryza [rhinitis/common cold] being the only condition mentioned. It could be treated by pouring Date-juice into the nostrils, or by rubbing the Nose with Peppermint-in-Dates; or, much more vigorously — not to mention picturesquely — by a combination of Magic, Medicine, and Surgery:

TO CHARM AWAY CORYZA
Spit it out, thou Slime, Son of Slime! Grasp the bones, touch the skull, smear [the head] with tallow, make seven openings in the patient's head, serve the god Ra, give thanks to the god Thoth. Then I have brought thy remedy for thee, thy drink for the [passage incomplete].

segments.title_inferred: Diseases of the Ear, Nose, and Mouth; Dental Remedies; Introduction to the Nervous System — Papyrus Ebers
segments.original_text: into the mouth of the Ancient Egyptian by the physicians of the day, it is remarkable to note how lightly that cavity (with its contents) escaped serious damage. The diseased tongue is dismissed DISEASES OF EAR, NOSE, AND MOUTH 111 with a few simple remedies. At first a Milk-gargle is deemed sufficient: BEGINNING OF THE REMEDIES TO DRIVE OUT DISEASES OF THE TONGUE; Milk Gargle and put on the earth (i.e. spit out). If the disease persisted, the Milk is reinforced by Goose-grease and the gargle turned into a chewing-remedy: ANOTHER amaa-erain Goose-grease Milk Chew. Another chewing-remedy was composed of Cow's Milk, Fresh Bread and Bullock's Fat; while still another could be prepared from Goose-Grease, Honey, Incense, Caraway-seeds, Fresh Lead-earth and Water, a specific instruction to chew it Nine Times being added. As for the teeth, all that these appeared to need from time to time was 'strengthening,' which, after all, was not to be wondered at. Powdered-Fruit-of-the-Dom-palm with Honey effected this, we learn: it was rubbed on the teeth. If it did not, then Incense, Verdigris, and Fresh Lead-earth was tried; while extra-stubborn cases could try a still grittier preparation in the shape of Pebbles-powdered-in-Honey. The 'strengthening' process could also be carried out by the chewing method; Crocus-in-Sweet-Beer was one of the remedies recommended. Gumboils (even the Ancients suffered from these painful afflictions!) were also found to yield to a chewing-remedy composed of Cow's Milk, Fresh Dates, and uah-grain, provided, however, it was kept moist and chewed Nine Times. And they also had 'Blisters-in-the-Teeth,' whatever they may mean. These were dealt with by a plaster which included Incense, Crocus, Cyperus, Onions, the Wood-of-Aloes, and Water, not to mention a variety of herbs of whose name and nature we are still in ignorance. Chapter XVIII THE NERVOUS SYSTEM THE proper interpretation to give to the word 'met' has exercised
segments.translation: Among the remedies placed into the mouths of ancient Egyptians by the physicians of the time, it is remarkable to note how little serious damage was done to the oral cavity and its contents. Diseases of the tongue are addressed with only a few simple remedies. At first, a milk gargle was deemed sufficient — the heading reads: BEGINNING OF THE REMEDIES TO DRIVE OUT DISEASES OF THE TONGUE: Milk. Gargle and spit out onto the ground. If the disease persisted, the milk was reinforced with goose-grease and the gargle transformed into a chewing-remedy: ANOTHER [amaa-erain, Egyptian term untranslated]: Goose-grease, Milk. Chew. Another chewing-remedy was composed of Cow's Milk, Fresh Bread, and Bullock's Fat; yet another could be prepared from Goose-Grease, Honey, Incense, Caraway-seeds, Fresh Lead-earth, and Water, with specific instruction to chew it nine times. As for the teeth, all they appeared to require from time to time was 'strengthening,' which is scarcely surprising given the diet. Powdered fruit of the Doum-palm mixed with Honey accomplished this, we are told; it was rubbed onto the teeth. If that failed, Incense, Verdigris, and Fresh Lead-earth were tried; while particularly stubborn cases could attempt an even grittier preparation in the form of Pebbles-powdered-in-Honey. The 'strengthening' process could also be performed by the chewing method; Crocus-in-Sweet-Beer was one of the remedies recommended. Gumboils — even the ancients suffered from these painful afflictions — were likewise found to yield to a chewing-remedy composed of Cow's Milk, Fresh Dates, and uah-grain [an unidentified Egyptian grain], provided it was kept moist and chewed nine times. There are also references to 'Blisters-in-the-Teeth,' the precise meaning of which remains unclear. These were treated with a plaster that included Incense, Crocus, Cyperus, Onions, the Wood-of-Aloes, and Water, as well as a variety of herbs whose names and natures remain unknown to us. Chapter XVIII: THE NERVOUS SYSTEM. The proper interpretation to be given to the word 'met' has long exercised [scholars — text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for the Met, the Nervous System, and the AAA Disease in the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: the Body' he set down several remedies. One, an internal remedy, was compounded of Figs, Grapes, and Onions, Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus, Blossoms-of-the-Cucumber, Incense, Wine, and Sweet Beer. It was taken for four days. Irregularities of the met were cured by the patient chewing uwah-grain while at the same time he clapped on to his buttocks a poultice of Goose's eggs— still another illustration of the Scribe's belief in the doctrine of like-curing-like. As to the possibility of the human body assimilating the foregoing remedies there appears to have been a doubt in the mind of the Scribe. At any rate he very properly provided, or attempted to provide, against that eventuality, with what success it is not very difficult to estimate even at this distance of time: THE NERVOUS SYSTEM A REMEDY TO ALLOW THE MET TO TAKE UP THE REMEDY Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son Let it stand in a New Vessel until the Cream separates. Then smear the Patient. Chapter XIX THE AAA DISEASE Of all the difficulties that beset the translators of the Papyrus Ebers the most difficult centred around the diagnosis of the conditions which were continually cropping up in the text under the names of the AAA disease, the uxedu-disease, and the uha-disease. Sometimes these conditions were described and treated separately. At other times they complicated each other sadly. Three facts led at first to the AAA disease being diagnosed as Bilharzia. These were firstly that throughout the papyrus the AAA disease is almost invariably qualified by the adjective 'deadly.' Secondly the disease was specifically related to 'worms'; for example on Plate 19 of the Papyrus there appears a 'remedy to kill worms in the body that have been caused by the AAA disease.' And thirdly that the symbol AAA was always followed by a Phallus as the 'determinative sign.' This diagnosis of Bilharzia, however, is discarded by Joachim, who holds that the AAA disease is in reality the common Egyptian scourge, Chlorosis.
segments.translation:
Regarding the body, the Scribe set down several remedies. One internal remedy was compounded of Figs, Grapes, and Onions, Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus [bread made from the fruit of Ziziphus lotus], Blossoms-of-the-Cucumber, Incense, Wine, and Sweet Beer. It was to be taken over four days. Irregularities of the 'met' [the Egyptian concept of bodily channels or vessels] were treated by having the patient chew 'uwah-grain' while simultaneously applying a poultice of Goose's eggs to his buttocks — yet another illustration of the Scribe's adherence to the doctrine of like-curing-like [similia similibus curantur]. As to whether the human body could actually assimilate these remedies, the Scribe himself appears to have harbored some doubt. In any case, he duly provided — or attempted to provide — against that contingency, with what success it is not difficult to judge even at this remove in time.

THE NERVOUS SYSTEM

A REMEDY TO ALLOW THE MET TO ABSORB THE REMEDY:
Milk of a Woman Who Has Borne a Son — let it stand in a New Vessel until the Cream separates. Then smear the Patient.

Chapter XIX: THE AAA DISEASE

Of all the difficulties confronting the translators of the Papyrus Ebers, the most formidable centered on the diagnosis of conditions that recur throughout the text under the designations of the 'AAA disease,' the 'uxedu-disease,' and the 'uha-disease.' These conditions are sometimes described and treated separately, and at other times they are confusingly interrelated. Three facts initially led scholars to identify the AAA disease with Bilharzia [schistosomiasis]: first, throughout the papyrus the AAA disease is almost invariably qualified by the adjective 'deadly'; second, the disease was specifically associated with 'worms' — for instance, Plate 19 of the Papyrus contains a 'remedy to kill worms in the body that have been caused by the AAA disease'; and third, the hieroglyphic symbol AAA was always followed by the Phallus as a 'determinative sign' [a hieroglyph indicating semantic category]. This diagnosis of Bilharzia is, however, rejected by Joachim, who argues that the AAA disease is in reality Chlorosis [iron-deficiency anemia], the common Egyptian affliction.

segments.title_inferred: Diagnosis and Remedies for the AAA Disease in the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: the authority Griesinger) afflicted one out of every four of the population of Egypt. The second he waves aside on the ground that the 'worms' mentioned could apply equally to the Hookworm as to the Bilharzia parasite. Then as to the third point he insists that the use of the Phallus as the 'determinative sign' following the symbol AAA must be viewed in the light of two other considerations: firstly that the Phallus is also the 'determinative sign' placed after AA, the symbol by which the much-mentioned Ass is designated in the Papyrus (a fact which very considerably diminishes the importance of its significance in the former case); and secondly that the use of the male generative organ in such a way is as easily applicable to Chlorosis Egyptica as to Bilharzia since delayed puberty, impotence in males, and sterility in females, were frequent sequelae of the Chlorosis. As to the uha-disease and the uxedu-disease, Joachim holds that they are merely manifestations of the AAA disease. The uxedu-disease, he declares, can be ascribed to the scourge in its early stages, although he is careful to point out that there are certain objections to that view which he cannot combat with satisfaction to himself. He feels on surer ground with the uha-disease, however, and unhesitatingly associates it with Chlorosis Aegyptica. THE PAPYRUS EBERS. But whatever the diagnosis, and the balance of evidence is in favour of Joachim's contention, the multitudinous remedies proposed for the AAA disease, whether by itself or complicated by the uha-disease or the uxedu-disease, brand it as a scourge of the first magnitude. Scores of 'remedies' are set down for combating its ravages, their number providing active proof (if such were needed) of the old adage that where there are many cures there is no cure. Added to which it can hardly be maintained that any one of the remedies proposed inspires a modern practitioner with any feeling of confidence in their success. To take a sample at random—
segments.translation: [(According to the authority Griesinger,)] [the AAA disease] afflicted one out of every four of the population of Egypt. The second objection [Joachim] waves aside on the ground that the 'worms' mentioned could apply equally to the Hookworm as to the Bilharzia parasite. Then, as to the third point, he insists that the use of the Phallus as the 'determinative sign' [hieroglyphic classifier] following the symbol AAA must be viewed in the light of two other considerations: firstly, that the Phallus is also the 'determinative sign' placed after AA, the symbol by which the much-mentioned Ass is designated in the Papyrus—a fact which very considerably diminishes the importance of its significance in the former case; and secondly, that the use of the male generative organ in such a symbolic capacity is as easily applicable to Chlorosis Aegyptica [Egyptian chlorosis, a severe iron-deficiency anaemia] as to Bilharzia [schistosomiasis], since delayed puberty, impotence in males, and sterility in females were frequent sequelae [consequences] of the Chlorosis. As to the uha-disease and the uxedu-disease, Joachim holds that they are merely manifestations of the AAA disease. The uxedu-disease, he declares, can be ascribed to the scourge in its early stages, although he is careful to point out that there are certain objections to that view which he cannot combat to his own satisfaction. He feels on surer ground with the uha-disease, however, and unhesitatingly associates it with Chlorosis Aegyptica. [Heading: THE PAPYRUS EBERS.] But whatever the diagnosis—and the balance of evidence is in favour of Joachim's contention—the multitudinous remedies proposed for the AAA disease, whether by itself or complicated by the uha-disease or the uxedu-disease, mark it as a scourge of the first magnitude. Scores of 'remedies' are set down for combating its ravages, their very number providing active proof (if such were needed) of the old adage that where there are many cures there is no cure. segments.title_inferred: Remedies for the Uxedu Disease and Related Conditions (Ebers Papyrus)
segments.original_text: taken, several ointments, and various suppositories. One mixture is in the nature of an infusion: ANOTHER FOR THE UXEDU Bark-of-the-Pomegranate-tree-soaked-in-Beer Keep moist in a jug of Water. Strain a sufficient amount each morning and let the Patient drink. There is no evidence that this remedy benefited the patient, but at least it could be drunk. This feat hardly seems possible with the next mixture in which the Flesh-of-a-Live-Cow was combined with Freshly-baked Bread, Incense, Lettuce, and Sweet Beer, and ordered to be 'drunk' for four days. Another mixture of Ass's Milk with Dough, Wine, and Bitter Beer, was also ordered to be 'drunk' for four days. Sometimes the uxedu caused spots to appear on the skin. A mixture of Onions, Turpentine, and Cow's Milk, drunk for four days, knocked the spots off. When the uxedu-disease was complicated by the uha-disease the Scribe was rather at a loss. He counselled a salve of Sea-salt, Honey, and Cow's Bile; but at a later stage, having doubts as to the potency of Cow's Bile in this connection, he proceeded to repeat the salve, substituting however Cow's Brain for Cow's Bile. Even now he was not satisfied that the remedy would stand the test and seemingly in despair he resorted to what many a harried modern practitioner is driven to do to-day—he recommended a palliative. In this case it was the old familiar household remedy, Castor Oil, that he employed; but instead of administering it in the old familiar household way, namely a tablespoonful internally overnight, he ordered the oil to be rubbed in. Albeit, he took a roundabout way of saying so: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT THE UXEDU Oil-expressed-from-the-Seeds-of-the-Castor-Oil-bush Smear therewith any person who has the uha-swelling.
segments.translation: The text describes several ointments and suppositories, as well as a series of mixtures prescribed for the 'uxedu' disease [an ancient Egyptian pathological category, exact identification uncertain]. One preparation takes the form of an infusion: ANOTHER REMEDY FOR THE UXEDU — Bark of the Pomegranate tree soaked in Beer, kept moist in a jug of Water. Each morning a sufficient quantity is to be strained off and given to the patient to drink. There is no evidence that this remedy benefited the patient, but at least it was drinkable. The same can hardly be said of the next mixture, in which the Flesh of a Live Cow was combined with Freshly-baked Bread, Incense, Lettuce, and Sweet Beer, and prescribed to be 'drunk' over four days. Another mixture consisting of Ass's Milk with Dough, Wine, and Bitter Beer was likewise prescribed to be 'drunk' over four days; it appears somewhat more liquid than the former preparation, though barely. When the uxedu produced spots on the skin, a mixture of Onions, Turpentine, and Cow's Milk, drunk over four days, was prescribed to eliminate the spots — a preparation that might also have served as a topical application. When the uxedu-disease was complicated by the uha-disease [another uncertain Egyptian pathological category, possibly involving swelling or abscess], the scribe appeared uncertain in his approach. He first prescribed a salve of Sea-salt, Honey, and Cow's Bile; then, having doubts about the efficacy of Cow's Bile in this context, he revised the formula, substituting Cow's Brain for Cow's Bile. Still unsatisfied that the remedy would prove effective, he fell back — much as many a pressed modern practitioner might — upon a palliative. The familiar household remedy of Castor Oil was employed; however, rather than administering it internally in the usual manner (a spoonful taken overnight), he prescribed that the oil be rubbed on externally. He expressed this instruction in an indirect manner: ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT THE UXEDU — Oil expressed from the Seeds of the Castor-Oil bush: Smear it upon any person who has the uha-swelling.

segments.title_inferred: The 'Wood-like Uha-Disease' Salves and the Heart & Circulatory System in the Ebers Papyrus
segments.original_text: desperate when he came to deal with the 'wood-like uha-disease' occurring by itself. Salves were all that he could offer against it. They made up in picturesqueness what they most assuredly lacked in utility. An ointment of Hippopotamus Oil and Honey would perhaps be more sticky than picturesque. Another of Soot-from-the-bet'a-Pot with Honey and Sweet Myrrh would seem to be not only sticky but dirty. Another made of Ass's Head, Corn, and Oil, can only be called asinine, surely. A fourth, wherein Stone-from-Memphis was ground up with Seeds and Grain in the Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son was without a doubt gritty. It had to be persisted in for seven days but does not seem to have been very successful since it was followed successively by other salves which had to be rubbed in for eight days, nine days, and ten days, respectively. Finally there remained a salve calculated to arouse feelings of homicide in every poetic breast; a salve which sought to combine Seeds-from-the-Blossoms-of-the-Earth and Fresh Cream with the Hoof-of-an-Ass! CHAPTER XX THE HEART AND CIRCULATORY SYSTEM To the student the most interesting section of the Papyrus is undoubtedly the chapter dealing with the Heart, or rather with the vascular system. At first sight one is in danger of assuming that the knowledge of the Heart and of its action enjoyed by these Ancients was much greater than it was in reality. But as one reads on it is clearly established that they made the fatal mistake of regarding the Heart not as a pump but as a mere well. Perhaps had they made a closer examination of the Heart itself they would have discovered the existence of the all-important valves, when (it is not too much to believe) they would in a flash have grasped the real function of the organ. For they had arrived at all save that. The section well merits setting down here at length. Unfortunately it cannot be set forth in its entirety for much of it is untranslatable, and indeed a great part of it remains so.
segments.translation: The ancient physician was at a loss when confronted with the so-called 'wood-like uha-disease' [Egyptian: uha, possibly a stiffening or sclerotic condition] occurring in isolation. Salves were the only remedies he could prescribe against it. These preparations possessed a certain vivid character that thoroughly compensated for their evident lack of therapeutic utility. An ointment compounded of hippopotamus fat and honey would have been more adhesive than picturesque. A second preparation, consisting of soot collected from the 'bet'a-pot' [a type of Egyptian cooking or heating vessel] combined with honey and sweet myrrh [Commiphora sp.], would appear to have been not merely adhesive but unclean. A third, composed of an ass's head, grain [corn], and oil, can only be described as foolish. A fourth, in which 'Stone-from-Memphis' [likely a calcareous or alabaster mineral from the Memphis region] was ground together with seeds and grain in the milk of a woman who had given birth to a son, was undoubtedly gritty in texture. This last preparation was to be applied persistently for seven days; however, it does not appear to have been particularly successful, since it was followed in succession by further salves requiring application for eight days, nine days, and ten days respectively. Finally, there remained one salve apt to provoke murderous feelings in every poetically-inclined physician: a preparation combining seeds from the blossoms of the earth [unidentified plant material] and fresh cream with the hoof of an ass. CHAPTER XX: THE HEART AND CIRCULATORY SYSTEM. For the student, the most interesting section of the Papyrus is undoubtedly the chapter treating the Heart—or rather, the vascular system as a whole. At first reading, one risks the erroneous assumption that the knowledge of the Heart and its function possessed by these ancient Egyptians was considerably more advanced than it actually was. Upon closer reading, however, it becomes clear that they committed the fundamental error of conceiving the Heart not as a pump but merely as a reservoir or well. Had they examined the Heart itself more closely, they might have discovered the existence of its all-important valves, and one may reasonably suppose that, upon doing so, they would have immediately grasped the true function of the organ—for in every other respect they had approached a correct understanding. This section deserves to be transcribed here at length. Unfortunately, it cannot be presented in its entirety, as much of it remains untranslatable, and indeed a considerable portion of the text has not yet been rendered into any modern language.

segments.title_inferred: Diseases of the Heart and Circulatory System: Egyptian Medical Diagnostics

It falls down and approaches his diaphragm, while his Heart feels an aversion. When the mast-disease is in the Heart, behold there is a contraction of the Chest. His Heart has in its place Blood from the Anus which protrudes a little because of the disease. It is a Fever of the Heart. segments.translation: The Heart [vessels/channels] utterly deny him their service. When [a patient's] clothes weigh him down [i.e., he feels burdened by the weight of his garments], behold it is the dehert-disease [a named Egyptian pathological category, possibly related to cardiac or thoracic heaviness]. When his dehert-disease erupts, behold it is dangerous. (Note: passage is uncertain in the source.) When the Heart is sick, behold there is an Oppression of the Heart [angina-like symptom], or it is an Overflow of Blood arising in the Heart. [The blood] falls downward and approaches his diaphragm, while his Heart feels an aversion [i.e., revulsion or discomfort]. When the mast-disease [another named Egyptian condition] is in the Heart, behold there is a contraction of the Chest. His Heart has in its place Blood from the Anus which protrudes slightly because of the disease. It is a Fever of the Heart. If his Heart quietens somewhat under the disease, he will eat, but only fastidiously [i.e., with little appetite or selectiveness]. When the Heart consumes itself, behold it is an Accumulation of Blood in the Heart. When there is mast-disease of the Heart caused by the uxedu [a pathological substance or humor in Egyptian medicine, possibly putrefaction or a morbid fluid], behold his Heart is small within his Belly. The uxedu falls upon his Heart. He is aat-sick [afflicted with the aat-condition]. He is mast-sick [afflicted with the mast-condition]. When there is Weakness resulting from old age, behold it is the uxedu [acting] at his Heart. When there is a rising of the Heart, behold it lifts itself at his left breast. It lifts itself upon its fat [pericardial fat]. It flies forth from its place. Its layers of fat are on his Left Side so that they accumulate in his shoulder. When the dehert-disease of his Heart appears frequently, behold his Heart swims and sinks downwards and is not in its place. When his Heart is in its right place, the Fat of the Heart is on the left side. It neither rises upward nor falls downward. It remains soundly in its place. When his Heart trembles and there is much fat beneath his left breast, behold it is his Heart causing some degree of sinking because his disease is spreading. When his Abdomen palpitates, it is caused by a swelling therein. When the Mouth is like Fire and becomes fatigued and the Heart is weary, behold it is because a Fire forces itself toward his Heart. His Heart burns with Fire like a person upon whom [text continues].

When dryness befalls his Heart, behold it is the dryness of Fire that befalls it. When it is Fate that he shall go Up-on-High, behold it is his Heart which determines that he shall go Up-on-High.

The section concludes with various remedies for affections of the heart, some of which are mixed up with a little 'anatomy':

Man has Twelve Heart-vessels which spread out to all his Limbs. There are Two Vessels in the region of his Breast which cause inflammation in the anus. Use against this: Fresh Dates, Leaves-of-the-Castor-Oil-Plant, Fruit-of-the-Sycamore — mix into one in Water, strain, and let it be taken for four days. There are Two Vessels to his Thigh. When he suffers from his Thigh and both Thighs are a-tremble, say thou for it: 'This Vessel that runs to the region of his Thigh has conceived the disease.' Use against it: Fresh Milk, Wormwood, Natron — Cook into one and make the Patient drink for four days. There are Two Vessels to his Arm. When he suffers in his Arm and his fingers tremble, then thou sayest to him: 'They are swollen glands.' Prepare against it:

When the Heart is miserable and beside itself, it is the breath [exhalation] of the heb-wxer [a ritual Egyptian priestly title] Priest that causes it, acting through the hollow of his hand. It penetrates all the way down to the Rectum, in such a manner that the Heart comes forth and loses its way under the disease. When dryness befalls his Heart, it is the dryness of Fire that befalls it. When his Heart is afflicted and has tasted sadness, his Heart is closed in and darkness is within his body, due to anger which is consuming his Heart. When his Flesh is entirely withered, like the stilled Heart of a Person who has Found-the-Way [i.e., died], it is his Flesh which has become immovable beneath it, like the immovable flesh of a Person who has gone Into-the-Wide [i.e., into the beyond]. When it is Fate that he shall go Up-on-High [i.e., die], it is his Heart which determines that he shall go Up-on-High.

The section concludes with various remedies for affections of the heart, some of which are interspersed with rudimentary 'anatomy':

A man has Twelve Heart-vessels [metu] which spread out to all his Limbs. There are Two Vessels in the region of his Breast which cause inflammation in the anus. Use against this: Fresh Dates, Leaves-of-the-Castor-Oil-Plant [Ricinus communis], Fruit-of-the-Sycamore [Ficus sycomorus] — combine into one preparation in Water, strain, and let it be administered for four days. There are Two Vessels leading to his Thigh. When he suffers in his Thigh and both Thighs are trembling, pronounce over it: 'This Vessel that runs to the region of his Thigh has contracted the disease.' Use against it: Fresh Milk, Wormwood [Artemisia sp.], Natron [natural sodium carbonate/bicarbonate salt] — cook together into one preparation and have the Patient drink it for four days.
