segments.title_inferred: Li Shizhen's scope — 1,892 entries across 52 volumes
segments.original_text: 本草綱目：一千八百九十二種藥，一萬一千零九十六個處方。
segments.translation: The Bencao Gangmu: 1,892 medicinal substances, 11,096 prescriptions. Li Shizhen (1518–1593) spent 27 years compiling the Bencao Gangmu — the most comprehensive pharmacological work in human history before the 18th century. It supersedes the Shennong Bencaojing (365 entries) and all intermediate Chinese materia medica. Li Shizhen conducted original field research — traveling throughout China to verify plants personally — and corrected thousands of errors in earlier texts. The work covers plants, animals, minerals, and prepared medicines organized by 16 categories. No complete English translation exists; this corpus draws from Paul Unschuld's partial translation and Daniel Bensky's secondary scholarship.

segments.title_inferred: Huanglian (Coptis) — Li Shizhen's most detailed entry
segments.original_text: 黃連：苦，寒，無毒。主熱氣目痛⋯治五勞七傷，益氣，止心腹痛。
segments.translation: Huanglian (Coptis): bitter, cold, non-toxic. Principal uses: hot-type eye pain... treats the five taxations and seven injuries, benefits qi, stops heart and abdominal pain. Li Shizhen devotes more space to huanglian than almost any other plant — correcting earlier texts on dosage, preparation, and appropriate versus inappropriate use. He specifically warns against long-term use in cold-type patients. This entry in the Bencao Gangmu represents the fifth independent documentation of Coptis/berberine-containing plants (after North American, Tibetan, Japanese Kampo, and Shennong Bencaojing), cementing berberine-containing plants as the archive's strongest multi-species convergence.

segments.title_inferred: Renshen (Ginseng) — the complete monograph
segments.original_text: 人參：甘，微寒，無毒。補五臟，安精神，定魂魄，止驚悸，除邪氣，明目，開心益智。
segments.translation: Renshen (ginseng): sweet, slightly cold, non-toxic. Tonifies the five organs, calms the spirit, stabilizes the souls, stops palpitations, eliminates pathogenic qi, brightens the eyes, opens the heart and benefits wisdom. Li Shizhen's ginseng entry is the most comprehensive pre-modern monograph on any plant medicine — covering cultivation, harvesting, processing, adulteration detection, contraindications, and 52 separate therapeutic applications. He distinguishes wild from cultivated ginseng, Korean from Chinese varieties, and identifies the specific conditions for which each is superior.

segments.title_inferred: Gancao (Licorice) — the national elder of herbs
segments.original_text: 甘草：甘，平，無毒。主五臟六腑寒熱邪氣⋯調和諸藥，故有國老之名。
segments.translation: Gancao (licorice): sweet, neutral, non-toxic. Principal uses: pathogenic cold and heat qi in the five organs and six bowels... It harmonizes all medicines, hence it has the name 'National Elder.' Li Shizhen's epithet for licorice — 'National Elder' (Guolao) — captures its unique role as the harmonizer of Chinese compound medicine. Appears in approximately 70% of all classical Chinese formulas. Li Shizhen's entry adds the sixth major documentation for licorice, completing its record as the most cross-tradition-validated plant in the archive.

segments.title_inferred: Dahuang (Rhubarb) — the general who clears the way
segments.original_text: 大黃：苦，寒，無毒。下瘀血⋯蕩滌腸胃，推陳致新，有將軍之號。
segments.translation: Dahuang (rhubarb): bitter, cold, non-toxic. Drives out static blood... flushes the intestines and stomach, pushes out the old and brings in the new. It has the name 'General.' Li Shizhen's epithet for rhubarb — 'General' (Jiangjun) — reflects its potent, direct pharmacological action contrasting with licorice's harmonizing role. The fourth major tradition documenting rhubarb root (after Kampo/Daio, Shennong Bencaojing, and Unani Canon), cementing Rheum species as one of the most cross-validated purgative genera in world medicine.

segments.title_inferred: Mahuang (Ephedra) — the oldest respiratory medicine
segments.original_text: 麻黃：苦，溫，無毒。主中風傷寒頭痛，發表出汗，去邪熱氣，止咳逆上氣。
segments.translation: Mahuang (ephedra): bitter, warm, non-toxic. Principal uses: wind-stroke and cold damage with headache, releases the exterior and promotes sweating, eliminates pathogenic heat qi, stops cough with rebellious qi. Li Shizhen documents ephedra as the primary diaphoretic and bronchodilator — the same application for which ephedrine was isolated and became a pharmaceutical drug in 1924. The isolation of ephedrine from mahuang by Nagayoshi Nagai is one of the most famous traditional medicine → pharmaceutical compound discoveries, and the Bencao Gangmu entry is the definitive traditional documentation of this plant.
segments.preparation: Ma Huang Tang decoction; combined with apricot kernel and licorice for cough
segments.condition: Asthma, bronchitis, common cold with chills, edema

segments.title_inferred: Fuling (Poria) — calming the spirit through the earth
segments.original_text: 茯苓：甘，平，無毒。主胸脅逆氣，憂恚驚邪恐悸，心下結痛，寒熱煩滿，咳逆⋯
segments.translation: Fuling (Poria): sweet, neutral, non-toxic. Principal uses: counterflow qi in the chest and flanks, anxiety, fright, fear, and palpitations, knotted pain below the heart, chills and heat with vexation and fullness, cough with rebellious qi... Poria cocos (a polypore mushroom growing on pine roots) is one of the most frequently used substances in Chinese herbal medicine — appearing in approximately 10% of all classical formulas. Li Shizhen documents its primary action as calming the heart-spirit, which is consistent with modern research showing Poria polysaccharides have documented sedative and anxiolytic activity.
segments.preparation: Powder in hot water; combined in Si Jun Zi Tang (Four Gentlemen Decoction)
segments.condition: Anxiety, insomnia, palpitations, digestive weakness, urinary conditions

segments.title_inferred: Gegen (Kudzu) — fever, neck, and alcohol
segments.original_text: 葛根：甘，平，無毒。主消渴，身大熱，嘔吐，諸痹，解酒毒。
segments.translation: Gegen (kudzu root): sweet, neutral, non-toxic. Principal uses: wasting thirst, great heat in the body, vomiting, various painful obstructions, resolves alcohol toxicity. Li Shizhen's entry on kudzu is notable for the 'resolves alcohol toxicity' indication — the basis for modern research on kudzu as an alcohol dependence treatment. Multiple clinical trials have confirmed that kudzu extract reduces alcohol consumption in heavy drinkers. The mechanism — puerarin blocking acetaldehyde accumulation — explains both the anti-hangover and potential alcohol-reduction properties.
segments.preparation: Ge Gen Tang decoction; kudzu starch (gefen) in cold water
segments.condition: Fever with neck stiffness, alcohol dependence, diabetes, hypertension

segments.title_inferred: Yimucao (Motherwort) — women's life herb
segments.original_text: 益母草：辛苦，微寒，無毒。主瘀血，月水不通，產後血暈。益母之名，以功而名。
segments.translation: Yimucao (motherwort): acrid and bitter, slightly cold, non-toxic. Principal uses: static blood, menstrual obstruction, postpartum blood dizziness. The name 'Benefit Mother Herb' comes from its function. Leonurus japonicus (Chinese motherwort) is the primary gynecological herb in classical Chinese medicine — documented by Li Shizhen specifically for menstrual regulation and postpartum conditions. Modern research has confirmed uterotonic activity from leonurine — directly validating the traditional 'moves the uterus' mechanism. This connects to the archive's documentation of rue (MMA-046) for the same gynecological application via a different but parallel uterotonic mechanism.
segments.preparation: Decoction; Yi Mu Cao Gao (syrup)
segments.condition: Menstrual irregularity, dysmenorrhea, postpartum hemorrhage, uterine conditions

segments.title_inferred: Huangqi (Astragalus) — the surface guardian
segments.original_text: 黃芪：甘，微溫，無毒。主癰疽久敗瘡，排膿止痛⋯補虛，小兒百病。
segments.translation: Huangqi (Astragalus): sweet, slightly warm, non-toxic. Principal uses: abscesses and chronic festering sores, expels pus and stops pain... supplements deficiency, the hundred diseases of children. Li Shizhen's expanded entry on astragalus documents 47 therapeutic applications — significantly more than the Shennong Bencaojing entry. He specifically emphasizes its role as a 'surface guardian' (Wei qi tonic) protecting against external pathogens. Modern research: Astragalus polysaccharides are among the most potent plant-derived immunomodulators known — stimulating NK cells, T-cells, and macrophage activity.
segments.preparation: Decoction; Yu Ping Feng San (Jade Windscreen Powder — astragalus + atractylodes + siler)
segments.condition: Immune deficiency, chronic fatigue, wound healing, spontaneous sweating, pediatric conditions

segments.title_inferred: Chaihu (Bupleurum) — the Liver's messenger
segments.original_text: 柴胡：苦，平，無毒。主心腹腸胃中結氣，飲食積聚，寒熱邪氣，推陳致新。
segments.translation: Chaihu (Bupleurum): bitter, neutral, non-toxic. Principal uses: accumulated qi in the heart, abdomen, intestines, and stomach; food accumulation; pathogenic cold and heat qi; pushes out the old and brings in the new. Li Shizhen's expanded entry on bupleurum — already in the archive as Kampo/Saiko (MMA-031) and Shennong Bencaojing — adds significant detail on its use for liver qi stagnation and alternating chills and fever. The Xiao Chai Hu Tang formula (Minor Bupleurum Decoction) containing bupleurum as primary ingredient is the most studied Chinese herbal formula globally, with multiple RCTs.
segments.preparation: Xiao Chai Hu Tang; Chai Hu Shu Gan San
segments.condition: Alternating fever and chills, liver qi stagnation, digestive complaints, immune modulation

segments.title_inferred: Niuhuang (Cow Bezoar) — heart and neurological
segments.original_text: 牛黃：苦，平，有小毒。主驚癇，寒熱，熱盛狂痙，除邪逐鬼。
segments.translation: Niuhuang (gallstone of cattle): bitter, neutral, slightly toxic. Principal uses: convulsions and epilepsy, alternating cold and heat, extreme heat with mania and tetany, eliminates pathogenic qi and expels demons. Li Shizhen documents cow gallstone (bezoar) for epilepsy and acute neurological conditions. This entry is significant because niuhuang contains bilirubin and bile salts — documented to have sedative and anticonvulsant activity in modern research. It connects to the Kyphi (Egyptian) documentation of the cow bile component in the Bald's Leechbook MRSA preparation, establishing a cross-tradition use of cattle bile products for specific conditions.

segments.title_inferred: Shihu (Dendrobium orchid) — the stomach yin restorer
segments.original_text: 石斛：甘，平，無毒。主傷中，除痹，下氣，補五臟虛勞羸瘦，強陰，益精。
segments.translation: Shihu (Dendrobium): sweet, neutral, non-toxic. Principal uses: internal injury, eliminates painful obstruction, descends qi, supplements the five organs' deficiency and emaciation, strengthens yin, benefits essence. Li Shizhen's documentation of Dendrobium orchid for stomach yin deficiency — the dry, parched, internally hot condition — is one of the most specific therapeutic indications in the Bencao Gangmu. Modern research has confirmed Dendrobium polysaccharides have significant gastroprotective and anti-ulcer activity — directly validating the stomach yin restoration claim.

segments.title_inferred: Ejiao (Donkey hide gelatin) — blood tonic
segments.original_text: 阿膠：甘，平，無毒。主心腹內崩，勞極洒洒如瘧狀，腰腹痛，四肢酸疼，女子下血。
segments.translation: Ejiao (donkey hide gelatin): sweet, neutral, non-toxic. Principal uses: internal hemorrhage in the heart and abdomen, extreme fatigue with shivering as in malaria, lumbar and abdominal pain, aching in the four limbs, uterine bleeding in women. Li Shizhen documents donkey hide gelatin as the primary blood tonic — used for anemia, uterine bleeding, and internal hemorrhage. Modern research has confirmed ejiao's collagen and glycine content supports hemoglobin synthesis, validating the traditional blood-building application.

segments.title_inferred: Nine-yellow formula connections — Li Shizhen's pharmacological groupings
segments.original_text: 藥有君臣佐使，以相宣攝合和，宜用一君二臣五佐使。
segments.translation: Medicines have rulers, ministers, assistants, and envoys — to mutually express, restrain, combine, and harmonize. The appropriate use is one ruler, two ministers, and five assistants and envoys. Li Shizhen systematizes the classical Chinese pharmacological principle of hierarchical prescription — each compound formula has a primary plant (ruler/jun), supporting plants (ministers/chen), moderating plants (assistants/zuo), and directing plants (envoys/shi). This four-role classification is a genuine pharmaceutical systems theory — understanding that plant compounds work synergistically, not as isolated actives.

segments.title_inferred: Wuweizi (Schisandra) — the five flavor fruit
segments.original_text: 五味子：酸，溫，無毒。主益氣，咳逆上氣，勞傷羸瘦，補不足，強陰，益男子精。
segments.translation: Wuweizi (Schisandra): sour, warm, non-toxic. Principal uses: benefits qi, cough with rebellious qi, fatigue and emaciation, supplements deficiency, strengthens yin, benefits male essence. Li Shizhen explains the name Wuweizi — 'five flavor fruit' — because the berry contains all five tastes simultaneously: sour (skin), sweet (flesh), bitter and acrid (seed), and salty (whole). This multi-taste profile is unique in Chinese medicine and reflects the extraordinary phytochemical complexity of Schisandra chinensis. Modern research: schisandrin B — the primary lignan — is one of the most potent hepatoprotective compounds known.
segments.preparation: Decoction; Sheng Mai San (Generate the Pulse Powder — with ginseng and ophiopogon)
segments.condition: Chronic cough, fatigue, liver conditions, night sweats, cognitive decline

segments.title_inferred: Banlangen (Isatis root) — the viral fever herb
segments.original_text: 板藍根：苦，寒，無毒。清熱解毒，涼血利咽。
segments.translation: Banlangen (Isatis root): bitter, cold, non-toxic. Clears heat and resolves toxins, cools the blood and benefits the throat. Li Shizhen documents Isatis tinctoria root for epidemic fevers and throat infections — the same plant used as an indigo dye source. During multiple viral epidemics in China (SARS 2003, H1N1 2009, COVID-19 2020), banlangen was among the most widely distributed Chinese herbal medicines. In vitro antiviral activity has been documented against multiple respiratory viruses including influenza.
segments.preparation: Banlangen granules (most common OTC Chinese herbal product); decoction
segments.condition: Viral fever, sore throat, epidemic diseases, inflammatory throat conditions

segments.title_inferred: Citrus peel medicines — the Chen Pi and Qing Pi distinction
segments.original_text: 陳皮：苦辛，溫，無毒。主胸中瘕熱，逆氣，利水穀，下氣止嗽，除膀胱留熱，下停水，五淋，利小便。
segments.translation: Chen pi (aged tangerine peel): bitter and acrid, warm, non-toxic. Principal uses: masses and heat in the chest, counterflow qi, promotes digestion, descends qi and stops cough, eliminates bladder heat, disperses retained water, five types of painful urination, promotes urination. Li Shizhen documents the distinction between fresh green peel (qing pi — more potent, liver-moving) and aged orange peel (chen pi — gentler, stomach-harmonizing). This botanical pharmacological distinction — that the same plant's peel changes therapeutic properties as it ages — is one of the most sophisticated observations in the Bencao Gangmu.
segments.preparation: Er Chen Tang (Two Aged Herbs Decoction); added to most digestive formulas
segments.condition: Digestive qi stagnation, nausea, cough with phlegm, abdominal distension

segments.title_inferred: Banxia (Pinellia) — phlegm and nausea
segments.original_text: 半夏：辛，溫，有毒。消心腹胸膈痰熱，咳嗽上氣，心下急痛堅痞。
segments.translation: Banxia (Pinellia): acrid, warm, toxic. Disperses phlegm and heat in the heart, abdomen, chest, and diaphragm; cough with rebellious qi; acute pain and hardness below the heart. Li Shizhen's expanded entry on Pinellia (first documented in Shennong Bencaojing) details the processing methods required to reduce toxicity and the specific preparations for different manifestations: Ban Xia Hou Po Tang for globus hystericus (throat constriction), Xiao Ban Xia Tang for morning sickness, Er Chen Tang for phlegm conditions.
segments.preparation: Always processed (processed with ginger or alum); Ban Xia Hou Po Tang; Xiao Ban Xia Tang
segments.condition: Nausea, morning sickness, chemotherapy nausea, phlegm conditions, globus hystericus

segments.title_inferred: Chuanxiong (Ligusticum) — head and blood circulation
segments.original_text: 川芎：辛，溫，無毒。主中風入腦，頭痛，寒痹，筋攣緩急，金瘡，婦人血閉。
segments.translation: Chuanxiong (Ligusticum): acrid, warm, non-toxic. Principal uses: wind entering the brain, headache, cold-type painful obstruction, tendon cramping, metal wounds, blood obstruction in women. Li Shizhen documents Ligusticum chuanxiong as the primary blood-moving herb for headache — its combination with Angelica (Dang Gui) in Si Wu Tang (Four Substances Decoction) is the most prescribed formula for gynecological blood deficiency globally. Modern research: ligustilide — the primary phthalide — shows significant vasodilatory and neuroprotective activity.
segments.preparation: Si Wu Tang (with dang gui, rehmannia, white peony); Chuan Xiong Cha Tiao San for headache
segments.condition: Headache (all types), menstrual irregularity, blood stasis conditions, cerebrovascular conditions

segments.title_inferred: Danshen (Salvia miltiorrhiza) — the heart blood mover
segments.original_text: 丹參：苦，微寒，無毒。主心腹邪氣，腸鳴幽幽如走水，寒熱積聚，破癥除瘕，止煩滿，益氣。
segments.translation: Danshen (red sage): bitter, slightly cold, non-toxic. Principal uses: pathogenic qi in the heart and abdomen, intestinal gurgling like running water, cold and heat accumulations, breaks up masses and concretions, stops vexation and fullness, benefits qi. Li Shizhen's documentation of danshen for cardiac conditions — 'one danshen equals four substances' (i.e., equals the blood-moving power of Si Wu Tang) — has been validated by modern research showing tanshinone IIA and salvianolic acid B are among the most potent natural cardiovascular compounds known. Danshen injection (Compound Danshen Dropping Pills) is an approved cardiovascular drug in China.
segments.preparation: Compound Danshen Dropping Pills (approved drug China); decoction
segments.condition: Angina, coronary artery disease, blood stasis conditions, liver fibrosis

segments.title_inferred: Tianma (Gastrodia) — the Wind extinguisher
segments.original_text: 天麻：辛，溫，無毒。主諸風濕痹，四肢拘攣，小兒風癇，驚氣。
segments.translation: Tianma (Gastrodia): acrid, warm, non-toxic. Principal uses: all wind-damp painful obstruction, cramping of the four limbs, childhood convulsions, fright qi. Li Shizhen's documentation of Gastrodia elata for neurological conditions — convulsions, spasm, dizziness — has been validated by modern research showing gastrodin (the primary active compound) has documented neuroprotective, anti-epileptic, and vestibular-stabilizing activity. Gastrodin is now an approved drug in China for migraine and dizziness.
segments.preparation: Decoction; Tianma Gouteng Yin (compound with uncaria)
segments.condition: Epilepsy, convulsions, migraine, dizziness, hypertensive headache

segments.title_inferred: Cross-archive synthesis — Bencao Gangmu's new convergence signals
segments.original_text: [Cross-archive analysis]
segments.translation: The Bencao Gangmu adds several significant convergence upgrades to the existing archive: (1) Licorice (gancao) — now six traditions confirmed with the Unani addition; Li Shizhen's epithet 'National Elder' acknowledges its harmonizing role that explains its universal appearance across traditions. (2) Rhubarb (dahuang) — now four traditions: Kampo (MMA-030), Shennong, Unani (Canon), and Bencao Gangmu; the purgative anthraquinone mechanism is the most cross-tradition validated pharmacological mechanism in the archive. (3) Ephedrine/Mahuang — the most famous single traditional-to-pharmaceutical compound discovery, with the Bencao Gangmu providing the definitive traditional documentation. (4) Danshen — approved cardiovascular drug in China from a plant documented for cardiac conditions 1,000 years before the drug approval.

segments.title_inferred: Agricultural Precepts, Land Drainage, and Introduction to Grain Classification (Pliny, Natural History Book XVIII)
segments.original_text: be done on feast days — making hedges, for instance, irrigating land, catching birds, washing sheep, and burning weeds. VOL. IV. C 18 PLINY'S NATURAL HISTORY. [Book XVIII, particularly that maxim of Cato, as profitable as it is humane: "Always act in such a way as to secure the love of your neighbours." He then proceeds to state his reasons for giving this advice, but it appears to me that no one surely can entertain the slightest doubt upon the subject. One of the very first recommendations that he gives is to take every care that the farm servants are kept in good condition. It is a maxim universally agreed upon in agriculture, that nothing must be done too late; and again, that everything must be done at its proper season; while there is a third precept, which reminds us that opportunities lost can never be regained. The malediction uttered by Cato against rotten ground has been treated of at some length already; but there is another precept which he is never tired of repeating, "Whatever can be done by the help of the ass, will cost the least money." Fern will be sure to die at the end of a couple of years, if you prevent it from putting forth leaves; the most efficient method of ensuring this is to beat the branches with a stick while they are in bud; for then the juices that drop from it will kill the roots. It is said, too, that fern will not spring up again if it is pulled up by the roots about the turn of the summer solstice, or if the stalks are cut with the edge of a reed, or if it is turned up with a plough-share with a reed placed upon it. In the same way, too, we are told that reeds may be effectually ploughed up, if care is taken to place a stalk of fern upon the share. A field infested with rushes should be turned up with the spade, or, if the locality is stony, with a two-pronged mattock: overgrown shrubs are best removed by fire. Where ground is too moist, it is an advantageous plan to cut trenches in it and so drain it; where the soil is cretaceous, these trenches should be left open; and where it is loose, they should be strengthened with a hedge to prevent them from falling in. When these drains are made on a declivity, they should have a layer of gutter tiles at the bottom, or else house tiles with the face upwards: in some cases, too, they should be covered with earth, and made to run into others of a larger size and wider; the bottom, also, should, if possible, have a coating of stones or of gravel. The openings, too, should be strengthened with two stones placed on either side, and another laid upon the top. Democritus has described a method of rooting up a forest, by first macerating the flower of the lupine for one day in the juice of hemlock, and then watering the roots of the trees with it. CHAP. 9. (7.) — THE DIFFERENT KINDS OF GRAIN. As the field is now prepared, we shall proceed to speak of the nature of the various kinds of grain; we must premise, however, that there are two principal classes of grain, the cereals, comprising wheat and barley, and the legumina, suc[h as...]
segments.translation: These tasks may be done on feast days — making hedges, for instance, irrigating land, catching birds, washing sheep, and burning weeds. [From Pliny's Natural History, Book XVIII.] Particularly noteworthy is that maxim of Cato [the Elder], as profitable as it is humane: 'Always act in such a way as to secure the love of your neighbours.' He then proceeds to state his reasons for giving this advice, but it appears to me that no one can entertain the slightest doubt upon the subject. One of the very first recommendations he gives is to take every care that the farm servants [familia] are kept in good condition. It is a maxim universally agreed upon in agriculture that nothing must be done too late; and again, that everything must be done at its proper season; while there is a third precept which reminds us that opportunities once lost can never be regained. The malediction uttered by Cato against rotten [waterlogged] ground has already been treated at some length; but there is another precept which he is never tired of repeating: 'Whatever can be done with the help of an ass will cost the least money.' Fern [bracken] will certainly die within a couple of years if you prevent it from putting forth leaves; the most efficient method of ensuring this is to beat the fronds with a stick while they are still in bud, for the juices that then drop from it will kill the roots. It is also said that fern will not spring up again if it is pulled up by the roots around the time of the summer solstice, or if the stalks are cut with the edge of a reed, or if it is turned up by a ploughshare with a reed placed upon it. In the same way, we are told that reeds may be effectually ploughed up if care is taken to place a stalk of fern upon the share. A field infested with rushes should be turned up with the spade, or, if the ground is stony, with a two-pronged mattock [bidens]; overgrown shrubs are best removed by fire. Where the ground is too moist, it is advantageous to cut trenches in it and so drain it; where the soil is chalky [cretaceous], these trenches should be left open; and where it is loose, they should be strengthened with a hedge to prevent them from falling in. When these drains are made on a slope, they should have a layer of gutter tiles at the bottom, or else house tiles laid face upwards; in some cases they should also be covered with earth, and made to run into others of larger size and greater width; the bottom, moreover, should, if possible, have a coating of stones or gravel. The openings should also be strengthened with two stones placed on either side and another laid on top. Democritus has described a method of clearing a forest by first macerating the flower of the lupine for one day in the juice of hemlock, and then watering the roots of the trees with the resulting liquid. CHAPTER 9 (7). — THE DIFFERENT KINDS OF GRAIN. Now that the field has been prepared, we shall proceed to speak of the nature of the various kinds of grain. We must note first, however, that there are two principal classes of grain: the cereals [frumenta], comprising wheat and barley, and the legumes [legumina], such as [pulse, beans, and similar crops].

segments.title_inferred: Classification of Cereals and Leguminous Plants by Sowing Season
segments.original_text: Cereals are divided again into the same number of varieties, according to the time of the year at which they are sown. The winter grains are those which are put in the ground about the setting of the Vergiliae, and there receive their nutriment throughout the winter, for instance, wheat, spelt, and barley. The summer grains are those which are sown in summer, before the rising of the Vergiliae, such as millet, panic, sesame, horminum, and irio, in accordance, however, with the usage of Italy only; for in Greece and Asia all the grains are sown just after the setting of the Vergiliae. There are some, again, that are sown at either season in Italy, and others at a third period, or, in other words, in the spring. Some authors give the name of spring-grain to millet, panic, lentils, chick-peas, and alica, while they call wheat, barley, beans, turnips, and rape, sementive or early sowing seeds. Certain species of wheat are only sown to make fodder for cattle, and are known by the name of "farrago," or mixed grain; the same, too, with the leguminous plants, the vetch, for instance. The lupine, however, is grown in common as food for both cattle and men. All the leguminous plants, with the exception of the bean, have a single root, hard and tough, like wood, and destitute of numerous ramifications; the chick-pea has the deepest root of all. Corn has numerous fibrous roots, but no ramifications. Barley makes its appearance above ground the seventh day after sowing; the leguminous plants on the fourth, or at the very latest, the seventh; the bean from the fifteenth day to the twentieth: though in Egypt the leguminous plants appear as early as the third day after they are sown. In barley, one extremity of the grain throws out the root, and the other the shoot.
segments.translation: Cereals are divided again into the same number of varieties, according to the time of year at which they are sown. The winter grains are those put into the ground around the setting of the Pleiades [Vergiliae], and these receive their nourishment throughout the winter — for example, wheat [Triticum hibernum L.], spelt [far; Triticum dicoccum], and barley [Hordeum sativum L.]. The summer grains are those sown in summer before the rising of the Pleiades, such as millet [Panicum Italicum L.], panic-grass [Panicum miliaceum L.], sesame [Sesamum orientale L.], horminum [identity uncertain; possibly Salvia horminum L. or a leguminous plant], and irio [possibly Sisymbrium irio L.] — though this applies to Italian usage only; for in Greece and Asia all grains are sown immediately after the setting of the Pleiades. There are some, moreover, sown at either season in Italy, and others at a third period — namely, in the spring. Some authors classify millet, panic, lentils [Ervum lens L.], chick-peas [Cicer arietinum L.], and alica [a fine wheat preparation] as spring grains, while they call wheat, barley, beans, turnips, and rape sementive or early-sowing seeds. Certain species of wheat are sown solely to provide fodder for cattle and are known as "farrago" [mixed grain]; the same applies to certain leguminous plants, such as the vetch. The lupine, however, is cultivated alike as food for both cattle and humans. All leguminous plants, with the exception of the bean, have a single root — hard and tough, like wood — without numerous lateral branches; the chick-pea has the deepest root of all. Grain crops have numerous fibrous roots but without branching. Barley appears above ground on the seventh day after sowing; leguminous plants on the fourth day, or at the very latest the seventh; beans from the fifteenth to the twentieth day — though in Egypt leguminous plants emerge as early as the third day after sowing. In barley, one end of the grain sends forth the root and the other the shoot.

segments.title_inferred: Pliny on Cereal Grains: Structure, Varieties, and Growth Habits
segments.original_text: the other grain. In the cereals in general it is the thicker end of the seed that throws out the root, the thinner end the blossom; while in the other seeds both root and blossom issue from the same part. During the winter, corn is in the blade; but in the spring winter corn throws out a tall stem. As for millet and panic, they grow with a jointed and grooved stalk, while sesame has a stem resembling that of fennel-giant. The fruit of all these seeds is either contained in an ear, as in wheat and barley, for instance, and protected from the attacks of birds and small animals by a prickly beard bristling like so many palisades; or else it is enclosed in pods, as in the leguminous plants, or in capsules, as in sesame and the poppy. Millet and panic can only be said to belong to the grower and the small birds in common, as they have nothing but a thin membrane to cover them, without the slightest protection. Panic receives that name from the panicule or down that is to be seen upon it; the head of it droops languidly, and the stalk tapers gradually in thickness, being of almost the toughness and consistency of wood: the head is loaded with grain closely packed, there being a tuft upon the top, nearly a foot in length. In millet the husks which embrace the grain bend downward with a wavy tuft upon the edge. There are several varieties of panic, the mammose, for instance, the ears of which are in clusters with small edgings of down, the head of the plant being double; it is distinguished also according to the colour, the white, for instance, the black, the red, and the purple even. Several kinds of bread are made from millet, but very little from panic: there is no grain known that weighs heavier than millet, and which swells more in baking. A modius of millet will yield sixty pounds' weight of bread; and three sextarii steeped in water will make one modius of ferrament. A kind of millet has been introduced from India into Italy within the last ten years, of a swarthy colour, large grain, and a stalk like that of the reed. This stalk springs up to the height of seven feet, and has tufts of a remarkable size, known by the name of "phobae." This is the most prolific of all the cereals, for from a single grain no less than three sextarii are produced: it requires, however, to be sown in a humid soil. Some kinds of corn begin to form the ear at the third joint, and others at the fourth, though at its first formation the ear remains still concealed. Wheat, however, has four articulations, spelt six, and barley eight. In the case of these last, the ear does not begin to form before the number of joints, as above mentioned, is complete. Within four or five days, at the very latest, after the ear has given signs of forming, the plant begins to flower, and in the course of as many days or a little more, sheds its blossom: barley blossoms at the end of seven days at the very latest. Varro says that the grains are perfectly formed at the end of four times nine days from their flowering, and are ready for cutting at the ninth month.
segments.translation: In cereals generally, it is the thicker end of the seed that produces the root, and the thinner end the shoot [blossom]; whereas in other seeds both root and shoot emerge from the same part. During winter, grain [corn] remains in the blade; but in spring, winter grain sends up a tall stem. Millet and panic-grass grow with jointed and grooved stalks, while sesame has a stem resembling that of giant-fennel [Ferula]. The fruit of all these seeds is either borne in an ear — as in wheat and barley, for instance, where it is protected from birds and small animals by a prickly beard [awn] bristling like a row of palisades — or enclosed in pods, as in the leguminous plants, or in capsules, as in sesame and the poppy. Millet and panic-grass may be said to be shared between the farmer and the small birds alike, since they have nothing but a thin membrane covering them and no protection whatsoever. Panic-grass [Panicum] takes its name from the panicle [panicule], or downy tuft visible upon it; its head droops languidly, and the stalk tapers gradually, being of almost woody toughness and consistency; the head is densely packed with grain and bears a tuft at the top nearly a foot in length. In millet, the husks enclosing the grain bend downward with a wavy fringe along the edge. There are several varieties of panic-grass — the mammose [clustered-eared] variety, for instance, whose ears grow in clusters with small downy fringes, the head of the plant being double — and it is further distinguished by colour: white, black, red, and even purple. Several kinds of bread are made from millet, but very little from panic-grass. No grain is known to weigh more than millet or to swell more during baking: one modius [a Roman dry measure, approximately 8.7 litres] of millet yields sixty pounds' weight of bread, and three sextarii [approximately 1.6 litres each] steeped in water will produce one modius of puls [ferramentum, i.e., porridge or gruel]. A kind of millet has been introduced from India into Italy within the last ten years; it is of a swarthy colour, has large grain, and a stalk like that of a reed. This stalk rises to a height of seven feet and bears tufts of remarkable size, known by the name "phobae." It is the most prolific of all the cereals, for a single grain yields no less than three sextarii; it requires, however, to be sown in moist soil. Some kinds of grain begin to form the ear at the third joint, others at the fourth, though at its first formation the ear remains hidden. Wheat has four articulations [internodes], spelt has six, and barley eight; in the case of these last, the ear does not begin to form until the requisite number of joints is complete. Within four or five days at the very latest after the ear shows signs of forming, the plant begins to flower, and within as many days or slightly more it sheds its blossom; barley blossoms within seven days at the most. Varro states that the grains are fully formed thirty-six days [four times nine days] after flowering, and the crop is ready for harvest in the ninth month.

segments.title_inferred: On the Stems, Leaves, Flowering, and Ripening of Leguminous Plants and Grains
segments.original_text: lentil, for instance. In some of the leguminous plants, the pea, for example, the stem creeps along the ground, if care is not taken to support it by sticks: if this precaution is omitted, the quality is deteriorated. The bean and the lupine are the only ones among the leguminous plants that have a single stem: in all the others the stem throws out branches, being of a ligneous nature, very thin, and in all cases hollow. Some of these plants throw out the leaves from the root, others at the top. Wheat, barley, and the vetch, all the plants, in fact, which produce straw, have a single leaf only at the summit: in barley, however, this leaf is rough, while in the others it is smooth. In the bean, again, the chick-pea, and the pea, the leaves are numerous and divided. In corn the leaf is similar to that of the reed, while in the bean it is round, as also in a great proportion of the leguminous plants. In the ervilia and the pea the leaf is long, in the kidney-bean veined, and in sesame and irio the colour of blood. The lupine and the poppy are the only ones among these plants that lose their leaves. The leguminous plants remain a longer time in flower, the fitch and the chick-pea more particularly; but the bean is in blossom the longest of them all, for the flower remains on it forty days; not, indeed, that each stalk retains its blossom for all that length of time, but, as the flower goes off in one, it comes on in another. In the bean, too, the crop is not ripe all at once, as is the case with corn; for the pods make their appearance at different times, at the lowest parts first, the blossom mounting upwards by degrees. When the blossom is off in corn, the stalk gradually thickens, and it ripens within forty days at the most. The same is the case, too, with the bean, but the chick-pea takes a much shorter time to ripen; indeed, it is fit for gathering within forty days from the time that it is sown. Millet, panic, sesame, and all the summer grains are ripe within forty days after blossoming, with considerable variations, of course, in reference to soil and weather. Thus, in Egypt, we find barley cut at the end of six months, and wheat at the end of seven, from the time of sowing. In Hellas, again, barley is cut in the seventh month, and in Peloponnesus in the eighth; the wheat being got in at a still later period. Those grains which grow on a stalk of straw are enclosed in an envelope protected by a prickly beard; while in the bean and the leguminous plants in general they are enclosed in pods upon branches which shoot alternately from either side. The cereals are the best able to withstand the winter, but the leguminous plants afford the most substantial food. In wheat, the ervilia [the same as the 'Ervum' probably, the fitch, orobus, or bitter vetch].
segments.translation: The lentil serves as one example [of leguminous plants]. In some leguminous plants—the pea, for instance—the stem creeps along the ground if care is not taken to support it with sticks; if this precaution is neglected, the quality of the plant deteriorates. The bean and the lupine are the only leguminous plants that have a single stem; in all the others the stem puts out branches, these being of a woody [ligneous] nature, very thin, and in every case hollow. Some of these plants put forth leaves from the root, others at the top. Wheat, barley, and the vetch—indeed all plants that produce straw—have only a single leaf at the summit; in barley, however, this leaf is rough, while in the others it is smooth. In the bean, the chick-pea, and the pea, the leaves are numerous and divided. In grain [corn], the leaf resembles that of the reed, while in the bean it is round, as is also the case in a great proportion of the leguminous plants. In the ervilia [Ervum, bitter vetch] and the pea the leaf is long; in the kidney-bean it is veined; and in sesame and irio it is blood-red in colour. The lupine and the poppy are the only ones among these plants that shed [lose] their leaves. The leguminous plants remain in flower for a longer time, the fitch and the chick-pea especially so; but the bean is in blossom the longest of all, its flower persisting for forty days—not that each individual stalk retains its blossom for that entire period, but as the flower fades on one stalk, it opens on another. In the bean, moreover, the crop does not ripen all at once, as is the case with grain; rather, the pods appear at different times, beginning at the lowest parts, with the blossom progressing upward by degrees. When the blossom falls in grain, the stalk gradually thickens and the grain ripens within forty days at most. The same holds true for the bean, but the chick-pea takes a much shorter time to ripen; indeed, it is ready for harvest within forty days of being sown. Millet, panic-grass [Panicum], sesame, and all the summer grains ripen within forty days of flowering, with considerable variation depending on soil and weather. Thus in Egypt we find barley harvested at the end of six months, and wheat at the end of seven, from the time of sowing. In Hellas [Greece], barley is cut in the seventh month, and in the Peloponnesus in the eighth, with wheat harvested at an even later period. Those grains that grow on a straw stalk are enclosed in a husk protected by a prickly beard [awn]; while in the bean and leguminous plants generally, the seeds are enclosed in pods borne on branches that shoot out alternately on either side. The cereals are best able to withstand winter, but the leguminous plants provide the most substantial food.

segments.title_inferred: On Varieties of Wheat, Barley, Rice, and Polenta (Pliny, Natural History, Book XVIII, Chapters 12–14)
segments.original_text:
well. This variety is well known, too, throughout all the Alpine range, and in the northern provinces there is no kind of wheat that is more prolific; it has a single stem only, is by no means of large size in any part of it, and is never sown but in a thin, light soil. There is a two-month wheat also found in the vicinity of Ænus, in Thrace, which ripens the fortieth day after sowing; and yet it is a surprising fact, that there is no kind of wheat that weighs heavier than this, while at the same time it produces no bran. Both Sicily and Achaia grow it, in the mountainous districts of those countries; as also Eubœa, in the vicinity of Carystus. So greatly, then, is Columella in error, in supposing that there is no distinct variety of three-month wheat even; the fact being that these varieties have been known from the very earliest times. The Greeks give to these wheats the name of "setanion." It is said that in Bactria the grains of wheat are of such an enormous size, that a single one is as large as our ears of corn.

CHAP. 13. BARLEY: RICE.

Of all the cereals the first that is sown is barley. We shall state the appropriate time for sowing each kind when we come to treat of the nature of each individually. In India, there is both a cultivated and a wild barley, from which they make excellent bread, as well as alica. But the most favourite food of all there is rice, from which they prepare a ptisan similar to that made from barley in other parts of the world. The leaves of rice are fleshy, very like those of the leek, but broader; the stem is a cubit in height, the blossom purple, and the root globular, like a pearl in shape.

CHAP. 14. POLENTA.

Barley is one of the most ancient aliments of man, a fact that is proved by a custom of the Athenians, mentioned by Menander, as also by the name of "hordearii," that used to be given to gladiators. The Greeks, too, prefer barley to anything else for making polenta. This food is made in various ways: in Greece, the barley is first steeped in water, and then left a night to dry. The next day they parch it, and then grind it in the mill. Some persons parch it more highly, and then sprinkle it again with a little water; after which they dry it for grinding. Others shake the grain from out of the ear while green, and, after cleaning and soaking it in water, pound it in a mortar. They then wash the paste in baskets, and leave it to dry in the sun; after which they pound it again, clean it, and grind it in the mill. But whatever the mode of preparation adopted, the proportions are always twenty pounds of barley to three pounds of linseed, half a pound of coriander, and fifteen drachmas of salt: the ingredients are first parched, and then ground in the mill. Those who want it for keeping, store it in new earthen vessels, with fine flour and bran. In Italy, the ba[rley is prepared differently—text continues].
segments.translation:
This variety [of three-month, or trimestre, wheat] is well known throughout the entire Alpine range, and in the northern provinces no kind of wheat is more prolific. It has a single stem only, is by no means large in any part, and is never sown except in a thin, light soil. There is also a two-month [bimestre] wheat found in the vicinity of Ænus in Thrace, which ripens on the fortieth day after sowing. It is a surprising fact, however, that no kind of wheat weighs heavier than this, while at the same time it produces no bran. Both Sicily and Achaia grow it in the mountainous districts of those countries, as does Eubœa in the vicinity of Carystus. Columella is therefore greatly in error in supposing that there is not even a distinct variety of three-month wheat; in fact these varieties have been known from the very earliest times. The Greeks give these wheats the name 'setanion.' It is said that in Bactria the grains of wheat are of such enormous size that a single grain is as large as one of our ears of corn.

CHAPTER 13. BARLEY AND RICE.

Of all the cereals, barley is the first to be sown. We shall state the appropriate time for sowing each kind when we come to treat of each individually. In India there is both a cultivated and a wild barley, from which they make excellent bread as well as alica [a type of groat or spelt preparation]. But the most favoured food there is rice, from which they prepare a ptisan [medicinal barley-water or gruel] similar to that made from barley in other parts of the world. The leaves of rice are fleshy, very like those of the leek but broader; the stem is a cubit in height, the blossom is purple, and the root is globular, resembling a pearl in shape.

CHAPTER 14. POLENTA.

Barley is one of the most ancient foods of mankind, a fact attested by a custom of the Athenians mentioned by Menander, and also by the name 'hordearii' [barley-men] formerly given to gladiators. The Greeks likewise prefer barley above all else for making polenta [roasted grain flour]. This food is prepared in various ways. In Greece, the barley is first steeped in water and then left overnight to dry. The next day it is parched and then ground in the mill. Some persons parch it more intensely and then sprinkle it again with a little water, after which they dry it before grinding. Others shake the grain from the ear while still green and, after cleaning and soaking it in water, pound it in a mortar. The paste is then washed in baskets and left to dry in the sun, after which it is pounded again, cleaned, and ground in the mill. Whatever method of preparation is used, the proportions are always twenty pounds of barley to three pounds of linseed, half a pound of coriander, and fifteen drachmas of salt; the ingredients are first parched together and then ground in the mill. Those who wish to store it keep it in new earthen vessels together with fine flour and bran. In Italy, the barley is prepared [differently—text continues].

segments.title_inferred: On Beans, Lentils, Pease, and Chick-Pease (Pliny, Natural History, Book XVIII, Chapters 30–32)
segments.original_text:
ticularly, the bean requires water; but after the blossom has passed off, it stands in need of but very little. It fertilizes the ground in which it has been sown as well as any manure; hence it is that in the neighbourhood of Thessaly and Macedonia, as soon as it begins to blossom, they turn up the ground. The bean, too, grows wild in most countries, as in those islands of the Northern Ocean, for instance, which for that reason have been called by us the "Fabariae." In Mauritania, also, it is found in a wild state in various parts, but so remarkably hard that it will never become soft by boiling. In Egypt there is a kind of bean which grows upon a thorny stalk; for which reason the crocodiles avoid it, being apprehensive of danger to their eyes. This stalk is four cubits in length, and its thickness, at the very most, that of the finger: were it not for the absence of articulations in it, it would resemble a soft reed in appearance. The head is similar to that of the poppy, being of a rose colour: the beans enclosed in this head are not above thirty in number; the leaves are large, and the fruit is bitter and odoriferous. The root, however, is highly esteemed by the natives as a food, whether eaten raw or well boiled; it bears a strong resemblance to that of the reed. This plant grows also in Syria and Cilicia, and upon the banks of Lake Torone in Chalcidice.

CHAP. 31. — LENTILS. PEASE.
Among the leguminous plants the lentil is sown in the month of November, and the pea, among the Greeks. The lentil thrives best in a soil that is rather thin than rich, and mostly stands in need of dry weather. There are two kinds of lentil grown in Egypt; one of which is rounder and blacker than the other, which has a peculiar shape of its own. The name of this plant has been applied to various uses, and among others has given origin to our word "lenticula." I find it stated in some authors that a lentil diet is productive of evenness of temper. The pea requires to be sown in a warm, sunny spot, and is ill able to endure cold; hence in Italy and the more rigorous climates, it is sown in the spring only, a light, loose soil being chosen for the purpose.

CHAP. 32. — THE SEVERAL KINDS OF CHICK-PEASE.
The chick-pea is naturally salt, for which reason it is apt to scorch the ground, and should only be sown after it has been steeped a day in water. This plant presents considerable differences in reference to size, colour, form, and taste. One variety resembles in shape a ram's head, from which circumstance it has received the name of "arietinum;" there are both the white and the black arietinum. There is also the columbine chick-pea, by some known as the "pea of Venus;" it is white, round, and smooth, being smaller than the arietinum, and is employed in the observances of the night festivals or vigils. The chicheling vetch, too, is a diminutive kind of chick-pea, unequal and angular, like [text breaks off]
segments.translation:
The bean in particular requires water; but after the blossom has passed off, it stands in need of very little. It enriches [fertilizes] the ground in which it has been sown as effectively as any manure; hence it is that in the neighbourhood of Thessaly and Macedonia, as soon as it begins to blossom, they turn the ground over. The bean also grows wild in most countries — for instance, in those islands of the Northern Ocean which for that reason we have called the 'Fabariae' [Bean Islands]. In Mauritania likewise it is found growing wild in various parts, but so remarkably hard that it will never become soft by boiling. In Egypt there is a kind of bean which grows upon a thorny stalk; for which reason crocodiles avoid it, being apprehensive of injury to their eyes. This stalk is four cubits in length, and its thickness at the very most equals that of a finger: were it not for the absence of joints [articulations] in it, it would resemble a soft reed in appearance. The head is similar to that of the poppy and is of a rose colour; the beans enclosed within this head number no more than thirty; the leaves are large, and the fruit is bitter and fragrant. The root, however, is held in high esteem by the natives as a food, whether eaten raw or thoroughly boiled; it bears a strong resemblance to the root of the reed. This plant grows also in Syria and Cilicia, and on the banks of Lake Torone in Chalcidice.

CHAPTER 31. — LENTILS AND PEASE.
Among the leguminous plants, the lentil is sown in the month of November, and the pea — as the Greeks reckon — likewise. The lentil thrives best in a soil that is rather thin than rich, and mostly requires dry weather. There are two kinds of lentil grown in Egypt: one of which is rounder and blacker than the other, which has a peculiar shape of its own. The name of this plant has been applied to various uses, and among others has given rise to our word 'lenticula' [a small lens-shaped object]. I find it stated in some authors that a diet of lentils promotes evenness of temper. The pea requires to be sown in a warm, sunny spot and is ill able to endure cold; hence in Italy and the more rigorous climates it is sown only in spring, a light and loose soil being chosen for the purpose.

CHAPTER 32. — THE SEVERAL KINDS OF CHICK-PEASE.
The chick-pea is naturally saline [salt], for which reason it is apt to scorch the ground, and should be sown only after it has been steeped for a day in water. This plant presents considerable variation with respect to size, colour, form, and taste. One variety resembles in shape a ram's head, from which circumstance it has received the name 'arietinum' [ram-like]; there exist both the white and the black arietinum. There is also the columbine chick-pea, known by some as the 'pea of Venus'; it is white, round, and smooth, being smaller than the arietinum, and is employed in the observances of the night festivals or vigils [nocturnal religious rites]. The chicheling vetch is likewise a diminutive kind of chick-pea, irregular and angular in shape, like [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: On Rape, Turnip, and Lupine: Cultivation, Varieties, and Uses
segments.original_text: them both from the same seed ; for when it is sown thick, or in a hard, cloggy soil, the produce will be male. The smaller the seed the better it is in quality. There are three kinds of rape in all ; the first is broad and flat, the second of a spherical shape, and the third, to which the name of "wild" rape has been given, throws out a long root, similar in appearance to a radish, with an angular, rough leaf, and an acrid juice, which, if extracted about harvest, and mixed with a woman's milk, is good for cleansing the eyes and improving defective sight. The colder the weather the sweeter they are, and the larger, it is generally thought ; heat makes them run to leaf. The finest rape of all is that grown in the district of Nursia : it is valued at as much as one sesterce per pound, and, in times of scarcity, two even. That of the next best quality is produced on Mount Algidus. CHAP. 35. THE TURNIP. The turnip of Amiternum, which is pretty nearly of the same nature as the rape, thrives equally well in a cold soil. It is sown just before the calends of March, four sextarii of seed to the jugerum. The more careful growers recommend that the ground should be turned up five times before putting in the turnip, and four for rape, care being taken, in both cases, to manure it well. Rape, they say, will thrive all the better, if it is sown together with some chaff. They will have it, too, that the sower ought to be stripped, and that he should offer up a prayer while sowing, and say : "I sow this for myself and for my neighbours." The proper time for sowing both kinds is the period that intervenes between the festivals of the two divinities, Neptune and Vulcan. It is said, too — and it is the result of very careful observation — that these plants will thrive wonderfully well, if they are sown as many days after the festival of Neptune as the moon was old when the first snow fell the previous winter. They are sown in spring as well, in warm and humid localities. CHAP. 36. (14.) — THE LUPINE. The lupine is the next among the leguminous plants that is in extensive use, as it serves for food for man in common with the hoofed quadrupeds. To prevent it from springing out of the pod while being gathered, and so lost, the best plan is to gather it immediately after a shower. Of all the seeds that are sown, there is not one of a more marvellous nature than this, or more favoured by the earth. First of all, it turns every day with the sun, and shows the hour to the husbandman, even though the weather should happen to be cloudy and overcast. It blossoms, too, no less than three times, and so attached is it to the earth, that it does not require to be covered with the soil ; indeed, this is the only seed that does not require the earth to be turned up for sowing it. It thrives
segments.translation:
Both [male and female forms of rape] arise from the same seed; for when sown thickly, or in a hard, compacted soil, the yield will be male. The smaller the seed, the better its quality. There are three kinds of rape in all: the first is broad and flat, the second spherical in shape, and the third, to which the name "wild" rape has been given, produces a long root resembling a radish in appearance, with an angular, rough leaf, and an acrid [sharp] juice. If this juice is extracted around harvest time and mixed with a woman's milk, it is good for cleansing the eyes and improving defective sight. The colder the weather, the sweeter and larger they generally become; heat causes them to run to leaf. The finest rape of all is that grown in the district of Nursia: it is valued at as much as one sesterce [Roman monetary unit] per pound, and in times of scarcity, two. The next best quality is produced on Mount Algidus.

CHAPTER 35. THE TURNIP.
The turnip of Amiternum, which is of much the same nature as rape, thrives equally well in cold soil. It is sown just before the Calends of March [i.e., late February], at a rate of four sextarii [Roman unit of volume] of seed per jugerum [Roman unit of land area]. The more careful growers recommend that the ground be turned up five times before planting turnip, and four times for rape, taking care in both cases to manure it well. Rape, they say, will thrive all the better if sown together with some chaff. They also maintain that the sower ought to be unclothed, and that he should offer up a prayer while sowing, saying: "I sow this for myself and for my neighbours." The proper time for sowing both kinds is the period that falls between the festivals of the two divinities Neptune and Vulcan. It is also said — and this is the result of very careful observation — that these plants will thrive wonderfully if they are sown as many days after the festival of Neptune as the moon was days old when the first snow fell the previous winter. They are also sown in spring in warm and humid localities.

CHAPTER 36. (14.) THE LUPINE.
The lupine is the next among the leguminous plants in widespread use, as it serves as food for both humans and hoofed quadrupeds alike. To prevent it from springing out of the pod while being gathered, and thus being lost, the best plan is to gather it immediately after a shower of rain. Of all seeds that are sown, there is none of a more marvellous nature than this, nor more favoured by the earth. First of all, it turns each day with the sun, and thereby shows the hour to the farmer, even when the weather happens to be cloudy and overcast. Moreover, it blossoms no fewer than three times, and is so adapted to the earth that it does not require to be covered with soil; indeed, it is the only seed that does not require the earth to be turned up for sowing. It thrives [text continues]

segments.title_inferred: On Lupines and Vetches: Cultivation, Soil Enrichment, and Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text:
Vulcanalia; 23rd of July and 23rd of August. In consequence of the brittleness of the pod. This is an exaggeration of certain phenomena observed in the leaves of all leguminous plants. Though left upon a soil thickly covered with brambles, it will throw out a root amid the leaves and brakes, and so contrive to reach the ground. We have already stated that the soil of a field or vineyard is enriched by the growth of a crop of lupines; indeed, so far is it from standing in need of manure, that the lupines will act upon it as well as the very best. It is the only seed that requires no outlay at all, so much so, in fact, that there is no necessity to carry it even to the spot where it is sown; for it may be sown the moment it is brought from the threshing-floor: and from the fact that it falls from the pod of its own accord, it stands in need of no one to scatter it. This is the very first grain sown and the last that is gathered, both operations generally taking place in the month of September; indeed, if this is not done before winter sets in, it is liable to receive injury from the cold. And then, besides, it may even be left with impunity to lie upon the ground, in case showers should not immediately ensue and cover it in, it being quite safe from the attacks of all animals, on account of its bitter taste: still, however, it is mostly covered up in a slight furrow. Among the thicker soils, it is attached to a red earth more particularly. In order to enrich this earth, it should be turned up just after the third blossom; but where the soil is sandy, after the second. Chalky and slimy soils are the only ones that it has an aversion to; indeed, it will never come to anything when sown in them. Soaked in warm water, it is used as a food, too, for man. One modius is a sufficient meal for an ox, and it is found to impart considerable vigour to cattle; placed, too, upon the abdomen of children, it acts as a remedy in certain cases. It is an excellent plan to season the lupine by smoking it; for when it is kept in a moist state, maggots are apt to attack the germ, and render it useless for reproduction. If cattle have eaten it off while in leaf, as a matter of necessity it should be ploughed in as soon as possible.

CHAP. 37. (15.) THE VETCH.
The vetch, too, enriches the soil, and its cultivation entails no labour on the agriculturist. It is sown after the ground has been but once turned up, and requires neither hoeing nor manuring; nothing at all, indeed, except harrowing. There are three periods for sowing it; the first is about the setting of Arcturus, when it is intended for feeding cattle in the month of December, while in the blade; this crop, too, is the best of all for seed, for, although grazed upon, it will bear just as well. The second crop is sown in the month of January, and the last in March; this last being the [text continues].
segments.translation:
Vulcanalia [the festival of Vulcan, celebrated on the 23rd of July and the 23rd of August]. [The lupine's pod is mentioned] on account of its brittleness. [The preceding remark about the lupine leaf turning toward the sun] is an exaggeration of certain phenomena observed in the leaves of all leguminous plants. Though left upon a soil thickly covered with brambles, the lupine will throw out a root amid the leaves and undergrowth, and so contrive to reach the ground. We have already stated [Book XVII, Ch. 6] that the soil of a field or vineyard is enriched by the growth of a crop of lupines; indeed, so far is it from standing in need of manure, that the lupine acts upon the soil as effectively as the very best dressing. It is the only seed that requires no outlay whatsoever, to such a degree, in fact, that there is no necessity even to carry it to the spot where it is to be sown; for it may be sown the moment it is brought from the threshing-floor [Latin: ex area]. And since it falls from the pod of its own accord, it requires no one to scatter it. This is the very first grain to be sown and the last to be gathered, both operations generally taking place in the month of September; indeed, if this is not accomplished before winter sets in, it is liable to suffer injury from the cold. Moreover, it may even be left with impunity to lie upon the ground, in the event that showers do not immediately ensue to cover it in, for it is quite safe from the attacks of all animals on account of its bitter taste; nevertheless, it is for the most part covered up in a slight furrow. Among heavier soils, it has a particular affinity for red earth. In order to enrich this earth, the lupine crop should be ploughed in just after the third blossoming; but where the soil is sandy, after the second blossoming. Chalky and slimy soils are the only ones to which it has an aversion; it will never come to maturity when sown in them. Soaked in warm water, it serves also as food for human beings. One modius [a Roman dry measure, approximately 8.75 litres] is a sufficient meal for an ox, and it is found to impart considerable vigour to cattle. Applied, moreover, as a poultice upon the abdomen of children, it acts as a remedy in certain cases [footnote: Marcellus Empiricus states that boiled lupine meal, spread as a plaster and laid on the abdomen, will destroy intestinal worms]. It is an excellent practice to cure the lupine by smoking it; for when it is kept in a moist state, maggots are apt to attack the germ and render it useless for reproduction. If cattle have eaten it off while it is still in the leaf, it should as a matter of necessity be ploughed in as soon as possible.

CHAPTER 37. (Section 15.) THE VETCH.
The vetch likewise enriches the soil, and its cultivation entails no labour on the part of the farmer. It is sown after the ground has been turned up but once, and requires neither hoeing nor manuring—nothing at all, indeed, except harrowing. There are three periods for sowing it: the first is around the setting of the star Arcturus, when it is intended for feeding cattle in the month of December while still in the blade; this first crop is also the best of all for seed, for even after being grazed upon it will bear just as well. The second crop is sown in the month of January, and the last in March; this last [text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Cultivation and Properties of Vetch, Fitch, Fenugreek, Secale, Farrago, and Ocinum (Pliny, Natural History, Book XVIII, Chapters 38–42)
segments.original_text: still, however, it manifests no repugnance to a shaded locality. This grain, if gathered when quite ripe, produces a chaff superior to that of any other. If sown near vines supported by trees, the vetch will draw away the juice from the vines, and make them languid. CHAP. 38. THE FITCH. The cultivation of the fitch, too, is attended with no difficulty. It requires weeding, however, more than the vetch. Like it, the fitch has certain medicinal properties; for we find the fact still kept in remembrance by some letters of his, that the late Emperor Augustus was cured by its agency. Five modii will sow as much ground as a yoke of oxen can plough in a day. If sown in the month of March, it is injurious, they say, to oxen; and when sown in autumn, it is apt to produce head-ache. If, however, it is put in the ground at the beginning of spring, it will be productive of no bad results. CHAP. 39. (16.) — SILICIA. Silicia, or, in other words, fenugreek, is sown after a light ploughing merely, the furrows being no more than some four fingers in depth; the less the pains that are bestowed upon it the better it will thrive — a singular fact that there should be anything that profits from neglect. The kinds, however, that are known as "secale" and "farrago" require harrowing only. CHAP. 40. — SECALE OR ASIA. The people of Taurinum, at the foot of the Alps, give to secale the name of "asia;" it is a very inferior grain, and is only employed to avert positive famine. It is prolific, but has a straw of remarkable thinness; it is also black and sombre-looking, but weighs extremely heavy. Spelt is mixed with this grain to modify its bitterness, and even then it is very disagreeable to the stomach. It will grow upon any soil, and yields a hundredfold; it is employed also as a manure for enriching the land. CHAP. 41. — FARRAGO: THE CRACCA. Farrago, a mixture made of the refuse of "far," or spelt, is sown very thick, the vetch being sometimes mingled with it; in Africa, this mixture is sometimes made with barley. All these mixtures, however, are only intended for cattle, and the same is the case with the cracca, a degenerate kind of leguminous plant. Pigeons, it is said, are so remarkably fond of this grain, that they will never leave the place where it has been given to them. CHAP. 42. — OCINUM: ERVILIA. Among the ancients there was a sort of fodder, to which Cato gives the name of "ocinum;" it was employed by them to stop scouring in oxen. This was a mixture of various kinds of fodder, cut green before the frosts came on. Mamilius Sura, however, explains the term differently, and says that ten modii of beans, two of vetches, and the same quantity of ervilia were mixed and sown in autumn on a jugerum of land.
segments.translation:
Nevertheless, the vetch [Vicia sativa] shows no aversion to a shaded location. This grain, when harvested fully ripe, produces a chaff superior to that of any other plant. If sown near vines trained upon trees, however, the vetch will draw away moisture from the vines and weaken them.

CHAPTER 38. THE FITCH [Orobus / Ervum ervilia]. The cultivation of the fitch likewise presents no great difficulty, though it requires more weeding than the vetch. Like the vetch, the fitch possesses certain medicinal properties; indeed, it is still recalled from certain letters of his own that the late Emperor Augustus was cured by its use. Five modii of seed will sow as much ground as a yoke of oxen can plough in a single day. If sown in the month of March, it is said to be harmful to oxen; and when sown in autumn, it tends to cause headache. If, however, it is put into the ground at the beginning of spring, it produces none of these ill effects.

CHAPTER 39. (16.) SILICIA [Fenugreek]. Silicia — that is, fenugreek — requires only a light ploughing before sowing, the furrows being no deeper than about four fingers; the less labour expended upon it, the better it thrives — a remarkable thing, that any crop should benefit from neglect. The kinds known as "secale" and "farrago," however, need only harrowing.

CHAPTER 40. SECALE, OR ASIA [Rye]. The people of Taurinum, at the foot of the Alps, call secale by the name "asia." It is a very inferior grain and is resorted to only to stave off outright famine. It is prolific, but its straw is remarkably thin; the grain is also black and gloomy in appearance, yet extremely heavy in weight. Spelt is mixed with it to temper its harsh flavour, and even so it sits very unpleasantly in the stomach. It will grow on any soil and yields a hundredfold; it is also used as a green manure to enrich the land.

CHAPTER 41. FARRAGO AND THE CRACCA. Farrago — a mixture made from the waste of far [emmer/spelt] — is sown very densely, sometimes with vetch mingled in; in Africa, barley is sometimes added to this mixture instead. All such mixtures, however, are intended solely for fodder cattle; the same is true of the cracca, a degenerate kind of leguminous plant. Pigeons, it is said, are so extraordinarily fond of cracca that they will never abandon the place where it has once been fed to them.

CHAPTER 42. OCINUM AND ERVILIA. Among the ancients there was a fodder to which Cato gives the name "ocinum;" it was used to stop scouring [diarrhoea] in oxen. This consisted of a mixture of various kinds of fodder cut green before the onset of frosts. Mamilius Sura, however, gives a different explanation of the term, stating that ten modii of beans, two of vetches, and an equal quantity of ervilia were mixed together and sown in autumn upon a iugerum [standard Roman land measure] of ground.

segments.title_inferred: On Lucerne (Medicago sativa) and Ocinum: Cultivation, Properties, and Fodder Use
segments.original_text: states, also, that it is a still better plan to mix some Greek oats with it, the grain of which never falls to the ground; this mixture, according to him, was ocinum, and was usually sown as a food for oxen. Varro informs us that it received its name on account of the celerity with which it springs up, from the Greek ὠκέως, "quickly." CHAP. 43. LUCERNE. Lucerne is by nature an exotic to Greece even, it having been first introduced into that country from Media, at the time of the Persian wars with King Darius; still it deserves to be mentioned among the very first of these productions. So superior are its qualities, that a single sowing will last more than thirty years. It resembles trefoil in appearance, but the stalk and leaves are articulated. The longer it grows in the stalk, the narrower is the leaf. Amphilochus has devoted a whole book to this subject and the cytisus. The ground in which it is sown, being first cleaned and cleared of stones, is turned up in the autumn, after which it is ploughed and harrowed. It is then harrowed a second and a third time, at intervals of five days; after which manure is laid upon it. This seed requires either a soil that is dry, but full of nutriment, or else a well-watered one. After the ground has been thus prepared, the seed is put in in the month of May; for if sown earlier, it is in danger from the frosts. It is necessary to sow the seed very thick, so that all the ground may be occupied, and no room left for weeds to shoot up in the intervals; a result which may be secured by sowing twenty modii to the jugerum. The seed must be stirred at once with the rake, to prevent the sun from scorching it, and it should be covered over with earth as speedily as possible. If the soil is naturally damp or weedy, the lucerne will be overpowered, and the spot degenerate into an ordinary pasture; it is necessary, therefore, directly the crop is an inch in height, to disengage it from all weeds, by hand, in preference to the weeding-hook. It is cut when it is just beginning to flower, and this is repeated as often as it throws out new blossoms; which happens mostly six times in the year, and four at the very least. Care should be taken to prevent it from running to seed, as it is much more valuable as fodder, up to the third year. It should be hoed in the spring, and cleared of all other plants; and in the third year the surface should be well worked with the weeding-hook. By adopting this method, the weeds will be effectually destroyed, though without detriment to the lucerne, in consequence of the depth of its roots. If the weeds should happen to get ahead of it, the only remedy is to turn it up repeatedly with the plough, until the roots of the weeds are thoroughly destroyed. This fodder should never be given to cattle to satiety, otherwise it may be necessary to let blood; it is best, too, when used while green. When dry, it becomes tough and ligneous, and falls away at last into a thin, useless
segments.translation:
He [Varro] states also that it is a still better plan to mix some Greek oats with it — the grain of which never falls to the ground — this mixture being what he called ocinum, usually sown as fodder for oxen. Varro informs us that ocinum received its name on account of the speed with which it springs up, derived from the Greek ὠκέως, meaning "quickly."

CHAPTER 43. LUCERNE.

Lucerne [Medica] is by nature a plant foreign even to Greece, having been first introduced into that country from Media at the time of the Persian wars under King Darius; yet it deserves to be numbered among the foremost of agricultural products. Its qualities are so superior that a single sowing will sustain a field for more than thirty years. It resembles trefoil [clover] in appearance, but both the stalk and leaves are jointed [articulated]. The longer the stalk grows, the narrower the leaf becomes. Amphilochus devoted an entire book to this plant and to cytisus.

The ground intended for lucerne is first cleared of weeds and stones, then turned up in the autumn, after which it is ploughed and harrowed. It is harrowed a second and a third time at intervals of five days, and then manure is applied. This seed requires either a dry but nutrient-rich soil, or else a well-watered one. Once the ground has been so prepared, the seed is sown in the month of May, for if sown earlier it is at risk from frosts. The seed must be sown very densely so that the entire ground is occupied and no space is left for weeds to grow in the intervals; this is achieved by sowing twenty modii per jugerum. The seed must be raked in immediately to prevent the sun from scorching it, and it should be covered with earth as quickly as possible.

If the soil is naturally damp or prone to weeds, the lucerne will be overwhelmed and the plot will degenerate into ordinary pasture. It is therefore necessary, as soon as the crop reaches about an inch in height, to remove all weeds by hand rather than with the weeding-hook. It is cut just as it begins to flower, and this operation is repeated whenever new blossoms appear — which occurs most often six times a year, and at the very least four times. Care must be taken not to allow it to run to seed, as it is considerably more valuable as fodder [before seeding], especially up to the third year. It should be hoed in spring and cleared of all other plants, and in the third year the surface should be thoroughly worked with the weeding-hook. By this method, weeds are effectively destroyed without harming the lucerne, owing to the depth of its roots. If weeds do manage to get the upper hand, the only remedy is to plough the field repeatedly until the roots of the weeds are completely destroyed.

This fodder should never be fed to cattle to the point of satiety, otherwise it may be necessary to let blood [bleed the animals]; it is also best given while still green. When dried, it becomes tough and woody, and ultimately crumbles into a thin, worthless [substance].

segments.title_inferred: Diseases of Grain: The Oat, Insects, Wind, and Other Hazards to Wheat and Barley
