linden-tree. It seems to me very probable, too, that it was from them that the Carthaginians borrowed the first hint for applying spartum [esparto grass] to a similar purpose.

CHAPTER 10. THE BULB ERIOPHORUS.

Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum, Book 13, Chapter 13) informs us that there is a kind of bulb which grows on the banks of rivers, and which encloses between its outer coat and the edible portion a sort of woolly substance, from which felt socks and other articles of clothing are made. However, in the manuscript copies I have been able to consult, no mention is made of the country in which it grows, nor of any further details concerning it, beyond the fact that it is called "eriophoron" [wool-bearer]. As for spartum, Theophrastus makes no mention of it whatsoever — though he recorded, with the greatest exactness, the history of all known plants three hundred and ninety years before our time, a fact to which I have already referred on other occasions. From this it would appear that spartum only came into common use after his day.

CHAPTER 11. PLANTS WHICH SPRING UP AND GROW WITHOUT A ROOT. PLANTS WHICH GROW BUT CANNOT BE REPRODUCED FROM SEED.

Having here begun to treat of the marvels of Nature, we shall proceed to examine them in detail. Among them, beyond all doubt the very greatest is the fact that any plant should spring up and grow without a root. Such, for instance, is the vegetable production known as the truffle [tuber]. Surrounded on every side by earth, it is connected with the soil by no fibres — not so much as a single thread — while the spot in which it grows presents neither protuberance nor cleft to the view. It is found, in fact, in no way adhering to the earth, but enclosed within an outer coat [cortex]; so much so, indeed, that though we cannot exactly pronounce it to be composed of earth itself, we must conclude that it is nothing other than a callous [hardened] concretion of the earth.

Truffles generally grow in dry, sandy soils and in spots thickly covered with shrubs. In size they are often larger than a quince, and are found to weigh as much as a pound. There are two kinds: one full of sand, and consequently injurious to the teeth; the other free from sand and all impurities. They are also distinguished by their colour, which is red or black, and white within. Those of Africa are the most highly esteemed. Whether the truffle grows gradually, or whether this blemish of the earth — for it can be looked upon as nothing else — at once assumes the globular form and magnitude it presents when found [the text breaks off here].

segments.title_inferred: Laserpitium, Laser, and Maspetum: On the Plant Silphium and Its Resin
segments.original_text: It was the produce of this last country, probably, that afforded the juice or gum resin here mentioned as 'laser,' and so highly esteemed by the ancients, as forming a component part of their perfumes. Fee is inclined to think that the Laserpitium here spoken of was the Thapsia silphium, and to mark it as a remarkable plant, known to the Greeks by the name of 'silphion,' and originally a native of the province of Cyrenaica. The juice of this plant is called 'laser,' and it is greatly in vogue for medicinal as well as other purposes, being sold at the same rate as silver. For these many years past, however, it has not been found in Cyrenaica, as the farmers of the revenue who hold the lands there on lease, have a notion that it is more profitable to depasture flocks of sheep upon them. Within the memory of the present generation, a single stalk is all that has ever been found there, and that was sent as a curiosity to the Emperor Nero. If it so happen that one of the flock, while grazing, meets with a growing shoot of it, the fact is easily ascertained by the following signs; the sheep, after eating of it, immediately falls asleep, while the goat is seized with a fit of sneezing. For this long time past, there has been no other laser imported into this country, but that produced in either Persis, Media, or Armenia, where it grows in considerable abundance, though much inferior to that of Cyrenaica; and even then it is extensively adulterated with gum, sacopenium, or pounded beans. I ought the less then to reject the more general opinion that it is identical with the Ferula asafoetida. Pliny has probably caused some confusion by blending the description of other writers with that given by Theophrastus, each having in view a different plant. Indeed, whatever the Laserpitium or Silphium of other countries may have been, it is not improbable that the odoriferous plant of Cyrenaica was not identical with the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus. The foliage of the Thapsia silphium is exactly similar to that of the Laserpitium as depicted on medals of Cyrenaica, still extant. We learn from Littré, that Dr. Guyon showed, in 1842, to the Académie des Sciences, a plant which the Arabs of Algeria employ as a purgative, and which they call bonnefa. It is the Thapsia Garganica of Desfontaines, and is considered by Guyon to be identical with the Silphium of the ancients. [Footnote 86: See B. xxii. c. 48. In the 'Rudens' of Plautus, the scene of which is near Cyrene, frequent allusion is made to the growth of laserpitium there, and the preparation and export of the resin, as forming the staple article of commerce.] [Footnote 87: Scribonius Largus, who lived in the time of Tiberius, speaks of using in a prescription laser of Cyrenaica, 'if it can be met with'; 'si poterit inveniri.'] [Footnote 88: 'In spem nascentis.'] [Footnote 89: Fee remarks that Pliny has not found this absurd story in any of the works from which he has compiled his account, but that it is entirely his own.] [Footnote 90: This was probably the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus.] [Footnote 91: See B. xx. c. 75.] —omit the facts, that in the consulship of C. Valerius and M. Herennius, there was brought to Rome, from Cyrenae, for the public service, thirty pounds' weight of laserpitium, and that the Dictator Caesar, at the beginning of the Civil War, took from out of the public treasury, besides gold and silver, no less than fifteen hundred pounds of [laserpitium].

[The commentator] Fee is inclined to identify the Laserpitium here described with Thapsia silphium, and to characterize it as a remarkable plant, known to the Greeks under the name 'silphion,' and originally native to the province of Cyrenaica. The juice of this plant is called 'laser,' and it was greatly in demand for medicinal as well as other purposes, being sold at the same price as silver. For many years past, however, it has not been found in Cyrenaica, since the tax-farmers who lease the lands there considered it more profitable to graze flocks of sheep upon them. Within living memory, only a single stalk has ever been found there, and that was sent as a curiosity to the Emperor Nero. Should it happen that one of the grazing flock encounters a growing shoot of the plant, the fact may be readily determined by the following signs: the sheep, having eaten of it, immediately falls asleep, while the goat is seized with a fit of sneezing. For a long time now, no laser other than that produced in Persis [Persia], Media, or Armenia has been imported into this country [Rome], where it grows in considerable abundance, though far inferior to that of Cyrenaica; and even this imported laser is extensively adulterated with gum, sacopenium [sagapenum], or ground beans. I have the less reason, therefore, to reject the more widely held opinion that it is identical with Ferula asafoetida. Pliny has probably introduced some confusion by combining the descriptions of other writers with that of Theophrastus, each of whom had a different plant in mind. Indeed, whatever the Laserpitium or Silphium of other regions may have been, it is not improbable that the odoriferous plant of Cyrenaica was not the same as the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus. The foliage of Thapsia silphium is exactly similar to that of the Laserpitium as it is depicted on medals of Cyrenaica still extant. We learn from Littré that Dr. Guyon presented to the Académie des Sciences in 1842 a plant which the Arabs of Algeria use as a purgative and call bonnefa. This is the Thapsia Garganica of Desfontaines, and is considered by Guyon to be identical with the Silphium of the ancients. [Footnote: See Book xxii, chapter 48. In Plautus's 'Rudens,' the scene of which is set near Cyrene, frequent reference is made to the cultivation of laserpitium there, and to the preparation and export of the resin as the chief article of commerce. — Scribonius Largus, who lived in the time of Tiberius, mentions using laser of Cyrenaica in a prescription 'if it can be obtained' ('si poterit inveniri'). — Fee remarks that Pliny has not found this curious story about sheep and goats in any of the sources he compiled, and that it is entirely his own addition. — The inferior laser of Persia and Media was probably the Ferula asafoetida of Linnaeus. — See Book xx, chapter 75 for sacopenium.] Not to omit the further facts: that in the consulship of C. Valerius and M. Herennius, thirty pounds' weight of laserpitium was brought to Rome from Cyrene for public use; and that the Dictator Caesar, at the outbreak of the Civil War, withdrew from the public treasury, in addition to gold and silver, no less than fifteen hundred pounds of [laserpitium].

segments.title_inferred: Laserpitium (Silphium) and Magydaris: Description, Properties, and Uses
segments.original_text:
remarkable fall of rain extended, it is said, over no less than four thousand stadia of the African territory; and upon this soil laserpitium began universally to grow, a plant that is in general wild and stubborn, and which, if attempted to be cultivated, will leave the spot where it has been sown quite desolate and barren. The roots of it are numerous and thick, the stalk being like that of fennel-giant, and of similar thickness. The leaves of this plant were known as "maspetum," and bore a considerable resemblance to parsley; the seeds of it were foliaceous, and the plant shed its leaves every year. They used to feed the cattle there upon it; at first it purged them, but afterwards they would grow fat, the flesh being improved in flavour in a most surprising degree. After the fall of the leaf, the people themselves were in the habit of eating the stalk, either roasted or boiled: from the drastic effects of this diet the body was purged for the first forty days, all vicious humours being effectually removed. The juices of this plant were collected two different ways, either from the root or from the stalk; in consequence of which these two varieties of the juice were known by the distinguishing names of "rhizias" and "caulias," the last being of inferior quality to the other, and very apt to turn putrid. Upon the root there was a black bark, which was extensively employed for the purposes of adulteration. The juice of the plant was received in vessels, and mixed there with a layer of bran; after which, from time to time it was shaken, till it had reached a proper state of maturity; indeed, if this precaution was neglected, it was apt to turn putrid. The signs that it had come to maturity were its colour, its dryness, and the absorption of all humidity. There are some authors, however, who state that the root of laserpitium was more than a cubit in length, and that it presented a tuberosity above the surface of the earth. An incision, they say, was made in this tuberosity, from which a juice would flow, like milk in appearance; above the tuberosity grew a stalk, to which they give the name of "magydaris;" the leaves that grew upon this stalk were of the colour of gold, and, falling at the rising of the Dog-star, when the south winds begin to prevail, they acted as seed for the purposes of reproduction. It was from these leaves, too, they say, that laserpitium was produced, the root and the stalk attaining their full growth in the space of one year. The same writers also state, that it was the practice to turn up the ground about the plant, and that it had no such effect as purging the cattle that were fed upon it; though one result of using it as food was, that such cattle as were ailing were either cured of their distempers, or else died immediately upon eating of it, a thing, however, that but rarely happened. The first description, however, is found to agree more nearly with the silphium that comes from Persis.

CHAP. 16. MAGYDARIS.
There is another variety of this plant, known as [magydaris].
segments.translation:
A remarkable fall of rain is said to have extended over no less than four thousand stadia [roughly 460 miles] of African territory; and upon this soil laserpitium [silphium] began to grow universally — a plant that is in general wild and stubborn, and which, if one attempts to cultivate it, will leave the ground where it was sown quite desolate and barren. Its roots are numerous and thick, and its stalk resembles that of giant fennel [ferula] in form and thickness. The leaves of this plant were known as "maspetum" and bore a considerable resemblance to parsley; its seeds were foliaceous [leaf-like], and the plant shed its leaves every year. In that region they used to feed cattle upon it; at first it purged them, but afterwards they would grow fat, the flesh being improved in flavour to a most surprising degree. After leaf-fall, the people themselves were accustomed to eating the stalk, either roasted or boiled: owing to the drastic effects of this diet, the body was purged for the first forty days, all corrupt humours being thoroughly expelled. The juices of this plant were collected in two different ways — either from the root or from the stalk — and accordingly these two varieties of juice were distinguished by the names "rhizias" [root-juice] and "caulias" [stalk-juice], the latter being of inferior quality to the former and very liable to putrefaction. Upon the root there was a black bark, which was widely used for purposes of adulteration. The juice of the plant was received into vessels and mixed with a layer of bran; it was then shaken periodically until it reached a proper state of maturity — for if this precaution were neglected, it was prone to turn putrid. The signs of its maturity were its colour, its dryness, and the absorption of all moisture.

Some authors, however, state that the root of laserpitium was more than a cubit in length and that it produced a tuberosity above the surface of the earth. An incision was made in this tuberosity, they say, from which a milky juice would flow; above the tuberosity grew a stalk to which they give the name "magydaris." The leaves growing upon this stalk were golden in colour and, falling at the rising of the Dog-star when the south winds begin to prevail, served as seed for reproduction. From these leaves, they say, laserpitium was also produced, the root and stalk reaching full growth within a single year. The same writers further state that it was customary to turn up the ground around the plant, and that it had no purgative effect upon the cattle fed upon it; though one consequence of its use as fodder was that ailing cattle were either cured of their ailments or died immediately upon eating it — an outcome, however, that occurred only rarely. The first description, however, is found to correspond more closely with the silphium that comes from Persia.

CHAPTER 16. MAGYDARIS.
There is another variety of this plant, known as [magydaris].

segments.title_inferred: On Laserpitium, Madder, and Radicula: Plants Used in Dyeing and Medicine
segments.original_text:
The whole of this paragraph has been borrowed from Theophrastus, Hist. Plant. B. vi. c. iii. Sprengel takes this to be the Laserpitium ferulaceum of Linnaeus, but Fee thinks it is more than doubtful if the identity can be established. From Theophrastus. Dioscorides says, on the other hand, that it grows in Libya. grows also upon Mount Parnassus, in great abundance, a plant to which some persons give the name of "laserpitium:" by means of all these varieties, adulterations are effected of a production that is held in the highest esteem for its salutary qualities and its general usefulness. The chief proofs of its genuineness consist in its colour, which ought to be slightly red without, and when broken quite white and transparent within; the drops of it, too, should melt very rapidly on the application of spittle. It is extensively employed for medicinal purposes.

CHAP. 17. MADDER.
There are two other plants also, which are but little known to any but the herd of the sordid and avaricious, and this because of the large profits that are derived from them. The first of these is madder, the employment of which is necessary in dyeing wool and leather. The madder of Italy is the most esteemed, and that more particularly which is grown in the suburbs of the City; nearly all our provinces, too, produce it in great abundance. It grows spontaneously, but is capable of reproduction by sowing, much after the same manner as the fitch. The stem, however, is prickly, and articulated, with five leaves arranged round each joint: the seed is red. Its medicinal properties we shall have occasion to mention in the appropriate place.

CHAP. 18. THE RADICULA.
The plant known to us by the name of "radicula," is the second of these productions. It furnishes a juice that is extensively employed in washing wool, and it is quite wonderful how greatly it contributes to the whiteness and softness of wool. It may be produced anywhere by cultivation, but that which grows spontaneously in Asia and Syria, upon rugged, rocky sites, is more highly esteemed. That, however, which is found beyond the Euphrates has the highest repute of all. The stalk of it is ferulaceous and thin, and is sought by the inhabitants of those countries as an article of food. It is employed also for making unguents, being boiled up with the other ingredients, whatever they may happen to be. In leaf it strongly resembles the olive. The Greeks...
segments.translation:
This passage is drawn in its entirety from Theophrastus, Natural History of Plants, Book VI, Chapter III. Sprengel identifies the plant in question as Laserpitium ferulaceum of Linnaeus, but Fée considers it more than doubtful that this identification can be firmly established. According to Theophrastus, while Dioscorides states that it grows in Libya, it also grows upon Mount Parnassus in great abundance — a plant to which some persons give the name 'laserpitium.' Through all these various species, adulteration is carried out of a product held in the highest esteem for its health-giving qualities and its general utility. The chief marks of its genuineness lie in its colour, which ought to be slightly reddish on the outside, and when broken, quite white and transparent within; moreover, drops of it should dissolve very rapidly upon contact with saliva. It is extensively employed for medicinal purposes.

CHAPTER 17. MADDER.
There are two further plants which are little known except to the greedy and avaricious, owing to the great profits that can be derived from them. The first of these is madder [Rubia tinctorum], the use of which is indispensable in dyeing wool and leather. The madder of Italy is the most highly regarded, and especially that grown in the environs of the City [Rome]; nearly all our provinces likewise produce it in great abundance. It grows wild of its own accord, but can also be propagated by sowing, in much the same manner as the fitch [vetch]. Its stem, however, is prickly and jointed, with five leaves arranged around each node; the seed is red. We shall have occasion to discuss its medicinal properties in the appropriate place.

CHAPTER 18. THE RADICULA.
The plant known to us by the name 'radicula' [little root] — though in reality, as Pliny notes, it possesses a large root — is the second of these productions. It yields a juice that is extensively used in washing wool, and it is quite remarkable how greatly it contributes to the whiteness and softness of that material. It may be cultivated almost anywhere, but the plant that grows wild in Asia and Syria, on rough and rocky ground, is held in higher esteem. That found beyond the Euphrates, however, enjoys the greatest reputation of all. Its stalk is ferulaceous [fennel-like] and slender, and is sought out by the inhabitants of those regions as a foodstuff. It is also employed in the preparation of unguents, being boiled together with whatever other ingredients are used. In its leaves it closely resembles the olive. The Greeks...

segments.title_inferred: On Gardens: Historical and Cultural Notes from Pliny's Natural History, Book XIX

The kings of Rome cultivated their gardens with their own hands; indeed, it was from his garden that Tarquinius Superbus sent to his son that cruel and sanguinary message of his. In our laws of the Twelve Tables, we find the word 'villa,' or 'farm,' nowhere mentioned; it is the word 'hortus' that is always used with that signification, while the term 'heredium' we find employed for 'garden.' There are certain religious impressions, too, that have been attached to this species of property, and we find that it is in the garden and the Forum only that statues of satyrs are consecrated, as a protection against the evil effects of spells and sorcery; although in Plautus, we find the gardens spoken of as being under the tutelage of Venus. At the present day, under the general name of gardens, we have pleasure-grounds situate in the very heart of the City, as well as extensive fields and villas. segments.translation: The garden is a subject recommended by its own intrinsic merits to our notice. For we find that even in remote antiquity nothing was looked upon with a greater degree of admiration than the gardens of the Hesperides, those of the kings Adonis and Alcinous, and the Hanging Gardens — whether these last were the work of Semiramis or of Cyrus, king of Assyria — a subject of which we shall have occasion to speak in another work. The kings of Rome cultivated their gardens with their own hands; indeed, it was from his garden that Tarquinius Superbus sent his son that cruel and bloody message. In the laws of the Twelve Tables, the word 'villa' [farm] is nowhere mentioned; it is always the word 'hortus' [kitchen-garden] that carries that meaning, while 'heredium' is the term employed for 'garden.' Certain religious associations have also attached themselves to this kind of property: it is in the garden and in the Forum alone that statues of satyrs are consecrated, as a protection against the harmful effects [effascinationes] of spells and sorcery — though in Plautus the gardens are spoken of as being under the protection of Venus. In our own day, under the general name of gardens, we have pleasure-grounds situated in the very heart of the City, as well as extensive fields and country villas. Epicurus, that connoisseur in the pleasures of a life of ease, was the first to lay out a garden at Athens; until his time it had never been conceived to dwell amid countryside in the middle of the city. At Rome, on the other hand, the garden constituted in itself the poor man's field, and it was from the garden that the lower classes obtained their daily sustenance — how innocently procured! And yet, no doubt, it is far better to dive into the abysses of the [text breaks off].

segments.title_inferred: On Gardens, Garden Layout, and the Natural History of Garden Plants
segments.original_text: and that no Indian peppers were in request with them, or any of those other condiments which we are in the habit of seeking beyond the seas. In former times the lower classes of Rome, with their mimic gardens in their windows, day after day presented the reflex of the country to the eye, when as yet the multitudes of atrocious burglaries, almost innumerable, had not compelled us to shut out all such sights with bars to the passers by. Let the garden, then, have its due meed of honour, and let not things, because they are common, enjoy for that the less share of our consideration — and the more so, as we find that from it men of the very highest rank have been content to borrow their surnames even; thus in the Valerian family, for instance, the Lactucini have not thought themselves disgraced by taking their name from the lettuce. Perhaps, too, our labours and research may contribute some slight recommendation to this our subject; although, with Virgil, we are ready to admit how difficult it is, by language however elevated, to ennoble a subject that is so humble in itself. CHAP. 20. — THE LAYING OUT OF GARDEN GROUND. There is no doubt that the proper plan is, to have the gardens adjoining the country-house; and they should be watered, more particularly, by a river running in front of it, if possible; or else with water drawn from a well by the aid of a wheel or of pumps, or by swipes [tolleno: a counterbalanced beam used for raising water]. The ground should be opened just as the west winds are beginning to prevail; fourteen days after which it should be got ready for autumn, and then before the winter solstice it should have another turning up. It will require eight men to dig a jugerum [Roman unit of area, approximately 0.25 hectares], manure being mixed with the earth to a depth of three feet: the ground, too, should be divided into plots or beds with raised and rounded edges, each of which should have a path dug round it, by means of which access may be afforded to the gardener and a channel formed for the water needed for irrigation. CHAP. 21. PLANTS OTHER THAN GRAIN AND SHRUBS. Among the garden plants there are some that recommend themselves by their bulbs, others by the head, others by the stalk, others by the leaf, others by both: some, again, are valued for their seed, others for the outer coat, others for their membranous tissues, others for their cartilaginous substance, others for the firmness of their flesh, and others for the fleshy tunics in which they are enveloped. CHAP. 22. THE NATURAL HISTORY OF TWENTY DIFFERENT KINDS OF PLANTS WHICH GROW IN GARDENS — THE PROPER METHODS TO BE FOLLOWED IN SOWING THEM RESPECTIVELY. Of some plants the fruits are in the earth, of others both in the earth and out of it, and of others, again, out of the earth solely. Some of them increase as they lie upon the ground, gourds and cucumbers, for instance; the same products will grow also in a hanging position, but they are much heavier even then than any of the fruits that grow upon trees. The cucumber, however, is composed of cartilage and a fleshy substance.
segments.translation: And that no Indian peppers were sought after by them, nor any of those other condiments which we are accustomed to seeking from beyond the seas. In former times the common people of Rome, with their imitation gardens in their windows, presented day after day a reflection of the countryside to the eye, before the multitude of atrocious burglaries — almost beyond counting — had compelled us to shut out all such sights from passers-by with iron bars. Let the garden, then, receive its proper measure of honour, and let things not be held in lesser esteem merely because they are commonplace — especially since we find that men of the very highest rank have been content to borrow their family surnames from it; so, in the Valerian family, the Lactucini did not consider it a disgrace to take their name from the lettuce [Lactuca sativa]. Perhaps, too, our own labours and research may lend some small recommendation to this subject of ours; though, with Virgil, we are prepared to acknowledge how difficult it is, even with the most elevated language, to ennoble a subject so humble in itself. CHAPTER 20. — THE LAYING OUT OF GARDEN GROUND. There is no doubt that the proper plan is to situate gardens adjacent to the country house; and they should be watered, most advantageously, by a river flowing before it if possible; or else with water drawn from a well by means of a wheel, pumps, or swipes [tolleno: a counterbalanced beam-and-bucket device for raising water]. The ground should be broken open just as the west winds begin to prevail; fourteen days after that it should be prepared for autumn, and then before the winter solstice it should be turned up again. It will require eight men to dig a jugerum [approximately 0.25 hectares], with manure mixed into the earth to a depth of three feet. The ground should also be divided into plots or beds with raised and rounded edges, each of which should have a path dug around it, providing the gardener with access and forming a channel for the water required for irrigation. CHAPTER 21. PLANTS OTHER THAN GRAIN AND SHRUBS. Among garden plants, some recommend themselves by their bulbs, others by the head, others by the stalk, others by the leaf, and others by both stalk and leaf. Some are valued for their seed, others for their outer coat, others for their membranous tissues, others for their cartilaginous substance, others for the firmness of their flesh, and others for the fleshy tunics in which they are enclosed. CHAPTER 22. THE NATURAL HISTORY OF TWENTY DIFFERENT KINDS OF PLANTS GROWN IN GARDENS, AND THE PROPER METHODS TO BE FOLLOWED IN SOWING EACH OF THEM. The fruits of some plants lie in the earth, those of others both in and out of the earth, and those of others entirely out of the earth. Some increase as they spread along the ground — gourds and cucumbers, for instance; these same plants will also grow in a suspended position, but even then their fruits are considerably heavier than any fruit borne on trees. The cucumber, however, is composed of cartilage and a fleshy substance.

segments.title_inferred: Pliny on Cucumbers, Pepones, and Melopepos: Varieties, Properties, and Cultivation
segments.original_text: growing, may be trained to take any form that may be wished: in Italy the cucumbers are green and very small, while those grown in some of the provinces are remarkably large, and of a wax colour or black. Those of Africa, which are also remarkably prolific, are held in high esteem; the same, too, with the cucumbers of Moesia, which are by far the largest of all. When the cucumber acquires a very considerable volume, it is known to us as the 'pepo.' Cucumbers when eaten remain on the stomach till the following day, and are very difficult of digestion; still, for all that, in general they are not considered very unwholesome. By nature they have a wonderful hatred to oil, and no less affection for water, and this after they have been cut from the stem even. If water is within a moderate distance of them, they will creep towards it, while from oil, on the other hand, they will shrink away: if any obstacle, too, should happen to arrest their progress, or if they are left to hang, they will grow curved and crooked. Of these facts we may be satisfactorily convinced in a single night even, for if a vessel filled with water is placed at four fingers' distance from a cucumber, it will be found to have descended to it by the following morning; but if the same is done with oil, it will have assumed the curved form of a hook by the next day. If hung in a tube while in blossom, the cucumber will grow to a most surprising length. It is only of late, too, that a cucumber of entirely new shape has been produced in Campania, it having just the form of a quince. It was quite by accident, I am told, that the first one acquired this shape in growing, and it was from the seed of this that all the others have been reproduced. The name given to this variety is 'melopepo.' These last do not grow hanging, but assume their round shape as they lie on the ground. A thing that is very remarkable in them, in addition to their shape, colour, and smell, is the fact that, when ripe, although they do not hang from the stem, they separate from it at the stalk. Columella has given us a plan of his, by which we may have cucumbers the whole year round: the largest bramble-bush that can be procured is transplanted to a warm, sunny spot, and then cut down, about the time of the vernal equinox, to within a couple of fingers of the ground; a cucumber-seed is then inserted in the pith of the bramble, and the roots are well moulded up with fine earth and manure, to withstand the cold. According to the Greeks, there are three kinds of cucumbers, the La[conian...]

segments.title_inferred: On Gourds and Cucumbers: Cultivation, Varieties, and Growth Habits
segments.original_text: the produce, they say, will be sure to grow without seeds. CHAP. 24. — GOURDS. Gourds resemble the cucumber in nature, at least in their manner of growing; they manifest an equal aversion to the winter, too, while they require constant watering and manure. Both cucumbers and gourds are sown in holes a foot and a half deep, between the vernal equinox and the summer solstice, at the time of the Parilia or more particularly. Some persons, however, think it better to sow gourds after the calends of March, and cucumbers after the nones, and at the time of the Quinquatria. The cucumber and the gourd climb upwards in a precisely similar manner, their shoots creeping along the rough surface of the walls, even to the very roof, so great is their fondness for elevated spots. They have not sufficient strength, however, to support themselves without the aid of stays. Shooting upwards with the greatest rapidity, they soon cover with their light shade the arched roofs of the houses and the trellises on which they are trained. From this circumstance it is that we find the gourd classified into two primary kinds, the roof-gourd, and the common gourd, which creeps upon the ground. In the first kind, from a stalk of remarkable thinness is suspended a fruit of considerable weight and volume, and quite immoveable by the action of the wind. The gourd, too, as well as the cucumber, admits of being lengthened to any extent, by the aid of osier tubes more particularly. Just after the blossom has fallen off, the plant is introduced into these tubes, and as it grows it can be made to assume any form that may be wished, that of a serpent coiled up being the one that is mostly preferred; if left at liberty to grow as it hangs, it has been known before now to attain to no less than nine feet in length. The cucumber flowers gradually, blossom succeeding blossom; and it adapts itself perfectly well to a dry soil.
segments.translation: The produce, they say, will be sure to grow without seeds. CHAPTER 24. — GOURDS. Gourds resemble the cucumber in nature, at least in their manner of growing; they manifest an equal aversion to winter, and similarly require constant watering and manuring. Both cucumbers and gourds are sown in holes a foot and a half deep, between the vernal equinox and the summer solstice, most particularly at the time of the Parilia [a festival celebrated on the nineteenth of April, the anniversary of the founding of Rome]. Some persons, however, think it better to sow gourds after the Calends of March [the first of March], and cucumbers after the Nones [the seventh of March], at the time of the Quinquatria. The cucumber and the gourd climb upwards in a precisely similar manner, their shoots creeping along the rough surfaces of walls, even up to the very roof, so great is their tendency toward elevated positions. They lack sufficient strength, however, to support themselves without the aid of props. Shooting upward with the greatest rapidity, they soon cover with their light shade the arched roofs of houses and the trellises on which they are trained. It is from this circumstance that the gourd is classified into two primary kinds: the roof-gourd [camerarium], and the common gourd [plebeium], which creeps upon the ground. In the first kind, from a stalk of remarkable thinness is suspended a fruit of considerable weight and volume, quite unmoved by the action of the wind. The gourd, like the cucumber, may also be made to grow to any desired length, most effectively by means of osier [willow-wicker] tubes. Just after the blossom has fallen off, the young fruit is inserted into these tubes, and as it grows it can be made to assume any form desired, the shape of a coiled serpent being the most commonly preferred; if left free to grow as it hangs, the fruit has been known to attain no less than nine feet in length. The cucumber flowers gradually, blossom succeeding blossom, and it adapts perfectly well to a dry soil.

segments.title_inferred: On Gourds, Cucumbers, Rape, and Turnips
segments.original_text:
to be used in baths for jugs and pitchers, but for this long time past they have been employed as casks for keeping wine. The rind is tender while the fruit is green, but still it is always scraped off when the gourd is used for food. It admits of being eaten several ways, and forms a light and wholesome aliment, and this although it is one of those fruits that are difficult of digestion by the human stomach, and are apt to swell out those who eat of them. The seeds which lie nearest to the neck of the gourd produce fruit of remarkable length, and so do those which lie at the lower extremities, though not at all comparable with the others. Those, on the other hand, which lie in the middle, produce gourds of a round shape, and those on the sides fruit that are thick and short. The seeds are dried by being placed in the shade, and when wanted for sowing, are steeped in water first. The longer and thinner the gourd is, the more agreeable it is to the palate, and hence it is that those which have been left to grow hanging are reckoned the most wholesome: these, too, have fewer seeds than the others, the hardness of which is apt to render the fruit less agreeable for eating. Those which are intended for keeping seed, are usually not cut before the winter sets in; they are then dried in the smoke, and are extensively employed for preserving garden seeds, and for making other articles for domestic use. There has been a method discovered, also, of preserving the gourd for table, and the cucumber as well, till nearly the time when the next year's crop is ripe; this is done by putting them in brine. We are assured, too, that if put in a hole dug in a place well shaded from the sun, with a layer of sand beneath, and dry hay and earth on the top of them, they may be kept green for a very long time. We also find wild cucumbers and gourds; and, indeed, the same is the case with pretty nearly all the garden plants. These wild varieties, however, are only possessed of certain medicinal properties, and for this reason we shall defer any further mention of them till we come to the Books appropriated to that subject.

CHAP. 25. RAPE. TURNIPS.

The other plants that are of a cartilaginous nature are concealed, all of them, in the earth. In the number of these is the rape, a subject upon which it would almost appear that we have treated at sufficient length already, were it not that we think it as well to observe, that medical men call those which are round "male," while those which are larger and more elongated, are known to them as "female" rape: these last are superior in sweetness, and better for keeping, but by successive sowings they are changed into male rape. The same authors, too, have distinguished five different varieties of the turnip: the Corinthian, the Cleonaean, the Liothasian, the Boeotian, and the one which they have characterized as peculiarly the "green" turnip. The Corinthian turnip grows to a very large size,
segments.translation:
to be used in baths as jugs and pitchers, but for a long time now they have been employed as casks [dolia] for keeping wine. The rind is tender while the fruit is green, but it is always scraped off when the gourd is used for food. It may be eaten in several ways, and forms a light and wholesome nourishment [alimentum], though it is one of those fruits difficult of digestion by the human stomach and apt to cause bloating in those who eat it. The seeds lying nearest to the neck of the gourd produce fruit of remarkable length, as do those at the lower extremities, though these are not comparable with the former. Those lying in the middle, on the other hand, produce gourds of a round shape, and those at the sides produce fruit that is thick and short. The seeds are dried in the shade, and when needed for sowing are first steeped in water. The longer and thinner the gourd, the more agreeable it is to the palate, and hence those that have been allowed to grow hanging are considered the most wholesome; these also have fewer seeds than the others, the hardness of which tends to render the fruit less pleasant for eating. Those intended for keeping seed are usually not cut before the onset of winter; they are then dried in smoke and are extensively used for preserving garden seeds and for making various domestic articles. A method has also been discovered for keeping the gourd and the cucumber at table until nearly the time when the following year's crop is ripe; this is accomplished by placing them in brine [muria]. It is also reported that if placed in a hole dug in a spot well shaded from the sun, with a layer of sand beneath and dry hay and earth placed over them, they may be kept green for a very long time. Wild cucumbers and gourds are also found; and indeed the same is true of nearly all garden plants. These wild varieties, however, possess only certain medicinal properties, and for this reason further mention of them will be deferred until the Books devoted to that subject.

CHAPTER 25. RAPE. TURNIPS.

The other plants of a cartilaginous [cartilaginosus] nature are all concealed within the earth. Among these is the rape [rapa], a subject upon which it might appear we have already treated at sufficient length, were it not useful to note that medical men call those which are round "male" [mas], while those which are larger and more elongated are known to them as "female" [femina] rape; the latter are superior in sweetness and better for keeping, though through successive sowings they revert to male rape. The same authorities have also distinguished five varieties of the turnip [napus]: the Corinthian, the Cleonaean, the Liothasian, the Boeotian, and that which they have characterized as particularly the "green" turnip. The Corinthian turnip grows to a very large size,

segments.title_inferred: On Turnips and Radishes: Varieties, Properties, and Uses
segments.original_text:
stands extreme cold the best of all. Next to it, the Boeotian kind is the sweetest; it is remarkable, also, for the roundness of its shape and its shortness; while the Cleonaean turnip, on the other hand, is of an elongated form. Those, in general, which have a thin, smooth leaf, are the sweetest; while those, again, the leaf of which is rough, angular, and prickly, have a pungent taste. There is a kind of wild turnip, also, the leaves of which resemble those of rocket. At Rome, the highest rank is given to the turnips of Amiternum, and those of Nursia; after them, those grown in the neighbourhood of the City are held in the next degree of esteem. The other particulars connected with the sowing of the turnip have been already mentioned by us when speaking of the rape.

CHAP. 26. RADISHES.

Radishes are composed of an outer coat and a cartilaginous substance, and in many instances the rind is found to be thicker than the bark of some trees. This plant is remarkable for its pungency, which increases in proportion to the thickness of the rind: in some cases, too, the surface of it assumes a ligneous nature. Radishes are flatulent to a remarkable degree, and are productive of eructations; hence it is that they are looked upon as an aliment only fit for low-bred people, and this more particularly if cole worts are eaten directly after them. If, on the other hand, they are eaten with green olives, the eructations produced are not so frequent, and less offensive. In Egypt the radish is held in very high esteem, on account of the abundance of oil that is extracted from the seed. Indeed, the people of that country sow this plant in preference to any other, whenever they can get the opportunity, the profits derived from it being very considerable.
segments.translation:
This variety [of turnip] withstands extreme cold best of all. Next to it, the Boeotian kind is the sweetest; it is also notable for the roundness of its shape and its shortness; while the Cleonaean turnip, on the other hand, is of an elongated form. Those varieties whose leaves are thin and smooth are, in general, the sweetest; those whose leaves are rough, angular, and prickly have a pungent taste. There is also a kind of wild turnip [or wild radish] whose leaves resemble those of rocket [arugula]. At Rome, the highest esteem is given to the turnips of Amiternum and those of Nursia; after these, those grown in the vicinity of the City are held in the next degree of regard. The other particulars concerning the sowing of the turnip have already been mentioned when we spoke of the rape.

CHAPTER 26. RADISHES.

Radishes are composed of an outer skin and a cartilaginous inner substance, and in many cases the rind is found to be thicker than the bark of certain trees. This plant is remarkable for its pungency, which increases in proportion to the thickness of its rind; in some cases, furthermore, the surface takes on a woody [ligneous] character. Radishes are remarkably flatulent and cause eructations [belching]; hence they are regarded as a food fit only for common or low-bred people [cibus illiberalis], and this is especially so when cole worts [colewort/cabbage] are eaten directly after them. If, on the other hand, they are eaten together with green olives, the eructations produced are less frequent and less offensive. In Egypt the radish is held in very high esteem on account of the abundance of oil extracted from its seed. Indeed, the Egyptians sow this plant in preference to almost any other whenever opportunity permits, the profits derived from it being considerable.

segments.title_inferred: Varieties of Radish, Their Cultivation, and Agronomic Notes
segments.original_text: leaf of which is smooth, but shorter than that of the others; it is round also, grows in great abundance, and spreads like a shrub. The taste of this last variety is acrid, and it acts medicinally as a strong purgative. In the first kind, again, there are certain differences, determined by the seed, for in some varieties the seed is of an inferior quality, and in others remarkably small: these defects, however, are only found to exist in the kind that has the crisped leaf. Our own people, again, have found other varieties of the radish: there is the Algidan radish, long and transparent, so called from the place of its growth: another, similar to the rape in form, is known as the Syrian radish; it is pretty nearly the mildest and the most tender of them all, and is well able to bear the winter. The very best of all, however, is the one that has been brought from Syria, very recently it would seem, as we do not find it mentioned by any of our writers: it lasts the whole of the winter through. In addition to these kinds, there is another, a wild variety, known by the Greeks as "agrion," and to the people of Pontus as "armon," while others, again, call it "leuce," and our people "armoracia"; it has more leaves, however, than root. In testing the quality of the radish, it is the stem more particularly that is looked at; in those which are acrid to the taste, for instance, it is rounder and thicker than in the others, and grooved with long channels, while the leaves are more unsightly to the eye, being angular and covered with prickles. The radish requires to be sown in a loose, humid soil, has a great aversion to manure, and is content with a dressing solely of chaff: so fond is it of the cold, that in Germany it is known to grow as large as an infant in size. For the spring crop, it is sown immediately after the ides of February; and then again about the time of the Vulcanalia, this last crop being looked upon as the best: many persons, however, sow radishes in March, April, and September. When the plant begins to grow to any size, it is considered a good plan to cover up the leaves successively, and to earth up the root as well; for the part of it which appears above ground is apt to become hard and pithy. Aristomachus recommends the leaves to be taken off in winter, and the roots to be well moulded up, to prevent the water from accumulating about them; and he says, that by using these precautions, they will be all the finer in summer. Some authors have mentioned a plan of making a hole with a dibble, and covering it at the bottom with a layer of chaff, six fingers in depth; upon this layer the seed is put, and then covered over with manure and earth; the result of which is
segments.translation: The leaf of this [last] variety is smooth, but shorter than that of the others; it is also round, grows in great abundance, and spreads like a shrub. The taste of this last variety is acrid [pungent], and it acts medicinally as a strong purgative [laxative]. In the first kind, again, there are certain differences determined by the seed: in some varieties the seed is of inferior quality, and in others remarkably small; these defects, however, are found only in the kind that has the crisped [curly] leaf. Our own people have moreover found other varieties of the radish: there is the Algidan radish—long and transparent, so named from the place of its growth [Mount Algidus, near Tusculum, some fifteen miles from Rome]; another, similar in form to the rape [turnip], is known as the Syrian radish; it is very nearly the mildest and most tender of them all, and bears the winter well. The very best of all, however, is the one recently imported from Syria—so recently, it seems, that we find it mentioned by none of our earlier writers—and it keeps through the whole of the winter. Beyond these varieties there is yet another, a wild kind, called by the Greeks "agrion" [i.e., wild], by the people of Pontus "armon," by others "leuce" [i.e., white, from the extreme whiteness of its roots], and by our own people "armoracia" [horseradish; probably meaning 'radish of Armorica']; this plant has more leaf, however, than root. In judging the quality of the radish, it is above all the stem [stalk] that is examined: in those varieties that are acrid to the taste, the stem is rounder and thicker than in the others, and furrowed with long channels, while the leaves are more unsightly to the eye, being angular and covered with prickles. The radish requires a loose, humid soil; it has a great aversion to dung-manure and is content with a dressing of chaff alone. So fond is it of cold that in Germany it is known to grow as large as an infant. For the spring crop it is sown immediately after the Ides of February, and again about the time of the Vulcanalia [festival of Vulcan, 23 August], this latter crop being regarded as the best; many persons, however, sow radishes in March, April, and September. As the plant grows to any size, it is considered good practice to draw the leaves successively aside and to earth up the root as well, since the portion left above ground is apt to become hard and pithy. Aristomachus recommends removing the leaves in winter and heaping the soil well up around the roots to prevent water from collecting about them; by these precautions, he maintains, the roots will be all the finer in summer. Some writers describe a method of boring a hole with a dibble and lining the bottom with a layer of chaff six fingers deep; upon this the seed is placed and then covered with manure and earth; the result of which is [text continues in next segment].

The salt, too, has the similar effect of removing all their pungency, and when thus treated, they become very similar in their qualities to radishes that have been boiled: for when boiled they become sweet and mild, and eat, in fact, just like turnips. Medical men recommend raw radishes to be eaten fasting, with salt, for the purpose of collecting the crude humours of the viscera; and in this way they prepare them for the action of emetics. It is said, too, that the juices of this plant are absolutely necessary for the cure of certain diseases of the diaphragm; for it has been found by experiment, in Egypt, that the phthiriasis which attaches itself to the internal parts of the heart, cannot possibly be eradicated by any other remedy, the kings of that country having ordered the bodies of the dead to be opened and examined, for the purpose of enquiring into certain diseases. Such, too, is the frivolity of the Greeks, that, in the temple of Apollo at Delphi, it is said, the radish is so greatly preferred to all other articles of diet, as to be represented there in gold, the beet in silver, and the rape in lead. You might be very sure that Manius Curius was not a native of that country, the general whom, as we find stated in our Annals, the ambassadors of the Samnites found busy roasting rape at the fire, when they came to offer him the gold which he so indignantly refused. Moschion, too, a Greek author, has written a volume on the subject of the radish. These vegetables are considered a very useful article of food during the winter, but they are at all times very injurious to the teeth, as they are apt to wear them away; at all events, they give a polish to ivory. There is a great antipathy between the radish and the vine; which last will shrink from the radish, if sown in its vicinity. CHAP. 27. — PARSNIPS. Salt likewise has the similar effect of removing all their pungency, and when thus treated they become very similar in their qualities to radishes that have been boiled; for when boiled, radishes become sweet and mild, and taste, in fact, just like turnips.

Medical men recommend that raw radishes be eaten fasting, with salt, for the purpose of collecting the crude humours [undigested or phlegmatic matter] of the viscera; and in this way they prepare the stomach for the action of emetics. It is said, moreover, that the juices of this plant are absolutely necessary for the cure of certain diseases of the diaphragm; for it has been found by experiment in Egypt that phthiriasis [morbus pediculosus, i.e., louse-disease] which attaches itself to the internal parts of the heart cannot possibly be eradicated by any other remedy, the kings of that country having ordered the bodies of the dead to be opened and examined for the purpose of investigating certain diseases. Such, too, is the frivolity of the Greeks, that in the temple of Apollo at Delphi it is said the radish is so greatly preferred above all other articles of diet as to be represented there in gold, the beet in silver, and the rape in lead. You might be quite sure that Manius Curius was not a native of that country — the general whom, as we find recorded in our Annals, the ambassadors of the Samnites found busy roasting rape at the fire when they came to offer him the gold which he so indignantly refused. Moschion, too, a Greek author, has written an entire volume on the subject of the radish. These vegetables are considered a very useful article of food during winter, but they are at all times very injurious to the teeth, being apt to wear them away; at all events, they give a polish to ivory. There is a great antipathy between the radish and the vine; the vine will shrink away from the radish if it is sown in its vicinity.

CHAPTER 27. — PARSNIPS.

The other kinds which we have classified among the cartilaginous plants are of a more ligneous [woody] nature; and it is a singular thing that they all have a strong flavour. Among these, there is one kind of wild parsnip that grows spontaneously; by the Greeks it is known as "staphylinos" [Daucus carota, wild carrot or wild parsnip]. Another kind of parsnip is cultivated either from the root transplanted, or else from seed, at the beginning of spring or in the autumn; Hyginus states that this may be done in February, August, September, and October, the ground being dug to a [considerable depth].

segments.title_inferred: Hibiscum, Skirret, and Elecampane: Roots and Their Medicinal and Culinary Uses
segments.original_text:
is found quite impossible to get rid of. The hibiscum differs from the parsnip in being more slender: it is rejected as a food, but is found useful for its medicinal properties. There is a fourth kind, also, which bears a similar degree of resemblance to the parsnip; by our people it is called the "gallica," while the Greeks, who have distinguished four varieties of it, give it the name of "daucus." We shall have further occasion to mention it among the medicinal plants.

CHAP. 28. THE SKIRRET.
The skirret, too, has had its reputation established by the Emperor Tiberius, who demanded a supply of it every year from Germany. It is at Gelduba, a fortress situate on the banks of the Rhenus, that the finest are grown; from which it would appear that they thrive best in a cold climate. There is a string running through the whole length of the skirret, and which is drawn out after it is boiled; but still, for all this, a considerable proportion of its natural pungency is retained; indeed, when modified by the addition of honied wine, this is even thought to impart to dishes an additional relish. The larger parsnip has also a similar sting inside, but only when it is a year old. The proper time for sowing the skirret is in the months of February, March, April, August, September, and October.

CHAP. 29. ELECAMPANE.
Elecampane is not so elongated as the preceding roots, but more substantial and more pungent; eaten by itself it is very injurious to the stomach, but when mixed with other condiments of a sweet nature, it is extremely wholesome. There are several methods employed for modifying its natural acridity and rendering it agreeable to the palate: thus, for instance, when dried it is reduced to a fine flour, and then mixed with some sweet liquid or other, or else it is boiled in vinegar and water, or kept in soak in it; it is also steeped in various other ways, and then mixed with boiled grape-juice, or else incorporated with honey or raisins, or dates with plenty of meat on them. Other persons, again, have a method of preparing it with quinces, or else sorbs or plums, while sometimes the flavour is varied by the addition of pepper or thyme. This plant is particularly good for weakness of the stomach, and it has acquired a high reputation from the circumstance that Julia Augusta used to eat it daily. The seed of it is quite useless, as the plant is reproduced, like the reed, from eyes extracted from the root. This vegetable, as well as the skirret and the parsnip, is sown both in spring and autumn, a considerable distance being left between the plants; indeed, for elecampane, a space of no less than three feet is required, as [text ends mid-sentence; cf. Inula Helenium].
segments.translation:
…[the pungency] is found quite impossible to get rid of. The hibiscum [marsh-mallow, probably Althaea officinalis] differs from the parsnip in being more slender: it is rejected as a food, but is found useful for its medicinal properties. There is a fourth kind as well, which bears a similar degree of resemblance to the parsnip; our people call it the 'gallica,' while the Greeks, who have distinguished four varieties of it, give it the name 'daucus' [carrot]. We shall have further occasion to mention it among the medicinal plants.

CHAPTER 28. THE SKIRRET.
The skirret [Sium sisarum], too, has had its reputation established by the Emperor Tiberius, who demanded a supply of it every year from Germany. It is at Gelduba, a fortress situated on the banks of the Rhine, that the finest specimens are grown; from which it would appear that they thrive best in a cold climate. There is a fibrous cord running through the whole length of the skirret, which is drawn out after the root is boiled; but even so, a considerable proportion of its natural pungency is retained. Indeed, when this pungency is tempered by the addition of honeyed wine [mulsum], it is thought to impart an additional relish to dishes. The larger parsnip has a similar sharpness inside as well, but only when it is a year old. The proper times for sowing the skirret are the months of February, March, April, August, September, and October.

CHAPTER 29. ELECAMPANE.
Elecampane [Inula helenium] is not as elongated as the roots described above, but is more substantial and more pungent. Eaten on its own, it is very injurious to the stomach, but when mixed with other condiments of a sweet nature it is extremely wholesome. Several methods are employed to moderate its natural acridity and render it agreeable to the palate: for instance, when dried it is ground to a fine flour and then mixed with some sweet liquid; or else it is boiled in vinegar and water, or kept steeped therein; it is also prepared by steeping in various other ways and then mixed with boiled grape-juice [defrutum], or incorporated with honey, raisins, or well-fleshed dates. Others prepare it with quinces, or with sorb-apples [service berries] or plums, while sometimes the flavour is varied by the addition of pepper or thyme. This plant is particularly beneficial for weakness of the stomach, and it has acquired great renown from the fact that Julia Augusta [Livia] was accustomed to eat it daily. Its seed is quite useless for propagation, as the plant is reproduced—like the reed—from buds [eyes] extracted from the root. This vegetable, as well as the skirret and the parsnip, is sown both in spring and in autumn, with considerable spacing between plants; for elecampane, an interval of no less than three feet is required, as… [passage breaks off].

The modern Pharmacopoeias give the receipt of a conserve of elecampane, which, however, is no longer used. [Note 15: "Defrutum." Must, boiled down to one half.] [Note 17: The daughter of Augustus Caesar.]

It throws out its shoots to a very considerable distance. Skirrets, however, are best transplanted.

CHAP. 30. — BULBS, SQUILLS, AND ARUM.

Next in affinity to these plants are the bulbs, which Cato, speaking in high terms of those of Megara, recommends most particularly for cultivation. Among these bulbs, the squill, we find, occupies the very highest rank, although by nature it is medicinal, and is employed for imparting an additional sharpness to vinegar; indeed, there is no bulb known that grows to a larger size than this, or is possessed of a greater degree of pungency. There are two varieties of it employed in medicine, the male squill, which has white leaves, and the female squill, with black ones. There is a third kind also, which is good to eat, and is known as the Epimenidian squill; the leaf is narrower than in the other kinds, and not so rough. All the squills have numerous seeds, but they come up much more quickly if propagated from the offsets that grow on the sides. To make them attain a still greater size, the large leaves that grow around them are turned down and covered over with earth; by which method all the juices are carried to the heads. Squills grow spontaneously and in vast numbers in the Baleares and the island of Ebusus, and in the Spanish provinces. The philosopher Pythagoras has written a whole volume on the merits of this plant, setting forth its various medicinal properties; of which we shall have occasion to speak more at length in the succeeding Book.

The other species of bulbs are distinguished by their colour, size, and sweetness; indeed, there are some that are eaten raw even — those found in the Tauric Chersonesus, for instance. Next to these, the bulbs of Africa are held in the highest esteem, and after them those of Apulia. The Greeks have distinguished the following varieties: the bulbine, the setanion, the upition, the cyix, the leucoion, the aegilips, and the syrinchion — in the last there is this remarkable feature, that the extremities of the roots increase in winter, but during [text continues].

being pickled in brine, or else in the juice of rue, which, as Fee [the commentator A.-L.-A. Fée] remarks, would produce nothing more nor less than a veritable poison. The modern Pharmacopoeias provide the formula for a conserve of elecampane [Inula helenium], which, however, is no longer in use. [Note: "Defrutum" — grape must boiled down to half its volume. Note: Julia, daughter of Augustus Caesar.]

Elecampane throws out its shoots to a very considerable distance. Skirrets [Sium sisarum], however, are best propagated by transplanting.

CHAPTER 30. — BULBS, SQUILLS, AND ARUM.

Closely related to these plants are the bulbs, which Cato — speaking in high praise of those from Megara — recommends most particularly for cultivation. Among these bulbs, the squill holds the very highest rank, although it is by nature a medicinal plant and is also employed for imparting additional sharpness to vinegar. Indeed, no bulb is known that grows to a larger size than this, or possesses a greater degree of pungency. Two varieties are employed in medicine: the male squill, which has white [scale-]leaves, and the female squill, which has black [i.e., reddish or darker] ones. There is also a third kind, which is edible, known as the Epimenidian squill [after the philosopher Epimenides, or as named by Theophrastus]; its leaf is narrower than in the other kinds and not so rough. All squills produce numerous seeds, but they propagate much more quickly from the offsets that grow on their sides. To make the bulbs grow still larger, the broad outer leaves surrounding them are bent downward and covered with earth, so that all the plant's juices are directed into the heads [bulbs]. Squills grow wild and in vast numbers in the Balearic Islands, the island of Ebusus [Ibiza], and throughout the Spanish provinces. The philosopher Pythagoras wrote an entire volume on the virtues of this plant, setting forth its various medicinal properties, of which we shall have occasion to speak at greater length in the following Book [Book XX].

The other species of bulbs are distinguished by their colour, size, and sweetness; indeed, some are eaten raw — for example, those found in the Tauric Chersonesus [Crimea]. Next to these, the bulbs of Africa are held in the highest esteem, followed by those of Apulia. The Greeks have distinguished the following varieties: the bulbine, the setanion, the upition, the cyix [perhaps a kind of onion-like plant], the leucoion ["white violet" bulb], the aegilips, and the syrinchion — in the last of which there is a remarkable feature: the extremities of the roots increase in winter, but during [the text breaks off here].

segments.title_inferred: Bulbs, Roots, Flowers, and Leaves of Garden Plants (Pliny, Natural History, Book XIX, Chapters 30–31)
segments.original_text:
of it is of a milder nature, so much so, indeed, as to admit of being eaten raw. Bulbs are taken up before the spring, for if not, they are apt to spoil very quickly. It is a sign that they are ripe when the leaves become dry at the lower extremities. When too old they are held in disesteem; the same, too, with the long and the smaller ones; those, on the other hand, which are red and round are greatly preferred, as also those of the largest size. In most of them there is a certain degree of pungency in the upper part, but the middle is sweet. The ancients have stated that bulbs are reproduced from seed only, but in the champaign country of Praeneste they grow spontaneously, and they grow to an unlimited extent in the territory of the Remi.

CHAP. 31. (6.) THE ROOTS, FLOWERS, AND LEAVES OF ALL THESE PLANTS. GARDEN PLANTS WHICH LOSE THEIR LEAVES.

Nearly all the garden plants have a single root only, radishes, beet, parsley, and mallows, for example; it is lapathum, however, that has the longest root of them all, it attaining the length of three cubits even. The root of the wild kind is smaller and of a humid nature, and when up it will keep alive for a considerable period. In some of these plants, however, the roots are fibrous, as we find the case in parsley and mallows, for instance; in others, again, they are of a ligneous nature, as in ocimum, for example; and in others they are fleshy, as in beet, and in saffron even more so. In some, again, the root is composed of rind and flesh, as in the radish and the rape; while in others it is jointed, as in hay grass. Those plants which have not a straight root throw out immediately a great number of hairy fibres, orage and blite, for instance: squills again, bulbs, onions, and garlic never have any but a vertical root. Among the plants that grow spontaneously, there are some which have more numerous roots than leaves, spalax, for example, pellitory, and saffron. Wild thyme, southernwood, turnips, radishes, mint, and rue blossom all at once; while others, again, shed their blossom directly they have begun to flower. Ocimum blossoms gradually.

Those that are too old are held in low esteem; the same applies to the long and the smaller ones; those, on the other hand, that are red and round are greatly preferred, as are those of the largest size. In most of them there is a certain degree of pungency in the upper part, but the middle is sweet. The ancients stated that bulbs are reproduced from seed only; however, in the flat country around Praeneste they grow spontaneously, and they grow to an unlimited extent in the territory of the Remi [a people of Gaul].

CHAPTER 31. (6.) THE ROOTS, FLOWERS, AND LEAVES OF ALL THESE PLANTS. GARDEN PLANTS WHICH SHED THEIR LEAVES.

Nearly all garden plants have only a single root — radishes, beet, parsley, and mallows, for example. It is lapathum [dock or sorrel], however, that has the longest root of them all, reaching a length of three cubits. The root of the wild kind is smaller and of a moist nature, and once pulled it will remain alive for a considerable time. In some of these plants the roots are fibrous, as is the case with parsley and mallows; in others they are of a woody [ligneous] nature, as in ocimum [basil]; and in others they are fleshy, as in beet, and even more so in saffron. In some, again, the root is composed of rind and flesh, as in the radish and the rape [turnip]; while in others it is jointed, as in hay grass [gramen]. Those plants that do not have a straight root immediately throw out a great number of hairy fibres — orage [Atriplex, orach] and blite [Blitum], for instance. Squills, bulbs, onions, and garlic, on the other hand, never have anything but a vertical root. Among plants that grow spontaneously, some have more numerous roots than leaves — spalax, pellitory, and saffron, for example. Wild thyme, southernwood, turnips, radishes, mint, and rue all blossom at once; while others shed their blossom as soon as they have begun to flower. Ocimum blossoms gradually.

In some plants the flower is white, in others yellow, and in others purple. The leaves fall first from the upper part in wild-marjoram and elecampane, and in rue sometimes, when it has been injured accidentally. In some plants the leaves are hollow, the onion and the scallion more particularly.

CHAP. 32. VARIETIES OF THE ONION.

Garlic and onions are invoked by the Egyptians, when taking an oath, in the number of their deities. The Greeks have many varieties of the onion, the Sardian onion, the Samothracian, the Alsidenian, the setanian, the schistan, and the Ascalonian, so called from Ascalon, a city of Judaea. They have, all of them, a pungent smell, which draws tears from the eyes, those of Cyprus more particularly, and those of Cnidos the least of all. In all of them the body is composed of a cartilage of an unctuous nature. The variety known as the setanian is the smallest of them all, with the exception of the Tusculan onion, but it is sweet to the taste. The schistan and the Ascalonian kinds are used for storing. The schistan onion is left during the winter with the leaves on; in the spring it is stripped of them, upon which offsets make their appearance at the same divisions as the leaves; it is to this circumstance that this variety owes its name. Taking the hint from this fact, it is recommended to strip the other kinds of their leaves, to make them bulb all the better, instead of running to seed. The Ascalonian onion is of a peculiar nature, being barren in some measure in the root; hence it is that the Greeks have recommended it to be reproduced from seed, and not from roots: the transplanting, too, they say, should be done later in the spring, at the time the plant germinates, the result being that it bulbs with all the greater rapidity, and hastens, as it were, to make up for lost time; great dispatch, however, is requisite in taking it up, for when ripe it rots with the greatest rapidity. If propagated…
segments.translation:
This passage, from Pliny's Natural History Book XIX, Chapter 32, concerns the varieties of the onion and related bulbous plants, as well as observations on flowering and leaf-fall in various herbs.

On the subject of flowering: in some plants, flowering begins at the lower parts and proceeds upward, which is why it lasts so long — as is also the case with the plant called heliotropium [Heliotropium Europaeum]. In some plants the flower is white, in others yellow, in others purple. In wild-marjoram and elecampane, the leaves fall first from the upper part, and sometimes in rue as well, when it has been accidentally damaged. In some plants the leaves are hollow, most notably the onion and the scallion [Allium schoenoprasum].

CHAPTER 32. VARIETIES OF THE ONION.

Garlic and onions are invoked by the Egyptians among their deities when taking an oath. The inhabitants of Pelusium in particular were devoted to the worship of the onion; they held it, along with garlic, in great aversion as a food. At Pelusium there was also a temple in which the sea-squill was worshipped.

The Greeks recognize many varieties of the onion: the Sardian, the Samothracian, the Alsidenian, the setanian, the schistan, and the Ascalonian — the last so named from Ascalon, a city of Judaea (and the source of the English word 'scallion'). All of these have a pungent smell that draws tears from the eyes — this is due to the acetic acid contained in the bulb, which acts upon the membranes of the eye — most notably the Cyprian variety, and least of all those from Cnidos. In all of them, the body of the bulb is composed of a cartilaginous, unctuous [fatty/oily] material. The setanian variety is the smallest of all, with the possible exception of the Tusculan onion, but is sweet in taste. The schistan and Ascalonian kinds are used for storing. The schistan onion (its name derived from the Greek σχίζω, meaning 'to divide' or 'tear off') is left through the winter with its leaves on; in the spring the leaves are stripped away, whereupon offsets appear at the same points where the leaves had been — a circumstance from which the variety takes its name. Taking this as a lesson, it is recommended to strip other varieties of their leaves as well, so that they bulb more vigorously rather than going to seed.

The Ascalonian onion is peculiar in nature, being in some measure barren at the root; therefore the Greeks have recommended that it be reproduced from seed rather than from roots. They also advise that transplanting be done later in spring, at the time when the plant is germinating, so that it bulbs all the more rapidly, hastening as it were to make up for lost time. Great speed, however, is required in lifting it, for when ripe it rots exceedingly quickly. If propagated…

segments.title_inferred: On Onions and Leeks: Varieties, Cultivation, and Uses
segments.original_text:
a long stalk and run to seed; in fact, they differ from the Ascalonian kind only in the sweetness of their flavour. Among us there are two principal varieties known of the onion; the scallion, employed for seasonings, is one, known to the Greeks by the name of "gethyon," and by us as the "pallacana;" it is sown in March, April, and May. The other kind is the bulbed or headed onion ["capitata"]; it is sown just after the autumnal equinox, or else after the west winds have begun to prevail. The varieties of this last kind, ranged according to their relative degrees of pungency, are the African onion, the Gallic, the Tusculan, the Ascalonian, and the Amiternian: the roundest in shape are the best. The red onion, too, is more pungent than the white, the stored than the fresh, the raw than the cooked, and the dried than the preserved. The onion of Amiternum is cultivated in cold, humid localities, and is the only one that is reproduced from heads, like garlic, the other kinds being grown from seed. This last kind yields no seed in the ensuing summer, but a bulb only, which dries and keeps; but in the summer after, the contrary is the case, for seed is produced, while the bulb very quickly spoils. Hence it is that every year there are two separate sowings, one of seed for the reproduction of bulbs, and one of bulbs for the growth of seed; these onions keep best in chaff. The scallion has hardly any bulb at all, but a long neck only — hence it is nothing but leaf, and is often cut down, like the leek; for this reason, too, like the leek, it is grown from seed, and not from plants. In addition to these particulars, it is recommended that the ground intended for sowing onions should be turned up three times, care being taken to remove all roots and weeds; ten pounds of seed is the proper proportion for a jugerum. Savory too, they say, should be mixed with them, the onions being all the finer for it; the ground, too, should be stubbed and hoed four times at least, if not oftener. In Italy, the Ascalonian onion is sown in the month of February. The seed of the onion is gathered when it begins to turn black, and before it becomes dry and shrivelled.

CHAP. 33. THE LEEK.

While upon this subject, it will be as well, too, to speak of the leek, on account of the affinity which it bears to the plants just mentioned, and more particularly because cut-leek has recently acquired considerable celebrity from the use made of it by the Emperor Nero. That prince, to improve his voice, used to eat leeks and oil every month, upon stated days, abstaining from every other kind of food, and not touching so much as a morsel of bread even. Leeks are reproduced from seed, sown just after the autumnal equinox; if they are intended for cutting, the seed is sown thicker than otherwise. The leeks in the same bed are cut repeatedly, till it is quite exhausted, and they are always kept well manured.
