…dedicated to Maecenas, that it is not advisable to touch rue, cunila [savory], mint, or ocimum [basil] with any implement of iron. These absurd notions are borrowed from Theophrastus, De Causis, chapter 8. Or, according to some manuscript readings, "limodorum," a parasitical plant, probably the Lathraea phelypea of Sprengel. Fee suggests that this plant may be the Polygonum convolvulus of Linnaeus, or else one of the Cuscutae [dodders], or a variety of Orobanche [broomrape]. "Scabies" [a fungous excrescence]: Fee thinks this is now known as "puccinia" or "uredo" [rust fungi].

CHAPTER 58. THE PROPER REMEDIES FOR THESE MALADIES. HOW ANTS ARE BEST DESTROYED. THE BEST REMEDIES AGAINST CATERPILLARS AND FLIES.

The same author [Columella] recommends, as a remedy against ants—which are by no means the least of the plagues in a garden that is not kept well watered—that the mouths of their holes be stopped up with sea-slime or ashes. But the most efficient way of destroying them is with the aid of the plant heliotropium [Heliotropium europaeum]; some persons, moreover, are of the opinion that water in which an unburnt brick has been soaked is harmful to them. The best protection for turnips is to sow a few fitches [bitter vetches, Vicia ervilia] with them, and for cabbages to sow chick-peas alongside them, as these have the effect of keeping caterpillars away. If, however, this precaution has been omitted and the caterpillars have already appeared, the best remedy is to pour over the vegetables a decoction of wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], or else of house-leek [Sempervivum tectorum], known to some as "aizoum," a herb already mentioned by us. If cabbage-seed, before it is sown, is steeped in the juice of house-leek, the cabbages, it is said, will certainly not be attacked by any insect. It is also said that all caterpillars may be effectually exterminated if the skull of a beast of burden is set up upon a stake in the garden, care being taken to use only that of a female animal. There is also a story that a river crab, hung up in the middle of the garden, is a preservative against caterpillar attacks. Furthermore, there are those who are in the habit of touching with slips of blood-red cornel [Cornus sanguinea] such plants as they wish to keep free from caterpillars. Flies [culices, including both flies and gnats] also infest well-watered gardens, and especially so if there happen to be any shrubs growing there; they may be driven away, however, by burning galbanum [resin of Ferula galbaniflua]. Regarding the deterioration to which seed is subject, there are some seeds that keep better than others—for instance, those of coriander, beet, leeks, cresses, mustard, rocket, cunila [savory], and nearly all of the pungent-flavored plants.

segments.title_inferred: On Plants Benefitted by Salt or Fresh Water, Proper Watering Methods, and the Properties of Cultivated vs. Wild Plants
segments.original_text: CHAP. 59. WHAT PLANTS ARE BENEFITTED BY SALT WATER. A peculiar remedy for the maladies to which radishes, beet, rue, and cunila are subject, is salt water, which has also the additional merit of conducing very materially to their sweetness and fertility. Other plants, again, are equally benefitted by being watered with fresh water, the most desirable for the purpose being that which is the coldest and the sweetest to drink: pond and drain-water, on the other hand, are not so good, as they are apt to carry the seeds of weeds along with them. It is rain, however, that forms the principal aliment of plants; in addition to which, it kills the insects as they develope themselves upon them. CHAP. 60. (12.) THE PROPER METHOD OF WATERING GARDENS. The proper times for watering are the morning and the evening, to prevent the water from being heated by the sun; with the sole exception, however, of ocimum, which requires to be watered at midday; indeed, this plant, it is generally thought, will grow with additional rapidity, if it is watered with boiling water when sown. All plants, when transplanted, grow all the better and larger for it, leeks and turnips more particularly. Transplanting, too, is attended with certain remedial effects, and acts as a preservative to certain plants, such as scallions, for instance, leeks, radishes, parsley, lettuces, rape, and cucumbers. All the wild plants are generally smaller in the leaf and stalk than the cultivated ones, and have more acrid juices, cunila, wild marjoram, and rue, for example. Indeed, it is only the lapathum that is better in a wild state than cultivated: in its cultivated state it is the same plant that is known to us as the "rumix," being the most vigorous by far of all the plants that are grown; so much so, indeed, that it is said that when it has once taken root, it will last for ever, and can never be extirpated from the soil, more particularly if water happens to be near at hand. Its juices, which are employed only in ptisans, as an article of food, have the effect of imparting to them a softer and more exquisite flavour. The wild variety is employed for many medicinal purposes. So true it is, that the careful research of man has omitted nothing, that I have even met with a poem, in which I find it stated, that if pellets of goats' dung, the size of a bean, are hollowed out, and the seed of leeks, rocket, lettuces, parsley, endive, and cresses is inserted in them, and then sown, the plants will thrive in a marvellous degree. Plants in a wild state, it is generally thought, are more dry and acrid than when cultivated. CHAP. 61. THE JUICES AND FLAVOURS OF GARDEN [PLANTS].
segments.translation:
CHAPTER 59. WHAT PLANTS ARE BENEFITTED BY SALT WATER. A particular remedy for the diseases to which radishes, beet, rue, and cunila [savory] are prone is salt water, which also has the additional benefit of greatly contributing to their sweetness and fertility. Other plants are equally benefitted by being watered with fresh water, the most desirable of which is the coldest and sweetest to drink; pond water and drain water, on the other hand, are less suitable, as they tend to carry the seeds of weeds with them. It is rainwater, however, that constitutes the principal nourishment of plants; it also has the effect of killing insects as they develop upon the plants.

CHAPTER 60. THE PROPER METHOD OF WATERING GARDENS. The proper times for watering are morning and evening, to prevent the water from being heated by the sun; the sole exception is ocimum [basil], which requires watering at midday; indeed, this plant is generally thought to grow with greater speed if watered with boiling water at the time of sowing. All plants, when transplanted, grow better and larger for the process, leeks and turnips most especially. Transplanting also carries certain restorative effects and acts as a preservative for certain plants, such as scallions, leeks, radishes, parsley, lettuces, rape, and cucumbers. All wild plants are generally smaller in leaf and stalk than cultivated ones, and possess more acrid juices—cunila [savory], wild marjoram, and rue being examples. Indeed, it is only lapathum [dock] that is superior in its wild state to its cultivated form: in cultivation, it is the same plant known to us as 'rumix' [sorrel/dock], being by far the most vigorous of all cultivated plants; so much so that it is said once it has taken root it will persist forever and can never be eradicated from the soil, especially when water is close at hand. Its juices, used solely in ptisans [barley-gruel decoctions] as a foodstuff, impart to them a softer and more refined flavour. The wild variety is employed for many medicinal purposes. So thoroughly has human inquiry left nothing unexamined, that I have even encountered a poem stating that if pellets of goats' dung the size of a bean are hollowed out, and seeds of leeks, rocket, lettuces, parsley, endive, and cresses are placed inside them and then sown, the plants will thrive to a marvellous degree. Plants in a wild state are generally considered to be more dry and acrid than those that are cultivated.

CHAPTER 61. THE JUICES AND FLAVOURS OF GARDEN [PLANTS].

segments.title_inferred: On Piperitis, Libanotis, and Smyrnium; Flavours of Plants; Transition to Medicinal Properties

the large leaves which characterize the varieties of dock. Dishes made of rice or barley. See B. xviii. c. 13. See B. xx. c. 85. Chap. 62. PIPERITIS, LIBANOTIS, AND SMYRNIUM.

taury, bitter; in cucumbers, gourds, and lettuces, watery; and in parsley, anise, and fennel, pungent and odoriferous. The salt flavour is the only one that is not to be found in plants, with the sole exception, indeed, of the chicheling vetch, though even then it is to be found on the exterior surface only of the plant, in the form of a kind of dust which settles there.

Chap. 62. Piperitis, Libanotis, and Smyrnium. To come to a full understanding, too, both here as elsewhere, how unfounded are the notions which are generally entertained, I shall take this opportunity of remarking that panax has the flavour of pepper, and siliquastrum even more so, a circumstance to which it owes its name of piperitis: libanotis, again, has just the odour of frankincense, and smyrnium of myrrh. As to panax, we have spoken of it at sufficient length already. Libanotis grows in a thin, crumbly soil, and is generally sown in spots exposed to the falling dews; the root, which is just like that of olusatrum, has a smell in no way differing from that of frankincense; when a year old, it is extremely wholesome for the stomach; some persons give it the name of rosmarinum. Smyrnium is a garden herb that grows in similar soils, and has a root which smells like myrrh: siliquastrum, too, is grown in a similar manner. Other plants, again, differ from the preceding ones, both in smell and taste, anise for example; indeed, so great is the difference in this respect, and in their relative virtues, that not only are the properties of each modified by the other, but quite neutralized even. It is in this way that our cooks correct the flavour of vinegar in their dishes with parsley, and our butlers employ the same plant, enclosed in sachets, for removing a bad odour in wine.

Thus far, then, we have treated of the garden plants, viewed as articles of food only; it remains for us now (for up to the present we have only spoken of their various methods of cultivation, with some succinct details relative thereto), to enlarge upon the more elaborate operations of Nature in this respect; it being quite impossible to come to a full understanding as to the true characteristics of each individual plant, without a knowledge of its medicinal effects, a sublime and truly mysterious manifestation of the wisdom of the Deity, than which nothing can possibly be found of a nature more elevated. It is upon principle that we have thought proper not to enlarge upon the medicinal properties of each plant when treating of
segments.translation:
Chapter 62. Piperitis, Libanotis, and Smyrnium.

[Continuation of the discussion of flavours in plants:] centaury [taury] is bitter; cucumbers, gourds, and lettuces are watery; parsley, anise, and fennel are pungent and odoriferous. The salt flavour is the only one not found in plants, with the sole exception of the chicheling vetch [Lathyrus sativus], and even there it is found only on the exterior surface of the plant, in the form of a kind of dust that settles upon it.

In order to arrive at a full understanding here, as elsewhere, of how unfounded the notions commonly held tend to be, I shall take this opportunity to remark that panax has the flavour of pepper, and siliquastrum [pepper-wort] even more so — a circumstance to which it owes its name piperitis ["pepper-plant"]. Libanotis, for its part, has precisely the odour of frankincense, and smyrnium that of myrrh. Regarding panax, we have already spoken of it at sufficient length. Libanotis grows in thin, crumbly soil, and is generally sown in spots exposed to the falling dews; its root, which closely resembles that of olusatrum [alexanders], has a smell in no way different from that of frankincense; when one year old, it is extremely beneficial to the stomach. Some give it the name rosmarinum [rosemary, or "sea-dew"]. Smyrnium is a garden herb that grows in similar soils and has a root smelling of myrrh; siliquastrum likewise is cultivated in a similar manner. Still other plants differ from the foregoing both in smell and taste — anise, for example — and so great is the difference in this regard, and in their relative virtues, that not only are the properties of each modified by the other, but they are even entirely neutralized. It is in this way that cooks correct the sharpness of vinegar in their dishes by means of parsley, and that household stewards [butlers] use the same plant, enclosed in small bags [sachets], to remove an unpleasant odour from wine.

Thus far we have treated of garden plants considered solely as articles of food. It now remains for us — since up to this point we have spoken only of the various methods of their cultivation and some brief details connected therewith — to enlarge upon the more elaborate workings of Nature in this domain; for it is quite impossible to arrive at a full understanding of the true characteristics of each individual plant without a knowledge of its medicinal effects, which represent a sublime and truly mysterious manifestation of divine wisdom, beyond which nothing of a more exalted nature can be found. It is on principle that we have judged it proper not to treat of the medicinal properties of each plant when discussing [its cultivation and use as food] ...

segments.title_inferred: The Wild Cucumber and the Preparation of Elaterium: Twenty-Six Remedies
segments.original_text:
descending from the contemplation of a loftier sphere, that the loadstone possesses the property of attracting iron, and another stone, again, that of repelling it; and that the diamond, that pride of luxury and opulence, though infrangible by every other object, and presenting a resistance that cannot be overcome, is broken asunder by a he-goat's blood — in addition to numerous other marvels of which we shall have to speak on more appropriate occasions, equal to this or still more wonderful even. My only request is that pardon may be accorded me for beginning with objects of a more humble nature, though still so greatly conducive to our health — I mean the garden plants, of which I shall now proceed to speak.

CHAP. 2. (1.) THE WILD CUCUMBER; TWENTY-SIX REMEDIES.

We have already stated that there is a wild cucumber, considerably smaller than the cultivated one. From this cucumber the medicament known as 'elaterium' is prepared, being the juice extracted from the seed. To obtain this juice the fruit is cut before it is ripe — indeed, if this precaution is not taken at an early period, the seed is apt to spirt out and be productive of danger to the eyes. After it is gathered, the fruit is kept whole for a night, and on the following day an incision is made in it with a reed. The seed, too, is generally sprinkled with ashes, with the view of retaining in it as large a quantity of the juice as possible. When the juice is extracted, it is received in rain water, where it falls to the bottom; after which it is thickened in the sun, and then divided into lozenges.
segments.translation:
Descending from the contemplation of a loftier sphere: the loadstone [magnet] possesses the property of attracting iron, while another stone [the theamedes] has the property of repelling it; and the diamond, that pride of luxury and opulence, though unbreakable by every other means and presenting a resistance that cannot be overcome, is nonetheless shattered by the blood of a he-goat — in addition to numerous other marvels of which we shall have occasion to speak more appropriately elsewhere, marvels equal to these or still more wonderful. My only request is that pardon may be granted me for beginning with objects of a more humble nature, though still greatly conducive to our health — I mean the garden plants, of which I shall now proceed to speak.

CHAPTER 2. (1.) THE WILD CUCUMBER; TWENTY-SIX REMEDIES.

We have already stated that there exists a wild cucumber, considerably smaller than the cultivated one. From this cucumber the medicament known as 'elaterium' [elaterium] is prepared, being the juice extracted from the seed. To obtain this juice, the fruit is cut before it is ripe — indeed, if this precaution is not observed early enough, the seed is apt to spurt out and cause danger to the eyes. After being gathered, the fruit is kept whole overnight, and on the following day an incision is made in it with a reed. The seed is also generally sprinkled with ashes, with the aim of retaining as large a quantity of juice within it as possible. When the juice is extracted, it is collected in rain water, where it settles to the bottom; after which it is thickened in the sun and then divided into lozenges.

segments.title_inferred: Wild Cucumber, Elaterium, and Their Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text: wild cucumber, boiled in vinegar, is employed in fomentations for the gout, and the juice of it is used as a remedy for tooth-ache. Dried and mixed with resin, the root is a cure for impetigo and the skin diseases known as "psora" and "lichen:" it is good, too, for imposthumes of the parotid glands and inflammatory tumours, and restores the natural colour to the skin when a cicatrix has formed. — The juice of the leaves, mixed with vinegar, is used as an injection for the ears, in cases of deafness. CHAP. 3. ELATERIUM; TWENTY-SEVEN REMEDIES. The proper season for making elaterium is the autumn; and there is no medicament known that will keep longer than this. It begins to be fit for use when three years old; but if it is found desirable to make use of it at an earlier period than this, the acridity of the lozenges may be modified by putting them with vinegar upon a slow fire, in a new earthen pot. The older it is the better, and before now, as we learn from Theophrastus, it has been known to keep so long as two hundred years. Even after it has been kept so long as fifty years, it retains its property of extinguishing a light; indeed, it is the proper way of testing the genuineness of the drug to hold it to the flame and make it scintillate above and below, before finally extinguishing it. The elaterium which is pale, smooth, and slightly bitter, is superior to that which has a grass-green appearance and is rough to the touch. It is generally thought that the seed of this plant will facilitate conception if a woman carries it attached to her person, before it has touched the ground; and that it has the effect of aiding parturition, if it is first wrapped in ram's wool, and then tied round the woman's loins, without her knowing it, care being taken to carry it out of the house the instant she is delivered. Those persons who magnify the praises of the wild cucumber say that the very best is that of Arabia, the next being that of Arcadia, and then that of Cyrenae: it bears a resemblance to the heliotropium, they say, and the fruit, about the size of a walnut, grows between the leaves and branches. The seed, it is said, is very similar in appearance to the tail of a scorpion thrown back, but is of a whitish hue. Indeed, there are some persons who give to this cucumber the name of "scorpionium," and say that its seed, as well as the elaterium, is remarkably efficacious as a cure for the sting of the scorpion. As a pu[rgative — text breaks off]
segments.translation:
Wild cucumber, boiled in vinegar, is employed in fomentations [warm topical applications] for gout, and its juice is used as a remedy for toothache. When dried and mixed with resin, the root is a cure for impetigo [a contagious skin infection] and the skin diseases known as 'psora' [itch-scab] and 'lichen' [a scabrous skin condition resembling moss or lichen in appearance]. It is also beneficial for abscesses [imposthumes] of the parotid glands and inflammatory tumours [pani], and it restores the natural colour of the skin after a scar [cicatrix] has formed. The juice of the leaves, mixed with vinegar, is used as an ear injection in cases of deafness.

CHAPTER 3. ELATERIUM; TWENTY-SEVEN REMEDIES.

The proper season for preparing elaterium is autumn, and no medicament is known to keep longer than this. It begins to be fit for use at three years of age; but if one wishes to use it sooner, the acridity of the lozenges may be reduced by heating them slowly with vinegar in a new earthen pot over a low fire. The older it is, the better — as we learn from Theophrastus, it has been known to keep for as long as two hundred years. Even after fifty years of storage it retains its property of extinguishing a flame; indeed, the proper method of testing the genuineness of the drug is to hold it to a flame and make it scintillate above and below before it finally extinguishes the light. Elaterium that is pale, smooth, and slightly bitter is superior to that which appears grass-green and is rough to the touch.

It is generally believed that the seed of this plant will facilitate conception if a woman carries it on her person before it has touched the ground, and that it aids parturition [childbirth] if first wrapped in ram's wool and tied around the woman's loins without her knowledge — care being taken to remove it from the house the instant she has been delivered.

Those who speak most highly of the wild cucumber say that the best variety comes from Arabia, followed by that of Arcadia, and then that of Cyrene. It is said to resemble the heliotropium, and the fruit — about the size of a walnut — grows between the leaves and branches. The seed is said to closely resemble the curled-back tail of a scorpion, but is of a whitish colour. Some persons call this cucumber 'scorpionium,' and maintain that both its seed and the elaterium prepared from it are remarkably effective as a cure for scorpion stings. As a pu[rgative — text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Anguine/Erratic Cucumber, Cultivated Cucumber, and Pepones: Medicinal Remedies (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 4–6)
segments.original_text: the cure of quinsy and affections of the trachea. CHAP. 4. (2.) THE ANGUINE OR ERRATIC CUCUMBER: FIVE REMEDIES. Many authors are of opinion that the wild cucumber is identical with the plant known among us as the "anguine," and by some persons as the "erratic" cucumber. Objects sprinkled with a decoction of this plant will never be touched by mice. The same authors say, too, that a decoction of it in vinegar, externally applied, gives instantaneous relief in cases of gout and diseases of the joints. As a remedy, too, for lumbago, the seed of it is dried in the sun and pounded, being given in doses of twenty denarii to half a sextarius of water. Mixed with woman's milk and applied as a liniment, it is a cure for tumours which have suddenly formed. Elaterium promotes the menstrual discharge; but if taken by females when pregnant, it is productive of abortion. It is good, also, for asthma, and, injected into the nostrils, for the jaundice. Rubbed upon the face in the sun, it removes freckles [lentigo] and spots upon the skin. CHAP. 5. — THE CULTIVATED CUCUMBER: NINE REMEDIES. Many persons attribute all these properties to the cultivated cucumber as well, a plant which even without them would be of very considerable importance, in a medicinal point of view. A pinch of the seed, for instance, in three fingers, beaten up with cummin and taken in wine, is extremely beneficial for a cough: for phrenitis, also, doses of it are administered in woman's milk, and doses of one acetabulum for dysentery. As a remedy for purulent expectorations, it is taken with an equal quantity of cummin; and it is used with hydromel for diseases of the liver. Taken in sweet wine, it is a diuretic; and, in combination with cummin, it is used as an injection for affections of the kidneys. CHAP. 6. PEPONES: ELEVEN REMEDIES. The fruit known as pepones are a cool and refreshing diet, and are slightly relaxing to the stomach. Applications are used of the pulpy flesh in defluxions or pains of the eyes. The root, too, of this plant cures the hard ulcers known to
segments.translation:
— for the cure of quinsy [angina] and affections of the trachea.

CHAPTER 4. (2.) THE ANGUINE OR ERRATIC CUCUMBER: FIVE REMEDIES.

Many authors are of the opinion that the wild cucumber is identical with the plant known among us as the 'anguine,' and by some persons as the 'erratic' cucumber. Objects sprinkled with a decoction of this plant will never be touched by mice. The same authors also state that a decoction of it in vinegar, applied externally, gives instantaneous relief in cases of gout and diseases of the joints. As a remedy for lumbago, the seed is dried in the sun and pounded, then given in doses of twenty denarii [by weight] to half a sextarius of water. Mixed with woman's milk and applied as a liniment, it cures tumours that have formed suddenly. Elaterium [the dried juice of the wild cucumber] promotes the menstrual discharge; but if taken by pregnant women, it causes abortion. It is also beneficial for asthma, and, when injected into the nostrils, for jaundice [morbus regius, literally 'the royal disease']. Rubbed upon the face in the sun, it removes freckles [lentigo] and blemishes of the skin.

CHAPTER 5. THE CULTIVATED CUCUMBER: NINE REMEDIES.

Many persons attribute all these same properties to the cultivated cucumber as well, a plant which, even setting these aside, would be of very considerable medicinal importance. A pinch of the seed taken in three fingers, beaten up with cummin and taken in wine, is extremely beneficial for a cough. For phrenitis [inflammation of the mind/brain fever], doses are administered in woman's milk; and doses of one acetabulum are given for dysentery. As a remedy for purulent expectorations [suppurative cough], it is taken with an equal quantity of cummin; and it is used with hydromel [honey-water] for diseases of the liver. Taken in sweet wine, it acts as a diuretic; and, in combination with cummin, it is used as an injection [enema or instillation] for affections of the kidneys.

CHAPTER 6. PEPONES: ELEVEN REMEDIES.

The fruit known as pepones [a type of large melon or gourd] make a cool and refreshing food, and are mildly relaxing to the stomach. Applications of the pulpy flesh are used for defluxions [discharges/inflammations] or pains of the eyes. The root of this plant also cures the hard ulcers known as — [text breaks off].

segments.title_inferred: Remedies from Cucumbers, Gourds, Somphos, and Colocynthis (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 6–8)
segments.original_text:
a result which is equally produced by an external application of the leaves of all the cultivated cucumbers. These leaves, mixed with honey, are employed for the cure of the pustules known as "epinyctis;" steeped in wine, they are good, too, for the bites of dogs and of multipedes, insects known to the Greeks by the name of "seps," of an elongated form, with hairy legs, and noxious to cattle more particularly; the sting being followed by swelling, and the wound rapidly putrifying. The smell of the cucumber itself is a restorative in fainting fits. It is a well-known fact, that if cucumbers are peeled and then boiled in oil, vinegar, and honey, they are all the more pleasant eating for it.

CHAP. 7. (3.) — THE GOURD: SEVENTEEN REMEDIES. THE SOMPHOS: ONE REMEDY.
There is found also a wild gourd, called "somphos" by the Greeks, empty within (to which circumstance it owes its name), and long and thick in shape, like the finger: it grows nowhere except upon stony spots. The juice of this gourd, when chewed, is very beneficial to the stomach.

CHAP. 8. — THE COLOCYNTHIS: TEN REMEDIES.
There is another variety of the wild gourd, known as the "colocynthis:" this kind is full of seeds, but not so large as the cultivated one. The pale colocynthis is better than those of a grass-green colour. Employed by itself when dried, it acts as a very powerful purgative; used as an injection, it is a remedy for all diseases of the intestines, the kidneys, and the loins, as well as for paralysis. The seed being first removed, it is boiled down in hydromel to one half; after which it is used as an injection, with perfect safety, in doses of four oboli. It is good, too, for the stomach, taken in pills composed of the dried powder and boiled honey. In jaundice seven seeds of it may be taken with beneficial effects, with a draught of hydromel immediately after. The pulp of this fruit, taken with wormwood and salt, is a remedy for toothache, and the juice of it, warmed with vinegar, has the effect of strengthening loose teeth. Rubbed in with oil, it removes pains of the spine, loins, and hips: in addition to which, really a marvellous thing to speak of! the seeds of it, in even numbers, attached to the body in a linen cloth, will cure, it is said, the fevers to which the Greeks have given the name of "periodic." The juice, too, of the cultivated [colocynthis…]
segments.translation:
The same result [as previously described remedies] is equally produced by the external application of the leaves of all cultivated cucumbers. These leaves, mixed with honey, are used to cure the pustules called 'epinyctis' [nocturnal pustules: red or whitish skin eruptions accompanied by sharp pain, appearing at night and disappearing by day]. Steeped in wine, they are also beneficial against the bites of dogs and of multipedes [many-legged creatures], insects known to the Greeks as 'seps' [from the Greek σήπειν, 'to rot'], which are elongated in form, with hairy legs, and are particularly harmful to cattle; their sting is followed by swelling, and the wound rapidly putrefies. The smell of the cucumber itself serves as a restorative [remedy] in cases of fainting. It is well known that cucumbers, if peeled and boiled in oil, vinegar, and honey, become all the more pleasant to eat for having undergone this preparation.

CHAPTER 7 (3). — THE GOURD: SEVENTEEN REMEDIES. THE SOMPHOS: ONE REMEDY.
There is also a wild gourd called 'somphos' by the Greeks, hollow within — a property to which it owes its name [from the Greek σομφός, meaning porous, spongy, or hollow] — and long and thick in shape, resembling a finger. It grows only on rocky ground. The juice of this gourd, when chewed, is very beneficial to the stomach.

CHAPTER 8. — THE COLOCYNTHIS: TEN REMEDIES.
There is another variety of wild gourd known as the 'colocynthis' [bitter apple / bitter gourd]: this type is full of seeds but is not as large as the cultivated gourd. The pale colocynthis is superior to those of a grass-green colour. When dried and used alone, it acts as a very powerful purgative. Administered as an enema [injection], it is a remedy for all diseases of the intestines, the kidneys, and the loins, as well as for paralysis. The seeds being first removed, it is boiled down in hydromel [honey-water] to half its volume, after which it may be administered as an enema quite safely in doses of four oboli. It is also beneficial for the stomach when taken in pills made of the dried powder and boiled honey. In cases of jaundice, seven seeds of it may be taken with good effect, followed immediately by a draught of hydromel. The pulp of this fruit, taken with wormwood and salt, is a remedy for toothache; and its juice, warmed with vinegar, has the effect of strengthening loose teeth. When rubbed in with oil, it relieves pains of the spine, loins, and hips. Furthermore — truly a marvellous thing to relate! — the seeds of it, taken in even numbers and bound to the body in a linen cloth, will cure, it is said, those fevers the Greeks call 'periodic' [i.e., intermittent or recurring fevers, such as quartan or tertian fever]. The juice, too, of the cultivated [colocynthis…]

segments.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Gourd, Rape, and Wild Rape (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 8–10)
segments.original_text:
or Coloquintida, so remarkable for its bitterness. It is an extremely drastic, and indeed violent purgative. Recurring at stated times. The absurdity of this statement does not require discussion. gourd shred in pieces, applied warm, is good for ear-ache, and the flesh of the inside, used without the seed, for corns on the feet and the suppurations known to the Greeks as "apostemata." When the pulp and seeds are boiled together, the decoction is good for strengthening loose teeth, and for preventing toothache; wine, too, boiled with this plant, is curative of defluxions of the eyes. The leaves of it, bruised with fresh cypress-leaves, or the leaves alone, boiled in a vessel of potters' clay and beaten up with goose-grease, and then applied to the part affected, are an excellent cure for wounds. Fresh shavings of the rind are used as a cooling application for gout, and burning pains in the head, in infants more particularly; they are good, too, for erysipelas, whether it is the shavings of the rind or the seeds of the plant that are applied to the part affected. The juice of the scrapings, employed as a liniment with rose-oil and vinegar, moderates the burning heats of fevers; and the ashes of the dried fruit applied to burns are efficacious in a most remarkable degree. Chrysippus, the physician, condemned the use of the gourd as a food: it is generally agreed, however, that it is extremely good for the stomach, and for ulcerations of the intestines and of the bladder.

CHAP. 9. RAPE; NINE REMEDIES.

Rape, too, has its medicinal properties. Warmed, it is used as an application for the cure of chilblains, in addition to which, it has the effect of protecting the feet from cold. A hot decoction of rape is employed for the cure of cold gout; and raw rape, beaten up with salt, is good for all maladies of the feet. Rape-seed, used as a liniment, and taken in drink, with wine, is said to have a salutary effect against the stings of serpents, and various narcotic poisons; and there are many persons who attribute to it the properties of an antidote, when taken with wine and oil. Democritus has entirely repudiated the use of rape as an article of food, in consequence of the flatulence which it produces; while Diocles, on the other hand, has greatly extolled it, and has even gone so far as to say that it acts as an aphrodisiac. Dionysius, too, says the same of rape, and more particularly if it is seasoned with rocket; he adds, also, that roasted, and then applied with grease, it is excellent for pains in the joints.

CHAP. 10. WILD RAPE; ONE REMEDY.

Wild rape is mostly found growing in the fields; it has a tufted top, with a white seed, twice as large as that of the poppy. This plant is often employed
segments.translation:
or Coloquintida [Citrullus colocynthis], so remarkable for its bitterness. It is an extremely drastic, and indeed violent purgative. [The text claims it acts at recurring, fixed intervals — the absurdity of this statement requires no discussion.]

The cultivated gourd, shred in pieces and applied warm, is good for ear-ache; the flesh of the interior, used without the seed, is good for corns on the feet and for the suppurations the Greeks call "apostemata" [aposthumes, i.e., abscesses]. When the pulp and seeds are boiled together, the resulting decoction is good for strengthening loose teeth and for preventing toothache; wine also, boiled with this plant, is curative of discharges [defluxions] from the eyes. The leaves, bruised together with fresh cypress-leaves — or the leaves alone, boiled in an earthenware vessel and beaten up with goose-grease — and then applied to the affected part, are an excellent cure for wounds. Fresh shavings of the rind serve as a cooling application for gout and for burning pains in the head, especially in infants; they are also good for erysipelas [ignis sacer, "sacred fire," also called St. Anthony's fire], whether it is the shavings of the rind or the seeds that are applied to the affected area. The juice of the scrapings, used as a liniment together with rose-oil and vinegar, moderates the burning heat of fevers; and the ashes of the dried fruit applied to burns are efficacious to a most remarkable degree. Chrysippus the physician condemned the use of the gourd as a food; it is generally agreed, however, that it is extremely beneficial to the stomach and for ulcerations of the intestines and bladder.

CHAPTER 9. RAPE; NINE REMEDIES.

Rape likewise has its medicinal properties. Warmed, it is applied as a remedy for chilblains, and it also has the effect of protecting the feet from cold. A hot decoction of rape is used for the cure of cold gout, and raw rape beaten up with salt is good for all ailments of the feet. Rape-seed, employed as a liniment and also taken as a drink in wine, is said to be beneficial against the stings of serpents and various narcotic poisons; and many persons attribute to it the qualities of an antidote when taken with wine and oil. Democritus entirely repudiated the use of rape as food on account of the flatulence it produces; Diocles, on the other hand, greatly extolled it and went so far as to say that it acts as an aphrodisiac. Dionysius likewise says the same of rape, and more particularly when it is seasoned with rocket [arugula, Eruca sativa]; he adds, furthermore, that rape roasted and then applied with grease is excellent for pains in the joints.

CHAPTER 10. WILD RAPE; ONE REMEDY.

Wild rape is found growing mostly in the fields; it has a tufted head and a white seed twice as large as that of the poppy. This plant is often employed [text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Medicinal Properties of Turnip, Bunion, Bunias, Wild Radish (Armoracia), and Cultivated Radish
segments.original_text:
that of anise, and known by them as "bunion," is good for promoting the menstrual discharge in females and for affections of the bladder; it acts, also, as a diuretic. For these purposes, a decoction of it is taken with hydromel, or else one drachma of the juice of the plant. The seed, parched, and then beaten up, and taken in warm water, in doses of four cyathi, is a good remedy for dysentery; it will stop the passage of the urine, however, if linseed is not taken with it. The other kind of turnip is known by the name of "bunias," and bears a considerable resemblance to the radish and the rape united, the seed of it enjoying the reputation of being a remedy for poisons; hence it is that we find it employed in antidotes.

CHAP. 12. THE WILD RADISH, OR ARMORACIA: ONE REMEDY.
We have already said that there is also a wild radish. The most esteemed is that of Arcadia, though it is also found growing in other countries as well. It is only efficacious as a diuretic, being in other respects of a heating nature. In Italy, it is known also by the name of "armoracia."

CHAP. 13. THE CULTIVATED RADISH: FORTY-THREE REMEDIES.
The cultivated radish, too, in addition to what we have already said of it, purges the stomach, attenuates the phlegm, acts as a diuretic, and detaches the bilious secretions. A decoction of the rind of radishes in wine, taken in the morning in doses of three cyathi, has the effect of breaking and expelling calculi of the bladder. A decoction, too, of this rind in vinegar and water, is employed as a liniment for the stings of serpents. Taken fasting in the morning with honey, radishes are good for a cough. Parched radish-seed, as well as...
segments.translation:
...and known by them as 'bunion' [Latin: bunion; possibly Bunium pumilum or related umbellifera]. It is good for promoting the menstrual discharge in women and for disorders of the bladder; it also acts as a diuretic. For these purposes, a decoction of it is taken with hydromel [honey-water], or alternatively one drachma of the plant's juice is administered. The seed, when parched and then ground up and taken in warm water in doses of four cyathi [approximately 180 ml], is a good remedy for dysentery; it will, however, suppress the passage of urine unless linseed is taken alongside it.

The other variety of turnip is known by the name 'bunias' [Brassica napo-brassica of Linnaeus], and closely resembles a cross between the radish and the rape. Its seed has the reputation of being an antidote to poisons, and for this reason it is incorporated into antidote preparations.

CHAPTER 12. THE WILD RADISH, OR ARMORACIA: ONE REMEDY.
As we have already noted, there is also a wild radish [Cochlearia Armoracia of Linnaeus; modern horseradish]. The most highly regarded variety comes from Arcadia, though it is found growing in other regions as well. It is effective only as a diuretic; in other respects it is of a heating nature. In Italy, it is also known by the name 'armoracia.'

CHAPTER 13. THE CULTIVATED RADISH: FORTY-THREE REMEDIES.
The cultivated radish, in addition to what has already been said of it, purges the stomach, thins the phlegm, acts as a diuretic, and promotes the discharge of bilious secretions. A decoction of the rind of radishes in wine, taken in the morning in doses of three cyathi [approximately 135 ml], has the effect of breaking up and expelling bladder stones [calculi]. A decoction of this same rind in vinegar and water is employed as a topical liniment for serpent stings. Taken on an empty stomach in the morning with honey, radishes are beneficial for a cough. Parched radish seed, as well as...

segments.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of the Radish (Raphanus sativus) from Pliny's Natural History
segments.original_text: remedy for phthiriasis. Pounded radishes, too, are employed as a liniment for inflammations under the skin, and the rind, mixed with honey, for bruises of recent date. Lethargic persons are recommended to eat them as hot as possible, and the seed, parched and then pounded with honey, will give relief to asthmatic patients. Radishes, too, are useful as a remedy for poisons, and are employed to counteract the effects of the sting of the cerastes and the scorpion: indeed, after having rubbed the hands with radishes or radish-seed, we may handle those reptiles with impunity. If a radish is placed upon a scorpion, it will cause its death. Radishes are useful, too, in cases of poisoning by fungi or henbane; and according to Nicander, they are salutary against the effects of bullock's blood, when drunk. The two physicians of the name of Apollodorus prescribe radishes to be given in cases of poisoning by mistletoe; but whereas Apollodorus of Citium recommends radish-seed pounded in water, Apollodorus of Tarentum speaks of the juice. Radishes diminish the volume of the spleen, and are beneficial for maladies of the liver and pains in the loins: taken, too, with vinegar or mustard, they are good for dropsy and lethargy, as well as epilepsy and melancholy. Praxagoras recommends that radishes should be given for the iliac passion, and Plistonicus for the coeliac disease. Radishes are good, too, for curing ulcerations of the intestines and suppurations of the thoracic organs, if eaten with honey. Some persons say, however, that for this purpose they should be boiled in earth and water; a decoction which, according to them, promotes the menstrual discharge. Taken with vinegar or honey, radishes expel worms from the intestines; and a decoction of them boiled down to one-third, taken in wine, is good for intestinal hernia. Employed in this way, too, they have the effect of drawing off the superfluous blood. Medius recommends them to be given boiled to persons troubled with spitting of blood, and to women who are suckling, for the purpose of increasing the milk. Hippocrates recommends females whose hair falls off, to rub the head with radishes, and he says that for pains of the uterus, they should be applied to the navel.
segments.translation: Radishes are cited as a remedy for phthiriasis [louse infestation]. Pounded radishes are employed as a liniment for phlegmonic inflammations [subcutaneous inflammatory swellings characterized by heat, redness, swelling, and pain], and the rind, mixed with honey, is used for fresh bruises. Lethargic [veternosus: a state of somnolence regarded as a prelude to apoplexy] persons are recommended to eat them as hot as possible, and the seed, parched and then pounded with honey, is said to give relief to asthmatic patients. Radishes are also useful as a remedy against poisons, and are employed to counteract the effects of the sting of the cerastes [Coluber cerastes, the horned viper] and the scorpion; indeed, after rubbing the hands with radishes or radish-seed, one may handle those reptiles with impunity. If a radish is placed upon a scorpion, it is said to cause its death. Radishes are useful, too, in cases of poisoning by fungi or henbane; and according to Nicander, they are beneficial against the effects of drinking bullock's blood. [Note: Nicander in his Alexipharmaca actually attributes these properties to cabbage, not radish; Pliny may have confused the Greek words for cabbage (raphanos) and radish (raphanis).] The two physicians named Apollodorus prescribe radishes in cases of poisoning by mistletoe: Apollodorus of Citium recommends radish-seed pounded in water, while Apollodorus of Tarentum recommends the juice. Radishes diminish the volume of the spleen, and are beneficial for liver ailments and pains in the loins; taken with vinegar or mustard, they are good for dropsy and lethargy, as well as epilepsy and melancholy. Praxagoras recommends them for the iliac passion [intestinal obstruction/colic], and Plistonicus for coeliac disease. Radishes are also good for curing ulcerations of the intestines and suppurations of the thoracic organs, if eaten with honey. Some hold that for this purpose they should be boiled in earth and water, a decoction which is also said to promote the menstrual discharge. Taken with vinegar or honey, radishes expel intestinal worms; and a decoction reduced to one-third, taken in wine, is good for intestinal hernia. Used in this way they also have the effect of drawing off superfluous blood. Medius recommends giving them boiled to persons with spitting of blood, and to nursing women to increase milk production. Hippocrates recommends that women suffering from hair loss rub the head with radishes, and states that for pains of the uterus, radishes should be applied to the navel.

segments.original_text:

Epilepsy it is said, was so called because, if any person was seized with it at the 'Comitia,' or public assemblies of the Roman people, it was the custom to adjourn the meeting to another day. From μέλας, 'black,' and χολή, 'bile.' Melancholy, or bad spirits, was so called from a notion that it was owing to a predominance of an imaginary secretion called by the ancients 'black bile.' The coeliac flux, Fee says, is symptomatic of chronic enteritis; and is a species of diarrhoea, in which the chyme is voided without undergoing any change in passing through the intestines. 'Praecordiorum.' 'Enterocele.' De Morb. Mulier. B. ii. c. 67. Eating or corroding ulcers. Hippocrates, De Diaeta, B. ii. cc. 25, 26, says that radishes are of a cold, and hyssop of a warm, nature. PLINY'S NATURAL HISTORY. [Book XX] dulness of hearing, too, radish-juice is injected into the ear. To promote vomiting, it is extremely beneficial to eat radishes fasting.

CHAP. 14. THE PARSNIP: FIVE REMEDIES. THE HIBISCUM, WILD MALLOW, OR PLISTOLOCHIA: ELEVEN REMEDIES.

The hibiscum, by some persons known as the wild mallow, and by others as the 'plistolochia,' bears a strong resemblance to the parsnip; it is good for ulcerations of the cartilages, and is employed for the cure of fractured bones. The leaves of it, taken in water, relax the stomach; they have the effect, also, of keeping away serpents, and, employed as a liniment, are a cure for the stings of bees, wasps, and hornets. The root, pulled up before sunrise, and wrapped in wool of the colour known as 'native,' taken from a sheep which has just dropped a ewe lamb, is employed as a bandage for scrofulous swellings, even after they have suppurated. Some persons are of opinion, that for this purpose the root should be dug up with an implement of gold, and that care should be taken not to let it touch the ground. Celsus, too, recommends this root to be boiled in wine, and applied in cases of gout unattended with swelling.

CHAP. 15. (5.) — THE STAPHYLINOS, OR WILD PARSNIP: TWENTY-TWO REMEDIES.

The staphylinos, or, as some persons call it, 'erratic parsnip,' is another kind. The seed of this plant, pounded and taken in wine, reduces swelling of the abdomen, and alleviates hysterical suffocations and pains, to such a degree as to restore the uterus to its natural condition. Used as a liniment, also, with raisin wine, it is good for pains of the bowels in females; for men, too, beaten up with an equal proportion of bread, and taken in wine, it may be found beneficial for similar pains. It is a diuretic also, and it will arrest the progress of phagedaenic ulcers, if applied fresh with honey, or else dried and sprinkled on them with meal. Dietiches recommends the root of it to be given, with hydromel, for affections of the liver and spleen, as also the sides, loins, and kidneys.
segments.translation:
There is an antipathy between these two plants [radish and hyssop]. 'Morbus comitialis' — literally the 'comitial disease' — is epilepsy; it was so called because, if anyone was seized with it at the Comitia [the public assemblies of the Roman people], it was customary to adjourn the meeting to another day. [Melancholia derives] from the Greek μέλας ('black') and χολή ('bile'): melancholy, or low spirits, was so named from the belief that it resulted from a predominance of an imaginary secretion the ancients called 'black bile.' The coeliac flux, according to Fee, is symptomatic of chronic enteritis, and is a species of diarrhoea in which the chyme [partially digested food] is voided without undergoing any change in passing through the intestines. [References to praecordiorum (the region about the heart/diaphragm), enterocele (intestinal hernia), De Morb. Mulier. Book II, ch. 67, and corroding/eating ulcers are noted.] Hippocrates, in De Diaeta, Book II, chapters 25–26, states that radishes are of a cold nature and hyssop of a warm nature. Furthermore, for dullness of hearing, radish-juice is injected into the ear. To promote vomiting, it is extremely beneficial to eat radishes on an empty stomach.

CHAPTER 14. THE PARSNIP: FIVE REMEDIES. THE HIBISCUM, WILD MALLOW, OR PLISTOLOCHIA: ELEVEN REMEDIES.

The hibiscum — known to some as the wild mallow [moloche agria] and to others as the 'plistolochia' — closely resembles the parsnip. It is beneficial for ulcerations of the cartilages and is used in the treatment of fractured bones. Its leaves, taken in water, relax the stomach; they also have the property of repelling serpents, and when applied as a liniment, they cure the stings of bees, wasps, and hornets. The root, pulled up before sunrise and wrapped in undyed wool ('native' coloured) taken from a sheep that has just given birth to a ewe lamb, is applied as a bandage to scrofulous [tuberculosis of the lymph nodes] swellings, even after these have suppurated [discharged pus]. Some hold that for this purpose the root must be dug up with a golden implement and care must be taken not to let it touch the ground. Celsus likewise recommends that this root be boiled in wine and applied in cases of gout unaccompanied by swelling.

CHAPTER 15. (5.) THE STAPHYLINOS, OR WILD PARSNIP: TWENTY-TWO REMEDIES.

The staphylinos — which some call the 'erratic' (or wild) parsnip — is another variety. The seed of this plant, pounded and taken in wine, reduces abdominal swelling and relieves hysterical suffocations and pains to such a degree as to restore the uterus to its natural position. Applied as a liniment with raisin wine, it is also good for bowel pains in women; for men too, when beaten together with an equal portion of bread and taken in wine, it may be found helpful for similar pains. It is also diuretic, and it arrests the progress of phagedaenic [rapidly spreading, corroding] ulcers when applied fresh with honey, or dried and sprinkled on them with meal. Dietiches recommends that its root be given with hydromel [honey-water] for affections of the liver and spleen, and likewise for complaints of the sides, loins, and kidneys.

segments.title_inferred: Properties and Remedies of Staphylinos, Gingidion, and Skirret
segments.original_text:
eaten of it will receive no hurt from them. Mixed with axle-grease, it is applied to parts of the body stung by reptiles; and the leaves of it are eaten as a remedy for indigestion. Orpheus has stated that the staphylinos acts as a philtre, most probably because, a very well-established fact, when employed as a food, it is an aphrodisiac; a circumstance which has led some persons to state that it promotes conception. In other respects the cultivated parsnip has similar properties; though the wild kind is more powerful in its operation, and that which grows in stony soils more particularly. The seed, too, of the cultivated parsnip, taken in wine, or vinegar and water, is salutary for stings inflicted by scorpions. By rubbing the teeth with the root of this plant, tooth-ache is removed.

CHAP. 16. — GINGIDION: ONE REMEDY.
The Syrians devote themselves particularly to the cultivation of the garden, a circumstance to which we owe the Greek proverb, "There is plenty of vegetables in Syria." Among other vegetables, that country produces one very similar to the staphylinos, and known to some persons as "gingidion," only that it is smaller than the staphylinos and more bitter, though it has just the same properties. Eaten either raw or boiled, it is very beneficial to the stomach, as it entirely absorbs all humours with which it may happen to be surcharged.

CHAP. 17. THE SKIRRET: ELEVEN REMEDIES.
The wild skirret, too, is very similar to the cultivated kind, and is productive of similar effects. It sharpens the stomach, and, taken with vinegar flavoured with silphium, or with pepper and hydromel, or else with garum, it promotes the appetite. According to Opion, it is a diuretic, and acts as an aphrodisiac. Diocles is also of the same opinion; in addition to which, he says that it possesses cordial virtues for convalescents, and is extremely beneficial after frequent vomitings. Heraclides has prescribed it against the effects of mercury, and for occasional impotence, as also generally for patients when convalescent. Hicesius says that skirrets would appear to be prejudicial to the stomach, because no one is able to eat three of them following; still, however, he looks upon them as beneficial to patients who are just resuming the use of wine. The juice of the cultivated skirret, taken in goats' milk, arrests looseness of the stomach.
segments.translation:
Whoever has eaten of it [staphylinos] will receive no harm from them [reptiles]. Mixed with axle-grease [axungia: swine's grease used to lubricate chariot axles], it is applied to parts of the body stung by reptiles; and its leaves are eaten as a remedy for indigestion. Orpheus stated that the staphylinos acts as a love-philtre [aphrodisiac agent], most probably because it is a well-established fact that when used as food it acts as an aphrodisiac — a circumstance which has led some persons to claim that it promotes conception. In other respects the cultivated parsnip has similar properties, though the wild kind is more powerful in its operation, and especially so when it grows in stony soils. The seed of the cultivated parsnip, taken in wine, or in vinegar and water [posca / oxycrate], is also beneficial against the stings of scorpions. Toothache is relieved by rubbing the teeth with the root of this plant.

CHAPTER 16. — GINGIDION: ONE REMEDY.
The Syrians are particularly devoted to garden cultivation, a circumstance which gave rise to the Greek proverb, "There is plenty of vegetables in Syria." Among other vegetables, that country produces one very similar to the staphylinos, known to some as "gingidion," though it is smaller than the staphylinos and more bitter, while possessing the same properties. Whether eaten raw or boiled, it is very beneficial to the stomach, as it thoroughly absorbs whatever excess of humours [superfluous bodily fluids] may be present.

CHAPTER 17. THE SKIRRET: ELEVEN REMEDIES.
The wild skirret is also very similar to the cultivated kind and produces similar effects. It stimulates [sharpens] the stomach, and taken with vinegar flavoured with silphium, or with pepper and hydromel [honey-water], or with garum [fish sauce], it promotes the appetite. According to Opion, it is diuretic and acts as an aphrodisiac. Diocles shares this opinion, and adds that it possesses restorative [cordial] virtues for convalescents and is extremely beneficial after repeated vomiting. Heraclides prescribed it against the effects of mercury [mercurial poisoning], for occasional impotence, and generally for patients who are convalescing. Hicesius holds that skirrets appear to be harmful to the stomach, since no one is able to eat three of them in succession; yet he nonetheless considers them beneficial to patients who are just resuming the use of wine. The juice of the cultivated skirret, taken in goats' milk, arrests looseness of the stomach [diarrhoea].

segments.title_inferred: Sile or Hartwort, Elecampane, and Onions: Remedies from Pliny's Natural History (Book XX, Chapters 18–20)
segments.original_text:
CHAP. 18. SILE, OR HARTWORT: TWELVE REMEDIES. As the similitude which exists between their Greek names has caused most persons to mistake the one for the other, we have thought it as well to give some account here of sile or hartwort, though it is a plant which is very generally known. The best hartwort is that of Massilia, the seed of it being broad and yellow; and the next best is that of Æthiopia, the seed of which is of a darker hue. The Cretan hartwort is the most odoriferous of the several kinds. The root of this plant has a pleasant smell; the seed of it is eaten by vultures, it is said. Hartwort is useful to man for inveterate coughs, ruptures, and convulsions, being usually taken in white wine; it is employed also in cases of opisthotony, and for diseases of the liver, as well as for griping pains in the bowels and for strangury, in doses of two or three spoonfuls at a time. The leaves of this plant are useful also, and have the effect of aiding parturition — in animals even: indeed, it is generally said that roes, when about to bring forth, are in the habit of eating these leaves in particular. They are topically applied, also, in erysipelas; and either the leaves or the seed, taken fasting in the morning, are very beneficial to the digestion. Hartwort has the effect, too, of arresting looseness in cattle, either bruised and put into their drink, or else eaten by them after it has been chewed with salt. When oxen are in a diseased state, it is beaten up and poured into their food.

CHAP. 19. ELECAMPANE: ELEVEN REMEDIES. Elecampane, too, chewed fasting, has the effect of strengthening the teeth, if, from the moment that it is plucked, it is not allowed to touch the ground: a confection of it is a cure for cough. The juice of the root boiled is an expellent of intestinal tapeworm; and dried in the shade and reduced to powder, the root is curative in cases of cough, convulsions, flatulency, and affections of the trachea. It is useful too, for the bites of venomous animals; and the leaves steeped in wine are applied topically for pains in the loins.

CHAP. 20. ONIONS: TWENTY-SEVEN REMEDIES. There are no such things in existence as wild onions. The cultivated onion is employed for the cure of dimness of sight, the patient being made to smell at it till tears come into the eyes: it is
segments.translation:
CHAPTER 18. SILE, OR HARTWORT: TWELVE REMEDIES. Because the similarity between their Greek names [Σίσαρον, 'skirret,' and Σέσελι / Σελι / Σίλι, 'hartwort'] has caused most people to confuse one plant with the other, we have thought it appropriate to include here some account of sile or hartwort [Seseli tortuosum L.], even though it is a plant that is very widely known. The finest hartwort is that of Massilia [Marseilles], its seed being broad and yellow; the next best is that of Æthiopia, whose seed is of a darker colour. The Cretan hartwort is the most fragrant of the several varieties. The root of this plant has a pleasant odour; its seed is eaten by vultures, so it is said. Hartwort is beneficial to humans for chronic [inveterate] coughs, ruptures, and convulsions, and is ordinarily taken in white wine; it is also used in cases of opisthotonos [a form of severe muscular spasm], and for diseases of the liver, as well as for griping pains in the bowels and for strangury [painful, obstructed urination], in doses of two or three spoonfuls at a time. The leaves of this plant are likewise useful, and have the property of aiding parturition [childbirth] — even in animals: indeed, it is commonly reported that does [roes], when about to give birth, are accustomed to eat these leaves in particular. They are also applied topically in cases of erysipelas [a skin infection]; and either the leaves or the seed, taken fasting in the morning, are very beneficial to digestion. Hartwort also has the effect of checking looseness of the bowels in cattle, either bruised and added to their drink, or eaten by them after being chewed with salt. When oxen are in a diseased state, it is beaten up and poured into their feed.

CHAPTER 19. ELECAMPANE: ELEVEN REMEDIES. Elecampane [Inula helenium L.], too, chewed fasting, has the effect of strengthening the teeth, provided that from the moment it is pulled it is not allowed to touch the ground; a confection made from it is a remedy for cough. The juice of the root, when boiled, expels intestinal tapeworm; and when dried in the shade and reduced to powder, the root is curative in cases of cough, convulsions, flatulence, and affections of the trachea [windpipe]. It is also useful for the bites of venomous animals; and the leaves steeped in wine are applied topically for pains in the loins.

segments.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Onion and Cutleek (Pliny, Natural History, Chapters 20–21)
segments.original_text:
end of a couple of days. Applied, too, in the same way, they are good for healing excoriations. Roasted in hot ashes, many persons have applied them topically, with barley meal, for defluxions of the eyes and ulcerations of the genitals. The juice, too, is employed as an ointment for sores of the eyes, albugo, and argema. Mixed with honey, it is used as a liniment for the stings of serpents, and all kinds of ulcerous sores. In combination with woman's milk, it is employed for affections of the ears; and in cases of singing in the ears and hardness of hearing, it is injected into those organs with goose-grease or honey. In cases where persons have been suddenly struck dumb, it has been administered to them to drink, mixed with water. In cases, too, of toothache, it is sometimes introduced into the mouth as a gargle for the teeth; it is an excellent remedy also for all kinds of wounds made by animals, scorpions more particularly. In cases of alopecy and itch-scab, bruised onions are rubbed on the parts affected: they are also given boiled to persons afflicted with dysentery or lumbago. Onion peelings, burnt to ashes and mixed with vinegar, are employed topically for stings of serpents and multipedes. In other respects, there are remarkable differences of opinion among medical men. The more modern writers have stated that onions are good for the thoracic organs and the digestion, but that they are productive of flatulency and thirst. The school of Asclepiades maintains that, used as an aliment, onions impart a florid colour to the complexion, and that, taken fasting every day, they are promoters of robustness and health; that as a diet, too, they are good for the stomach by acting upon the spirits, and have the effect of relaxing the bowels. He says, too, that, employed as a suppository, onions disperse piles, and that the juice of them, taken in combination with juice of fennel, is wonderfully beneficial in cases of incipient dropsy. It is said, too, that the juice, taken with rue and honey, is good for quinsy, and has the effect of dispelling lethargy. Varro assures us that onions, pounded with salt and vinegar and then dried, will never be attacked by worms.

CHAP. 21. (6.) — CUTLEEK: THIRTY-TWO REMEDIES.
Cutleek has the effect of stanching bleeding at the nose.

Roasted in hot ashes, many persons have applied them topically, mixed with barley meal, for defluxions [discharges/inflammations] of the eyes and ulcerations of the genitals. The juice is also employed as an ointment for sores of the eyes, albugo [a whitening or opacity of the cornea], and argema [a white speck within the pupil of the eye]. Mixed with honey, it is used as a liniment for the stings of serpents and all kinds of ulcerous sores. Combined with woman's milk, it is employed for affections of the ears; and in cases of tinnitus [ringing in the ears] and hardness of hearing, it is injected into the ears with goose-grease or honey. In cases where persons have been suddenly struck dumb, it has been administered as a drink mixed with water. In cases of toothache, it is sometimes introduced into the mouth as a gargle; it is also held to be an excellent remedy for all kinds of wounds inflicted by animals, and especially by scorpions. In cases of alopecia [fox-evil; hair loss] and itch-scab, bruised onions are rubbed on the affected parts; they are also given boiled to persons suffering from dysentery or lumbago. Onion peelings, burnt to ashes and mixed with vinegar, are applied topically for stings of serpents and millipedes. In other respects, there are remarkable differences of opinion among physicians. More recent writers have stated that onions are beneficial for the thoracic organs and digestion, but that they cause flatulence and thirst. The school of Asclepiades holds that, when used as food, onions impart a florid [healthy reddish] colour to the complexion, and that taken fasting every day they promote robustness and health; that as a dietary article they are also good for the stomach by acting upon the vital spirits, and have the effect of relaxing the bowels. He also states that, employed as a suppository, onions disperse piles, and that their juice, taken in combination with the juice of fennel, is wonderfully beneficial in cases of incipient dropsy. It is also said that the juice, taken with rue and honey, is good for quinsy [peritonsillar abscess/throat inflammation], and has the effect of dispelling lethargy. Varro assures us that onions, pounded with salt and vinegar and then dried, will never be attacked by worms.

CHAPTER 21. (6.) — CUTLEEK [Porrum sectivum]: THIRTY-TWO REMEDIES.
Cutleek has the property of stanching bleeding at the nose.

segments.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Leeks (Cut and Bulbed) and Garlic — Pliny's Natural History
segments.original_text:
known also as 'syce,' situate in the corner of the eye, from which there is a continual running: some persons, however, give this name to livid pustules, which cause great restlessness in the night. Other kinds of ulcers, too, are treated with leeks beaten up with honey: used with vinegar, they are extensively employed also for the bites of wild beasts, as well as of serpents and other venomous creatures. Mixed with goats' gall, or else honied wine in equal proportions, they are used for affections of the ears, and, combined with woman's milk, for singing in the ears. In cases of head-ache, the juice is injected into the nostrils, or else into the ear at bed-time, two spoonfuls of juice to one of honey. This juice is taken too with pure wine, for the stings of serpents and scorpions, and, mixed with a semi-sextarius of wine, for lumbago. The juice, or the leek itself, eaten as a food, is very beneficial to persons troubled with spitting of blood, phthisis, or inveterate catarrhs; in cases also of jaundice or dropsy, and for nephretic pains, it is taken in barley-water, in doses of one acetabulum of juice. The same dose, too, mixed with honey, effectually purges the uterus. Leeks are eaten, too, in cases of poisoning by fungi, and are applied topically to wounds: they act also as an aphrodisiac, allay thirst, and dispel the effects of drunkenness; but they have the effect of weakening the sight and causing flatulency, it is said, though, at the same time, they are not injurious to the stomach, and act as an aperient. Leeks impart a remarkable clearness to the voice.

CHAP. 22. BULBED LEEK: THIRTY-NINE REMEDIES.
Bulbed leek produces the same effects as cut-leek, but in a more powerful degree. To persons troubled with spitting of blood, the juice of it is given, with powdered nut-galls or frankincense, or else gum acacia. Hippocrates, however, prescribes it without being mixed with anything else, and expressed himself of opinion that it has the property of opening the uterus when contracted, and that taken as an aliment by females, it is a great promoter of fecundity. Beaten up and mixed with honey, it cleanses ulcerous sores. It is good for the cure of coughs, catarrhs, and all affections of the lungs and of the trachea, whether given in the form of a ptisan, or eaten raw, the head excepted: it must be taken, however, without bread, and upon alternate days, and this even if there should be purulent expectorations. Taken in this form, it greatly improves the voice, and acts as an aphrodisiac, and as a promoter of sleep. The heads, boiled in a couple of waters, arrest looseness of the bowels, and fluxes of long standing; and a decoction of the outer coat acts as a dye upon grey hair.

CHAP. 23. GARLIC: SIXTY-ONE REMEDIES.
Garlic has very powerful properties, and is of great utility to persons on changes of water or locality. The very smell of it drives away serpents and scorpions, and, according to what some persons say, it is a cure for wounds made by [text breaks off].
segments.translation:
The condition known also as 'syce' [from Greek σῦκον, 'fig'], situated in the corner of the eye, causes a continual running discharge; some authors, however, apply this name to livid pustules that cause great restlessness at night. Other kinds of ulcers are likewise treated with leeks beaten up with honey. Used with vinegar, leeks are also extensively employed for the bites of wild beasts, serpents, and other venomous creatures. Mixed with goats' gall, or alternatively with honeyed wine [mulsum] in equal proportions, they are used for affections of the ears; combined with woman's milk, they are used for ringing in the ears. In cases of headache, the juice is injected into the nostrils or into the ear at bedtime — two spoonfuls of juice to one of honey. This juice is also taken with undiluted wine [merum] for the stings of serpents and scorpions, and mixed with a semi-sextarius of wine for lumbago. The juice, or the leek itself eaten as food, is very beneficial to persons troubled with spitting of blood, phthisis [pulmonary consumption], or chronic catarrhs; in cases of jaundice or dropsy, and for nephritic pains, it is taken in barley-water in doses of one acetabulum of juice. The same dose, mixed with honey, effectually purges the uterus. Leeks are also eaten in cases of poisoning by fungi — though they would be of no real utility for this — and are applied topically to wounds. They also act as an aphrodisiac (a claim considered unfounded), allay thirst, and dispel the effects of drunkenness; but they are said to weaken the eyesight and cause flatulence, though at the same time they are not injurious to the stomach and act as an aperient [mild laxative]. Leeks impart a remarkable clearness to the voice.

CHAPTER 22. BULBED LEEK: THIRTY-NINE REMEDIES.
Bulbed leek [Allium ampeloprasum or similar bulbing variety] produces the same effects as cut-leek, but in a more powerful degree. For persons troubled with spitting of blood, its juice is given together with powdered nut-galls or frankincense, or else gum acacia. Hippocrates, however, prescribes it without any admixture, and was of the opinion that it has the property of opening a contracted uterus and that, taken as food by women, it greatly promotes fecundity. Beaten up and mixed with honey, it cleanses ulcerous sores. It is beneficial for coughs, catarrhs, and all affections of the lungs and trachea, whether administered in the form of a ptisan [medicinal barley-gruel] or eaten raw — excluding the head — and must be taken without bread, on alternate days, even when there are purulent expectorations. Taken in this manner, it greatly improves the voice, acts as an aphrodisiac, and promotes sleep. The heads, boiled in two changes of water, arrest looseness of the bowels and long-standing fluxes; and a decoction of the outer skin [tunics] serves as a dye for grey hair.

CHAPTER 23. GARLIC: SIXTY-ONE REMEDIES.
Garlic has very powerful properties and is of great utility to persons adjusting to changes of water or locality. The very smell of it is said to drive away serpents and scorpions, and according to some authorities it is a cure for wounds inflicted by [text breaks off].

We shall not be surprised too, that it acts as a powerful remedy for the bite of the shrew-mouse, when we find that it has the property of neutralizing aconite, otherwise known as "pardalianches." It neutralizes henbane, also, and cures the bites of dogs, when applied with honey to the wound. It is taken in drink also for the stings of serpents; and of its leaves, mixed with oil, a most valuable liniment is made for bruises on the body, even when they have swelled and formed blisters. Diocles prescribes it, in combination with centaury, for dropsy, and to be taken in a split fig, to promote the alvine evacuations: taken fresh, however, in unmixed wine, with coriander, it is still more efficacious for that purpose. segments.translation: The medicinal power of garlic is so great that it is one of the most potent vermifuges [worm-expelling agents] known; yet at the same time it is so strong a stimulant that it is liable to cause worse troubles than even the presence of intestinal worms. [It is effective against] every kind of wild beast's venom, whether administered in drink or food, or applied externally. Taken in wine, it is a remedy especially against the sting of the haemorrhois [a venomous serpent], acting as an emetic. Nor should we be surprised that it acts as a powerful remedy for the bite of the shrew-mouse, given that it has the property of neutralizing aconite, also known as "pardalianches" [panther-strangle]. It likewise counteracts henbane, and cures dog bites when applied with honey to the wound. It is also taken in drink for serpent stings; and from its leaves, mixed with oil, a most valuable liniment is made for bruises on the body, even when they have swollen and formed blisters. Hippocrates likewise held that fumigations with garlic bring away the afterbirth [placenta]; and he used the ashes of garlic mixed with oil for the cure of running [suppurating] ulcers of the head. Some physicians have prescribed boiled garlic for asthmatic patients, while others have given it raw. Diocles prescribes it in combination with centaury for dropsy, and taken in a split fig to promote bowel evacuations [alvine evacuations]; taken fresh, however, in undiluted wine with coriander, it is still more effective for that purpose. Some have given it beaten up in milk for asthma. Praxagoras used to prescribe garlic mixed with wine for jaundice, and with oil and pottage for the iliac passion [intestinal obstruction/colic]; he also used it in a similar preparation as a liniment for scrofulous swellings of the neck. The ancients gave raw garlic in cases of madness, and Diocles administered it boiled for phrenitis [acute delirium/brain fever]. Beaten up and taken in vinegar and water, it is very useful as a gargle for quinsy [peritonsillar abscess]. Three heads of garlic beaten in vinegar give relief in toothache; and a similar result is obtained by rinsing the mouth with a decoction of garlic and inserting pieces of it into hollow teeth. Juice of garlic is sometimes injected into the ears with goose-grease, and taken in drink, or similarly injected in combination with vinegar and nitre [sodium nitrate/saltpeter], it arrests phthiriasis [louse infestation] and porrigo [scalp disease/ringworm]. Boiled with milk, or beaten and mixed with soft cheese, it is a cure for catarrhs. Taken in a similar manner with peas or beans, it is good for hoarseness; in general, however, it is found to be more serviceable cooked than raw, and boiled than roasted—in the roasted state, however, it is more beneficial to the voice. Boiled in oxymel [a preparation of honey and vinegar], it expels tapeworm and other intestinal worms; and a pottage made of it is a cure for tenesmus [painful straining at stool]. A decoction of garlic is applied topically for pains in the temples; and first boiled then beaten with honey, it is good for blisters. A decoction of it, with s[text breaks off].

segments.title_inferred: Medicinal Uses of Garlic, Lettuce, and Related Plants (Pliny, Natural History, Book XX, Chapters 23–25)
segments.original_text:
The cure of suspected tumours. Mixed with sulphur and resin, garlic draws out the humours from fistulous sores, and employed with pitch, it will extract an arrow even from the wound. In cases of leprosy, lichen, and eruptions of the skin, it acts as a detergent, and effects a cure, in combination with wild marjoram, or else reduced to ashes, and applied as a liniment with oil and garum. It is employed in a similar manner, too, for erysipelas; and, reduced to ashes, and mixed with honey, it restores contused or livid spots on the skin to their proper colour. It is generally believed, too, that taken in the food and drink, garlic is a cure for epilepsy, and that a clove of it, taken in astringent wine, with an obolus' weight of silphium, will have the effect of dispelling quartan fever. Garlic cures coughs also, and suppressions of the chest, however violent they may be; to obtain which result, another method is followed, it being boiled with broken beans, and employed as a diet till the cure is fully effected. It is a soporific also, and in general imparts to the body an additional ruddiness of colour. Garlic acts as an aphrodisiac, beaten up with fresh coriander, and taken in pure wine. The inconveniences which result from the use of it, are dimness of the sight and flatulency; and if taken in too large quantities, it does injury to the stomach, and creates thirst. In addition to these particulars, mixed with spelt flour, and given to poultry in their food, it preserves them from attacks of the pip. Beasts of burden, it is said, will void their urine all the more easily, and without any pain, if the genitals are rubbed with garlic.

CHAP. 24. — THE LETTUCE: FORTY-TWO REMEDIES. THE GOAT-LETTUCE: FOUR REMEDIES.

The first kind of lettuce which grows spontaneously, is the one that is generally known as "goat-lettuce;" thrown into the sea, this vegetable has the property of instantaneously killing all the fish that come into its vicinity. The milky juice of this lettuce, left to thicken and then put into vinegar, is given in doses of two oboli, with the addition of one cyathus of water, to patients for dropsy. The stalk and leaves, bruised and sprinkled with salt, are used for the cure of wounds of the sinews.
