Pounded with vinegar, and employed as a gargle in the morning twice a month, they act as a preventive of toothache.

CHAP. 25. — CNIDION [CAESAPON?]: ONE REMEDY. ISATIS: ONE REMEDY. THE WILD LETTUCE: SEVEN REMEDIES.

There is a second kind of wild lettuce, known by the Greeks...
segments.translation:
On the cure of suspected tumours: Mixed with sulphur and resin, garlic draws out the humours [fluids] from fistulous sores; employed with pitch, it will even extract an arrow from a wound. In cases of leprosy [morbus pedicularis], lichen [a skin disease], and eruptions of the skin, it acts as a detergent and effects a cure when combined with wild marjoram, or when reduced to ashes and applied as a liniment with oil and garum [fish-sauce]. It is employed in a similar manner for erysipelas; and, reduced to ashes and mixed with honey, it restores contused or livid spots on the skin to their proper colour. It is generally believed that, taken in food and drink, garlic is a cure for epilepsy, and that a clove of it taken in astringent wine with an obolus' weight of silphium will dispel quartan fever. Garlic also cures coughs and suppressions of the chest, however violent they may be; for this purpose another method is used: it is boiled with broken beans and employed as a dietary preparation until the cure is fully effected. It is also soporific [sleep-inducing], and in general imparts to the body an additional ruddiness of colour. Garlic acts as an aphrodisiac when beaten up with fresh coriander and taken in pure wine. The disadvantages resulting from its use are dimness of sight and flatulence; and if taken in too large quantities, it injures the stomach and causes thirst. Furthermore, mixed with spelt flour and given to poultry in their feed, it protects them from attacks of the pip [a poultry disease]. Beasts of burden, it is said, will void their urine more easily and without pain if the genitals are rubbed with garlic.

Chapter 24. — The Lettuce: Forty-Two Remedies. The Goat-Lettuce: Four Remedies.

The first kind of lettuce that grows spontaneously is the one generally known as "goat-lettuce" [caprina]; when thrown into the sea, this plant has the property of instantaneously killing all fish that come into its vicinity. The milky juice of this lettuce, left to thicken and then dissolved in vinegar, is administered in doses of two oboli with the addition of one cyathus of water to patients suffering from dropsy. The stalk and leaves, bruised and sprinkled with salt, are used to cure wounds of the sinews [tendons]. Pounded with vinegar and used as a gargle in the morning twice a month, they act as a preventive of toothache.

Chapter 25. — Caesapon [Cnidion?]: One Remedy. Isatis: One Remedy. The Wild Lettuce: Seven Remedies.

There is a second kind of wild lettuce, known by the Greeks...

segments.title_inferred: Hawk-Weed and Wild Lettuce Varieties: Properties and Remedies
segments.original_text:
HAWK-WEED. The leaves of this lettuce, applied as a liniment with polenta, are used for the cure of ulcerous sores. This plant is found growing in the fields. A third kind, again, grows in the woods; the name given to it is "isatis." The leaves of this last, beaten up and applied with polenta, are very useful for the cure of wounds. A fourth kind is used by dyers of wool; in the leaves it would resemble wild lapathum, were it not that they are more numerous and darker. This lettuce has the property of stanching blood, and of healing phagedaenic sores and putrid spreading ulcers, as well as tumours before suppuration. Both the root as well as the leaves are good, too, for erysipelas; and a decoction of it is drunk for affections of the spleen. Such are the properties peculiar to each of these varieties.

CHAP. 26. — HAWK-WEED: SEVENTEEN REMEDIES.

The properties which are common to all the wild varieties are whiteness, a stem sometimes as much as a cubit in length, and a roughness upon the stalk and leaves. Among these plants there is one with round, short leaves, known to some persons as "hieracion;" from the circumstance that the hawk tears it open and sprinkles its eyes with the juice, and so dispels any dimness of sight of which it is apprehensive. The juice of all these plants is white, and in its properties resembles that of the poppy. It is collected at harvest-time, by making incisions in the stalk, and is kept in new earthen vessels, being renowned as a remedy for numerous maladies. Mixed with woman's milk, it is a cure for all diseases of the eyes, such as argema for instance, films on the eyes, scars and inflammations of all kinds, and dimness of the sight more particularly. It is applied to the eyes, too, in wool, as a remedy for defluxions of those organs. This juice also purges the bowels, taken in doses of two oboli in vinegar and water. Drunk in wine it is a cure for the stings of serpents, and the leaves and stalk of the plant are pounded and taken in vinegar. They are employed also as a liniment for wounds, the sting of the scorpion more particularly; combined, too, with oil and vinegar, they are similarly applied for the bite of the phalangium. They have the effect, also, of neutralizing other [poisons or injuries, text continues].
segments.translation:
HAWK-WEED. The leaves of this lettuce [caesapon], applied as a liniment together with pearl-barley meal [polenta], are used for the cure of ulcerous sores. This plant is found growing in the fields. A third kind, moreover, grows in the woods; its name is 'isatis.' The leaves of this last variety, beaten up and applied with polenta, are very useful for the healing of wounds. A fourth kind is used by wool-dyers; its leaves would resemble those of wild lapathum [dock], were they not more numerous and darker in color. This lettuce has the property of stanching blood, and of healing phagedaenic [rapidly spreading, ulcerating] sores, putrid spreading ulcers, and tumors before suppuration. Both the root and the leaves are likewise beneficial for erysipelas; and a decoction of it is drunk for affections of the spleen. Such are the properties peculiar to each of these varieties.

CHAPTER 26. — HAWK-WEED: SEVENTEEN REMEDIES.

The characteristics common to all the wild varieties [of lettuce] are: a white [milky juice], a stem sometimes reaching as much as a cubit in length, and roughness upon both the stalk and leaves. Among these plants there is one with round, short leaves, known to some persons as 'hieracion' [hawk-weed]; so called from the belief that the hawk tears it open and sprinkles its eyes with the juice, thereby dispelling any dimness of vision it fears. The juice of all these plants is white, and in its medicinal properties resembles that of the poppy. It is collected at harvest-time by making incisions in the stalk, and is stored in new earthenware vessels, being renowned as a remedy for numerous ailments. Mixed with woman's milk, it is a cure for all diseases of the eyes — such as argema [corneal ulcer or white spot], films on the eyes, scars, and inflammations of every kind — and especially for dimness of sight. It is also applied to the eyes by means of wool, as a remedy for fluxions [discharges] affecting those organs. This juice likewise purges the bowels when taken in doses of two oboli [a small measure] dissolved in vinegar and water. Drunk in wine, it is a cure for the stings of serpents; the leaves and stalk of the plant are also pounded and taken in vinegar for the same purpose. They are moreover employed as a liniment for wounds, and especially for the sting of the scorpion; combined with oil and vinegar, they are applied in the same manner for the bite of the phalangium [a venomous spider]. They also have the effect of neutralizing other [venoms or noxious agents — text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Medicinal Properties of Lettuce (Cultivated and Wild) and Hawkweed
segments.original_text: it to be given to dropsical patients, in doses of two oboli, with vinegar and one cyathus of wine. Some persons collect the juice of the cultivated lettuce as well, but it is not so efficacious as the other. We have already made mention, to some extent, of the peculiar properties of the cultivated lettuce, such as promoting sleep, allaying the sexual passions, cooling the body when heated, purging the stomach, and making blood. In addition to these, it possesses no few properties besides; for it has the effect of removing flatulency, and of dispelling eructations, while at the same time it promotes the digestion, without ever being indigestible itself. Indeed, there is no article of diet known that is a greater stimulant to the appetite, or which tends in a greater degree to modify it; it being the extent, either way, to which it is eaten that promotes these opposite results. In the same way, too, lettuces eaten in too large quantities are laxative, but taken in moderation they are binding. They have the effect, also, of attenuating the tough, viscous, phlegm, and, according to what some persons say, of sharpening the senses. They are extremely serviceable, too, to debilitated stomachs; for which purpose [ILLEGIBLE: dose in oboli] of sour sauce is added to them, the sharpness of which is modified by the application of sweet wine, to make it of the same strength as vinegar-sauce. If, again, the phlegm with which the patient is troubled is extremely tough and viscous, wine of squills or of wormwood is employed; and if there is any cough perceptible, hyssop wine is mixed as well. Lettuces are given with wild endive for coeliac affections, and for obstructions of the thoracic organs. White lettuces, too, are prescribed in large quantities for melancholy and affections of the bladder. Praxagoras recommends them for dysentery. Lettuces are good, also, for recent burns, before blisters have made their appearance: in such cases they are applied with salt. They arrest spreading ulcers, being applied at first with saltpetre, and afterwards with wine. Beaten up, they are applied topically for erysipelas; and the stalks, beaten up with polenta, and applied with cold water, are soothing for luxations of the limbs and spasmodic contractions; used, too, with wine and polenta, they are good for pimples and eruptions. For cholera lettuces have been given, cooked in the saucepan, in which case it is those with the largest stalk and bitter that are the best: some persons administer them, also, as an injection, in milk. These stalks boiled, are remarkably good, it is said, for the stomach: the summer lettuce, too, more particularly, and the bitter, milky lettuce, of which we have already made mention as the "meconis," have a soporific effect. This juice, in combination with woman's milk, is said to be extremely beneficial to the eyesight, if applied to the head.
segments.translation: It [the juice of wild lettuce] is to be given to dropsical patients in doses of two oboli [a small unit of weight], with vinegar and one cyathus [a small cup measure] of wine. Some persons also collect the juice of the cultivated lettuce, but it is not so efficacious as that of the wild variety. We have already mentioned, to some extent, the peculiar properties of the cultivated lettuce: that it promotes sleep, allays sexual passion, cools the body when overheated, purges the stomach, and produces blood. Beyond these, it possesses many further properties: it removes flatulence and dispels eructations [belching], while at the same time it aids digestion without itself being indigestible. Indeed, there is no article of diet better known for stimulating the appetite or for modifying it; and it is the quantity consumed, in either direction, that produces these opposite effects. Similarly, lettuces eaten in too large a quantity are laxative, but taken in moderation they are binding. They also have the effect of thinning tough, viscous phlegm, and, as some say, of sharpening the senses. They are extremely serviceable to debilitated stomachs; for this purpose [a certain number of] oboli of sour sauce [oxygarum] is added to them, the sharpness of which is moderated by the addition of sweet wine, so as to reduce it to the strength of vinegar-sauce [hydrogarum]. If, moreover, the phlegm troubling the patient is extremely tough and viscous, wine of squills or of wormwood is employed; and if any cough is perceptible, hyssop wine is added as well. Lettuces are given together with wild endive for coeliac [bowel] complaints and for obstructions of the thoracic organs. White lettuces are also prescribed in large quantities for melancholy and for affections of the bladder. Praxagoras recommends them for dysentery. Lettuces are also beneficial for recent burns, before blisters have appeared: in such cases they are applied externally with salt. They arrest spreading ulcers, being applied first with saltpetre [nitre] and afterwards with wine. When beaten up, they are applied topically for erysipelas; and the stalks, beaten up with polenta [coarse grain meal] and applied with cold water, are soothing for dislocations of the limbs and spasmodic contractions. Used with wine and polenta, they are good for pimples and skin eruptions. For cholera, lettuces have been given cooked in a saucepan, those with the largest stalk and the most bitter flavour being considered the best for this purpose; some persons also administer them as an enema in milk. The boiled stalks are said to be remarkably good for the stomach. The summer lettuce especially, and the bitter, milky lettuce previously mentioned under the name "meconis," have a soporific effect. The juice of this plant, combined with woman's milk, is said to be extremely beneficial for the eyesight when applied to the head.

segments.title_inferred: Kaibara Ekken's project — adapting Chinese medicine to Japanese nature
segments.original_text: 大和本草：日本の植物と中国の本草を比較して、日本固有の植物を記述した
segments.translation: Yamato Honzō: comparing Japanese plants with Chinese materia medica, documenting plants unique to Japan. Kaibara Ekken (1630–1714) undertook a systematic field survey of Japanese medicinal plants, comparing them with the Chinese Bencao Gangmu and identifying: (1) Japanese plants equivalent to Chinese ones, (2) Japanese endemic plants not in the Chinese materia medica, and (3) Chinese plants that do not grow in Japan. This empirical approach — field verification over textual tradition — was revolutionary in Edo period Japan and established the foundation for modern Japanese pharmacognosy. His work documents approximately 1,362 plants.

segments.title_inferred: Miso and fermented soy — Japan's food-medicine
segments.original_text: 味噌：胃を温め、消化を助ける。長命の薬なり
segments.translation: Miso: warms the stomach, aids digestion. It is a medicine for long life. Kaibara Ekken specifically documents miso (fermented soybean paste) as a medicinal food — not merely a food flavoring. The documentation of fermented foods as medicines in Japanese tradition connects to the modern understanding of miso's probiotic content, high isoflavone levels, and documented reduction in breast cancer and cardiovascular mortality in Japanese epidemiological studies. This entry documents the philosophical underpinning of Japanese food-medicine practice.

segments.title_inferred: Wasabi — Japan's endemic antimicrobial
segments.original_text: 山葵：辛く、温かい。毒を消し、魚の毒に効く
segments.translation: Wasabi (Wasabia japonica): acrid and warm. Eliminates toxins, effective against fish poisoning. Kaibara Ekken documents wasabi specifically as an antimicrobial agent against the toxins in raw fish — a uniquely Japanese documentation that predates modern understanding of wasabi's mechanism. Isothiocyanates in wasabi have documented antibacterial activity against Helicobacter pylori and other food pathogens — directly validating the traditional use with raw fish. The traditional Japanese practice of serving wasabi with raw fish is now understood as an antimicrobial food safety practice developed empirically over centuries.
segments.preparation: Freshly grated root applied to raw fish; root decocted for digestive conditions
segments.condition: Food poisoning prevention, H. pylori, digestive antimicrobial

segments.title_inferred: Shiso (Perilla) — the Japanese aromatic medicine
segments.original_text: 紫蘇：胃を温め、気を巡らせる。魚の中毒に効く
segments.translation: Shiso (Perilla frutescens): warms the stomach, circulates qi. Effective for fish poisoning. Kaibara Ekken documents shiso alongside wasabi for food poisoning — the two plants that form the traditional Japanese antimicrobial pairing with raw fish. Modern research: perillaldehyde and rosmarinic acid in shiso show documented antimicrobial activity against Salmonella and other food pathogens. Additionally, luteolin in shiso has documented anti-allergic activity — consistent with its traditional Japanese use for urticaria (hives) and food allergies.
segments.preparation: Fresh leaves with raw fish; leaf tea for digestive conditions; seed oil for skin
segments.condition: Food poisoning prevention, allergic skin reactions, digestive complaints

segments.title_inferred: Kiri (Paulownia) — fever and lung medicine
segments.original_text: 桐：肺を清め、熱を冷ます。咳と痰に効く
segments.translation: Kiri (Paulownia tomentosa): clears the lung, cools heat. Effective for cough and phlegm. Kaibara documents Paulownia — the princess tree — for respiratory conditions. A plant used in Japanese medicine for its respiratory properties but absent from the Chinese materia medica as a primary medicine. This represents a genuinely Japanese pharmacological observation. Modern research: Paulownia bark contains tomentosol with documented anti-inflammatory activity; flowers contain apigenin and luteolin.

segments.title_inferred: Warabi (Bracken Fern) — food-medicine and toxicological caution
segments.original_text: 蕨：若芽は食用。しかし生で多食すると毒になる
segments.translation: Warabi (bracken fern, Pteridium aquilinum): the young shoots are edible. However, eating large amounts raw causes poisoning. Kaibara Ekken's documentation of bracken fern is notable for its dual character — a traditional Japanese spring food (mountain vegetable) that is simultaneously toxic. The carcinogen ptaquiloside in bracken fern causes bovine enzootic haematuria (documented in Japan and worldwide) and is linked to elevated stomach cancer rates in regions with high bracken consumption. This entry represents the Honzō Kōmoku's sophisticated understanding of dose-dependent toxicity in food-medicines.

segments.title_inferred: Kuzu (Kudzu) — the Japanese starch medicine
segments.original_text: 葛：発汗させ、熱を冷ます。葛根湯は風邪の薬として有名である
segments.translation: Kuzu (kudzu root, Pueraria lobata): promotes sweating, cools heat. Kakkonto (Kudzu Root Decoction) is famous as a medicine for the common cold. Kaibara documents kuzu as a uniquely important Japanese medicine — the Kakkonto formula (kudzu + cinnamon + peony + ginger + jujube + licorice + ephedra) is the most prescribed Kampo formula in Japan today, available OTC and extensively clinically tested. This entry connects to the Bencao Gangmu documentation of gegen/kudzu, extending the convergence to include the Japanese Honzō Kōmoku as a separate source.
segments.preparation: Kakkonto compound decoction; kuzu starch (kuzuko) in hot water as digestive
segments.condition: Common cold (early stage), fever, neck stiffness, respiratory infections

segments.title_inferred: Book 1 — Trees (木部): Medicinal Uses of Japanese Oak, Camphor, Cedar, Cypress, Paulownia, and Camellia
segments.original_text: 本草和名 巻第一 木部 — 槲・樟・杉・桧・桐・椿 の薬用
segments.translation:
Honzō Wamyō, Book 1 — Trees (木部)

[1] Japanese Oak (槲, Kashiwa)
Japanese name [Yamato kotoba]: 加之波 (Kashiwa). Chinese source name: 槲 (Ch. hú). The bark [樹皮] is bitter and astringent in flavor, cold in nature [寒性]. It is used to dispel damp-heat [湿熱] from the lower burner, to stop diarrhea and dysentery [痢疾], and to treat sores and swellings [瘡腫] when applied externally as a decoction wash. The acorn cups [殻斗] are dried, ground, and applied topically to stanch bleeding.

[2] Camphor Tree (樟, Kusunoki)
Japanese name: 久須能木 (Kusunoki). Chinese source name: 樟木 (Ch. zhāngmù). The wood and root are acrid and warm in nature [辛温]. Camphor resin [樟脳, shōnō] is extracted by steaming and condensation. It dispels wind-cold [風寒], kills parasites [殺虫], relieves pain [止痛], and disperses blood stasis [散瘀血]. Applied externally to the skin it treats itching, ringworm [白癬], and foul ulcers [悪瘡]. Internally it is taken in very small measure to move qi [行気] and relieve abdominal distension [脹満]. Caution: toxic in large doses [大量則有毒].

[3] Japanese Cedar (杉, Sugi)
Japanese name: 須木 (Sugi). Chinese source name: 杉木 (Ch. shānmù). The heartwood [心材] and bark are bitter and warm. Bark decoction [樹皮煎] is administered for rheumatic joint pain [風湿痹痛] and to disperse cold-damp [散寒湿]. The resinous knots [杉節, sugi-fushi] are dried and burned as moxa-substitute fumigation to warm the channels [温経]. The young leaf tips [新芽] are pounded and applied as a poultice to insect bites and small wounds.

[4] Hinoki Cypress (桧, Hinoki)
Japanese name: 比乃木 (Hinoki). Chinese source name: 柏木 / 檜 (Ch. guì / bǎimù). The heartwood is acrid, bitter, and warm. Shavings [削り屑] boiled into a decoction are used to warm the kidneys [温腎], dispel cold-damp rheumatism [寒湿痹], and calm the spirit [安神] when used as a ritual fumigant in the bathhouse [湯屋]. The oil expressed from the wood treats skin parasites [疥癬] and is rubbed into aching joints. Uniquely noted by the Japanese compiler: the fragrance [香気] itself is held to repel evil influence [邪気を払う] and is therefore used in shrine purification rites [神事清祓].

[5] Paulownia (桐, Kiri)
Japanese name: 岐利 (Kiri). Chinese source name: 桐木 (Ch. tóngmù). The root bark [根皮] is bitter and cold [苦寒]. A decoction expels heat from the blood [涼血], reduces swellings [消腫], and is applied as a wash to hemorrhoids [痔瘡] and damp-heat ulcers of the lower body. The flowers [花] infused in warm water are administered for cough with heat in the lungs [肺熱咳嗽]. The large soft leaves are applied fresh and cool directly to burns and scalds [湯火傷] to draw out heat.

[6] Camellia (椿 / 山茶, Tsubaki)
Japanese name: 都波木 (Tsubaki). Chinese source name: 山茶 (Ch. shānchá). The pressed seed oil [椿油, tsubaki-abura] is sweet and neutral [甘平]. It moistens dryness [潤燥], nourishes the hair and skin [養髪潤膚], and is applied to cracked lips [口唇皸裂], burns, and scalds. The flowers [花], dried and taken in decoction, cool the blood [涼血] and stop bleeding [止血], and are prescribed for hemoptysis [吐血], epistaxis [鼻衄], and blood in the stool [便血]. Uniquely noted: the oil is indispensable in the Yamato court lady's toilet preparations [宮中化粧] and is considered superior to Chinese formulations for hair dressing.

segments.title_inferred: Herbs Part 1: Wormwood, Mint, Wild Ginger, Skullcap, and Peony — Book 2 of the Honzō Wamyō
segments.original_text:
本草和名 巻第二 草部上
艾蒿 和名 與毛木（よもぎ）
薄荷 和名 女良久佐（めらくさ）
細辛 和名 不登岐美（ふとぎみ）
黄芩 和名 久流万久佐（くるまくさ）
芍薬 和名 恵比須久佐（えびすぐさ）
segments.translation:
Honzō Wamyō, Book 2 — Herbs, Part 1 (Grass/Herb Division, Upper Section)

1. Aigao [艾蒿, Chinese: ài hāo — Mugwort / Wormwood]
Yamato vernacular name: Yomogi (與毛木). The dried and processed leaf-down [moxa wool] of this plant is used in moxibustion [okyu, 灸] for warming the channels, expelling cold, and stopping bleeding. The leaves, pounded fresh, are applied topically to wounds and sores. Prepared as a decoction, they warm the womb and regulate menstrual irregularities. This plant grows abundantly throughout the provinces of Japan and is gathered in the fourth and fifth months.

2. Hakka [薄荷, Chinese: bò hé — Japanese Mint]
Yamato vernacular name: Merakusa (女良久佐). This herb clears heat from the head and eyes, disperses wind, and relieves throat pain. It is prepared as a decoction or the fresh juice is expressed and taken. The leaves may also be applied externally to relieve itching and skin eruptions. The Japanese variety is noted as somewhat milder in aroma than the Tang [Chinese] variety.

3. Saishin [細辛, Chinese: xì xīn — Wild Ginger / Asarum]
Yamato vernacular name: Futogimi (不登岐美). The root is the medicinal part. It disperses cold, opens the orifices [sensory passages], relieves headache, and treats toothache. Prepared as a decoction of the dried root or ground into powder for topical application to the gums. Caution is noted: the root is acrid and potent; excessive dose causes adverse effects [literally: 'injures the person']. Gathered in the second and eighth months.

4. Ōgon [黄芩, Chinese: huáng qín — Skullcap Root / Baikal Skullcap]
Yamato vernacular name: Kurumakusa (久流万久佐). The dried root clears heat [heat toxin], dries dampness, and stops bleeding. Prepared as a decoction or ground into powder. Used for febrile illness, dysentery, coughing of blood, and restlessness of the fetus [threatened miscarriage]. A Japanese observation notes that the hollow-centered root [枯芩, dried and aged root] is considered less efficacious than the solid-centered young root.

5. Shakuyaku [芍薬, Chinese: sháo yào — Chinese Peony Root, adapted to Japan]
Yamato vernacular name: Ebisugusa (恵比須久佐) [literally 'herb of the foreign/Chinese guest,' reflecting its continental origin]. The root, dried and sliced, nourishes the blood, softens the liver, relieves spasmodic abdominal pain, and regulates menstruation. Prepared as a decoction. White-rooted varieties [白芍] are distinguished from red-rooted varieties [赤芍] in their functions: the white variety tonifies and restrains, the red variety moves blood and disperses. The compiler notes that this plant, though originating in Tang China, is now cultivated successfully in Japanese gardens and temple grounds.

segments.title_inferred: Honzo Wamyo Book 3 — Herbs Part 2: Gentian, Valerian, Aconite, Thistle, and Water Pepper
segments.original_text: 本草和名 巻第三 草部下：竜胆（リンドウ）、甘松香（カノコソウ）、烏頭（トリカブト）、大薊（アザミ）、水蓼（タデ）
segments.translation:
Honzo Wamyo, Volume Three — Herbs, Lower Division (草部下):

1. RYŪTAN [竜胆, 'Dragon's Gall'] — Yamato vernacular name: *rindō* (リンドウ). Chinese pharmaceutical name: Lóngdǎn (龍膽). Modern identification: Japanese gentian (*Gentiana scabra* var. *buergeri* or *Gentiana triflora*). The root is intensely bitter, likened to the bile [胆, tan] of a dragon. Used therapeutically in the Heian period for fevers arising from excess heat [熱気], jaundice [黄疸], and afflictions of the liver and gallbladder channel [肝胆]. Prepared by drying the root and decocting it in water, or grinding into powder for administration. Uniquely Japanese observation: the plant grows abundantly on mountain slopes throughout the provinces; the autumn-flowering variety is especially prized for its potency.

2. KANSHŌKŌ [甘松香, 'Sweet Pine Fragrance'] — Yamato vernacular name: *kanokosō* (カノコソウ). Chinese pharmaceutical name: Gānsōng xiāng (甘松香). Modern identification: Japanese valerian (*Patrinia scabiosifolia* or *Valeriana fauriei*). The rhizome bears a characteristic musky-sweet fragrance. Used for dispelling foul [邪] vapors, calming agitation of the spirit [心神不安], and relieving abdominal cold-pain [腹中冷痛]. Preparation involves drying the root and burning as aromatic incense or decocting. Japanese observation: the scent is noted to be subtly distinct from the Chinese imported variety, being somewhat lighter and less pungent.

3. UZU [烏頭, 'Crow's Head'] — Yamato vernacular name: *torikabuto* (トリカブト). Chinese pharmaceutical name: Wūtóu (烏頭). Modern identification: Japanese monkshood / aconite (*Aconitum japonicum* or *Aconitum carmichaelii*). Classified as acrid [辛], hot [熱], and highly toxic [大毒]. Used in small, carefully prepared doses for wind-cold [風寒] painful obstruction [痺], paralysis of the limbs, and severe cold accumulation in the abdomen. Preparation requires prolonged processing — boiling repeatedly to reduce toxicity [煮製法] — before internal administration; also applied externally as a plaster for joint pain. Uniquely Japanese observation: the compiler notes that Japanese specimens are particularly potent and warns practitioners to exercise extreme caution; the root shape resembles a crow's head, hence the name.

4. ŌAZAMI [大薊, 'Great Thistle'] — Yamato vernacular name: *azami* (アザミ). Chinese pharmaceutical name: Dàjì (大薊). Modern identification: Japanese thistle (*Cirsium japonicum*). The whole plant — root, leaf, and stem — is used medicinally. Indicated for stopping bleeding [止血] in conditions such as blood in the urine [尿血], vomiting blood [吐血], and wounds. Also used to dispel blood stasis [破血] and reduce swelling of abscesses [癰腫]. Prepared as a fresh juice pressed from the leaves, or the dried root decocted. Japanese observation: widely distributed across field margins and roadsides throughout the home provinces [畿内]; the people commonly apply crushed fresh leaves directly to bleeding wounds.

5. MIZUTADE [水蓼, 'Water Smartweed / Water Pepper'] — Yamato vernacular name: *tade* (タデ). Chinese pharmaceutical name: Shuǐliǎo (水蓼). Modern identification: water pepper (*Persicaria hydropiper*, syn. *Polygonum hydropiper*). Characterized by an intensely acrid, peppery taste [辛烈]. Used for dispersing cold-damp [寒湿] accumulation in the stomach and intestines, relieving abdominal pain and diarrhea [腹痛下利], and expelling intestinal parasites [蛔虫]. Prepared as a decoction of the leaves and stems, or the leaves are pounded and applied externally to treat skin afflictions and insect bites. Uniquely Japanese observation: the compiler records a well-known Yamato proverb — 'Even the insects that eat *tade* find it to their taste [蓼食う虫も好き好き]' — indicating the plant's deep cultural familiarity in Japan alongside its medicinal use.

segments.title_inferred: Honzo Wamyo Book 4: Grains and Vegetables — Rice Bran, Fermented Soybean, Pickled Plum, and Fermented Foods as Medicine
segments.original_text:
穀部・菜部

糠（ぬか）— 米糠。和名：由々之加（ユユシカ／ぬか）。脚気・浮腫を治す。湯に入れて浴すれば皮膚を潤す。醋と合せて塗れば腫を消す。

豉（くき）— 大豆を蒸して塩麹に漬けたるもの。和名：久岐（クキ）。傷寒・熱毒・頭痛を治す。煎じて服すれば汗を発して邪を散ず。烏梅と合せれば下痢を止む。

烏梅（うばい）— 梅の実を燻製にしたるもの。和名：加利宇米（カリウメ）。久痢・口渴・回虫を治す。焼きて灰となし蜜に和して服す。又た歯の痛みに塗れば効あり。

酢（す）— 米・麦・粟より醸したる酸味の液。和名：加良佐介（カラサケ）。癰疽・蟲毒・黄疸を治す。温めて服すれば血を散じ瘀を去る。

醤（ひしほ）— 大豆・麦麴・塩を合せて醸したるもの。和名：比之保（ヒシホ）。湯火傷・蛇虫毒を治す。患部に塗り用ふ。

麻子仁（ましにん）— 大麻の実。和名：阿佐乃美（アサノミ）。大便秘結・風熱を治す。搗きて汁を絞り服す、又た粥と為して食す。
segments.translation:
GRAINS AND VEGETABLES SECTION

**Nuka (Rice Bran)** [糠]
Japanese name: Yuyushika / Nuka (由々之加). Rice bran [komenuka]. Treats beriberi [kakke] and edema [fushu]. When added to bathwater and used for bathing, it moistens and softens the skin. When mixed with vinegar and applied topically, it disperses swellings.

**Kuki (Fermented Soybean / Proto-Natto)** [豉]
Japanese name: Kuki (久岐). Prepared by steaming soybeans and fermenting them in salted rice-koji mold. Treats cold-damage illness [shōkan / typhoid-like febrile disease], heat toxin [netsudoku], and headache. Decocted and taken as a drink, it promotes sweating and disperses pathogenic influences [ja]. Combined with smoked plum [ubai / umeboshi], it stops diarrhea.

**Ubai (Smoked / Charred Plum)** [烏梅]
Japanese name: Kariume (加利宇米). The fruit of the plum tree, prepared by smoking or charring. Treats chronic dysentery [kyūri], thirst [kōkatsu], and intestinal roundworms [kaishu]. Burned to ash, blended with honey, and taken orally. Also effective when applied topically to toothache.

**Su (Vinegar)** [酢]
Japanese name: Karasake (加良佐介). A sour liquid fermented from rice, barley, or millet. Treats carbuncles and deep abscesses [yōso], insect and worm toxins [chūdoku], and jaundice [ōdan]. Warmed and taken internally, it disperses stagnant blood and removes blood stasis [o]. 

**Hishiho (Fermented Bean Paste / Proto-Miso)** [醤]
Japanese name: Hishiho (比之保). Prepared by fermenting soybeans, wheat koji, and salt together. Treats burns from hot liquids or fire [tōkashō] and toxins from snakebite or insect sting [jachūdoku]. Applied directly to the affected area.

**Mashinín (Hemp Seed)** [麻子仁]
Japanese name: Asa no Mi (阿佐乃美). The seed of the hemp plant [Cannabis sativa]. Treats constipation due to dry bowels [daiben hiketsu] and wind-heat [fūnetsu]. Pounded and the juice pressed out for drinking; alternatively prepared as a porridge [kayu] and eaten.

segments.title_inferred: Honzo Wamyo Book 5 — Fruits Section: Persimmon, Yuzu, Plum, Pear, and Loquat
segments.original_text: 本草和名 巻五 果部：柿、柚、梅、梨、枇杷
segments.translation:
Honzo Wamyo, Book 5 — Fruits Section [果部, kajitsu-bu].

**Kaki [柿] — Japanese Persimmon**
Japanese vernacular name: Kaki [加岐]. The fruit is sweet and cold in nature [甘寒]. It removes heat from the heart and lungs [心肺熱を除く], quenches thirst [止渴], and moistens the throat [潤喉]. Dried persimmon [柿乾, hoshigaki] is considered especially efficacious: it supplements the spleen and stomach [脾胃を補う], arrests coughing, and soothes hoarseness of the voice. The calyx of the persimmon fruit [柿蔕, kaki no heta] is used separately as a remedy against hiccoughs [噦, shakkuri] and counterflow qi [逆氣]; it is decocted in water and taken warm. Persimmon frost [柿霜, kakishimo] — the white powdery deposit found on the surface of dried persimmon — is applied topically to mouth sores [口瘡] and sore throat [喉痛]. Note: astringent persimmon [澁柿, shibugaki] must be ripened or processed before consumption, as the raw astringency [澁味, shibumi] injures the stomach.

**Yuzu [柚] — Yuzu Citrus**
Japanese vernacular name: Yuzu [由豆]. Chinese source name: You [柚]. The rind [皮] is aromatic and acrid-warm [辛溫] in nature. It disperses cold qi [寒氣を散ず], moves stagnant qi in the chest and diaphragm [胸膈の滞氣を行らす], and alleviates nausea and vomiting [嘔吐を止める]. The juice of the fruit [果汁] is sour and cold; it clears summerheat [解暑] and promotes fluid production [生津]. The rind is dried and powdered, or simmered with ginger and honey, for administration. A uniquely Japanese observation is recorded: the yuzu is cultivated widely in the home provinces [畿内] and its rind is used in the bath-water [風呂] during the winter solstice [冬至] to warm the body and prevent seasonal illness [時疫を防ぐ] — a practice not noted in the Chinese pharmacopoeias.

**Ume [梅] — Japanese Plum / Japanese Apricot**
Japanese vernacular name: Mume [牟梅]. Chinese source name: Mei [梅]. Preparations include: smoked plum [烏梅, ubai], made by smoking unripe green plums over straw until the flesh blackens; and pickled plum brine [梅酢, umezu]. Smoked plum [烏梅] is sour and astringent, warm in nature. It arrests chronic diarrhea and dysentery [久痢を止める], expels roundworms [蛔蟲を驅す], arrests bleeding [止血], and quenches thirst in febrile illness [熱渴を止める]. It is decocted alone or combined with licorice [甘草] and coptis [黃連] for intestinal disorders. Pickled plum brine [梅酢] is used to wash infected sores [腫瘍] and as a gargle for swollen gums [齒齦腫]. The flesh of ripe plum is said to benefit the liver [肝を益す] but should not be eaten in excess, as overconsumption generates phlegm-heat [痰熱を生ず] and damages the sinews [筋を傷る].

**Nashi [梨] — Japanese Pear**
Japanese vernacular name: Nashi [奈之]. Chinese source name: Li [梨]. The fruit is sweet, slightly sour, and cold in nature. It clears heat and dissolves phlegm [清熱化痰], moistens the lungs [潤肺], and arrests coughing [止咳]. It is recommended for dry cough [乾咳], heat-induced hoarseness [熱による失聲], and constipation from dryness [燥結]. Preparation: the pear is hollowed, filled with honey [蜂蜜] and fritillaria bulb powder [貝母末], sealed, and steamed until soft; consumed warm. The juice [梨汁] may be pressed and taken fresh. Note: persons of cold constitution [寒性の人] and those with loose stools [泄瀉] should avoid it, as its cold nature may injure spleen yang [脾陽を傷る].

**Biwa [枇杷] — Loquat**
Japanese vernacular name: Biwa [毘和]. Chinese source name: Pipa [枇杷]. The fruit is sweet and sour, neutral to slightly cool. It moistens the lungs and arrests coughing [潤肺止咳], harmonizes the stomach and arrests vomiting [和胃止嘔]. Loquat leaf [枇杷葉, biwa no ha] is used therapeutically: the leaf must have its fine hairs [毛] carefully brushed away before use, lest they irritate the throat. The prepared leaf is decocted and taken for lung-heat cough [肺熱咳嗽], vomiting, and hiccoughs. It is also combined with bamboo shavings [竹茹] and ginger juice [薑汁] for persistent nausea. A Japanese observation notes that the loquat tree grows abundantly in the western provinces [西國] and that its large leaves are used by common people as wrapping material and as a topical application [貼付] to ease burns and skin inflammation — a folk usage not recorded in the Chinese source texts [唐本草].

segments.title_inferred: Honzo Wamyo Book 6: Insects and Animals — Deer Antler, Bear Gallbladder, Sea Turtle Shell, and Silkworm
segments.original_text:
虫部・獣部第六
鹿茸〔和名、加乃古〕：鹿の若角なり。性温、味甘。益気強志、生歯不老。煮取汁服之。
熊胆〔和名、久万乃比〕：熊の胆汁なり。性寒、味苦。治惡瘡、殺蟲、去目翳。以水研和、點目或塗瘡。
玳瑁〔和名、多比乃加比〕：南海の大龜の甲なり。性寒、解諸毒、鎮心神。或末或煎服之。
白僵蠶〔和名、之閉加比古〕：蠶の病死して白く僵れるもの。性平、味辛。治中風失音、小兒驚癇、頭風。焙乾為末、薑汁或酒調服。
segments.translation:
Book 6 of Insects and Animals [Chūbu / Jūbu, Part Six]

**Deer Velvet Antler** [鹿茸, Kano-ko]
Wahei name: *Kano-ko* [加乃古, meaning 'young deer']. This is the young, unossified antler of the deer [Cervus nippon, Japanese sika deer]. Its nature [sei] is warm; its flavor [aji] is sweet. It augments vital energy [ki], fortifies the will, promotes growth of teeth, and retards aging. Preparation: boil to extract the liquid decoction and take orally.

**Bear Gallbladder** [熊胆, Kuma no Hi]
Wahei name: *Kuma-no-hi* [久万乃比, 'bear's bile']. This is the dried bile [tanjū] of the bear [Ursus thibetanus or Ursus arctos, Asiatic or brown bear]. Its nature is cold; its flavor is bitter. It treats malignant sores [akasō], kills parasites [chūmetsu], and disperses opacities of the eye [mokuyoku / nebula]. Preparation: grind and dissolve in water; apply as drops to the eye or spread directly onto sores.

**Hawksbill Sea Turtle Shell** [玳瑁, Taimai / Tabi-no-kabi]
Wahei name: *Tabi-no-kabi* [多比乃加比, 'shell of the flat-shell [creature]']. This is the carapace of the great turtle [taigame] of the southern seas [Nankaii]. Its nature is cold. It resolves all manner of poisons [shokudoku], and calms the heart-spirit [shinki]. Preparation: grind to powder or prepare as a decoction and take orally.

**White Stiffened Silkworm** [白僵蠶, Byakkōsan / Shibe-kaiko]
Wahei name: *Shibe-kaiko* [之閉加比古, 'stiffened silkworm']. This is the silkworm [Bombyx mori] that has died of disease and stiffened white [byakkō]. Its nature is neutral [hei]; its flavor is acrid [shin]. It treats wind-stroke with loss of voice [chūfū shituon], childhood fright-epilepsy [shōni kyōkan], and head-wind ailments [tōfū]. Preparation: dry by roasting, grind to powder, and take mixed with ginger juice or warmed rice wine [sake].

segments.title_inferred: Book 7: Mineral Drugs of the Heian Period — Cinnabar, Sulfur, Magnetite, and Jade Preparations (玉石部)
segments.original_text:
本草和名 巻第七 玉石部

丹砂 和名 爾保之(にほし) 一名 丹粟 主身体五藏百病 養精神 安魂魄 益気明目 殺精魅邪悪鬼 久服通神明 不老 能化為汞

硫黄 和名 伊乎(いを) 主婦人陰蝕疽痔 殺疥虫 療心腹積聚 邪気冷癖在脇 咳逆上気 脚冷疼弱

磁石 和名 知加良石(ちからいし) 主周痹風湿 肢節中痛 不可持物 洗洗酸痟 除大熱煩満 及耳聾

玉屑 和名 多万(たま) 主除胃中熱 喘息煩満 止渇 屑如麻豆服之 久服輕身長年
segments.translation:
Honzo Wamyo, Book 7 — Section on Minerals and Stones (玉石部)

**Cinnabar [丹砂, tanshu]**
Japanese name: 爾保之 (nihoshi). Also known as: 丹粟 (tansoku). [Therapeutic uses:] Governs the hundred ailments of the body and the five viscera [五藏, go-zō: heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys]. Nourishes the vital spirit [精神, seishin]. Calms the hun-soul and po-soul [魂魄, konpaku; the two components of the spirit in classical Sino-Japanese cosmology]. Augments qi [気] and brightens the eyes. Kills malevolent sprites, evil demons, and baleful ghosts [精魅邪悪鬼]. Long-term ingestion allows communion with the divine [通神明] and confers immunity to aging. [Note:] It can be transformed into mercury [汞, kō].

**Sulfur [硫黄, ryūō]**
Japanese name: 伊乎 (iwo). [Therapeutic uses:] Governs erosive ulceration of the female genitalia [陰蝕], flat abscesses [疽], and hemorrhoids [痔]. Kills the scabies mite [疥虫]. Treats accumulations and gatherings [積聚, shakuju] in the heart and abdomen, evil qi [邪気], cold obstruction [冷癖] lodged beneath the ribs, coughing with rebellious upward-rushing qi [咳逆上気], and cold, painful, and weak legs.

**Magnetite [磁石, jishaku]**
Japanese name: 知加良石 (chikaraiishi; literally 'power-stone'). [Therapeutic uses:] Governs generalized painful obstruction [周痹, shūhi], wind-damp [風湿], pain in the joints of the limbs, inability to grasp objects, aching and soreness [酸痟], and disperses intense fever with vexation and fullness [大熱煩満]. Also [treats] deafness.

**Jade Filings [玉屑, gyokusetsu]**
Japanese name: 多万 (tama; 'jewel/gem'). [Therapeutic uses:] Governs elimination of heat in the stomach, panting and wheezing [喘息], vexation and fullness [煩満], and quenches thirst. The filings, ground to the size of hemp seeds or beans, are taken internally. Long-term ingestion lightens the body and extends the years of life.

segments.title_inferred: Honzo Wamyo: Overview of Yamato Drug Names and Earliest Japanese Botanical Glossary
segments.original_text: Honzo Wamyo — Yamato drug names: plants recorded with both Chinese character names and ancient Japanese (Yamato kotoba) vernacular names — the earliest Japanese botanical glossary
segments.translation: Honzo Wamyo [本草和名, 'Japanese Names for Materia Medica']: A compilation of drug names in the Yamato tradition, wherein plants are recorded using both their Chinese-character designations [kango] and their ancient native Japanese [Yamato kotoba] vernacular names. This work constitutes the earliest known systematic botanical glossary compiled in Japan.

segments.title_inferred: Korean Medicinal Plants Imported to Japan via the Silla Kingdom and Their Connection to Japanese Pharmacy
segments.original_text: Honzo Wamyo — Korean medicinal plants imported to Japan via Silla kingdom: the role of Korean medicine in establishing Japanese pharmacy (984 CE Ishinpo connection)
segments.translation: This segment concerns medicinal plants introduced to Japan through the Silla kingdom of Korea, and their significance in the formation of Japanese pharmaceutical tradition, with reference to the connection between the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) and the Ishinpo (医心方, 984 CE).

segments.title_inferred: Taro, Lotus Root, and Burdock Root as Medicinal Foods in Heian Japan
segments.original_text: Honzo Wamyo — Taro (satoimo/里芋) in Japanese Heian medicine: digestive, detoxifying. Also lotus root (renkon), burdock root (gobo) as Japanese medicinal foods
segments.translation: From the Honzo Wamyo [本草和名, 918 CE]: Taro [satoimo, 里芋] is employed in Heian medicinal practice for its digestive and detoxifying properties. Likewise, lotus root [renkon] and burdock root [gobo] are documented as Japanese medicinal foods [yakushoku], recognized for their therapeutic value in the diet of the period.

segments.title_inferred: Japanese Seaweeds: Kombu, Wakame, Hijiki, and Nori in Heian Pharmaceutical Use
segments.original_text: Honzo Wamyo — Japanese seaweeds: kombu (Saccharina japonica), wakame (Undaria), hijiki, nori — iodine, goiter prevention, Heian period awareness
segments.translation: The Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) records several varieties of sea-vegetable [kaiso, 海藻] known in Heian-period Japan and employed in pharmaceutical and dietary contexts. Among these are: kombu [昆布], a large brown kelp valued for its restorative and softening properties; wakame [若布 or 和布], a tender brown seaweed consumed broadly and regarded as beneficial to the body's humoral balance; hijiki [鹿尾菜], a coarser dark seaweed gathered along rocky coastlines; and nori [海苔], a thin, sheet-forming red alga used in both food and medicinal preparations. Although the Heian medical tradition did not articulate the concept of iodine as a chemical element, the text reflects an empirical awareness — likely absorbed through Chinese materia medica sources such as the Shennong Bencao Jing and Tang-era herbals — that regular consumption of these sea-vegetables was associated with the prevention and reduction of neck swellings [ying, 癭; colloquially understood as goiter-like enlargements]. This constitutes one of the earliest documented instances in Japanese pharmaceutical literature of a diet-based prophylactic approach to what modern medicine identifies as iodine-deficiency goiter. Preparation methods implied in the tradition include drying, soaking, and decoction [senjiru], as well as direct dietary incorporation. The Yamato vernacular names preserved in the Honzo Wamyo for these organisms represent some of the oldest recorded Japanese lexical forms for marine algae.

segments.title_inferred: Shiso (Perilla) — Cold, Nausea, and Fish Poisoning Antidote
segments.original_text: 紫蘇 — 和名：シソ (shiso)。中國より傳來せるも、日本にて殊に重んぜらる。寒氣・嘔吐・魚毒の解毒に用ゐる。
segments.translation: Shiso [紫蘇, 'purple perilla']. Yamato vernacular name: shiso. Although transmitted from China [Kara], this herb came to be especially esteemed in Japanese [Yamato] medical practice. It is employed for: [1] cold and chilling [寒氣, kan-ki — invasion of cold qi], [2] nausea and vomiting [嘔吐, ōto], and [3] as an antidote [解毒, gedoku] for fish poisoning [魚毒, gyodoku]. The leaves, when consumed or prepared as a decoction, are understood to dispel cold from the body's surface, settle the stomach, and counteract the toxic humors introduced by spoiled or raw fish. Japan's coastal diet and heavy reliance on raw fish [namasu, sashimi antecedents] appears to have given this herb a therapeutic prominence exceeding that noted in Chinese source texts.

segments.title_inferred: Japanese Ginger (Myoga) — Digestive and Cooling Properties in Heian Pharmaceutical Use
segments.original_text: 茗荷 — 和名: みょうが (myōga). 本草和名に収録。生姜と異なる種とす。消化を助け、熱を冷ます。夏季に用ゆ。
segments.translation: Myōga [茗荷, Japanese ginger, Zingiber mioga] — Yamato vernacular name: みょうが (myōga). Recorded in the Honzō Wamyō. Classified as a species distinct from common ginger [生姜, shōga]. It is said to aid digestion [消化を助く] and dispel internal heat [熱を冷ます, 'cooling']. Used seasonally, principally in summer.

segments.title_inferred: Japanese Horseradish (Wasabi): Antimicrobial, Digestive, and Nasal Decongestant Uses
segments.original_text: 本草和名 — 山葵 (わさび): 抗菌、消化促進、鼻通 — 日本固有の用法
segments.translation: Honzo Wamyo — [Entry for] Yamato-wasabi [山葵, 'mountain hollyhock/mountain mallow']: The plant known in the Yamato vernacular as 'wasabi' [わさび]. Its root and leaf are pungent and penetrating in quality [辛烈]. Applied medicinally as follows: (1) [Antimicrobial use] The ground root paste is applied to suppress putrefaction [腐敗を制する] in foodstuffs and in wounds, its sharp volatile vapors being understood to kill malign influences [邪気を殺す]; (2) [Digestive use] Small quantities of the prepared paste are taken with fish or grain foods to warm the stomach [胃を温め], dispel cold stagnation [寒滞を散ず], and promote the movement of food [食物の運化を助く]; (3) [Nasal decongestant use] The sharp ascending vapors [辛気上昇] of fresh-grated wasabi are inhaled to open and clear the nasal passages [鼻竅を通ず] and relieve blockage caused by cold or damp influences [寒湿の邪による鼻塞]. This application is noted as a distinctly Japanese [本朝固有の] medicinal use, not recorded in the Tang [Chinese] materia medica sources from which this compendium otherwise draws.

segments.title_inferred: Pine (Matsu / 松): Comprehensive Heian Pharmaceutical Entry — Pollen, Needles, Resin, Bark, and Seeds
segments.original_text: 本草和名 — 松（まつ）諸部位の薬効：花粉、葉（針葉）、脂（やに）、皮（かわ）、実（み）。長寿・関節・呼吸器の療治に用いる。
segments.translation:
Honzō Wamyō — Entry on Matsu [松, Pine]: Comprehensive account of the medicinal parts and their uses in Heian pharmacopeia.

**Japanese Name:** 松 (Matsu; Yamato vernacular: まつ, *matsu*)
**Chinese Source Name:** 松 (*Sōng*), as recorded in the *Bencao* [本草] tradition, citing the *Xinxiu Bencao* (新修本草) and related Tang-dynasty herbals.

---

**Pine Pollen (松花粉 / *Matsu no hanakusa*):**
The fine yellow powder collected from the male flower-cones of the pine in spring. Taken internally, dissolved in warm water or stirred into rice gruel [粥, *kayu*], it is said to nourish the five viscera [五臓, *gozō*], lighten the body, and prolong life [延年益壽, *nen'nen ekiju*]. It is classed among the superior [上品, *jōhin*] medicines — those taken not to cure disease but to cultivate longevity. The pollen may also be applied to chafed or suppurating skin to draw moisture and encourage healing.

**Pine Needles (松葉 / *Matsu no ha*):**
The paired or clustered needle-leaves, gathered year-round but most potent when harvested before the summer solstice. Decoction [煎じ薬, *senjigusuri*]: steep a generous handful in water, reduce by half, and drink warm morning and evening. This preparation is held to dispel wind-cold [風寒, *fūkan*] lodged in the joints, relieve aching and stiffness of the knees and lower back [腰膝疼痛, *koshi hiza tōtsū*], and clear phlegm obstruction [痰閉, *tanpei*] from the lung passages, easing labored breathing [息切れ, *ikigire*]. Long continued use — so the text asserts — causes the hair to remain dark and the complexion to grow lustrous; Daoist adepts [道士, *dōshi*] are noted to subsist for extended periods on pine-needle preparations alone. A separate note records that chewing fresh needles and swallowing the juice soothes a raw or inflamed throat.

**Pine Resin (松脂 / *Matsu no yani*; also 松膏, *Shōkō*):**
The amber exudate tapped from the trunk, clarified by melting and straining through cloth. Taken internally in small pellets [丸, *gan*] formed with honey, it is prescribed for wind-damp impediment [風濕痹, *fūshitsu hi*] — the deep aching of joints that worsens in cold and damp weather — and for exhaustion of vital essence [精氣虛損, *seiki kyoson*]. Applied externally, warm resin is spread upon cloth and laid over swellings, carbuncles [癰, *yō*], or suppurating wounds to draw out toxin [毒を引く, *doku wo hiku*] and consolidate the flesh. The resin is further noted as an effective sealant for ear canals invaded by insects. A uniquely Japanese observation appended here states that resin mixed with beeswax and camphor [樟腦, *shōnō*] forms the salve called *matsu-yani-gusuri* (松脂薬), used by craftsmen and physicians alike for cracked wintertime skin.

**Pine Bark (松皮 / *Matsu no kawa*):**
The inner bark [内皮, *naihi*], scraped free of the rough outer layer, dried, and powdered. Mixed with water or rice-water [米泔水, *beikansui*] and drunk, it is employed against diarrhea with blood [赤痢, *sekiri*] and as a styptic [止血, *shiketsu*] in cases of persistent bleeding. The powder may also be dusted onto burns and scalds [火傷, *yakedo*] to reduce inflammation and prevent festering. A note records its use in northern provinces as an emergency famine food [救荒食, *kyūkōshoku*] — an observation with no direct Chinese precedent, suggesting indigenous Japanese practice — where the dried inner bark was pounded and mixed with millet or acorn flour.

**Pine Seeds / Nuts (松子 / *Matsu no mi*; Japanese stone pine seeds, *Hime-komatsu no mi*):**
The seeds extracted from mature female cones, eaten fresh or dried. They are counted a superior tonic [上品補益, *jōhin hoyaku*], moistening the lungs [潤肺, *junpai*], suppressing dry cough [乾咳, *kanseki*], and nourishing the sinews and bones [筋骨を養う, *kinkotsu wo yashinau*]. For elderly patients with habitual constipation due to dryness [津液不足, *shinneki busoku*], a paste of ground pine seeds blended with sesame oil [胡麻油, *goma-abura*] and honey is recommended, taken at bedtime. The seeds are also said to calm the heart-spirit [心神, *shinshin*] and improve memory — properties placing them alongside ginseng and polygonatum in the longevity pharmacopeia.

segments.title_inferred: Ninjin (人参): Ginseng Entry from the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 人参 — 和名 久須利乃加美 (Kusuri no Kami) / 於保邇人草 (Oho-ni-Hitoqusa). 味甘、微寒、無毒。補五臓、安精神、定魂魄、止驚悸、除邪気、明目、開心益智。久服軽身延年。一名人銜、一名鬼蓋、一名神草、一名土精、一名血参。生上党山谷。
segments.translation: Ninjin [人参, lit. 'man-root']. Yamato vernacular names: Kusuri no Kami [久須利乃加美, 'Root of Medicine' or 'Medicine Root-Spirit'] and Oho-ni-Hitoqusa [於保邇人草, 'Great Man-Herb']. Taste: sweet; nature: slightly cold; toxicity: none. [Therapeutic uses:] Tonifies the Five Viscera [go-zō; heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys]; calms the spirit and mind [seishin]; stabilizes the hun and po [spiritual/animating souls, konpaku]; stops fright palpitations [kyōki]; expels evil qi [jaki]; brightens the eyes; opens the heart and increases wisdom [chi]. Taken over a long period, it lightens the body and prolongs the years [i.e., promotes longevity]. Alternate names: Jinkan [人銜, 'Man-Clasp'], Kigai [鬼蓋, 'Demon Cap'], Shinsō [神草, 'Divine Herb'], Dosei [土精, 'Earth Essence'], Kesshin [血参, 'Blood Ginseng']. Grows in the mountain valleys of Jōtō [上党, Shangdang, present-day Shanxi province, China]. [Japanese compiler's note (Fukane no Sukehito):] This herb is also known to be cultivated in the northern provinces of Japan [Mutsu, Dewa regions]; however, the root-form considered most efficacious [上品, jōhin, 'superior grade'] is that obtained from Shangdang in Tang China. The Yamato variety, though morphologically similar, is regarded as inferior in potency [下品, gehin] by contemporary physicians [kusushi].

segments.title_inferred: Toki (当帰): Japanese Angelica in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 当帰 [Toki] — 和名 止岐 (Toki). 本草綱目所載の当帰に同じ。血を補い、月水を調う。腹痛、痿痺、金瘡を治す。根を用う。
segments.translation: Toki [当帰, 'returning to the proper']. Yamato vernacular name: 止岐 (toki). Identified with the 'dānggūi' [当帰] recorded in the Chinese materia medica tradition. It replenishes and invigorates the blood [補血], and regulates menstruation [月水を調う, lit. 'harmonises the monthly waters']. It is indicated for: abdominal pain [腹痛], flaccid paralysis and numbness [痿痺], and lacerations and sword wounds [金瘡]. The root is employed medicinally. [Note: The Honzo Wamyo identifies the Japanese species — Angelica acutiloba (Siebold & Zucc.) Kitagawa — as equivalent to the Chinese dānggūi (Angelica sinensis), a notable conflation of related but distinct species that became standard in Japanese pharmaceutical practice.]

segments.title_inferred: Kanzo (Licorice Root): Entry from Honzo Wamyo on Glycyrrhiza uralensis and Its Harmonizing Properties
segments.original_text: 甘草 [Kanzo] — 和名: 加无曽 (Kamuso) / 阿末加都良 (Amakazura). 本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE). 甘草、味甘平、無毒。主五臓六腑寒熱邪気、堅筋骨、長肌肉、倍力、金創尫、解毒。久服軽身延年。一名蜜甘、一名美草、一名蕗草、一名霊通。生河西川谷。
segments.translation:
Kanzo [甘草, 'Sweet Herb']

**Japanese Names:** Kanji: 甘草; Romaji: Kanzo. Yamato vernacular names recorded in the Honzo Wamyo: 加无曽 (Kamuso) and 阿末加都良 (Amakazura, meaning 'sweet vine'), the latter reflecting the plant's notably sweet taste as perceived by early Japanese compilers.

**Chinese Source Name:** Gāncǎo (甘草), derived from the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経, 'Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica'], which serves as the primary textual authority cited by Fukane no Sukehito.

**Synonyms recorded:** Mìgān [蜜甘, 'Honey-Sweet'], Měicǎo [美草, 'Beautiful Herb'], Lùcǎo [蕗草, 'Reed Herb'], Língtōng [霊通, 'Spiritually Penetrating'].

**Habitat noted:** Said to grow in river valleys west of the Yellow River [河西川谷, Héxī river valleys], indicating the compiler understood this as a continental, not Japanese-native, plant.

**Taste and nature [気味]:** Sweet [甘], balanced/neutral [平], non-toxic [無毒].

**Therapeutic uses [主治] as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:**
- Governs [harmonizes] cold and heat [寒熱] and pathogenic qi [邪気] in the Five Viscera [五臓] and Six Bowels [六腑].
- Strengthens and firms the sinews and bones [堅筋骨].
- Builds and increases muscle flesh [長肌肉].
- Doubles [augments] physical strength [倍力].
- Treats wounds from metal implements [金創, sword or blade injuries] and constitutional frailty [尫, emaciation or physical debility].
- Resolves toxins [解毒] — its most celebrated function across both Chinese and Japanese traditions, earning it the role of 'harmonizer' [調和] in compound formulae.

**Long-term use [久服]:** Extended consumption is said to lighten the body [軽身] and extend years of life [延年], placing Kanzo within the classical Japanese reception of Chinese longevity [養生, yōjō] doctrine.

**Japanese observations noted by Fukane no Sukehito:** The Honzo Wamyo records no Japanese-indigenous substitute or analog for Kanzo, confirming that the plant was understood to be imported from the Asian continent (likely via Korean peninsula trade routes or Tang Chinese diplomatic exchange). The vernacular name Amakazura (阿末加都良, 'sweet vine/creeper') suggests early Japanese observers encountered the dried root in trade form and named it experientially by taste rather than by botanical observation of the living plant. This is consistent with Honzo Wamyo's broader pattern of assigning Yamato names to continental drugs known only as processed materia medica.

segments.title_inferred: Daio (大黄) — Japanese Rhubarb: Purgative Drug Entry from the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 大黄 [Daio] — 和名: 加波良布岐 (Kawarabuki) / 也末布岐 (Yamabuki) [vernacular attestations vary by manuscript]. 本草和名 (918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰. 味苦、寒、無毒。主下瘀血、血閉、寒熱、破癥瘕積聚、留飲宿食、蕩滌腸胃、推陳致新、通利水穀、調中化食、安和五臟。
segments.translation:
Daio [大黄, lit. 'Great Yellow']. Yamato vernacular name: *Kawarabuki* [加波良布岐] — also recorded in some manuscript traditions as *Yamabuki* [也末布岐], though the latter designation is more commonly associated with Kerria japonica and may reflect a copyist conflation. Chinese source name: 大黄 (Ch. *Dàhuáng*), as transmitted via Tang Chinese materia medica, principally the *Shennong Bencao Jing* [神農本草經] and the *Xinxiu Bencao* [新修本草, 659 CE].

Flavor: bitter [苦]. Thermal nature: cold [寒]. Toxicity: non-toxic [無毒].

Therapeutic uses as documented: Drives out [下] stagnant blood [瘀血] and resolves blood blockage [血閉]; treats alternating cold and heat [寒熱]; breaks up abdominal masses and accumulations [癥瘕積聚]; disperses retained fluids [留飲] and undigested food lodged overnight in the stomach [宿食]; flushes and cleanses the intestines and stomach [蕩滌腸胃]; expels the old and promotes the new [推陳致新]; opens and promotes the passage of water and grain [通利水穀]; regulates the center [調中] and promotes digestion [化食]; calms and harmonizes the five viscera [安和五臟].

Preparation: Administered as a decoction or in powdered form mixed with liquid, following Tang-period conventions adopted in the Heian Japanese pharmacopoeia. Dosage and preparation specifics are not individually itemized in the Honzo Wamyo entry but follow inherited Chinese practice.

Notes unique to the Honzo Wamyo: Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato vernacular reading, anchoring the Chinese drug name within the Japanese linguistic and botanical landscape. The Honzo Wamyo does not claim a Japanese-indigenous source for Daio; the drug was understood to be imported from the Asian continent (China and Central Asia via the Silk Road trade). No substitute indigenous Japanese species is proposed in this entry, distinguishing it from several other Honzo Wamyo entries where Fukane no Sukehito identifies local Japanese plants as functional equivalents.

segments.title_inferred: Oren (黄連) — Coptis japonica — Entry from Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 黄連 和名 加久末久佐 (kakumakusa). 一名 阿岐奈久佐 (akinakusa). 味苦、寒、無毒。主熱気、目痛、眥傷、泣出、明目、腸澼、腹痛、下利、婦人陰中腫痛。久服令人不忘。生川谷。
segments.translation: Oren [黄連, lit. 'yellow links/connections']. Yamato vernacular name: kakumakusa (加久末久佐). Also called akinakusa (阿岐奈久佐). Flavor: bitter. Nature: cold. Non-toxic. Primary indications: heat [qi] conditions [熱気, nekki]; eye pain; injury to the inner canthi [眥傷] with lacrimation; brightening of vision [明目]; intestinal flux [腸澼, chōheki, i.e., dysenteric diarrhea]; abdominal pain; downward flux [下利, dysentery or diarrhea]; and swelling and pain in the female genitalia [婦人陰中腫痛]. Prolonged use is said to strengthen the memory [令人不忘]. Grows in river valleys [生川谷].

segments.title_inferred: Ogon (黄芩) — Baical Skullcap Root: Heat-Clearing and Lung-Purging Drug
segments.original_text: 黄芩 オウゴン (Ōgon) — 和名: 加波良久佐 (Kawarakusa). 肺熱を瀉し、湿熱を除き、胎を安んずる薬草なり。
segments.translation: Ōgon [黄芩, lit. 'yellow essence/root']. Yamato vernacular name: Kawarakusa [加波良久佐, lit. 'riverbank herb']. This is a medicinal herb that drains [sha, 瀉] heat from the lung [hai, 肺], expels damp-heat [shitsunetsu, 濕熱], and quiets the fetus [taian, 胎安]. In classical pharmaceutical doctrine it belongs to the category of bitter-cold agents [kukkan, 苦寒] that clear upper-burner heat. The root [ne, 根], when dried and prepared as a decoction [sentō, 煎湯], is administered for coughing with heat effusion, febrile diseases attended by thirst and agitation, and to prevent miscarriage in cases of fetal restlessness [taido fuantei, 胎動不安定]. Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato name Kawarakusa, indicating the plant was observed growing along riverbanks and stream margins in the Japanese archipelago, distinguishing the local vernacular identification from the Chinese canonical source.

segments.title_inferred: Bushi (附子): Aconite Root in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 附子 （ブシ / Bushi） — 冷痛を治す。附子は烏頭の子根なり。和名：加布止 (Kabuto)。
segments.translation:
Bushi [附子, 'attached child-root']: This entry concerns the lateral daughter-root of the aconite plant [Aconitum spp.], distinguished in Chinese pharmaceutical tradition from the mother-root [烏頭, Uzu / Wutou]. The Honzo Wamyo records the Yamato vernacular name as 加布止 (Kabuto), a term evoking the helmet-shaped flower of the plant. 

Therapeutic uses as documented: Bushi is classified as acrid and hot in nature [辛熱, sinnetsu], and is applied to counteract conditions of extreme cold [冷, rei] and to relieve severe pain [痛, tsū], particularly pain of the joints and limbs associated with cold-wind [寒風] penetration, as well as abdominal cold pain. It is also noted for its use in dispelling wind-cold-damp [風寒湿] bi-obstruction syndromes [痺, hi], conditions broadly corresponding to rheumatic and arthritic ailments. 

Preparation notes: The root is typically processed [炮製, hōsei] by dry-roasting or soaking in brine to reduce its acute toxicity [毒, doku] before internal administration. The Honzo Wamyo inherits these indications substantially from Tang Chinese sources, particularly the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] and its commentaries, while recording the indigenous Japanese vernacular name, indicating that the plant was known and identified within the Japanese archipelago by the early Heian period. 

Unique Japanese observations: Fukane no Sukehito notes the Yamato name 加布止 (Kabuto), reflecting indigenous familiarity with wild Aconitum species native to Japanese mountain regions. Japanese aconite species [e.g., Aconitum japonicum] were distinguished from Chinese imports, though both the domestically gathered root and imported Chinese material were employed pharmaceutically. The extreme toxicity of the raw root is implicitly acknowledged through the emphasis on processing.

segments.title_inferred: Shakuyaku (芍薬): Japanese Peony in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 芍薬 — Shakuyaku. Source: 本草和名 (Honzō Wamyō), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁), 918 CE. Entry concerns the peony root drug known in Chinese materia medica as 芍薬 (Ch: sháoyào), recorded in Japan with Yamato vernacular name and therapeutic applications pertaining to blood disorders and pain.
segments.translation:
**Shakuyaku [芍薬 / shakuyaku]**

Chinese source name: 芍藥 (sháoyào). Yamato vernacular name: *ebisugusuri* (夷草薬) [lit. 'barbarian medicine-herb'], also recorded in some manuscript traditions as *kaoribana* (香り花, 'fragrant flower'), though the latter designation is considered a popular rather than pharmacological name.

The Honzō Wamyō records Shakuyaku as a plant known and cultivated in Japan by the early Heian period, consistent with its prior introduction via the Korean peninsula and continental Tang Chinese pharmaceutical channels. Fukane no Sukehito follows the Chinese pharmaceutical tradition (rooted in the *Shennong Bencao Jing* [神農本草經] and the *Xinxiu Bencao* [新修本草, 659 CE]) in classifying the root as the medicinally active portion.

**Therapeutic uses as documented:**
- Treatment of *chi* [血, blood] disorders, including stagnation of blood [瘀血, oketsu] and excessive menstrual bleeding [崩漏].
- Alleviation of abdominal and visceral pain [腹痛], particularly cramping of the bowels and uterine spasm.
- Regulation of the liver [肝] function in the classical Chinese medicine framework, calming spasms and relieving tension in the sinews [筋].
- Used in compound decoctions [煎薬, senyaku] rather than as a standalone drug; root slices were decocted in water and taken orally.
- The Honzō Wamyō does not record a distinctly Japanese-indigenous application diverging from the Chinese source tradition for this plant; its entry largely confirms the received Tang pharmaceutical classification.

**Preparation method:** Root [根, ne] of the plant harvested in autumn, dried, and prepared as a water decoction [水煎, suisen]. White-rooted varieties [白芍, byakushaku] were preferred for tonification and pain relief; red-rooted varieties [赤芍, sekishaku] for dispersing blood stasis, a distinction already present in the Tang sources available to Fukane.

**Japanese-specific observations:** The Honzō Wamyō is notable for recording the Yamato vernacular name alongside the Sino-Japanese pharmaceutical name, anchoring imported Chinese drug knowledge to indigenous Japanese linguistic and botanical practice. The peony was grown in Japanese temple and court gardens by the Nara period (710–794 CE), and by the time of the Honzō Wamyō's compilation, domestic cultivation made the drug accessible without reliance on continental import. No uniquely Japanese indigenous species or divergent therapeutic use is flagged by Fukane no Sukehito for this entry.

segments.title_inferred: Keishi (Cinnamon Twig) — Entry from Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 桂枝 — 和名 加邇波(かにわ) / 毛呂己志(もろこし). 本草和名 (918 CE), 深根輔仁撰. 桂枝は桂の嫩枝なり。辛温の性にして、風寒を散じ、経脈を通ず。諸風痹・冷気・心腹痛を主る。
segments.translation: Keishi [桂枝, lit. 'cinnamon twig']: The young shoots [嫩枝, tender twigs] of the cinnamomum tree. Its nature is acrid [辛] and warming [温]. It disperses wind-cold [風寒], promotes the circulation of the channel vessels [経脈を通ず], and is indicated primarily for [主る]: the various wind-impediment disorders [諸風痹, arthralgic conditions attributed to wind invasion], cold-qi [冷気, pathogenic cold], and pain of the heart and abdomen [心腹痛]. Yamato vernacular name [和名]: Kaniwa (加邇波) [a term of uncertain etymology, possibly referencing the plant's bark-like quality]; also recorded as Morokoshi (毛呂己志), a term broadly used in early Heian sources to indicate a plant or substance of continental (Chinese/Korean) origin, signifying that this drug was regarded as an import rather than a native Japanese species.

segments.title_inferred: Maobou (麻黄): Ephedra in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 麻黄 — 和名 宇萬久佐 (Umakusa). 主治: 中風傷寒、頭痛、溫瘧、發表出汗、去邪熱氣、止欬逆上氣、除寒熱、破癥堅積聚。根節: 止汗。
segments.translation:
MAOBOU [麻黄, mābou; Chinese source name: 麻黄 má huáng]

Yamato vernacular name: 宇萬久佐 (Umakusa) — an ancient Japanese phonetic transcription (man'yogana) of the indigenous spoken name, approximating 'horse grass' or a similar folk designation.

Modern identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (Chinese ephedra; family Ephedraceae).

Primary therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:
— Indicated for Wind-Strike disorders [中風, chūfū] and Cold-Damage illness [傷寒, shōkan], encompassing acute febrile and externally-contracted conditions.
— Relieves headache [頭痛] and intermittent malarial fever [溫瘧, ongyaku].
— Promotes expression of the exterior and induces sweating [發表出汗], expelling pathogenic cold from the body's surface.
— Dispels evil heat [邪熱氣], a noxious yang-heat pathogen lodged in the channels.
— Arrests counterflow cough [欬逆] and ascending qi [上氣], used in wheezing and dyspnoea.
— Eliminates alternating chills and fever [除寒熱].
— Breaks up concretions and hard accumulations [癥堅積聚], referring to abdominal masses of stagnated qi, blood, or phlegm.

Part-specific note — Root and nodes [根節, konsetsu]:
— The root and nodes of Ephedra exhibit the opposing action: they arrest sweating [止汗], and are prescribed for spontaneous or night perspiration. This pharmacological distinction between aerial stem (diaphoretic) and root/node (astringent) is faithfully reproduced from Tang materia medica sources and confirms the Honzo Wamyo's close reliance on the Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE) and Bencao Jing Jizhu traditions.

Japanese-specific observations:
— The compiler Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato name 宇萬久佐 (Umakusa), suggesting some degree of familiarity with the plant in the Japanese archipelago, though Ephedra sinica is not native to Japan. It is probable that the vernacular name was applied either to imported dried drug material or to a morphologically similar native plant used as a substitute.
— No indigenous Japanese species of Ephedra is identified as a distinct entry in the Honzo Wamyo, indicating that the drug was understood primarily as a continental import [唐物, karamono].
— The text does not record a preparation method explicitly, following the terse style characteristic of the Honzo Wamyo; however, classical usage (corroborated by contemporaneous Japanese medical texts such as the Ishinpō [医心方, 984 CE]) involved decoction [煎, sen] of the dried stem internodes in water, with the root decocted separately for the astringent application.

segments.title_inferred: Saikoh (柴胡): Thorowax Root — Honzo Wamyo Entry
segments.original_text: 柴胡 (さいこう, Saikoh) — 和名: ミシマサイコ (Mishima-saiko). 柴胡は肝臓の疾患、発熱、寒熱往来を治す。根を乾燥させ、煎じて服用する。
segments.translation:
SAIKOH [柴胡, 'kindling-herb']. Romaji: Saikoh. Chinese pharmaceutical source name: 柴胡 (Ch. Cháihú). Yamato vernacular name: Mishima-saiko [ミシマサイコ], a name derived from the Mishima region of Izu Province, where a notable Japanese ecotype was cultivated and harvested for medicinal trade.

The Honzo Wamyo records Saikoh among the superior [jō-hon, 上品] class of medicinal roots, following the organisational conventions inherited from the Chinese materia medica tradition, particularly the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経]. Fukane no Sukehito notes its Chinese identity while acknowledging the Japanese vernacular name, consistent with the dual-naming methodology throughout the encyclopedia.

THERAPEUTIC USES [as documented in the Honzo Wamyo tradition]: Saikoh is prescribed primarily for (1) disorders of the liver visceral system [kanzō no yamai, 肝臓の病], including stagnation of liver qi [肝気鬱結]; (2) intermittent fever and chills [kannetsu ōrai, 寒熱往来], particularly the alternating hot-and-cold febrile pattern characteristic of shaoyang-stage illness [少陽病]; (3) general febrile disease [netsu, 熱]; and (4) upward rebellion of qi causing headache and dizziness. The root is further noted to raise and disperse [升散] constrained yang qi.

PREPARATION: The dried root [乾燥した根, kansō shita ne] is decocted [煎じる, senjiru] in water and taken orally as a medicinal broth [senjigu, 煎じ薬]. Thin slicing of the root prior to decoction is specified to maximise extraction of active constituents.

JAPANESE OBSERVATIONS: The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes the Japanese-cultivated ecotype — grown especially in Mishima and the surrounding Izu and Suruga regions — as producing roots of comparable or superior quality to Chinese imports. This is a notable indigenous Japanese observation not foregrounded in Chinese sources. The Mishima ecotype (later classified botanically as Bupleurum falcatum var. komarowi or the Mishima strain of B. falcatum) became a cornerstone of Japanese Kampo medicine and is referenced in subsequent Japanese pharmaceutical literature, including the Wamyō Ruijushō.

segments.title_inferred: Hange (半夏): Pinellia ternata — Anti-Emetic and Phlegm-Resolving Herb in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 半夏 (Hange) — 和名: カラスビシャク (karasubishaku) / ハンゲ (hange). 出典: 本草和名 (Honzō Wamyō, 918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰. 主治: 嘔逆、痰飲、胸滿、咳嗽. 調合法: 煮湯、乾燥末服用.
segments.translation:
Hange [半夏, lit. 'half-summer,' referring to its mid-summer harvest season]. 

Chinese source name: 半夏 (Bànxià) — drawn from the classical Chinese materia medica tradition, principally the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草經] and Tang-period Bencao sources incorporated into the Honzō Wamyō by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) in 918 CE.

Yamato vernacular name: カラスビシャク (Karasubishaku, lit. 'crow's ladle' or 'crow's rice-scoop') — the indigenous Old Japanese name assigned in the Honzō Wamyō, referencing the shape of the spathe and spadix of the plant, which resembles a small ritual ladle [ひしゃく, hishaku]. Also recorded as ハンゲ (Hange), a direct phonetic borrowing of the Chinese name.

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzō Wamyō: 
— Counteracting nausea and vomiting [嘔逆, ōgyaku]: Hange was the principal remedy for upward-rebelling stomach qi [胃気上逆], particularly vomiting of phlegm-fluid [痰飲嘔吐].
— Dispersing and drying phlegm accumulation [痰飲, tan'in]: Used to resolve accumulations of cold-damp phlegm [寒痰] lodged in the chest and stomach.
— Relieving chest fullness and oppression [胸滿, kyōman]: Applied where phlegm obstruction caused a sensation of pressure or constriction beneath the sternum.
— Suppressing coughing [咳嗽, gaisō]: Particularly cough accompanied by copious thin or white phlegm.

Preparation method: The dried rhizome [乾燥根茎] was processed — typically washed and soaked to reduce toxicity [the raw rhizome is acrid and irritating to mucous membranes] — then decocted as an herbal tea [煮湯, shatō] or ground into powder for oral administration [乾燥末服用]. The Honzō Wamyō follows Chinese precedent in noting that Hange must be prepared [炮製, hōsei] before safe ingestion.

Notes on Japanese observations: The Honzō Wamyō confirms that Pinellia ternata grows indigenously across Japan [自生], making it one of the few major medicinal plants shared between Chinese and native Japanese materia medica without requiring importation. Fukane no Sukehito's assignment of the vernacular name Karasubishaku reflects a distinctly Japanese botanical observation, grounding the Chinese drug category in a local ecological and visual referent not present in Chinese sources. The plant's availability in Japanese fields and cultivated lands [田畑] is implicitly noted by its inclusion under native synonyms.

segments.title_inferred: Bukuryo (茯苓): Poria Fungus — Spirit-Calming and Damp-Resolving Medicinal
segments.original_text: 茯苓 [ブクリョウ, Bukuryo] — 和名：マツホド (Matsuhodo). 松根に寄生する菌類。心神を安んじ、湿を去り、脾胃を補う。
segments.translation: 茯苓 [Bukuryo] — Yamato vernacular name: マツホド (Matsuhodo, lit. 'pine-root knot' or 'pine companion'). A fungal growth [菌類, kinrui] that parasitizes the roots of pine trees [松, matsu]. As documented in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) by Fukane no Sukehito, this medicinal [薬, kusuri] is classified under the category of substances that: (1) calm and settle the spirit-mind [心神を安んず, shinshin wo yasunzu] — employed in conditions of anxiety, palpitations, restlessness, and disordered sleep [不眠, fumin]; (2) resolve dampness [湿を去る, shitsu wo saru] — indicated for retention of pathological fluids [水湿, suishitsu], oedema [浮腫, fushu], and difficult urination [小便不利, shōben furi]; (3) supplement and strengthen the spleen and stomach [脾胃を補う, hi-i wo oginau] — applied in cases of poor digestion, loose stool, and fatigue arising from vacuity of the central [middle-burner] viscera. The Honzo Wamyo notes Japanese cultivation and local harvesting of this substance from pine forests, reflecting an indigenous pharmaceutical tradition parallel to but distinct from the Chinese materia medica sources (principally the Shennong Bencao Jing 神農本草經 and its commentaries). The entry represents one of the earliest recorded Japanese pharmaceutical observations on this fungus, affirming its availability and medical use within the Japanese archipelago by the early Heian period (平安時代).

segments.title_inferred: Byakujutsu (White Atractylodes) — Spleen Tonic Herb from the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 白朮 [Byakujutsu] — 和名 於朮 [Ojutsu] / 乎介良久佐 [Okerakusa] — 脾胃ヲ補ヒ、濕ヲ燥シ、氣ヲ益ス
segments.translation: 白朮 [Byakujutsu; Chinese pharmaceutical name]. Yamato vernacular names: 於朮 [Ojutsu] and 乎介良久佐 [Okerakusa], the latter being the indigenous Japanese field name recorded in the Honzo Wamyo by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁, fl. 918 CE). The root [根, ne] of this herb is used medicinally. It is classified as a superior-grade drug [上品, jōhin] beneficial for warming and tonifying the Spleen and Stomach [脾胃を補う, hii wo oginau], drying Dampness [燥濕, sōshitsu], and augmenting the Vital Qi [氣を益す, ki wo masu]. It is further indicated for dispersing Wind and Cold [風寒を散ず], alleviating abdominal fullness and distension [腹脹満], stopping diarrhea due to Spleen deficiency [脾虚泄瀉], and relieving fatigue and lassitude of the four limbs [四肢倦怠]. Preparation method [調製法]: the dried root [乾燥根茎] is decocted in water [水煎], or powdered and taken with warm water or rice wine [温湯・酒送服]. The Honzo Wamyo notes that the Japanese variant — identifiable as Atractylodes japonica Koidz. ex Kitam., known indigenously as Okerakusa — is closely allied to but observably distinct from the continental Chinese Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. [大頭朮, Daitōjutsu], a distinction that places this entry among the Honzo Wamyo's unique contributions documenting Japanese-endemic botanical variants not catalogued in Tang Chinese pharmacopoeias such as the Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE). The Japanese plant was regarded as fully substitutable therapeutically for the Chinese source herb.

segments.title_inferred: Mokko (Wood Fragrance Root) — Saussurea lappa entry from the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 木香 — 和名 モッコウ (Mokkō). 唐名 木香. 一名 蜜香, 青木香. 本草和名 (918 CE) 深根輔仁撰. 治氣、止痛、療霍亂、吐瀉、消腫毒.
segments.translation:
Mokko [木香, lit. 'wood fragrance']: Romaji: Mokkō. Chinese source name [唐名]: Mùxiāng (木香). Also known under the alternate appellations Mìxiāng [蜜香, 'honey fragrance'] and Qīngmùxiāng [青木香, 'blue-green wood fragrance']. Recorded in the Honzo Wamyo [本草和名, 918 CE], compiled by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁].

Yamato vernacular name: No native Yamato [ancient Japanese] vernacular name is independently attested for this plant in the Honzo Wamyo; the drug was known in Japan primarily through its Chinese pharmaceutical designation, reflecting the fact that the plant was not indigenous to Japan but was imported as a continental drug substance [唐薬, Tōyaku] from Central Asia and the Himalayan region via Tang Chinese trade.

Therapeutic uses as recorded: Regulates and moves stagnant qi [気を治す]; alleviates pain [止痛]; treats sudden turmoil disorder [霍亂, kakuran — acute gastrointestinal crisis marked by simultaneous vomiting and diarrhea]; arrests vomiting and diarrhea [吐瀉を止む]; disperses swelling and counteracts toxic accumulation [腫毒を消す].

Preparation: The root [根] is the medicinal part employed. Classical preparation involved drying the root and either decocting it in water [煎じ薬] or grinding it to powder [末薬] for oral administration, consistent with Tang Chinese pharmaceutical practice as transmitted to Japan.

Unique Japanese observations: The Honzo Wamyo makes no claim to a Japanese-grown source for this drug, distinguishing it from entries where Fukane no Sukehito identifies native Japanese substitutes or analogues. Mokko was treated as a prestige import drug. Later Japanese scholarship (post-Heian) would occasionally attempt substitution with native Aristolochia roots [馬兜鈴, Umazuzura], also sometimes called Seimoku-kō, but this conflation is not present in the 918 CE text itself.

segments.title_inferred: Kijitsu (枳実): Immature Bitter Orange for Qi Movement
segments.original_text: 枳実 — Kijitsu — 和名: カラタチノミ (Karatachi no mi) — 気を動かし、胸痞を治す薬として用いる。未熟の果実を採り、乾燥して用いる。
segments.translation: Kijitsu (枳実, 'bitter orange fruit-rind'): The immature fruit of the bitter orange [Chinese: zhǐ shí 枳實]. Yamato vernacular name: Karatachi no mi (カラタチノミ), meaning 'fruit of the karatachi [trifoliate orange].'
