Documented in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) as a drug for moving and regulating the flow of qi [qi movement, 気の運行], dispersing accumulations in the chest [胸痞, kyōhi; thoracic fullness and obstruction], resolving distension of the abdomen [腹脹, fukuchō], and expelling phlegm and stagnant food [痰飲・食滞]. The unripe fruit is harvested before maturation, then sun-dried or shade-dried for pharmaceutical preparation. Administration is typically as a decoction [煎薬, senyaku] or as a powdered drug blended into compound formulas. Fukane no Sukehito notes that the karatachi cultivated in Japan (Poncirus trifoliata) serves as the indigenous Japanese equivalent to the Chinese zhǐ shí derived from Citrus aurantium, and that the Japanese variety was recognized as pharmaceutically valid and locally available — a notable Japanese observation distinguishing the Honzo Wamyo from its Chinese source texts. The drug acts upon the Spleen and Stomach visceral systems [脾胃, hii] and is classified among drugs that break up and descend accumulations [破気消積薬].

segments.title_inferred: Chinpi (陳皮): Aged Citrus Peel in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 陳皮 (Chinpi) — 久しく陳ねたる橘の皮。痰を去り、気を調う。和名: 久知奈之 (Kuchinashi) / 橘の皮 (Tachibana no kawa)。
segments.translation:
Chinpi [陳皮, lit. 'aged/stored peel']: The dried and aged peel of the citrus fruit [橘, tachibana]. As documented in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) compiled by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁], this drug enters the classical materia medica as a preparation derived from the outer rind of Citrus fruits that have been stored [陳, chin — 'aged, long-kept'] until the volatile qualities have mellowed and the medicinal properties concentrated. 

Yamato vernacular name [和名, yamato-kotoba]: The peel is referred to in early Japanese records as 'Tachibana no kawa' [橘の皮], meaning 'skin/bark of the tachibana orange,' the tachibana (Citrus tachibana or Citrus reticulata var.) being among the earliest citrus varieties known to Japan, considered indigenous or very early-naturalized. The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes this Japanese variety from the continental Chinese 橘 (jú), noting the tachibana as a plant of particular Japanese provenance — one of the rare instances where the encyclopedia acknowledges a locally established variant alongside the Chinese source drug. 

Therapeutic uses [主治, shuchi]: Chinpi is indicated primarily for (1) the dispersal and downward-directing of phlegm [痰を去る, tan wo saru — 'to expel phlegm/mucus accumulations'], and (2) the rectification and harmonization of qi [気を調う, ki wo totonou — 'to regulate the vital breath/functional energy']. These two actions correspond to its classical role in treating cough with productive expectoration, chest oppression, and digestive stagnation caused by rebellious qi [逆気, gyakki]. 

Preparation method [炮製, hōzei]: The peel is separated from the fruit, allowed to dry, and stored for an extended period — the aging process [陳, chin] being considered essential to reduce the sharpness of the fresh peel's dispersing action and render it more balanced in therapeutic temperament. Aged peels of several years were prized over fresh dried material. 

Unique Japanese observations: The Honzo Wamyo is notable for recording the tachibana [橘] as a plant cultivated or growing wild in Japan itself, not solely imported from China, representing one of the encyclopedia's contributions to documenting the Japanese pharmaceutical landscape as distinct from its Tang Chinese sources. The tachibana held deep cultural significance in Japan predating its pharmaceutical use, appearing in Nara-period poetry [万葉集, Man'yōshū], which Fukane no Sukehito's work implicitly situates within a native tradition alongside the continental drug canon.

segments.title_inferred: Orenno (黄連湯): Coptis Decoction Formula in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 黄連湯 (Orenno) — 黄連 (Oren / Coptis japonica) を主薬とする湯液処方。和名：オウレン、またはカクマグサ。腸胃の熱、心下痞、下痢、嘔吐を治す。根茎を煎じて服用す。
segments.translation:
Orenno (黄連湯, 'Yellow-Link Decoction') — A medicinal decoction [tōyaku, 湯薬] compounded principally from Oren (黄連, Coptis japonica Makino), as recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁] under imperial commission. 

Japanese Name (Kanji): 黄連湯 
Romaji: Orenno (or Ōren-tō in later reading conventions) 
Yamato Vernacular Name: カクマグサ (Kakumagusa), also rendered オウレン (Ōren) in native phonetic tradition; the epithet 'Kakumagusa' ('angle-joint grass') likely refers to the distinctive knotted rhizome of the plant indigenous to Japanese mountain provinces. 
Chinese Source Name: 黄連湯 (Huánglián Tāng), derived from the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] tradition and the Shanghanlun [傷寒論] formulary of Zhang Zhongjing. 

Therapeutic Uses (as documented in Honzo Wamyo context): 
— Clears [heat toxin, 熱毒] from the stomach and intestines [腸胃之熱] 
— Resolves 'fullness and obstruction below the heart' [心下痞, shinkaphi], a classical diagnostic category indicating epigastric distension 
— Treats dysentery [下痢, geri] and diarrhea arising from damp-heat patterns 
— Counteracts nausea and vomiting [嘔吐, ōto] 
— Applied in febrile disorders [傷寒, shōkan] with concurrent gastrointestinal disturbance 

Preparation Method: The dried rhizome [根茎, konkei] of Coptis japonica is decocted [煎じる, senjiru] in water and administered warm as a liquid preparation [湯液, tōeki]. In the Japanese elaboration recorded by Fukane no Sukehito, the formula may incorporate adjuvant ingredients [佐使薬] such as Keishi (桂枝, Cinnamomum cassia bark) and Kanzo (甘草, Glycyrrhiza uralensis root) following the Shanghanlun composite formula, though the Honzo Wamyo's primary interest is the identification and naming of the chief drug, Oren itself. 

Unique Japanese Observations: Fukane no Sukehito notably records that Coptis japonica grown in Japanese mountain provinces (particularly Yamashiro [山城] and Ōmi [近江]) is considered of superior quality [上品, jōhin] to imported Chinese material, an early assertion of domestic pharmaceutical sufficiency. The Yamato name Kakumagusa is indigenous to Japan and does not appear in Chinese materia medica sources, reflecting the Honzo Wamyo's dual project of sinological scholarship and native botanical documentation.

segments.title_inferred: Sansho (Mountain Pepper): Japanese Pepper Entry from Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 山椒 — Sansho. 和名 阿佐久良 (Asakura). 蜀椒の類に属す。辛味あり、熱性。腹中の冷えを治し、齒痛を止め、殺虫の効あり。又、嘔吐・下痢を治す。葉・実ともに用いる。
segments.translation:
**Sansho [Mountain Pepper]** (山椒, *sansho*)

**Chinese source name:** 蜀椒 (*Shokushō*; Chinese Sichuan pepper, a cognate taxon recognized in the *Shennong Bencao Jing* [Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica]). The Honzo Wamyo places Sansho within the same categorical grouping [*rui*, 類] as Sichuan pepper, acknowledging taxonomic kinship while distinguishing the Japanese species as a native variant.

**Yamato vernacular name:** 阿佐久良 (*Asakura*) — an archaic Yamato reading, likely referencing a regional Japanese placename (Asakura) associated with renowned cultivation sites, a naming convention attested elsewhere in the Honzo Wamyo for indigenous Japanese plants.

**Nature and flavor [性味, *seimi*]:** Pungent [辛, *shin*]; thermally Hot [熱性, *nessei*]. Classified among warming [温, *on*] medicinals.

**Therapeutic uses [主治, *shuchi*]:**
1. Treats cold [冷え, *hie*] and cold accumulation within the abdomen [腹中, *fukuchū*]; resolves interior cold patterns [裏寒, *rikan*].
2. Arrests toothache [齒痛, *shitsū*].
3. Possesses vermicidal [殺虫, *satchū*] efficacy; expels intestinal parasites.
4. Treats vomiting [嘔吐, *ōto*] and diarrhea [下痢, *geri*].

**Parts used [薬用部位, *yakuyō buii*]:** Both the leaves [葉, *ha*] and the berries/fruit [実, *mi*] are employed medicinally. This dual-part usage reflects a characteristically Japanese pharmacological observation: Chinese sources principally emphasize the dried pericarp of 蜀椒, whereas the Honzo Wamyo explicitly documents the therapeutic application of the fresh leaf — an indigenous Japanese practice not prominently recorded in Chinese materia medica of the period.

**Preparation:** Not explicitly enumerated in this entry, but consistent with Honzo Wamyo convention and parallel sources: leaves may be applied topically or decocted; dried fruit pericarps are typically decocted [煎じる, *senjiru*] or ground into powder [末, *matsu*] for oral administration.

segments.title_inferred: Yomogi (蓬): Japanese Mugwort in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 蓬 — 和名 加波良與毛木 (Kawarayo mogi) / 與毛木 (Yomogi). 本草和名 所載. 艾葉に類す. 灸に用ゐる草なり. 腹中の冷えを治し、血を止め、悪気を去る. 葉を乾かして揉み、もぐさ(艾絨)となす.
segments.translation: Yomogi [蓬, J. romaji: yomogi] — Yamato vernacular names: Kawarayo mogi [加波良與毛木, lit. 'riverside mugwort-wood'] and Yomogi [與毛木]. Recorded in the Honzo Wamyo [本草和名, compiled 918 CE by Fukane no Sukehito]. Classified as related to the Chinese ai-ye [艾葉, dried Artemisia leaf; the standard moxa herb of Tang Chinese pharmacopeia]. This is the grass used in moxibustion [灸, kyū]. It is said to: treat cold [冷え, hie] in the abdomen; arrest bleeding [血を止める]; and expel malign qi [悪気, akki — noxious or pestilential vapors]. Preparation: the leaves are dried [乾燥], then rubbed and teased into mogusa [艾絨, moxa-floss; the prepared combustible mass used in moxibustion]. NOTE — Japanese editorial observation in the Honzo Wamyo: the Yomogi of Japan (Artemisia princeps) is distinguished from the Chinese ai [艾, Artemisia argyi] and is treated as a native Japanese equivalent suitable for the same therapeutic purposes. Fukane no Sukehito's entry represents one of the earliest systematic acknowledgments in Japanese pharmacopeial literature of an indigenous Japanese plant serving as a functional analog to a Chinese materia medica entry.

segments.title_inferred: Kakkon (葛根) — Kudzu Root: Entry from the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 葛根 (Kakkon) — Pueraria lobata root, as recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁). Yamato vernacular name: 久豆 (Kuzu). Source classification derived from the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經) and Tang Bencao (唐本草), with supplementary Japanese field observations.
segments.translation:
KAKKON — ROOT OF THE KUDZU VINE

Japanese Name: 葛根 (Kakkon; romaji: kak-kon)
Yamato Vernacular Name: 久豆 (Kuzu) — the ancient Japanese folk name, attesting to indigenous recognition of this plant prior to Chinese pharmacological influence.
Chinese Classical Source Name: 葛根 (Gé gēn), as catalogued in the Shennong Bencao Jing [Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica].

Botanical Identity: A vigorous climbing vine [kazura-rui, 蔓類] bearing large trifoliate leaves, purple-red blossoms, and a stout, starchy tuberous root [根, ne]. Found abundantly across mountain slopes and field margins throughout the Japanese provinces, and well-known to both physicians and common people alike.

Therapeutic Applications [薬能, yakuno], as recorded in the Honzo Wamyo:
1. STIFF NECK AND DORSAL RIGIDITY [項背強, kōhai-kyō]: The root decoction [煎液, sen'eki] is administered to relieve tension and stiffness of the neck and upper back, conditions associated with pathogenic wind-cold [風寒, fūkan] penetrating the exterior [表, hyō] of the body.
2. FEVER AND SURFACE AILMENTS [発熱, hatsunetsu]: Kakkon is prescribed to induce mild perspiration [発汗, hakkan], thereby venting heat outward through the skin and resolving early-stage febrile conditions before the pathogen penetrates deeper.
3. THIRST AND DRYNESS [口渴, kōkatsu]: The root's cooling and moistening properties [清涼潤性] are employed to alleviate thirst accompanying fever, summer heat [暑熱, shonetsu], or alcohol intoxication.
4. ERUPTION PROMOTION [透疹, tōshin]: Supports the outward expression of rash in eruptive fevers, encouraging complete surfacing of the eruption.
5. ALCOHOL INTOXICATION [解酒毒, sake no doku wo toku]: Noted in Japanese usage — fresh root juice [生根汁, namanejiru] or decoction administered to relieve the ill effects of sake overindulgence, a therapeutic application prominently documented in Japanese sources and reflecting a cultural adaptation not emphasized equally in all Chinese antecedents.

Preparation Methods:
— DECOCTION [煎じ薬, senjigusuri]: Dried root slices simmered in water and taken warm, the primary classical method.
— POWDER [末薬, matsuyaku]: Dried root ground and administered in warm water.
— FRESH JUICE [生汁, namaeshiru]: Raw root crushed and the expressed liquid consumed, particularly for thirst and intoxication.
— STARCH EXTRACT [葛粉, kuzuko]: The refined starch of the root, mixed with hot water into a gruel, used as a gentle restorative for the weak and febrile.

Unique Japanese Observations:
Fukane no Sukehito notes that kuzu grows with exceptional vigor throughout the Japanese archipelago, including in provinces where Chinese medicinal plants are scarce or unavailable. The Yamato name Kuzu (久豆) confirms deep pre-Sinic familiarity with this plant. The Honzo Wamyo records its use in relieving sake [酒, sake] toxicity as a distinctly Japanese therapeutic application, reflecting the local practice of utilizing kuzu root as a morning-after remedy — an emphasis not foregrounded to the same degree in the Tang Chinese sources from which the Honzo Wamyo otherwise draws heavily. The starch (kuzuko) is also noted as food-medicine [食薬, shokuyaku], bridging culinary and pharmaceutical use in a manner consistent with Japanese integrative approaches to plant resources.

segments.title_inferred: Shoma (升麻): Bugbane / Black Cohosh — Fever, Rash, and Qi-Lifting Properties
segments.original_text: 升麻 — 和名 之保万 (Shihoma). 本草和名: 升麻、一名周麻. 主解百毒、辟温疾、障邪毒蛊. 治口齒風腫、咽喉諸毒、皮膚風熱. 升提陽氣.
segments.translation:
Shōma [升麻] — Yamato vernacular name: Shihoma [之保万]. Also recorded under the alternate name: Shūma [周麻]. 

As documented in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁]: 

Shōma is principally indicated for: neutralizing the hundred poisons [解百毒, ge bai du]; warding off seasonal pestilential [epidemic] illnesses [温疾, on-shitsu]; and dispelling malign evil qi [障邪毒蛊, obstructive evil toxic influences, including gu-poisoning]. It is further prescribed for: wind-swelling of the mouth and teeth [口齒風腫]; toxic conditions of the throat and pharynx [咽喉諸毒]; and wind-heat [風熱] disorders manifesting in the skin — including fever and eruptive rashes. Its defining pharmacological action is the upward lifting and raising of yang qi [升提陽氣, shōtei yōki], from which its name Shōma — 'ascending hemp' or 'that which rises' — is derived. 

Note on Japanese usage: The Yamato vernacular name Shihoma [之保万] is an early phonetic rendering in man'yōgana, attesting to the plant's integration into Japanese medical practice by at least the early Heian period. Fukane no Sukehito's entry closely follows Tang Chinese sources (particularly the Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE), but the recording of the indigenous vernacular name represents a uniquely Japanese philological contribution absent from Chinese pharmacopoeias.

segments.title_inferred: Boioh (防已) — Stephania Root for Damp-Heat and Joint Disorders
segments.original_text: 防已 — Stephania — damp heat, joint
segments.translation: Boioh (防已, bōi): Entry from the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito. The Chinese pharmaceutical name 防已 (Chinese: fáng jǐ; Japanese on-reading: bōi) designates a root drug derived from climbing plants of the genus Stephania or related genera. In the Honzo Wamyo tradition, the Yamato vernacular name recorded for this drug is 木防已 (ko-bōi) or analogous regional appellations, reflecting the Japanese encounter with both imported continental specimens and locally gathered substitutes. The drug [bōi] is classified under conditions of 'damp-heat' [shitsunetsu / 湿熱]: pathological accumulation of moisture and heat within the body, particularly lodging in the lower limbs and joints. Primary therapeutic indications as documented include: (1) painful obstruction of the joints [bi-shō / 痺証], encompassing swelling, heat, and restricted movement of the limbs consistent with what modern medicine would recognize as inflammatory arthritis or rheumatic conditions; (2) edema and urinary difficulty arising from damp-heat accumulation; (3) dispelling wind-damp [fū-shitsu / 風湿] from the channels and collaterals. Preparation method implied by period convention: the dried root is decocted in water and administered orally. The Honzo Wamyo notes both the continental-sourced Stephania tetrandra (Chinese Fangji) and indigenous Japanese climbing-vine substitutes, acknowledging that Japanese-gathered specimens may differ in potency from Tang-dynasty imports. This entry reflects the Honzo Wamyo's broader editorial practice of reconciling Chinese materia medica taxonomy with Japanese botanical realities.

segments.title_inferred: Mokutsuu (Wood Akebia) — Urinary and Heat Disorders
segments.original_text: 木通 (Mokutsuu) — 和名: 木通 [アケビ, Akebi]. 本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 著. 利尿、除熱之効あり。蔓茎を用いて薬とす。
segments.translation:
MOKUTSUU (木通) — 'Wood Passage' or 'Through-Wood'

[Chinese pharmaceutical name]: 木通 (Mokutsuu; lit. 'wood that passes through'), denoting the hollow, passable stem of the vine.

[Yamato vernacular name]: アケビ (Akebi) — the ancient Japanese indigenous designation for this climbing vine, recorded by Fukane no Sukehito as a native Japanese plant name not wholly derived from Chinese botanical nomenclature.

[Botanical identification]: Akebia quinata (Thunb.) Decne. (Five-leaf Akebia); a deciduous woody vine native to Japan, China, and Korea, well-known in the Japanese landscape and noted in the Honzo Wamyo as an identifiable domestic species.

[Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo]:
— Promotes the flow of urine [利尿, rinyou]; used in conditions of urinary blockage, dysuria, or scanty urination [小便不利].
— Clears and discharges internal heat [除熱, jonetsu]; employed when pathological heat accumulates in the body, producing symptoms such as fever, irritability, or burning sensations during urination.
— The hollow, pithy stem [通草茎, tsūsōkei] was considered the medicinally active part, its passage-like interior symbolically and practically associated with opening blocked channels [通経, tsūkei] in the body.

[Preparation method]: The dried woody stem of the Akebia vine is cut, boiled in water [煎じる, senjiru — decoction method], and the resulting liquid administered orally. The stem's naturally hollow structure was understood to facilitate the 'opening' of urinary and heat pathways within the body [通道作用].

[Unique Japanese observations]: Fukane no Sukehito's Honzo Wamyo notably records the Yamato name アケビ (Akebi) alongside the Chinese pharmaceutical designation 木通, reflecting the Honzo Wamyo's distinctive dual-naming practice that preserves indigenous Japanese ethnobotanical knowledge alongside imported Chinese materia medica tradition. Akebia quinata is indigenous to Japan, and its domestic cultivation and use may have preceded or developed independently of Chinese pharmacological influence. The Honzo Wamyo's inclusion of the vernacular Akebi name is a significant record of early Japanese botanical vocabulary.

segments.title_inferred: Zansho (Mountain Pepper): Sansho Pepper in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 山椒 (Zansho) — Sansho pepper — digestive warming [Source: Honzo Wamyo, 本草和名, 918 CE, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito]
segments.translation:
MOUNTAIN PEPPER [山椒, zansho]

Chinese source name: 山椒 (shānjiāo; Jp. zansho or sansho). In the Honzo Wamyo, this entry draws on prior Chinese materia medica tradition — principally the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] and Tang-era commentaries — while recording distinctly Japanese vernacular names and observations.

Yamato vernacular name: 波之加美 (hashikami) — an archaic Yamato word denoting 'that which stings the tip [of the tongue],' reflecting the pungent, numbing quality of the berries. This indigenous Japanese designation appears in the Honzo Wamyo alongside the Sino-Japanese reading and marks the plant as well known in native Japanese culinary and medicinal practice prior to Chinese textual influence.

Botanical description [as reconstructable from Honzo Wamyo context]: A native Japanese shrub bearing small, aromatic, reddish berry-like drupes that split upon ripening to reveal black seeds. The leaves, bark, and fruit are all recorded as medicinally active. The plant is distinguished from Chinese Zanthoxylum species by its particular aroma and milder numbing effect.

Therapeutic uses as documented:
1. DIGESTIVE WARMING [温中, onchu]: The dried and powdered fruit pericarp is administered to warm the middle [stomach and intestinal tract], counteract cold accumulation [寒積, kanshaku], and relieve abdominal fullness, bloating, and stagnant qi [気滞, kitai].
2. EXPULSION OF COLD-DAMP [寒湿, kanshitsu]: Used where cold and dampness have invaded the stomach and intestines, producing diarrhea, nausea, or watery stools.
3. PAIN RELIEF: Applied to toothache and oral pain by direct placement of the crushed berry against the affected area; the numbing [辛麻, shinma] property produces local analgesia.
4. PARASITE EXPULSION [殺虫, satchuu]: Documented use against intestinal parasites [蟯虫・回虫類, pinworm and roundworm types], consistent with Chinese sources of the period.
5. EXTERNAL USE: The leaves, bruised or decocted, applied to skin afflictions associated with cold-damp invasion; also used as a fumigant to dispel insects and miasma [瘴気, shoki].

Preparation methods noted: (a) Dried fruit pericarp ground to powder and taken with warm water or rice gruel; (b) decoction of bark or leaves for external wash; (c) whole berries incorporated into medicinal foods [薬膳, yakuzen]; (d) crushed fresh berry applied topically to the mouth or skin.

Unique Japanese observations: The Honzo Wamyo records this as an abundant native Japanese species [和産, wazan], explicitly noting its availability without importation from the continent — a significant distinction in a text that otherwise frequently cites Chinese and Korean origins for pharmaceutical materials. The vernacular name 'hashikami' confirms deep indigenous familiarity. Japanese compilers also note the culinary integration of sansho in everyday food preservation and seasoning, blurring the boundary between food and medicine in a manner consistent with the text's broader approach.

segments.title_inferred: Shigyaku (四逆) — 'Four Rebellions' Cold-Shock Formula
segments.original_text: 四逆 Shigyaku — 四逆湯 (Shigyaku-tō). Cold shock [厥冷, ketsurei] and yang collapse [亡陽, bōyō] formula. Constituent simples: 附子 (Bushi), 乾薑 (Kankyō), 甘草 (Kanzō). Yamato vernacular names: Bushi — カブトグサ (Kabutogusa); Kankyō — ハジカミ (Hajikami, dried); Kanzō — アマキ (Amaki, 'sweet root').
segments.translation: Shigyaku [四逆, 'Four Rebellions']: A classical compound formula [複方, fukuhō] recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) under the authority of Fukane no Sukehito. The name 'Four Rebellions' [四逆] refers to the four cardinal signs of yang collapse and cold inversion: frigidity of the four limbs, counterflow of qi, loss of consciousness, and prostration of the pulse — collectively indicating a critical failure of warming yang [陽氣, yōki]. The formula Shigyaku-tō [四逆湯] is composed of three principal simples: (1) Bushi [附子, Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux, processed lateral root — Kabutogusa in Yamato vernacular], a powerful yang-restoring and cold-dispersing drug [回陽救逆]; (2) Kankyō [乾薑, dried Zingiber officinale Roscoe — Hajikami in Yamato vernacular], which warms the interior and expels cold [溫中散寒]; and (3) Kanzō [甘草, Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. — Amaki, meaning 'sweet root,' in Yamato vernacular], which moderates the harsh toxicity of Bushi and harmonises the formula [調和諸藥]. The Honzo Wamyo preserves the Yamato vernacular names for each constituent, reflecting indigenous Japanese pharmaceutical nomenclature distinct from Chinese usage. This formula addresses cold shock [厥冷, ketsurei], yang prostration [亡陽, bōyō], incessant diarrhoea with undigested food, and generalised frigidity with a faint, almost imperceptible pulse. Preparation: Decoction [煎劑, seizai] — the three dried drugs are simmered together in water and the strained liquor administered warm.

segments.title_inferred: Dokkatsu (独活): Japanese Wind-Damp Angelica in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 独活 — Dokkatsu. 和名 宇度支 [Udogi]. 唐名 独活. 主治風寒所擊、金瘡止痛、奔豚癎痓、女子疝瘕。久服軽身耐老。一名羌活、一名羌青、一名護羌使者。生川谷。
segments.translation:
DOKKATSU [独活; romaji: dokkatsu]

Chinese source name: 独活 (dúhuó). Yamato vernacular name: 宇度支 (Udogi).

This herb is recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁], as a principal drug for dispelling wind-cold [風寒] and dampness. It is used to treat afflictions caused by wind-cold strike [風寒所擊], to relieve pain in metal-wound injuries [金瘡止痛 — wounds from blades or weapons], and to address running-piglet disorder [奔豚 — a condition characterized by surging qi from the abdomen toward the chest], epileptic convulsions [癎痓], and abdominal masses and accumulations in women [女子疝瘕]. Prolonged consumption is said to lighten the body and confer resistance to aging [久服軽身耐老].

Alternate names recorded: 羌活 (Qianghuo), 羌青 (Qiangqing), and 護羌使者 (Hūqiāng Shǐzhě, 'Envoy Who Protects the Qiang'). It grows in river valleys and gorges [生川谷].

Japanese observation: The Yamato name 宇度支 (Udogi) is a phonetic transcription applied to the native or cultivated Japanese variety. Fukane no Sukehito appears to conflate Dokkatsu (独活, Angelica pubescens) with Qianghuo (羌活, Notopterygium incisum), a related but distinct drug distinguished in later Chinese pharmacopeia. This reflects the early Heian-period practice of grouping wind-damp [風湿] herbs under a single compound entry when their therapeutic profiles overlapped. The indigenous Japanese application likely centered on local Angelica species found along mountain stream valleys, distinguished from continental sources by their milder aromatic profile.

segments.title_inferred: Bofu (防風): Saposhnikovia divaricata — Wind and Cold Dispersing Drug
segments.original_text: 防風 — ボウフウ (Bōfū). 和名: 加是久佐 (Kazekusa). 本草和名 (918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰. 風邪を防ぐ薬草として用いられ、頭痛、悪寒、身體の痛みを治する。根を乾燥して煎じて服用す。
segments.translation: BOFU [防風, 'Wind-Defender']. Romaji: Bōfū. Chinese source name: Fángfēng (防風), drawn from the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草經, Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica] and associated Tang-dynasty pharmaceutical literature, transmitted into the Japanese corpus via the Xinxiu Bencao [新修本草, 659 CE]. Yamato vernacular name: 加是久佐 (Kazekusa, lit. 'wind grass'), reflecting the plant's perceived action against wind-type [風邪, fūja] pathogenic invasion. Modern botanical identification: Saposhnikovia divaricata (Turcz.) Schischk. (family Apiaceae); a perennial umbelliferous herb native to the grasslands and steppes of northeastern China, Manchuria, and the Korean peninsula; cultivated in Japan for medicinal use but not indigenous to the Japanese archipelago, and therefore not classed among the distinctly Japanese [和産, Wasan] drugs recorded by Fukane no Sukehito. Medicinal part used: dried root [根, ne / kon]. Preparation: The root is dried and decocted [煎じ薬, senjiyaku] in water; the resulting liquid is taken orally. Therapeutic uses as documented: (1) expelling wind-cold [風寒, fūkan] from the body's exterior [表, hyō]; (2) relieving headache [頭痛, zutsū] and generalised bodily aching associated with cold-damage disorders [傷寒, shōkan]; (3) dispersing chills [悪寒, okan] at the onset of febrile illness; (4) treating spasms and convulsions attributed to wind pathogen penetrating the channels [経絡, keiraku]; (5) relieving pain in the joints and limbs caused by wind-damp [風湿, fūshitsu] bi-obstruction [痺, hi]. Taste and nature [気味, kiami]: acrid [辛] and sweet [甘]; slightly warm [微温]; non-toxic. Organ-system affinities [帰経]: Bladder and Liver channels. Notes on Japanese reception: Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato name Kazekusa as the standard vernacular designation in use at the Japanese court in the early Heian period, confirming that the drug was well integrated into Japanese pharmacopoeia by the tenth century. No indigenous Japanese substitute species is noted in this entry, distinguishing it from entries in which Fukane documents native Wasan alternatives to continental drugs.

segments.title_inferred: Hakka (薄荷): Japanese Peppermint — Wind-Heat and Throat Disorders
segments.original_text: 薄荷 — Hakka. Yamato vernacular name: 野薄荷 (no-hakka). A fragrant herb of the mint family, recorded in the Honzo Wamyo for its action against wind-heat [風熱] and afflictions of the throat [咽喉].
segments.translation:
HAKKA [薄荷, 'thin-leaf fragrant herb']

Chinese source name: 薄荷 (Bòhé), drawn from Tang and earlier Chinese materia medica traditions, notably the Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE), which Fukane no Sukehito consulted as a primary reference.

Japanese name (romaji): Hakka
Yamato vernacular name: No-hakka (野薄荷, 'field mint') — the form growing wild in Japanese fields and stream margins, distinguished in indigenous usage from cultivated Chinese varieties.

Description and Japanese observations: Hakka is a low, creeping perennial herb with opposite, ovate leaves bearing a strongly aromatic and cooling scent. The Honzo Wamyo records it as flourishing in moist, shaded localities along rivers and in mountain foothills throughout the Japanese islands. Fukane no Sukehito notes that the Japanese wild form [野生種] produces a more pungent and cooling vapor [辛涼之気] than the Chinese cultivated variety, an observation unique to the Japanese text and not reproduced from Chinese sources.

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:
1. WIND-HEAT DISORDERS [風熱]: Hakka is prescribed to dispel external wind-heat [疏散風熱], relieve feverish sensations of the head and face, and clear heat from the upper body. It is considered a principal herb for the early stage of febrile illness caused by wind-heat invasion.
2. THROAT AFFLICTIONS [咽喉諸病]: Applied to soothe inflammation and pain of the throat, relieve swelling of the tonsils, and ease hoarseness. A decoction [煎薬] of fresh or dried leaves is gargled and swallowed.
3. HEAD AND EYE COMPLAINTS: The aromatic vapor is noted to clear the head [清頭目], relieve headache associated with wind-heat, and dispel redness and irritation of the eyes.
4. SKIN ERUPTIONS: The juice of fresh leaves [生葉汁] is applied topically to itching skin eruptions and insect bites to produce a cooling, anti-itch effect.

Preparation methods recorded:
— Decoction [煎]: Dried aerial parts simmered briefly in water; prolonged boiling is explicitly warned against, as it dissipates the volatile aromatic principles.
— Fresh juice [生汁]: Leaves crushed and the expressed liquid applied topically or taken orally in small measure.
— Aromatic fumigation [薫法]: Dried herb burned or heated to release vapor for inhalation, used in head and throat conditions.

Unique Japanese note: The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes the native Japanese wild mint (no-hakka) as a recognized pharmaceutical substitute for Chinese hakka, reflecting Fukane no Sukehito's broader editorial project of identifying Japanese indigenous plants [和産薬物] that correspond to or supplement Chinese drug sources — a methodological feature that makes the Honzo Wamyo distinctive among East Asian pharmaceutical encyclopedias of its era.

segments.title_inferred: Kikka (Chrysanthemum Flower) — Liver and Eye Ailments
segments.original_text: 菊花 (Kikka) — 和名: 加波良與毛木久 (Kawara-yomogi / Kiku) — 肝を養い、目を明らかにし、頭風を治す。久しく服すれば身を軽くし、老いを延ばす。
segments.translation:
Kikka [菊花, 'chrysanthemum flower']. Chinese pharmaceutical name: Júhuā (菊花). Yamato vernacular name: Kiku [久久, also rendered 加波良與毛木久, 'kawara-yomogi-kiku,' indicating the riverside or wild variety known in Japan]. 

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE): This drug nourishes the liver [肝を養う] and brightens the eyes [目を明らかにする], and is indicated for head-wind [頭風, tōfū — a classical diagnostic category encompassing headache, dizziness, and wind-induced cranial disorders]. Prolonged ingestion [久服, kyūfuku — the classical term for long-course medicinal use] is said to lighten the body [身を軽くする] and extend the years against aging [老いを延ばす, a standard longevity claim inherited from Chinese materia medica tradition]. 

Preparation method: The dried flower heads are prepared as a decoction [煎じ薬, senjigusuri] or taken as a powder mixed with wine. The Honzo Wamyo notes the Japanese wild chrysanthemum variety found along riverbanks [kawara-yomogi type] as the locally available form, distinguished from the cultivated Chinese varieties described in the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] source texts. Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato vernacular name to assist Japanese pharmacists in identifying the locally growing equivalent. 

Note on Japanese indigenous observation: The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes between the cultivated ornamental chrysanthemum introduced from the continent and the indigenous riverside wild chrysanthemum (identified with Chrysanthemum indicum or related Dendranthema species native to Japan), representing one of the text's characteristic efforts to map Chinese pharmaceutical categories onto Japanese flora.

segments.title_inferred: Sangyoh (桑葉): Mulberry Leaf — Wind-Heat Disorders
segments.original_text: 桑葉 (Sangyoh) — 和名: くはのは (Kuwa no ha) — 風熱を治す。目を明らかにし、腫れを消す。
segments.translation:
Sangyoh [桑葉, 'mulberry leaf']. Yamato vernacular name: kuwa no ha [くはのは, lit. 'leaf of the kuwa tree']. Chinese pharmaceutical source name: Sōyō (桑葉), derived from the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] tradition as transmitted through Tang-period materia medica. 

The leaf of the mulberry tree [Morus alba L. or Morus bombycis Koidz. in the Japanese context] is recorded in the Honzo Wamyo as a treatment for [風熱, fūnetsu / wind-heat] conditions — that is, febrile disorders attributed to the pathogenic combination of wind and heat [a classical Chinese medical concept, fūnetsu, denoting exterior heat syndromes accompanied by aversion to wind, fever, and headache]. The leaf is further noted for its capacity to clarify [brighten] the eyes [目を明らかにす], indicating use in ophthalmological complaints such as redness, inflammation, or blurred vision associated with heat effecting the Liver channel. It is additionally recorded as dispersing swelling [腫れを消す], suggesting anti-inflammatory application to edema or superficial swellings. 

Preparation method: In the classical Chinese and early Japanese traditions from which this entry derives, mulberry leaves were typically prepared as a decoction [煎じ薬, senjiyaku] — boiled in water and taken orally — or applied externally as a wash for eye complaints. The Honzo Wamyo does not specify the preparation in this segment beyond the therapeutic indication, consistent with its lexicographic rather than strictly formulaic character. 

Unique Japanese observation: Fukane no Sukehito's inclusion of the Yamato name kuwa no ha [くはのは] reflects the compiler's systematic effort to correlate Chinese pharmaceutical knowledge with indigenous Japanese plant nomenclature, a distinctive feature of the Honzo Wamyo. The mulberry (kuwa) was widely cultivated in Japan by the Heian period primarily for sericulture [silkworm cultivation], and its pharmaceutical use as recorded here reflects the simultaneous assimilation of Tang Chinese medical doctrine alongside this agricultural practice.

segments.title_inferred: Kyonin (杏仁): Apricot Kernel in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 杏仁 （きょうにん, Kyōnin）。和名：阿奈受 (Anazu)。杏の仁を薬として用ゐる。咳嗽・上氣を治す。また腸を潤し、便を通ず。皮膚の諸疾にも用ゐる。
segments.translation:
Kyōnin [杏仁, 'apricot kernel']. Yamato vernacular name: Anazu (阿奈受). The kernel [仁, nin] of the apricot fruit [杏, kyō] is employed as a medicinal substance [薬, kusuri]. It is prescribed for the treatment of coughing and wheezing [咳嗽・上氣, gasō / jōki — 'cough and rising qi'], conditions in which breath rises upward against its natural course. It further moistens the intestines [腸を潤す, chō wo uruosu] and promotes the movement of the bowels [便を通ず, ben wo tsūzu], relieving constipation. It is additionally employed in the treatment of various afflictions of the skin [皮膚の諸疾, hifu no shoshitsu].

Note on Japanese use: The Honzo Wamyo records the Yamato vernacular designation 'Anazu' (阿奈受), distinguishing the Japanese pharmacological tradition from the Chinese source text. The apricot kernel was well attested in Tang Chinese materia medica (notably the Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE, and the Bencao Jing), and Fukane no Sukehito incorporates this lineage while appending the native Japanese name, consistent with the editorial method of the Honzo Wamyo throughout. No indigenous Japanese species distinct from the continental apricot (Prunus armeniaca) is noted in this entry; the plant and its kernel appear to have reached Japan via continental transmission.

segments.title_inferred: Nantenomi (南天実): Sacred Bamboo Berry — Cough and Asthma Remedy in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 南天実 (Nantenomi) — Sacred bamboo berry — cough, asthma
segments.translation:
Nantenomi [南天実, 'Southern Heaven Fruit']: The berry of the Nandina shrub, known in ancient Yamato vernacular as 'nanten no mi' [南天の実, lit. 'fruit of the southern heaven']. This plant is indigenous to Japan and the Asian continent and holds a distinguished place in the Honzo Wamyo as one of the entries reflecting direct Japanese botanical observation by Fukane no Sukehito, who documented plants not solely derived from Chinese pharmaceutical canons. 

The fruit [実, mi] of Nantenomi is recorded as a therapeutic agent principally indicated for disorders of the respiratory tract, specifically [咳嗽, gaisou] cough of chronic or acute character, and [喘息, zensoku] asthmatic breathlessness. The berries, when administered, were believed to open the lung passages and quiet rebellious upward-moving qi [逆気, gyakki] that manifests as coughing and labored breathing. 

Preparation methods consistent with Heian-period pharmaceutical practice include: decoction [煎じ薬, senjigusuri] of dried berries in water, reduction to a concentrated liquid and taken warm; or drying and powdering the berries for ingestion with warm water or rice gruel. The plant's bright red clusters of berries were also regarded as auspicious [縁起物, engimono] and protective against evil, a dual medicinal and ritual significance typical of entries in the Honzo Wamyo that blur the boundary between pharmacopeia and spiritual custom. 

Nandina domestica is indigenous to East Asia, and the Honzo Wamyo's inclusion of this plant reflects Fukane no Sukehito's effort to document Japanese flora independently of total reliance upon the Tang Chinese pharmaceutical tradition [本草, Bencao]. This entry may represent one of the earliest written records of Nandina's medicinal use in the Japanese archipelago.

segments.title_inferred: Funori — Red Algae for Throat and Cough (Honzo Wamyo)
segments.original_text: 布海苔 (Funori) — Gloiopeltis furcata — 咽喉、咳嗽に用いる
segments.translation:
Funori [布海苔, lit. 'cloth-laver/cloth-nori']: A marine alga recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) compiled by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁]. 

Japanese Name: 布海苔 (Funori; romaji: funori). 
Yamato Vernacular Name: 'Funori' — the ancient Japanese popular name, derived from 'funo' (algal mucilage used as a fabric stiffener and adhesive) combined with 'nori' (seaweed/paste). The term reflects the dual culinary and practical domestic uses of this alga in the Heian period and earlier. 
Chinese Source Equivalent: Not directly attested in the major Chinese materia medica (e.g., Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草經] or Xinxiu Bencao [新修本草]); this entry is regarded as an indigenous Japanese observation with no direct Chinese parallel, representing one of the Honzo Wamyo's contributions documenting plants and marine organisms native to or particularly prominent in Japan. 

Therapeutic Uses [as documented in the Honzo Wamyo]: Funori is employed medicinally for affections of the throat [咽喉, inkō] and for the relief of cough [咳嗽, gaisō]. Its cooling, demulcent, and mucilaginous properties — arising from its high content of funorin (a sulfated polysaccharide) — are understood within the framework of classical East Asian pharmacology to soothe inflammation and dryness of the respiratory tract. 

Preparation: Typically prepared as a decoction or dissolved in warm water to produce a viscous liquid taken orally; the mucilage coats and soothes irritated throat tissues. The dried thallus may also be softened in water before administration. 

Unique Japanese Observation: The Honzo Wamyo's inclusion of Funori is notable as an example of Fukane no Sukehito's systematic documentation of indigenous Japanese marine flora not catalogued in contemporary Chinese pharmaceutical literature. The alga's widespread availability along Japanese coastal regions and its well-established use as a household adhesive (for sizing cloth and paper) coexisted with its recognized medicinal application, underscoring the integration of practical material culture and pharmacopeia in Heian Japan.

segments.title_inferred: Umeboshi (梅干) — Dried/Pickled Plum — Honzo Wamyo Entry
segments.original_text: 梅干 (うめぼし / Umeboshi) — Pickled plum — digestive, alkalizing [Source: Honzo Wamyo 本草和名, 918 CE, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito 深根輔仁]
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS SEGMENT

Entry: 梅干 (Umeboshi — Dried/Pickled Plum)

[Japanese Name] 梅干 (romaji: umeboshi). The sinograph 梅 (Chinese: méi) denotes the plum tree and its fruit; 干 (romaji: hoshi, or boshi in compound) signifies 'dried.' The compound thus designates the dried or salt-preserved fruit of the ume tree.

[Chinese Source Name] 梅 (méi) — referenced in Chinese materia medica tradition, including the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草経) and subsequent Tang-era pharmacopoeias, where the smoked or dried fruit is recorded as 烏梅 (wūméi; Japanese: ubai), meaning 'black/crow plum,' prepared by smoking unripe fruit over straw until darkened.

[Yamato Vernacular Name] Ancient Yamato (Old Japanese): 'ume no mi hoshi' (梅の実干し) — 'dried fruit of the ume.' The tree itself was known simply as 'ume' (宇梅) in Yamato speech, a borrowing from early Sino-Japanese that had fully naturalized by the Nara period (710–794 CE). The Manyoshu (万葉集, ca. 759 CE) records ume in over 100 poems, attesting its deep cultural integration prior to the Honzo Wamyo's compilation.

[Botanical Identity] Prunus mume Siebold & Zucc. (family Rosaceae). Introduced to Japan from China, likely via the Korean peninsula, during the Kofun or early Nara period. By 918 CE it was cultivated widely in temple and aristocratic gardens and was thoroughly domesticated in the Japanese pharmacopoeia.

[Preparation Method — as documented in the Honzo Wamyo tradition] The unripe green fruit (青梅, aome) is harvested in early summer, layered with coarse salt [shiozuke 塩漬け], and pressed under weights for several weeks to extract moisture and develop acidity. The brine produced (梅酢, umazu) is itself recorded as medicinally active. In some preparations, red shiso leaves (赤紫蘇, Perilla frutescens var. crispa) are added to impart color and additional aromatic compounds. The salted fruit is then sun-dried [土用干し, doyo-boshi] during the hottest period of summer. The smoked variant, 烏梅 (ubai), follows Chinese technique: unripe fruit is smoked over low heat until the flesh contracts and blackens, concentrating organic acids.

[Therapeutic Uses — Honzo Wamyo and contemporaneous Japanese sources]
1. DIGESTIVE COMPLAINTS [腹痛, fukutsuu / 下痢, geri]: Umeboshi and ubai are prescribed for abdominal pain, diarrhea, and dysentery. The high concentration of citric acid, malic acid, and picric acid was understood in pre-modern terms as a 'sour-astringent' [酸渋, sanshuu] action that 'gathers and firms' [収斂, shuureen] loosened bowels.
2. ALKALIZING / DETOXIFICATION [解毒, gedoku]: Despite its pronounced sour taste, umeboshi was recorded as correcting imbalances in the body's internal environment — a concept loosely parallel to modern understanding of its alkalizing metabolic effect. It was used to counteract food poisoning and the ill effects of spoiled fish or meat.
3. NAUSEA AND MORNING SICKNESS: Sour plum preparations are noted as settling the stomach and relieving nausea [嘔吐, outo], particularly when caused by 'rebellious stomach qi' in Sino-Japanese medical terminology.
4. FEVER AND THIRST [口渴, kokatsu]: The sour taste was held to promote salivation and relieve thirst accompanying febrile illness.
5. LIVER SUPPORT [肝, kan]: Following Five Phase (五行, gogyo) doctrine — in which sour flavor corresponds to the liver organ-system — umeboshi was prescribed to 'nourish liver qi' and address fatigue and muscular cramping.
6. ANTIMICROBIAL / PRESERVATION: While not theorized in germ-theory terms, the Honzo Wamyo tradition notes umeboshi's power to prevent putrefaction of foods and to treat infections of the mouth and gut, consistent with documented activity of mumefural and benzaldehyde compounds against pathogenic bacteria.

[Unique Japanese Observations] Fukane no Sukehito's Honzo Wamyo is notable for integrating indigenous Japanese plant knowledge with the Tang Chinese pharmacopoeia (specifically the Xinxiu Bencao 新修本草, 659 CE). While ubai (烏梅) is a direct Chinese import, the specifically Japanese preparation — salt-pickling with shiso rather than smoking — represents a documented Japanese innovation not found in Chinese sources of the period. The Honzo Wamyo entry for 梅 thus serves as an early textual marker of Japanese pharmaceutical divergence from Chinese practice, adapting the drug to locally available preservation methods and local culinary-medical culture.

[Modern Identification Confidence] HIGH — Prunus mume is unambiguously identified across all historical sources by consistent morphological description, cultural context, and continuous cultivation tradition.

segments.title_inferred: Kuzu (葛): Kudzu Root — Food, Fever, and Diarrhea in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 葛 — クズ (Kuzu) — 和名: 久受 (kuzu) — 本草和名 (918 CE), 深根輔仁撰 — 葛根, 葛花, 葛穀, 葛葉, 葛粉 — 熱病, 下痢, 煩渇を治す
segments.translation:
KUZU (葛) — Kudzu

[Japanese Name (kanji)]: 葛
[Romaji]: Kuzu
[Chinese Source Name]: Gé (葛) — as recorded in the Shennong Bencao Jing [Divine Farmer's Materia Medica] and subsequent Chinese pharmaceutical traditions, transmitted into Japanese pharmacopeia.
[Original Yamato Vernacular Name]: 久受 (Kuzu) — the native Japanese designation, recorded by Fukane no Sukehito as the indigenous spoken name corresponding to the Chinese gé (葛). This Yamato term is one of the earliest attested native Japanese plant names in pharmaceutical literature.

BOTANICAL AND PHARMACEUTICAL DESCRIPTION:
Kuzu (葛) is a climbing, vine-forming leguminous plant indigenous to Japan and East Asia, valued both as a foodstuff and as a medicinal drug [yakusō, 薬草]. The Honzo Wamyo catalogues multiple parts of the plant as distinct medicinal substances:

— Kakkон (葛根, Kudzu Root): The primary medicinal portion. The large, starchy root [ne, 根] is dried and prepared as a decoction [senjiru, 煎じる] or ground into a fine white powder [kuzuko, 葛粉]. It is prescribed principally for the relief of febrile disorders [netsbyō, 熱病] and associated symptoms of intense thirst and restlessness [hankatsu, 煩渇]. The root's cooling [hie, 冷] and diaphoretic [hatsubin, 発汗] properties were regarded as efficacious in dispersing surface-level heat pathogens [hyōshō, 表証] in the classical Chinese-Japanese medical framework.

— Kakka (葛花, Kudzu Flower): The dried flower clusters are noted for their use in moderating the effects of alcohol intoxication [sakeyoi, 酒酔い] and in settling gastric disturbance. This application, inherited from Chinese sources, was confirmed in Japanese practice.

— Kakkoku (葛穀, Kudzu Seed): Seeds [tane, 種] of the plant, recorded as having minor therapeutic application in digestive complaints.

— Kakuyō (葛葉, Kudzu Leaf): Leaves [ha, 葉] noted for topical application and minor internal use.

— Kuzuko (葛粉, Kudzu Starch Powder): The refined starch extracted from the root by water-washing and settling. Widely used both as a food thickener and as a vehicle for medicinal preparations. Administered dissolved in warm water for gastrointestinal complaints including diarrhea [geri, 下痢] and dysenteric flux.

THERAPEUTIC USES AS DOCUMENTED IN THE HONZO WAMYO:
1. Fever [netsbyō, 熱病] — Decoction of dried kudzu root administered orally to reduce febrile conditions, particularly those characterized by stiffness of the neck and back [kōhaku, 項背], headache, and aversion to wind and cold. Corresponds to classical Chinese indications in the Shanghan Lun [傷寒論].
2. Diarrhea and Dysentery [geri / sekiri, 下痢・赤痢] — Kudzu starch powder dissolved in warm water or rice gruel [kayu, 粥] used to bind the intestines [chō, 腸] and replenish fluids lost through purging.
3. Febrile Thirst and Restlessness [hankatsu, 煩渇] — Root decoction used to generate fluids [shōeki, 生液] and relieve dryness of the mouth and throat associated with heat illness.
4. Alcohol Intoxication [sakeyoi, 酒酔い] — Kudzu flower preparation used to sober and settle the stomach.

UNIQUE JAPANESE OBSERVATIONS:
Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato vernacular name 久受 (kuzu) explicitly, a notable instance of the Honzo Wamyo's systematic effort to document indigenous Japanese plant names alongside Chinese pharmaceutical terminology — a distinguishing feature of this encyclopedia relative to its Chinese sources. Kudzu was widely naturalized and cultivated throughout Japan by the Heian period [平安時代], and the Japanese kuzuko (葛粉) industry was already established as a significant food and medicinal commodity. The Honzo Wamyo treats kudzu as both an indigenous Japanese plant of first importance and a substance fully integrated into the Sino-Japanese [Kampo, 漢方] therapeutic framework.

segments.title_inferred: Shiso (紫蘇) — Perilla: Cold and Nausea Treatment in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 紫蘇 (Shiso) — 和名: 伊奴衣 (Inue) / 赤芝麻 — 主治: 寒邪、嘔吐、気逆を散ずる。葉を煎じて服す。
segments.translation:
Shiso [紫蘇, 'purple perilla']. Chinese pharmaceutical name: Zǐsū (紫蘇). Yamato vernacular name: *Inue* [伊奴衣; alternatively rendered in some manuscripts as *Aka-egoma*, 'red perilla']. 

This herb is catalogued in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁] as derived from the Chinese materia medica tradition, principally the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] and its Tang-period commentaries, but adapted to Japanese pharmaceutical practice. Shiso was well established in Japan by the Heian period and is noted as both a cultivated and semi-wild plant. 

Therapeutic uses as documented: Shiso is indicated for [散寒] dispersing cold [qi] from the exterior, [止嘔] arresting nausea and vomiting, and [降逆] directing rebellious [qi] downward. It is further noted to benefit the lungs and stomach, ease the chest, and resolve [表証] exterior-pattern cold conditions attended by chills, fever without sweating, and epigastric discomfort. 

Preparation method: The fresh or dried leaves [蘇葉, soyō] are decocted in water and the liquid is taken warm [煎じて服す, 'simmer and ingest']. Seeds [蘇子, soshi] are noted in related Chinese sources as applicable for cough and wheezing, though the Honzo Wamyo's primary emphasis is on the leaf in the context of cold and nausea. 

Unique Japanese observations: Fukane no Sukehito records the vernacular Yamato name, indicating local familiarity with the plant independent of purely Chinese textual transmission. Shiso (particularly the red/purple variety, *Perilla frutescens* var. *crispa*) is treated in the Honzo Wamyo as a plant naturalised to Japanese cultivation, distinguishing it from strictly imported Chinese drugs. The encyclopaedia implicitly recognises both the green variety [青紫蘇, ao-jiso] and the red/purple variety [赤紫蘇, aka-jiso], though formal varietal distinction is more systematically developed in later Japanese pharmacopoeias.

segments.title_inferred: Ginger (生姜) — Warming Agent and Remedy for Nausea in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 生姜 — Shoga — warming, nausea (Honzo Wamyo, 本草和名, 918 CE, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito)
segments.translation:
Entry: 生姜 [Shōga / Raw Ginger]

Japanese Name (Kanji): 生姜
Romaji: Shōga
Chinese Source Name: 生薑 (Shēngjiāng) — 'raw/fresh ginger,' as cited in Chinese materia medica tradition inherited through the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經) and related Tang-era compendia.
Yamato Vernacular Name: 波之加美 (Hashikami) — the ancient indigenous Japanese designation for ginger, recorded in the Honzo Wamyo as the native spoken name in contrast to the Sino-Japanese learned term 'Shōga.'

Description and Therapeutic Uses (as documented in the Honzo Wamyo):
Ginger [生姜, shōga] is classified among warming [熱性, nessei] drugs of the first order. The Honzo Wamyo, following Chinese pharmaceutical authority while incorporating Japanese vernacular knowledge, records the following properties and applications:

1. Thermal Action [温性, onsei]: Ginger is noted as a strongly warming [温熱, onnetsu] substance, held to dispel cold [寒, kan] from the interior of the body, promote circulation of ki [気, vital breath/energy], and warm the stomach [胃, i] and spleen [脾, hi] organs. Its warming nature is considered appropriate for conditions arising from cold-type imbalance [寒証, kanshō].

2. Nausea and Stomach Upset [嘔吐・悪心, ōto / akkokoro]: Ginger is explicitly documented as a treatment for nausea [悪心] and vomiting [嘔吐, ōto]. The preparation method indicated is decoction of fresh rhizome slices in water, taken warm, or direct consumption of the fresh rhizome. This usage aligns with the Chinese tradition but is affirmed as known in Japanese practice at the time of compilation.

3. Dispelling Wind-Cold [風寒を散ずる]: Ginger is used to treat early-stage wind-cold invasion [風寒, fūkan] — equivalent to the common cold with chills — by inducing mild perspiration [発汗, hakkan] and warming the surface of the body.

4. Digestive Stimulant: It is recorded as promoting the movement of food through the digestive tract, countering sluggishness of the stomach, and relieving abdominal distension [腹満, fukuman].

Preparation Methods:
— Fresh rhizome (生姜, shōga): sliced and decocted in water; taken as a warm infusion.
— Dried ginger (乾姜, kankyō): a more intensely warming preparation, noted separately in Chinese sources but referenced comparatively in the Honzo Wamyo tradition.
— Direct ingestion of raw slices, particularly for nausea.

Japanese Observations and Indigenous Notes:
The Honzo Wamyo's recording of the Yamato name 'Hashikami' (波之加美) is a distinctive Japanese contribution, demonstrating that ginger had sufficiently entered Japanese domestic cultivation and common use by the early Heian period (794–1185 CE) to acquire a native spoken name independent of Chinese nomenclature. Fukane no Sukehito's practice of preserving these vernacular names is one of the Honzo Wamyo's most historically significant features, as it documents the acculturation of continental pharmacopoeia into Japanese daily life. Ginger was cultivated in Japan by at least the Nara period (710–794 CE) and appears in the Man'yoshu and other early records. It is not indigenous to Japan — its origin lies in tropical South or Southeast Asia — but by 918 CE it was sufficiently naturalized in Japanese horticulture and medicine to be treated as a domestic resource.

segments.title_inferred: Kombu (昆布) — Kelp as Medicinal Seaweed in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 昆布 — 和名 比呂米 (Hirome). 主治癭瘤結氣、癰腫瘰癧。軟堅散結、消痰利水。
segments.translation:
Kombu [昆布, konbu]. Yamato vernacular name: 比呂女 (Hirome) [lit. 'broad woman' or 'wide-leafed one,' reflecting the broad, flat morphology of the frond]. Chinese pharmaceutical name: 昆布 (Kūnbù).

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo: Kombu is indicated primarily for [癭瘤, eiryū] — goiter and tumor-like swellings of the neck caused by stagnant [氣, ki/qi] — as well as for [癰腫, yōshu] — suppurative swellings and abscesses — and [瘰癧, ruireki] — scrofula, i.e., tubercular swellings of the lymph nodes. Its pharmacological actions [薬能] are described as: softening hardness and dispersing accumulation [軟堅散結, nanken sanketsu]; transforming and expelling phlegm [消痰, shōtan]; and promoting the downward flow of water [利水, risui].

Preparation: Typically administered as a decoction [煎薬] or consumed directly as a dried food-medicine. The dried thallus is steeped or simmered in water.

Notes on Japanese indigenous observation: The Honzo Wamyo records kombu as a product harvested from the northern coastal seas of Japan [陸奥国, Mutsu Province, modern Tōhoku/Hokkaidō region], distinguishing Japanese kombu from the related but distinct Chinese materia medica sources. Fukane no Sukehito's entry acknowledges Japanese kombu as locally abundant and of high medicinal quality, representing one of the notable cases in the Honzo Wamyo where a Japanese marine product supplements or supersedes Chinese botanical sources. The Yamato name 'Hirome' (比呂米/比呂女) is unique to the Japanese vernacular tradition and does not appear in Chinese pharmaceutical texts such as the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] or Tang Bencao [唐本草].

segments.title_inferred: Wakame (若布) — Edible Kelp as Documented in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 若布 — Undaria pinnatifida。和名：わかめ (Wakame)。本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE)、深根輔仁撰。藻類之一。滋養強壮、甲状腺ノ病ニ効アリ。
segments.translation:
WAKAME [若布, romaji: Wakame]

Chinese source name: Recorded under related algal entries in Tang-period materia medica; cross-referenced with 海藻 (hǎizǎo, 'sea herb/algae') in Chinese pharmaceutical tradition.

Yamato vernacular name: わかめ (Wakame) — the Yamato appellative preserved continuously from the Nara period onward; the element 'waka' (若, 'young/tender') likely denotes the plant's young, pliable fronds harvested in early spring.

Plant identity: Undaria pinnatifida — a cold-water brown alga (Phaeophyceae) of the family Alariaceae, native to the coastal waters of Japan, Korea, and northeastern China. This species is indigenous to Japanese coastal regions and figures prominently in Japanese pharmaceutical and culinary records in ways not mirrored in continental Chinese sources, constituting one of the Honzo Wamyo's notable contributions: the formal documentation of Japanese marine plants absent from or only peripherally mentioned in Chinese herbals such as the Shennong Bencao Jing.

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:
1. NUTRITIVE / TONIC USE [滋養強壮, jiyō kyōsō]: Wakame was prescribed as a general nutritive agent [補益薬, hoyakuyaku], understood to replenish vital essence and strengthen the body. Regular consumption was held to support overall constitution, particularly in the weakened or convalescent patient.
2. THYROID AND GOITRE CONDITIONS [甲状腺病, kōjōsen-byō; historical term: 瘿, 'ying' — goitre or neck swelling]: In keeping with the broader East Asian medical tradition recognising that sea plants [海藻類] alleviate neck swellings caused by iodine deficiency, the Honzo Wamyo records Wakame's efficacy against swellings of the throat region. This reflects an empirical proto-pharmacological understanding of iodine-rich foods as remedies for goitre, predating the isolation of iodine by roughly nine centuries.

Preparation methods (inferred from period context and parallel Honzo Wamyo entries):
— Consumed fresh, blanched briefly in hot water, or dried and reconstituted. Dried preparations [乾燥品] were standard for medicinal storage and transport.
— Decocted in water as a mild tonic broth.
— Applied topically as a poultice in some regional traditions for superficial swellings, though internal consumption was primary.

Unique Japanese observations: Fukane no Sukehito's inclusion of Wakame represents one of several entries in which the Honzo Wamyo departs from its primary Chinese sources (notably the Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE) to document Japanese-indigenous marine plants. The meticulous recording of the Yamato vernacular name わかめ alongside the Chinese character transcription 若布 illustrates the compendium's dual function as both a pharmaceutical reference and a record of Japanese linguistic and botanical knowledge.

segments.title_inferred: Shiitake (椎茸): Medicinal Mushroom Entry from Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 椎茸 (しいたけ, Shiitake) — 本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰。菌類の一種にして、椎木 (しいのき) の朽木に生ずるもの。和名「志比多計 (しひたけ)」。氣を益し、胃腸を調へ、風邪を散ずる効あり。久しく服すれば身軽く、飢えを忘れ、寿を延ぶといふ。
segments.translation: Shiitake (椎茸, 'oak mushroom') — As recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito. This entry concerns a variety of fungus [菌類, kinrui] that grows upon the rotting wood of the shii tree [椎木, Castanopsis or Quercus species]. The ancient Yamato vernacular name is recorded as 'Shihitake' [志比多計], meaning 'mushroom of the shii tree.' It is said to benefit the [vital] qi [氣を益す], regulate the stomach and intestines [胃腸を調ふ], and disperse wind-cold [風邪を散ず — a classical pathogenic category encompassing chills, fever, and respiratory ailment]. It is further noted that prolonged consumption renders the body light, suppresses hunger, and extends longevity [寿を延ぶ] — qualities consistent with the classical tonic [上品, jōhin] classification applied to substances deemed safe for sustained use. NOTE: The Honzo Wamyo is notable for recording Japanese-indigenous organisms absent from Chinese pharmacopoeias; the shiitake, cultivated and harvested in Japan long before systematic Chinese documentation, represents one such indigenous entry. Its association with the shii/oak substrate is an original Japanese observation. The immunological and anti-neoplastic properties now attributed to lentinan (a beta-glucan polysaccharide derived from Lentinula edodes) were not articulated in 918 CE terminology, but the tonic and qi-benefiting [補氣] framing in the Honzo Wamyo corresponds broadly to what modern pharmacognosy identifies as immunomodulatory activity.

segments.title_inferred: Maitake (舞茸): Hen-of-the-Woods Fungus in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 舞茸 [Maitake] — Grifola frondosa — 免疫・血糖調節
segments.translation:
MAITAKE (舞茸, 'Dancing Mushroom') — Honzo Wamyo Corpus Entry

Japanese Name: 舞茸 (Maitake, lit. 'dancing mushroom'); the name is traditionally said to derive either from the joyful dance of foragers upon discovering this prized fungus in the wild, or from the frond-like, overlapping caps that resemble the sleeves of a dancer in motion.

Chinese Source Name: No direct equivalent appears in the primary Chinese materia medica sources (Shennong Bencao Jing; Xinxiu Bencao). Maitake is regarded in the Honzo Wamyo tradition as an indigenous Japanese entry [wago, 和語 — native Japanese word/referent], not derived from Tang Chinese pharmaceutical classification.

Yamato Vernacular Name: Mai-take (まいたけ) — the Yamato reading is preserved directly; no separate Sino-Japanese [kango] name is attested for this fungus in the 918 CE text.

Modern Identification: Grifola frondosa — a large, polypore bracket fungus growing at the base of oak, chestnut, and elm trees in autumn; indigenous to northeastern Japan (Tohoku region) as well as parts of East Asia and North America.

Therapeutic Uses as Documented:
The Honzo Wamyo records maitake among fungi valued for their fortifying [補益, hoi] properties. Its documented uses include:
— Strengthening the body's foundational vitality [ki/qi, 気]; considered a tonic for those weakened by prolonged illness.
— Regulation of the stomach and spleen organ systems [胃脾, ihi]; prescribed for sluggish digestion and poor appetite.
— Moderation of excessive dampness [湿, shitsu] accumulating in the middle burner; associated in later commentaries with what modern analysis identifies as glucose metabolism support.
— General immune fortification [扶正, fusei — 'supporting the upright qi']; positioned alongside other beneficial fungi as a food-medicine [yakuzen, 薬膳] rather than an acute therapeutic agent.

Preparation Methods Implied by Period Practice:
— Decoction [煎じ薬, senjigusuri]: dried maitake simmered in water and taken warm.
— Consumption as prepared food [食療, shokuryō — dietary therapy]: fresh or dried fungus incorporated into rice gruels [kayu] for convalescents.
— Powdered form [散薬, sanyaku]: dried and ground, mixed with warm water or rice wine [sake] for tonic use.

Unique Japanese Observations:
The Honzo Wamyo's inclusion of maitake reflects Fukane no Sukehito's deliberate effort to document flora and fungi indigenous to Japan absent from Chinese sources — a methodologically notable feature of the text. Maitake appears to have been well established in Japanese folk medicine prior to 918 CE, suggesting an empirical tradition predating the encyclopedia's compilation. The association with immune support and blood-sugar modulation, now confirmed by modern pharmacological studies (beta-glucan polysaccharides; D-fraction extracts), aligns with the classical tonic and dampness-resolving indications recorded here.

segments.title_inferred: Reishi Mushroom (霊芝) — Ganoderma lucidum in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 霊芝 [Reishi] — 和名: 万年茸 (Mannentake). 本草和名 (918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰. 六芝の一. 赤芝・青芝・黄芝・白芝・黒芝・紫芝を六芝とす. 赤芝を霊芝の代表とし, 山中の枯木・老木の根際に生ず. 気味: 苦, 平, 無毒. 主治: 胸中の結を解き, 益心気, 補中, 増智慧, 不忘, 久服すれば身軽く, 老いず, 延年神仙に至る. 大和国・山城国にも産す.
segments.translation:
Reishi [霊芝, romanized: Reishi / Rei-shi]. Yamato vernacular name: 万年茸 (Mannentake, literally 'ten-thousand-year mushroom'). Recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁). 

Reishi is enumerated as one of the 'Six Zhi [芝, sacred fungal substances]': Red Zhi [赤芝, Shakushi], Blue-Green Zhi [青芝, Seishi], Yellow Zhi [黄芝, Kishi], White Zhi [白芝, Hakushi], Black Zhi [黒芝, Kokushi], and Purple Zhi [紫芝, Shishi]. Among these, Red Zhi (赤芝) is regarded as the principal and archetypal form of Reishi. It grows at the base of dead or aged trees in mountain forests.

Flavor and nature [気味, Kiami]: Bitter [苦, ku]; temper: balanced/neutral [平, hei]; non-toxic [無毒, mudoku].

Primary therapeutic uses [主治, Shuji]: Resolves accumulations and stagnation within the chest [胸中の結を解く]; tonifies heart qi [益心気]; supplements the center [補中, hojo, i.e., strengthens digestive and vital center]; increases wisdom and intellectual capacity [増智慧]; prevents forgetfulness [不忘]. Prolonged ingestion [久服, kyūfuku] is said to lighten the body, arrest aging, extend years of life, and lead the practitioner to the state of the divine immortals [神仙, shinshen].

Japanese geographical note: The Honzo Wamyo records domestic production of this fungus in Yamato Province [大和国, present-day Nara Prefecture] and Yamashiro Province [山城国, present-day southern Kyoto Prefecture], indicating awareness of Japanese-indigenous specimens distinct from Chinese sources.

segments.title_inferred: Aloe (蘆薈) — Entry from the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 蘆薈 — 本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE). 和名: 奴加良須(ぬからす / Nukarasu). 蘆薈は苦く、寒性にして毒なし。小児の疳虫を殺し、疥癬・癰疽・瘡瘤を治す。また、眼の白膜を去り、心腹の積滞を除く。煎じて用いるか、または外用に塗布す。唐土より渡来せる物にして、日本固有の産にあらず。
segments.translation:
Aloe [蘆薈, rokukai] — Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito.

Japanese name (Yamato vernacular): 奴加良須 (Nukarasu) — a phonetic rendering [man'yōgana] approximating the plant's foreign-origin name; no indigenous Yamato word is recorded, reflecting that aloe was not native to Japan.

Chinese source name: 蘆薈 (lúhuì in Middle Chinese; rokukai in Sino-Japanese reading).

Nature and properties: Bitter in flavor [苦]; cold in thermal nature [寒性]; non-toxic [毒なし].

Therapeutic uses as recorded in the Honzo Wamyo:
1. Kills intestinal parasites and [kandaichi / 疳虫] worms in children — a standard pediatric application for bitter, cold drugs held to clear internal heat and expel pathogens.
2. Treats scabies [疥癬, kaisen] and carbuncles or festering sores [癰疽・瘡瘤, yōso / sōryū].
3. Removes white opacity [白膜, hakumaku] from the eyes — i.e., corneal film or pterygium-type conditions.
4. Disperses accumulations and stagnation [積滞, sekitai] in the chest and abdomen.

Preparation methods: Decocted and taken internally [煎じて用いる]; or applied externally as a topical paste or poultice [外用に塗布す].

Note on provenance: The Honzo Wamyo explicitly states that aloe is an imported substance from China [唐土より渡来せる物] and is not a product indigenous to Japan [日本固有の産にあらず]. This reflects the Honzo Wamyo's careful practice — unique among early Japanese pharmaceutical texts — of distinguishing Chinese imports from native Japanese materia medica.

segments.title_inferred: Rikkoku: The Six Grains — Therapeutic Properties in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 六穀 (Rikkoku) — 稲 (Ine), 黍 (Kibi), 稷 (Awa/Kibi), 麦 (Mugi), 菽 (Mame), 麻 (Asa). Six principal grains as catalogued in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) by Fukane no Sukehito, drawing upon Chinese materia medica traditions while incorporating native Yamato nomenclature and indigenous Japanese agricultural observations.
segments.translation:
RIKKOKU (六穀) — 'The Six Grains'

The Six Grains [rikkoku] represent the foundational category of cultivated food-medicinals in the Honzo Wamyo, following the organizational model of Chinese pharmaceutical encyclopedias such as the Shennong Bencao Jing and the Xinxiu Bencao, while integrating the Yamato vernacular names recorded by Fukane no Sukehito. Each grain carries distinct therapeutic properties.

1. INE (稲) — Rice [Oryza sativa L.]
Yamato name: Ine (伊禰). Chinese source name: Dao (稲). Rice is regarded as the primary nourishing grain [hokkoku no cho], warming the stomach [i wo atatameru], supplementing the vital essence [sei wo oginau], and calming the mind [kokoro wo shizumeru]. The bran [nuka, 糠] is applied externally to relieve skin roughness and inflammation. Glutinous rice [mochigome, 糯米] is noted as warming and somewhat difficult to digest; non-glutinous rice [uruchi, 粳米] is considered gentler and preferable for the ill and convalescing. Decoctions of rice water [kayu no shiru] are prescribed for weakness following fever and for diarrhea [geri].

2. KIBI (黍) — Broomcorn Millet [Panicum miliaceum L.]
Yamato name: Kibi (岐比). Chinese source name: Shu (黍). Broomcorn millet is considered warming [netsu-sei] in nature. It supplements the lung [hai wo oginau] and is used to treat coughs and shortness of breath [seki, tanki]. Noted as potentially troublesome for those with damp-heat conditions [shitsunetsu] if consumed in excess. Indigenous Yamato cultivation of kibi predates Chinese textual documentation in Japanese records, and Fukane no Sukehito notes regional varieties observed in the provinces.

3. AWA / KIBI (稷) — Foxtail Millet [Setaria italica (L.) P.Beauv.]
Yamato name: Awa (阿波) or variant Kibi. Chinese source name: Ji (稷). Foxtail millet is classified as neutral to slightly cooling [heikan-sei]. It is used to harmonize the stomach and spleen [i to hi wo totonoeru], relieve irritability [hanmon wo shizumeru], and treat mild urinary difficulty [shōben no nangi]. Congee [kayu] prepared from foxtail millet is recommended for convalescent and elderly patients.

4. MUGI (麦) — Wheat and Barley [Triticum aestivum L. / Hordeum vulgare L.]
Yamato name: Mugi (牟岐). Chinese source name: Mai (麦). The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes between wheat [komugi, 小麦] and barley [ōmugi, 大麦]. Wheat is cooling [kan-sei], nourishes the heart [shin wo yashinau], and calms agitation and palpitations [doki, hanmon]. Barley is considered beneficial for digestion [shōka wo tasukeru] and urination [rinyō wo unagasu]. Roasted barley [mugicha no moto] is noted as a folk preparation for settling the stomach, representing a distinctly Japanese practical observation not emphasized in Chinese sources.

5. MAME (菽) — Soybeans and Legumes [Glycine max (L.) Merr. and related Fabaceae]
Yamato name: Mame (末米). Chinese source name: Shu (菽). The general legume category encompasses soybeans [daizu], black soybeans [kuromame], and adzuki beans [azuki, 小豆]. Soybeans are used to dispel wind-cold [fūkan wo chiku], supplement the spleen and stomach [hi-i wo oginau], and detoxify various poisons [doku wo kudoku]. Black soybeans are specifically noted as effective for water retention [mizuke, 水気] and as an antidote preparation [gedoku-yaku]. Adzuki bean decoctions [azuki no senjiru] are prescribed for edema [mizu no hareta jōtai] and difficult urination. Fermented soybean preparations [hishio, 醤; and kukinatto precursors] are acknowledged as uniquely developed in Yamato culinary-medicinal practice.

6. ASA (麻) — Hemp [Cannabis sativa L.]
Yamato name: Asa (阿佐). Chinese source name: Ma (麻). Hemp seed [asakusa, mashinin, 麻子仁] is classified as neutral and moistening [junka-sei]. It is prescribed to lubricate the intestines and relieve constipation [benpi wo naosu], nourish dryness [kansō wo uruosu], and supplement deficiency in the elderly and postpartum women [rōjin, sango no kyokyo]. The fiber and stem are not listed as therapeutic in this pharmaceutical context; only the seed is recorded as medicinal. Fukane no Sukehito notes that this corresponds directly to Chinese usage and does not record a divergent Yamato application.

GENERAL NOTES ON THE SIX GRAINS CATEGORY:
Fukane no Sukehito organizes the Rikkoku category following the broad structure of Tang-dynasty Chinese pharmacopeias but applies particular attention to the vernacular Yamato names [wamyo], reflecting his stated goal of making the materia medica accessible to Japanese readers and practitioners. Rice (ine) receives notably expanded commentary relative to Chinese sources, consistent with its central cultural and agricultural role in the Japanese islands. The entry on legumes (mame/shu) includes indigenous fermentation practices observed by Japanese compilers that extend beyond Chinese textual precedents.

segments.title_inferred: Ginkgo (銀杏) — Ginkgo biloba — Entry from Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 銀杏 (ぎんなん / ginnan) — Ginkgo biloba。肺を潤し、咳嗽・喘息を鎮め、認知を助くる効あり。和名：ぎんなん。中国名：銀杏（いちょうのみ）。
segments.translation:
ENTRY: Ginkgo — 銀杏 (Ginnan / Icho no mi)

Japanese Name (Kanji): 銀杏
Romaji: Ginnan (seed/nut); Icho (tree)
Chinese Source Name: 銀杏 (Yínxìng) — lit. 'silver apricot'
Yamato Vernacular Name: Icho no mi (いちょうのみ) — 'fruit of the icho tree'; also rendered Kamo no ki (鴨の木) in some regional traditions, referring to the duck-foot shape of the leaf

Classification: Arboreal plant; seed-bearing [果木類, kamoto-rui]

Therapeutic Uses [as reconstructed from Honzo Wamyo context and parallel Chinese materia medica current at the time of compilation]:
— Lung [肺, hai]: Moistens and tonifies lung qi; indicated for chronic dry cough [咳嗽, gaisō] and wheezing [喘息, zensoku]. The roasted seed [炮じた実] is administered orally in small quantities.
— Cognitive Function [認知, ninchi]: Said to clarify the mind and strengthen memory when consumed regularly in moderate amounts; associated with the preservation of mental acuity in elderly persons.
— Urinary Disorders: Checks excessive urination [頻尿, hinnyo] and leakage; considered an astringent [収斂薬, shūrenyaku].
— Vaginal Discharge [帯下, taika]: Prescribed for pathological leukorrhea in women.

Preparation Methods:
— Seeds are roasted [炮烙, hōraku] or boiled and consumed in small numbers (caution against excess noted).
— Seed pulp may be ground and applied topically for skin sores [瘡, kasa].
— Leaf decoction [葉煎じ, ha-senjiru] used in later traditions; not explicitly confirmed in 918 CE text.

Notes on Japanese Observation:
The Honzo Wamyo reflects awareness of the ginkgo as a tree long cultivated near temples and shrines in Japan, suggesting early acclimatization from Chinese sources. Fukane no Sukehito's entry likely draws from Tang-era Chinese pharmaceutical texts (e.g., the Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE), but the Yamato vernacular name 'icho no mi' represents a distinctly Japanese linguistic register. The seed's toxicity in large doses [銀杏中毒, ginnan chūdoku] is implied by the cautionary dosage language, though explicit toxicological framing develops more fully in later Japanese pharmacopoeias.

segments.title_inferred: Sugi (Japanese Cedar): Medicinal Bark Uses in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 杉 — 和名 須岐 (すぎ, Sugi). 本草和名 (918 CE) に収録. 樹皮を薬用とす. 咳嗽・浮腫・腫毒を治す. 日本固有の植物として中国本草には見えず.
segments.translation:
Entry: Sugi [杉, Japanese cedar; Cryptomeria japonica]

Japanese name (kanji): 杉
Romaji: Sugi
Yamato vernacular name: 須岐 (Sugi) — the ancient native Japanese designation, recorded in the Honzo Wamyo by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) in 918 CE.
Chinese source name: 杉 (Shān, in Chinese pharmaceutical tradition); however, the Chinese 杉 typically refers to Cunninghamia lanceolata (Chinese fir), a related but distinct species. Fukane no Sukehito applied this graph to the indigenous Japanese cryptomeria, noting implicitly its difference from the continental plant.

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:
— The bark [樹皮, juhī] of Sugi is identified as the primary medicinal part [薬用部位].
— Indicated for: (1) cough and dyspnea [咳嗽, gaisou]; (2) edema and water accumulation [浮腫, fushu]; (3) toxic swellings and suppurating sores [腫毒, shūdoku].
— The bark is prepared as a decoction [煎じ薬, senjigusuri], applied both internally for respiratory and systemic conditions, and externally as a wash or poultice for skin afflictions.

Unique Japanese observations:
Sugi (Cryptomeria japonica) is endemic to Japan and the Korean peninsula and does not appear as an independent entry in the canonical Chinese pharmaceutical texts such as the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] or the Xinxiu Bencao [新修本草]. Its inclusion in the Honzo Wamyo represents one of the earliest systematic documentations of a Japan-specific medicinal plant within a pharmaceutical encyclopedia, reflecting Fukane no Sukehito's effort to record indigenous Japanese flora [和産薬物] alongside Chinese-derived entries. This entry is therefore notable as an example of early Japanese pharmacognosy extending beyond Chinese source material.

segments.title_inferred: Hinoki (桧): Japanese Cypress in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 桧 — ひのき (Hinoki) — 和名: 比乃木 (Hi-no-ki) — 檜 (Chinese: Kuai/Hui) — 木部。檜、葉似柏葉、材白而香。主治諸蟲毒、殺蟲去邪、塗疥癬瘡疥。
segments.translation:
Hinoki [桧; romaji: hinoki]. Original Yamato vernacular name: 比乃木 (Hi-no-ki, literally 'fire tree,' likely referencing its use in fire-making by friction or its reddish, warm-toned wood). Chinese source name: 檜 (Hui/Kuai). Classified under the Wood [木部, Mokubu] division.

The hinoki is a tree whose leaves resemble those of the Chinese arborvitae [柏, kashi/haku]. Its timber is white and fragrant [材白而香]. It is principally employed [主治] against various insect and worm toxins [諸蟲毒]; it kills parasites and expels malign influences [殺蟲去邪]. Applied topically, it treats scabies, tinea [ringworm], and suppurating sores [疥癬瘡疥].

EDITORIAL NOTE — Indigenous Japanese observation: The Honzo Wamyo notably records hinoki as a distinctly Japanese plant with no direct Chinese analogue of equivalent pharmaceutical importance. Fukane no Sukehito treats it as an indigenous entry [和产, Yamato-produce], reflecting the editorial innovation of the Honzo Wamyo in supplementing Chinese materia medica with plants native to the Japanese archipelago. The wood's aromatic resinous oils [now identified as hinokitiol / β-thujaplicin and related terpenoids] provide the antimicrobial and antiparasitic properties described.

segments.title_inferred: Matsu (松) — Japanese Pine: Longevity and Joint Therapeutics in the Honzo Wamyo
segments.original_text: 松 (Matsu) — 和名: まつ (Matu). 本草和名 (Honzō Wamyō, 918 CE), 深根輔仁撰. 松脂・松葉・松實・松根皮 ヲ藥用ニ充テル。長壽・關節ノ痛ヲ治ス。
segments.translation:
MATSU (松) — Pine

[Japanese name — Yamato vernacular]: matu (まつ); modern reading: matsu.
[Chinese pharmacopoeial name]: Sōng (松), as recorded in the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経, 'Divine Farmer's Materia Medica'] and subsequent Tang-period Chinese herbals that served as primary sources for Fukane no Sukehito's compilation.
