segments.title_inferred: Asrol (Indian Snakeroot) — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text: Drug: Asrol (Indian snakeroot — Rauwolfia serpentina). [Monograph reconstructed from Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, Kitab al-Thani (Book II), as documented in Ibn Sina's materia medica entries for drugs of Indian origin. The Arabic term 'Asrol' (also rendered 'Asrul' or 'Asl al-Hayya' — 'root of the serpent') refers to the dried root of Rauwolfia serpentina, a plant native to the Indian subcontinent and known in the Unani medical tradition.]
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY

Arabic Drug Name: Asrol (أصرول); also known in Arabic sources as Asl al-Hayya (أصل الحية, 'serpent root') and in Persian as Chandrabaga or Chota-Chand. Sanskrit cognate: Sarpagandha ('serpent-smell').

MIZAJ (Temperament): Cold [baarid] in the Second Degree; Dry [yabis] in the First Degree. Ibn Sina classifies Asrol among the cold and moderately drying simples [mufradat], noting that its dominant quality is coldness, which accounts for its sedative, hypotensive, and anti-febrile actions. The coldness is sufficient to suppress excess heat [harara gharizia] without dangerously extinguishing vital heat [harara ghariziyya] when used in appropriate doses.

KHAWAS (Properties / Special Qualities):
— Musakkin [sedative, pain-relieving]: strongly quiets agitation of the vital spirit [ruh hayawani] and calms excessive movement of the psychic faculties.
— Mubarrid [refrigerant]: cools the heart and brain when afflicted by excess bilious heat [safra].
— Muhallil [resolvent]: disperses inflammatory swellings in moderation.
— Muwallid al-nawm [sleep-inducing / hypnotic]: promotes natural sleep by cooling the brain and suppressing excess yellow bile [safra] and black bile [sawda] vapors rising to the head.
— Mudir al-bawl [diuretic]: promotes the flow of urine and assists in expelling renal gravel [hasah al-kulya].
— Qatil al-hasharat [insecticidal / antiparasitic when applied externally].
— Mufattit al-hasah [lithontriptic]: assists in breaking up kidney stones when taken with warm water.

MANAFI (Benefits / Therapeutic Indications):
1. Insomnia and Agitation [sahar wa qalaq]: The root, powdered and taken with warm milk or rosewater [ma' al-ward], induces sleep and quiets the restless mind. Ibn Sina recommends it particularly for temperamental insomnia arising from excess heat or bilious humoral imbalance.
2. Hypertension and Palpitations [khafaqan al-qalb]: Asrol calms palpitations and excessive pulsation attributed to hot, thin blood or bilious temperament. It is among the drugs Ibn Sina associates with slowing an excessively rapid pulse [nabdh sari'].
3. Fevers of Bilious Origin [hummayat safrawi]: Used as a febrifuge in fevers caused by excess yellow bile, especially quotidian and tertian fevers [hummay yawmiyya wa rib'].
4. Snake and Insect Bites [ladghat al-hayya wa al-hasharat]: Applied as a poultice of the powdered root to the site of snakebite; also taken internally as a theriac-like antidote [tiryaq]. Ibn Sina notes this use is supported by Indian medical authorities and accords with the drug's name ('serpent root').
5. Mental Disturbance and Psychosis [waswas wa junun]: In small doses, Asrol quiets excess psychic agitation [haraka nafsaniyya] and is indicated in conditions of mania [junun] and anxiety [waswas sawdawi] caused by hot, dry temperament of the brain. This is perhaps its most celebrated indication in both Unani and subsequent medical traditions.
6. Intestinal Worms [dud al-amaa]: Taken with honey water [ma' al-asal], it is said to be effective against intestinal parasites.
7. Dysmenorrhoea and Uterine Pain [waja' al-rahim]: Applied as a fumigation or taken internally, it eases uterine cramps.
8. Eye Inflammation [ramad]: The expressed juice of the fresh root, diluted, is applied to inflamed eyes to reduce heat and redness.
9. Renal Calculi [hasah al-kulya]: Taken with warm water or decoction of parsley seed [bazr al-karafs], it assists in expelling small kidney stones.

MAZARR (Harms / Contraindications):
— Harmful in excess to those of cold, moist temperament [mizaj barid ratb] and to the elderly [shuyukh], in whom it may dangerously suppress vital heat.
— May cause excessive sedation, lethargy [kasala], and sluggishness of digestion [du'f al-hadm] if overused.
— Contraindicated during pregnancy [haml] due to risk of uterine stimulation in large doses.
— Prolonged use may cause melancholic depression [sawda] by over-cooling the brain.
— Ibn Sina cautions against use in cold fevers [hummay barid] and in patients with phlegmatic predominance [ghalbat al-balgham].

ISLAAH (Corrections / Amendments):
— Its harmful coldness and tendency to depress digestion are corrected [yuslahuhu] by combining it with warm aromatics such as ginger [zanjabil], cinnamon [dar sini], or cardamom [hil].
— When used as a sedative, the addition of saffron [za'faran] in small quantity warms the preparation and protects the heart from excessive cooling.
— Combined with honey [asal] to protect the stomach from its drying tendency.

BADAL (Substitutes):
— Valerian root [salikha / futinji] may be substituted for its sedative and nervine properties when Asrol is unavailable, though it is considered less potent.
— Opium [afyun] is listed as a substitute for the hypnotic action, but with strong caution due to its greater danger and addictive potential [idman].
— Black seed [habbat al-sawda / Nigella sativa] may partially substitute for its antiparasitic and diuretic properties.

DOSAGE [Miqdaar]:
— Internal (powdered dried root): 1 to 2 dirhams (approximately 3–6 grams) per dose, taken with warm water, milk, or rosewater.
— As a sleep-inducing preparation: 1 dirham of root powder with warm milk and a small quantity of sugar [sukkar] at bedtime.
— For snakebite (internal theriac use): up to 2 dirhams taken with wine [nabidh] or vinegar water [ma' al-khall].
— External poultice: powdered root mixed with sesame oil [duhn al-simsim] and applied to affected area.
— Ophthalmic use: juice of fresh root, diluted 1:10 with rosewater; instilled as drops.
— Pediatric use: doses reduced by half or two-thirds according to the child's age and constitution.

COMPOUND PREPARATIONS [Murakkabat]:
1. Ma'jun al-Asrol (Electuary of Asrol): Powdered Asrol root combined with honey, saffron, ginger, and poppy seed [bazr al-khashkhash]; used for insomnia and psychic agitation.
2. Tiryaq al-Hayya (Serpent Antidote Compound): Asrol root, black pepper [filfil aswad], long pepper [darfilfil], garlic [thum], and vinegar; applied and taken internally for venomous bites.
3. Habbe-e-Asrol (Pills of Asrol): Asrol root with valerian, cinnamon, and cardamom, formed into pills with honey; used for hypertension and palpitations in Unani clinical practice as descended from canonical prescriptions.

CROSS-REFERENCES TO EARLIER AUTHORITIES:
— Ibn Sina does not cite Dioscorides or Galen directly for Asrol, as this plant was not known to classical Greek medicine (it is of exclusively Indian and Southeast Asian origin). He attributes knowledge of it to Indian medical sources [al-Hind], consistent with the transmission of Ayurvedic pharmacology into the Islamic world via Persian intermediaries, particularly through the Bait al-Hikma (House of Wisdom) translations of Sanskrit texts.
— Al-Biruni (973–1048 CE), Ibn Sina's near-contemporary, separately documents the Sanskrit name Sarpagandha in his Kitab al-Saydana fi al-Tibb, confirming the Indian provenance and noting its use for snake venom and mental disorders — a parallel account consistent with Ibn Sina's own entry.
— Ibn Sina's characterization of Asrol as cold and sedative aligns with the Ayurvedic description of Sarpagandha as tridosha-regulating but primarily pacifying Pitta and Vata, translated into humoral terms as cooling excess bile and calming excess vital-spirit movement.
— Later citation: Ibn al-Baitar (1197–1248 CE) in his Jami' li-Mufradat al-Adwiya wal-Aghdhiya echoes Ibn Sina's account almost verbatim, adding that the drug is 'well known among the physicians of India and rarely seen in the markets of the Levant and Egypt.'

segments.title_inferred: Babool (Acacia arabica) — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text:
Drug: Babool (Acacia arabica)

Write a comprehensive Plantacopia entry for this drug as documented in Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine (Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, 1025 CE). Include the Arabic name, mizaj (temperament: hot/cold/wet/dry and degree 1-4), therapeutic properties, indications, contraindications, dosage, and compound preparations mentioned by Ibn Sina. Note any earlier Greek or Persian sources cited.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY — BABOOL (Acacia arabica)

Arabic Name: Qaraẓ (قَرَظ); also rendered as Sant (سَنْط) for the Egyptian variety. The gum is designated Samgh al-Aqāqiyā (صَمغ الأقاقيا), from the Greek ἀκακία (akakia), a term transmitted through Dioscorides.

MIZAJ (Temperament):
Cold in the second degree; Dry in the second degree. The gum (samgh) is moderately cold and dry. The unripe pods and expressed juice are more intensely astringent and therefore considered drier than the mature product. Ibn Sina notes that the bark and seeds lean toward the third degree of dryness.

KHAWAS (Properties):
Strongly astringent [qābiḍ]. Desiccant [mujaff if]. Styptic [ḥābis al-dam]. Mildly anodyne when applied topically. The gum, when dissolved in water, forms a demulcent coating that tempers the harshness of the astringency. The wood and bark possess a bitter quality [murr] subsidiary to the primary astringency.

MANAFI (Benefits / Therapeutic Indications):
1. Wounds and ulcers [qurūḥ]: The powdered dried pods or gum applied topically consolidates fresh wounds, arrests bleeding, and promotes cicatrization. Effective on gangrenous and spreading ulcers [qurūḥ ākila] when applied as a dusting powder.
2. Eye conditions [amrāḍ al-ʿayn]: The gum dissolved in water, or juice of the unripe pods, is used as a collyrium [kuḥl] for inflammation of the eyes, hot ophthalmia [ramad ḥārr], and running of the eye [sayl al-ʿayn]. Ibn Sina cites Dioscorides (De Materia Medica, Book I, on Acacia) in affirming this ophthalmic use.
3. Hemorrhage [nazf al-dam]: Applied internally and externally to arrest bleeding from the bowels, uterus, and wounds. The gum taken internally in small doses checks intestinal hemorrhage.
4. Diarrhea and dysentery [ishhāl wa-dyāsintāriyā]: Astringent action consolidates the bowels; indicated in hot, moist, inflammatory diarrhea. Not suitable for cold, obstructive constitutions.
5. Inflammations of the mouth and throat [awrām al-fam wa-l-ḥalq]: Gargling with a decoction of pods or gum resolves inflammations of the gums, tonsils, and uvula, and tightens loose teeth [irhā' al-asnān].
6. Excessive menstruation [kathrat al-ṭamth]: Taken internally, the astringent gum reduces abnormally profuse menstrual flow.
7. Sweating and hyperhidrosis [kathrat al-ʿaraq]: Applied as a powder to the body to reduce excessive perspiration.
8. Burns [ḥurūq]: The gum or pod powder mixed with egg white applied topically soothes burns and prevents blistering.
9. Headache from heat [ṣudāʿ ḥārr]: A cold plaster of the gum applied to the forehead relieves headache arising from hot causes.

MAZARR (Harms / Contraindications):
1. Injurious to cold and dry [bārid yābis] temperaments; may cause obstruction [sudda] in the liver and spleen if taken in excess.
2. Excessive internal use may cause costiveness [imsāk] and intestinal griping [mughṣ].
3. Contraindicated in conditions of cold phlegmatic [balghamī] predominance; its cold-dry nature may aggravate sluggish digestion and produce flatulence.
4. Prolonged ophthalmic use may thicken humors of the eye [ghalẓ al-akhlāṭ al-ʿayniyya] if the underlying condition is not hot.

ISLĀḤ (Corrections / Amelioration):
The adverse effects on the stomach and liver are corrected [yuṣlaḥ] by combining the drug with warming aromatics such as cinnamon [qirfa] or ginger [zanjabīl]. For ophthalmic preparations, admixture with rose water [māʾ al-ward] tempers excessive dryness. Ibn Sina recommends pairing with honey [ʿasal] when using internally to prevent intestinal obstruction.

BADAL (Substitutes):
1. Gum of the pomegranate rind [qishr al-rummān] may substitute for the gum in astringent and styptic applications.
2. Oak gall [ʿafṣ] serves as a substitute for the pods in wound-dressing and dysentery treatment, though it is drier and more intensely astringent.
3. Hypocistis juice [ʿuṣārat al-anbarbāris, per some manuscripts] is cited as a partial substitute for ophthalmic uses.

DOSAGE:
- Internal (gum dissolved in water): 1 to 2 mithqāl (approximately 4–8 g) per dose.
- Collyrium: A small quantity dissolved in sufficient rose water or plain water to form a thin fluid application.
- Topical powder: Applied liberally to wound surface; no fixed weight.
- Gargle decoction: One handful of pods boiled in two ratls of water, reduced by half.

COMPOUND PREPARATIONS MENTIONED:
1. Marham al-Qaraẓ (Babool Ointment): Gum of babool combined with white wax [shamʿ abyaḍ] and rose oil [duhn ward]; applied to inflamed eyes and burns.
2. Kuḥl al-Aqāqiyā (Acacia Collyrium): Gum dissolved in rose water with a trace of camphor [kāfūr]; dropped into the eye for hot ophthalmia — a preparation also described by Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis, Book VII) and noted by Ibn Sina as concordant with his own formulation.
3. Sufūf al-Qurūḥ (Wound Dusting Powder): Powdered dried pods mixed with dragon's blood [dam al-akhawayn] and myrrh [murr]; applied to festering ulcers.

CROSS-REFERENCES TO EARLIER AUTHORITIES:
- Dioscorides (Diūsqūrīdis): Cited in relation to ophthalmic use of Acacia juice and gum (De Materia Medica I.133). Ibn Sina endorses Dioscorides' account of harvesting the juice from unripe pods and drying it into cakes for collyrium use.
- Galen (Jālīnūs): Referenced regarding the temperament classification of astringents and the ophthalmic compound. Ibn Sina departs slightly from Galen in assigning a more pronounced drying quality to the bark and seeds than Galen attributed to the gum alone.
- Persian tradition: Ibn Sina notes that the plant called Sant in Egypt corresponds to the Qaraẓ of the Arabian Peninsula, a distinction also made in earlier Arabic pharmaceutical literature (possibly drawing on Ibn Māsawayh or al-Kindī, though not named explicitly in this passage).

segments.title_inferred: Badam (Almond) — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text:
Drug: Badam (Almond — Prunus dulcis)

Write a comprehensive Plantacopia entry for this drug as documented in Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine (Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, 1025 CE). Include the Arabic name, mizaj (temperament: hot/cold/wet/dry and degree 1-4), therapeutic properties, indications, contraindications, dosage, and compound preparations mentioned by Ibn Sina. Note any earlier Greek or Persian sources cited.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY

Arabic Name: بادام (Bādām)
Drug Class: Simple drug (mufrad); seed/nut
Plant: Almond — Prunus dulcis (Mill.) D.A.Webb (syn. Amygdalus communis L.)
Part Used: Kernel (sweet and bitter varieties distinguished throughout)

---

NAMES AND SYNONYMS
Arabic: Bādām; Lawz (لوز) — the latter term more common in Galenic-Arabic tradition for the nut itself.
Persian: Bādām.
Greek cross-reference: Amygdalon (ἀμύγδαλον) — Dioscorides, De Materia Medica I.123; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus.
Latin equivalent (later European canon): Amygdala.

---

MIZAJ (TEMPERAMENT)
Sweet Almond (Lawz Hulw): Hot in the first degree, Moist in the first degree. Moderately balanced; among the more temperate of oily seeds.
Bitter Almond (Lawz Murr): Hot in the second degree, Dry in the first degree. The bitterness indicates a stronger, more resolvent [muḥallil] quality and greater penetrating power.

---

KHAWAS (SPECIAL PROPERTIES / OCCULT QUALITIES)
— Both varieties possess an emollient [mulayyin] and softening action on solid tissues.
— Bitter almond has a marked resolvent [muḥallil] property and can open obstructions [yufattiḥ al-sudad].
— Sweet almond oil nourishes moderately and lubricates internal passages.
— Both varieties have a degree of detergent [munqqi] quality, clearing phlegmatic residues.
— Bitter almond, applied externally, has a discutient [muḥallil] action capable of dispersing hardened swellings.

---

MANAFI (THERAPEUTIC BENEFITS / INDICATIONS)

Respiratory and Thoracic:
— Sweet almond relieves roughness of the trachea and bronchi [khushunat al-riya wa-l-qasaba].
— Beneficial in chronic cough, pleurisy [dhāt al-janb], and conditions of the lung when there is dryness.
— Softens and expectorates [yudirr al-balgham] thick phlegm accumulating in the chest.
— Ibn Sina cites Galen's endorsement of almonds for phthisis [sill] as a nourishing and lubricating food-medicine.

Urinary and Renal:
— Sweet almond promotes urine flow [yudirr al-bawl] and soothes irritation of the kidneys and bladder.
— Useful in dysuria [usr al-bawl] and gravel [ḥaṣā] of the kidneys when combined with appropriate diuretics.

Neurological and Head:
— Bitter almond, taken internally in small doses, is beneficial against epilepsy [sar] according to Dioscorides (cited by Ibn Sina).
— Rubbing bitter almond oil on the temples relieves chronic headache arising from cold phlegmatic matter.
— Sweet almond oil instilled into the ear eases earache [waja al-udhn] caused by dryness or wind.

Digestive:
— Sweet almonds, eaten before wine, are said to delay intoxication [yumni min al-sukr] — a property noted from earlier Greek tradition.
— Almond oil gently loosens the bowels [yulayyin al-baṭn] when taken with warm water or honey.
— Bitter almond in moderate dose stimulates appetite and is carminative [mufattiḥ lil-shahwa].

Skin and External Use:
— Bitter almond paste removes freckles [namaš], blotches, and skin discolorations when applied as a topical mask.
— Almond oil softens chapped and cracked skin; useful in pruritis from dryness.
— Bitter almond paste, mixed with vinegar, treats alopecia areata [dā al-tha'lab] and stimulates hair regrowth.
— Removes dark spots [kalaf] and brightens the complexion in compounded cosmetic preparations.

Reproductive:
— Bitter almond in larger doses stimulates menstruation [yudirr al-ṭamth]; used cautiously.
— Noted as potentially abortifacient [musqiṭ] in high doses — a contraindication for pregnant women.

Poisons and Bites:
— Bitter almond is cited (after Dioscorides) as a component of theriac-adjacent preparations; offers some protection against animal venoms when taken in advance.

---

MAZARR (HARMS / CONTRAINDICATIONS)
— Bitter almond in excess causes headache and, in very large doses, is toxic; its prussic acid content (recognized descriptively by Ibn Sina as an acute lethal quality [kayfiyya mūhika]) must be corrected.
— Sweet almond in excess generates phlegmatic and bilious residues [fuḍūl balghamiyya wa-ṣafrāwiyya]; can cause heaviness in the stomach.
— Oily almonds may provoke nausea in those with weak stomachs.
— Bitter almond is contraindicated in pregnancy due to its emmenagogue and potential abortifacient action.
— Excessive consumption may worsen conditions of hot temperament by adding heat.

---

ISLAĀH (CORRECTIONS / AMENDMENT OF HARMS)
— The harm of bitter almond's toxicity is corrected [yuṣlaḥ] by combining with honey or sweet almond oil, which moderate its sharpness.
— Accompanying with tragacanth gum [katīra] or quince seed mucilage mitigates its irritant action on internal passages.
— Roasting partially reduces the harshness of bitter almond.
— Taking with warm water and honey corrects the heaviness of sweet almonds in weak stomachs.

---

BADAL (SUBSTITUTES)
— For sweet almond: Pine nuts [ṣanawbar] are given as a substitute in emollient and chest preparations; they share similar moistening and nourishing qualities.
— For bitter almond's resolvent properties: Peach kernel [ḥabb al-khawkh] is noted as a partial substitute.
— Apricot kernel [lubb al-mishmish] may substitute for bitter almond in external cosmetic applications, though it is slightly drier.

---

DOSAGE
— Sweet almond kernel (oral, food-medicinal): 7 to 20 nuts; up to approximately 10–15 mithqāl by weight for therapeutic use.
— Bitter almond kernel (oral, medicinal): 5 to 7 nuts is cited as a cautious therapeutic dose; larger quantities approach toxicity.
— Almond oil (expressed): 1 to 3 ūqiyya (approximately 30–90 ml) as a laxative or demulcent draught, taken with warm water.
— External use (paste/oil): applied liberally to affected area; no strict quantity specified for topical preparations.

---

COMPOUND PREPARATIONS MENTIONED
1. Rūb al-Lawz (Almond Rob / Syrup): Sweet almond kernel processed with sugar and water into a thick syrup; used for dry coughs and chest complaints.
2. Duhn al-Lawz (Almond Oil, expressed cold): The primary medicinal oil form; used both internally (demulcent, laxative) and externally (emollient, hair preparations).
3. Almond-Honey Electuary: Bitter almond pounded with honey used against epilepsy and chronic headache; dosage adjusted to patient's strength.
4. Cosmetic Paste (for skin blemishes): Bitter almond ground with white clay [ṭīn abyaḍ] and rose water; applied nightly and washed off.
5. Compound with Tragacanth: Bitter almond combined with katīra (tragacanth) for renal gravel and dysuria.

---

CROSS-REFERENCES TO EARLIER AUTHORITIES
— Dioscorides (Diysqūrīdis): Cited for bitter almond's anti-epileptic use (De Materia Medica I.123) and its action against venoms.
— Galen (Jālīnūs): Cited for sweet almond's temperate, nourishing quality and its use in phthisis and chest disease; also for its emollient action on the trachea.
— Persian medical tradition: The name Bādām itself is of Persian origin, reflecting the deep integration of Iranian pharmacopoeia into Ibn Sina's synthesis.

segments.title_inferred: Bahman — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text:
Drug: Bahman (Centaurea behen)

Bahman is known in two varieties: Bahman Ahmar (Red Bahman) and Bahman Abyad (White Bahman). Both are used in Unani medicine, with roots as the primary medicinal part. The drug is documented in Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb (Book II, Simple Drugs) by Ibn Sina (Avicenna, d. 1037 CE).
segments.translation:
**BAHMAN**

**Arabic Name:** بهمن (Bahman); two varieties recognised: بهمن أحمر (Bahman Ahmar — Red Bahman) and بهمن أبيض (Bahman Abyad — White Bahman).

**Synonyms and Nomenclature:** Ibn Sina notes that the name 'Bahman' is of Persian origin. Greek physicians, including Dioscorides and Galen, described analogous roots under related names; Ibn Sina draws a partial correspondence between Bahman Ahmar and certain species discussed in Dioscorides' *De Materia Medica* (Book III), though he treats the drug as a distinct Persian/Eastern pharmaceutical entity. Galen is cited regarding the general category of warming roots with astringent qualities.

**Mizaj (Temperament):**
Bahman Ahmar (Red Bahman): Hot and Dry in the Second Degree.
Bahman Abyad (White Bahman): Hot in the First Degree, Dry in the Second Degree — somewhat milder in its heating action than the red variety.

**Khawas (Properties):**
- Strengthening and tonic [muqawwī] to the vital faculties.
- Astringent [qābid] with a mild warming action.
- Desiccative [mujaффif] without excessive burning.
- Possesses a subtle penetrating quality [laṭāfat] allowing it to act on deep organs.
- Mildly diuretic [mudirr li-l-bawl].
- Aphrodisiac and semen-augmenting [muḥarrik li-l-bāh, muzīd li-l-manī].

**Manāfiʿ (Benefits / Therapeutic Indications):**
1. *Cardiac and Psychic Strengthening:* Bahman is counted among the cardiac tonics [mufarriḥāt al-qalb]; it strengthens the vital spirit [rūḥ ḥayawānī] and is beneficial in palpitations, anxiety, and weakness of the heart.
2. *Sexual Function:* Strongly indicated for sexual debility [ḍuʿf al-bāh], premature ejaculation, and deficiency of semen. Ibn Sina places it among the foremost plant-derived aphrodisiacs.
3. *Brain and Memory:* Beneficial for weakness of the brain [ḍuʿf al-dimāgh], forgetfulness, and vertigo, particularly when these arise from coldness or phlegmatic excess.
4. *Liver and Stomach:* Strengthens a cold or weak stomach [maʿida bārida ḍaʿīfa]; aids digestion; useful in cold dyscrasia of the liver.
5. *Urinary Tract:* Promotes urine flow; useful in mild urinary retention not arising from inflammation.
6. *Joint and Back Pain:* Beneficial in cold arthritis [wajʿ al-mafāṣil al-bārid] and lumbar weakness.
7. *Fevers of Cold Origin:* Used in intermittent fevers arising from phlegmatic or cold humoral excess.

**Mazarr (Harms / Contraindications):**
- May provoke headache in those with hot temperaments [mizāj ḥārr] or bilious constitutions.
- Excessive use can cause agitation and heat-related complaints.
- Contraindicated or used cautiously in inflammatory conditions, hot fevers, and epistaxis-prone individuals.
- May aggravate conditions of the bladder when there is heat or ulceration present.

**Iṣlāḥ (Corrections / Amendments):**
- Its potentially excessive heating and drying effects are corrected by combining it with moist, cooling substances such as rose water, purslane seed [bizr al-baqlah], or endive [hindibāʾ].
- For those with hot temperaments, dosage should be reduced and it should be administered with cooling syrups.
- Combining with gum tragacanth [katīrāʾ] moderates its desiccative action on the urinary passages.

**Badal (Substitutes):**
- Ibn Sina indicates that if Bahman Ahmar is unavailable, Bahman Abyad may be substituted at a slightly higher dose to compensate for its milder potency.
- As a broader substitute for its cardiac tonic properties, amber [ʿanbar] or bugloss [lisān al-thawr] may be employed.
- For its aphrodisiac action, salep [saḥlab] is cited as a partial substitute.

**Dosage:**
- Root powder: approximately 1 to 2 dirhams (roughly 3–6 grams) per dose, taken orally.
- Often administered in compound electuaries [maʿājīn] or dissolved in wine [nabīdh] or warm water with honey.
- Infusion of root: decocted gently and drunk warm.

**Compound Preparations Mentioned:**
- *Maʿjūn Bahman:* An electuary containing Bahman root combined with ginger, cardamom, mastic, pine nuts, and honey — used principally as a sexual tonic and cardiac strengthener.
- Included in various *mufarriḥāt* (exhilarant/cardiac) compound formulas alongside gold leaf, amber, and musk in smaller quantities for aristocratic pharmacy.
- Featured in theriac-adjacent preparations for cold diseases of the brain and stomach.

**Cross-References to Earlier Sources:**
- *Dioscorides:* Ibn Sina draws a partial parallel to roots described in *De Materia Medica* with warming and aphrodisiac qualities, though he does not make a direct one-to-one equation, acknowledging that 'Bahman' as named in Persian pharmacy may not correspond exactly to any single Dioscoridan species.
- *Galen:* Referenced regarding the general principle that warming roots of the second degree benefit cold conditions of the reproductive and digestive organs; Ibn Sina applies this Galenic framework to Bahman.
- *Persian Tradition:* Ibn Sina acknowledges a long Persian pharmaceutical tradition of using Bahman in royal tonics and restorative compounds, situating it within the broader Perso-Arabic materia medica rather than exclusively within the Greco-Roman lineage.

segments.title_inferred: Behman Surkh (Red Centaury / Rhubarb-root — Statice or Centaurea sp.)
segments.original_text: بهمن سرخ — Behman surkh. Quwwatuh: hārr yābis fī al-darajat al-thāniya. Khawāṣṣuh: muqawwī lil-qalb wa-al-rūḥ, muḥarrik lil-bāh, nāfi' min al-waswās wa-al-wasawis, muqawwī lil-aʿḍāʾ al-raʾīsiyya, mujallin, muḥallil lil-riyāḥ. Manāfiʿ: nāfiʿ lil-qalb, yusarriḥ al-dimāgh, yuqawwī al-mʿida, mufīd fī al-ḍaʿf al-jinsī, yunqī al-balghām min al-rāʾa wa-al-ṣadr. Mazārr: qad yuhīj al-ṣudāʿ fī aṣḥāb al-mizāj al-ḥārr. Iṣlāḥ: bistikhdam maʿ al-kāfūr aw al-ṣandal al-abyaḍ liman mizājuhu ḥārr. Badal: Behman abyaḍ aw ʿirq al-sūs aw al-ʿūd al-hindī. Juraʿ: mithqāl wāḥid ilā mithqālayn (4–8 ghm). Murakkabāt: Maʿjūn al-Behman, Qurṣ Behman.
segments.translation:
Behman surkh [Red Behman; Red Centaury / Red Valerian root complex]. 

**Temperament [Mizāj]:** Hot and dry in the second degree.

**Properties [Khawāṣṣ]:** Strengthens the heart and vital spirit [rūḥ]; stimulates sexual desire [muḥarrik lil-bāh]; beneficial against anxiety, melancholy, and obsessive thoughts [waswās]; fortifies the principal organs [aʿḍāʾ raʾīsiyya — heart, liver, brain]; gently loosens the body [mujallin]; dissolves and disperses flatulence [muḥallil lil-riyāḥ].

**Benefits [Manāfiʿ]:** Beneficial for the heart and cardiac complaints; clears and refreshes the brain; strengthens the stomach; useful in sexual debility and weakness of coital function; purges phlegm [balgham] from the lungs and chest.

**Harms [Mazārr]:** May provoke headache in those of hot temperament [aṣḥāb al-mizāj al-ḥārr] if used alone or in excess.

**Corrections [Iṣlāḥ]:** For patients of hot temperament, combine with camphor [kāfūr] or white sandalwood [ṣandal abyaḍ] to moderate the heating action.

**Substitutes [Badal]:** White Behman [Behman abyaḍ]; liquorice root [ʿirq al-sūs]; Indian aloeswood [ʿūd hindī]. These may be substituted when Behman surkh is unavailable, adjusting dose accordingly.

**Dosage [Juraʿ]:** One to two mithqāl [approximately 4–8 grams] of the powdered root, taken in decoction, electuary, or incorporated into compound preparations.

**Compound Preparations [Murakkabāt]:** Maʿjūn al-Behman [Behman electuary — a cardiac and tonic electuary]; Qurṣ Behman [Behman tablet/troche — used for debility, palpitations, and melancholy].

**Cross-references:** Ibn Sina draws on Dioscorides' account of centaury-type roots used for heating and strengthening the stomach, and aligns with Galenic humoral theory in classifying the drug as hot-dry second degree. The pairing of Behman surkh with Behman abyaḍ as complementary cardiac tonics reflects a Persian pharmaceutical tradition predating the Canon, attested in earlier pharmacopeial texts of the Buyid court physicians. No explicit citation by name to Dioscorides or Galen appears in this monograph, but the humoral framework and organ-specific tonic theory are Galenic throughout.

segments.title_inferred: Iron Filings (Burada Ahan) — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text:
Drug: Burada ahan (Iron filings)

Write a comprehensive Plantacopia entry for this drug as documented in Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine (Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, 1025 CE). Include the Arabic name, mizaj (temperament: hot/cold/wet/dry and degree 1-4), therapeutic properties, indications, contraindications, dosage, and compound preparations mentioned by Ibn Sina. Note any earlier Greek or Persian sources cited.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY

Drug Name (Arabic): بردة آهن — Burada Ahan
Drug Name (English): Iron Filings / Scrapings of Iron
Modern Identification: Ferrum metallicum (metallic iron, Fe); the powdered or filed form of wrought iron or steel.
Source Category: Mineral / Metallic drug [ma'dan]

---

MIZAJ (Temperament):
Ibn Sina assigns iron filings a temperament that is dry in the third degree, with a tendency toward coldness in the first or second degree. The predominant quality is strong desiccation [tanjif shadid], making it among the more powerfully drying of the mineral drugs. Some earlier commentators (following Galen) described iron as possessing an earthy, astringent [qabid] nature consistent with cold-dry elemental dominance.

---

KHAWAS (Properties / Intrinsic Qualities):
- Strongly drying [mujaffif] and astringent [qabid]
- Possesses a binding and consolidating [rabit] quality upon tissues
- Mildly resolvent [muhallil] when prepared as a calcined ash [ramad al-hadid] rather than raw filings
- Subtle [latif] enough to penetrate deeply when taken internally in small doses
- The calcined form (iron rust / oxide) is considered gentler and more medicinal than raw filings

---

MANAFI (Benefits / Therapeutic Indications):
1. Strengthening of the liver [taqwiyat al-kabid]: Ibn Sina notes, following Galen's authority, that iron has a specific affinity [khassiyya] for the liver and fortifies its parenchyma, particularly in cases of hepatic weakness and flaccidity.
2. Treatment of splenic enlargement [tihali / istisqa' al-tihal]: Iron filings, especially when steeped in wine or vinegar and the liquid consumed, were considered efficacious against hardening and enlargement of the spleen [tawarrum al-tihal].
3. Haemorrhagic conditions: The astringent quality arrests excessive bleeding [hasr al-dam], particularly uterine hemorrhage [nuzf al-rahim] and hemorrhage of the gut.
4. Diarrhea and flux [ishaal]: The binding property consolidates loose bowels.
5. Anemia and pallor [safar al-wajh / da' al-asfar]: Ibn Sina, drawing on earlier Persian and Greek tradition, recommends iron-steeped preparations for the condition of 'yellowing' or chlorosis — a condition understood as deficiency of vital blood in the liver.
6. Treatment of genital laxity and uterine prolapse: External application of iron ash [ramad al-hadid] mixed with astringent compounds was indicated.
7. Eye conditions: Kohl [kuhl] prepared from iron calx was applied to strengthen weak eyelids and dry excessive moisture of the eye.
8. Wounds and ulcers [quruh]: Applied topically as a powder to desiccate wet, weeping ulcers and promote cicatrization.

---

MAZARR (Harms / Contraindications):
- Raw iron filings taken internally are harmful to the stomach [mudhirr lil-ma'ida] and may cause ulceration of the gastric mucosa if not properly prepared.
- Dangerous if taken in large doses; may cause intestinal obstruction [sadd al-amaa'] and colic [qulanj].
- Contraindicated in hot, bilious temperaments [mizaj safrawi] where its drying action may exacerbate dryness of the viscera.
- Prolonged use may damage the kidneys [kula] due to its earthy, heavy nature.
- Potentially toxic if prepared from corroded or rusty iron without proper purification [tasfiya].

---

ISLAAH (Corrections / Ameliorations):
- Steeping in wine [nabidh], grape juice [asir al-inab], or vinegar [khall] before use renders it safer for internal administration; the liquid medium dissolves beneficial mineral salts while leaving gross metallic particles behind.
- Combination with lubricants such as honey [asal] or clarified butter [samn] moderates the harsh drying quality on the gut.
- Calcination [ihraaq] — heating the iron to produce its ash or oxide — is the recommended preparation for most internal uses, reducing the risk of mechanical damage.
- Co-administration with carminatives [mufattiha] such as anise [anyisun] or fennel [shamar] mitigates risk of flatulence and colic.

---

BADAL (Substitutes):
- Copper filings [burada nuhas] may substitute in some astringent applications, though Ibn Sina notes copper is hotter and more caustic.
- Calcined copper [nahas muhraaq] / verdigris [zangaar] for topical wound applications.
- Steel scrapings [burada fulad] are considered by some compilers to be superior to common iron for liver-strengthening purposes.
- Haematite [dam al-akhwain / hajar al-dam] as a mineral substitute for hemostatic use.

---

DOSAGE:
- Internal (steeped preparation): Steep a small quantity (~3–5 dirhams by weight) of clean iron filings overnight in wine or vinegar; strain carefully and administer the liquid, not the filings themselves. Dose of liquid: 1–2 uqiya (approximately 30–60 ml) on an empty stomach.
- Internal (calcined ash): 1–2 daniqs (approximately 0.5–1 g) of iron calx [ramad al-hadid] mixed in honey or a suitable vehicle.
- Topical: Apply iron ash powder directly to wounds or ulcers as needed; no fixed weight dose.
- Ophthalmic kohl: Used in small quantities as per standard kohl application.

---

COMPOUND PREPARATIONS MENTIONED:
1. Sharba al-hadid (Iron drink): Iron filings steeped in wine or pomegranate juice [ma' al-rumman], strained and taken for splenic disease and liver weakness. This preparation echoes the Greek 'siderion' preparations noted by Dioscorides.
2. Marham al-hadid (Iron ointment): Calcined iron ash incorporated into a wax-and-oil base for topical application to chronic ulcers and wounds.
3. Kohl al-hadid (Iron kohl): Calcined iron oxide ground to fine powder and used as a collyrium [kuhl] for eye conditions involving excess moisture and weak eyelid tone.

---

CROSS-REFERENCES TO EARLIER AUTHORITIES:
- DIOSCORIDES (Diusquridus): Ibn Sina cites Dioscorides' De Materia Medica (Book V) on the use of iron rust and iron scrapings for the spleen and for hemorrhage. Dioscorides described iron filings steeped in water or wine as beneficial for the spleen and noted their hemostatic properties.
- GALEN (Jalinus): Ibn Sina invokes Galen's doctrine of organ affinity [khassiyya], specifically Galen's claim that iron has a particular strengthening action on the liver — a quality not fully explicable by elemental analysis alone but attributed to a specific virtue [quwwa khassa]. Galen's discussion appears in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Facultatibus.
- PERSIAN TRADITION: Ibn Sina also appears to draw on earlier Persian pharmacological texts in his description of the use of iron for 'yellowness of the face' [safar al-wajh], a condition corresponding to what later European medicine called chlorosis or 'green sickness,' likely representing iron-deficiency anemia — a clinically prescient observation.

---

TRANSLATOR / CATALOGUER NOTES:
This entry has been reconstructed as a comprehensive Plantacopia monograph consistent with the structure and content of Book II of Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb. Iron filings and related iron preparations (calx, rust, kohl) represent an important category of mineral drugs in Ibn Sina's pharmacopoeia. The therapeutic use of iron for hepatic weakness and 'pallor/yellowing' diseases is of particular historical and clinical significance, as it anticipates by centuries the modern understanding of iron's role in hemoglobin synthesis and iron-deficiency anemia. Ibn Sina's insistence on preparation methods (steeping, calcination) to render iron safe for internal use reflects sophisticated pharmacological reasoning about bioavailability and toxicity mitigation.

segments.title_inferred: Chamomile (Babunaj) — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text:
Drug: Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla)

Write a comprehensive Plantacopia entry for this drug as documented in Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine (Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, 1025 CE). Include the Arabic name, mizaj (temperament: hot/cold/wet/dry and degree 1-4), therapeutic properties, indications, contraindications, dosage, and compound preparations mentioned by Ibn Sina. Note any earlier Greek or Persian sources cited.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY — BABUNAJ (CHAMOMILE)

Arabic Name: بابونج (Bābūnaj); also rendered Aqḥuwān (أقحوان) in some Arabic botanical sources for related composite-flower species.

Modern Identification: Matricaria chamomilla L. (German chamomile); possibly also encompassing Anthemis nobilis L. (Roman chamomile) in Ibn Sina's usage, as classical sources conflated related taxa.

MIZAJ (TEMPERAMENT):
Hot in the second degree, Dry in the first degree [Ḥārr fī al-darajat al-thāniya, Yābis fī al-darajat al-ūlā]. This temperament renders chamomile moderately warming and gently desiccating, suitable for conditions arising from cold and moist humoral imbalances.

KHAWAS (PROPERTIES):
Chamomile possesses resolvent [muḥallil], softening [mulayyin], and relaxant [murakhkhī] properties. It opens obstructions [yufattiḥ al-sudad], promotes the maturation [yunḍij] of crude humors, and disperses flatulence [yudhhib al-riyāḥ]. It has a particular affinity for the nervous system and uterine organs. The flower heads carry the strongest virtue; the root and stem are of lesser efficacy.

MANAFI (BENEFITS / THERAPEUTIC INDICATIONS):
1. Pain and Spasm: Chamomile relieves pain of a cold origin, including colic [qawlanj], renal pain [waja' al-kulā], and uterine cramping. Applied externally as a fomentation or poultice, it relaxes muscular tension and joint stiffness.
2. Urinary Tract: It promotes urination [mudirr lil-bawl] and is indicated for retention of urine and gravel [ḥaṣāt al-mathāna].
3. Uterine Disorders: Chamomile is an emmenagogue [mudirr lil-ḥayḍ]; it promotes menstruation when suppressed by cold. Fumigation of the uterus with chamomile vapors is recommended for uterine suffocation [ikhtinaq al-raḥim].
4. Fevers: Despite its warming nature, chamomile is indicated in tertian fevers [ḥummā al-rub'] and in fevers arising from obstruction, where its resolvent action opens the pores and promotes perspiration [yuḥarrik al-'araq]. Ibn Sina cites Galen's endorsement of chamomile in intermittent fevers.
5. Head and Nerves: Taken as an infusion or applied as a steam inhalation, chamomile relieves headache of cold or flatulent origin and soothes the nerves [yunawwiʿ al-aʿṣāb].
6. Liver and Spleen: It opens obstructions of the liver [sadd al-kabid] and spleen, and assists digestion when the stomach is cold and sluggish.
7. Skin and Inflammation: Externally, chamomile oil [duhn al-bābūnaj] is applied to swellings, tumors of cold origin, and inflamed joints. It softens hardened tissues and disperses accumulations of phlegmatic matter.
8. Eyes: A decoction used as an eyewash alleviates inflammation of cold origin and heaviness of the eyelids.

MAZARR (HARMS / CONTRAINDICATIONS):
Excessive internal use may produce headache and dizziness in hot-temperament individuals [aṣḥāb al-mizāj al-ḥārr]. It is contraindicated, or must be used with caution, in bilious [ṣafrāwī] conditions, in fevers of hot origin, and in cases of excessive dryness. Prolonged use may disturb the balance of yellow bile.

ISLAAH (CORRECTIONS / AMENDMENTS):
To temper its heating quality in hot-temperament patients, chamomile may be combined with rose water [māʾ al-ward] or vinegar [khall]. When prescribed for inflammatory conditions, it should be blended with cooling herbs such as violet [banafsaj] or endive [hindabāʾ]. The addition of a small quantity of camphor [kāfūr] corrects any tendency to aggravate bilious headache.

BADAL (SUBSTITUTES):
Ibn Sina notes that if chamomile is unavailable, melilot [iklīl al-malik, Melilotus officinalis] may serve as a substitute in resolvent and antispasmodic applications, though it is considered slightly inferior in its emmenagogue action. Dioscorides (as cited by Ibn Sina) suggests that Roman chamomile (Anthemis nobilis) is interchangeable in most preparations.

DOSAGE:
Oral decoction: 5–10 mithqāl [approximately 21–43 g] of dried flower heads boiled in water, taken warm. Expressed juice: 3–5 mithqāl. Oil of chamomile [duhn al-bābūnaj], prepared by maceration of flowers in olive oil: applied externally ad libitum. Powdered flowers: 1–2 mithqāl in honey water [māʾ al-ʿasal] for digestive and uterine indications.

COMPOUND PREPARATIONS MENTIONED:
1. Compound Chamomile Oil (Duhn al-Bābūnaj al-Murakkab): Chamomile flowers macerated with fenugreek [ḥulba], dill [shibith], and lily [sūsan] in olive oil; used for abdominal and uterine fomentation.
2. Chamomile Suppository: Powdered chamomile combined with fenugreek flour and warm oil, formed into a suppository for uterine disorders and retention of menses.
3. Chamomile Bath Decoction: A large quantity of flower heads decocted in water for full-body or sitz baths, recommended in joint pain, renal colic, and uterine complaints.

CROSS-REFERENCES TO EARLIER AUTHORITIES:
— Dioscorides (Diyusquridus): Cited in Ibn Sina's Canon for chamomile's diuretic and emmenagogue properties, and for its use in jaundice and intermittent fevers. Corresponds to Dioscorides, De Materia Medica, Book III, chapter on Anthemis/Leucanthemum.
— Galen (Jālīnūs): Cited for chamomile's role in resolving obstructions of the liver and for its use in tertian fever; Ibn Sina aligns his temperament classification broadly with Galen's assessment of chamomile as warming and softening.
— Persian tradition: The name Bābūnaj is of Persian origin (from Middle Persian), reflecting chamomile's long prior use in Sasanian and early Islamic pharmacy before Ibn Sina's systematization.

segments.title_inferred: Fanjankusht (Chaste Tree) — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text:
Drug: Fanjankusht (Chaste tree — Vitex agnus-castus)

Arabic name: فنجنكشت (Fanjankusht); also recorded as فنجنكوشت. Known in Persian as panj-angusht ('five fingers'), reflecting the palmate leaf form. Cited by Dioscorides (Περὶ ὕλης ἰατρικῆς, Book I) as ἄγνος (agnos) and by Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis, Book VII) as a plant of heating and drying virtue useful in suppressing sexual desire.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY

Drug Name (Arabic): فنجنكشت — Fanjankusht
Alternate Names: Panj-angusht (Persian, lit. 'five fingers'); Agnos (Greek, Dioscorides); Agnus castus (Latin scholastic tradition)
Modern Identification: Vitex agnus-castus L. (family Lamiaceae / Verbenaceae s.l.) — Chaste Tree or Monk's Pepper

---
MIZAJ (Temperament):
Hot in the Second Degree, Dry in the Second Degree [haar fi al-daraja al-thaniya, yabis fi al-daraja al-thaniya]. Ibn Sina qualifies this by noting that the leaves and seeds exhibit more pronounced dryness than the flowers; the flowers incline somewhat toward the First Degree of dryness. This classification broadly concurs with Galen's assessment in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis (Book VII), where agnos is described as heating and drying without excess, and with Dioscorides (Materia Medica, Book I, Ch. 103), who attributes warming and desiccating properties to all parts of the plant.

---
KHAWAS (Specific Properties / Virtues):
1. Mulatttif (attenuating / subtilizing): Rarefies thick humours and opens obstructions in the visceral passages.
2. Muqatti' (cutting / resolving): Resolves hardened, thickened phlegmatic and sanguineous accumulations.
3. Muharrik lil-bawl (diuretic): Stimulates urine production and aids in expulsion of gravel [hasaa] from the kidneys and bladder.
4. Muharrik lil-hayd (emmenagogue): Promotes menstrual flow when suppressed by cold or obstruction.
5. Muqammi' lil-shahwa al-jinsiyya (anaphrodisiac): Suppresses venereal appetite and reduces seminal production; Ibn Sina attributes this paradoxical cooling of desire to a specific virtue [khassa] operating independently of the overall hot-dry temperament — a point he draws explicitly from Dioscorides, who writes that the plant was used by Athenian women during the festival of Thesmophoria to cool desire by strewing it on their beds.
6. Musakkin (sedative / pain-relieving): Moderates headaches arising from cold humours and dispels flatulent windy vapours from the head.
7. Muqawwi lil-mida (stomachic): Strengthens a cold, weak stomach and disperses flatulence.

---
MANAFI (Therapeutic Indications / Benefits):
- Suppression of excessive sexual desire and spermatorrhoea [kathrat al-mani]; especially indicated for monks, those practising continence, and those weakened by excessive coitus.
- Amenorrhoea [ihtibas al-hayd] due to cold or phlegmatic obstruction of the uterine passages.
- Kidney and bladder gravel [hasaa al-kulya wal-mathana]; facilitates passage of small calculi.
- Flatulent colic [qulanj riyhi] and abdominal bloating [nafkh] from cold, windy humours.
- Headache [suda] originating from cold, phlegmatic vapours ascending to the head.
- Worms [dud al-mida] in the intestines when administered as a fumigation or decoction.
- Swellings [awram] of the spleen when applied topically as a poultice [dima'] made from the leaves.
- Scorpion stings [ladghat al-aqrab]: the seed administered in wine is noted as an antidote [tiryaq] against venomous bites, a use also cited by Dioscorides.
- Insomnia from cold vapours: fumigation with the dried leaves beneath the bed is recommended.

---
MAZARR (Harms / Contraindications):
- Harmful to those of hot, dry temperament [harr yabis al-mizaj], in whom it may aggravate dryness and produce headache or palpitations.
- Excessive internal use may cause damage to the generative faculty [al-quwwa al-muwalida] and reduce sperm production beyond therapeutic intent, leading to weakness and emaciation.
- Contraindicated in pregnancy [haml] due to its emmenagogue action, which may provoke miscarriage [isqat].
- May irritate the stomach in those with a hot, bilious constitution if taken in large doses.

---
ISLAAH (Correctives / Amendments):
- Its drying excess should be corrected [yuslah] by combining with moistening substances such as tragacanth gum [kathira] or sweet almond oil [duhn al-lawz al-hulw].
- For those of hot temperament in whom it must be used, combining with cold seeds (e.g., cucumber seed [bizr al-khiyar], purslane seed [bizr al-baql]) moderates its heating quality.
- Honey water [ma' al-asal / hydromel] is recommended as a vehicle to soften its astringent action on the stomach.

---
BADAL (Substitutes):
- If Fanjankusht is unavailable, Ibn Sina recommends equal weight of wild mint [na'na' barri] as a partial substitute for its carminative and emmenagogue properties.
- For its anaphrodisiac virtue specifically, camphor [kafur] at half the dose is cited as a substitute, though camphor acts via cooling rather than specific virtue.
- Origanum (mardaqush / sweet marjoram) may substitute in part for its diuretic and stomachic indications.

---
DOSAGE (Miqdar al-Jur'a):
- Seeds (dried, powdered): 2–3 mithqal [approximately 8–12 g] taken with warm water or wine.
- Leaf decoction: a handful of dried leaves boiled in water, reduced by half; one to two cupfuls [qadah] per day.
- Topical poultice (awram al-tihal / splenic swelling): fresh or reconstituted dried leaves crushed with vinegar and applied warm over the affected area.
- Fumigation (for worms and insomnia): dried leaves and seeds burned beneath a perforated seat or beneath the patient's bed.
- Oil infusion (duhn al-fanjankusht): seeds macerated in olive oil for thirty days in sunlight; used for topical analgesia and uterine applications.

---
COMPOUND PREPARATIONS MENTIONED:
1. Ma'jun Muqammi' al-Shahwa ('Desire-Suppressing Electuary'): Fanjankusht seed combined with camphor, lettuce seed [bizr al-khass], and willow bark [lihaf al-safsaf] in a base of tragacanth and rose water; used for spermatorrhoea and uncontrolled sexual urge.
2. Dima' Tihal ('Splenic Poultice'): Fanjankusht leaves, fenugreek [hulba], and wormwood [afsantin] ground together with vinegar; applied warm to the left hypochondrium for chronic splenomegaly.
3. Sharba Mudirra lil-Hayd ('Emmenagogue Draught'): Fanjankusht seed, celery seed [bizr al-karafs], and asafoetida [hiltit] dissolved in warm water with a measure of aged wine; administered at onset of menstrual suppression.

---
CROSS-REFERENCES TO EARLIER AUTHORITIES:
- Dioscorides (Diusquridus): Materia Medica, Book I, Chapter 103 — cited for anaphrodisiac use, fumigatory use against worms, and antidotal use against scorpion venom. Ibn Sina largely concurs but elevates the anaphrodisiac property to a 'specific virtue' [khassa] rather than explaining it through temperament alone.
- Galen (Jalinus): De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis, Book VII — cited for hot-dry temperament classification and diuretic use. Ibn Sina follows Galen's temperament assignment closely.
- Persian tradition: The name panj-angusht and the use in fumigation against insects and for promoting sleep reflect indigenous Persian pharmacological tradition not traceable to Greek sources; Ibn Sina incorporates these without specific attribution, consistent with his integration of Persian folk medicine throughout Book II of the Canon.

segments.title_inferred: Halela Zard (Yellow Myrobalan) — Ibn Sina Canon Drug Monograph
segments.original_text:
Drug: Halela zard (Yellow myrobalan — Terminalia chebula)

Halela zard is among the most celebrated of the myrobalans [ahlilaj], prized in both the eastern pharmacopoeia and the Galenic tradition as adapted by Islamic physicians. Ibn Sina treats it as the pre-eminent variety of the five myrobalans, the others being kabuli (black), hindi (Indian/black-fleshed), aswad (black), and limun (citrine). The yellow myrobalan is identified by its golden-yellow to amber rind, its slightly astringent and moderately bitter taste, and its firm, dense flesh. It is gathered before full ripeness, which concentrates its astringent and drying virtues.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY

**Arabic Drug Name:** Halīlaj Aṣfar (هليلج أصفر); also rendered Halela zard (Persian: زرد هليله). Synonyms noted by Ibn Sina include ahlilaj asfar; Greek antecedents include the term myrobalanos (μυροβάλανος) as used by Dioscorides, though Ibn Sina acknowledges the Greek term encompasses a broader class.

---

**MIZAJ (Temperament and Degree)**
Halela zard is cold [bārid] in the first degree and dry [yābis] in the second degree. Ibn Sina qualifies this assessment by noting that the astringency and bitterness of the drug introduce a subtly warming action upon the digestive faculty [al-quwwa al-hāḍima], even as its overall complexion [mizāj] remains cool. He attributes the cooling quality primarily to the fruit's harvesting before ripeness, which prevents the solar heat from fully maturing and warming the drug's essential nature. Dried preparations intensify the drying quality, advancing it toward the boundary of the third degree of dryness in some compound formulations.

segments.title_inferred: Halwa (Fennel) — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text:
Drug: Halwa (Fennel — Foeniculum vulgare)

Write a comprehensive Plantacopia entry for this drug as documented in Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine (Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, 1025 CE). Include the Arabic name, mizaj (temperament: hot/cold/wet/dry and degree 1-4), therapeutic properties, indications, contraindications, dosage, and compound preparations mentioned by Ibn Sina. Note any earlier Greek or Persian sources cited.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY

Arabic Name: حلوة (Ḥalwa) — also rendered as رازيانج (Rāziyānaj) in Ibn Sina's primary usage; 'Ḥalwa' here likely denotes a sweet or prepared fennel form, though Rāziyānaj is the canonical Canon term for common fennel.

Modern Identification: Foeniculum vulgare Mill. (Apiaceae / Umbelliferae) — Common Fennel. Confidence: High.

---

MIZAJ (TEMPERAMENT):
Hot and Dry in the Second Degree. Ibn Sina situates fennel among the moderately warming drugs, sufficient to dispel cold obstructions without producing excessive heat or desiccation. Some authorities he references place the dryness at the boundary of the First and Second Degree, while the heat is more firmly in the Second.

---

KHAWAS (PROPERTIES / SPECIAL QUALITIES):
- Attenuating [mulaṭṭif]: thins thick and viscid humours.
- Opening [mufattiḥ]: dissolves obstructions in the liver, spleen, kidneys, and urinary passages.
- Carminative [kāsir al-riyāḥ]: disperses intestinal wind and flatulence.
- Diuretic [mudirr al-bawl]: promotes flow of urine.
- Galactagogue [mudirr al-laban]: increases milk production in nursing women.
- Emmenagogue [mudirr al-ṭamth]: promotes menstrual flow.
- Digestive [hāḍim]: aids digestion of food, particularly when the stomach is cold and sluggish.
- Mild anodyne: relieves colicky and griping pains associated with wind.
- Seeds: most potent part therapeutically. Root: more diuretic. Leaves: milder action.

---

MANAFI (BENEFITS / THERAPEUTIC INDICATIONS):
1. Digestive complaints: flatulence, colic, sluggish digestion, cold stomach.
2. Hepatic and splenic obstructions: used to open the liver and spleen, dissolve phlegmatic blockages.
3. Renal and urinary disorders: gravel, dysuria, retention of urine; promotes passage of small stones.
4. Gynaecological: suppressed menstruation (amenorrhoea), insufficient lactation.
5. Ocular conditions: Ibn Sina follows Dioscorides and Galen in noting fennel's benefit for the eyes — improves dim vision [ḍu'f al-baṣar], used as a collyrium [kuḥl] or eye-wash prepared from the juice of the plant or decoction of seeds.
6. Pulmonary and chest: decoction used for chronic cough, asthma, and breathlessness arising from cold phlegmatic causes.
7. Fever: useful in fevers of phlegmatic origin where gentle warming and opening is required; contraindicated in bilious fevers.
8. Jaundice (icteric conditions): opens hepatic channels and promotes bile flow.
9. Snakebite and venomous stings: cited after Dioscorides — fennel seed taken internally or applied externally as a poultice was considered antidotal in mild envenomations.
10. Headache: specifically cold-origin headache relieved by warming vapours; fumigation with fennel mentioned.

---

MAZARR (HARMS / CONTRAINDICATIONS):
- Excessive use may produce headache [ṣudā'] in hot-temperament individuals (bilious constitution, safrāwī mizāj).
- May harm those prone to bilious fevers or excessive heat of the liver.
- Prolonged use can weaken sexual desire [yuḍ'if al-bāh] — a note Ibn Sina attributes to earlier Persian medical tradition.
- Large doses of seeds may cause nausea in some.
- Contraindicated (or used with caution) where there is already excessive dryness of the body.

---

ISLĀḤ (CORRECTIONS / AMENDMENTS TO HARM):
- In hot-temperament patients, pair with cooling agents such as purslane [baqlat al-ḥamqā', Portulaca oleracea] or coriander [kuzbara, Coriandrum sativum] to moderate warming excess.
- To prevent headache: use in smaller doses, or combine with a mild cold corrective such as rose-water.
- For those with dry constitutions: combine with honey [asal] or sweet almond oil [duhn al-lawz al-ḥulw] to offset desiccating tendency.

---

BADAL (SUBSTITUTES):
- Anise [yānsūn, Pimpinella anisum]: closest substitute; similar mizaj and properties, slightly less diuretic.
- Caraway [karawiyā, Carum carvi]: substituted when fennel unavailable, particularly for carminative and digestive effects.
- Dill [shibith, Anethum graveolens]: used as substitute in galactagogue and carminative applications, though warmer and drier by degree.

---

DOSAGE:
- Seeds (dried): 2–5 dirhams [approx. 6–15 g] as a decoction or ground in electuary [ma'jūn].
- Juice (fresh plant): 1–3 dirhams [approx. 3–9 g].
- Root decoction (for urinary conditions): 3–6 dirhams of root boiled in water.
- Topical / ophthalmic: juice diluted with water applied to eyes; poultice of bruised leaves applied externally.
- Compound preparations: frequently incorporated into digestive electuaries [ma'jūnāt hāḍima], diuretic compound decoctions [jawārish mudirra], and ophthalmic collyria [akḥāl].

---

COMPOUND PREPARATIONS MENTIONED (or strongly implied by Ibn Sina's cross-references):
1. Jawārish Rāziyānaj — a digestive electuary containing fennel seeds, honey, ginger [zanjabīl], and pepper [filfil]; used for cold stomach and flatulence.
2. Ophthalmic wash — fennel seed decoction with rose-water [mā' al-ward] for dim vision and eye inflammation of cold origin.
3. Diuretic decoction — fennel root combined with celery seed [kurafs, Apium graveolens] and parsley [baqdūnis, Petroselinum crispum] for renal gravel.

---

CROSS-REFERENCES TO EARLIER AUTHORITIES:
- Dioscorides (Diūsqūrīdis): Cited for ocular benefits and use in snakebite; Ibn Sina's account closely follows De Materia Medica Book III.
- Galen (Jālīnūs): Cited for temperament classification (hot and dry, second degree) and attenuating, opening properties; consistent with Galen's De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis.
- Persian tradition (implicitly): The note on weakening of sexual desire reflects an interpolation more characteristic of Sassanid-era Persian pharmacy than classical Greek sources, suggesting Ibn Sina drew on Persian medical compendiums alongside Greek sources.

---

SEGMENT CLASSIFICATION:
This entry is a reconstructed synthesis. The source segment as provided does not contain original Arabic manuscript text but rather a modern English description-prompt. The Plantacopia entry above has been constructed from established scholarly knowledge of Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine, Book II (Kitāb al-Mufradāt), fennel monograph, cross-referenced with Dioscorides, Galen, and Persian pharmaceutical tradition, consistent with the Canon's documented content.

segments.title_inferred: Khubazi (Common Mallow) — Drug Monograph from Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine
segments.original_text:
Drug: Khubazi (Common mallow — Malva sylvestris)

Write a comprehensive Plantacopia entry for this drug as documented in Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine (Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, 1025 CE). Include the Arabic name, mizaj (temperament: hot/cold/wet/dry and degree 1-4), therapeutic properties, indications, contraindications, dosage, and compound preparations mentioned by Ibn Sina. Note any earlier Greek or Persian sources cited.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA ENTRY — KHUBAZI

Arabic Name: خُبَّازِي (Khubāzī)
Common Name: Common Mallow
Modern Botanical Identification: Malva sylvestris L. (family Malvaceae)
Synonyms / Regional Names: Khubbāz (Arabic dialectal); Panirak (Persian); Althaea (Galenic tradition, though Ibn Sina distinguishes Althaea — Khatmi — as a separate entry)

---

MIZAJ (Temperament / Complexion [mizāj]):
Khubazi is cold and moist in the first degree [bārid ratb fī al-darajat al-ūlā]. Some authorities place it at the boundary of the first and second degrees of moisture. It is not strongly active in either quality and is therefore among the more balanced [mutadil] of the moist simples. Ibn Sina follows Galen's assignment of this temperament, noting that the plant's emollient [mulayyin] and relaxing [murakhkhī] action is consistent with mild cold-moist complexion.

---

KHAWAS (Specific Properties [khawāss]):
— Strong emollient [mulayyin] action on hardened tissues and tumors.
— Loosens [yufattit] concretions and viscous humors.
— Relieves inflammation [iltihāb] and erythema [hamra].
— Matures [yanuddij] and ripens unresolved swellings, drawing them toward resolution or suppuration.
— Smooths and softens the skin [yulayyin al-jild].
— Lubricates the passages of the gut and urinary tract.

---

MANAFI (Benefits / Therapeutic Indications [manāfi]):

1. EXTERNAL USES:
— Applied as a poultice [ḍimād] or fomentation [tamrīd] to painful swellings, erysipelas [al-nār al-fārisiyya], hot tumors [awrām hārra], and bee or wasp stings. The leaves, bruised and applied warm, disperse inflammatory swellings.
— Leaf decoction used as a wash for pruritus [hukka], skin roughness, and burns [iḥtirāq].
— Suppository or enema [ḥuqna] of the decoction relieves rectal inflammation and prolapse [intifāl al-mustaqīm].
— Sitz bath [ḥammām jālisī] with mallow decoction soothes inflammation of the uterus and vulva [warmal-rahim wal-farj].
— Gargle [madmada / gharghara] of the decoction alleviates throat inflammation, tonsil swelling [khunāq], and hoarseness [buḥḥa].

2. INTERNAL USES:
— Drinking the decoction of leaves and roots relieves cough [su'āl], hoarseness, pleurisy [dhāt al-janb], and inflammation of the lungs [waram al-ri'a] by virtue of its emollient and moistening action on the pulmonary passages.
— Aids in voiding gravel [ḥaṣāt] and small kidney and bladder stones by lubricating the urinary tract [masālik al-bawl].
— Taken as a drink, it softens the bowels and loosens costiveness [imsāk] in patients who cannot tolerate more drastic purgatives.
— The seeds [buzūr], ground and taken in water, are especially effective for urinary retention and dysuria [iḥtibās al-bawl].
— Decoction of the root relieves intestinal cramps and tenesmus [zahr].
— Useful in cases of scorpion sting [ladghat al-aqrab] when drunk or applied as a poultice.

3. COMPOUND PREPARATIONS (cited or inferred from Canon context):
— Combined with fenugreek [ḥulba] and linseed [bazr al-kattān] for poultices against deep-seated hard swellings and scrofulous nodes [khanāzīr].
— Mixed with oil of roses [duhn al-ward] for application to hot painful joints.
— Included in compound enemas [ḥuqan murakkaba] for colitis [qūlanj] and intestinal inflammation.

---

MAZARR (Harms / Contraindications [maḍārr]):
— Excessive internal use weakens digestion [haḍm] and may generate superfluities [fuḍūl] in cold-moist temperaments [al-mizāj al-bārid al-raṭb], particularly in phlegmatic [balghamī] constitutions.
— Prolonged topical application may excessively relax tissue tone [yurkhī al-'aḍal] and impede the natural tension [tashannuj] of muscles.
— Not suitable as a primary agent for conditions dominated by excess cold and phlegm without correction.

---

ISLAAH (Corrections [iṣlāḥ]):
— Its cold-moist excess is corrected by combining with warming, drying agents such as honey [asal], ginger [zanjabīl], or aromatic seeds [buzūr muʿaṭṭara].
— When used in enemas, combining with a small quantity of salt or natron [nāṭrūn] prevents excessive relaxation of the rectal sphincter.
— In phlegmatic patients, prescribe alongside digestive carminatives [muqawwiyāt al-haḍm].

---

BADAL (Substitutes [badal]):
— Marshmallow root [Khatmi — Althaea officinalis] is the primary substitute, being warmer and more powerfully emollient; double the quantity of mallow may substitute for marshmallow in external applications.
— Violet [Banafsaj — Viola odorata] may be substituted in pulmonary and cough indications where a milder agent is preferred.
— Fenugreek [Ḥulba] for external emollient poultices.

---

DOSAGE:
— Decoction for internal use: 30–50 dirhams [approx. 90–150 ml] of leaf-and-root decoction drunk twice daily.
— Seeds [buzūr]: 2–3 mithqāls [approx. 8–12 g] ground, in warm water.
— Poultice: crushed fresh leaves applied liberally; renewed every 2–3 hours for acute inflammatory conditions.
— Enema: decoction of 10 dirhams of leaves/root in 200 ml water, administered warm.

---

CROSS-REFERENCES TO EARLIER SOURCES:
— Dioscorides (De Materia Medica, Book II, Ch. 118): Cited by Ibn Sina as 'Diyāsqūrīdus' for the emollient and drawing properties of mallow leaves and the use of its decoction against bee stings and skin roughness. Ibn Sina generally endorses Dioscorides' account while adding the urological and pulmonary indications more systematically.
— Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum, Book VII): Referenced as 'Jālīnūs' for the assignment of cold-moist temperament in the first degree and for the use of mallow in hot swellings. Ibn Sina follows Galen's theoretical framework but reorganizes the indications according to his own physiological system of organs and faculties.
— Paul of Aegina and al-Razi (Rhazes) are implicitly in the background of the Canon's mallow entry, though not named in this monograph; al-Razi's Kitab al-Hawi records similar uses that Ibn Sina may be consolidating.
