Traditional indications: alternating fever and chills, bitter taste in mouth, dry throat, nausea, chest and hypochondriac fullness. Modern applications: extensively studied in Japan for chronic hepatitis B and C — multiple large RCTs showing hepatoprotective effects, reduction of hepatitis B surface antigen levels, and reduction in liver cancer risk in cirrhotic patients. Mechanism: saikosaponins modulate NF-kB inflammatory pathway, Scutellaria baicalensis (baicalin) — antiviral and anti-inflammatory. Contraindication discovered 1994: should not be combined with interferon — severe interstitial pneumonia risk. This contraindication discovery from post-marketing surveillance in Japan is a model for traditional medicine pharmacovigilance.
segments.translation: Sho-saiko-to [Small Bupleurum Decoction; Chinese: Xiao Chai Hu Tang] — Minor Bupleurum Decoction. This is a classical Kampo [Japanese traditional medicine] compound formula, documented from the earliest period of Japanese adaptation of Chinese medical tradition. Constituent ingredients and standard dosages: Saikou [柴胡, Bupleurum root] 7 g; Hange [半夏, Pinellia rhizome] 5 g; Shokyo [生姜, fresh Ginger rhizome] 4 g; Ogon [黄芩, Scutellaria root] 3 g; Ninjin [人参, Ginseng root] 3 g; Kanzo [甘草, Licorice root] 2 g; Taiso [大棗, Jujube fruit] 3 g. Traditional indications [as recognised in classical Kampo nosology]: alternating fever and chills [a 'Shao-yang stage' [少陽病] presentation], bitter taste in the mouth, dry throat, nausea, and a sensation of fullness in the chest and hypochondriac [subcostal] regions. Modern clinical applications: the formula has been extensively investigated in Japan for the treatment of chronic hepatitis B and C. Multiple large randomised controlled trials have demonstrated hepatoprotective effects, reduction in hepatitis B surface antigen [HBsAg] levels, and a measurable reduction in the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma [liver cancer] in cirrhotic patients. Proposed pharmacological mechanisms: saikosaponins [the principal active constituents of Bupleurum] modulate the NF-κB inflammatory signalling pathway; baicalin, derived from Scutellaria baicalensis [Chinese skullcap], exerts antiviral and anti-inflammatory actions. Important contraindication identified in 1994: co-administration with interferon [α-interferon, used in antiviral therapy] is contraindicated owing to the risk of severe interstitial pneumonia. The identification of this contraindication through post-marketing surveillance [pharmacovigilance] in Japan has been cited as a paradigmatic example of safety monitoring for traditional medicine products.

segments.title_inferred: Kakkonto (Kudzu Combination Decoction): Formula Composition, Traditional Indications, and Modern Applications
segments.original_text: 葛根湯 (Kakkon-to) — Kudzu Combination Decoction. Most widely recognised Kampo formula in Japan. Components: 葛根 (Kakkon/Pueraria lobata root) 8g + 麻黄 (Mao/Ephedra) 4g + 桂枝 (Keishi/Cinnamon) 3g + 芍薬 (Shakuyaku/Peony) 3g + 大棗 (Taiso/Jujube) 4g + 生姜 (Shokyo/Ginger) 4g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Licorice) 2g. Traditional indications: early stage cold or flu with stiffness of the back of the neck and upper back, fever without sweating, aversion to cold. Documented in Ishinho (984 CE) and standard Kampo practice throughout Japanese history. Modern applications: most frequently prescribed Kampo formula for common cold. Over-the-counter availability in Japan. Active in early viral infection stage. Puerarin (from kudzu root): vasodilatory, anti-platelet effects studied for cardiovascular applications. Used in Japanese emergency medicine for early-stage fever. Multiple observational studies showing shorter duration of common cold symptoms.
segments.translation: Kakkonto [葛根湯, 'Kudzu Combination Decoction']: The most widely recognised formula [処方, shohō] in the Japanese Kampo [漢方] tradition. Composition: Kakkon [葛根, Pueraria lobata root] 8g; Mao [麻黄, Ephedra herb] 4g; Keishi [桂枝, Cinnamomum cassia twig] 3g; Shakuyaku [芍薬, Paeonia lactiflora root] 3g; Taiso [大棗, Ziziphus jujuba fruit] 4g; Shokyo [生姜, fresh Zingiber officinale rhizome] 4g; Kanzo [甘草, Glycyrrhiza uralensis root] 2g. Traditional indications [適応症]: early-stage cold or influenza presenting with rigidity of the nape of the neck and upper back, fever without perspiration, and aversion to cold [悪寒, okan]. First documented in the Ishinhō [医心方] (compiled 984 CE) and employed continuously in standard Kampo clinical practice throughout Japanese medical history. Contemporary applications: the most frequently prescribed Kampo formula for the common cold; available without prescription [over-the-counter] in Japan. Indicated during the early stage of viral infection. The constituent compound puerarin [プエラリン], derived from kudzu root [葛根], has been investigated for vasodilatory and anti-platelet properties with potential cardiovascular applications. The formula is also employed in Japanese emergency medicine settings for early-stage febrile illness. Multiple observational studies report a reduction in the duration of common cold symptoms following its administration.

segments.title_inferred: Gardenia Fruit (Sanshishi / Zhi Zi) in Honzo Wamyo: Properties, Indications, and Active Compounds
segments.original_text: 山梔子 (Sanshishi / Zhi Zi) — Gardenia jasminoides fruit. Honzo Wamyo: 山梔子、和名 久知奈之。苦、寒。主五内邪気、胃中熱気、面赤酒皰皶鼻、白癩赤癩瘡瘍。
segments.translation: Sanshishi [Mountain Gardenia Fruit; Chin. Zhi Zi], Japanese vernacular name: Kuchinashi [久知奈之]. Flavor: bitter. Thermal nature: cold. Indicated for: pathogenic qi [邪気, evil qi] in the five viscera [五内, i.e., the five organ systems]; heat qi [熱気] in the stomach; redness of the face and rosacea [酒皰皶鼻, lit. 'wine-blotch red nose,' i.e., acne rosacea]; white leprosy and red leprosy [白癩・赤癩, likely referring to distinct forms of leprous or depigmenting skin disease]; sores and ulcers [瘡瘍]. Principal use in Kampo medicine: a heat-clearing [清熱] herb for inflammatory conditions. Active constituent of pharmacological note: genipin, studied for neuroprotective effects (anti-inflammatory activity in the central nervous system), hepatoprotection, antidepressant activity (in animal models), and antidiabetic activity. Cross-traditional note: gardenia fruit is employed with essentially identical indications in both Chinese medicine and the Japanese Kampo tradition, constituting a within-East-Asian convergence signal for this drug's therapeutic profile.

segments.title_inferred: Byakujutsu (White Atractylodes): Honzo Wamyo Entry and Kampo Clinical Notes
segments.original_text: 白术 (Byakujutsu / Bai Zhu) — Atractylodes japonica (Japanese species) / A. macrocephala (Chinese species). Kampo uses Japanese species — distinct from Chinese use, representing independent clinical development. Honzo Wamyo: 白术、和名 於介良。苦、温。主風寒湿痺死肌、痙疸、止汗除熱、消食。Byakujutsu, Japanese name Okera. Bitter, warm. Main: wind-cold-damp obstruction with insensate flesh, spasms and jaundice, stops sweating, eliminates heat, promotes food digestion. The Japanese Kampo preference for A. japonica over A. macrocephala developed during the Edo period of isolation and is documented by Koho school physicians as reflecting genuinely different clinical properties. Active: atractylodin, beta-eudesmol — anti-inflammatory, gastroprotective, immune-modulating. Major digestive herb in Kampo formulas.
segments.translation:
Byakujutsu [白术; Japanese: Byakujutsu; Chinese: Bai Zhu] — species: Atractylodes japonica (Japanese) or A. macrocephala (Chinese). Kampo medicine employs the Japanese species, representing an independent clinical tradition distinct from Chinese practice.

From the Honzo Wamyo [本草和名, 'Japanese Names for Materia Medica']: Byakujutsu; Japanese vernacular name: Okera [於介良]. Flavor: bitter. Thermal nature: warm. Primary indications: obstruction [bi 痺] due to wind, cold, and dampness with insensate or necrotic flesh [死肌]; convulsions/spasms [痙]; jaundice [疸]; arrests spontaneous sweating; eliminates [pathogenic] heat; promotes digestion and dispels food stagnation.

The Japanese Kampo preference for A. japonica over A. macrocephala emerged during the Edo period of national isolation and is attested by physicians of the Koho [古方, 'Ancient Formula'] school, who regarded the two species as possessing genuinely distinct clinical properties. Known active constituents include atractylodin and beta-eudesmol, with documented anti-inflammatory, gastroprotective, and immune-modulating activity. Byakujutsu functions as a principal digestive herb in numerous Kampo compound formulas.

segments.title_inferred: Ren Shen (Ginseng): Classical Shennong Bencaojing Entry with Kampo Elaboration
segments.original_text: 人參，味甘，微寒。主補五臟，安精神，定魂魄，止驚悸，除邪氣，明目，開心益智。久服輕身延年。一名人銜，一名鬼蓋。生山谷。
segments.translation:
Ren Shen [人參, 'Man-Root']

**Chinese Name:** Rén Shēn (人參)
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Ninjin (ニンジン / 人参)
**Modern Botanical Identification:** Panax ginseng C.A.Mey. (family Araliaceae)
**Classical Category:** Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — suitable for long-term use without toxicity; nourishes life and corresponds to Heaven.

---

**Classical Text (Shennong Bencaojing, c. 200 CE):**
'Ren Shen: flavor sweet [甘], slightly cold [微寒]. It principally supplements the five viscera [五臟], quiets the essence-spirit [安精神], stabilizes the hun and po souls [定魂魄], arrests fright palpitations [止驚悸], dispels evil qi [除邪氣], brightens the eyes [明目], opens the heart [開心], and augments wisdom [益智]. Long-term ingestion lightens the body and prolongs years. Alternate names: Rén Xián [人銜, 'Man-Clasp'] and Guǐ Gài [鬼蓋, 'Ghost-Cover']. It grows in mountain valleys [生山谷].'

---

**Five Flavors [五味, Wǔ Wèi]:** Sweet [甘, gān]; some later commentators (e.g., Li Shizhen, Bencao Gangmu, 1596) add a slight bitterness [微苦, wēi kǔ].

**Four Natures [四氣, Sì Qì]:** The Shennong Bencaojing states slightly cold [微寒, wēi hán]; however, the dominant post-Tang consensus, including the Mingyi Bielu [名醫別錄] and the Kampo tradition, reclassifies it as slightly warm [微溫, wēi wēn], reflecting its qi-supplementing, yang-supporting clinical profile. Both readings are preserved in the scholarly record.

**Channel Tropism [歸經, Guī Jīng]:** Spleen [脾], Lung [肺], Heart [心], Kidney [腎] — as systematized by Jin-Yuan and later Ming-Qing physicians and adopted in Kampo formulation theory.

---

**Therapeutic Actions (Classical and Kampo Synthesis):**
1. *Powerfully supplements the original qi [大補元氣]:* The foremost herb for acute collapse of qi, profuse sweating, and devastated yang — used alone as Dú Shēn Tāng [獨參湯, 'Lone Ginseng Decoction'] in emergency presentations.
2. *Supplements the spleen and augments the lung [補脾益肺]:* Addresses fatigue, reduced appetite, loose stool, and shortness of breath due to spleen-lung qi vacuity.
3. *Generates fluids and alleviates thirst [生津止渴]:* Applied in internal heat damaging fluids, and in wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě] disorders.
4. *Quiets the spirit and augments wisdom [安神益智]:* Treats palpitations, insomnia, forgetfulness, and restless spirit [心神不安].
5. *Stabilizes the hun and po souls, dispels evil qi:* In classical cosmological terms, secures the psycho-spiritual substrates housed in the liver (hun) and lung (po), and expels pathogenic influences.
6. *Brightens the eyes, lightens the body, prolongs years:* Classical longevity [養生, yǎng shēng] and adaptogenic claims consistent with Superior Herb status.

---

**Preparation Methods (Classical and Kampo):**
- *Decoction [煎湯, jiān tāng]:* Most common; typically 3–9 g dried root, simmered separately from other herbs and the decoction added at the end to preserve volatile constituents [另煎兌入].
- *Powder [散, sǎn]:* Ground and taken with warm water.
- *Pills [丸, wán]:* Incorporated into honey pills for long-term supplementation.
- *Steeped in liquor [浸酒]:* Ginseng wine for gradual tonic effect.
- *Lone Decoction [獨參湯]:* 15–30 g simmered alone for emergency qi collapse.
- *Steamed whole root [蒸製]:* Produces red ginseng [紅參, hóng shēn], considered warmer and more strongly tonifying in both Chinese and Kampo traditions.

---

**Key Classical Formulas (Kampo and TCM):**
| Formula Name | Characters | Primary Indication |
|---|---|---|
| Sijunzi Tang | 四君子湯 | Spleen-stomach qi vacuity; fatigue, poor appetite |
| Shenling Baizhu San | 參苓白朮散 | Spleen vacuity with dampness |
| Shengmai San | 生脈散 | Qi and yin vacuity with sweating and thirst |
| Renshen Yangrong Tang | 人參養榮湯 | Qi and blood dual vacuity |
| Buzhong Yiqi Tang | 補中益氣湯 | Central qi sinking; prolapse, fatigue |
| Dushen Tang | 獨參湯 | Acute qi collapse, hemorrhagic shock |
| Ren Shen Tang (Kampo) | 人參湯 | Spleen-stomach cold vacuity (≈ Lizhong Tang 理中湯) |
| Hachimijiogan (Kampo) | 八味地黃丸 | Kidney qi and yang vacuity |

*Kampo note:* In contemporary Japanese Kampo, Ninjin (人参) most often refers to Panax ginseng root and features prominently in Rikkunshito [六君子湯], Hochuekkito [補中益氣湯], and Ninjin'yoeito [人參養榮湯], among many others. Japanese practitioners generally follow the slightly warm nature classification.

---

**Alternate Classical Names:**
- 人銜 (Rén Xián) — 'Man-Clasp'
- 鬼蓋 (Guǐ Gài) — 'Ghost-Cover'
- 神草 (Shén Cǎo) — 'Divine Herb' (later appellation)
- 地精 (Dì Jīng) — 'Earth-Essence' (later appellation)

**Habitat (Classical):** Mountain valleys [生山谷]; the Shennong Bencaojing specifies Shangdang [上黨] (modern Shanxi province) as the premier source, though Manchurian (Jilin province) and Korean origins became dominant in later centuries.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Ren Shen 人參

segments.title_inferred: Dang Gui (當歸) — Chinese Angelica Root: Blood Tonic and Menstrual Regulator
segments.original_text: 當歸，味甘辛，性溫，無毒。主咳逆上氣，溫瘧寒熱洗洗在皮膚中，婦人漏下絕子，諸惡瘡瘍金瘡，煮飲之。一名乾歸。生川谷。
segments.translation:
Dang Gui [當歸; 'returning to proper order' / 'making what is due return']. 

**Chinese Name:** Dāng Guī (當歸) 
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Tōki (当帰) 
**Modern Identification:** Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels — Chinese angelica root 
**Classical Category:** Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — herbs of moderate potency used to tonify and regulate; suitable for longer-term use with attention to dosage. 

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Sweet [甘, gān] and Acrid/Pungent [辛, xīn]; some classical sources additionally note a mild Bitter [苦, kǔ] quality in the body of the root. 

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Warm [溫, wēn]; non-toxic [無毒]. 

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Liver [肝], Heart [心], Spleen [脾]. Post-Shennong elaborations (Tang–Song dynasties onward, consolidated in Kampo practice) consistently assign primary affinity to the Liver channel, governing the Blood; secondary affinity to the Heart (which 'commands' the Blood) and Spleen (source of Blood production). 

**Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE):** 
'It governs: cough with Qi counterflow and ascending Qi [咳逆上氣]; warm malaria [溫瘧] with alternating chills and fever, with [a sensation of chills] washing over the skin [洗洗在皮膚中]; in women: uterine bleeding [漏下] and infertility / cessation of conception [絕子]; all manner of malign sores, ulcerations [諸惡瘡瘍], and metal-wound injuries [金瘡]. Administered as a decoction [煮飲之].' 
Alternate name: Gān Guī [乾歸, 'Dry Gui']. Habitat: grows in river valleys [生川谷]. 

**Subsequent Elaborations (Ben Cao Jing Ji Zhu, Tang Bencao, Bencao Gangmu; Kampo tradition):** 
Later authorities expanded and systematized the actions of Dang Gui considerably beyond the Shennong core indications: 
1. **Nourishing and Tonifying the Blood [補血, bǔ xuè]:** Principal blood tonic in Chinese and Kampo medicine; addresses Blood Deficiency [血虛] manifesting as pallor, dizziness, palpitations, and dry skin. 
2. **Invigorating and Moving the Blood [活血, huó xuè]:** Relieves Blood Stasis [血瘀]; used in dysmenorrhea, amenorrhea, and traumatic injury with pain. 
3. **Regulating Menstruation [調經, tiáo jīng]:** Core herb for all menstrual irregularities — the classical phrase 'Dang Gui is the primary herb for women's diseases' [婦科要藥] reflects centuries of practice. 
4. **Moistening the Intestines and Unblocking the Bowels [潤腸通便]:** Used in constipation due to Blood Deficiency with intestinal dryness. 
5. **Dispersing Cold and Relieving Pain [散寒止痛]:** Combined with warming herbs for cold-type abdominal pain, cold bi-syndrome [寒痹], and cold-womb infertility. 
6. **Reducing Swelling and Expelling Pus [消腫排膿]:** Addresses the Shennong indication for sores and ulcerations; used in early or unruptured abscesses. 

**Preparation:** Principally used as a decoction [湯, tāng]; also prepared as powder, wine-maceration [酒浸, jiǔ jìn — enhances Blood-moving action], honey-prepared pills, and topical poultice for skin and wound applications. The root head [歸頭] is said to arrest bleeding; the root body [歸身] to nourish Blood; the root tails [歸尾] to move Blood and disperse Stasis — a classical distinction emphasized in both Chinese and Kampo sources. 

**Key Classical Formulas:** 
- **Sì Wù Tāng [四物湯, 'Four Substances Decoction']:** The foundational Blood-tonifying formula of Chinese medicine; Dang Gui combined with Shu Di Huang [熟地黃], Bái Sháo [白芍], and Chuān Xiōng [川芎]. Kampo: Shimotsu-tō (四物湯). 
- **Dāng Guī Sháo Yào Sǎn [當歸芍藥散]:** For women's abdominal pain, edema, and Blood-fluid disharmony; canonical in Kampo gynecology as Tōki Shakuyaku San. 
- **Dāng Guī Jiān Zhōng Tāng [當歸建中湯]:** Augments the Middle Burner and nourishes Blood; post-partum deficiency. 
- **Bā Zhēn Tāng [八珍湯, 'Eight Treasure Decoction']:** Qi and Blood double tonic combining Four Substances with Four Gentleman formula. 
- **Wēn Jīng Tāng [溫經湯, 'Menses-Warming Decoction']:** Warms the Penetrating and Conception Vessels; treats cold-type menstrual disorders and infertility. Kampo: Unkei-tō (温経湯); one of the most used Kampo formulas in modern gynecology. 
- **Dāng Guī Sì Nì Tāng [當歸四逆湯]:** For cold extremities due to Blood Deficiency with cold obstructing the channels. 
- **Bǔ Zhōng Yì Qì Tāng [補中益氣湯]:** Qi-tonifying formula including Dang Gui to anchor and harmonize the Blood; Kampo: Hochuekkito (補中益氣湯), widely prescribed in modern Japan.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Dang Gui 當歸

segments.title_inferred: Chuan Xiong (川芎): Blood-Moving Herb for Headache and Gynecological Disorders
segments.original_text: 川芎 (Chuān Xiōng) — 芎藭 — 辛，溫。歸肝、膽、心包經。活血行氣，祛風止痛。主治頭痛，風寒濕痺，月經不調，經閉，痛經，癥瘕腹痛，胸脅刺痛，跌撲腫痛。
segments.translation:
Chuan Xiong (川芎; archaic name 芎藭, Xiōng Qióng): Flavor: acrid [xīn 辛]. Nature: warm [wēn 溫]. Channel Tropism [guī jīng 歸經]: Liver (足厥陰), Gallbladder (足少陽), and Pericardium (手厥陰) channels.

Classical Category: Recorded among the middle-grade herbs [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, indicating medicinal value with some caution warranted in prolonged use.

Therapeutic Actions [主治 zhǔ zhì]:
1. Invigorates the blood and moves qi [活血行氣 huó xuè xíng qì]: Treats blood stasis manifesting as irregular menstruation [月經不調], amenorrhea [經閉], dysmenorrhea [痛經], and abdominal masses [癥瘕腹痛]. Regarded as the foremost herb for gynecological blood-stasis disorders.
2. Expels wind and alleviates pain [祛風止痛 qū fēng zhǐ tòng]: Treats headache [頭痛] — particularly vertex and migraine-type headaches — and wind-cold-damp painful obstruction [風寒濕痺]. Classical texts note it 'ascends to the vertex and descends to the sea of blood,' reflecting its dual upward and downward movement.
3. Alleviates chest and hypochondriac stabbing pain [胸脅刺痛] due to qi stagnation and blood stasis.
4. Reduces swelling and pain from traumatic injury [跌撲腫痛].

Shennong Bencaojing Tradition (c.200 CE): The Bencaojing lists 芎藭 (the classical name) as treating 'central wind, entry of brain wind, headache, cold and heat [alternation], accumulations in the sinews and bones, and malign sores and scrofula-type swellings — promoting long use for lightening the body.'

Kampo (Japanese) Elaboration: Known in Japanese Kampo as センキュウ (Senkyū). Extensively employed in Kampo gynecology and neurology. Key Kampo applications include:
- Treatment of 'blood deficiency with cold' [血虛寒證] patterns, particularly in postpartum and menstrual disorders.
- Combined with Angelica sinensis [当帰, Tōki] as a foundational blood-nourishing and blood-moving pair.
- Recognized as a chief herb [君薬] or deputy herb [臣薬] in numerous classical formulas.

Key Classical Formulas [主要方剤]:
1. Sì Wù Tāng 四物湯 (Four-Substance Decoction; Kampo: Shimotsu-tō 四物湯): The primary formula for blood deficiency and blood stasis; contains Chuan Xiong, Dang Gui [当帰], Bai Shao [白芍], and Shu Di Huang [熟地黄].
2. Chuān Xiōng Chá Tiáo Sǎn 川芎茶調散 (Chuan Xiong Tea-Blended Powder): Classical formula targeting wind-cold headache, in which Chuan Xiong is the principal herb.
3. Dāng Guī Sháo Yào Sǎn 当帰芍薬散 (Toki Shakuyaku-san in Kampo): Treats gynecological blood deficiency with dampness; one of the most widely used Kampo formulas in modern clinical practice.
4. Wēn Jīng Tāng 溫經湯 (Warming the Menses Decoction; Kampo: Unkeito 温経湯): Treats cold-type menstrual irregularity and infertility.
5. Xuè Fǔ Zhú Yū Tāng 血府逐瘀湯 (Drive Out Stasis from the Mansion of Blood Decoction): Treats blood stasis in the chest and upper body.

Preparation: Typically decocted [煎服 jiān fú] at 3–10 g per dose; may also be ground into powder for external application or taken in pill form. Caution: Contraindicated in cases of yin deficiency with ascendant yang, excessive menstrual bleeding without stasis, and pregnancy (used with caution).
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Chuan Xiong 川芎

segments.title_inferred: Bai Shao (White Peony Root): Classical Materia Medica Entry
segments.original_text: 白芍 — 味苦，平。主邪氣腹痛，除血痹，破堅積，寒熱疝瘕，止痛，利小便，益氣。生川谷。
segments.translation:
Bai Shao [White Peony Root; 白芍; pinyin: bái sháo]. Japanese Kampo name: Shakuyaku (芍薬).

Classical Category: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — capable of both nourishing and treating disease; suitable for extended use with care.

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Bitter [苦, kǔ]; in later elaborations also sour [酸, suān] — the sour quality became predominant in post-Han systematization and Kampo usage.

Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Slightly cold [微寒, wēi hán]; some classical sources record it as neutral/balanced [平, píng].

Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Liver [肝, gān]; Spleen [脾, pí]; in Kampo elaboration also Lung [肺, fèi].

Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c.200 CE): 'It governs [pathogenic] evil qi [邪氣] causing abdominal pain; eliminates blood impediment [血痹, xuě bì]; breaks up stubborn accumulations [堅積, jiān jī]; [addresses] cold-heat [alternating patterns], mounting disorder [疝, shàn], and concretion-conglomeration [瘕, jiǎ]; relieves pain [止痛]; promotes urination [利小便]; and replenishes qi [益氣]. It grows in river valleys [生川谷].'

Expanded Classical and Kampo Actions: (1) Nourishes and regulates the Blood [養血調血] — used for blood deficiency patterns, pallor, dizziness, and menstrual irregularities; (2) Softens the Liver and alleviates pain [柔肝止痛, róu gān zhǐ tòng] — indicated for Liver constraint with hypochondriac, epigastric, or abdominal cramping pain; (3) Calms Liver Yang [平肝陽] — addresses ascending Liver Yang with headache or dizziness; (4) Preserves Yin and arrests sweating [斂陰止汗] — for spontaneous or night sweating due to yin deficiency.

Key Classical Formulas: (1) Gui Zhi Tang [桂枝湯] — Cinnamon Twig Decoction; paired with gui zhi to harmonize Ying and Wei; (2) Shao Yao Gan Cao Tang [芍藥甘草湯] — White Peony and Licorice Decoction; classical formula for acute spasmodic pain, especially of the legs and abdomen; (3) Si Wu Tang [四物湯] — Four Substances Decoction; foundational blood-nourishing formula; (4) Xiao Jian Zhong Tang [小建中湯] — Minor Construct the Middle Decoction; for deficiency cold of the middle jiao with abdominal pain; (5) Dang Gui Shao Yao San [當歸芍藥散] — Dang Gui and Peony Powder; Kampo formula prominent for gynecological blood-water disharmony.

Preparation: Dried and sliced root, typically decocted in water [煎服]. May be used raw [生用] to cool the blood, or dry-fried [炒用, chǎo yòng] to moderate its cold nature and enhance tonifying action. Dose in classical practice: approximately 6–30 g per decoction.

Modern Botanical Identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (family Paeoniaceae); root of cultivated white peony. Distinct in Chinese pharmacy from Chi Shao [赤芍, Red Peony Root], which emphasizes blood-moving and heat-clearing actions over nourishment.

Cautions (classical): Contraindicated with Li Lu [藜蘆, Veratrum]; one of the classical 'eighteen antagonisms' [十八反, shí bā fǎn].
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Bai Shao 白芍

segments.title_inferred: Shu Di Huang — Prepared Rehmannia Root: Blood and Yin Tonic
segments.original_text: 熟地黃 — 味甘，微溫。主填骨髓，長肌肉，生精血，補五臟內傷不足，通血脈，益氣力，利耳目。久服輕身不老。
segments.translation:
Shu Di Huang (熟地黃, Prepared Rehmannia Root). Flavor: sweet [gan 甘]. Nature: slightly warm [wei wen 微溫]. Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Heart [xin 心], Liver [gan 肝], Kidney [shen 腎].

Primary actions: Fills and replenishes the bone marrow [tian gu sui 填骨髓]; promotes growth of muscle and flesh [zhang ji rou 長肌肉]; engenders essence and blood [sheng jing xue 生精血]; supplements the five viscera [wu zang 五臟] where there is internal damage and insufficiency [nei shang bu zu 內傷不足]; frees and regulates the blood vessels [tong xue mai 通血脈]; boosts qi and physical strength [yi qi li 益氣力]; sharpens the ears and brightens the eyes [li er mu 利耳目]. Taken over a long period, it lightens the body and arrests aging [jiu fu qing shen bu lao 久服輕身不老].

Classical category: Superior herb [shang pin 上品] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition.

Preparation: Raw Rehmannia root (Sheng Di Huang 生地黃) is steamed repeatedly with rice wine [huang jiu 黃酒] and dried — traditionally nine cycles of steaming and sun-drying [jiu zheng jiu shai 九蒸九曬] — yielding the prepared, blackened, glutinous root known as Shu Di Huang. This processing transforms the nature from cold (raw form) to slightly warm, and shifts the primary action from cooling blood-heat toward nourishing and replenishing yin and blood.

Kampo name (Japanese): Juku Jio (熟地黄, じゅくじおう).

Therapeutic indications (classical and Kampo elaboration): (1) Yin deficiency [yin xu 陰虛] with deficiency-heat patterns: night sweats, tidal fever, afternoon fever, hot sensations in the palms and soles; (2) Blood deficiency [xue xu 血虛]: pallid or sallow complexion, palpitations, dizziness, insomnia; (3) Liver and Kidney yin deficiency: soreness and weakness of the lumbar region and knees, seminal emission, tinnitus, premature greying of hair; (4) Essence-blood insufficiency affecting reproduction: infertility, irregular menstruation, uterine bleeding; (5) Wasting-thirst disorder [xiao ke 消渴] associated with yin depletion.

Key classical formulas: Liu Wei Di Huang Wan (六味地黃丸, Six-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill) — the foundational Kampo and TCM formula for Liver-Kidney yin deficiency; Ba Wei Di Huang Wan (八味地黃丸, Eight-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill, also called Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan 金匱腎氣丸) — for Kidney yang deficiency built upon a yin-nourishing base; Si Wu Tang (四物湯, Four-Substance Decoction) — cardinal blood-nourishing formula; Zuo Gui Wan (左歸丸) — for profound Kidney yin and essence depletion; Da Bu Yin Wan (大補陰丸) — for yin deficiency with vigorous fire.

Cautions: Shu Di Huang is cloying [zi ni 滋膩] in nature and may impede digestion and the free flow of qi in the middle burner [zhong jiao 中焦]. Classical texts advise combining it with qi-moving or dampness-transforming herbs (e.g., Chen Pi 陳皮, Sha Ren 砂仁) when administered to patients with Spleen deficiency, poor appetite, or phlegm-damp constitutions. Contraindicated or used with caution in patterns of qi stagnation, phlegm accumulation, or diarrhea due to Spleen-Stomach weakness.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Shu Di Huang 熟地黃

segments.title_inferred: Fu Ling (Poria): Classical Materia Medica Entry — Spirit Calming, Dampness Resolving, Digestion Supporting
segments.original_text: 茯苓 — 味甘，平。主胸脅逆氣，憂恚，驚邪，恐悸，心下結痛，寒熱，煩滿，咳逆，口焦舌乾，利小便。久服安魂養神，不飢延年。
segments.translation:
Fu Ling [茯苓, Poria]. Flavor: sweet [gan 甘]. Nature: neutral/balanced [ping 平].

PRIMARY ACTIONS (Shennong Bencaojing, c.200 CE): Governs [zhu 主] counterflow qi [ni qi 逆氣] in the chest and flanks; melancholy and resentment [you hui 憂恚]; fright and pathogenic influences [jing xie 驚邪]; fear and palpitations [kong ji 恐悸]; binding pain below the heart [xin xia jie tong 心下結痛]; alternating cold and heat [han re 寒熱]; vexation and fullness [fan man 煩滿]; coughing with counterflow [ke ni 咳逆]; dry mouth and parched tongue [kou jiao she gan 口焦舌乾]. Promotes urination [li xiao bian 利小便].

LONG-TERM USE: Quiets the ethereal soul [an hun 安魂], nourishes the spirit-mind [yang shen 養神], eliminates hunger, and prolongs years [yan nian 延年].

— CLASSICAL CATEGORY: Superior herb [shang pin 上品]; non-toxic; suitable for prolonged consumption.

— FIVE FLAVORS [wu wei 五味]: Sweet [gan 甘].

— FOUR NATURES [si qi 四氣]: Neutral/balanced [ping 平]; neither warming nor cooling, making it broadly applicable across constitutions.

— CHANNEL TROPISM [gui jing 歸經] (elaborated in later Bencao and Kampo traditions): Heart [xin 心], Lung [fei 肺], Spleen [pi 脾], Kidney [shen 腎] channels. Some Kampo sources additionally note the Stomach channel.

— JAPANESE KAMPO NAME: Bukuryo [茯苓]; used identically with the Chinese tradition. The Kampo tradition (derived via Tang and Song dynasty texts, formalized in Japan from the 7th century onward) places particular emphasis on Fu Ling's role in resolving dampness [qu shi 祛濕], harmonizing the middle burner [zhong jiao 中焦], and calming the spirit [an shen 安神], consistent with its classical profile.

— MODERN IDENTIFICATION: The dried sclerotium of Wolfiporia extensa (formerly Poria cocos), a polypore fungus parasitic on pine tree roots. Primary bioactive constituents include beta-glucans (pachyman), triterpenoids (pachymic acid), and ergosterol derivatives.

— PREPARATION METHODS (classical and Kampo): Dried sclerotium sliced or powdered; decocted in water [shui jian 水煎] as the standard preparation. White Poria [bai fu ling 白茯苓] (inner white portion) is most commonly used for spirit-calming and spleen-harmonizing actions. Red Poria [chi fu ling 赤茯苓] (reddish inner layer) is preferentially used for draining dampness and promoting urination. Fu Shen [茯神] (the portion growing around a pine root) is specifically favored in both Chinese and Kampo practice for cardiac palpitations, insomnia, and spirit disturbance.

— KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS:
1. Si Jun Zi Tang [四君子湯, 'Four Gentlemen Decoction'] — flagship spleen-qi tonic; Fu Ling paired with Ren Shen, Bai Zhu, Zhi Gan Cao.
2. Wu Ling San [五苓散, 'Five-Ingredient Powder with Poria'] — for water metabolism disorders, urinary difficulty, edema; appears in Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghan Lun (c.220 CE); central Kampo formula [gohyakusan in Japanese].
3. Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wan [桂枝茯苓丸] — for blood stasis with dampness; gynecological applications; Kampo: Keishi-bukuryo-gan.
4. Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang [苓桂朮甘湯] — for phlegm-fluid [tan yin 痰飲] with palpitations and dizziness; Kampo: Ryokei-jutsu-kanto.
5. Shen Ling Bai Zhu San [參苓白朮散] — spleen-strengthening with dampness-resolving; digestive applications.
6. Zhu Ling Tang [豬苓湯] — urinary disorders with heat and yin deficiency; Kampo: Chorei-to.
7. Ban Xia Hou Po Tang [半夏厚朴湯] — for plum-pit qi [mei he qi 梅核氣], anxiety, globus sensation; Kampo: Hange-koboku-to.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Fu Ling 茯苓

segments.title_inferred: Bai Zhu (White Atractylodes): Classical Materia Medica Entry
segments.original_text: 白朮，味苦，溫。主風寒濕痹，死肌，痙，疸，止汗，除熱，消食。作煎餌，久服輕身延年不饑。一名山薊，生山谷。
segments.translation:
Bai Zhu [White Atractylodes, 白朮]

**Chinese Name:** Bái Zhú (白朮)
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Byakujutsu (ビャクジュツ / 白朮)
**Classical Category:** Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — suitable for long-term consumption; associated with nourishing life and prolonging years without toxicity.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Bitter [苦, kǔ]; in later elaborations (post-Tao Hongjing, c. 500 CE, and through the Jin-Yuan and Kampo traditions) also Sweet [甘, gān]. The primary classical flavor is bitter, with sweetness increasingly emphasized in the Kampo tradition to underscore its tonifying action on the Spleen.

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Warm [溫, wēn].

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Spleen [脾, pí] and Stomach [胃, wèi] channels, primary. Later Kampo and Ming-Qing elaborations additionally assign affinity to the Triple Burner [三焦, sān jiāo] in the context of fluid metabolism regulation.

**Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE):**
1. Governs [Wind-Cold-Damp Impediment [風寒濕痹, fēng hán shī bì]] — addresses painful obstruction of the sinews and joints caused by the invasion of wind, cold, and dampness.
2. [Dead flesh / necrotic muscle [死肌, sǐ jī]] — indicated for areas of insensate or atrophied musculature associated with chronic damp obstruction.
3. [Tetanic spasm / convulsion [痙, jìng]] — addresses spasmodic conditions, understood in the classical framework as resulting from damp-heat or damp-cold binding the sinews.
4. [Jaundice [疸, dǎn]] — specifically damp-type jaundice, where the Spleen's failure to transform dampness leads to pathological yellowing.
5. Stops sweating [止汗, zhǐ hàn] — consolidates the exterior by strengthening Spleen Qi and its command over the wei [defensive] layer; particularly indicated for spontaneous perspiration from Qi vacuity.
6. Eliminates heat [除熱, chú rè] — clears damp-heat accumulated in the Middle Jiao through the mechanism of drying dampness rather than direct heat-clearing.
7. Disperses food [消食, xiāo shí] — promotes digestion and resolves food stagnation by fortifying Spleen transportation and transformation functions.

**Preparation (Classical):** 'Prepared as a decocted cake [作煎餌, zuò jiān ěr]' — the classical text specifies preparation as a cooked medicinal food (a type of paste or baked preparation), suitable for prolonged use. Standard decoction (6–12 g in modern Kampo practice) is the predominant modern method. Dry-frying [炒白朮, chǎo bái zhú] enhances its Spleen-fortifying and dampness-drying properties; stir-frying with earth [土炒, tǔ chǎo] further emphasizes Spleen tropism and reduces any drying tendency.

**Prolonged Use:** 'Long-term consumption causes the body to feel light, prolongs years, and prevents hunger [久服輕身延年不饑].' This classical claim reflects the herb's classification as a Superior Herb and its role in nurturing the Spleen, the root of post-natal Qi production.

**Alternate Classical Name:** Shān Jì [山薊] — 'Mountain Thistle.' Habitat noted as mountain valleys [生山谷].

**Subsequent Kampo Elaborations:**
In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Byakujutsu (白朮) is regarded as the primary herb for 'fortifying the Spleen and drying dampness [健脾燥濕].' Kampo pharmacology distinguishes Byakujutsu from Sōjutsu (蒼朮, Atractylodes lancea / Cang Zhu), with Byakujutsu preferred when tonification of Spleen Qi is paramount, and Sōjutsu preferred when vigorous drying and dispersing of dampness takes precedence. This distinction, formalized in China by the Jin-Yuan physician Wang Haogu (c. 13th century CE), was adopted wholesale into Edo-period Kampo scholarship.

Additional Kampo-recognized actions include: calming the fetus [安胎, ān tāi] in cases of restless fetus due to Spleen Qi vacuity; and promoting urination [利水, lì shuǐ] to drain pathological accumulation of fluids in the Middle and Lower Jiao.

**Key Classical Formulas:**
- *Si Jun Zi Tang* [四君子湯] — foundational Spleen Qi tonic; Bai Zhu as principal dampness-drying constituent alongside Ren Shen, Fu Ling, and Zhi Gan Cao.
- *Liu Jun Zi Tang* [六君子湯] — extends Si Jun Zi Tang for Spleen vacuity with phlegm-dampness.
- *Bai Zhu San* [白朮散] — classical formula for gestational Spleen vacuity and threatened miscarriage.
- *Ling Gui Zhu Gan Tang* [苓桂朮甘湯] — Kampo formula for phlegm-rheum [痰飲] with Spleen Yang vacuity; one of the most frequently prescribed Kampo formulas in modern Japanese clinical practice.
- *Yu Ping Feng San* [玉屏風散] — exterior-consolidating formula for recurrent wind invasion due to Wei Qi vacuity; Bai Zhu reinforces Spleen's production of Wei Qi.
- *Fang Ji Huang Qi Tang* [防己黃耆湯] — for wind-damp painful impediment and edema with Qi vacuity; reflects the Bencaojing's primary indication of wind-cold-damp bi syndrome.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Bai Zhu 白朮

segments.title_inferred: Gan Cao (Licorice Root): Classical Bencaojing and Kampo Profile
segments.original_text: 甘草 — 味甘，平。主五臟六腑寒熱邪氣，堅筋骨，長肌肉，倍氣力，金瘡腫，解毒。久服輕身益年。生川谷。
segments.translation:
Gan Cao [Sweet Herb / Licorice Root]: Flavor sweet [gan], nature balanced/neutral [ping]. Governs cold and heat evil qi [xie qi] in the five viscera [wu zang] and six bowels [liu fu]; strengthens sinews [jin] and bones [gu]; promotes growth of flesh and muscle [ji rou]; doubles qi and physical strength; treats metal-wound swellings [jin chuang zhong, i.e., wounds from bladed weapons]; resolves toxins [jie du]. Prolonged consumption lightens the body [qing shen] and extends years [yi nian]. Grows in river valleys [chuan gu].

— JAPANESE KAMPO ELABORATION —
Japanese Kampo name: Kanzō (甘草, カンゾウ). Regarded as the quintessential 'harmonizing herb' [調和薬, chōwayaku] in Kampo tradition, Kanzō is incorporated into the majority of classical compound formulas precisely because it mediates and balances the actions of other constituent herbs, moderates harsh or toxic ingredients, and soothes the middle burner [chū-shō, 中焦]. Kampo physicians inherited and elaborated the Bencaojing designation of Gan Cao as a superior-class herb [jōhon, 上品], signifying low toxicity and suitability for prolonged use. Its detoxifying [gedoku, 解毒] and spasm-relieving [keiren-kanwa, 痙攣緩和] functions are especially emphasized in the Kampo clinical literature.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Gan Cao 甘草

segments.title_inferred: Da Huang (大黃) — Rhubarb Root: Classical Properties and Kampo Elaboration
segments.original_text: 大黃 — 味苦，寒。主下瘀血，血閉，寒熱，破癥瘕積聚，留飲宿食，蕩滌腸胃，推陳致新，通利水穀，調中化食，安和五臟。
segments.translation:
Da Huang [大黃, 'Great Yellow']. Flavor: bitter [ku 苦]. Nature: cold [han 寒]. Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Large Intestine, Stomach, Spleen, Heart, Liver.

Classical Category [Shennong Bencaojing tradition]: Inferior herb [xia pin 下品] — potent, not for long-term use without direction.

Japanese Kampo name: Daio (大黄).

Modern botanical identification: Rheum palmatum L., Rheum tanguticum Maxim. ex Balf., or Rheum officinale Baill. (family Polygonaceae); dried rhizome and root used medicinally.

Primary classical actions [zhu zhi 主治] per Shennong Bencaojing tradition:
1. Purges downward [xia 下] static blood [yu xue 瘀血] and resolves blood obstruction [xue bi 血閉].
2. Addresses alternating sensations of cold and heat [han re 寒熱].
3. Breaks up and disperses concretions, accumulations, and gatherings [zheng jia ji ju 癥瘕積聚] — referring to fixed and mobile abdominal masses.
4. Expels retained fluids [liu yin 留飲] and stagnant undigested food [su shi 宿食].
5. Flushes and cleanses the Stomach and Intestines [dang di chang wei 蕩滌腸胃] — the classical basis for its strong purgative action.
6. 'Expels the old and ushers in the new' [tui chen zhi xin 推陳致新] — a canonical phrase indicating renewal of physiological function through elimination of pathological accumulation.
7. Promotes the free passage of water and grains [tong li shui gu 通利水穀].
8. Harmonizes the Middle [Jiao] and transforms food [tiao zhong hua shi 調中化食].
9. Pacifies and harmonizes the Five Viscera [an he wu zang 安和五臟].

Kampo elaborations (Japanese tradition, post-Tang through Edo period):
Daio is classified as a drastic purgative [gekizai 劇剤] and is considered one of the most important herbs in Kampo for conditions of excess [jitsu 実] and heat accumulation [netsu jitsu 熱実]. Later Kampo systematization (notably by Yoshimasu Todo, 1702–1773, and the Koho-ha [古方派] school) emphasized empirical formula-based usage over theoretical elaboration. Daio is regarded as indispensable in abdominal examination [fukushin 腹診]-guided prescribing, particularly where abdominal resistance and tenderness indicate interior excess.

Key classical formulas featuring Da Huang / Daio:
- Da Cheng Qi Tang [大承氣湯] (Major Order the Qi Decoction): Da Huang + Mang Xiao + Zhi Shi + Hou Po — for yangming bowel excess with severe constipation, tidal fever, delirium.
- Xiao Cheng Qi Tang [小承氣湯] (Minor Order the Qi Decoction): Da Huang + Zhi Shi + Hou Po — milder purgation for constipation with fullness.
- Tiao Wei Cheng Qi Tang [調胃承氣湯] (Regulate the Stomach and Order the Qi Decoction): Da Huang + Mang Xiao + Zhi Gan Cao — for constipation with stomach heat, less severe.
- Da Huang Mu Dan Tang [大黃牡丹湯]: Da Huang + Mu Dan Pi + Tao Ren + Mang Xiao + Dong Gua Ren — for intestinal abscess [chang yong 腸癰], appendicitis pattern.
- Tao He Cheng Qi Tang [桃核承氣湯]: Da Huang + Tao Ren + Gui Zhi + Mang Xiao + Gan Cao — for lower jiao blood stasis with heat.
- San Huang Xie Xin Tang [三黃瀉心湯] (Kampo: Sanō Shashin-tō): Da Huang + Huang Lian + Huang Qin — for heat in the chest, epistaxis, hypertension pattern (especially prominent in Kampo use).
- Fang Feng Tong Sheng San [防風通聖散] (Kampo: Bōfū Tsūshō-san): compound formula including Da Huang for obesity, constipation, skin conditions — a signature Kampo prescription.

Preparation notes: Typically decocted [jian 煎]; added near the end of decoction (brief cooking) to preserve purgative anthraquinone glycosides; longer cooking attenuates purgative action and emphasizes blood-moving effects. Prepared Da Huang [Zhi Da Huang 製大黃], wine-processed, reduces cold nature and enhances blood-invigorating action. Raw Da Huang [Sheng Da Huang 生大黃] maximizes purgation.

Contraindications (classical): Not suitable for deficiency-cold [xu han 虛寒] constitutions, pregnancy [pregnancy risk — anthraquinones, uterotonic potential], or prolonged use without monitoring.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Da Huang 大黃

segments.title_inferred: Huang Lian (Coptis): Classical Chinese and Kampo Monograph
segments.original_text: 黃連，味苦，寒。主熱氣，目痛，眥傷，泣出，明目，腸澼，腹痛，下利，婦人陰中腫痛。久服令人不忘。一名王連。生川谷。
segments.translation:
CHINESE NAME: Huáng Lián (黃連) — 'Yellow Link [rhizome]'
JAPANESE KAMPO NAME: Ōren (黄連)
CLASSICAL CATEGORY: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — herbs of moderate potency used therapeutically with some caution regarding prolonged use.

FIVE FLAVORS [五味, wǔ wèi]: Bitter [苦, kǔ]
FOUR NATURES [四氣, sì qì]: Cold [寒, hán]
CHANNEL TROPISM [歸經, guī jīng]: Heart [心], Liver [肝], Stomach [胃], Large Intestine [大腸] — as elaborated in post-classical materia medica and Kampo tradition.

SHENNONG BENCAOJING PASSAGE (reconstructed, c. 200 CE): 'Huang Lian: flavor bitter, [nature] cold. Governs [pathological] heat qi; eye pain; injury to the inner canthi [眥傷] with flowing tears; brightens vision [明目]; intestinal flux [腸澼, cháng pì — dysenteric conditions]; abdominal pain; downward diarrhea [下利]; and swelling and pain in women's genitalia [婦人陰中腫痛]. Long-term consumption causes one not to forget [i.e., sharpens memory/cognition]. Alternate name: Wáng Lián [王連]. Grows in river valleys [川谷].'

THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (classical and Kampo elaboration):
1. CLEARS HEAT AND ELIMINATES FIRE [清熱瀉火]: Primary action against excess heat patterns, especially Heart and Stomach fire. Addresses high fever, irritability, restlessness, and insomnia driven by pathogenic heat.
2. DRIES DAMPNESS AND RESOLVES TOXICITY [燥濕解毒]: Effective against damp-heat conditions of the Stomach and Large Intestine, including dysentery [痢疾], gastroenteritis, and intestinal infections. The antimicrobial activity of berberine — the primary alkaloid — corresponds closely to this classical action.
3. COOLS THE BLOOD AND STOPS BLEEDING [涼血止血]: Used in heat-driven hemorrhagic conditions including hematemesis and epistaxis.
4. BRIGHTENS THE EYES [明目]: Classical use for inflammatory, heat-driven eye disorders (conjunctivitis, red and painful eyes), corroborated in both Bencaojing tradition and Kampo ophthalmological applications.
5. CALMS THE SPIRIT [安神]: Heart fire causing palpitations, insomnia, anxiety, and agitation — notably elaborated in Kampo formulas such as Ōren-dokusan (黄連解毒湯, Huáng Lián Jiě Dú Tāng).

KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS:
- Huáng Lián Jiě Dú Tāng [黄連解毒湯 / Kampo: Ōren-gedoku-tō]: 'Coptis Decoction to Relieve Toxicity' — treats raging fire and heat-toxin in all three burners; fever, delirium, dysentery, skin lesions.
- Huáng Lián Tāng [黃連湯]: Treats disharmony between upper heat and lower cold; epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting.
- Xiè Xīn Tāng [瀉心湯 / Kampo: Shashin-tō]: 'Drain the Epigastrium Decoction' — addresses epigastric focal distention driven by heat; also used for bleeding due to heat in the blood.
- Jiāo Tài Wán [交泰丸]: Combines Huang Lian with Ròu Guì [肉桂, cinnamon bark] in a yin-yang balancing formula for Heart-Kidney disharmony with insomnia.
- Zuǒ Jīn Wán [左金丸]: Huang Lian with Wú Zhū Yú [吳茱萸] — treats Liver fire invading the Stomach; acid reflux, flank pain, bitter taste.
- Huáng Lián Ē Jiāo Tāng [黄連阿膠湯 / Kampo: Ōren-e-kyō-tō]: Nourishes yin and clears Heart fire; insomnia, irritability in deficiency-heat patterns.

PREPARATION METHODS (classical): Primarily decocted [煎, jiān] in water as part of compound formulas. Also prepared as powder [散, sǎn], pill [丸, wán], or topical wash for eye conditions. Kampo tradition notes dry-frying [炒, chǎo] to moderate cold nature when treating Spleen-deficient patients. Ginger-processed [薑製] form used to reduce nausea-inducing properties.

MODERN IDENTIFICATION: Coptis chinensis Franch. (Chinese goldthread), family Ranunculaceae. Also C. japonica (Thunb.) Makino used preferentially in Japanese Kampo. Active constituents include berberine, coptisine, palmatine, and worenine — collectively responsible for antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, hypoglycemic, and neuroprotective activities documented in modern pharmacology.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Huang Lian 黃連

segments.title_inferred: Huang Bai (Yellow Phellodendron Bark): Classical and Kampo Monograph
segments.original_text: 黃柏，味苦，寒。主五臟腸胃中結熱，黃疸，腸痔，止泄利，女子漏下赤白，陰陽蝕瘡。久服通神。一名檀桓。生山谷。
segments.translation:
Huang Bai [Yellow Phellodendron Bark]

**Chinese Name:** Huáng Bǎi / Huáng Bò (黃柏); also written 黃蘗 (huáng bò) in older texts. Alternate classical name: Tán Huán (檀桓).

**Japanese Kampo Name:** Ōbaku (黄柏)

**Classical Category (Shennong Bencaojing):** Middle-grade herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — potent medicinal virtue with some toxicity considerations under prolonged use; capable of treating disease but requiring judicious application.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Bitter [苦, kǔ]

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Cold [寒, hán]

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Kidney [腎], Urinary Bladder [膀胱], Large Intestine [大腸]. Later elaborations in the Jin-Yuan and Ming periods added: Liver [肝] and Heart [心] channels.

**Classical Shennong Bencaojing Actions (c. 200 CE):**
Huang Bai governs the clearing of accumulated heat [結熱, jié rè] in the five viscera [五臟] and the stomach and intestines. It treats:
— Jaundice [黃疸, huáng dǎn]: heat and dampness lodged in the liver and gallbladder manifesting as yellowing of skin and eyes.
— Intestinal hemorrhoids [腸痔, cháng zhì]: heat accumulation in the large intestine.
— Diarrhea and dysentery [泄利, xiè lì]: stops leakage of fluids due to damp-heat in the lower burner.
— Abnormal uterine discharge in women [女子漏下赤白, nǚzǐ lòu xià chì bái]: red and white vaginal discharge attributed to damp-heat in the lower jiao [下焦].
— Corrosive sores of the yin and yang [陰陽蝕瘡, yīn yáng shí chuāng]: ulcerative lesions of the genitalia and surrounding regions caused by damp-heat toxin.
Prolonged use [久服] is said to bring about clarity of spirit [通神, tōng shén].

**Subsequent Elaborations (Tang, Song, Jin-Yuan, Ming Dynasties and Kampo Tradition):**
Later materia medica texts — notably the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目] of Li Shizhen (1578) and Japanese Kampo sources — significantly expanded the therapeutic profile:

1. **Draining Kidney Fire [瀉腎火]:** Huang Bai is regarded as the pre-eminent herb for clearing fire arising from Kidney yin deficiency [腎陰虛]. Symptoms include steaming bone fever [骨蒸潮熱], night sweats [盜汗], afternoon fever [午後發熱], nocturnal emissions [遺精], and spermatorrhea [滑精].

2. **Clearing Damp-Heat in the Lower Burner [清下焦濕熱]:** Treats burning, difficult, or painful urination; vaginal discharge [帶下]; lower limb atrophy or weakness [痿證, wěi zhèng] due to damp-heat pouring downward.

3. **Resolving Toxin and Treating Sores [解毒療瘡]:** Applied internally and topically for damp-heat skin conditions, eczema [濕疹], furuncles, and genital sores.

4. **Kampo (Japanese) Elaborations:** In the Kampo tradition, Ōbaku (黄柏) is frequently combined with Ōren (黄連, Coptis) and Ōgon (黄芩, Scutellaria) in the classical triad of 'three yellows' [三黄, sān huáng]. It is considered essential for damp-heat patterns descending to the lower jiao and for cooling ministerial fire [相火] in conditions of yin vacuity with false heat.

**Key Classical Formulas:**
— **Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan (知柏地黃丸):** Huang Bai + Zhi Mu + Liu Wei Di Huang Wan base; for Kidney yin deficiency with effulgent fire — canonical formula for steaming bone fever, night sweats, nocturnal emissions.
— **Er Miao San (二妙散):** Huang Bai + Cang Zhu; foundational formula for damp-heat in the lower burner, lower limb weakness, vaginal discharge, genital itching.
— **San Miao Wan (三妙丸):** Er Miao + Huai Niu Xi; adds downward-directing action for lower limb atrophy [痿] and joint swelling.
— **Si Miao Wan (四妙丸):** San Miao + Yi Yi Ren; further resolves dampness and relaxes sinews in damp-heat Bi [痹] syndrome.
— **Huang Bai Wan / Ōbaku-to variants (Kampo):** Used in various Japanese formulations for genital sores, urinary infections, and inflammatory conditions of the lower body.
— **Long Dan Xie Gan Tang (龍膽瀉肝湯):** Contains Huang Bai as a supporting herb to drain liver-gallbladder damp-heat.

**Preparation Methods:**
— Internal use: Decoction [煎服, jiān fú], typically 3–12 g dried bark per dose.
— Pill or powder form for chronic conditions (e.g., Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan).
— Topical: Ground powder mixed with sesame oil or water applied directly to damp, weeping skin lesions or genital ulcers.
— Salt-processed [鹽炒, yán chǎo]: Stir-frying with salt water is said to enhance the herb's ability to enter the Kidney channel and strengthen the downward-directing, fire-draining action — the most common classical processing method.
— Charred [黃柏炭]: Reduces cold nature slightly; used to stop bleeding in damp-heat hemorrhagic conditions.

**Habitat and Sourcing:** Grows in mountain valleys [生山谷]. The drug consists of the dried inner bark [樹皮] of Phellodendron amurense Rupr. (northern China, Japan — Amur Cork Tree) or Phellodendron chinense Schneid. (southern China). The bark is harvested in spring or early summer, the outer cork stripped, and the inner bark dried. Color is distinctly yellow due to the alkaloid berberine [小檗鹼, xiǎo bò jiǎn].

**Modern Pharmacological Correlates:** The principal active constituent, berberine, demonstrates broad-spectrum antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antidiabetic activity in contemporary research, providing some scientific basis for the classical damp-heat clearing and toxin-resolving actions.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Huang Bai 黃柏

segments.title_inferred: Huang Qin (Scutellaria Root): Classical and Kampo Monograph
segments.original_text: 黃芩 — 味苦，平。主諸熱，黃疸，腸澼，洩利，逐水，下血閉，惡瘡，疽蝕，火瘍。一名腐腸。生川谷。
segments.translation:
Huang Qin [Yellow Scutellaria Root; 黃芩]. Flavor: bitter [ku 苦]. Nature: cold [han 寒; the Shennong Bencaojing records 'ping / neutral,' but canonical post-Han commentators, including the Mingyi Bielu and all major Kampo sources, emend this to cold, which aligns with consistent clinical use]. Classical category: middle herb [zhong pin 中品].

Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Lung [fei 肺], Gallbladder [dan 膽], Stomach [wei 胃], Large Intestine [da chang 大腸], and — in extended Kampo usage — the Heart [xin 心] and Small Intestine [xiao chang 小腸].

Japanese Kampo name: Ōgon [黄芩], read in on'yomi; the dried root of Scutellaria baicalensis is the standard Kampo drug, regulated under the Japanese Pharmacopoeia.

Primary classical indications (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE): 'It governs [zhu 主] the various heat [conditions] [zhu re 諸熱]; jaundice [huang dan 黃疸]; intestinal dysentery with bloody flux [chang pi 腸澼]; diarrhea and discharge [xie li 洩利]; expelling water accumulation [zhu shui 逐水]; breaking up blood closure / amenorrhea [xia xue bi 下血閉]; malignant sores [e chuang 惡瘡]; deep-rooted abscesses and erosive ulcers [ju shi 疽蝕]; and fire-scalds / burns [huo yang 火瘍].' Alternate classical name: 'Rotten-gut herb' [fu chang 腐腸], referring to the hollow, decay-prone interior of aged roots. Habitat recorded as: mountain valleys and river gorges [sheng chuan gu 生川谷].

Five Flavors [wu wei 五味]: Bitter [ku 苦] — the sole flavor assigned in the classical text; bitterness is held to drain, dry, and descend, making Huang Qin suited to clearing damp-heat accumulations.

Four Natures [si qi 四氣]: Cold [han 寒]. Clears heat and drains fire [qing re xie huo 清熱瀉火]; resolves toxicity [jie du 解毒]; dries dampness [zao shi 燥濕]; cools the blood and stops bleeding [liang xue zhi xue 涼血止血]; and, in fetal medicine, calms restless fetus [an tai 安胎].

Therapeutic actions elaborated in Kampo tradition: (1) Clears heat from the Upper Burner [shang jiao 上焦], particularly the Lung — used for cough with yellow phlegm, chest oppression, and febrile respiratory conditions. (2) Dries dampness and clears damp-heat in the Stomach and Intestines — principal drug for dysentery, diarrhea, jaundice with a damp-heat etiology. (3) Cools blood heat to stop hemorrhage — epistaxis, hemoptysis, hematuria, metrorrhagia. (4) Clears heat toxin in sores and swellings — applied internally and occasionally topically. (5) Quiets the fetus in threatened miscarriage attributed to heat in the Chong and Ren vessels.

Key classical and Kampo formulas [fang ji 方劑]: 
• Xiao Chaihu Tang [小柴胡湯] (Minor Bupleurum Decoction) — harmonizes Shaoyang; Huang Qin clears Gallbladder heat alongside Chaihu.
• Huangqin Tang [黃芩湯] — Shanghan Lun formula for Taiyang-Shaoyang diarrhea with abdominal pain.
• Gegen Huangqin Huanglian Tang [葛根黃芩黃連湯] — clears intestinal heat; treats febrile diarrhea.
• Da Chaihu Tang [大柴胡湯] — stronger Shaoyang-Yangming formula; Kampo mainstay for biliary and gastrointestinal inflammation.
• Wen Jing Tang [溫經湯] variants and Baitouweng Tang [白頭翁湯] also incorporate Huang Qin in some traditions.
• In Kampo, Ōgon features prominently in Saikokeishito [柴胡桂枝湯] and Ōgontō [黃芩湯] for inflammatory and febrile pediatric conditions.

Preparation notes: The dried root [gen 根] is used. Raw [sheng 生] Huang Qin is preferred for clearing heat and draining fire; wine-fried [jiu chao 酒炒] to ascend to the Upper Burner and reduce cold injury to the Stomach; charred [tan 炭] to stop bleeding. Typical decoction dose in Kampo: 3–9 g dried root.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Huang Qin 黃芩

segments.title_inferred: Zhi Mu (Anemarrhena rhizome): Classical and Kampo Entry
segments.original_text: 知母 — 味苦，寒。主消渴熱中，除邪氣，肢體浮腫，下水，補不足，益氣。（神農本草經）
segments.translation:
Zhi Mu [知母, Anemarrhena asphodeloides Bunge, rhizoma]. Flavor: bitter [苦, kǔ]. Nature: cold [寒, hán]. Japanese Kampo name: Chimo (チモ / 知母). Classical category: Middle herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing hierarchy.

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Bitter (苦). Some later commentators (e.g., Li Shizhen, Bencao Gangmu, 1596) add a subsidiary sweet quality.

Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Cold (寒). Classified as markedly cold in yin-nourishing applications.

Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Enters the Lung (肺), Stomach (胃), and Kidney (腎) channels. Post-Tang elaborations also associate it with the Triple Burner (三焦) in the context of draining ministerial fire.

Primary Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE): 'Governs wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě] and internal heat [熱中, rè zhōng]; eliminates pathogenic qi [邪氣]; disperses swelling and edema of the limbs [肢體浮腫]; promotes downward movement of water; supplements insufficiency [補不足]; and augments qi [益氣].'

Kampo Elaborations: In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Chimo (知母) is principally employed to: (1) clear heat and drain fire [清熱瀉火] — particularly lung fire manifesting as high fever, irritability, and thirst; (2) nourish yin and moisten dryness [滋陰潤燥] — addressing lung and kidney yin deficiency with symptoms of dry cough, afternoon fever, night sweats, and steaming bone disorder [骨蒸, gǔ zhēng]; (3) generate fluids and relieve thirst — used in patterns of stomach heat consuming fluids, as in diabetes-adjacent wasting-thirst syndromes. The Kampo tradition particularly values its action on 'yin fire' (陰火) — deficiency heat arising from kidney yin depletion — distinguishing it from herbs that clear repletion heat.

Key Classical Formulas: (1) Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan [知柏地黃丸] — with Phellodendron (黃柏) and Rehmannia (地黃); treats kidney yin deficiency with ascending fire, night sweats, steaming bones. (2) Bai Hu Tang [白虎湯, White Tiger Decoction] — with Gypsum (石膏), Japonica rice (粳米), and Licorice (甘草); primary formula for Yang Ming stage heat with high fever, profuse sweating, intense thirst, and flooding pulse. (3) Bai Hu Jia Ren Shen Tang [白虎加人參湯] — same base with added Ginseng (人參); for qi and fluid depletion accompanying yang ming heat. (4) Er Mu San [二母散] — with Fritillaria (貝母); clears lung heat and moistens dryness for cough with scanty, difficult sputum. (5) Zhi Mu Fu Ling Wan [知母茯苓丸] — used in Kampo for lung-dryness cough patterns.

Preparation: The rhizome is harvested in spring or autumn, dried, and used raw [生知母, shēng zhī mǔ] for clearing heat and draining fire, or salt-processed [鹽知母, yán zhī mǔ — stir-fried with brine] to direct the herb's action downward to the kidney channel and enhance its yin-nourishing properties. Standard decoction dose: 6–12 g. Contraindicated in spleen-stomach deficiency cold with loose stools.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Zhi Mu 知母

segments.title_inferred: Sheng Di Huang (Raw Rehmannia Root): Classical Monograph and Kampo Elaboration
segments.original_text: 生地黃 — 味甘，寒。主折跌，絕筋，傷中，逐血痺，填骨髓，長肌肉，作湯除寒熱積聚，除痺，生者尤良。久服輕身不老。
segments.translation:
Sheng Di Huang [Raw Rehmannia Root; 生地黃]

Chinese Name (Pinyin): Shēng Dì Huáng | Characters: 生地黃
Japanese Kampo Name: Jio (生地黄, しょうじおう / じおう)
Classical Category: Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — among the highest rank, suitable for long-term use without toxicity

---

FIVE FLAVORS [五味, wǔ wèi]:
Sweet [甘, gān]; subsidiary bitterness noted in later commentaries.

FOUR NATURES [四氣, sì qì]:
Cold [寒, hán].

CHANNEL TROPISM [歸經, guī jīng]:
Heart [心], Liver [肝], and Kidney [腎] channels. Later Tang and Song elaborations (e.g., the Bencao Yanyi and Japanese Kampo lineages) further emphasize the Kidney and Liver channels as primary seats of Yin nourishment and blood cooling.

---

THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE):

1. Treats fractures, ruptures of sinews, and internal injury [主折跌、絕筋、傷中]: The raw form was held to promote the knitting of sinew and bone following trauma.
2. Expels blood impediment [逐血痺]: Drives out stagnant blood obstructing the channels and network vessels [絡脈], relieving numbness and pain.
3. Fills the bone marrow [填骨髓]: Replenishes the Kidney Essence [腎精] that engenders marrow, supporting structural vitality.
4. Builds flesh and muscle [長肌肉]: Nourishes the post-heavenly nutritive qi [營氣] and Blood, thereby generating muscle tissue.
5. Prepared as a decoction [作湯], eliminates cold-heat accumulations [除寒熱積聚] and relieves impediment conditions [除痺]: The liquid preparation was considered especially effective for clearing accumulations of pathogenic heat and cold lodged in the interior.
6. The fresh/raw form is superior [生者尤良]: The Shennong Bencaojing explicitly privileges the freshly harvested, unprocessed root over the dried form for its stronger blood-cooling and fluid-generating actions.
7. Extended use lightens the body and prevents aging [久服輕身不老]: A classical tonic claim characteristic of Superior Herbs.

---

KAMPO ELABORATIONS (Japanese tradition, Edo period and onward):

In the Kampo tradition, Jio (生地黄) is distinguished sharply from Kan Jio (乾地黄, dried rehmannia) and Juku Jio (熟地黄, processed/prepared rehmannia). Sheng Di Huang in its raw or lightly dried state is valued above all for:
- Cooling Blood heat [涼血, liáng xuè]: Canonical in febrile disease patterns where heat has penetrated the Blood level [血分], causing reckless movement of blood (hemorrhage, purpura).
- Nourishing Yin and generating fluids [滋陰生津]: Addresses Kidney and Liver Yin deficiency with symptoms of dry mouth, afternoon fever, night sweats, and emaciation.
- Stopping bleeding [止血]: Both direct hemostasis and by cooling the Blood to prevent its reckless movement.

Kampo texts such as the Honzō Kōmoku Keimō (本草綱目啓蒙, 1803, Ono Ranzan) and the Yakuchū Bencao Ganmu tradition further classify Sheng Di Huang as an herb that 'enters the Blood division and cools without drying' — distinguishing it from bitter-cold herbs that damage fluids.

---

KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS:

1. Sì Wù Tāng [四物湯, 'Four-Substance Decoction'] — foundational Blood-nourishing formula; the raw or dried form anchors the Blood-cooling action alongside Dang Gui, Bai Shao, and Chuan Xiong.
2. Liù Wèi Dì Huáng Wán [六味地黃丸, 'Six-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill'] — though this formula traditionally uses Shú Dì Huáng (processed), some variant traditions substitute Shēng Dì Huáng for stronger Yin-cooling.
3. Xī Jiǎo Dì Huáng Tāng [犀角地黃湯, 'Rhinoceros Horn and Rehmannia Decoction'] — the paradigmatic formula for Blood-level heat with hemorrhage; Shēng Dì Huáng is indispensable here for cooling Blood and stopping bleeding.
4. Qīng Yíng Tāng [清營湯, 'Clear the Nutritive Level Decoction'] — Warm Disease school formula using Shēng Dì Huáng to cool the nutritive level and protect Yin.
5. Zhú Yè Shí Gāo Tāng [竹葉石膏湯] variant uses — for febrile exhaustion of fluids.

---

PREPARATION NOTES:

- Fresh juice [鮮汁]: Pressed from the raw root; strongest cooling and hemostatic action.
- Decoction [湯劑]: Standard preparation; the Shennong Bencaojing specifically recommends decoction for accumulation and impediment conditions.
- Raw dried [生乾地黃]: Light drying preserves most of the cooling nature.
- Contraindicated with: Lai Fu Zi [萊菔子, radish seed] per classical incompatibility lists; use with caution in Spleen-deficient patients with loose stools, as the cold, moist nature may aggravate digestive weakness.

MODERN IDENTIFICATION: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) Libosch. ex Steud. (Family Orobanchaceae / formerly Scrophulariaceae). The fresh or lightly processed tuberous root. Confidence: HIGH.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Sheng Di Huang 生地黃

segments.title_inferred: Xuan Shen (Figwort Root): Classical Materia Medica Entry
segments.original_text: 玄參 — 味苦，微寒。主腹中寒熱積聚，女子產乳餘疾，補腎氣，令人目明。一名重臺。生川谷。
segments.translation:
Xuan Shen [Dark Parameter / Figwort Root]

**Chinese Name:** Xuān Shēn (玄參)
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Genjin (玄参)
**Classical Category:** Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — capable of nourishing the human constitution; may be used over extended periods with care.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Bitter [苦, kǔ]; some classical sources additionally note a faint saltiness [微鹹].
**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Slightly Cold [微寒, wēi hán].
**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Kidney [腎], Lung [肺], Stomach [胃] — as elaborated in post-Shennong commentary and codified in the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596) and subsequent Kampo systematization.

**Shennong Bencaojing Therapeutic Actions (c.200 CE):**
Governing [主]: accumulations of cold and heat within the abdomen [腹中寒熱積聚]; residual disorders following childbirth and lactation in women [女子產乳餘疾]; supplements Kidney Qi [補腎氣]; brightens the eyes [令人目明].
Alternate classical name: Chóngtái [重臺, 'layered platform']. Habitat: grows in river valleys and mountain gorges [生川谷].

**Elaborated Therapeutic Actions (Kampo and post-classical tradition):**
1. Clears Heat and cools the Blood [清熱涼血]: addresses febrile disease [溫病] at the Ying [營] and Blood [血] levels; high fever, eruptions, purpura.
2. Nourishes Yin and reduces Deficiency Fire [滋陰降火]: dry throat, oral ulcers, sore throat due to Kidney-Yin deficiency allowing Ministerial Fire to rise.
3. Resolves toxins and disperses nodules [解毒散結]: scrofula [瘰癧], goiter [癭瘤], hard swellings, sore throat with toxin accumulation [咽喉腫痛].
4. Moistens dryness: constipation due to Intestinal dryness from Yin deficiency.
5. Softens hardness [軟堅]: classical application in masses and accumulations, consistent with the Shennong indication of 寒熱積聚.

**Key Classical Formulas [方劑]:**
- *Zengye Tang* [增液湯] (Wenbing Tiaobian, 1798): Xuan Shen + Sheng Di Huang + Mai Dong — cardinal formula for Yin-deficiency constipation and fluid insufficiency.
- *Yangyin Qingfei Tang* [養陰清肺湯]: Xuan Shen featured prominently; used for diphtheria and dry, painful throat.
- *Xiao Luo Wan* [消瘰丸]: Xuan Shen + Mǔ Lì + Bèi Mǔ — for scrofula and phlegm-fire nodules.
- *Qingying Tang* [清營湯] (Ye Tianshi tradition): Xuan Shen in formulas for heat entering the Ying level.
- In Kampo (Japan), Genjin appears in *Keishi-bukuryo-gan* variants and in formulas targeting 'yin fire' [陰火] syndromes as theorized by Li Dongyuan and transmitted through the Go-san-ke school.

**Preparation Methods:**
Classically decocted raw [生用, shēng yòng] for heat-clearing and toxin-resolving actions. May be prepared with salt-frying [鹽炒] to direct action to the Kidney channel. Standard decoction dose in classical sources: 9–15g (up to 30g in severe cases). Contraindicated with [反] Black Veratrum Root [藜蘆, lí lú] per classical incompatibility rules [十八反, shíbā fǎn].
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Xuan Shen 玄參

segments.title_inferred: Mai Dong (Ophiopogon japonicus) — Classical and Kampo Monograph
segments.original_text: 麥冬 — 味甘，平。主心腹結氣，傷中傷飽，胃絡脈絕，羸瘦短氣。久服輕身，不老，不飢。生川谷及堤阪。
segments.translation:
Mai Dong [麥冬, 'wheat winter']. Flavor: sweet [gan 甘]. Nature: balanced/neutral [ping 平], tending toward mild cold [wei han 微寒] in later elaborations. Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Lung [Fei 肺], Stomach [Wei 胃], Heart [Xin 心].

Classical category: Superior herb [shang pin 上品] — suitable for prolonged use without toxicity.

Primary actions according to the Shennong Bencaojing tradition: Governs [zhu 主] binding and stagnation of qi [jie qi 結氣] in the chest and abdomen; injuries [shang 傷] to the interior caused by overeating [shang bao 傷飽]; severance or collapse of the stomach's network vessels [wei luo mai jue 胃絡脈絕]; emaciation [lei shou 羸瘦]; and shortness of breath [duan qi 短氣]. Prolonged consumption [jiu fu 久服] lightens the body [qing shen 輕身], prevents aging [bu lao 不老], and suppresses hunger [bu ji 不飢]. It grows in river valleys and along embankments and slopes [sheng chuan gu ji di ban 生川谷及堤阪].

Kampo (Japanese) elaboration: In Kampo medicine Mai Dong (Jp. Bakumondō 麦門冬) is classified as a yin-nourishing, fluid-engendering [yang yin sheng jin 養陰生津] herb of the first rank. Key actions include: (1) nourishing Lung yin and moistening dryness [run fei zhi ke 潤肺止咳] — used in patterns of dry, non-productive cough with scanty sticky sputum, often from Lung heat or Lung dryness; (2) nourishing Stomach yin and generating fluids [yang wei sheng jin 養胃生津] — indicated in febrile disease injuring stomach fluids, causing thirst, dry mouth, and constipation; (3) clearing Heart heat and calming the spirit [qing xin chu fan 清心除煩] — used in yin deficiency patterns with vexation, insomnia, and palpitations.

Key classical formulas featuring Mai Dong:
— Maimendō-tō (麦門冬湯 / Mài Mén Dōng Tāng, Jingui Yaolüe): Principal herb; treats ascending counterflow of Lung qi with cough, spitting of saliva, dyspnea. Combined with Pinellia (Ban Xia), Ginseng (Ren Shen), Licorice (Gan Cao), Jujube (Da Zao), Japonica rice (Geng Mi).
— Zengye Tang (增液湯): Nourishes yin and increases fluids in febrile disease; combined with Xuanshen (Scrophularia) and Shengdihuang (Rehmannia).
— Qingying Tang (清營湯): Clears nutritive-level heat; Mai Dong supports fluid preservation alongside Shengdihuang and Xuanshen.
— Tianwang Buxin Dan (天王補心丹): Classical formula nourishing Heart yin and calming the spirit; Mai Dong is a supporting herb.
— Shengmai San (生脈散): Tonifies qi, nourishes yin, stops sweating; Mai Dong combined with Ren Shen (Ginseng) and Wu Wei Zi (Schisandra). Widely used in Kampo for cardiac and pulmonary debility.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Mai Dong 麥冬

segments.title_inferred: Tian Dong (Heavenly Asparagus Root): Classical Yin Tonic for Lung and Kidney
segments.original_text: 天冬 — 味苦平。主諸暴風濕偏痹，強骨髓，殺三蟲，去伏尸。久服輕身益氣延年。一名顛勒。生山谷。
segments.translation:
Tian Dong [Heavenly Winter / Asparagus Root] — Flavor: bitter, with sweet undertones; Nature: greatly cold. It is classed among the Superior Herbs [shang pin 上品] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition. It governs [zhu 主]: sudden [violent] wind-damp painful obstruction [feng shi pian bi 風濕偏痹], strengthens the bone marrow [gu sui 骨髓], eliminates the three worms [san chong 三蟲], and expels hidden corpse-[toxin] [fu shi 伏尸]. Prolonged consumption lightens the body [qing shen 輕身], augments qi [yi qi 益氣], and extends years [yan nian 延年]. An alternate name is Dian Le [顛勒]. It grows in mountain valleys.

— FIVE FLAVORS [wu wei 五味]: Bitter, sweet.
— FOUR NATURES [si qi 四氣]: Greatly cold [da han 大寒].
— CHANNEL TROPISM [gui jing 歸經]: Lung [fei 肺], Kidney [shen 腎].

— CLASSICAL THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS: (1) Nourishes yin and moistens dryness [zi yin run zao 滋陰潤燥]: indicated for lung-kidney yin deficiency with vacuous heat [xu re 虛熱], dry cough, expectoration of blood-streaked sputum, and consumptive taxation [lao zhai 勞瘵]. (2) Clears lung heat and downbears fire [qing fei jiang huo 清肺降火]: addresses fire flaming upward due to yin deficiency, manifesting as sore throat, dry mouth, and afternoon tidal fever. (3) Moistens the intestines and frees the stool [run chang tong bian 潤腸通便]: used for constipation arising from fluid insufficiency or yin depletion in the elderly. (4) Strengthens bone marrow and sinews: the classical text attributes tonification of the marrow and elimination of wind-damp obstruction, reflecting its deep action on the Kidney essence [jing 精].

— JAPANESE KAMPO ELABORATION [Kanpō 漢方]: Known in Kampo as Ten-Mon-Dō [天門冬, Tenmondō]. Classified as a superior-grade yin tonic. Kampo physicians of the Edo period (notably in the Kohō-ha [古方派] tradition) employed it primarily for lung-dryness cough, night sweats, steaming bone disorder [bone-steaming taxation heat], and senile constipation. It is distinguished from Mai Dong [麥冬, Bakumondō] in that Tian Dong acts more powerfully on the Kidney and is more cold in nature, making it suited to more severe yin deficiency with substantial fire.

— KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS: (1) San Cai Feng Sui Dan [三才封髓丹] — combines Tian Dong, Di Huang [地黃], and Ren Shen [人參] to replenish the 'three treasures' of Heaven, Earth, and Humanity, tonifying yin and anchoring ministerial fire. (2) Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan [天王補心丹] — a principal formula for heart-kidney yin deficiency with palpitations, insomnia, and anxiety; Tian Dong serves as a key yin-nourishing constituent alongside Sheng Di Huang, Dan Shen, and Suan Zao Ren. (3) Yue Hua Wan [月華丸] — classical formula for lung consumption [fei lao 肺癆] and blood-streaked sputum; Tian Dong moistens and cools the lung while arresting cough. (4) Er Dong Gao [二冬膏] — a prepared syrup pairing Tian Dong and Mai Dong, used in Kampo and late imperial Chinese medicine for chronic dry cough and yin-deficiency thirst.

— PREPARATION: The fresh tuberous roots are harvested in autumn or winter, steamed or blanched to remove the outer skin, and dried. They may be used raw [sheng yong 生用] to emphasize clearing and cooling, or prepared with honey-frying [mi zhi 蜜炙] to augment their moistening and tonifying properties. Contraindicated in patterns of cold from deficiency of the Spleen and Stomach, or in diarrhea due to yang deficiency.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Tian Dong 天冬

segments.title_inferred: Gou Ji (Wolfberry / Goji Berry): Classical Chinese and Kampo Monograph
segments.original_text: 枸杞 — 味苦，寒。主五內邪氣，熱中消渴，周痹，久服堅筋骨，輕身不老，耐寒暑。生平澤及丘陵阪岸。
segments.translation:
Gou Ji [枸杞; pinyin: gǒu qǐ; Japanese Kampo: Kukoshi / 枸杞子 (Kukoshi) for the fruit, Jikoppi / 地骨皮 (Jikoppi) for the root bark]

**Classical Category:** Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — suitable for prolonged use to promote health and longevity without toxicity.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Sweet [甘, gān] (fruit / berry); Bitter [苦, kǔ] (root bark, per earliest Shennong tradition); slightly acrid in some later commentaries.

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Neutral to slightly Cool [平/微寒, píng / wēi hán]. The fruit (Gou Qi Zi) is generally considered neutral-warm; the root bark (Di Gu Pi, 地骨皮) is cold.

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Liver [肝, gān], Kidney [腎, shèn], Lung [肺, fèi].

**Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE) Core Statement:** 'Flavor: bitter; nature: cold. Governs the evil qi [pathogenic influences] of the five viscera [五內, wǔ nèi], heat in the center [熱中, rè zhōng], wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě], and generalized painful obstruction [周痹, zhōu bì]. Taken over a long period, it strengthens the sinews and bones, lightens the body, prevents aging, and confers resistance to cold and heat. It grows in level marshes and on hillside banks.'

**Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition):**
1. Nourishes Liver and Kidney yin [滋補肝腎]: Addresses deficiency patterns of the liver and kidney, including lumbar soreness, weakness of the knees, and diminished vitality.
2. Benefits essence and brightens the eyes [益精明目]: Classical primary indication for blurred vision, diminished visual acuity, and floaters arising from liver-kidney yin deficiency failing to nourish the eyes.
3. Moistens the Lung [潤肺]: Used in patterns of lung yin deficiency with dry cough or consumptive disorders.
4. Clears deficiency heat [清虛熱] (root bark / Di Gu Pi): The root cortex is specifically directed at steaming bone fever [骨蒸潮熱, gǔ zhēng cháo rè], night sweats, and childhood nutritive heat.
5. Relieves wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě]: Corresponds broadly to diabetic or hyperglycemic presentations in modern clinical correlation.
6. Expels pathogenic qi from the five viscera and governs generalized painful obstruction [周痹].

**Kampo (Japanese) Elaboration:**
In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Kukoshi [枸杞子] is classified as a tonic [補益薬] herb, primarily employed to supplement liver and kidney yin-essence [精血]. It appears in formulas addressing visual deterioration in the elderly, reproductive insufficiency, and debility following chronic illness. Jikoppi [地骨皮] (root bark) is separately employed as a heat-clearing, yin-preserving agent.

**Key Classical Formulas:**
- **Qi Ju Di Huang Wan [杞菊地黄丸]**: 'Wolfberry and Chrysanthemum Rehmannia Pill' — principal formula for liver-kidney yin deficiency with visual disturbance, dry eyes, and dizziness. Gou Qi Zi and Ju Hua [菊花] are added to the foundational Liu Wei Di Huang Wan.
- **Wu Zi Yan Zong Wan [五子衍宗丸]**: 'Five-Seed Progeny Pill' — classical male reproductive tonic featuring Gou Qi Zi alongside Tu Si Zi, Wu Wei Zi, Fu Pen Zi, and Che Qian Zi.
- **Di Gu Pi Tang [地骨皮湯]** (various): Root bark decoctions for febrile and consumptive heat disorders.

**Modern Identification:** Lycium barbarum L. (family Solanaceae) — Barbary wolfberry / Goji berry — for the fruit and shoot; Lycium chinense Mill. is the alternate species accepted in Chinese Pharmacopoeia for both fruit and root bark.

**Preparation Methods (classical):**
- Fruit (Gou Qi Zi): Dried ripe berries, taken in decoction [煎服, jiān fú], infused in medicinal wine [藥酒], or consumed directly as a food-medicine.
- Root bark (Di Gu Pi): Dried and decocted, often combined with heat-clearing and yin-nourishing herbs.
- Tender shoots and leaves: Also used as a vegetable and tonic food in classical dietetics [食療].

**Dosage (classical range):** Fruit: 6–18 g per decoction; Root bark: 6–15 g per decoction.
segments._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Gou Ji 枸杞

segments.title_inferred: Shan Zhu Yu (山茱萸) — Cornelian Cherry Fruit: Kidney and Liver Tonic, Astringent
segments.original_text: 山茱萸 (Shān Zhū Yú) — 味酸，平。主心下邪氣，寒熱，溫中，逐寒濕痹，去三蟲，久服輕身。一名蜀酸棗。生山谷。
segments.translation:
Shan Zhu Yu [Mountain Cornelian Cherry, 山茱萸]. Flavor: sour; Nature: neutral [ping, 平; later classical tradition refines this to slightly warm]. 

Primary actions according to the Shennong Bencaojing: Governs [zhǔ, 主] evil qi [xié qì] below the heart; dispels cold-heat [hán rè] alternation; warms the center [wēn zhōng]; expels cold-damp impediment [hán shī bì, 寒濕痹]; eliminates the three worms [sān chóng, 三蟲]; prolonged use lightens the body [jiǔ fú qīng shēn, 久服輕身].

Alternate classical name: Shǔ Suān Zǎo [蜀酸棗, 'Sichuan sour jujube']. Habitat: grows in mountain valleys [shān gǔ, 山谷].

--- CLASSICAL CATEGORY ---
Superior Herb [shàng pǐn, 上品]: classed among tonifying, life-prolonging substances. Non-toxic.

--- FIVE FLAVORS (Wǔ Wèi, 五味) ---
Sour [suān, 酸]: primary flavor. Sour flavor governs astriction and consolidation [shōu sè, 收澀], anchoring essence [jīng, 精] and preventing leakage.

--- FOUR NATURES (Sì Qì, 四氣) ---
Slightly warm [wēi wēn, 微溫] (as standardized in post-Bencaojing tradition, e.g., the Bencao Gangmu and Kampo scholarship, correcting the earlier 'neutral' designation). Non-toxic.

--- CHANNEL TROPISM (Guī Jīng, 歸經) ---
Enters the Kidney [shèn jīng, 腎經] and Liver [gān jīng, 肝經] channels. Some Kampo sources additionally note an affinity for the Pericardium channel in the context of essence-securing formulas.

--- THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (Shennong / Kampo synthesis) ---
1. Tonifies and supplements the Liver and Kidney [bǔ yì gān shèn, 補益肝腎]: indicated for deficiency patterns of both organs, including lumbar soreness, weak knees, dizziness, and tinnitus arising from liver-kidney yin or yang insufficiency.
2. Astringes essence and stops seminal emission [sè jīng gù tuō, 澀精固脫]: classical use for spermatorrhea [yí jīng, 遺精], premature ejaculation, and urinary frequency due to kidney vacuity failing to consolidate.
3. Stabilizes menses and stops bleeding [gù jīng zhǐ xuè, 固經止血]: for excessive uterine bleeding [bēng lòu, 崩漏] and irregular menstruation from kidney-liver deficiency.
4. Restrains sweating and stems desertion [liǎn hàn gù tuō, 斂汗固脫]: used in critical patterns of spontaneous sweating [zì hàn] or night sweats [dào hàn], and in collapse syndromes. This is the action flagged in the source label ('sweating').
5. Warms the center and expels cold-damp impediment [wēn zhōng qū hán shī bì]: archaic Bencaojing indication, less emphasized in mature Kampo but retained in cold-pattern bi-syndrome contexts.
6. Eliminates the three worms [qū sān chóng]: archaic antiparasitic indication from the Shennong corpus; rarely foregrounded in Kampo clinical use.

--- KAMPO (Japanese) ELABORATION ---
Japanese Kampo name: Sanshuyu [サンシュユ / 山茱萸]. Regarded as a critical kidney-essence astringent in Japanese Kampo tradition, particularly via the formulas Rokumigan [六味丸, Liù Wèi Wán] and Hachimijiogan [八味地黄丸, Bā Wèi Dì Huáng Wán], where Sanshuyu provides the consolidating, essence-retaining counterweight to the formula's yin-supplementing and yang-warming actions. Kampo texts emphasize its dual capacity to nourish liver blood while astringing kidney essence, making it indispensable in age-related deficiency disorders.

--- KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS ---
