In 2002, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration reclassified cascara sagrada preparations as prescription-only, citing insufficient long-term safety data. The case is considered one of the most direct and well-documented transfers from indigenous traditional medicine to formal pharmaceutical development in North American medical history, in which recorded traditional use led unambiguously to pharmaceutical adoption and subsequent identification of the active pharmacological mechanism.

segments.title_inferred: Lobelia inflata: Multi-Nation Traditional Use for Asthma and Respiratory Conditions
segments.original_text: Lobelia inflata — Indian Tobacco / Pukeweed. Cherokee: root tea for asthma and whooping cough. Iroquois: root for respiratory complaints. Penobscot: smoked for asthma. Densmore (1928): Chippewa use of Lobelia smoke for chest conditions. Active: lobeline (piperidine alkaloid) — acts on nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, producing bronchodilation and expectorant effects at therapeutic doses, emesis at higher doses. Used as smoking cessation aid in early 20th century before nicotine replacement therapy. Modern research: lobeline and analogs actively studied for ADHD and drug dependence (methamphetamine antagonism via dopaminergic reward pathway). Three independent nations identified same plant for asthma — strong internal convergence.
segments.translation: Lobelia inflata — commonly known as Indian Tobacco or Pukeweed. Cherokee usage: a tea prepared from the root was employed for asthma and whooping cough. Iroquois usage: the root was used for respiratory complaints. Penobscot usage: the plant was smoked as a treatment for asthma. Densmore (1928) records Chippewa [Ojibwe] use of Lobelia smoke for chest conditions. The principal active constituent is lobeline, a piperidine alkaloid that acts upon nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, producing bronchodilation [widening of the airways] and expectorant [mucus-clearing] effects at therapeutic doses, and inducing emesis [vomiting] at higher doses. The plant was employed as a smoking cessation aid in the early twentieth century, prior to the development of nicotine replacement therapy. Contemporary research: lobeline and its chemical analogs are under active investigation for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and drug dependence, particularly for antagonism [blocking] of methamphetamine via the dopaminergic reward pathway. Three independent Indigenous nations independently identified the same plant as a treatment for asthma, constituting strong internal convergence of traditional knowledge.

segments.title_inferred: Usnea Lichen as Antimicrobial Wound Treatment in Pacific Northwest Indigenous Traditions
segments.original_text: Usnea species — Old Man's Beard Lichen. Moerman database. Cowlitz, Okanagan-Colville, Nlaka'pamux, Blackfoot: Usnea applied to infected wounds. Consistent documentation across geographically separated Pacific Northwest nations for the same application. Active: usnic acid (dibenzofuran) — broad-spectrum antimicrobial against gram-positive bacteria including Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Currently under investigation for drug-resistant tuberculosis. Also antiprotozoal (Leishmania), anti-inflammatory, antiviral. Indigenous use pharmacologically precise — usnic acid targets gram-positive bacteria, the most common wound pathogens.
segments.translation: Usnea species — commonly known as Old Man's Beard Lichen, documented in the Moerman ethnobotanical database. Among the Cowlitz, Okanagan-Colville, Nlaka'pamux, and Blackfoot peoples, Usnea was applied directly to infected wounds. There is consistent documentation of this same application across geographically separated Pacific Northwest nations. The pharmacologically active compound is usnic acid (a dibenzofuran derivative), which exhibits broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against gram-positive bacteria, including Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Usnic acid is currently under investigation as a potential treatment for drug-resistant tuberculosis. It also demonstrates antiprotozoal activity (against Leishmania), anti-inflammatory properties, and antiviral activity. The indigenous application is pharmacologically precise: usnic acid specifically targets gram-positive bacteria, which are the most common pathogens in wound infections.

segments.title_inferred: American Ginseng: Ethnobotanical Uses and Cross-Cultural Pharmacological Convergence
segments.original_text: Panax quinquefolius — American Ginseng. Cherokee: tonic, aphrodisiac, headache. Iroquois: fever, digestive, female disorders. Ojibwe: tonic for weak and elderly. Meskwaki: menstrual cramps. Active: ginsenosides (different profile from Asian P. ginseng — higher Rb1 vs Rg1 in Asian). Multiple trials: blood sugar regulation (Type 2 diabetes — positive); cognitive function (modest positive); immune modulation. Convergence: Panax ginseng documented in Chinese and Kampo traditions; Panax quinquefolius documented independently by multiple North American nations — same genus independently identified on opposite sides of the Pacific with similar medicinal properties. One of the clearest examples of convergent pharmacological discovery across civilisations.
segments.translation: Panax quinquefolius — American Ginseng. Cherokee uses: general tonic [restorative], aphrodisiac, and treatment of headache. Iroquois uses: fever reduction, digestive complaints, and female [gynecological] disorders. Ojibwe uses: tonic for the weak and elderly. Meskwaki uses: menstrual cramps. Active constituents: ginsenosides [steroidal saponins], with a distinct phytochemical profile compared to Asian P. ginseng — American ginseng exhibits a higher ratio of ginsenoside Rb1 relative to Rg1, which predominates in the Asian species. Multiple clinical trials have demonstrated: positive results for blood sugar regulation in Type 2 diabetes; modest positive results for cognitive function; and immune modulation. Convergence note: Panax ginseng has been independently documented within Chinese and Kampo [Japanese traditional medicine] traditions, while Panax quinquefolius has been independently documented by multiple North American indigenous nations — representing the same genus identified on opposite sides of the Pacific Ocean, with similar medicinal properties attributed to each. This constitutes one of the clearest known examples of convergent pharmacological discovery across distinct civilisations.

segments.title_inferred: Sweat Lodge Thermal Medicine in North American Indigenous Traditions
segments.original_text: Sweat Lodge Medicine — documented across Plains, Southwest, Northwest Coast, Eastern Woodlands, Subarctic traditions. Lakota: inipi. Medical applications: fever management; respiratory conditions (steam with medicinal plants); skin diseases; joint pain; mental health clearing. Plants added to steam (Densmore 1928): cedar (antimicrobial), sweetgrass (anxiolytic aroma), sage/Artemisia (antimicrobial). Modern research: hyperthermia activates heat-shock proteins with immune-modulating effects; sauna therapy validated for cardiovascular health; steam inhalation with plant aromatics documented for respiratory benefit. The sweat lodge as a systematic thermal medical institution documented independently across multiple North American cultural groups represents the most sophisticated pre-Columbian thermal medicine technology in North America.
segments.translation: Sweat Lodge Medicine — attested across the Plains, Southwest, Northwest Coast, Eastern Woodlands, and Subarctic cultural traditions. Known in Lakota as inipi. Medical applications include: management of fever; treatment of respiratory conditions by means of steam infused with medicinal plants; skin diseases; joint pain; and mental or emotional restoration. Plants introduced into the steam (per Densmore 1928): cedar [antimicrobial properties], sweetgrass [aromatic, anxiolytic], and sage/Artemisia [antimicrobial]. Contemporary research indicates that hyperthermia activates heat-shock proteins with immune-modulating effects; sauna therapy has been validated for cardiovascular health; and steam inhalation incorporating plant aromatics has been documented to confer respiratory benefit. The sweat lodge, as a systematic institution of thermal medicine independently attested across multiple North American cultural groups, represents the most sophisticated pre-Columbian thermal medical technology in North America.

segments.title_inferred: Medicinal Applications of Tobacco Among Indigenous Peoples of North America
segments.original_text: Nicotiana rustica / N. tabacum — Tobacco (medicinal applications distinct from ritual). Iroquois, Cherokee: leaves as wound poultice. Crow: smoke blown into painful ear. Blackfoot: leaves for insect bites and bee stings. Zuni: topical anesthetic before minor procedures (documented by Stevenson, 1909). Pharmacology of medicinal use: nicotine as insecticide (insect bites); topical nicotine blocks pain signaling at acetylcholine receptors (surgical anesthetic mechanism); cotinine (metabolite) anti-inflammatory. The Zuni topical anesthetic application represents a sophisticated empirical pharmacological discovery — nicotine blocks nAChR at neuromuscular junctions, producing localised insensitivity. Indigenous medicine carefully distinguished between medicinal and ritual tobacco use.
segments.translation: Nicotiana rustica / N. tabacum — Tobacco (medicinal applications, treated here as distinct from ritual use). Iroquois and Cherokee peoples applied the leaves as a poultice to wounds. The Crow people blew smoke into a painful ear [as a therapeutic intervention]. The Blackfoot applied leaves to insect bites and bee stings. The Zuni used tobacco as a topical anesthetic prior to minor procedures (as documented by Stevenson, 1909). Regarding the pharmacology of these medicinal uses: nicotine functions as an insecticide in the context of insect bites; applied topically, nicotine inhibits pain signaling at acetylcholine [nicotinic acetylcholine] receptors, which accounts for its mechanism as a surgical anesthetic; and cotinine, a metabolite of nicotine, possesses anti-inflammatory properties. The Zuni practice of topical anesthesia represents a sophisticated empirical pharmacological discovery, as nicotine blocks nicotinic acetylcholine receptors [nAChR] at neuromuscular junctions, producing localized insensitivity [anesthesia]. Indigenous medical practice carefully distinguished between the medicinal and the ritual uses of tobacco.

segments.title_inferred: Moerman's database — 4,029 uses across 291 nations
segments.original_text: [Moerman, D.E. 1998. Native American Ethnobotany. Timber Press.]
segments.translation: Daniel Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany database documents 4,029 medicinal plant uses across 291 indigenous North American nations — the most comprehensive documented indigenous pharmacopoeia in the world. Key statistical finding: plants used by more than five independent nations show significantly higher rates of confirmed pharmacological activity in modern research (Moerman, 1996) — validating the archive's convergence methodology. The most widely documented plants across the most nations: yarrow (46 nations), elderberry (27 nations), wild bergamot (21 nations), sage (19 nations), and echinacea (13 nations). These represent the highest-convergence indigenous North American plants.

segments.title_inferred: Yarrow — 46 nations, the most widely used North American plant
segments.original_text: [Achillea millefolium — documented across 46 independent nations by Moerman]
segments.translation: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) is documented by 46 independent North American nations — more than any other plant in the Moerman database. Applications span: wound healing and hemostasis (most common), fever reduction, respiratory infections, digestive complaints, and ceremonial use. The Navajo, Ojibwe, Cherokee, Cheyenne, and Blackfoot nations — geographically separated by thousands of miles — all independently documented yarrow for wound bleeding. This represents the archive's single highest documented convergence for any one plant: 46 independent North American nations plus Celtic/Norse (Bald's Leechbook) plus Greek/Roman (the genus named for Achilles) — potentially 48+ independent traditions for one therapeutic application.
segments.preparation: Fresh leaves packed into wounds to stop bleeding; decoction for fever; steam inhalation for respiratory
segments.condition: Wound bleeding (hemostasis), fever, respiratory infections, digestive complaints

segments.title_inferred: Echinacea — 13 nations, the Plains immune plant
segments.original_text: [Echinacea angustifolia / E. purpurea — documented by Moerman across 13 Great Plains nations]
segments.translation: Echinacea (Echinacea angustifolia primarily) is documented across 13 Great Plains nations including the Comanche, Cheyenne, Dakota, Kiowa, and Omaha. Traditional applications: toothache, sore throat, snake bite, infectious conditions, and as a general strengthener. The Comanche used it for toothache; the Cheyenne for sore throat; the Dakota for infections. The shared immune-strengthening application across independent nations led to the development of one of the world's best-selling herbal supplements. This entry adds specificity to the existing archive entry (MMA-025) with Moerman's documentation.
segments.preparation: Root chewed or decocted for toothache; root tea for infections; root applied topically for snake bite
segments.condition: Immune support, toothache, sore throat, infections, snake bite

segments.title_inferred: Wild Bergamot (Monarda) — 21 nations
segments.original_text: [Monarda fistulosa — documented across 21 nations by Moerman]
segments.translation: Wild bergamot (Monarda fistulosa) is documented across 21 North American nations including Blackfoot, Cheyenne, Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe, and Potawatomi. Applications: respiratory infections, fever, digestive complaints, and topical antiseptic. The Blackfoot used it for respiratory infections; the Iroquois for fever; multiple nations used it as a wound antiseptic. Modern research: thymol and carvacrol — the same compounds that validate oregano and thyme in the Mediterranean and Andean traditions — are the primary active compounds. This is the North American parallel to Mediterranean oregano, an independent pharmacological discovery of the same thymol/carvacrol antimicrobial class.
segments.preparation: Fresh or dried leaves decocted for internal use; leaves applied topically for antiseptic; steam inhalation
segments.condition: Respiratory infections, fever, digestive complaints, wound antiseptic

segments.title_inferred: Sage (Salvia / Artemisia) — 19 nations, the ceremonial-medical plant
segments.original_text: [Salvia species and Artemisia species — documented across 19+ nations]
segments.translation: Multiple sage species (Salvia and Artemisia) are documented across 19+ nations for both medicinal and ceremonial purposes — the distinction between them being culturally variable. White sage (Salvia apiana) is used for purification ceremonies across Southwest nations; common sage (Salvia officinalis) for digestive and respiratory conditions across Eastern nations; sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) across Great Basin nations for fever and respiratory. The medicinal applications — antimicrobial, digestive, respiratory — are consistent across all sage species and connect to Mediterranean sage (Salvia officinalis) in the Greek/Roman tradition for the same applications.

segments.title_inferred: Goldenseal — berberine in the Eastern Woodlands
segments.original_text: [Hydrastis canadensis — Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe documentation in Moerman]
segments.translation: Goldenseal (Hydrastis canadensis) is documented by Cherokee, Iroquois, and Ojibwe nations for: eye infections, mouth conditions, digestive complaints, and as a general antimicrobial. The Cherokee specifically used goldenseal root tea for eye inflammation — the same application as Coptis/goldthread (MMA-068) documented in North American and Tibetan traditions. Both plants share berberine as the primary active compound — the same compound now studied for Type 2 diabetes (comparable to metformin in RCTs). This entry extends the berberine convergence to include goldenseal as a distinct North American species alongside Coptis trifolia, creating a documented five-species, five-tradition berberine convergence.
segments.preparation: Root tea for eye infections (eyewash); root decoction for internal conditions; root powder applied topically
segments.condition: Eye infections, mouth conditions, digestive complaints, skin infections

segments.title_inferred: Sweetgrass — the ceremonial medicine of the Plains
segments.original_text: [Hierochloe odorata — Blackfoot, Ojibwe, Lakota documentation]
segments.translation: Sweetgrass (Hierochloe odorata) — documented across Plains and Eastern Woodland nations for both ceremonial and medicinal use. The Blackfoot used it for colds and coughs; the Ojibwe for ceremonial smudging and respiratory conditions; the Lakota for wound healing. Sweetgrass smoke was documented as an antimicrobial fumigation — consistent with the documented antimicrobial activity of coumarin (the compound responsible for sweetgrass's distinctive vanilla-hay scent). This connects to the archive's documentation of Tonka bean (coumarin source) in South American traditions — an independent discovery of the same antimicrobial compound from a different plant.

segments.title_inferred: Willow bark — pre-aspirin pain medicine across North America
segments.original_text: [Salix species — documented across multiple nations for pain and fever]
segments.translation: Willow bark (Salix species) is documented across multiple North American nations for pain and fever — the same application documented in Egyptian, Greek/Roman, and Celtic/Norse traditions. The Ojibwe used willow bark tea for headache and fever; the Iroquois for joint pain; the Cherokee for fever. This multi-continental convergence for willow — pain and fever across Egyptian, Greek/Roman, Celtic, and North American independent traditions — is validated by salicin/salicylic acid pharmacology, and represents one of the most geographically extensive convergences in the archive. Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) was synthesized from salicylic acid in 1897.
segments.preparation: Inner bark decocted in water for tea; bark chewed directly
segments.condition: Headache, fever, joint pain, general pain — validated by salicin mechanism

segments.title_inferred: Introductory and editorial matter for the Bundahishn (Creation / Knowledge from the Zand)
segments.original_text: The Bundahishn ("Creation"), or Knowledge from the Zand. This electronic edition prepared by Joseph H. Peterson, 1995. Last updated: Feb 24, 2022. Translated by E. W. West, from Sacred Books of the East, volume 5, Oxford University Press, 1897. NOTE: A new translation by Domenico Agostini and Samuel Thrope is now available from Oxford University Press. NOTE: Also available in PDF version and ePub version. Concerning this Pahlavi text, Mary Boyce has written: "A much more important and fundamental work of compilation is the Bundahishn ('Creation'), also called Zand-agahih ('Knowledge from the Zand'), which survives in two recensions, the Great (or Iranian) Bundahishn and a shortened version, the Indian Bundahishn (deriving from a different MS. tradition). One of the two great Zoroastrian compilations, this work probably grew through different redactions, from some time after the Arab conquest down to 1178 A.C. (when a few additions were made in imperfect Middle Persian). The last important redaction belongs to about the end of the 9th century. The Bundahishn has three main themes: creation, the nature of earthly creatures, and the Kayanians (their lineage and abodes, and the vicissitudes befalling their realm of Eranshahr). The compiler does not name individual sources, but shows an encyclopaedic knowledge of the Zand, and exemplifies excellently the process whereby treatises on chosen themes were created out of the scriptures. Many passages evidently derive fairly closely from the Middle Persian translation, for an Avestan syntax underlies them and one section consists simply of the translation of the 1st chapter of the Vendidad coinciding (except in small details) with the canonical Zand. Glosses and commentaries provide part of the continuous text, and in these, foreign learning is adduced. There are also a few isolated attempts to bring the work up to date, by the identification of traditional (and even mythical) geographical names with Arabic ones. In the main, however, the absorbing interest of the Bd. lies in the antiquity of its material. Here is preserved an ancient, in part pre-Zoroastrian picture of the world, conceived as saucer-shaped, with its rim one great mountain-range, a central peak thrusting up, star-encircled, to cut off the light of the sun by night; a world girdled by two great rivers, from which all other waters flow; in which yearly the gods fight against demons to end drought and famine, and to bring protection to man. Natural phenomena..."
segments.translation: The Bundahishn ("Creation"), or Knowledge from the Zand. This electronic edition was prepared by Joseph H. Peterson in 1995 and last updated on February 24, 2022. Translated by E. W. West, from Sacred Books of the East, volume 5, Oxford University Press, 1897. Note: A new translation by Domenico Agostini and Samuel Thrope is now available from Oxford University Press. Note: Also available in PDF and ePub versions. Regarding this Pahlavi text, Mary Boyce has written: "A much more important and fundamental work of compilation is the Bundahishn ('Creation'), also called Zand-agahih ('Knowledge from the Zand'), which survives in two recensions: the Great (or Iranian) Bundahishn and a shortened version, the Indian Bundahishn (deriving from a different manuscript tradition). One of the two great Zoroastrian compilations, this work probably grew through different redactions from some time after the Arab conquest down to 1178 CE (when a few additions were made in imperfect Middle Persian). The last important redaction belongs to approximately the end of the 9th century. The Bundahishn has three main themes: creation, the nature of earthly creatures, and the Kayanians [the legendary Iranian royal dynasty] (their lineage and abodes, and the vicissitudes befalling their realm of Eranshahr). The compiler does not name individual sources, but displays encyclopaedic knowledge of the Zand [the Middle Persian commentary on the Avesta] and exemplifies excellently the process by which treatises on chosen themes were created out of the scriptures. Many passages evidently derive fairly closely from the Middle Persian translation, for an Avestan syntax underlies them, and one section consists simply of the translation of the 1st chapter of the Vendidad [Zoroastrian legal/religious text], coinciding (except in small details) with the canonical Zand. Glosses and commentaries provide part of the continuous text, and in these, foreign learning is adduced. There are also a few isolated attempts to bring the work up to date by identifying traditional (and even mythical) geographical names with Arabic ones. In the main, however, the absorbing interest of the Bundahishn lies in the antiquity of its material. Here is preserved an ancient, in part pre-Zoroastrian, picture of the world conceived as saucer-shaped, with its rim forming one great mountain-range and a central peak thrusting up, star-encircled, to cut off the light of the sun by night; a world girdled by two great rivers from which all other waters flow; a world in which yearly the gods fight against demons to end drought and famine and to bring protection to mankind. Natural phenomena [text continues]..."

segments.title_inferred: Bundahishn Chapter 35: Creation, the Opposition of Spirits, and the Origin of Evil
segments.original_text: 0. In the name of the creator Ohrmazd. 1. The Zand-akas ('Zand-knowing or tradition-informed'), which is first about Ohrmazd's original creation and the antagonism of the evil spirit, and afterwards about the nature of the creatures from the original creation till the end, which is the future existence (tanu-i pasino). 2. As revealed by the religion of the Mazdayasnians, so it is declared that Ohrmazd is supreme in omniscience and goodness, and unrivaled in splendor; the region of light is the place of Ohrmazd, which they call 'endless light,' and the omniscience and goodness of the unrivaled Ohrmazd is what they call 'revelation.' 3. Revelation is the explanation of both spirits together; one is he who is independent of unlimited time, because Ohrmazd and the region, religion, and time of Ohrmazd were and are and ever will be; while Ahriman in darkness, with backward understanding and desire for destruction, was in the abyss, and it is he who will not be; and the place of that destruction, and also of that darkness, is what they call the 'endlessly dark.' 4. And between them was empty space, that is, what they call 'air,' in which is now their meeting. 5. Both are limited and unlimited spirits, for the supreme is that which they call endless light, and the abyss that which is endlessly dark, so that between them is a void, and one is not connected with the other; and, again, both spirits are limited as to their own selves. 6. And, secondly, on account of the omniscience of Ohrmazd, both things are in the creation of Ohrmazd, the finite and the infinite; for this they know is that which is in the covenant of both spirits. 7. And, again, the complete sovereignty of the creatures of Ohrmazd is in the future existence, and that also is unlimited for ever and everlasting, and the creatures of Ahriman will perish at the time when the future existence occurs, and that also is eternity. 8. Ohrmazd, through omniscience, knew that Ahriman exists, and whatever he schemes he infuses with malice and greediness till the end; and because He accomplishes the end by many means, He also produced spiritually the creatures which were necessary for those means, and they remained three thousand years in a spiritual state, so that they were unthinking and unmoving, with intangible bodies. 9. The evil spirit, on account of backward knowledge, was not aware of the existence of Ohrmazd; and, afterwards, he arose from the abyss, and came in unto the light which he saw. 10. Desirous of destroying, and because of his malicious nature, he rushed in to destroy that light of Ohrmazd unassailed by fiends, and he saw its bravery and glory were greater than his own; so he fled back to the gloomy darkness, and formed many demons and fiends; and the creatures of the destroyer arose for violence. 11. Ohrmazd, by whom the creatures of the evil spirit were seen, creatures terrible, corrupt, and bad, also considered them not commendable (burzishnik). 12. Afterwards, the evil spirit [text ends mid-sentence].
segments.translation: 0. In the name of the creator Ohrmazd. 1. The Zand-akas [literally 'Zand-knowing,' or 'informed by tradition'], which treats first of Ohrmazd's original creation and the antagonism of the evil spirit, and afterwards of the nature of creatures from the original creation until the end, which is the future existence [Pahlavi: tanu-i pasino, i.e., the final bodily resurrection]. 2. As revealed by the religion of the Mazdayasnians [Zoroastrians], it is declared that Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda] is supreme in omniscience and goodness, and unrivaled in splendor; the region of light is the place of Ohrmazd, which they call 'endless light,' and the omniscience and goodness of the unrivaled Ohrmazd is what they call 'revelation.' 3. Revelation is the explanation of both spirits together; one is he who is independent of unlimited time, because Ohrmazd and the region, religion, and time of Ohrmazd were and are and ever will be; while Ahriman [Angra Mainyu, the evil spirit] in darkness, with backward understanding and desire for destruction, dwelt in the abyss, and it is he who will not be; and the place of that destruction, and also of that darkness, is what they call the 'endlessly dark.' 4. And between them was empty space, that is, what they call 'air,' in which their meeting now takes place. 5. Both are at once limited and unlimited spirits: the supreme realm is that which they call endless light, and the abyss is that which is endlessly dark, so that between them is a void, and one is not connected with the other; and again, both spirits are limited with respect to their own selves. 6. And secondly, on account of the omniscience of Ohrmazd, both things exist within the creation of Ohrmazd — the finite and the infinite — for this, they know, is what is contained in the covenant between both spirits. 7. And again, the complete sovereignty over the creatures of Ohrmazd belongs to the future existence, which also is unlimited, for ever and everlasting; and the creatures of Ahriman will perish at the time when the future existence comes to pass, and that too is eternity. 8. Ohrmazd, through omniscience, knew that Ahriman exists, and that whatever Ahriman schemes he infuses with malice and greediness until the end; and because Ohrmazd accomplishes that end by many means, He also produced spiritually the creatures that were necessary for those means, and they remained in a spiritual state for three thousand years, so that they were unthinking and unmoving, with intangible bodies. 9. The evil spirit, on account of his backward knowledge, was not aware of the existence of Ohrmazd; but afterwards he arose from the abyss and came toward the light which he perceived. 10. Desirous of destroying, and on account of his malicious nature, he rushed in to destroy the light of Ohrmazd — that light unassailed by fiends — but when he saw that its bravery and glory were greater than his own, he fled back into the gloomy darkness and formed many demons and fiends; and thus the creatures of the destroyer arose for the purpose of violence. 11. Ohrmazd, who perceived the creatures of the evil spirit — creatures terrible, corrupt, and bad — considered them not commendable [Pahlavi: burzishnik, i.e., not praiseworthy or not to be exalted]. 12. Afterwards, the evil spirit [text breaks off mid-sentence].

segments.title_inferred: The Rush of Ahriman (the Destroyer) upon Creation: The Bundahishn Account
segments.original_text: 1. On the rush of the destroyer at the creatures it is said, in revelation, that the evil spirit, when he saw the impotence of himself and the confederate (ham-dast) demons, owing to the righteous man, became confounded, and seemed in confusion three thousand years. 2. During that confusion the arch-fiends of the demons severally shouted thus: 'Rise up, thou father of us! for we will cause a conflict in the world, the distress and injury from which will become those of Ohrmazd and the archangels.' 3. Severally they twice recounted their own evil deeds, and it pleased him not; and that wicked evil spirit, through fear of the righteous man, was not able to lift up his head until the wicked Jeh came, at the completion of the three thousand years. 4. And she shouted to the evil spirit thus: 'Rise up, thou father of us! for I will cause that conflict in the world wherefrom the distress and injury of Ohrmazd and the archangels will arise.' 5. And she twice recounted severally her own evil deeds, and it pleased him not; and that wicked evil spirit rose not from that confusion, through fear of the righteous man. 6. And, again, the wicked Jeh shouted thus: 'Rise up, thou father of us! for in that conflict I will shed thus much vexation on the righteous man and the laboring ox that, through my deeds, life will not be wanted, and I will destroy their living souls (nismo); I will vex the water, I will vex the plants, I will vex the fire of Ohrmazd, I will make the whole creation of Ohrmazd vexed.' 7. And she so recounted those evil deeds a second time, that the evil spirit was delighted and started up from that confusion; and he kissed Jeh upon the head, and the pollution which they call menstruation became apparent in Jeh. 8. He shouted to Jeh thus: 'What is thy wish? so that I may give it thee.' And Jeh shouted to the evil spirit thus: 'A man is the wish, so give it to me.' 9. The form of the evil spirit was a log-like lizard's (vazak) body, and he appeared a young man of fifteen years to Jeh, and that brought the thoughts of Jeh to him. 10. Afterwards, the evil spirit, with the confederate demons, went towards the luminaries, and he saw the sky; and he led them up, fraught with malicious intentions. 11. He stood upon one-third of the inside of the sky, and he sprang, like a snake, out of the sky down to the earth. 12. In the month Frawardin and the day Ohrmazd he rushed in at noon, and thereby the sky was as shattered and frightened by him, as a sheep by a wolf. 13. He came on to the water which was arranged below the earth, and then the middle of this earth was pierced and entered by him. 14. Afterwards, he came to the vegetation, then to the ox, then to Gayomard, and then he came to fire; so, just like a fly, he rushed out upon the whole creation; and he made the world quite as injured and dark at midday as though it were in dark night. 15. And noxious creatures were diffused by him over the earth, biting and venomous, such as the snake, scorpion, frog [text breaks off].
segments.translation: 1. Concerning the assault of the Destroyer [Ahriman] upon the creatures, it is stated in revelation that the Evil Spirit, perceiving his own powerlessness and that of his allied [ham-dast] demons in the face of the righteous man, fell into bewilderment and remained in a state of confusion for three thousand years. 2. During that confusion the chief demons each cried out in turn: 'Arise, thou our father! For we shall bring strife into the world, and the suffering and harm resulting therefrom shall fall upon Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda] and the archangels [Amesha Spentas].' 3. Each of them recounted his own evil deeds twice over, but this did not please him; and that wicked Evil Spirit, out of fear of the righteous man, was unable to raise his head until the wicked Jeh came, at the end of the three thousand years. 4. And she cried out to the Evil Spirit thus: 'Arise, thou our father! For I shall bring about that strife in the world from which the suffering and harm of Ohrmazd and the archangels shall arise.' 5. And she likewise recounted her own evil deeds twice, each in turn, but this did not please him; and that wicked Evil Spirit did not rise from his confusion, out of fear of the righteous man. 6. And once more the wicked Jeh cried out: 'Arise, thou our father! For in that strife I shall inflict such vexation upon the righteous man and the laboring ox that, through my deeds, life itself shall not be desired, and I shall destroy their living souls [nismo]; I shall vex the water, I shall vex the plants, I shall vex the fire of Ohrmazd, I shall make the whole creation of Ohrmazd vexed.' 7. And she recounted those evil deeds a second time, in such a manner that the Evil Spirit was gladdened and sprang up from his confusion; and he kissed Jeh upon the head, and the defilement which is called menstruation became manifest in Jeh. 8. He cried out to Jeh: 'What is thy desire? that I may grant it thee.' And Jeh cried out to the Evil Spirit: 'A man is my desire — give him to me.' 9. The form of the Evil Spirit was that of a thick, log-like lizard's [vazak] body, yet he appeared to Jeh as a young man of fifteen years, and this turned Jeh's thoughts toward him. 10. Thereafter the Evil Spirit, together with his allied demons, advanced toward the luminaries and beheld the sky; and he led them upward, filled with malicious intent. 11. He stationed himself upon one-third of the inner expanse of the sky, and then sprang, like a snake, out of the sky down to the earth. 12. In the month of Frawardin and on the day of Ohrmazd he broke in at noon, and thereby the sky was as shattered and terrified by him as a sheep is by a wolf. 13. He came upon the water that lay arranged beneath the earth, and then pierced and entered into the very midst of the earth. 14. Thereafter he came to the vegetation, then to the ox, then to Gayomard [the primordial man], and then to fire; and, just like a fly, he rushed out upon the whole of creation; and he made the world as injured and dark at midday as though it were the darkest night. 15. And noxious creatures were spread by him across the earth, biting and venomous — such as the snake, the scorpion, the frog [text breaks off].

segments.title_inferred: The Soul of the Primeval Ox (Goshorun) and the Promise of Zarathustra
segments.original_text: 1. This also is said, that when the primeval ox passed away it fell to the right hand, and Gayomard afterwards, when he passed away, to the left hand. 2. Goshorun, as the soul of the primeval ox came out from the body of the ox, stood up before the ox and cried to Ohrmazd, as much as a thousand men when they sustain a cry at one time, thus: 'With whom is the guardianship of the creatures left by thee, when ruin has broken into the earth and vegetation is withered, and water is troubled? Where is the man of whom it was said by thee thus: I will produce him, so that he may preach carefulness?' 3. And Ohrmazd spoke thus: 'You are made ill, O Goshorun! you have the illness which the evil spirit brought on; if it were proper to produce that man in this earth at this time, the evil spirit would not have been oppressive in it.' 4. Forth Goshorun walked to the star station (payak) and cried in the same manner, and forth to the moon station and cried in the same manner, and forth to the sun station, and then the guardian spirit of Zartosht was exhibited to her, and Ohrmazd said thus: 'I will produce for the world him who will preach carefulness.' 5. Contented became the spirit Goshorun, and assented thus: 'I will nourish the creatures;' that is, she became again consenting to a worldly creation in the world.
segments.translation: 1. It is also related that when the primeval ox died, it fell to the right side, and Gayomard afterwards, when he died, fell to the left side. 2. Goshorun — being the soul of the primeval ox — came forth from the body of the ox, stood before it, and cried out to Ohrmazd with a voice as loud as that of a thousand men crying out together, saying: 'To whom have you entrusted the guardianship of your creatures, now that destruction has broken into the earth, vegetation has withered, and the waters are fouled? Where is the man of whom you said: I shall bring him forth so that he may teach righteousness [carefulness]?' 3. And Ohrmazd replied: 'You have been made ill, O Goshorun! You suffer the sickness that the evil spirit has inflicted; were it fitting to bring that man into this world at this time, the evil spirit would not have been able to oppress it.' 4. Then Goshorun went forth to the station [payak] of the stars and cried out in the same manner, and then went forth to the station of the moon and cried out in the same manner, and then went forth to the station of the sun — and there the guardian spirit [fravashi] of Zarathustra was revealed to her, and Ohrmazd declared: 'I shall bring forth for the world the one who will teach righteousness [carefulness].' 5. The spirit Goshorun was satisfied and consented, saying: 'I will nourish the creatures' — meaning that she once again agreed to the continuance of worldly creation in the world.

segments.title_inferred: The Evil Spirit's Assault on the Earth and the Formation of Mountains
segments.original_text: 0. On the conflict which the evil spirit waged with the earth. 1. As the evil spirit rushed in, the earth shook, and the substance of mountains was created in the earth. 2. First, Mount Alburz arose; afterwards, the other ranges of mountains (kofaniha) of the middle of the earth; for as Alburz grew forth all the mountains remained in motion, for they have all grown forth from the root of Alburz. 3. At that time they came up from the earth, like a tree which has grown up to the clouds and its root to the bottom; and their root passed on that way from one to the other, and they are arranged in mutual connection. 4. Afterwards, about that wonderful shaking out from the earth, they say that a great mountain is the knot of lands; and the passage for the waters within the mountains is the root which is below the mountains; they forsake the upper parts so that they may flow into it, just as the roots of trees pass into the earth; a counterpart (anguni-aitak) of the blood in the arteries of men, which gives strength to the whole body. 5. In numbers, apart from Alburz, all the mountains grew up out of the earth in eighteen years, from which arises the perfection of men's advantage.
segments.translation: 0. Concerning the conflict which the Evil Spirit waged against the earth. 1. When the Evil Spirit rushed in, the earth trembled, and the substance of mountains was brought into being within the earth. 2. Mount Alburz arose first; thereafter, the other mountain ranges [kofaniha] of the middle of the earth followed; for as Alburz grew upward, all the other mountains were set in motion, since they have all grown forth from the root of Alburz. 3. At that time they rose up from the earth like a tree that has grown into the clouds while its root reaches to the very depths; and their roots extended in this manner from one range to the next, so that they are arranged in mutual interconnection. 4. Subsequently, concerning that remarkable upheaval from the earth, it is said that a great mountain is the knot or nexus of the lands; and the passage for waters running through the mountains is the root lying beneath them — the waters leave the upper parts so as to flow down into these channels, just as the roots of trees extend into the earth — a counterpart [anguni-aitak] of the blood in the arteries of men, which imparts strength to the whole body. 5. In total, apart from Alburz itself, all the mountains grew up out of the earth over the course of eighteen years, and from this arises the fullness of benefit to mankind.

segments.title_inferred: On the Nature and Origin of Plants, Disease-Countering Herbs, and the Gokard Tree
segments.original_text: [On the nature of plants] 1. The conflict waged with plants was that when they became quite dry. 2. Amerodad the archangel, as the vegetation was his own, pounded the plants small, and mixed them up with the water which Tishtar seized, and Tishtar made that water rain down upon the whole earth. 3. On the whole earth plants grew up like hair upon the heads of men. 4. Ten thousand of them grew forth of one special description, for keeping away the ten thousand species of disease which the evil spirit produced for the creatures; and from those ten thousand, the 100,000 species of plants have grown forth. 5. From that same germ of plants the tree of all germs was given forth, and grew up in the wide-formed ocean, from which the germs of all species of plants ever increased. 6. And near to that tree of all germs, the Gokard [=Gokaren] tree was produced, for keeping away deformed (dushpad) decrepitude; and the full perfection of the world arose therefrom.
segments.translation: [On the nature of plants] 1. The conflict waged against plants was that they had become entirely dry. 2. Ameretat [Amerodad], the archangel [Amesha Spenta] who held dominion over vegetation, ground the plants into a fine powder and mixed them with the waters that the star-deity Tishtrya [Tishtar] had gathered; and Tishtrya caused those waters to rain down upon the whole earth. 3. Across the entire earth, plants then grew up like hair upon the heads of men. 4. Ten thousand of them grew forth of a single particular kind [or class], for the purpose of warding off the ten thousand species of disease that the evil spirit [Ahriman] had inflicted upon living creatures; and from those ten thousand species, the one hundred thousand varieties of plants subsequently grew forth. 5. From that same primordial seed [germ] of plants, the Tree of All Seeds was brought forth and grew up in the wide-formed ocean [Vourukasha], from which the seeds of all species of plants have ever since been multiplied. 6. And near to that Tree of All Seeds, the Gokard [Gokaren] tree was produced, for the purpose of warding off 'dushpad' [malformed or deformed] decrepitude [old age and physical deterioration]; and the full perfection of the world arose from it.

segments.title_inferred: Origin of Plants, Medicinal Herbs, and Animals from the Primeval Ox
segments.original_text: 0. On the conflict waged with the primeval ox. 1. As it passed away, owing to the vegetable principle (chiharak) proceeding from every limb of the ox, fifty and five species of grain and twelve species of medicinal plants grew forth from the earth, and their splendour and strength were the seminal energy (tokhmih) of the ox. 2. Delivered to the moon station, that seed was thoroughly purified by the light of the moon, fully prepared in every way, and produced life in a body. 3. Thence arose two oxen, one male and one female; and, afterwards, two hundred and eighty-two species of each kind became manifest upon the earth. 4. The dwelling (manist) of the birds is in the air, and the fish are in the midst of the water.
segments.translation: 0. Concerning the conflict waged against the primeval ox. 1. As it perished, by virtue of the vegetable principle [chiharak] emanating from every limb of the ox, fifty-five species of grain and twelve species of medicinal plants grew forth from the earth; their splendour and vital force derived from the seminal energy [tokhmih] of the ox. 2. Conveyed to the lunar station [moon station], that seed was thoroughly purified by the light of the moon, fully prepared in every respect, and brought forth life within a body. 3. From thence arose two oxen, one male and one female; and thereafter, two hundred and eighty-two species of each kind appeared upon the earth. 4. The dwelling [manist] of the birds is in the air, and the fish dwell in the midst of the water.

segments.title_inferred: The Seven Regions (Keshvars) of the Earth and the Primacy of Khvaniras
segments.original_text: 1. On the nature of the earth it says in revelation, that there are thirty and three kinds of land. 2. On the day when Tishtar produced the rain, when its seas arose therefrom, the whole place, half taken up by water, was converted into seven portions; this portion, as much as one-half, is the middle, and six portions are around; those six portions are together as much as Khvaniras. 3. The name keshvar ('zone or region') is also applied to them, and they existed side by side (kash kash); as on the east side of this portion (Khvaniras) is the Savah region, on the west is the Arzah region; the two portions on the south side are the Fradadafsh and Vidadafsh regions, the two portions on the north side are the Vorubarsht and Vorujarsht regions, and that in the middle is Khvaniras. 4. And Khvaniras has the sea, for one part of the wide-formed ocean wound about around it; and from Vorubarsht and Vorujarsht a lofty mountain grew up; so that it is not possible for any one to go from region to region. 5. And of these seven regions every benefit was created most in Khvaniras, and the evil spirit also produced most for Khvaniras, on account of the superiority (sarih) which he saw in it. 6. For the Kayanians and heroes were created in Khvaniras; and the good religion of the Mazdayasnians was created in Khvaniras, and afterwards conveyed to the other regions; Soshyans is born in Khvaniras, who makes the evil spirit impotent, and causes the resurrection and future existence.
segments.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of the earth, it is stated in revelation that there are thirty-three kinds of land. 2. On the day when Tishtar [the star Sirius, associated with rain] produced the rain, when seas arose from it, the entire expanse—half of which was taken up by water—was divided into seven portions. This central portion amounts to one half, and the six portions surrounding it together equal Khvaniras [the central, inhabited zone]. 3. These are also called keshvar [literally 'zone' or 'region'], and they lay side by side (kash kash). On the east side of Khvaniras lies the Savah region; on the west, the Arzah region; the two portions to the south are the Fradadafsh and Vidadafsh regions; the two portions to the north are the Vorubarsht and Vorujarsht regions; and the central one is Khvaniras. 4. Khvaniras is bounded by the sea, for one arm of the vast primordial ocean winds around it; and between Vorubarsht and Vorujarsht a great mountain rose up, making it impossible for anyone to travel from one region to another. 5. Of these seven regions, every good thing was created in greatest abundance in Khvaniras; yet the evil spirit also directed his greatest efforts against Khvaniras, because of the preeminence (sarih) he perceived in it. 6. For the Kayanian [legendary Iranian] kings and heroes were created in Khvaniras; the good religion of the Mazdayasnians [Zoroastrians] was established in Khvaniras and thereafter transmitted to the other regions. Soshyans [the eschatological savior] will be born in Khvaniras—he who will render the evil spirit powerless and bring about the resurrection and the future existence [i.e., the final renovation of the world].

segments.title_inferred: On the Nature and Origin of Mountains (Bundahishn, Chapter 46)
segments.original_text: 1. On the nature of mountains it says in revelation, that, at first, the mountains have grown forth in eighteen years; and Alburz ever grew till the completion of eight hundred years; two hundred years up to the star station (payak), two hundred years to the moon station, two hundred years to the sun station, and two hundred years to the endless light. 2. While the other mountains have grown out of Alburz, in number 2244 mountains, and are Hugar the lofty, Terak of Alburz, Chakad-i Daitik, and the Arezur ridge, the Ausindom mountain, Mount Aparsen which they say is the mountain of Pars, Mount Zarid also which is Mount Manush, Mount Airach, Mount Kaf, Mount Vadges, Mount Aushdashtar, Mount Arezur-bum, Mount Royishn-homand, Mount Padashkhvargar which is the greatest in Khvarih, the mountain which they call Chino, Mount Revand, Mount Darspet the Bakyir mountain, Mount Kabed-shikaft, Mount Siyak-muimand, Mount Vafar-homand, Mount Spendyad and Kondrasp, Mount Asnavand and Kondras, Mount Sichidav, a mountain among those which are in Kangdez, of which they say that they are a comfort and delight of the good creator, the smaller hills. 3. I will mention them also a second time; Alburz is around this earth and is connected with the sky. 4. The Terak of Alburz is that through which the stars, moon, and sun pass in, and through it they come back. 5. Hugar the lofty is that from which the water of Aredvivsur leaps down the height of a thousand men. 6. The Ausindom mountain is that which, being of ruby (khun-ahino), of the substance of the sky, is in the midst of the wide-formed ocean, so that its water, which is from Hugar, pours down into it (the ocean). 7. Chakad-i-Daitik ('the judicial peak') is that of the middle of the world, the height of a hundred men, on which the Cinwad bridge stands; and they take account of the soul at that place. 8. The Arezur ridge [of the Alburz mountain] is a summit at the gate of hell, where they always hold the concourse of the demons. 9. This also is said, that, excepting Alburz, the Aparsen mountain is the greatest; the Aparsen mountain they call the mountain of Pars, and its beginning is in Sagastan and its end in Khujistan. 10. Mount Manush is great; the mountain on which Manushchihar was born. 11. The remaining mountains have chiefly grown from those; as it is said that the elevation (afsarih) of the districts had arisen most around those three mountains. 12. Mount Airach is in the middle from Hamadan to Khvarizem, and has grown from Mount Aparsen. 13. Mount Chino, which is on its east, on the frontier of Turkistan, is connected also with Aparsen. 14. Mount Kaf has grown from the same Mount Aparsen. 15. Mount Aushdashtar is in Sagastan. 16. Mount Arezur is that which is in the direction of Arum. 17. The Padashkhvargar mountain is that which is in Taparistan and the side of Gilan. 18. The Revand mountain is in Khurasan, on which the Burzin fire was established; and its name Revand means this, that it is glorious.
segments.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of mountains, it is said in revelation [i.e., in sacred Zoroastrian scripture] that, at the beginning, the mountains grew forth over eighteen years; and Alburz continued to grow until the completion of eight hundred years — two hundred years up to the station of the stars [payak], two hundred years to the station of the moon, two hundred years to the station of the sun, and two hundred years to the endless light [i.e., the highest heaven]. 2. From Alburz all other mountains have grown — 2,244 mountains in number — among them: Hugar the Lofty, the Terak of Alburz, Chakad-i Daitik [the Judicial Peak], the Arezur ridge, the Ausindom mountain, Mount Aparsen (which they call the mountain of Pars), Mount Zarid (which is also Mount Manush), Mount Airach, Mount Kaf, Mount Vadges, Mount Aushdashtar, Mount Arezur-bum, Mount Royishn-homand, Mount Padashkhvargar (the greatest in Khvarih), the mountain called Chino, Mount Revand, Mount Darspet (the Bakyir mountain), Mount Kabed-shikaft, Mount Siyak-muimand, Mount Vafar-homand, Mount Spendyad and Kondrasp, Mount Asnavand and Kondras, Mount Sichidav, and a mountain among those in Kangdez — of which they say these are a comfort and delight of the Good Creator — and the smaller hills. 3. I shall mention them also a second time: Alburz encircles this earth and is connected with the sky. 4. The Terak of Alburz is that through which the stars, the moon, and the sun pass inward, and through it they return. 5. Hugar the Lofty is that from which the water of Aredvivsur [the mythical cosmic river, Avestan: Ardvi Sura Anahita] leaps down from a height equal to a thousand men. 6. The Ausindom mountain is made of ruby [khun-ahino], being of the substance of the sky, and stands in the midst of the wide-formed ocean, so that the water coming from Hugar pours down into the ocean. 7. Chakad-i Daitik ('the Judicial Peak') stands at the middle of the world, at a height of a hundred men, and upon it stands the Cinwad bridge [the bridge of judgment]; it is there that the account of the soul is reckoned. 8. The Arezur ridge [of the Alburz mountain] is a summit at the gate of hell, where the concourse of demons is always held. 9. It is also said that, apart from Alburz, the Aparsen mountain is the greatest; the Aparsen mountain they call the mountain of Pars, and its beginning is in Sagastan and its end in Khujistan [Khuzistan]. 10. Mount Manush is great; it is the mountain upon which Manushchihar was born. 11. The remaining mountains have chiefly grown from those; for it is said that the high elevations [afsarih] of the districts arose mostly around those three mountains. 12. Mount Airach lies in the middle [of the territory] from Hamadan to Khvarizem [Khwarezm], and has grown from Mount Aparsen. 13. Mount Chino, which lies to its east on the frontier of Turkistan, is also connected with Aparsen. 14. Mount Kaf has grown from that same Mount Aparsen. 15. Mount Aushdashtar is in Sagastan. 16. Mount Arezur is that which lies in the direction of Arum [i.e., Rome/Byzantium]. 17. The Padashkhvargar mountain is that which lies in Taparistan [Tabaristan] and on the side of Gilan. 18. The Revand mountain is in Khurasan, upon which the Burzin fire [one of the three sacred Zoroastrian fires] was established; and its name 'Revand' signifies that it is glorious.

segments.title_inferred: On the Five Classes of Animals and the Plants Arising from the Primeval Ox (Bundahishn, Chapter 48)
segments.original_text: 1. On the nature of the five classes of animals (gospend) it says in revelation, that, when the primeval ox passed away, there where the marrow came out grain grew up of fifty and five species, and twelve species of medicinal plants grew; as it says, that out of the marrow is every separate creature, every single thing whose lodgment is in the marrow. 2. From the horns arose peas (mijuk), from the nose the leek, from the blood the grapevine from which they make wine — on this account wine abounds with blood — from the lungs the rue-like herbs, from the middle of the heart thyme for keeping away stench, and every one of the others as revealed in the Avesta. 3. The seed of the ox was carried up to the moon station; there it was thoroughly purified, and produced the manifold species of animals. 4. First, two oxen, one male and one female, and, afterwards, one pair of every single species was let go into the earth, and was discernible in Eranvej for a Hasar ('mile'), which is like a Parasang ('league'); as it says, that, on account of the value of the ox, it was created twice, one time as an ox, and one time as the manifold species of animals. 5. A thousand days and nights they were without eating, and first water and afterwards herbage (aurvar) were devoured by them. 6. And, afterwards, the three classes (kardak) of animals were produced therefrom, as it says that first were the goat and sheep, and then the camel and swine, and then the horse and ass. 7. For, first, those suitable for grazing were created therefrom, those are now kept in the valley (lai); the second created were those of the hill summits (sar-i dez), which are wide-travelers, and habits (nihadak) are not taught to them by hand; the third created were those dwelling in the water. 8. As for the genera (khadunak), the first genus is that which has the foot cloven in two, and is suitable for grazing; of which a camel larger than a horse is small and new-born. 9. The second genus is ass-footed, of which the swift horse is the largest, and the ass the least. 10. The third genus is that of the five-dividing paw, of which the dog is the largest, and the civet-cat the least. 11. The fourth genus is the flying, of which the griffin of three natures is the largest, and the chaffinch the least. 12. The fifth genus is that of the water, of which the Kar fish is the largest, and the Nemadu the least. 13. These five genera are apportioned out into two hundred and eighty-two species (sardak). 14. First are five species of goat, the ass-goat, the milch-goat, the mountain-goat, the fawn, and the common goat. 15. Second, five species of sheep, that with a tail, that which has no tail, the dog-sheep, the wether, and the Kurishk sheep, a sheep whose horn is great; it possesses a grandeur like unto a horse, and they use it mostly for a steed (bara), as it is said that Manuschihar kept a Kurishk as a steed. 16. Third, two species of camel, the mountain one and that suitable for grazing; for one is fit [text ends]
segments.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of the five classes of animals [gospend], it is stated in revelation that when the primeval ox died, wherever its marrow came forth, fifty-five species of grain grew up, and twelve species of medicinal plants grew; for it is said that out of the marrow comes every distinct creature, every single thing whose origin lies in the marrow. 2. From the horns arose peas [mijuk], from the nose the leek, from the blood the grapevine from which wine is made — for this reason wine is rich in blood — from the lungs grew rue-like herbs, from the center of the heart grew thyme for warding off foul odor, and all the rest as revealed in the Avesta. 3. The seed of the ox was borne up to the station of the moon; there it was thoroughly purified and gave rise to the manifold species of animals. 4. First, two oxen, one male and one female, and thereafter one pair of every single species was released upon the earth, and could be perceived in Eranvej [the primordial land] within a Hasar [a 'mile'], which is comparable to a Parasang [a 'league']; for it is said that, on account of the worth of the ox, it was created twice: once as an ox, and once as the manifold species of animals. 5. For a thousand days and nights they went without eating, consuming first water and then herbage [aurvar]. 6. Thereafter, the three classes [kardak] of animals were produced from them: first the goat and the sheep, then the camel and the swine, and then the horse and the ass. 7. For first were created those suitable for grazing, which are now kept in the valleys [lai]; second were created those of the hill summits [sar-i dez], which are wide-ranging travelers and whose habits [nihadak] cannot be taught by the hand of man; third were created those dwelling in the water. 8. As for the genera [khadunak]: the first genus is that which has a foot cloven in two and is suitable for grazing, of which even a camel that is larger than a horse is considered small and newborn. 9. The second genus is the ass-footed kind, of which the swift horse is the largest member and the ass the smallest. 10. The third genus is that of the five-divided paw, of which the dog is the largest and the civet-cat the smallest. 11. The fourth genus is the flying kind, of which the griffin of three natures is the largest and the chaffinch the smallest. 12. The fifth genus is that of the water, of which the Kar fish is the largest and the Nemadu the smallest. 13. These five genera are divided into two hundred and eighty-two species [sardak]. 14. First are five species of goat: the ass-goat, the milch-goat, the mountain-goat, the fawn, and the common goat. 15. Second are five species of sheep: that with a tail, that which has no tail, the dog-sheep, the wether, and the Kurishk sheep, a sheep whose horn is great; it possesses a stature comparable to a horse, and is used chiefly as a riding animal [bara], as it is said that Manuschihar kept a Kurishk as a mount. 16. Third are two species of camel: the mountain camel and that suitable for grazing; for one is fit [text ends]

segments.title_inferred: The Origin of Humanity: Gayomard's Seed, Mashye and Mashyane, and the First Corruption
segments.original_text: 1. On the nature of men it says in revelation, that Gayomard, in passing away, gave forth seed; that seed was thoroughly purified by the motion of the light of the sun, and Neryosang kept charge of two portions, and Spandarmad received one portion. 2. And in forty years, with the shape of a one-stemmed Rivas-plant, and the fifteen years of its fifteen leaves, Matro [Mashye] and Matroyao [Mashyane] grew up from the earth in such a manner that their arms rested, behind on their shoulders (dosh), and one joined to the other they were connected together and both alike. 3. And the waists of both of them were brought close and so connected together that it was not clear which is the male and which the female, and which is the one whose living soul (nismo) of Ohrmazd is not away. 4. As it is said thus: Which is created before, the soul (nismo) or the body? And Ohrmazd said that the soul is created before, and the body after, for him who was created; it is given into the body that it may produce activity, and the body is created only for activity; hence the conclusion is this, that the soul (ruban) is created before and the body after. 5. And both of them changed from the shape of a plant into the shape of man, and the breath (nismo) went spiritually into them, which is the soul (ruban); and now, moreover, in that similitude a tree had grown up whose fruit was the ten varieties of man. 6. Ohrmazd spoke to Mashye and Mashyane thus: 'You are man, you are the ancestry of the world, and you are created perfect in devotion by me; perform devotedly the duty of the law, think good thoughts, speak good words, do good deeds, and worship no demons!' 7. Both of them first thought this, that one of them should please the other, as he is a man for him; and the first deed done by them was this, when they went out they washed themselves thoroughly; and the first words spoken by them were these, that Ohrmazd created the water and earth, plants and animals, the stars, moon, and sun, and all prosperity whose origin and effect are from the manifestation of righteousness. 8. And, afterwards, antagonism rushed into their minds, and their minds were thoroughly corrupted, and they exclaimed that the evil spirit created the water and earth, plants and animals, and the other things as aforesaid. 9. That false speech was spoken through the will of the demons, and the evil spirit possessed himself of this first enjoyment from them; through that false speech they both became wicked, and their souls are in hell until the future existence. 10. And they had gone thirty days without food, covered with clothing of herbage (giyah); and after the thirty days they went forth into the wilderness, came to a white-haired goat, and milked the milk from the udder with their mouths. 11. When they had devoured the milk Mashye said to Mashyane thus: 'My delight was owing to it when I had not devoured the milk, and my delight is more delightful now when it is devoured by my vile body.' 12. That [text ends]
segments.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of men, it is stated in revelation that Gayomard, at the moment of his death, emitted his seed; that seed was thoroughly purified by the movement of the light of the sun, and Neryosang kept charge of two portions of it, while Spandarmad received one portion. 2. After forty years, in the form of a single-stemmed Rivas-plant [rhubarb], with fifteen leaves corresponding to fifteen years of growth, Mashye and Mashyane [the first human pair, here called Matro and Matroyao] grew up from the earth in such a manner that their arms rested behind on their shoulders [dosh], and joined one to the other they were conjoined and alike in every respect. 3. The waists of both were drawn together and so conjoined that it was not discernible which was male and which female, nor which was the one from whom the living soul [nismo] of Ohrmazd had not yet departed. 4. As it is stated: Which was created first, the soul [nismo] or the body? And Ohrmazd replied that the soul was created first and the body afterward, for the one who was created; the soul is given into the body so that the body may produce activity, and the body is created for the sole purpose of activity. Hence the conclusion is that the soul [ruban] is created before the body. 5. And both of them changed from the form of a plant into the form of human beings, and the breath [nismo] entered them spiritually, which is the soul [ruban]; and furthermore, in that similitude a tree had grown whose fruit comprised the ten varieties of humankind. 6. Ohrmazd spoke to Mashye and Mashyane thus: 'You are human; you are the progenitors of the world, and you have been created by me in perfect devotion. Perform the duty of the law devotedly, think good thoughts, speak good words, do good deeds, and worship no demons!' 7. Their first thought was that each should please the other, as one is a companion to the other; and the first act they performed was to wash themselves thoroughly when they went out; and the first words they spoke were these: that Ohrmazd created water and earth, plants and animals, the stars, the moon, and the sun, and all prosperity whose origin and effect derive from the manifestation of righteousness. 8. Afterward, however, antagonism rushed into their minds, their minds were thoroughly corrupted, and they cried out that the evil spirit had created water and earth, plants and animals, and the other things aforementioned. 9. That false speech was uttered through the will of the demons, and the evil spirit thereby seized upon this first act of enjoyment from them; through that false speech they both became wicked, and their souls remain in hell until the future existence [the final renovation]. 10. For thirty days they went without food, clothed in garments of herbage [giyah]; and after those thirty days they went out into the wilderness, encountered a white-haired goat, and drew milk from its udder with their mouths. 11. When they had consumed the milk, Mashye said to Mashyane: 'My delight was greater before I had consumed the milk than it is now that it has been consumed by my vile body.' 12. That [text ends mid-sentence]

segments.title_inferred: On the Nature of Generation, Seed, and Biological Classification of Male and Female
segments.original_text: 1. On the nature of generation it says in revelation, that a woman when she comes out from menstruation, during ten days and nights, when they go near unto her, soon becomes pregnant. 2. When she is cleansed from her menstruation, and when the time for pregnancy has come, always when the seed of the man is the more powerful a son arises from it; when that of the woman is the more powerful, a daughter; when both seeds are equal, twins and triplets. 3. If the male seed comes the sooner, it adds to the female, and she becomes robust; if the female seed comes the sooner, it becomes blood, and the leanness of the female arises therefrom. 4. The female seed is cold and moist, and its flow is from the loins, and the color is white, red, and yellow; and the male seed is hot and dry, its flow is from the brain of the head, and the color is white and mud-colored (hashgun). 5. All the seed of the females which issues beforehand, takes a place within the womb, and the seed of the males will remain above it, and will fill the space of the womb; whatever refrains therefrom becomes blood again, enters into the veins of the females, and at the time any one is born it becomes milk and nourishes him, as all milk arises from the seed of the males, and the blood is that of the females. 6. These four things, they say, are male, and these female: the sky, metal, wind, and fire are male, and are never otherwise; the water, earth, plants, and fish are female, and are never otherwise; the remaining creation consists of male and female. 7. As regards the fish it says that, at the time of excitement, they go forwards and come back in the water, two and two, the length of a mile (hasar), which is one-fourth of a league (parasang), in the running water; in that coming and going they then rub their bodies together, and a kind of sweat drops out betwixt them, and both become pregnant.
segments.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of generation, it is stated in revelation [i.e., the sacred Zoroastrian scriptures] that a woman, upon emerging from her menstruation, may become pregnant if approached during the following ten days and nights. 2. When she is purified from her menstruation and the time for conception has arrived: whenever the seed [semen] of the man is the more potent, a son results; when that of the woman is the more potent, a daughter; when both seeds are equal in power, twins or triplets are produced. 3. If the male seed arrives first, it augments the female seed and the woman becomes robust [in body]; if the female seed arrives first, it turns to blood, and the thinness [leanness] of the female body results from this. 4. The female seed is cold and moist in nature; it flows from the loins, and its color is white, red, and yellow. The male seed is hot and dry in nature; it flows from the brain of the head, and its color is white and mud-colored [hashgun]. 5. All of the female seed that is emitted first settles within the womb, and the male seed remains above it and fills the cavity of the womb; whatever of the male seed is not used in this way reverts to blood, enters the veins of the female, and at the time of birth becomes milk and nourishes the infant — for all milk arises from the seed of males, and blood is that of the females. 6. These four things are said to be inherently male, and these inherently female: the sky, metal, wind, and fire are male, and are never otherwise; water, earth, plants, and fish are female, and are never otherwise; the remainder of creation consists of both male and female. 7. Concerning fish, it is said that at the time of their sexual excitement they swim forward and back in running water, two by two, over the length of a hasar [mile], which is one-fourth of a parasang [league]; in this to-and-fro swimming they rub their bodies together, and a kind of sweat exudes between them, and both thereby become pregnant.

segments.title_inferred: On the Gokaren Tree, the Kar Fish, the Vas of Panchasadvaran, and the Tree of Many Seeds
segments.original_text: 1. On the nature of the tree they call Gokaren it says in revelation, that it was the first day when the tree they call Gokaren grew in the deep mud within the wide-formed ocean; and it is necessary as a producer of the renovation of the universe, for they prepare its immortality therefrom. 2. The evil spirit has formed therein, among those which enter as opponents, a lizard as an opponent in that deep water, so that it may injure the Haoma. 3. And for keeping away that lizard, Ohrmazd has created there ten Kar fish which, at all times, continually circle around the Haoma, so that the head of one of those fish is continually towards the lizard. 4. And together with the lizard those fish are spiritually fed, that is, no food is necessary for them; and till the renovation of the universe they remain in contention. 5. There are places where that fish is written of as 'the Ariz of the water;' as it says that the greatest of the creatures of Ohrmazd is that fish, and the greatest of those proceeding from the evil spirit is that lizard; with the jaws of their bodies, moreover, they snap in two whatever of the creatures of both spirits has entered between them, except that one fish which is the Vas of Panchasadvaran. 6. This, too, is said, that those fish are so serpent-like in that deep water, they know the scratch (malishn) of a needle's point by which the water shall increase, or by which it is diminishing. 7. Regarding the Vas of Panchasadvaran it is declared that it moves within the wide-formed ocean, and its length is as much as what a man, while in a swift race, will walk from dawn till when the sun goes down; so much that it does not itself move the length of the whole of its great body. 8. This, too, is said, that the creatures of the waters live also specially under its guardianship. 9. The tree of many seeds has grown amid the wide-formed ocean, and in its seed are all plants; some say it is the proper-curing, some the energetic-curing, some the all-curing. 10. Between these trees of such kinds is formed the mountain with cavities, 9999 thousand myriads in number, each myriad being ten thousand. 11. Unto that mountain is given the protection of the waters, so that water streams forth from there, in the rivulet channels, to the land of the seven regions, as the source of all the sea-water in the land of the seven regions is from there.
segments.translation: 1. Concerning the nature of the tree called Gokaren, it is stated in revelation that on the first day the tree called Gokaren grew in the deep mud within the wide-formed ocean; it is indispensable as the agent of the renovation of the universe, for immortality is prepared from it. 2. The evil spirit has placed among its opponents a lizard in that deep water, so that it may harm the Haoma. 3. To ward off that lizard, Ohrmazd created ten Kar fish there, which at all times continually circle around the Haoma, such that the head of one of those fish is always directed toward the lizard. 4. Both the lizard and those fish are sustained spiritually—that is, no physical food is necessary for them—and until the renovation of the universe they remain in contention. 5. In some passages that fish is referred to as 'the Ariz of the water.' It is stated that the greatest of Ohrmazd's creatures is that fish, and the greatest of those originating from the evil spirit is that lizard; with the jaws of their bodies they snap in two whatever creatures of either spirit come between them, with the exception of that one fish which is the Vas of Panchasadvaran. 6. It is also said that those fish are so serpent-like in that deep water that they can detect the disturbance [malishn, lit. 'scratch'] of a needle's point, by which they know whether the water is rising or diminishing. 7. Concerning the Vas of Panchasadvaran, it is declared that it moves within the wide-formed ocean, and its length is equal to the distance a man running swiftly would cover from dawn until sunset; indeed, it does not itself travel a distance equal to the full length of its great body. 8. It is also said that the creatures of the waters live especially under its protection. 9. The tree of many seeds has grown in the midst of the wide-formed ocean, and within its seed are contained all plants; some call it the 'proper-curing' [tree], some the 'energetic-curing,' and some the 'all-curing.' 10. Between these various trees there is formed a mountain filled with cavities, 9,999 thousand myriads in number, each myriad being ten thousand. 11. The protection of the waters is entrusted to that mountain, so that water flows forth from it, through rivulet channels, to the land of the seven regions; for the source of all sea-water in the land of the seven regions originates there.

segments.title_inferred: The Three-Legged Ass, Ox Hadhayosh, and Bird Chamrosh: Mythical Creatures of the Bundahishn
segments.original_text: 1. Regarding the three-legged ass they say, that it stands amid the wide-formed ocean, and its feet are three, eyes six, mouths nine, ears two, and horn one, body white, food spiritual, and it is righteous. 2. And two of its six eyes are in the position of eyes, two on the top of the head, and two in the position of the hump; with the sharpness of those six eyes it overcomes and destroys. 3. Of the nine mouths three are in the head, three in the hump, and three in the inner part of the flanks; and each mouth is about the size of a cottage, and it is itself as large as Mount Alvand. 4. Each one of the three feet, when it is placed on the ground, is as much as a flock (gird) of a thousand sheep comes under when they repose together; and each pastern is so great in its circuit that a thousand men with a thousand horses may pass inside. 5. As for the two ears it is Mazendaran which they will encompass. 6. The one horn is as it were of gold and hollow, and a thousand branch horns have grown upon it, some befitting a camel, some befitting a horse, some befitting an ox, some befitting an ass, both great and small. 7. With that horn it will vanquish and dissipate all the vile corruption due to the efforts of noxious creatures. 8. When that ass shall hold its neck in the ocean, its ears will terrify (asahmed), and all the water of the wide-formed ocean will shake with agitation, and the side of Ganavad will tremble (shivaned). 9. When it utters a cry all female water-creatures, of the creatures of Ohrmazd, will become pregnant; and all pregnant noxious water-creatures, when they hear that cry, will cast their young. 10. When it stales in the ocean all the sea-water will become purified, which is in the seven regions of the earth — it is even on that account when all asses which come into water stale in the water — as it says thus: 'If, O three-legged ass! you were not created for the water, all the water in the sea would have perished from the contamination which the poison of the evil spirit has brought into its water, through the death of the creatures of Ohrmazd.' 11.
