Tishtar seizes the water more completely from the ocean with the assistance of the three-legged ass. 12. Of ambergris also (ambar-ich) it is declared, that it is the dung of the three-legged ass; for if it has much spirit food, then also the moisture of the liquid nourishment goes through the veins pertaining to the body into the urine, and the dung is cast away. 13. Of the ox Hadhayosh, which they call Sarsaok, it says, that in the original creation men passed from region to region upon it, and in the renovation of the universe they prepare Hush (the beverage producing immortality) from it. 14. It is said, that life is in the hand of that foremost man, at the end of his years, who has constructed the most defenses around this earth, until the renovation of the universe is requisite. 15. Regarding the bird Chamrosh it says, that it is on the summit of Mount Alburz; and every three ye[ars...]
segments.translation: 1. Regarding the three-legged ass, they say that it stands in the midst of the wide-formed ocean; its feet number three, its eyes six, its mouths nine, its ears two, and its horn one; its body is white, its sustenance spiritual, and it is a righteous creature. 2. Of its six eyes, two are in the normal position of eyes, two are on the top of the head, and two are in the position of the hump; by the keenness of those six eyes it overcomes and destroys [evil]. 3. Of the nine mouths, three are in the head, three in the hump, and three in the inner part of the flanks; each mouth is roughly the size of a cottage, while the creature itself is as large as Mount Alvand. 4. Each of the three feet, when set upon the ground, covers an area sufficient for a flock [gird] of a thousand sheep resting together; and each pastern is so vast in its circumference that a thousand men with a thousand horses could pass within it. 5. As for its two ears, they will encompass all of Mazendaran. 6. Its single horn is as if made of gold and is hollow, and upon it a thousand branch-horns have grown — some of a size befitting a camel, some a horse, some an ox, some an ass — both great and small. 7. With that horn it will vanquish and scatter all the foul corruption brought about by the strivings of noxious creatures. 8. When the ass plunges its neck into the ocean, its ears will strike terror [asahmed], and all the water of the wide-formed ocean will heave with agitation, and the region of Ganavad will tremble [shivaned]. 9. When it utters its cry, all female water-creatures among the creations of Ohrmazd will become pregnant; and all pregnant noxious water-creatures, upon hearing that cry, will miscarry their young. 10. When it urinates into the ocean, all the sea-water throughout the seven regions of the earth will be purified — it is on this very account that all asses which enter water urinate in the water — as the text says: 'If, O three-legged ass, you had not been created for the water, all the water in the sea would have perished from the contamination which the poison of the evil spirit introduced into it through the death of the creatures of Ohrmazd.' 11. The star Tishtar draws water more completely from the ocean with the aid of the three-legged ass. 12. Regarding ambergris [ambar-ich], it is declared that it is the dung of the three-legged ass; for since it consumes much spiritual food, the moisture of its liquid nourishment passes through the body's veins into the urine, and the solid dung is expelled. 13. Regarding the ox Hadhayosh, also called Sarsaok, it is said that in the original creation men traveled from region to region upon it, and at the renovation of the universe the drink Hush — the beverage that produces immortality — will be prepared from it. 14. It is said that life rests in the hands of that foremost man, at the end of his years, who has built the greatest defenses around this earth, pending the renovation of the universe. 15. Regarding the bird Chamrosh, it is said that it dwells upon the summit of Mount Alburz; and every three ye[ars... (text breaks off)]

segments.title_inferred: The Seventeen Liquid Species, Sacred Rivers, and the Purification of Water
segments.original_text: 1. In revelation they mention seventeen species of liquid (maya), as one liquid resides in plants; second, that which is flowing from the mountains that is, the rivers; third, that which is rain-water; fourth, that of tanks and other special constructions; fifth, the semen of animals and men, sixth, the urine of animals and men; seventh, the sweat of animals and men; the eighth liquid is that in the skin of animals and men; ninth, the tears of animals and men; tenth, the blood of animals and men; eleventh, the oil in animals and men, a necessary in both worlds; twelfth, the saliva of animals and men, with which they nourish the embryo; the thirteenth is that which is under the bark of plants, as it is said that every bark has a liquid, through which a drop appears on a twig (tekh) when placed four finger-breadths before a fire; fourteenth, the milk of animals and men. 2. All these, through growth, or the body which is formed, mingle again with the rivers, for the body which is formed and the growth are both one. 3. This, too, they say, that of these three rivers, that is, the Arag river, the Marv river, and the Veh river, the spirits were dissatisfied, so that they would not flow into the world, owing to the defilement of stagnant water (armesht) which they beheld, so that they were in tribulation through it until Zartosht was exhibited to them, whom I (Ohrmazd) will create, who will pour six-fold holy-water (zor) into it and make it again wholesome; he will preach carefulness. 4. This, too, it says, that, of water whose holy-water is more and pollution less, the holy-water has come in excess, and in three years it goes back to the sources; that of which the pollution and holy-water have both become equal, arrives back in six years; that of which the pollution is more and holy-water less, arrives back in nine years. 5. So, also, the growth of plants is connected, in this manner, strongly with the root; so, likewise, the blessings (afrin) which the righteous utter, come back, in this proportion, to themselves. 6. Regarding the river Nahvtak it says, that Frasiyav of Tur conducted it away; and when Ushedar comes it will flow again suitable for horses; so, also, will the fountains of the sea Kyansih. 7. Kyansih is the one where the home (jinak) of the Kayanian race is.
segments.translation: 1. In revelation, seventeen species of liquid [maya] are mentioned: the first liquid resides in plants; the second is that which flows from the mountains, that is, rivers; the third is rain-water; the fourth is that of tanks and other purpose-built constructions; the fifth is the semen of animals and men; the sixth is the urine of animals and men; the seventh is the sweat of animals and men; the eighth liquid is that found in the skin of animals and men; the ninth is the tears of animals and men; the tenth is the blood of animals and men; the eleventh is the oil [fat] in animals and men, which is a necessity in both worlds; the twelfth is the saliva of animals and men, with which they nourish the embryo; the thirteenth is that which lies beneath the bark of plants — for it is said that every bark contains a liquid, by virtue of which a drop appears on a twig [tekh] when the twig is held four finger-breadths before a fire; the fourteenth is the milk of animals and men. [Note: the text as transmitted lists only fourteen of the promised seventeen; the remaining three are absent in this passage.] 2. All of these, through growth or through the body that is formed, mingle again with the rivers; for the body that is formed and growth are, in essence, one and the same. 3. They also say that of these three rivers — namely the Arag river, the Marv river, and the Veh river — the spirits were displeased, and would not flow into the world on account of the defilement of stagnant water [armesht] that they beheld; they remained in tribulation because of it until Zartosht [Zoroaster] was revealed to them — he whom I [Ohrmazd] shall bring into existence — who will pour six-fold holy-water [zor] into the rivers and render them wholesome once more; he will teach the people to exercise caution. 4. It also states that water in which the holy-water is greater and the pollution lesser — where the holy-water is in excess — returns to the sources in three years; that in which the pollution and holy-water have become equal returns in six years; and that in which the pollution is greater and the holy-water lesser returns in nine years. 5. Likewise, the growth of plants is in this manner strongly bound to the root; and similarly, the blessings [afrin] which the righteous pronounce return to themselves in the same proportion. 6. Concerning the river Nahvtak, it is said that Frasiyav of Tur diverted it; but when Ushedar comes, it will flow again in a manner fit for horses; and so too will the fountains of the sea Kyansih. 7. Kyansih is the sea where the ancestral home [jinak] of the Kayanian race is located.

segments.title_inferred: Chieftainship of Men, Animals, Plants, Waters, and Created Things
segments.original_text: On the chieftainship of men and animals and every single thing it says in revelation, that first of the human species Gayomard was produced, brilliant and white, with eyes which looked out for the great one, him who was here the Zarathushtrotema (chief high-priest); the chieftainship of all things was from Zartosht. 2. The white ass-goat, which holds its head down, is the chief of goats, the first of those species created. 3. The black sheep which is fat and white-jawed is the chief of sheep; it was the first of those species created. 4. The camel with white-haired knees and two humps is the chief of camels. 5. First the black-haired ox with yellow knees was created; he is the chief of oxen. 6. First the dazzling white (arus) horse, with yellow ears, glossy hair, and white eyes, was produced; he is the chief of horses. 7. The white, cat-footed ass is the chief of asses. 8. First of dogs the fair (arus) dog with yellow hair was produced; he is the chief of dogs. 9. The hare was produced brown (bur); he is the chief of the wide-travelers. 10. Those beasts which have no dread whatever of the hand are evil. 11. First of birds the griffin of three natures was created, not for here (this world), for the Karshipt is the chief, which they call the falcon (chark), that which revelation says was brought to the enclosure formed by Yim [Jamshed]. 12. First of fur animals the white ermine was produced; he is the chief of fur animals; as it says that it is the white ermine which came unto the assembly of the archangels. 13. The Kar-fish, or Arizh, is the chief of the water-creatures. 14. The Daitya river is the chief of streams. 15. The Daraja river is the chief of exalted rivers, for the dwelling of the father of Zartosht was on its banks, and Zartosht was born there. 16. The hoary forest is the chief of forests. 17. Hugar the lofty, on which the water of Aredvisur flows and leaps, is the chief of summits, since it is that above which is the revolution of the constellation Sataves, the chief of reservoirs. 18. The Haoma which is out-squeezed is the chief of medicinal plants. 19. Wheat is the chief of large-seeded grains. 20. The desert wormwood is the chief of non-medicinal plants. 21. The summer vetch, which they also call 'pag' (gavirs), is the chief of small-seeded grains. 22. The kusti (sacred thread-girdle) is the chief of clothes. 23. The Bazayvana is the chief of seas. 24. Of two men, when they come forward together, the wiser and more truthful is chief. 25. This, too, it says in revelation, that Ohrmazd created the whole material world one abode, so that all may be one; for there is much splendor and glory of industry in the world. 26. Whatsoever he performs, who practices that which is good, is the value of the water of life; since water is not created alike in value, for the undefiled water of Aredvisur is worth the whole water of the sky and earth of Xwaniratha, except the Arag river, created by Ohrmazd.
segments.translation: 1. Concerning the chieftainship of men and animals and of every individual thing: revelation states that Gayomard, the first of the human species, was produced brilliant and white, with eyes that watched for the great one — he who on earth was the Zarathushtrotema [chief high-priest]; the chieftainship of all things derived from Zartosht [Zoroaster]. 2. The white ass-goat, which holds its head lowered, is the chief of goats, being the first of that species created. 3. The black sheep that is fat and white-jawed is the chief of sheep; it was the first of that species created. 4. The camel with white-haired knees and two humps is the chief of camels. 5. The black-haired ox with yellow knees was created first; he is the chief of oxen. 6. The dazzling white [arus] horse, with yellow ears, glossy hair, and white eyes, was produced first; he is the chief of horses. 7. The white, cat-footed ass is the chief of asses. 8. The fair [arus] dog with yellow hair was the first of dogs produced; he is the chief of dogs. 9. The hare was produced brown [bur]; he is the chief of the wide-travelers [far-ranging animals]. 10. Those beasts that show no dread whatsoever of the human hand are evil. 11. Of birds, the griffin of three natures was created first — though not for this world — for the Karshipt [a mythic bird] is the chief of birds, which they call the falcon [chark], the one that revelation says was brought to the enclosure [vara] formed by Yim [Jamshed]. 12. Of fur animals, the white ermine was produced first; it is the chief of fur animals — as it is said that it was the white ermine that came unto the assembly of the archangels [Amesha Spentas]. 13. The Kar-fish, also called the Arizh, is the chief of water-creatures. 14. The Daitya river is the chief of streams. 15. The Daraja river is the chief of exalted rivers, for the dwelling of the father of Zartosht stood upon its banks, and Zartosht was born there. 16. The hoary [ancient, grey] forest is the chief of forests. 17. Hugar the Lofty, upon which the water of Aredvisur [Anahita] flows and leaps, is the chief of mountain summits, for above it moves the revolution of the constellation Sataves, the chief of reservoirs. 18. The Haoma [pressed juice of the haoma plant] that is out-squeezed is the chief of medicinal plants. 19. Wheat is the chief of large-seeded grains. 20. The desert wormwood is the chief of non-medicinal plants. 21. The summer vetch, which they also call 'pag' [gavirs], is the chief of small-seeded grains. 22. The kusti [sacred thread-girdle] is the chief of garments. 23. The Bazayvana is the chief of seas. 24. Of two men, when they come forward together, the wiser and more truthful is chief. 25. Revelation also states that Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda] created the entire material world as one single abode, so that all may be united; for there is great splendor and glory of productive activity [industry] in the world. 26. Whatsoever is performed by one who practices what is good is of the value of the water of life; for not all water is created equal in value — the undefiled water of Aredvisur is worth the totality of the water of the sky and earth of Xwaniratha [the central of the seven climes], except for the Arag river, which was created by Ohrmazd.

segments.title_inferred: The Religious Calendar and Division of the Year into Seasons, Day-Periods, and Summer/Winter
segments.original_text: The religious calendar 1. On matters of religion it says in revelation thus: 'The creatures of the world were created by me complete in three hundred and sixty-five days,' that is, the six periods of the Gahambars which are completed in a year. 2. It is always necessary first to count the day and afterwards the night, for first the day goes off, and then the night comes on. 3. And from the season (gas) of Maidyoshahem, which is the auspicious day Khwarshed of the month Tishtar, to the season of Maidyarem, which is the auspicious day Warharan of the month Din [Dae]-- the shortest day -- the night increases; and from the season of Maidyarem to the season of Maidyoshahem the night decreases and the day increases. 4. The summer day is as much as two of the shortest winter days, and the winter night is as much as two of the shortest summer nights. 5. The summer day is twelve Hasars, the night six Hasars; the winter night is twelve Hasars, the day six; a Hasar being a measure of time and, in like manner, of land. 6. In the season of Hamaspathmaidyem, that is, the five supplementary days at the end of the month Spandarmad, the day and night are again equal. 7. As from the auspicious day Ohrmazd of the month Frawardin to the auspicious day Anagran of the month Mihr is the summer of seven months, so from the auspicious day Ohrmazd of the month Aban to the auspicious month Spandarmad, on to the end of the five supplementary days, is the winter of five months. 8. The priest fulfills the regulation (vachar) about a corpse and other things, by this calculation as to summer and winter. 9. In those seven months of summer the periods (gas) of the days and nights are five since one celebrates the Rapithwin namely, the period of day-break is Hawan, the period of midday is Rapithwin, the period of afternoon is Uzerin, when the appearance of the stars has come into the sky until midnight is the period of Aiwisruthrem, from midnight until the stars become imperceptible is the period of Ushahin. 10. In winter are four periods, for from daybreak till Uzerin is all Hawan, and the rest as I have said; and the reason of it is this, that the appearance of winter is in the direction of the north, where the regions Wourubareshti and Wourujareshti are; the original dwelling of summer, too, is in the south, where the regions Fradadhafshu and Widadhafshu are; on the day Ohrmazd of the auspicious month Aban the winter acquires strength and enters into the world, and the spirit of Rapithwin goes from above-ground to below-ground, where the spring (khani) of waters is, and diffuses warmth and moisture in the water, and so many roots of trees do not wither with cold and drought. 11. And on the auspicious day Adar of the month Din the winter arrives, with much cold, at Eranvej; and until the end, in the auspicious month Spandarmad, winter advances through the whole world; on this account they kindle a fire everywhere on the day Adar of the month Din, and it forms an indication tha[t...]
segments.translation: The Religious Calendar. 1. Concerning matters of religion, it is stated in revelation as follows: 'The creatures of the world were created by me complete in three hundred and sixty-five days' — that is, the six periods of the Gahambars [sacred festival seasons], which are completed in the course of one year. 2. It is always necessary to count the day first and the night afterwards, for the day departs first and then the night follows. 3. From the season [gāh] of Maidyoshahem [Mid-Summer], which falls on the auspicious day Khwarshed of the month Tishtar, to the season of Maidyarem [Mid-Year/Winter], which falls on the auspicious day Warharan of the month Din [Dae] — the shortest day — the night increases in length; and from the season of Maidyarem back to the season of Maidyoshahem, the night decreases and the day increases. 4. The summer day is equal in length to two of the shortest winter days, and the winter night is equal in length to two of the shortest summer nights. 5. The summer day is twelve Hasars [a unit of time] long and the night six Hasars; the winter night is twelve Hasars long and the day six; a Hasar being a measure both of time and, likewise, of distance on land. 6. In the season of Hamaspathmaidyem — that is, the five intercalary [supplementary] days appended at the end of the month Spandarmad — the day and night are again equal. 7. Just as the period from the auspicious day Ohrmazd of the month Frawardin to the auspicious day Anagran of the month Mihr constitutes the summer of seven months, so the period from the auspicious day Ohrmazd of the month Aban through the auspicious month Spandarmad, including the end of the five supplementary days, constitutes the winter of five months. 8. The priest fulfills the prescribed regulation [vachar] concerning a corpse and other matters by means of this calculation of summer and winter. 9. During those seven months of summer, the periods [gāh] of the days and nights are five in number, since one celebrates the Rapithwin [mid-day watch]: namely, the period of daybreak is Hawan; the period of midday is Rapithwin; the period of afternoon is Uzerin; the period from the appearance of the stars in the sky until midnight is Aiwisruthrem; and from midnight until the stars become imperceptible is the period of Ushahin. 10. In winter there are four periods, for from daybreak until Uzerin counts entirely as Hawan, and the rest remain as I have stated; and the reason for this is that the domain of winter lies in the direction of the north, where the regions Wourubareshti and Wourujareshti are situated; likewise, the original seat of summer is in the south, where the regions Fradadhafshu and Widadhafshu are. On the day Ohrmazd of the auspicious month Aban, winter gains strength and enters the world, and the spirit of Rapithwin descends from above the earth to below the earth, where the spring [khani] of waters lies, and there diffuses warmth and moisture through the water, so that the roots of many trees do not wither from cold and drought. 11. And on the auspicious day Adar of the month Din, winter arrives at Eranvej [the mythical primordial homeland] with great cold; and continuing to the end of the auspicious month Spandarmad, winter advances throughout the entire world. For this reason, fires are kindled everywhere on the day Adar of the month Din, and this serves as a sign that... [TEXT ENDS ABRUPTLY]

segments.title_inferred: On the Evil-Doing of Ahriman and the Demons (Bundahishn Chapter 62)
segments.original_text: On the evil-doing of Ahriman and the demons 1. On the evildoing of Ahriman and the demons it says in revelation, that the evil which the evil spirit has produced for the creation of Ohrmazd it is possible to tell by this winter; and his body is that of a lizard (vazagh) whose place is filth (kalch). 2. He does not think, nor speak, nor act for the welfare (nadukih) of the creatures of Ohrmazd; and his business is unmercifulness and the destruction of this welfare, so that the creatures which Ohrmazd shall increase he will destroy; and his eyesight (chashm michihsn) does not refrain from doing the creatures harm. 3. As it says that, 'ever since a creature was created by us, I, who am Ohrmazd, have not rested at ease, on account of providing protection for my own creatures; and likewise not even he, the evil spirit, on account of contriving evil for the creatures.' 4. And by their devotion to witchcraft (yatuk-dinoih) he seduces mankind into affection for himself and disaffection to Ohrmazd, so that they forsake the religion of Ohrmazd, and practice that of Ahriman. 5. He casts this into the thoughts of men, that this religion of Ohrmazd is naught, and it is not necessary to be steadfast in it. 6. Whoever gives that man anything, in whose law (dad) this saying is established, then the evil spirit is propitiated by him, that is, he has acted by his pleasure. 7. The business of Akoman is this, that he gave vile thoughts and discord to the creatures. 8. The business of the demon Andar is this, that he constrains the thoughts of the creatures from deeds of virtue, just like a leader who has well-constrained (sardar-i khup afsardo); and he casts this into the thoughts of men, that it is not necessary to have the sacred shirt [sudre] and thread-girdle [kusti]. 9. The business of the demon Savar, that is a leader of the demons, is this, that is, misgovernment, oppressive anarchy, and drunkenness. 10. The business of the demon Naikiyas is this, that he gives discontent to the creatures; as it says, that should this one give anything to those men whose opinion (dad) is this, that it is not necessary to have the sacred shirt and thread-girdle, then Andar, Savar, and Naikiyas are propitiated by him. 11. The demon Taprev is he who mingles poison with plants and creatures, as it says thus: 'Taprev the frustrater, and Zairich the maker of poison.' 12. All those six, it is said, are arch-fiends of the demons; the rest are cooperating and confederate with them. 13. This, too, it says, that should one give anything to a man who says that it is proper to have one boot, and in his law walking with one boot is established, then the fiend Taprev is propitiated by him. 14. The demon Taromat is he who produces disobedience; the demon Mitrokht is the liar (drojan) of the evil spirit; the demon Arashk ('malice') is the spiteful fiend of the evil eye. 15. Theirs are the same appliances as the demon Eshm's, as it says that seven powers are given to Eshm, that he.
segments.translation: On the Evil-Doing of Ahriman and the Demons. 1. Concerning the evil-doing of Ahriman and the demons, it is stated in revelation that the evil which the evil spirit has wrought against the creation of Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda] may be known through this: his body is that of a lizard [vazagh], whose dwelling place is filth [kalch]. 2. He neither thinks, nor speaks, nor acts for the wellbeing [nadukih] of the creatures of Ohrmazd; his occupation is mercilessness and the destruction of that wellbeing, so that whatever creatures Ohrmazd causes to increase, he will destroy; and his gaze [chashm michihsn] does not refrain from doing harm to the creatures. 3. As it is said: 'Ever since a creature was created by us, I, who am Ohrmazd, have not rested at ease, on account of providing protection for my own creatures; and likewise, not even he—the evil spirit—on account of contriving evil for the creatures.' 4. And through devotion to witchcraft [yatuk-dinoih], he seduces mankind into love of himself and hatred of Ohrmazd, so that they forsake the religion of Ohrmazd and practice that of Ahriman. 5. He instils into the minds of men the idea that the religion of Ohrmazd is nothing, and that it is not necessary to remain steadfast in it. 6. Whoever gives anything to a man in whose law [dad] this saying is established thereby propitiates the evil spirit—that is, he has acted according to the evil spirit's will. 7. The work of Akoman [the demon of Evil Mind] is this: that he imparts vile thoughts and discord to the creatures. 8. The work of the demon Andar is this: that he constrains the thoughts of the creatures away from virtuous deeds, just as a leader who has bound his followers well [sardar-i khup afsardo]; and he instils into the minds of men that it is not necessary to wear the sacred shirt [sudre] and the sacred thread-girdle [kusti]. 9. The work of the demon Savar—who is a chief among the demons—is this: misgovernment, oppressive anarchy, and drunkenness. 10. The work of the demon Naikiyas is this: that he imparts discontent to the creatures; as it is said, should anyone give anything to those men who hold the opinion [dad] that it is not necessary to have the sacred shirt and thread-girdle, then Andar, Savar, and Naikiyas are propitiated by him. 11. The demon Taprev is he who mingles poison with plants and living creatures, as it is said: 'Taprev the frustrater, and Zairich the maker of poison.' 12. All these six, it is said, are arch-fiends among the demons; the remainder are cooperating with and allied to them. 13. It also says that should one give anything to a man who maintains that it is proper to wear only one boot, and in whose law walking with one boot is established, then the fiend Taprev is propitiated by him. 14. The demon Taromat is he who produces disobedience; the demon Mitrokht is the liar [drojan] of the evil spirit; the demon Arashk ('Malice') is the spiteful fiend of the evil eye. 15. Their instruments are the same as those of the demon Eshm [Wrath], as it is said that seven powers are given to Eshm, that he [text breaks off].

segments.title_inferred: On the Resurrection and Future Existence (Bundahishn, Chapter 64)
segments.original_text: On the resurrection and future existence. 1. On the nature of the resurrection and future existence it says in revelation, that, whereas Mashye and Mashyane, who grew up from the earth, first fed upon water, then plants, then milk, and then meat, men also, when their time of death has come, first desist from eating meat, then milk, then from bread, till when they shall die they always feed upon water. 2. So, likewise, in the millennium of Ushedarmah, the strength of appetite (az) will thus diminish, when men will remain three days and nights in superabundance (sirih) through one taste of consecrated food. 3. Then they will desist from meat food, and eat vegetables and milk; afterwards, they abstain from milk food and abstain from vegetable food, and are feeding on water; and for ten years before Soshyant comes they remain without food, and do not die. 4. After Soshyant comes they prepare the raising of the dead, as it says, that Zartosht asked of Ohrmazd thus: 'Whence does a body form again, which the wind has carried and the water conveyed (vazhid)? and how does the resurrection occur?' 5. Ohrmazd answered thus: 'When through me the sky arose from the substance of the ruby, without columns, on the spiritual support of far-compassed light; when through me the earth arose, which bore the material life, and there is no maintainer of the worldly creation but it; when by me the sun and moon and stars are conducted in the firmament (andarvai) of luminous bodies; when by me corn was created so that, scattered about in the earth, it grew again and returned with increase; when by me color of various kinds was created in plants; when by me fire was created in plants and other things without combustion; when by me a son was created and fashioned in the womb of a mother, and the structure (pishak) severally of the skin, nails, blood, feet, eyes, ears, and other things was produced; when by me legs were created for the water, so that it flows away, and the cloud was created which carries the water of the world and rains there where it has a purpose; when by me the air was created which conveys in one's eyesight, through the strength of the wind, the lowermost upwards according to its will, and one is not able to grasp it with the hand out-stretched; each one of them, when created by me, was herein more difficult than causing the resurrection, for it is an assistance to me in the resurrection that they exist, but when they were formed it was not forming the future out of the past. 6. Observe that when that which was not was then produced, why is it not possible to produce again that which was? for at that time one will demand the bone from the spirit of earth, the blood from the water, the hair from the plants, and the life from fire, since they were delivered to them in the original creation.' 7. First, the bones of Gayomard are roused up, then those of Mashye and Mashyane, then those of the rest of mankind; in the fifty-seven years of Soshyant they prepare [the raising of the dead].
segments.translation: On the Resurrection and Future Existence. 1. Concerning the nature of the resurrection and future existence, it is stated in revelation that, just as Mashye and Mashyane [the primordial human pair], who grew up from the earth, first subsisted on water, then on plants, then on milk, and then on meat, so too will men, when their time of death has come, first cease eating meat, then milk, then bread, until at the moment of death they subsist on water alone. 2. Likewise, in the millennium of Ushedarmah [the second of the three saviors of the end times], the force of appetite [az] will so diminish that men will remain in a state of complete satiety [sirih] for three days and nights from a single taste of consecrated food. 3. Then they will abstain from meat and subsist on vegetables and milk; afterwards they will abstain from milk and then from vegetable food, living on water alone; and for ten years before the coming of Soshyant [the final savior] they will remain entirely without food, yet will not die. 4. After Soshyant comes, they will undertake the raising of the dead. As it is written, Zartosht [Zoroaster] asked Ohrmazd [Ahura Mazda]: 'From where does a body take form again, once the wind has scattered it and the water has carried it away [vazhid]? And how does the resurrection come to pass?' 5. Ohrmazd answered thus: 'When through me the sky arose from the substance of the ruby, without columns, upheld by the spiritual support of far-reaching light; when through me the earth arose, which sustains material life and without which there is no maintainer of worldly creation; when through me the sun, moon, and stars were set in motion in the firmament [andarvai] of luminous bodies; when through me grain was created so that, scattered into the earth, it grew again and came back multiplied; when through me color of many kinds was created in plants; when through me fire was created within plants and other things without combustion; when through me a son was created and shaped in the womb of a mother, and the structure [pishak] of the skin, nails, blood, feet, eyes, ears, and all other parts was formed individually; when through me motion was given to water so that it flows away, and the cloud was created to carry the water of the world and to rain wherever it is purposed; when through me the air was created, which by the force of the wind carries even the lowest things upward as it wills, so that one cannot grasp it with an outstretched hand — each one of these acts of creation, when accomplished by me, was more difficult than bringing about the resurrection. For their very existence now assists me in the resurrection; but when they were first formed, there was no precedent — no past thing from which to fashion the future. 6. Consider: if that which did not exist was then brought into being, why should it not be possible to bring back into being that which once existed? For at that time the bone will be demanded back from the spirit of the earth, the blood from the water, the hair from the plants, and the life from fire, since these were entrusted to them at the original creation.' 7. First, the bones of Gayomard [the primordial man] are raised up, then those of Mashye and Mashyane, then those of the rest of humanity; and in the fifty-seven years of Soshyant's ministry they complete [the raising of the dead].

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 1: The Sixteen Good Lands Created by Ahura Mazda and Their Counter-Plagues by Angra Mainyu
segments.original_text: Sixteen perfect lands created by Ahura Mazda, and as many plagues created by Angra Mainyu. 1. Ahura Mazda spake unto Spitama Zarathushtra, saying: I have made every land dear (to its people), even though it had no charms whatever in it: had I not made every land dear (to its people), even though it had no charms whatever in it, then the whole living world would have invaded the Airyana Vaeja. Notes: 1. Or Spitamide. Zarathushtra was descended from Spitama at the fifth generation. 2. 'Everyone fancies that the land where he was born and has been brought up is the best and fairest land that I have created' (Comm.) 3. Greater Bundahish: 'It is said in the Sacred Book: had I not created the Genius of the native place, all mankind would have gone to Eran-Vej, on account of its pleasantness.' — On Airyanem Vaeja or Eran-Vej, see following note. 2. The first of the good lands and countries which I, Ahura Mazda, created, was the Airyana Vaeja, by the Vanguhi Daitya. Thereupon came Angra Mainyu, who is all death, and he counter-created the serpent in the river and Winter, a work of the Daevas. 4. Clause 2 in the Vendidad Sada is composed of Zend quotations in the Commentary that illustrate the alternative process of creation: 'First, Ahura Mazda would create a land of such kind that its dwellers might like it, and there could be nothing more delightful. Then he who is all death would bring against it a counter-creation.' 5. Airyanem Vaeja, Iran-Vej, is the holy land of Zoroastrianism: Zarathushtra was born and founded his religion there (Bund. 20.32; 32.3): the first animal couple appeared there (Bund. 14.4; Zadspram, 9.8). From its name, 'the Iranian seed,' it seems to have been considered as the original seat of the Iranian race. It has been generally supposed to belong to Eastern Iran, like the provinces which are enumerated after it, chiefly on account of the name of its river, the Vanguhi Daitya, which was in the Sassanian times (as Veh) the name of the Oxus. But the Bundahish distinctly states that Iran-Vej is 'bordering upon Adarbajan' (29.12); now, Adarbaijan is bordered by the Caspian Sea on the east, by the Rangha provinces on the west, by Media proper on the south, and by Arran on the north. The Rangha provinces are out of question, since they are mentioned at the end of the Fargard (verse 20), and the climatic conditions of Iran-Vej with its long winter likewise exclude Media and suit Arran, where the summer lasts hardly two months (cf. § 4, note 6). The very name agrees, as the country known as Arran seems to have been known to the Greeks as Ariania (Stephanus Byz.), which brings it close to our Airyanem. On the Vanguhi Daitya, see following note. 6. The Vanguhi Daitya, belonging to Arran, must be the modern Aras (the classic Araxes). The Aras was named Vanguhi, like the Oxus, but distinguished from it by the addition Daitya, which made it 'the Vanguhi of the Law' (the Vanguhi by which Zarathushtra r[eceived his revelation — text truncated].
segments.translation:
Sixteen perfect lands created by Ahura Mazda, and as many plagues created by Angra Mainyu.

1. Ahura Mazda spoke unto Spitama [i.e., Spitamide] Zarathushtra, saying: I have made every land dear to its people, even though it had no charms whatever in it; for had I not made every land dear to its people, even though it had no charms whatever in it, then the whole living world would have invaded the Airyana Vaeja [the holy Iranian homeland].

[Translator's note 1: Or 'Spitamide.' Zarathushtra was descended from Spitama at the fifth generation.]
[Note 2: 'Everyone fancies that the land where he was born and has been brought up is the best and fairest land that I have created' (Commentary).]
[Note 3: The Greater Bundahish states: 'It is said in the Sacred Book: had I not created the Genius of the native place, all mankind would have gone to Eran-Vej, on account of its pleasantness.' — On Airyanem Vaeja or Eran-Vej, see the following note.]

2. The first of the good lands and countries which I, Ahura Mazda, created, was the Airyana Vaeja [Iranian seed-land], watered by the Vanguhi Daitya [the Lawful Good River]. Thereupon came Angra Mainyu [the Destructive Spirit], who is all death, and he counter-created the serpent in the river and Winter — a work of the Daevas [evil spirits].

[Note 4: In the Vendidad Sada, clause 2 is composed of Avestan quotations in the Commentary illustrating the alternating process of creation: 'First, Ahura Mazda would create a land of such a kind that its dwellers might like it, and there could be nothing more delightful. Then he who is all death would bring against it a counter-creation.']
[Note 5: Airyanem Vaeja — Iran-Vej — is the holy land of Zoroastrianism: Zarathushtra was born there and founded his religion there (Bundahish 20.32; 32.3); the first animal couple appeared there (Bundahish 14.4; Zadspram 9.8). From its name, meaning 'the Iranian seed,' it appears to have been regarded as the original seat of the Iranian race. It has generally been supposed to lie in Eastern Iran, like the provinces enumerated after it in this Fargard, principally because of the name of its river, the Vanguhi Daitya, which in Sassanian times (as 'Veh') was the name of the Oxus. However, the Bundahish explicitly states that Iran-Vej 'borders upon Adarbajan' (29.12); Adarbaijan [Azerbaijan] is bounded by the Caspian Sea to the east, by the Rangha provinces to the west, by Media proper to the south, and by Arran to the north. The Rangha provinces are excluded from consideration, since they are mentioned at the end of the Fargard (verse 20); and the climatic conditions of Iran-Vej — notably its prolonged winter — likewise exclude Media and point instead to Arran, where summer lasts barely two months (cf. § 4, note 6). The name itself is consistent with this identification, since the country known as Arran appears to have been called Ariania by the Greeks (Stephanus of Byzantium), which brings it close to Airyanem.]
[Note 6: The Vanguhi Daitya, belonging to the region of Arran, must then be identified with the modern Aras River (the classical Araxes). The Aras bore the name Vanguhi, like the Oxus, but was distinguished from it by the epithet Daitya, making it 'the Vanguhi of the Law' — that is, the Vanguhi beside which Zarathushtra [received his revelation — text truncated].]

segments.title_inferred: Myths of Yima (Jamshed): Fargard 2 of the Vendidad — Yima's Reign and the Expansion of the Earth
segments.original_text: Myths of Yima [Jamshed] I. Notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Who was the first mortal, before myself, Zarathushtra, with whom thou, Ahura Mazda, didst converse, whom thou didst teach the Religion of Ahura, the Religion of Zarathushtra? 2. Ahura Mazda answered: The fair Yima, the good shepherd, O holy Zarathushtra! he was the first mortal, before thee, Zarathushtra, with whom I, Ahura Mazda, did converse, whom I taught the Religion of Ahura, the Religion of Zarathushtra. 3. Unto him, O Zarathushtra, I, Ahura Mazda, spake, saying: 'Well, fair Yima, son of Vivanghat, be thou the preacher and the bearer of my Religion!' And the fair Yima, O Zarathushtra, replied unto me, saying: 'I was not born, I was not taught to be the preacher and the bearer of thy Religion.' 4. Then I, Ahura Mazda, said thus unto him, O Zarathushtra: 'Since thou dost not consent to be the preacher and the bearer of my Religion, then make thou my world increase, make my world grow: consent thou to nourish, to rule, and to watch over my world.' 5. And the fair Yima replied unto me, O Zarathushtra, saying: 'Yes! I will make thy world increase, I will make thy world grow. Yes! I will nourish, and rule, and watch over thy world. There shall be, while I am king, neither cold wind nor hot wind, neither disease nor death.' 6. Then I, Ahura Mazda, brought two implements unto him: a golden seal and a poniard inlaid with gold. Behold, here Yima bears the royal sway! 7. [Obscure.] 8. Thus, under the sway of Yima, three hundred winters passed away, and the earth was replenished with flocks and herds, with men and dogs and birds and with red blazing fires, and there was room no more for flocks, herds, and men. 9. Then I warned the fair Yima, saying: 'O fair Yima, son of Vivanghat, the earth has become full of flocks and herds, of men and dogs and birds and of red blazing fires, and there is room no more for flocks, herds, and men.' 10. Then Yima stepped forward, in light, southwards, on the way of the sun, and (afterwards) he pressed the earth with the golden seal, and bored it with the poniard, speaking thus: 'O Spenta Armaiti, kindly open asunder and stretch thyself afar, to bear flocks and herds and men.'
segments.translation:
Myths of Yima [Jamshed], Part I.

§1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Who was the first mortal, before myself, Zarathushtra, with whom thou, Ahura Mazda, didst converse [lit. 'on the Religion,' per the commentary], and whom thou didst teach the Religion of Ahura — the Religion of Zarathushtra?'

§2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The fair Yima [Jamshed], the good shepherd [the commentary explains: 'his being a good shepherd means that he held in good condition herds of men and herds of animals'], O holy Zarathushtra! He was the first mortal, before thee, Zarathushtra, with whom I, Ahura Mazda, did converse, and whom I taught the Religion of Ahura — the Religion of Zarathushtra.'

§3. Unto him, O Zarathushtra, I, Ahura Mazda, spoke, saying: 'Well, fair Yima, son of Vivanghat, be thou the preacher and the bearer of my Religion!' And the fair Yima, O Zarathushtra, replied unto me, saying: 'I was not born, I was not taught to be the preacher and the bearer of thy Religion.'

§4. Then I, Ahura Mazda, said thus unto him, O Zarathushtra: 'Since thou dost not consent to be the preacher and the bearer of my Religion, then make thou my world increase, make my world grow: consent thou to nourish, to rule, and to watch over my world.'

§5. And the fair Yima replied unto me, O Zarathushtra, saying: 'Yes! I will make thy world increase, I will make thy world grow. Yes! I will nourish, and rule, and watch over thy world. There shall be, while I am king, neither cold wind nor hot wind, neither disease nor death.'

§6. Then I, Ahura Mazda, brought two implements unto him: a golden seal [signet ring] and a poniard [dagger] inlaid with gold [these served as the symbol and instrument of sovereignty; the commentary notes: 'He reigned supreme by the strength of the ring and of the poniard' (Asp.); compare also Firdausi's account of Faridoon investing Iraj 'with the sword and the seal, the ring and the crown']. Behold, here Yima bears the royal sway!

§7. [ILLEGIBLE: passage marked obscure in source; content unclear or lost]

§8. Thus, under the sway of Yima, three hundred winters passed away, and the earth was replenished with flocks and herds, with men and dogs and birds and with red blazing fires, and there was room no more for flocks, herds, and men.

§9. Then I warned the fair Yima, saying: 'O fair Yima, son of Vivanghat, the earth has become full of flocks and herds, of men and dogs and birds and of red blazing fires, and there is room no more for flocks, herds, and men.'

§10. Then Yima stepped forward, in light, southwards, on the way of the sun, and afterwards he pressed the earth with the golden seal, and bored it with the poniard, speaking thus: 'O Spenta Armaiti [the divine personification of holy devotion/earth], [kindly open asunder and stretch thyself afar, to bear flocks and herds and men — text breaks off].'

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 3: The Earth — Places of Joy and Grief
segments.original_text:
The Earth

I. 1. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the first place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place whereon one of the faithful steps forward, O Spitama Zarathushtra! with the log in his hand, the Baresma in his hand, the milk in his hand, the mortar in his hand, lifting up his voice in good accord with religion, and beseeching Mithra, the lord of the rolling country-side, and Rama Hvastra.'

Notes:
1. 'The Genius of the Earth' (Comm.)
2. The wood for the fire altar.
3. The Baresma (now called barsom) is a bundle of sacred twigs which the priest holds in his hand while reciting the prayers. (See Vd19.18 seq. and notes.)
4. The so-called jîv or jîvâm, one of the elements of the Haoma sacrifice.
5. The Havana [hawan] or mortar used in crushing the Haoma or Hom.
6. Mithra, the Persian Apollo, sometimes like him identified with the Sun, is invoked here as making the earth fertile. 'Why do not you worship the Sun? King Yazdgard asked the Christians. Is he not the god who lights up with his rays all the world, and through whose warmth the food of men and cattle grows ripe?' (Elisaeus.)
7. The god that gives food its savour: he is an acolyte to Mithra.

2, 3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the second place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place whereon one of the faithful erects a house with a priest within, with cattle, with a wife, with children, and good herds within; and wherein afterwards the cattle continue to thrive, virtue to thrive, fodder to thrive, the dog to thrive, the wife to thrive, the child to thrive, the fire to thrive, and every blessing of life to thrive.'

8. With the domestic chaplain (the Panthaki).
9. By the performance of worship.

4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the third place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where one of the faithful sows most corn, grass, and fruit, O Spitama Zarathushtra! where he waters ground that is dry, or drains ground that is too wet.'

10. Under the Achaemanian kings countrymen who brought water to places naturally dry received the usufruct of the ground for five generations (Polybius, X, 28). But for those underground canals (called qanats), which bring water from the mountains all through the Iranian desert, Persia would starve.

5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the fourth place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where there is most increase of flocks and herds.'

6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the fifth place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where flocks and herds yield most dung.'

II. 7. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy one! Which is the first place where the Earth feels sorest grief?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the neck of Arezura, whe[re...]'
segments.translation:
THE EARTH

Part I.

§1. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the first place where the Earth [understood as the Genius of the Earth] feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where one of the faithful steps forward, O Spitama Zarathushtra! bearing the sacred log [fuel for the fire altar] in his hand, the Baresma [barsom — a bundle of sacred twigs held during prayer] in his hand, the milk [jîv or jîvâm, an element of the Haoma sacrifice] in his hand, and the mortar [Havana/hawan, used to crush the Haoma plant] in his hand — raising his voice in proper accordance with the religion, and calling upon Mithra [the Persian deity associated with the Sun and fertility of the earth], lord of the broad pasture-lands, and upon Rama Hvastra [the deity who imparts savour to food, attendant to Mithra].'

§§2–3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the second place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where one of the faithful builds a house with a priest [domestic chaplain, the Panthaki] within it, with cattle, with a wife, with children, and with good herds; and where thereafter the cattle continue to thrive, virtue [maintained through the performance of worship] to thrive, fodder to thrive, the dog to thrive, the wife to thrive, the child to thrive, the fire to thrive, and every blessing of life to thrive.'

§4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the third place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where one of the faithful sows the most corn, grass, and fruit, O Spitama Zarathushtra! — where he brings water to dry ground or drains ground that is overly wet.' [Note: Under the Achaemenid kings, peasants who brought water to naturally arid land received the usufruct of that land for five generations (Polybius X.28). The underground canals known as qanats, which carry water from the mountains across the Iranian desert, remain essential to Persian agriculture.]

§5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the fourth place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where there is the greatest increase of flocks and herds.'

§6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the fifth place where the Earth feels most happy?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the place where flocks and herds yield the most dung [used to fertilize the soil].'

Part II.

§7. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the first place where the Earth feels the deepest grief?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the neck of Arezura [a demonic locality, likely a mountain pass associated with evil spirits], where [the text continues beyond this excerpt]...'

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 4: Contracts, Their Types, and Penalties for Breach
segments.original_text: Contracts and offenses I. Notes: 1. He that does not restore a loan to the man who lent it, steals the thing and robs the man. This he doeth every day, every night, as long as he keep in his house his neighbour's property, as though it were his own. 1. He is a thief when he takes a view not to restore; he is a robber when, being asked to restore, he answers, 'I will not' (Comm.) 2. Every moment that he holds it unlawfully, he steals it anew. 'The basest thing with Persians is to lie; the next to it is to be in debt, for this reason among many others, that he who is so, must needs sink to lying at last' (Herod. I, 139). The debtor in question is of course the debtor of bad faith, 'he who says to a man, Give me this, I will restore it to thee at the proper time, and he says to himself: I will not restore it' (Comm.) Ia. 2. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How many in number are thy contracts, O Ahura Mazda? Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are six in number, O holy Zarathushtra. The first is the word-contract; the second is the hand-contract; the third is the contract to the amount of a sheep; the fourth is the contract to the amount of an ox; the fifth is the contract to the amount of a man [person]; the sixth is the contract to the amount of a field, a field in good land, a fruitful one, in good bearing.' 3. At first view it seems as if the classification were twofold, the contracts being defined in the first two clauses by their mode of being entered into, and in the last four by their amount. Yet it appears from the following clauses that even the word-contract and the hand-contract are indicative of a certain amount, which, however, the commentators did not, or were unable to, determine. 4. The word-contract may be a contract of which the object are words: the contract of jādangōi (ukhdho-vachah), by which one offers to speak and intervene for some one's benefit, or the contract between master and pupil (for teaching the sacred texts). 5. The contract for hiring labour (?). 6. 'Viz. to the amount of 3 istīrs [in weight],' (Comm.) An istīr (stathr) is as much as 4 dirhems (drachmae). 7. 'To the amount of 12 istīrs (=48 dirhems),' (Comm.) 8. 'To the amount of 500 dirhems.' The exact translation would be rather, 'The contract to the amount of a human being' (promise of marriage). 9. 'Upwards of 500 istīrs.' 10. A sort of gloss added to define more accurately the value of the object, and to indicate that it is greater than that of the preceding one. 3. The word-contract is fulfilled by words of mouth. It is cancelled by the hand-contract; he shall give as damages the amount of the hand-contract. 4. The hand-contract is cancelled by the sheep-contract; he shall give as damages the amount of the sheep-contract. The sheep-contract is cancelled by the ox-contract; he shall give as damages the amount of the ox-contract. The ox-contract is cancelled by the man-contract [text truncated].
segments.translation:
Contracts and Offenses — Part I.

Note 1: He who does not restore a loan to the man who lent it steals the thing and robs the man. He does this every day and every night, for as long as he keeps his neighbour's property in his house as though it were his own.

[Commentator's gloss:] He is a thief at the moment he decides not to restore it; he becomes a robber when, being asked to restore it, he answers, 'I will not.'

Note 2: Every moment he holds the property unlawfully, he steals it anew. 'The basest thing among Persians is to lie; the next basest is to be in debt, for this reason among many others: that one who is in debt must in the end resort to lying' (Herodotus I, 139). The debtor in question is, of course, the debtor acting in bad faith — 'he who says to a man, Give me this, I will restore it to you at the proper time, while saying to himself: I will not restore it' (Commentator).

Section Ia, Clause 2:
O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How many contracts are there, O Ahura Mazda?

Ahura Mazda answered: There are six in number, O holy Zarathushtra.
The first is the word-contract [ukhdho-vachah].
The second is the hand-contract.
The third is the contract to the value of a sheep.
The fourth is the contract to the value of an ox.
The fifth is the contract to the value of a man [person].
The sixth is the contract to the value of a field — a field in good land, a fruitful one, in good bearing.

Note 3: At first glance the classification appears twofold: the first two contracts are defined by the manner in which they are concluded, while the last four are defined by their monetary value. Yet it emerges from subsequent clauses that even the word-contract and the hand-contract imply a certain monetary amount, which the commentators either did not know or were unable to determine.

Note 4: The word-contract may be a contract whose subject matter consists of words — specifically the contract of jādangōi [ukhdho-vachah], by which one undertakes to speak and to intercede on someone's behalf, or the contract between master and pupil for the teaching of sacred texts.

Note 5: [The hand-contract may be] the contract for hiring labour (?).

Note 6: [The sheep-contract is] 'namely, to the value of 3 istīrs [by weight]' (Commentator). One istīr [stathr] equals 4 dirhems [drachmae].

Note 7: [The ox-contract is] 'to the value of 12 istīrs (= 48 dirhems)' (Commentator).

Note 8: [The man-contract is] 'to the value of 500 dirhems.' A more precise translation would be 'the contract to the value of a human being,' understood as a promise of marriage.

Note 9: [The field-contract is] 'upwards of 500 istīrs.'

Note 10: This is a kind of gloss appended to define the value of the field more precisely and to indicate that it exceeds that of the preceding contract.

Clause 3: The word-contract is fulfilled by spoken words. If it is violated, it is superseded by the hand-contract; the offender shall pay damages equal to the value of the hand-contract.

Clause 4: If the hand-contract is violated, it is superseded by the sheep-contract; the offender shall pay damages equal to the value of the sheep-contract. If the sheep-contract is violated, it is superseded by the ox-contract; the offender shall pay damages equal to the value of the ox-contract. If the ox-contract is violated, it is superseded by the man-contract [text truncated].

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 5: Purity Laws Concerning Corpse-Defilement by Animals, Water, and Fire
segments.original_text: Purity laws I. Notes: 1. There dies a man in the depths of the vale: a bird takes flight from the top of the mountain down into the depths of the vale, and it feeds on the corpse of the dead man there: then, up it flies from the depths of the vale to the top of the mountain: it flies to some one of the trees there, of the hard-wooded or the soft-wooded, and upon that tree it vomits and deposits dung. 2. Now, lo! here is a man coming up from the depths of the vale to the top of the mountain; he comes to the tree whereon the bird is sitting; from that tree he intends to take wood for the fire. He fells the tree, he hews the tree, he splits it into logs, and then he lights it in the fire, the son of Ahura Mazda. What is the penalty he shall pay? 1. For defiling the fire by bringing dead matter into it (see Vd7.25 seq.) contrarily to the rule, 'Put ye only proper and well-examined fuel (in the fire).' For the purification of unclean wood, see Vd7.28 seq. 3. Ahura Mazda answered: 'There is no sin upon a man for any Nasu that has been brought by dogs, by birds, by wolves, by winds, or by flies. 4. 'For were there sin upon a man for any Nasu that might have been brought by dogs, by birds, by wolves, by winds, or by flies, how soon all this material world of mine would be only one Peshotanu, bent on the destruction of righteousness, and whose soul will cry and wail! so numberless are the beings that die upon the face of the earth.' 2. 'People guilty of death [i.e. a mortal sin]' (Comm.) Cf. Yasna 53.9b. 3. After their death, 'When the soul, crying and beaten off, is driven far away from Paradise' (Comm.) This is imitated from the Gathas (Yasna 46.11c; 51.13b; cf. Vd13.8-9). 5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Here is a man watering a corn-field. The water streams down the field; it streams again; it streams a third time; and the fourth time, a dog, a fox, or a wolf carries some Nasu into the bed of the stream: what is the penalty that the man shall pay? 4. For defiling the earth and the water: 'If a man wants to irrigate a field, he must first look after the water-channel, whether there is dead matter in it or not. If the water, unknown to him, comes upon a corpse, there is no sin upon him. If he has not looked after the rivulet and the stream, he is unclean' (Saddar 75). 6. [Repeat st. 3.] 7. [Repeat st. 4.] IIa. 8. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Does water kill? Ahura Mazda answered: 'Water kills no man: Asto-vidhotu binds him, and, thus bound, Vayu carries him off; and the flood takes him up, the flood takes him down, the flood throws him ashore; then birds feed upon him. When he goes away, it is by the will of Fate he goes.' 5. Water and fire belong to the holy part of the world, and come from God; how then is it that they kill? 'Let a Gueber light a sacred fire for a hundred years, if he once fall into it, he shall be burnt.' Even the Mobeds, if we may trust Elisaeus, [ILLEGIBLE: text ends mid-sentence]
segments.translation:
Purity Laws, Part I.

Section 1. A man dies in the depths of a valley. A bird descends from the top of a mountain into the valley and feeds upon the corpse of the dead man there. It then flies back up from the valley to the mountaintop and alights upon one of the trees there, whether hardwood or softwood, and upon that tree it vomits and deposits its dung.

Section 2. Now, a man comes up from the valley to the mountaintop. He approaches the tree upon which the bird had perched; from that tree he intends to take wood for the sacred fire. He fells the tree, hews it, splits it into logs, and kindles the fire — that fire which is the son of Ahura Mazda. What penalty shall he incur?
[Footnote 1: For defiling the fire by introducing dead matter (Nasu) into it, contrary to the rule: 'Put only proper and carefully examined fuel into the fire.' See Vd. 7.25ff. for the purification of unclean wood: Vd. 7.28ff.]

Section 3. Ahura Mazda answered: 'There is no sin upon a man for any Nasu [corpse-defilement] that has been carried by dogs, birds, wolves, winds, or flies.'

Section 4. 'For if sin were to fall upon a man for any Nasu carried by dogs, birds, wolves, winds, or flies, how quickly would all this material world of mine become nothing but a host of Peshotanu — those bent on the destruction of righteousness, whose souls shall cry and wail after death — so numberless are the creatures that die upon the face of the earth.'
[Footnote 2: 'Peshotanu' means 'people guilty of a mortal sin' according to the Commentary. Cf. Yasna 53.9b.]
[Footnote 3: The crying and wailing occurs after death: 'When the soul, crying and beaten off, is driven far away from Paradise' (Commentary). This passage imitates the Gathas (Yasna 46.11c; 51.13b; cf. Vd. 13.8–9).]

Section 5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! A man irrigates a cornfield. The water flows down the field once, a second time, a third time; and on the fourth occasion, a dog, a fox, or a wolf carries some Nasu [corpse-matter] into the bed of the stream. What penalty shall the man incur?
[Footnote 4: For defiling the earth and water. 'If a man wishes to irrigate a field, he must first examine the water-channel to see whether there is dead matter in it or not. If the water reaches a corpse without his knowledge, there is no sin upon him. If he has not inspected the rivulet and the stream, he is unclean' (Saddar 75).]

Section 6. [Repeat Section 3.]

Section 7. [Repeat Section 4.]

Part IIa.

Section 8. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Does water kill? Ahura Mazda answered: 'Water kills no man. Asto-vidhotu [the demon of death] binds him, and, thus bound, Vayu [the divinity of wind/air] carries him off; the flood takes him downstream, the flood carries him up, the flood casts him ashore; then birds feed upon him. When he departs this life, it is by the will of Fate that he goes.'
[Footnote 5: Water and fire belong to the sacred portion of creation and proceed from God; how then can they kill? 'Let a Gueber [Zoroastrian] tend a sacred fire for a hundred years — if he once falls into it, he will be burned.' Even the Mobeds [Zoroastrian priests], if we may trust Elisaeus — [ILLEGIBLE: passage ends abruptly mid-sentence]]

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 6: Purity Laws Concerning Death Pollution of Ground
segments.original_text:
Purity laws I. 1. How long shall the piece of ground be fallow whereon dogs or men have died? Ahura Mazda answered: 'A year long shall the piece of ground be fallow whereon dogs or men have died, O holy Zarathustra!

2. 'A year long shall no worshipper of Mazda sow or water that piece of ground whereon dogs or men have died; he may sow as he likes the rest of the ground; he may water it as he likes.' [Cf. Vd7.45 seq.]

3. 'If within the year they shall sow or water the piece of ground whereon dogs or men have died, they are guilty of the sin of "burying the dead" towards the water, towards the earth, and towards the plants.' ['To the water which they pour out, to the earth which they plough, to the plants which they sow' (Comm.)]

4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If worshippers of Mazda shall sow or water, within the year, the piece of ground whereon dogs or men have died, what is the penalty that they shall pay?

5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are Peshotanus: two hundred stripes with the Aspahe-astra, two hundred stripes with the Sraosho-charana.' ['If they plough and sow it, one tanapuhr; if they pour water on it, one tanapuhr; if they plough, sow, and water it, two tanapuhrs' (Comm.)]

6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If worshippers of Mazda want to till that piece of ground again, to water it, to sow it, and to plough it, what shall they do? [Even when a year's space is past, the ground is not free ipso facto.]

7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall look on the ground for any bones, hair, dung, urine, or blood that may be there.'

8. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If they shall not look on the ground for any bones, hair, dung, urine, or blood that may be there, what is the penalty that they shall pay?

9. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are Peshotanus: two hundred stripes with the Aspahe-astra, two hundred stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

II. 10. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw on the ground a bone of a dead dog, or of a dead man, as large as the top joint of the little finger, and if grease or marrow flow from it on to the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

11. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty stripes with the Aspahe-astra, thirty stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

12. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw on the ground a bone of a dead dog, or of a dead man, as large as the top joint of the fore-finger, and if grease or marrow flow from it on to the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

13. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Fifty stripes with the Aspahe-astra, fifty stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

14. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw on the ground a bone of a dead dog, or of a dead man, as large as the top joint of the middle finger, and if grease or marrow flow from it on to the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

15. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Seventy stripes with the Aspahe-astra...'
segments.translation:
Purity Laws, Section I.

1. [Zarathustra asks:] How long shall a piece of ground remain fallow upon which dogs or men have died? Ahura Mazda answered: 'O holy Zarathustra, a piece of ground upon which dogs or men have died shall remain fallow for a full year.

2. 'For a full year, no worshipper of Mazda shall sow or irrigate that piece of ground upon which dogs or men have died; the remainder of the ground he may sow and irrigate as he pleases.' [Cross-reference: Vd7.45 seq.]

3. 'If within that year they shall sow or irrigate the piece of ground upon which dogs or men have died, they are guilty of the sin of "burying the dead" against the water, against the earth, and against the plants.' [Commentary explains: 'Against the water which they pour out, against the earth which they plough, against the plants which they sow.']

4. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If worshippers of Mazda shall sow or irrigate, within the year, a piece of ground upon which dogs or men have died, what penalty shall they pay?

5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are [guilty of the sin of] Peshotanu [a mortal sin]: two hundred stripes with the Aspahe-astra [horse-goad], two hundred stripes with the Sraosho-charana [discipline-rod of Sraosha].' [Commentary: 'If they plough and sow it — one tanapuhr [mortal sin]; if they pour water on it — one tanapuhr; if they plough, sow, and water it — two tanapuhrs.']

6. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If worshippers of Mazda wish to till that piece of ground once more — to irrigate it, to sow it, and to plough it — what shall they do? [Note: Even after a year has elapsed, the ground is not automatically free of pollution.]

7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall examine the ground for any bones, hair, dung, urine, or blood that may remain there.'

8. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If they shall fail to examine the ground for any bones, hair, dung, urine, or blood that may be there, what penalty shall they pay?

9. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They are Peshotanus: two hundred stripes with the Aspahe-astra, two hundred stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

Section II.

10. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw onto the ground a bone of a dead dog or of a dead man — a bone no larger than the topmost joint of the little finger — and if grease or marrow flows from it onto the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

11. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty stripes with the Aspahe-astra, thirty stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

12. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw onto the ground a bone of a dead dog or of a dead man — a bone no larger than the topmost joint of the forefinger — and if grease or marrow flows from it onto the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

13. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Fifty stripes with the Aspahe-astra, fifty stripes with the Sraosho-charana.'

14. [Zarathustra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man shall throw onto the ground a bone of a dead dog or of a dead man — a bone no larger than the topmost joint of the middle finger — and if grease or marrow flows from it onto the ground, what penalty shall he pay?

15. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Seventy stripes with the Aspahe-astra...' [Text ends here; passage continues beyond the provided excerpt.]

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 7: Purity Laws Concerning Death Pollution and the Druj Nasu
segments.original_text: Purity laws I. Notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! When a man dies, at what moment does the Druj Nasu rush upon him?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Directly after death, as soon as the soul has left the body, O Spitama Zarathushtra! the Druj Nasu comes and rushes upon him, from the regions of the north, in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail sticking out, droning without end, and like unto the foulest Khrafstras. 1. Hell lies in the north; see Vd2.10 n.; Vd3.7 n.; Vd19.1; Hadokht Nask, section 3.25; Bundahishn 15.19. 2. Khrafstra is a general denomination for noxious animals. [3. 'On him she stays until the dog has seen the corpse or eaten it up, or until the flesh-eating birds have taken flight towards it. When the dog has seen it or eaten it up, or when the flesh-eating birds have taken flight towards it, then the Druj Nasu rushes away to the regions of the north in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail sticking out, droning without end, and like unto the foulest Khrafstras.'] 3. Until the Sag-did has been performed (see Vd8.16 seq.) 4. The Sag-did may be performed by birds of prey as well as by dogs. The dog smites the Nasu when it brings its muzzle near to the dead, the bird (mountain hawk, sparrow (?), or eagle) when its shadow passes over the body (Comm. ad §2; cf. §29). §3 is from the Vendidad Sada. 4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If the man has been killed by a dog, or by a wolf, or by witchcraft, or by the artifices of hatred, or by falling down a precipice, or by the law, or by calumny, or by the noose, how long after death does the Druj Nasu come and rush upon the dead? 5. 'By poison' (Comm.) 6. Literally, 'by men;' that is to say, put to death by the community according to law (Comm.) 7. If he has been condemned unjustly. 8. If he has strangled himself. 5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'At the next watch after death, the Druj Nasu comes and rushes upon the dead, from the regions of the north, in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail sticking out, droning without end, and like unto the foulest Khrafstras.' 9. The day is divided into five watches or ratu. If the man dies a natural death, the Druj comes directly; if the death be violent and unlooked for, the Druj comes later (as the corruption does not set in so quickly). II. 6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If there be a number of men resting in the same place, on the same carpet, on the same pillows, be there two men near one another, or five, or fifty, or a hundred, close by one another; and of those people one happens to die; how many of them does the Druj Nasu envelope with corruption, infection, and pollution? 10. §§6-9 = Vd5.27-30. 7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'If the dead one be a priest, the Druj Nasu rushes forth, O Spitama Zarathushtra! she goes as far as the eleventh and [TEXT BREAKS OFF]'
segments.translation:
Purity Laws, Part I.

§1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! When a man dies, at what moment does the Druj Nasu [the Corpse Demoness] rush upon him?'

§2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Directly after death, as soon as the soul has left the body, O Spitama Zarathushtra! the Druj Nasu comes and rushes upon him from the regions of the north — for Hell lies in the north [cf. Vendidad 2.10, 3.7, 19.1; Hadokht Nask 3.25; Bundahishn 15.19] — in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail protruding, droning without end, and resembling the foulest of Khrafstras [Avestan: noxious or harmful animals, a general term for creatures considered impure or demonic].

§3. [From the Vendidad Sada:] Upon him she remains until a dog has seen the corpse or consumed it, or until flesh-eating birds have taken flight toward it. When the dog has seen it or eaten it, or when the flesh-eating birds have flown toward it, then the Druj Nasu retreats to the regions of the north in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail protruding, droning without end, and resembling the foulest of Khrafstras. [Note: This refers to the ritual known as Sag-did, 'dog-sight' (see Vendidad 8.16 and following). The Sag-did may be performed by birds of prey as well as by dogs. The dog dispels the Nasu by bringing its muzzle near the dead; a bird — whether mountain hawk, sparrow (?), or eagle — accomplishes the same when its shadow passes over the body (Commentary ad §2; cf. §29).]

§4. [Zarathushtra asked:] 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man has been killed by a dog, or by a wolf, or by witchcraft, or by the artifices of hatred [i.e., by poison, according to the Commentary], or by falling from a precipice, or by the law [i.e., put to death by the community according to law, literally "by men," per Commentary], or by calumny [i.e., if he has been unjustly condemned], or by the noose [i.e., if he has strangled himself] — how long after death does the Druj Nasu come and rush upon the dead?'

§5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'At the next watch after death [Note: The day is divided into five watches, or ratu. If a man dies a natural death, the Druj comes immediately; if the death is violent and unexpected, the Druj comes later, as physical corruption does not set in so quickly in such cases], the Druj Nasu comes and rushes upon the dead from the regions of the north, in the shape of a raging fly, with knees and tail protruding, droning without end, and resembling the foulest of Khrafstras.'

Part II [cf. Vendidad 5.27–30].

§6. [Zarathushtra asked:] 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a number of men are resting in the same place, on the same carpet, on the same pillows — whether two men near one another, or five, or fifty, or a hundred, lying close together — and one of those men happens to die, how many of them does the Druj Nasu envelop with corruption, infection, and pollution?'

§7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'If the dead one be a priest [athravan], the Druj Nasu rushes forth, O Spitama Zarathushtra! and goes as far as the eleventh and [TEXT BREAKS OFF — segment ends mid-sentence].'

segments.title_inferred: Funerary Purification Rites, Corpse Disposal, and Sacred Distances (Vendidad, Fargard 9)
segments.original_text:
Funerals and purification, unlawful sex

Notes:
1. If a dog or a man die under a hut of wood or a hut of felt, what shall the worshippers of Mazda do?
1. A movable shelter, by contradistinction to a fixed abode, something like the oba of the Tartars, one of those huts made of boards or felt and called thâruma by the Arabs, which served as pavilions for princes as well as tents for nomads.
2. That sort of abode, having only one room, can have no chamber for the dead (Vd5.10).

2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall search for a Dakhma, they shall look for a Dakhma all around. If they find it easier to remove the dead, they shall take out the dead, they shall let the house stand, and shall perfume it with Urvasna or Vohu-gaona, or Vohu-kereti, or Hadha-naepata, or any other sweet-smelling plant.

3. If there is a Dakhma in the proximity, they remove the corpse at once. If there is no Dakhma or the season prevents its access, they purify the hut first.
4. 'So, when a dog or a man dies, the first thing to do is to take the corpse out (from the house), and to purify the house, inside and outside, with perfumes burnt on the fire' (Comm.) See Vd11.4.
Urvâsna is the râsan plant, a sort of garlic; Vohû-gaona, Vohû-kereti, and Hadhâ-naêpata are respectively (according to Frâmjî) benzoin, aloe, and pomegranate.

3. 'If they find it easier to remove the house, they shall take away the house, they shall let the dead lie on the spot, and shall perfume the house with Urvasna, or Vohu-gaona, or Vohu-kereti, or Hadha-naepata, or any other sweet-smelling plant.'

II
