4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If in the house of a worshipper of Mazda a dog or a man happens to die, and it is raining, or snowing, or blowing, or it is dark, or the day is at its end, when flocks and men lose their way, what shall the worshippers of Mazda do?

5. 'No corpse must be taken to the Dakhma when rain is falling, or threatening. If one is overtaken by rain on the way, if there be a place to lay it down, they shall lay it down; if there be none, they must go on and take it to the Dakhma, they must not retrace their steps.... When arrived at the Dakhma, if they find it full of water, they may nevertheless lay down the corpse' (Comm.)
6. If it is the season of rain or snow. See Vd5.10 seq.

5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The place in that house whereof the ground is the cleanest and the driest, and the least passed through by flocks and herds, by the fire of Ahura Mazda, by the consecrated bundles of Baresma, and by the faithful;'

6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful?

7. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty paces from the fire; thirty paces from the water; thirty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma; three paces from the faithful;—

8. 'On that place they shall dig a grave.
segments.translation:
FUNERALS AND PURIFICATION (Vendidad, Fargard 9)

[Section I]

Question 1: If a dog or a man dies inside a wooden hut or a felt hut [i.e., a movable, single-room portable shelter — akin to the 'oba' of the Tartars, or the 'thâruma' tent used by Arabs, serving as both a prince's pavilion and a nomad's dwelling; such a structure, having only one room, cannot contain a separate chamber for the dead (cf. Vd. 5.10)] — what shall the worshippers of Mazda do?

Answer (Ahura Mazda): They shall seek out a Dakhma [Tower of Silence, Zoroastrian funerary exposure structure]; they shall search for a Dakhma in all directions. If they find it easier to remove the dead body, they shall take out the corpse, leave the house standing, and fumigate [purify] it with Urvasna, or Vohu-gaona, or Vohu-kereti, or Hadha-naepata, or any other fragrant [sweet-smelling] plant.

[Commentator's note:] If a Dakhma is nearby, the corpse is to be removed immediately. If no Dakhma exists or the season prevents access to one, the hut itself is purified first. "When a dog or a man dies, the first duty is to take the corpse out of the house and to purify the house, inside and outside, with perfumes burnt upon the fire." (Commentary; see also Vd. 11.4.)

[Botanical identifications per Frâmjî:] Urvâsna = the râsan plant, a species of garlic [Allium sativum or related Allium sp.]; Vohû-gaona = benzoin [Styrax benzoin or related Styrax sp.]; Vohû-kereti = aloe [Aloe vera or related Aloe sp.]; Hadhâ-naêpata = pomegranate [Punica granatum].

Alternate ruling: If they find it easier to remove the house [i.e., to dismantle or relocate the portable shelter] rather than the body, they shall take the house away, leave the corpse in place, and fumigate the house with Urvasna, or Vohu-gaona, or Vohu-kereti, or Hadha-naepata, or any other fragrant plant.

[Section II]

Question 4: O Creator of the material world, Most Holy One! If in the dwelling of a worshipper of Mazda a dog or a man happens to die, and at that time it is raining, or snowing, or stormy [blowing hard], or the night is dark, or the day is drawing to its close — the hour when both flocks and men lose their way — what then shall the worshippers of Mazda do?

[Commentator's note:] No corpse may be carried to the Dakhma while rain is falling or threatening. If those transporting the corpse are overtaken by rain on the road: if there is a suitable place to set the body down, they shall set it down; if there is no such place, they must proceed and deliver it to the Dakhma — they must not turn back. Upon arriving at the Dakhma, even if it is found to be filled with water, they may nonetheless lay down the corpse. [If it is the season of rain or snow, see Vd. 5.10 seq.]

Answer (Ahura Mazda): They shall choose the spot within that house where the ground is the cleanest and the driest, and where it is least frequented by flocks and herds, by the sacred fire of Ahura Mazda, by the consecrated bundles of Baresma [sacred ritual plant-bundles], and by the faithful [Zoroastrian believers].

Question 6: O Creator of the material world, Most Holy One! How far [must the temporary resting place of the corpse be] from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful?

Answer (Ahura Mazda): Thirty paces from the fire; thirty paces from the water; thirty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma; three paces from the faithful.

Further ruling (8): On that chosen spot they shall dig a grave [for temporary placement of the body, pending transfer to the Dakhma].

segments.title_inferred: The Nine Nights' Barashnum: Ritual Purification Ceremony (Vendidad, Fargard 11)
segments.original_text: The Nine Nights' Barashnum. 1a. Notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! To whom shall they apply here below, who want to cleanse their body defiled by the dead?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'To a pious man, O Spitama Zarathushtra! who knows how to speak, who speaks truth, who has learned the Holy Word [manthra], who is pious, and knows best the rites of cleansing according to the law of Mazda. That man shall fell the trees off the surface of the ground on a space of nine Vibazus square; 3. in that part of the ground where there is least water and where there are fewest trees, the part which is the cleanest and driest, and the least passed through by sheep and oxen, and by the fire of Ahura Mazda, by the consecrated bundles of Baresma, and by the faithful.' 4. How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful? 5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty paces from the fire, thirty paces from the water, thirty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma, three paces from the faithful. 6. Then thou shalt dig a hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. 7. Thou shalt dig a second hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a third hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a fourth hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a fifth hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a sixth hole, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come.' 8. How far from one another? 'One pace.' How much is the pace? 'As much as three feet.' 9. 'Then thou shalt dig three holes more, two fingers deep if the summer has come, four fingers deep if the winter and ice have come.' How far from the former six? 'Three paces.' What sort of paces? 'Such as are taken in walking.' How much are those (three) paces? 'As much as nine feet.' 10. 'Then thou shalt draw a furrow all around with a metal knife.' How far from the holes? 'Three paces.' What sort of paces? 'Such as are taken in walking.' How much are those (three) paces? 'As much as nine feet.' 11. 'Then thou shalt draw twelve furrows; three of which thou shalt draw to surround and divide [from the rest] (the [remainder of text cut off]).'
segments.translation: The Nine Nights' Barashnum [ritual purification]. Section 1a, with commentary notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! To whom shall those apply here below who wish to cleanse their body defiled by contact with the dead?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'To a pious man [i.e., a priest], O Spitama Zarathushtra — one who knows how to speak, who speaks truth, who has learned the Holy Word [manthra, the sacred liturgical formula], who is pious, and who knows best the rites of cleansing according to the law of Mazda. That man shall clear the trees from the surface of the ground over a space of nine Vibazus [nine ells, approximately; cf. Vd7.34] square. 3. This shall be in that part of the ground where there is the least water and the fewest trees — the part which is cleanest and driest, and least frequented by sheep and oxen, and [kept distant] from the fire of Ahura Mazda, from the consecrated bundles of Baresma [sacred twigs used in ritual], and from the faithful.' 4. How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful? 5. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thirty paces from the fire, thirty paces from the water, thirty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma, three paces from the faithful.' 6. 'Then thou shalt dig a hole [intended to receive liquid draining from the body being purified] — two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. [Note: In summer, the air and earth being dry, the hole may be shallower, since it is certain to remain empty enough to contain that liquid.] 7. Thou shalt dig a second hole, two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a third hole, two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a fourth hole, two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a fifth hole, two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come. Thou shalt dig a sixth hole [these six holes are to contain gomez, i.e., consecrated cattle urine; they must be dug from north to south, per the Commentary], two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come.' 8. How far from one another? 'One pace.' How much is a pace? 'As much as three feet.' 9. 'Then thou shalt dig three more holes [to contain water], two fingers deep if summer has come, four fingers deep if winter and ice have come.' How far from the previous six? 'Three paces.' What sort of paces? 'Such as are taken in walking.' How much are those three paces? 'As much as nine feet.' 10. 'Then thou shalt draw a furrow all around with a metal knife.' How far from the holes? 'Three paces.' What sort of paces? 'Such as are taken in walking.' How much are those three paces? 'As much as nine feet.' 11. 'Then thou shalt draw twelve furrows; three of which thou shalt draw to surround and divide [from the rest] (the [text continues beyond this segment]).'

segments.title_inferred: Formulaic recitations for ritual purification against corpse-defilement (Vendidad, Fargard 12)
segments.original_text: Formulas recited during the process of cleansing. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda! most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I fight against that Druj who from the dead rushes upon the living? How shall I fight against that Druj who from the dead defiles the living?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Say aloud those words in the Gathas that are to be said twice [Bish-âmrûta]. Say aloud those words in the Gathas that are to be said thrice [Thrish-âmrûta]. Say aloud those words in the Gathas that are to be said four times [Chathrush-âmrûta].' 3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which are those words in the Gathas that are to be said twice? 4. Ahura Mazda answered: 'These are the words in the Gathas that are to be said twice, and thou shalt twice say them aloud: ahya yasa ... urvanem (Y28.2). humatenam ... mahi (Y35.2), ashahya aad saire ... ahubya (Y35.8), yatha tu i ... ahura (Y39.4), humaim thwa ... hudaustema (Y41.3), thwoi staotaraska ... ahura (Y41.5). usta ahmai ... manangho (Y43.1), spenta mainyu ... ahuro (Y47.1), vohu khshathrem ... vareshane (Y51.1), vahista istis ... skyaothanaka (Y53.1).' [The Bish-âmrûta are the opening stanzas of the five Gathas and five stanzas in the Yasna Haptanghaiti.] 5. 'And after thou hast twice said those Bis-amrutas, thou shalt say aloud these victorious, most healing words: "I drive away Angra Mainyu from this house, from this borough, from this town, from this land; from the very body of the man defiled by the dead, from the very body of the woman defiled by the dead; from the master of the house, from the lord of the borough, from the lord of the town, from the lord of the land; from the whole of the world of Righteousness. 6. I drive away the Nasu, I drive away direct defilement, I drive away indirect defilement, from this house, from this borough, from this town, from this land; from the very body of the man defiled by the dead, from the very body of the woman defiled by the dead; from the master of the house, from the lord of the borough, from the lord of the town, from the lord of the land; from the whole of the world of Righteousness."' 7. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which are those words in the Gathas that are to be said thrice? 8. Ahura Mazda answered: 'These are the words in the Gathas that are to be said thrice, and thou shalt thrice say them aloud: ashem vohu ... (Y27.14), ye sevisto ... paiti (Y33.11), hukhshathrotemai ... vahistai (Y35.5), duzvarenais ... vahyo (Y53.9).' 9. 'After thou hast thrice said those Thris-amrutas, thou shalt say aloud these victorious, most healing words: "I drive away Indra, I drive away Sauru, I drive away the daeva Naunghaithya, from this...[text breaks off]"'
segments.translation:
Formulas recited during the process of ritual cleansing.

1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda! most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I fight against that Druj [Demon of Corruption] who from the dead rushes upon the living? How shall I fight against that Druj who from the dead defiles the living?'

2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Recite aloud those passages of the Gathas [sacred hymns] that are to be said twice [called the Bish-âmrûta, "twice-uttered"]. Recite aloud those passages of the Gathas that are to be said thrice [the Thrish-âmrûta, "thrice-uttered"]. Recite aloud those passages of the Gathas that are to be said four times [the Chathrush-âmrûta, "four-times-uttered"].'

3. 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which are those passages of the Gathas that are to be said twice?'

4. Ahura Mazda answered: 'These are the passages of the Gathas to be said twice, and thou shalt recite them aloud twice: ahya yasa ... urvanem (Yasna 28.2); humatenam ... mahi (Y35.2), ashahya aad saire ... ahubya (Y35.8), yatha tu i ... ahura (Y39.4), humaim thwa ... hudaustema (Y41.3), thwoi staotaraska ... ahura (Y41.5); usta ahmai ... manangho (Y43.1), spenta mainyu ... ahuro (Y47.1), vohu khshathrem ... vareshane (Y51.1), vahista istis ... skyaothanaka (Y53.1).' [Note: The Bish-âmrûta are the opening stanzas of each of the five Gathas together with five stanzas of the Yasna Haptanghaiti.]

5. 'And after thou hast twice recited those Bis-amrutas, thou shalt proclaim aloud these victorious, supremely healing words: "I drive away Angra Mainyu [the Destructive Spirit, chief of demons] from this house, from this borough [village], from this town, from this land; from the very body of the man defiled by the dead, from the very body of the woman defiled by the dead; from the master of the house, from the lord of the borough, from the lord of the town, from the lord of the land; from the whole of the world of Righteousness.

6. I drive away the Nasu [the demon of corpse-defilement], I drive away direct defilement, I drive away indirect defilement, from this house, from this borough, from this town, from this land; from the very body of the man defiled by the dead, from the very body of the woman defiled by the dead; from the master of the house, from the lord of the borough, from the lord of the town, from the lord of the land; from the whole of the world of Righteousness."'

7. 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which are those passages of the Gathas that are to be said thrice?'

8. Ahura Mazda answered: 'These are the passages of the Gathas to be said thrice, and thou shalt recite them aloud three times: ashem vohu ... (Y27.14), ye sevisto ... paiti (Y33.11), hukhshathrotemai ... vahistai (Y35.5), duzvarenais ... vahyo (Y53.9).'

9. 'After thou hast thrice recited those Thris-amrutas, thou shalt proclaim aloud these victorious, supremely healing words: "I drive away Indra [the daeva], I drive away Sauru [the daeva], I drive away the daeva Naunghaithya, from this... [text breaks off]"'

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 13: Special Formulas for Cleansing Persons, Animals, and Natural Elements
segments.original_text: Special formulas for cleansing several objects. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda! most beneficent spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? how the fire? how the water? how the earth? how the cow? how the tree? how the faithful man and the faithful woman? how the stars? how the moon? how the sun? how the boundless light? how all good things, made by Mazda, the offspring of the holy principle?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thou shalt chant the cleansing words, and the house shall be clean; clean shall be the fire, clean the water, clean the earth, clean the cow, clean the tree, clean the faithful man and the faithful woman, clean the stars, clean the moon, clean the sun, clean the boundless light, clean all good things, made by Mazda, the offspring of the holy principle. 3. [So thou shalt say these victorious, most healing words]; thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya five times: "The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness," &c. 'The Ahuna-Vairya preserves the person of man: '"Yatha ahu vairyo: - The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness," &c. '"Kem-na mazda: - What protector hast thou given unto me, O Mazda! while the hate of the wicked encompasses me?" &c. '"Ke verethrem-ja: - Who is the victorious who will protect thy teaching?" &c. '"Keep us from our hater, O Mazda and Armaiti Spenta!" &c. [Note 1: As in Vd. 8.19, 20.] 4. 'If thou wantest to cleanse the house, say these words aloud: "As long as the sickness lasts my great protector [is he who teaches virtue to the perverse]." [Note 2: Yasna 49.1. The allusion is not quite clear. This line was recited by the Genius of the sky at the moment when Ahriman was invading the sky (Gr. Bd.). Perhaps the small house of man is compared here with that large house, the world.] 'If thou wantest to cleanse the fire, say these words aloud: "Thy fire, first of all, do we approach with worship, O Ahura Mazda!" [Note 3: Yasna 36.1.] 5. 'If thou wantest to cleanse the water, say these words aloud: "Waters we worship, the Maekainti waters, the Hebvainti waters, the Fravazah waters." [Note 4: Yasna 38.3.] 'If thou wantest to cleanse the earth, say these words aloud: "This earth we worship, this earth with the women, this earth which bears us and those women who are thine, O Ahura!" [Note 5: Yasna 38.1. 'Who are thine,' that is, 'who are thy wives.'] 6. 'If thou wantest to cleanse the cow, say these words aloud: "The best of all works we will fulfil while we order both the learned and the unlearned, both masters and servants to secure for the cattle a good resting-place and fodder." [Note 6: Yasna 35.4. 'Let those excellent deeds be done for the behoof of cattle, that is to say, let stables be made, and water...'] 'If thou wantest to cleanse the trees, say these words aloud: "For him, as a reward, Mazda made the plants grow up."
segments.translation: Special Formulas for Cleansing Several Objects. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda! Most beneficent spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How the fire? How the water? How the earth? How the cow? How the tree? How the faithful man and the faithful woman? How the stars? How the moon? How the sun? How the boundless light? How all good things made by Mazda, the offspring of the holy principle?' 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thou shalt chant the cleansing words, and the house shall be clean; clean shall be the fire, clean the water, clean the earth, clean the cow, clean the tree, clean the faithful man and the faithful woman, clean the stars, clean the moon, clean the sun, clean the boundless light, clean all good things made by Mazda, the offspring of the holy principle. 3. [So thou shalt say these victorious, most healing words]; thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya five times: "The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness," etc. The Ahuna-Vairya preserves the person of man: "Yatha ahu vairyo: The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness," etc. "Kem-na mazda: What protector hast thou given unto me, O Mazda, while the hate of the wicked encompasses me?" etc. "Ke verethrem-ja: Who is the victorious one who will protect thy teaching?" etc. "Keep us from our hater, O Mazda and Armaiti Spenta!" etc. [As in Vendidad 8.19–20.] 4. If thou wouldst cleanse the house, say these words aloud: "As long as the sickness lasts, my great protector is he who teaches virtue to the perverse." [Yasna 49.1. The allusion is not entirely clear. This line was recited by the Genius of the sky at the moment when Ahriman was invading the sky (Greater Bundahishn). Perhaps the small house of man is here compared to that great house, the world.] If thou wouldst cleanse the fire, say these words aloud: "Thy fire, first of all, do we approach with worship, O Ahura Mazda!" [Yasna 36.1.] 5. If thou wouldst cleanse the water, say these words aloud: "Waters we worship — the Maekainti waters, the Hebvainti waters, the Fravazah waters." [Yasna 38.3.] If thou wouldst cleanse the earth, say these words aloud: "This earth we worship — this earth with the women; this earth which bears us and those women who are thine, O Ahura!" [Yasna 38.1. 'Who are thine' means 'who are thy wives.'] 6. If thou wouldst cleanse the cow, say these words aloud: "The best of all works we will fulfil while we direct both the learned and the unlearned, both masters and servants, to secure for the cattle a good resting-place and fodder." [Yasna 35.4. Note: Let those excellent deeds be done for the benefit of cattle — that is, let stables be built and water provided.] If thou wouldst cleanse the trees, say these words aloud: "For him, as a reward, Mazda made the plants grow up."

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 14: The Upaman — Mourning Periods and Purification Rites After Death
segments.original_text: The Upaman, how long it lasts for different relatives. 1. If one's father or mother dies, how long shall they stay [in mourning], the son for his father, the daughter for her mother? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners? Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall stay thirty days for the righteous, sixty days for the sinners.' Notes: 1. How long if the dead person died in a state of holiness (a dahma)? How long if in the state of a Peshotanu [mortal sin]? 2. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be clean again? Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times, you shall wash your clothes three times, you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire, you shall bind the bundles of Baresma, you shall bring libations to the good waters; then the house shall be clean, and then the waters may enter, then the fire may enter, and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!' 2. This refers probably to the sacrifice that is offered on each of the three days that follow the death of a Zoroastrian for the salvation of his soul. 3. All the other objects over which the Amesha-Spentas preside (such as the cow, the metals, &c.) 3. If one's son or daughter dies, how long shall they stay, the father for his son, the mother for her daughter? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners? Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall stay thirty days for the righteous, sixty days for the sinners.' 4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be clean again? Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times, you shall wash your clothes three times, you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire, you shall bind up the bundles of Baresma, you shall bring libations to the good waters; then the house shall be clean, and then the waters may enter, then the fire may enter, and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!' 5. If one's brother or sister dies, how long shall they stay, the brother for his brother, the sister for her sister? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners? Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall stay thirty days for the righteous, sixty days for the sinners.' 6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be clean again? Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times, you shall wash your clothes three times, you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire, you shall bind up the bundles of Baresma, you shall bring libations to the good waters; then the house shall be clean, and then the waters may enter, then the fire may enter, and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!' 7. If the master of the house dies, or if the mistress of the house dies, how long shall they stay? How long for the righteo[us? — text truncated]
segments.translation:
The Upaman [mourning period], and how long it lasts for different relatives.

§1. If one's father or mother dies, how long shall they [the survivors] remain [in mourning] — the son for his father, the daughter for her mother? How long [if the deceased was] righteous? How long [if the deceased was] a sinner?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall remain thirty days for the righteous, and sixty days for the sinners.'

[Annotator's note 1: 'How long if the dead person died in a state of holiness (a dahma [state of ritual purity])? How long if in the state of a Peshotanu [one guilty of mortal sin]?']

§2. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be made clean again?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times; you shall wash your clothes three times; you shall chant the Gathas [sacred hymns] three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire; you shall bind the bundles of Baresma [sacred barsom twigs]; you shall bring libations to the good waters. Then the house shall be clean; then the waters may enter; then the fire may enter; and then the Amesha-Spentas [Holy Immortals] may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!'

[Annotator's note 2: This refers probably to the sacrifice offered on each of the three days following the death of a Zoroastrian, for the salvation of his soul.]

[Annotator's note 3: 'All the other objects over which the Amesha-Spentas preside (such as the cow, the metals, etc.).']

§3. If one's son or daughter dies, how long shall they remain — the father for his son, the mother for her daughter? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall remain thirty days for the righteous, and sixty days for the sinners.'

§4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be made clean again?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times; you shall wash your clothes three times; you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire; you shall bind up the bundles of Baresma; you shall bring libations to the good waters. Then the house shall be clean; then the waters may enter; then the fire may enter; and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!'

§5. If one's brother or sister dies, how long shall they remain — the brother for his brother, the sister for her sister? How long for the righteous? How long for the sinners?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall remain thirty days for the righteous, and sixty days for the sinners.'

§6. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How shall I cleanse the house? How shall it be made clean again?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'You shall wash your bodies three times; you shall wash your clothes three times; you shall chant the Gathas three times; you shall offer up a sacrifice to my Fire; you shall bind up the bundles of Baresma; you shall bring libations to the good waters. Then the house shall be clean; then the waters may enter; then the fire may enter; and then the Amesha-Spentas may enter, O Spitama Zarathushtra!'

§7. If the master of the house dies, or if the mistress of the house dies, how long shall they remain? How long for the righteo[us? — text truncated]

segments.title_inferred: The Hedgehog and Tortoise in Zoroastrian Law: Sacred and Accursed Creatures (Vendidad, Fargard 15)
segments.original_text: The dog. Ia. Notes: 1. Which is the good creature among the creatures of the Good Spirit that from midnight till the sun is up goes and kills thousands of the creatures of the Evil Spirit? 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The dog with the prickly back, with the long and thin muzzle, the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka; this is the good creature among the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu] that from midnight till the sun is up goes and kills thousands of the creatures of the Evil Spirit.' 1. The hedgehog, according to the Bund. 19.28, is created in opposition to the ant that carries off grain, as it says that the hedgehog, every time that it voids urine into an ant's nest, will destroy a thousand ants (Bund. 19.28; cf. Saddar 57). When the Arabs conquered Saistan, the inhabitants submitted on the condition that hedgehogs should not be killed nor hunted for, as they got rid of the vipers which swarm in that country. Every house had its hedgehog (Yaqout, Dictionnaire de la Perse, p. 303). Plutarch counts the hedgehog amongst the animals sacred to the Magi (Quaestiones Conviviales, IV, 5, 2). 2. Dusaka is the popular name of the hedgehog (Pers. zuza). It is not without importance which name is given to a being: 'When called by its high name, it is powerful' (Comm.); cf. § 6, and Vd18.15. 3. 'And whosoever, O Zarathushtra! shall kill the dog with the prickly back, with the long and thin muzzle, the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka, kills his own soul for nine generations, nor shall he find a way over the Chinwad bridge, unless he has, while alive, atoned for his sin.' 3. The bridge leading to Paradise; see Vd19.30. 4. Cf. § 54. Framji translates: 'He cannot atone for it in his life even by performing a sacrifice to Sraosha' (cf. Vd9.56, text and note). 4. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man kill the dog with the prickly back, with the long and thin muzzle, the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka, what is the penalty that he shall pay? Ahura Mazda answered: 'A thousand stripes with the Aspahe-astra, a thousand stripes with the Sraosho-charana.' Ib. 5. Which is the evil creature among the creatures of the Evil Spirit that from midnight till the sun is up goes and kills thousands of the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu]? 6. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The daeva Zairimyangura, which evil-speaking people call the Zairimyaka, this is the evil creature among the creatures of the Evil Spirit that from midnight till the sun is up goes and kills thousands of the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu].' 5. The tortoise (Framji and Rivayats). 6. 'When not so called it is less strong' (Comm.) Zairimyaka is a lucky name, and means, as it seems, who lives in verdure; Zairimyangura seems to mean 'the verdure-devourer.' 7. 'And whosoever, O Zarathushtra! shall kill the daeva Zairimya[ngura]...'
segments.translation: The Dog [hedgehog]. Section Ia. Notes: 1. [Zarathushtra asks:] Which is the good creature among the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu] that, from midnight until sunrise, goes out and kills thousands of the creatures of the Evil Spirit? 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The dog with the prickly back and the long, thin muzzle — the dog Vanghapara [i.e., the hedgehog], which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka — this is the good creature among the creatures of the Good Spirit that from midnight until sunrise goes out and kills thousands of the creatures of the Evil Spirit.' [Translator's note 1:] The hedgehog. According to the Bundahishn 19.28, the hedgehog was created in opposition to the ant that carries off grain; that text states that the hedgehog, every time it voids urine into an ant's nest, will destroy a thousand ants (Bund. 19.28; cf. Saddar 57). When the Arabs conquered Saistan [Sistan], the inhabitants submitted on the condition that hedgehogs should not be killed or hunted, since these animals rid the country of the vipers that swarm there. Every household kept its own hedgehog (Yaqout, Dictionnaire de la Perse, p. 303). Plutarch numbers the hedgehog among the animals sacred to the Magi (Quaestiones Conviviales, IV, 5, 2). [Translator's note 2:] 'Duzaka' [also spelled Dusaka] is the common popular name of the hedgehog (Persian: zuza). The name given to a being is not without consequence: 'When called by its high name, it is powerful' (Commentary); cf. § 6 and Vd18.15. 3. [The text continues:] 'And whosoever, O Zarathushtra, shall kill the dog with the prickly back and the long, thin muzzle — the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka — kills his own soul for nine generations, and shall find no passage over the Chinwad bridge [the bridge leading to Paradise; see Vd19.30], unless he has made atonement for his sin during his lifetime.' [Translator's note 4:] Cf. § 54. Framji translates: 'He cannot atone for it in his lifetime even by performing a sacrifice to Sraosha' (cf. Vd9.56, text and note). 4. [Zarathushtra asks:] O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If a man kills the dog with the prickly back and the long, thin muzzle — the dog Vanghapara, which evil-speaking people call the Duzaka — what penalty shall he pay? Ahura Mazda answered: 'A thousand stripes with the Aspahe-astra [horse-whip], and a thousand stripes with the Sraosho-charana [the discipline of Sraosha, i.e., the religious scourge].' Section Ib. 5. [Zarathushtra asks:] Which is the evil creature among the creatures of the Evil Spirit that, from midnight until sunrise, goes out and kills thousands of the creatures of the Good Spirit [Spenta Mainyu]? 6. Ahura Mazda answered: 'The daeva [demon] Zairimyangura — which evil-speaking people call the Zairimyaka — this is the evil creature among the creatures of the Evil Spirit that from midnight until sunrise goes out and kills thousands of the creatures of the Good Spirit.' [Translator's note 5:] The tortoise (identified as such by Framji and in the Rivayats). [Translator's note 6:] 'When not called by that name, it is less powerful' (Commentary). Zairimyaka is an auspicious name meaning, apparently, 'one who dwells in verdure'; Zairimyangura appears to mean 'the verdure-devourer.' 7. [The text continues:] 'And whosoever, O Zarathushtra, shall kill the daeva Zairimya[ngura]...' [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Atonement for the killing of a water-dog (otter): penalties and ritual obligations
segments.original_text: Atoning for the murder of a water-dog. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! He who smites one of those water-dogs that are born one from a thousand dogs and a thousand she-dogs, so that he gives up the ghost and the soul parts from the body, what is the penalty that he shall pay?' Notes: 1. See preceding Fargard § 51. 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'He shall pay ten thousand stripes with the Aspahe-astra, ten thousand stripes with the Sraosho-charana. He shall godly and piously bring unto the fire of Ahura Mazda ten thousand loads of hard, well dried, well examined wood, to redeem his own soul. 2. He shall pay 50 tanapuhrs (= 15,000 istirs = 60,000 dirhems). If he can afford it, he will alone in the manner stated in the Avesta; if he cannot afford it, it will be sufficient to perform a complete Izashne [Yasna] (sacrifice).' (Comm.) 3. To the altar of the Warharan fire. 4. It is forbidden to take any ill-smelling thing to the fire and to kindle it thereon; it is forbidden to kindle green wood, and even though the wood were hard and dry, one must examine it three times, lest there may be any hair or any unclean matter upon it. (Gr. Riv.) Although the pious Arda Viraf had always taken the utmost care never to put on the fire any wood but such as was seven years old, yet, when he entered Paradise, Atar, the genius of fire, showed him reproachfully a large tank full of the water which that wood had exuded (see Arda Viraf 10). 3. 'He shall godly and piously bring unto the fire of Ahura Mazda ten thousand loads of soft wood, of Urvasna, Vohu-gaona, Vohu-kereti, Hadha-naepata, or any sweet-scented plant, to redeem his own soul.' 5. See above, Vd8.2, note 4. 4. 'He shall godly and piously tie ten thousand bundles of Baresma, to redeem his own soul. He shall offer up to the Good Waters ten thousand Zaothra libations with the Haoma and the milk, cleanly prepared and well strained, cleanly prepared and well strained by a pious man, and mixed with the roots of the tree known as Hadha-naepata, to redeem his own soul. 5. He shall kill ten thousand snakes of those that go upon the belly. He shall kill ten thousand Kahrpus, who are snakes with the shape of a dog. He shall kill ten thousand tortoises. He shall kill ten thousand land-frogs; he shall kill ten thousand water-frogs. He shall kill ten thousand corn-carrying ants; he shall kill ten thousand ants of the small, venomous mischievous kind.' 6. 'Mar banak snakes: they are dog-like, because they sit on their hindparts.' (Comm.) The cat (gurba = Kahrpu) seems to be the animal intended. In a paraphrase of this passage in a Parsi Rivayat, the cat is numbered amongst the Khrafstras which it is enjoined to kill to redeem a sin (India Office Library, VIII, 13); cf. G. du Chinozi, p. 462: 'Les animaux que les Gaures ont en horreur sont les serpents, les couleuvres, les lezars, et aut[res].'
segments.translation: Atonement for the killing of a water-dog [otter]. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! He who strikes and kills one of those water-dogs that are born but one in a thousand [ordinary] dogs and a thousand she-dogs, so that it gives up the ghost and the soul departs from the body — what penalty shall he pay?' [Note 1: See the preceding Fargard, §51.] 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'He shall receive ten thousand stripes with the Aspahe-astra [horse-whip], ten thousand stripes with the Sraosho-charana [the instrument of penance associated with the angel Sraosha]. He shall, in a godly and pious manner, bring unto the sacred fire of Ahura Mazda ten thousand loads of hard, well-dried, carefully examined wood, to redeem his own soul. [Note 2:] He shall pay fifty tanapuhrs [a major unit of religious penalty] (equivalent to 15,000 istirs, or 60,000 dirhems). If he has the means, he shall perform the atonement alone in the manner prescribed in the Avesta; if he lacks the means, it shall suffice to perform a complete Izashne [Yasna] (sacrifice).' (Commentary.) [Note 3:] This offering is made to the altar of the Warharan [Bahram] fire. [Note 4:] It is forbidden to bring any ill-smelling substance to the sacred fire or to kindle it thereon; it is forbidden to kindle green wood; and even if the wood is hard and dry, it must be examined three times lest any hair or unclean matter adhere to it. (Greater Rivayat.) Although the pious Arda Viraf had always taken the greatest care never to place upon the fire any wood less than seven years old, yet when he entered Paradise, Atar — the divine genius of fire — showed him reproachfully a great tank full of the moisture that had exuded from that wood (see Arda Viraf, chapter 10). 3. 'He shall, in a godly and pious manner, bring unto the fire of Ahura Mazda ten thousand loads of soft aromatic wood — of Urvasna [a sweet-scented shrub], Vohu-gaona [lit. 'of good colour'], Vohu-kereti [lit. 'of good cutting'], Hadha-naepata [a plant of the Ephedra or related genus], or any other sweet-scented plant — to redeem his own soul.' [Note 5: See above, Vendidad 8.2, note 4.] 4. 'He shall, in a godly and pious manner, tie ten thousand bundles of Baresma [barsom, the sacred bundle of twigs used in ritual], to redeem his own soul. He shall offer up to the Good Waters ten thousand Zaothra [libation] offerings composed of Haoma [the sacred plant beverage] and milk, cleanly prepared and well strained by a pious man, and mixed with the roots of the plant known as Hadha-naepata, to redeem his own soul. 5. He shall kill ten thousand snakes of those that go upon the belly. He shall kill ten thousand Kahrpus — creatures described as snakes having the form of a dog. He shall kill ten thousand tortoises. He shall kill ten thousand land-frogs; he shall kill ten thousand water-frogs. He shall kill ten thousand grain-carrying ants; he shall kill ten thousand ants of the small, venomous, mischievous kind.' [Note 6:] 'Mar banak snakes: they are called dog-like because they sit up on their haunches.' (Commentary.) The cat (gurba = Kahrpu) seems to be the animal actually intended here. In a paraphrase of this passage in a Parsi Rivayat, the cat is listed among the Khrafstras [noxious creatures] that one is enjoined to kill in order to atone for a sin (India Office Library, MS VIII, 13); cf. G. du Chinozi, p. 462: 'The animals which the Zoroastrians [Guebres] hold in abhorrence are serpents, adders, lizards, and oth[ers].'

segments.title_inferred: Five Sins Rendering a Man Peshotanu: Dogs, Women, and Sexual Transgressions (Vendidad, Fargard 17)
segments.original_text: Regarding certain sins and obligations. I. Notes: 1. How many are the sins that men commit and that, being committed and not confessed, nor atoned for, make their committer a Peshotanu? [Note 1: That is to say: he shall receive two hundred strokes with the Aspahe-ashtra or the Sraosho-charana; or pay three hundred istirs.] 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'There are five such sins, O holy Zarathushtra! It is the first of these sins that men commit when a man teaches one of the faithful another faith, another law, a lower doctrine, and he leads him astray with a full knowledge and conscience of the sin: the man who has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 2: The Commentary has, 'that is, a creed that is not ours.'] 3. 'It is the second of these sins when a man gives bones too hard or food too hot to a shepherd's dog or to a house-dog; 4. 'If the bones stick in the dog's teeth or stop in his throat; or if the food too hot burn his mouth or his tongue, he may come to grief thereby; if he come to grief thereby, the man who has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 3: He who gives too hot food to a dog so as to burn his throat is margarzan (guilty of death); he who gives bones to a dog so as to tear his throat is margarzan (Gr. Riv. 639).] 5. 'It is the third of these sins when a man smites a bitch big with young or affrights her by running after her, or shouting or clapping with the hands; 6. 'If the bitch fall into a hole, or a well, or a precipice, or a river, or a canal, she may come to grief thereby; if she come to grief thereby, the man who has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 4: If a bitch is big with young [pregnant] and a man shouts or throws stones at her, so that the whelps come to mischief and die, he is margarzan (Gr. Riv. 639).] 7. 'It is the fourth of these sins when a man has intercourse with a woman who has the whites or sees the blood, the man that has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 5: i.e. during menstruation. See Vd16.14 seq.] 8. 'It is the fifth of these sins when a man has intercourse with a woman quick with child, whether the milk has already come to her breasts or has not yet come: she may come to grief thereby; if she come to grief thereby, the man who has done the deed becomes a Peshotanu. [Note 6: When she has been pregnant for four months and ten days, as it is then that the child is formed and a soul is added to its body (Anquetil II, 563). Note 7: Or better: 'if the child die.' 'If a man come to his wife [during her pregnancy] so that she is injured and bring forth a still-born child, he is margarzan' (Old Riv. 115b).] IIa. 9. 'If a man come near unto a damsel, either dependent on the chief of the family or not dependent, either delivered [unto a husband] or not delivered, and she conceives by him, let her not, being ashamed of the people, produce in herself the menses, against the course of nature, by means of water and plants. [Note 8: Whether she has a husband in the house of her own parents or has none; wheth-]
segments.translation:
Concerning certain sins and their obligatory penalties. Section I. Question: How many are the sins that men commit which, if committed and neither confessed nor atoned for, render their perpetrator a Peshotanu [a great sinner subject to severe corporal or financial punishment: two hundred strokes with the Aspahe-ashtra [horse-goad] or the Sraosho-charana [instrument of discipline associated with Sraosha], or a fine of three hundred istirs [a unit of currency]]?

Ahura Mazda answered: 'There are five such sins, O holy Zarathushtra!

'The first of these sins is committed when a man teaches one of the faithful another faith, another law, a lower doctrine, and leads him astray with full knowledge and deliberate intention [lit. 'with a full knowledge and conscience of the sin']: the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [The Commentary explains: 'that is, a creed that is not ours.']

'The second of these sins is committed when a man gives bones too hard or food too hot to a shepherd's dog or to a house-dog. If the bones stick in the dog's teeth or lodge in his throat, or if the overly hot food burns his mouth or tongue, he may come to harm thereby; if he does come to harm, the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [Commentary: He who gives a dog food so hot as to burn his throat is margarzan [guilty of a capital offense]; he who gives bones to a dog so as to tear his throat is likewise margarzan (Gr. Riv. 639).]

'The third of these sins is committed when a man strikes a pregnant bitch [a female dog with young] or frightens her by chasing her, or by shouting, or by clapping his hands. If the bitch falls into a hole, a well, a precipice, a river, or a canal, she may come to harm thereby; if she does come to harm, the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [Commentary: If a pregnant bitch is shouted at or has stones thrown at her so that the whelps suffer injury and die, the offender is margarzan (Gr. Riv. 639).]

'The fourth of these sins is committed when a man has sexual intercourse with a woman who has a vaginal discharge [lit. 'the whites'] or is menstruating [lit. 'sees the blood']; the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [See Vendidad 16:14 and following for elaboration.]

'The fifth of these sins is committed when a man has intercourse with a woman who is quick with child [pregnant], whether her milk has already come to her breasts or has not yet come: she may come to harm thereby; if she does come to harm [or better: if the child should die], the man who has done this deed becomes a Peshotanu.' [Commentary: This applies when the woman has been pregnant for four months and ten days, at which point the child is considered formed and a soul has been added to its body (Anquetil II, 563). 'If a man come to his wife during her pregnancy so that she is injured and delivers a stillborn child, he is margarzan' (Old Riv. 115b).]

Section IIa. 'If a man has relations with a young woman—whether she is under the authority of the head of her family or not, whether she has been given in marriage or not—and she conceives by him, she must not, out of shame before the people, attempt to bring on her menses contrary to nature by means of water or plants [i.e., she must not attempt to procure an abortion using herbal or hydro-therapeutic means].' [Note: Whether she has a husband in the household of her own parents or has none; wheth- (text breaks off here)]

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 18: Purity Laws Concerning Menstruation and Ritual Isolation
segments.original_text: Purity laws regarding menstruation I. Notes: 1. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If there be in the house of a worshipper of Mazda a woman who has the whites or sees blood, what shall the worshippers of Mazda do? 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall clear the way of the wood there, both plants and trees; they shall strew dry dust on the ground; and they shall isolate a half, or a third, or a fourth, or a fifth part of the house, lest her look should fall upon the fire.' 1. The way of the Dashtanistan. 2. Lest the wood shall be touched and defiled by the woman on her way to the Dashtanistan. 3. Lest the earth shall be touched and defiled by her. Cf. Farg. 9.11. 4. Nowadays a room on the ground-floor is reserved for that use. 3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma? How far from the faithful? 4. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Fifteen paces from the fire, fifteen paces from the water, fifteen paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma, three paces from the faithful.' 5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from her shall he stay, who brings food to a woman who has the whites or sees the blood? 6. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Three paces from her shall he stay, who brings food to a woman who has the whites or sees the blood.' In what kind of vessels shall he bring her bread? In what kind of vessels shall he bring her barley-drink? 'In vessels of iron, or of lead, or of any common metal.' 5. The food is held out to her from a distance in a metal spoon. 5b. Dar: brass. -JHP 6. Earthen vessels, when defiled, cannot be made clean; but metal vessels can. See Farg. 7.73 seq. 7. How much bread shall he bring to her? How much barley-drink shall he bring? 'Two danares of dry bread, and one danare of liquor, lest she should get too weak. 'If a child has just touched her, they shall first wash his hands and then his body. 7. A danare is, according to Anquetil, as much as four tolas; a tola is from 105 to 175 grains. 8. 'Soshyos says: For three nights cooked meat is not allowed to her, lest the issue shall grow stronger. 9. A child whom she suckles. The meaning is, Even a child, if he has touched her, must undergo the rites of cleansing. The general rule is given in the Commentary: 'Whoever has touched a Dashtan woman must wash his body and his clothes with gomez and water.' The ceremony in question is the simple Ghosel, not the Barashnum, since the woman herself performs the former only (see below, § 11 seq.) II. 8. 'If she still see blood after three nights have passed, she shall sit in the place of infirmity until four nights have passed. 'If she still see blood after four nights have passed, she shall sit in the place of infirmity until five nights have passed. 9. 'If she still see blood after five nights have passed, she shall sit in the place of infirmity until six nights have passed.'
segments.translation:
Purity Laws Concerning Menstruation, Part I.

[Question] 1. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! If there be in the house of a worshipper of Mazda a woman who has the whites [leucorrhoea] or sees blood [menstrual bleeding], what shall the worshippers of Mazda do?

[Answer] 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'They shall clear the way of the wood there — both plants and trees — they shall strew dry dust upon the ground, and they shall isolate a half, or a third, or a fourth, or a fifth part of the house, lest her gaze should fall upon the fire.'
[Note 1: 'The way of the Dashtanistan' — i.e., the prescribed path to the place of menstrual seclusion.]
[Note 2: Lest the wood be touched and defiled by the woman on her way to the Dashtanistan.]
[Note 3: Lest the earth be touched and defiled by her. Cf. Fargard 9.11.]
[Note 4: In modern practice, a ground-floor room is reserved for this purpose.]

[Question] 3. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from the fire? How far from the water? How far from the consecrated bundles of Baresma [sacred ritual twigs]? How far from the faithful?

[Answer] 4. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Fifteen paces from the fire, fifteen paces from the water, fifteen paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma, three paces from the faithful.'

[Question] 5. O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! How far from her shall he remain, who brings food to a woman who has the whites or sees blood?

[Answer] 6. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Three paces from her shall he remain, who brings food to a woman who has the whites or sees the blood.' In what kind of vessels shall he bring her bread? In what kind of vessels shall he bring her barley-drink? 'In vessels of iron, or of lead, or of any common metal.'
[Note 5: The food is extended to her from a distance using a metal spoon.]
[Note 5b: Dar [Dārī/Middle Persian gloss]: brass. — J.H.P.]
[Note 6: Earthen vessels, once defiled, cannot be purified; metal vessels, however, can be. See Fargard 7.73 and following.]

[Question] 7. How much bread shall he bring to her? How much barley-drink shall he bring?

[Answer] 'Two danares [a unit of weight] of dry bread and one danare of liquor, lest she become too weak.'
'If a child has just touched her, they shall first wash his hands and then his entire body.'
[Note 7: A danare is, according to Anquetil, equivalent to approximately four tolas; a tola equals between 105 and 175 grains in weight.]
[Note 8: Soshyos [a Zoroastrian commentator] says: For three nights, cooked meat is not permitted to her, lest the [menstrual] discharge grow stronger.]
[Note 9: The child referred to is one whom she nurses. The meaning is: even a suckling infant who has touched her must undergo purification rites. The general rule stated in the Commentary is: 'Whoever has touched a Dashtan [menstruating] woman must wash his body and his clothes with gomez [ritual cow urine] and water.' The purification ceremony in question is the simple Ghosel [ritual washing], not the Barashnum [higher-order purification], since the woman herself undergoes only the former (see below, Section 11 and following).]

Part II.

8. 'If she still sees blood after three nights have passed, she shall remain in the place of infirmity [seclusion chamber] until four nights have passed.
'If she still sees blood after four nights have passed, she shall remain in the place of infirmity until five nights have passed.

9. 'If she still sees blood after five nights have passed, she shall remain in the place of infirmity until six nights have passed.'

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 19: Ritual Disposal of Hair and Nail Clippings
segments.original_text: Hair and nails. I. Notes: 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Which is the most deadly deed whereby a man offers up a sacrifice to the Daevas?' 1. Any offense to religion is considered an offering to the Daevas, whose strength is thereby increased. See Yt5.95. 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is when a man here below, combing his hair or shaving it off, or paring off his nails, drops them in a hole or in a crack. 2. Without performing the requisite ceremonies. 3. Doubtful. 3. 'Then by this transgression of the rites, Daevas are produced in the earth; by this transgression of the rites, those Khrafstras are produced in the earth which men call lice, and which eat up the corn in the corn-field and the clothes in the wardrobe. 4. 'Therefore, thou, O Zarathushtra! whenever here below thou shalt comb thy hair or shave it off, or pare off thy nails, thou shalt take them away ten paces from the faithful, twenty paces from the fire, thirty paces from the water, fifty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma. 5. 'Then thou shalt dig a hole, a disti deep if the earth be hard, a vitasti deep if it be soft; thou shalt take the hair down there and thou shalt say aloud these victorious words: "For him, as a reward, Mazda made the plants grow up." 4. A dishti = ten fingers. A vitasti = twelve fingers. 5. [at ah'yâi ashâ mazdå urvarå vaxshat.] See above, Vd11.6; the choice of this line was determined by the presence of the word plants in it: man was considered a microcosm, and every element in him had its counterpart in nature; the skin is like the sky, the flesh is like the earth, the bones are like the mountains, the veins are like the rivers, the blood in the body is like the water in the sea, the hair is like the plants, the more hairy parts are like the forests (Gr. Bund.) Cf. Rig-veda X, 16,3; Ilias VII, 99; Empedocles, fr. 378; Epicharmus ap. Plut. Consol. ad Apoll. 15; Edda, Grimnismal 40. 6. 'Thereupon thou shalt draw three furrows with a knife of metal around the hole, or six furrows or nine, and thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya three times, or six, or nine. II. 7. 'For the nails, thou shalt dig a hole, out of the house, as deep as the top joint of the little finger; thou shalt take the nails down there and thou shalt say aloud these victorious words: "The things that the pure proclaim through Asha and Vohu-mano." 6. [ashâ vohû mananghâ yâ sruyê parê magaonô.] Yasna 33.7; understood (with a play upon the word sruyê 'is heard,' and 'nails of both hands') as: 'O Asha, with Vohu-mano, the nails of the pure [are for you].' 8. 'Then thou shalt draw three furrows with a knife of metal around the hole, or six furrows or nine, and thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya three times, or six, or nine. 9. 'And then: "O Asho-zushta bird! these nails I [...]"
segments.translation:
On Hair and Nails. Part I.

Footnotes as integrated commentary:

1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! What is the most deadly deed by which a man offers sacrifice to the Daevas [demonic beings]?' [Footnote 1: Any offense against religion is considered an offering to the Daevas, whose power is thereby increased. See Yasht 5.95.]

2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is when a man here below, combing his hair or shaving it off, or paring his nails, lets them fall into a hole or into a crack [in the ground]. [Footnote 2: That is, without performing the requisite ritual ceremonies. Footnote 3: This detail is of doubtful interpretation.]

3. 'By this transgression of the rites, Daevas are generated in the earth; by this transgression of the rites, those Khrafstras [noxious creatures] are generated in the earth which men call lice, and which devour the grain in the cornfield and the garments in the wardrobe.

4. 'Therefore, O Zarathushtra! whenever here below thou dost comb thy hair or shave it off, or pare thy nails, thou shalt carry them away ten paces from the faithful [believers], twenty paces from the fire, thirty paces from the water, fifty paces from the consecrated bundles of Baresma [sacred ritual twigs].

5. 'Then thou shalt dig a hole — a dishti [ten finger-breadths] deep if the earth be hard, a vitasti [twelve finger-breadths] deep if it be soft; thou shalt place the hair therein and shalt say aloud these victorious words: "For him, as a reward, Mazda made the plants grow up." [Footnote 4: A dishti equals ten fingers' breadth; a vitasti equals twelve fingers' breadth. Footnote 5: The Avestan text reads: at ah'yâi ashâ mazdå urvarå vaxshat. See above, Vendidad 11.6. This verse was chosen because of the word "plants" contained in it: man was understood as a microcosm, each element of his body having a counterpart in nature — the skin corresponds to the sky, the flesh to the earth, the bones to the mountains, the veins to the rivers, the blood to the water of the sea, the hair to the plants, and the more densely haired parts to the forests. (Cf. Greater Bundahishn.) Compare also: Rig-Veda X.16.3; Iliad VII.99; Empedocles, fragment 378; Epicharmus as quoted by Plutarch, Consolatio ad Apollonium 15; Eddic poem Grímnismál, stanza 40.]

6. 'Thereupon thou shalt draw three furrows with a metal knife around the hole, or six furrows, or nine; and thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya prayer three times, or six, or nine.

Part II.

7. 'For the nails, thou shalt dig a hole outside the house, as deep as the topmost joint of the little finger; thou shalt place the nails therein and shalt say aloud these victorious words: "The things that the pure proclaim through Asha [Righteousness] and Vohu-mano [Good Mind]." [Footnote 6: Avestan text: ashâ vohû mananghâ yâ sruyê parê magaonô. Yasna 33.7. This is understood — with a pun on the word sruyê, meaning both "is heard" and "nails of both hands" — as: "O Asha, together with Vohu-mano, the nails of the pure [are for you]."]

8. 'Then thou shalt draw three furrows with a metal knife around the hole, or six furrows, or nine; and thou shalt chant the Ahuna-Vairya prayer three times, or six, or nine.

9. 'And then thou shalt say: "O Asho-zushta bird [the bird sacred to righteousness]! these nails I [give to thee...]" [Text breaks off here.]

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 20: The True Athravan and the Causes of Death
segments.original_text: 1. 'There is many a one, O holy Zarathushtra!' said Ahura Mazda, 'Who wears a wrong Paitidana, and who has not girded his loins with the Religion; When such a man says, "I am an Athravan," he lies; do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 2. 'He holds a wrong Khrafstraghna in his hand and he has not girded his loins with the Religion; when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies; do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 3. 'He holds a wrong twig in his hand and he has not girded his loins with the Religion; when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies; do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 4. 'He wields a wrong Ashtra mairya and he has not girded his loins with the Religion; when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies; do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 5. 'He who sleeps on throughout the night, neither performing the Yasna nor chanting the hymns, worshipping neither by word nor by deed, neither learning nor teaching, with a longing for (everlasting) life, he lies when he says, "I am an Athravan," do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda. 6. 'Him thou shalt call an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra! who throughout the night sits up and demands of the holy Wisdom, which makes man free from anxiety, and wide of heart, and easy of conscience at the head of the Chinwad bridge, and which makes him reach that world, that holy world, that excellent world of Paradise. 7. (Therefore) demand of me, thou upright one! of me, who am the Maker, the most beneficent of all beings, the best knowing, the most pleased in answering what is asked of me; demand of me, that thou mayst be the better, that thou mayst be the happier.' 8. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! What is it that brings in the unseen power of Death?' 9. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the man that teaches a wrong Religion; it is the man who continues for three springs without wearing the sacred girdle, without chanting the Gathas, without worshipping.'
segments.translation:
§1. 'There are many, O holy Zarathushtra,' said Ahura Mazda, 'who wear an incorrect Paitidana [the ritual mouth-veil worn by priests during sacred rites] and who have not girded their loins with the Religion [i.e., have not bound themselves to the Law as one binds on the Kusti, the sacred girdle]; when such a man says, "I am an Athravan [a Zoroastrian priest of the fire-tending class]," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§2. 'He who holds an incorrect Khrafstraghna [the ritual instrument used to strike noxious creatures] in his hand, and who has not girded his loins with the Religion — when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§3. 'He who holds an incorrect twig [i.e., the Baresma/barsom bundle, or the urvaram sacred branch, used in ritual] in his hand, and who has not girded his loins with the Religion — when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§4. 'He who wields an incorrect Ashtra mairya [the priestly rod, also called Aspahe-ashtra, with which the priest acting as Sraosho-varez chastises the guilty] and who has not girded his loins with the Religion — when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§5. 'He who sleeps throughout the night, performing neither the Yasna [the principal act of Zoroastrian worship] nor chanting the hymns, worshipping neither by word nor by deed, neither learning nor teaching, yet longing for everlasting life — when he says, "I am an Athravan," he lies. Do not call him an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra!' thus said Ahura Mazda.

§6. 'Him shalt thou call an Athravan, O holy Zarathushtra, who sits up throughout the night and seeks of the holy Wisdom — that Wisdom which frees man from anxiety, enlarges his heart, and eases his conscience when he stands at the head of the Chinwad bridge [the bridge of judgment crossed by souls after death], and which brings him to that world, that holy world, that excellent world of Paradise.'

§7. 'Therefore seek of me, thou upright one — of me, who am the Maker, the most beneficent of all beings, the best knowing, the most pleased to answer what is asked of me. Seek of me, so that thou mayst be the better, so that thou mayst be the happier.'

§8. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'O Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! What is it that summons the unseen power of Death?'

§9. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It is the man who teaches a false Religion. It is the man who for three springs [i.e., three years] continues without wearing the sacred girdle [the Kusti], without chanting the Gathas [the sacred hymns of Zarathushtra], without worship.'

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 21: Angra Mainyu's Attack on Zarathushtra and His Repulse
segments.original_text:
I. 1. From the region of the north, from the regions of the north, forth rushed Angra Mainyu, the deadly, the Daeva of the Daevas. And thus spake the evil-doer Angra Mainyu, the deadly: 'Druj, rush down and kill him,' O holy Zarathushtra! The Druj came rushing along, the demon Buiti, who is deceiving, unseen death.

Notes: 1. From hell; cf. II, 10n; III, 7n; Hadokht Nask 25; Bundahishn 15.19. 2. 'The fiend of fiends,' the arch-fiend. 3. Buiti is identified by the Greater Bundahishn with the Bût, the idol, worshipped by Budasp (a corruption of Bodhisativa). Buiti [Buddha] would be therefore a personification of Buddhism, which was flourishing in Eastern Iran in the two centuries before and after Christ. Buidhi (Vd11.9) may be another and more correct pronunciation of Bodhi. 4. Idolatry (cf. note 3) being the death of the soul.

2. Zarathushtra chanted aloud the Ahuna-Vairya: 'The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness. The gifts of Vohu-mano to the deeds done in this world for Mazda. He who relieves the poor makes Ahura king.' He offered the sacrifice to the good waters of the good Daitya! He recited the profession of the worshippers of Mazda! The Druj dismayed, rushed away, the demon Buiti, who is deceiving, unseen death.

Notes: 5. [i.e. Ahunwar], a prayer in frequent use, and considered of great efficacy. It was by reciting it that Ohrmazd in his first conflict with Ahriman drove him back to hell (Bund 1). 6. The river in Eranwej; see Vd1.3. 7. The Fravarane (Y11.16).

3. And the Druj said unto Angra Mainyu: 'Thou, tormenter, Angra Mainyu! I see no way to kill Spitama Zarathushtra, so great is the glory of the holy Zarathushtra.' Zarathushtra saw (all this) within his soul: 'The wicked, the evil-doing Daevas (thought he) take counsel together for my death.'

Ia. 4. Up started Zarathushtra, forward went Zarathushtra, unabated by Akem-mano, by the hardness of his malignant riddles; he went swinging stones in his hand, stones as big as a house, which he obtained from the Maker, Ahura Mazda, he the holy Zarathushtra. 'Whereat on this wide, round earth, whose ends lie afar, whereat dost thou swing (those stones), thou who standest by the upper bank of the river Dareja, in the mansion of Pourushaspa?'

Notes: 8. See Vd10.10n. 9. This is a fragment of an old legend in which Zarathushtra and Angra Mainyu played respectively the parts of Oedipus and the Sphinx. Cf. Yt5.81, where the same legend is told in nearly the same terms of the sorcerer Ahktya and Yavisht i Friyan. [See also the Pahlavi Text Yavisht i Friyan.] 10. The Commentary has, 'Some say, those stones are the Ahunwar.' If one keeps in mind how much the Moslem legend of Ibrahim owes to the legend of Zarathushtra, one may easily admit that this passage in our text is the origin of the story of how Iblis tempted Ibrahim, and was pelted away, whence he was named 'the stoned One' (ar-ragîmû). 11. 'The Daraja is the chief of the rivers, bec[ause...]'
segments.translation:
Part I. Section 1. From the region of the north, from the regions of the north [i.e., from hell], forth rushed Angra Mainyu [Ahriman], the deadly one, the Daeva [demon] of Daevas — the arch-fiend. And thus spoke the evil-doer Angra Mainyu, the deadly: 'Druj [Lie-demon], rush down and kill him' — O holy Zarathushtra! The Druj came rushing along: the demon Buiti, who is deceiving, unseen death [i.e., the demon of idolatry, identified with the idol Bût and, by extension, Buddhism — a personification of the spiritual death caused by idolatry].

Section 2. Zarathushtra chanted aloud the Ahuna-Vairya [Ahunwar prayer]: 'The will of the Lord is the law of righteousness. The gifts of Vohu-mano [Good Mind] to the deeds done in this world for Mazda. He who relieves the poor makes Ahura king.' He offered sacrifice to the good waters of the good Daitya [river of Eranwej]! He recited the profession of faith of the worshippers of Mazda [the Fravarane]! The Druj, dismayed, rushed away — the demon Buiti, who is deceiving, unseen death.

Section 3. And the Druj said unto Angra Mainyu: 'Thou, tormenter, Angra Mainyu! I see no way to kill Spitama Zarathushtra, so great is the glory of the holy Zarathushtra.' Zarathushtra perceived all this within his soul: 'The wicked, the evil-doing Daevas,' thought he, 'take counsel together for my death.'

Part Ia. Section 4. Up started Zarathushtra, forward went Zarathushtra, undaunted by Akem-mano [Evil Mind] and by the harshness of his malignant riddles [an allusion to the Oedipus-and-Sphinx type legend]; he went swinging stones in his hand — stones as big as a house — which he had obtained from the Maker, Ahura Mazda, he the holy Zarathushtra. [Angra Mainyu asked:] 'Whereat on this wide, round earth, whose ends lie afar, whereat dost thou swing those stones, thou who standest by the upper bank of the river Dareja, in the mansion of Pourushaspa [Zarathushtra's father]?'

[Translator's notes embedded in the source text indicate: the Ahunwar prayer was of supreme apotropaic efficacy in Zoroastrian tradition; the demon Buiti is equated with Buddhist idolatry flourishing in Eastern Iran around the turn of the Common Era; the legend of stone-throwing parallels the Islamic legend of Ibrahim pelting Iblis, suggesting textual influence; and the episode structurally echoes the riddle-contest of Oedipus and the Sphinx, as well as the Avestan tale of Yavisht i Friyan and the sorcerer Ahktya.]

segments.title_inferred: Thrita, the First Healer: Origin of Medicine and Healing Plants in the Vendidad
segments.original_text: Thrita, the first healer. 1. Zarathushtra asked Ahura Mazda: 'Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Maker of the material world, thou Holy One! Who was he who first of the healers, of the wise, the happy, the wealthy, the glorious, the strong, the Paradhatas, drove back sickness to sickness, drove back death to death; and first turned away the point of the sword and the fire of fever from the bodies of mortals?' [Footnote 2: Those who knew how to take care of their own bodies, like Isfandyar: some say that no sword could wound him (Comm.)] [Footnote 3: The Paradhata or Peshdad, the kings of the first Iranian dynasty.] [Footnote 4: That is to say, who kept sickness in bonds, who kept death in bonds (Comm.)] 2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'Thrita it was who first of the healers, of the wise, the happy, the wealthy, the glorious, the strong, the Paradhatas, drove back sickness to sickness, drove back death to death, and first turned away the point of the sword and the fire of fever from the bodies of mortals. 3. He asked for a source of remedies; he obtained it from Khshathra-Vairya, to withstand sickness and to withstand death; to withstand pain and to withstand fever; to withstand Sarana and to withstand Sarastya [headache and cold fever]; to withstand Azana and to withstand Azahva; to withstand Kurugha and to withstand Azivaka; to withstand Duruka and to withstand Astairya; to withstand the evil eye, rottenness, and infection which Angra Mainyu had created against the bodies of mortals. [Footnote 5: As Khshathra-Vairya presides over metals, it was a knife he received, 'of which the point and the base were set in gold.' He was therefore the first who healed with the knife, as well as the first who healed with herbs. As for the healing with the holy word, see §5 and seq.] 4. And I Ahura Mazda brought down the healing plants that, by many hundreds, by many thousands, by many myriads, grow up all around the one Gaokerena. [Footnote 7: There are two Haomas: one is the yellow or golden Haoma, which is the earthly Haoma, and which, when prepared for the sacrifice, is the king of healing plants; the other is the white Haoma or Gaokerena, which grows up in the middle of the sea Vouru-Kasha, where it is surrounded by the ten thousand healing plants, created by Ohrmazd in order to oppose so many diseases that had been created by Ahriman (Bundahishn 9; see Vd22.2). A frog goes swimming around the Gaokerena to gnaw it down: but two Kar Mahi (Vd19.42) keep watch and circle around the tree, so that the head of one of them is continually towards the frog (Bund. 18).] 5. All this do we achieve; all this do we order; all these prayers do we utter, for the benefit of the bodies of mortals. [Footnote 8: We do all that is necessary for healing; we give, as Dastobar (Dastur), the necessary prescriptions; we recite the needed prayers. — This section is a transition to the spell-medicine.] 6. To withstand sickness and to withstand death; to withstand pain and to withstand fever; to withstand Sar[ana and to withstand Sarastya...] [TEXT ENDS INCOMPLETE]
segments.translation:
Thrita, the First Healer. 1. Zarathushtra [Zoroaster] put this question to Ahura Mazda: 'Ahura Mazda, most beneficent Spirit, Creator of the material world, O Holy One! Who was the first among the healers — among the wise, the prosperous, the wealthy, the glorious, the mighty, the Paradhatas [kings of the first Iranian dynasty, the Peshdadians] — who drove back sickness upon sickness itself, who drove back death upon death itself, and who first turned away the edge of the sword and the burning heat of fever from the bodies of mortal men?' [Commentator's note: those who knew how to preserve their own bodies, like Isfandyar, of whom some say no sword could wound him. The Paradhata, or Peshdad, were the kings of the first Iranian dynasty. The meaning is: who kept sickness in bonds, who kept death in bonds.]

2. Ahura Mazda answered: 'It was Thrita who was first among the healers — among the wise, the prosperous, the wealthy, the glorious, the mighty, the Paradhatas — who drove back sickness upon sickness itself, who drove back death upon death itself, and who first turned away the edge of the sword and the burning heat of fever from the bodies of mortal men.

3. 'He petitioned for a source of remedies, and he received it from Khshathra-Vairya [the Aməša Spənta presiding over metals], to resist sickness and to resist death; to resist pain and to resist fever; to resist Sarana and to resist Sarastya [headache and cold fever]; to resist Azana and to resist Azahva; to resist Kurugha and to resist Azivaka; to resist Duruka and to resist Astairya; to resist the evil eye, putrefaction, and contagion which Angra Mainyu [Ahriman, the evil spirit] had fashioned against the bodies of mortal men.' [Commentator's note: Because Khshathra-Vairya presides over metals, what Thrita received was a knife, 'of which the point and the base were set in gold.' He was thus the first to heal with the knife, as well as the first to heal with herbs; for healing by the holy word, see §5 and following.]

4. 'And I, Ahura Mazda, caused the healing plants to descend — those that grow up in their hundreds, their thousands, their myriads, all about the one Gaokerena [the sacred white Haoma tree].' [Commentator's note: There are two Haomas — the yellow or golden Haoma, which is the earthly Haoma and, when prepared for sacrifice, is the king of all healing plants; and the white Haoma, or Gaokerena, which grows in the midst of the sea Vouru-Kasha, surrounded by ten thousand healing plants created by Ohrmazd to counter the diseases created by Ahriman (Bundahishn 9; cf. Vd. 22.2). A frog swims about the Gaokerena attempting to gnaw it down, but two Kar Mahi fish (cf. Vd. 19.42) keep watch and circle the tree so that one of them always faces the frog (Bundahishn 18).]

5. 'All this we accomplish; all this we ordain; all these prayers we recite, for the benefit of the bodies of mortal men.' [Commentator's note: We perform all that is required for healing; we issue, in the capacity of Dastobar [Dastur, priestly authority], the necessary prescriptions; we recite the requisite prayers. — This section marks a transition to medicine by incantation.]

6. 'To resist sickness and to resist death; to resist pain and to resist fever; to resist Sara[na and to resist Sarastya...]' [TEXT ENDS INCOMPLETE]

segments.title_inferred: Vendidad Fargard 23: Invocations for Rain, Healing, and Purification
segments.original_text: I. Notes: 1. Hail, bounteous bull! Hail to thee, beneficent bull! Hail to thee, who makest increase! Hail to thee, who makest growth! Hail to thee, who dost bestow his part upon the righteous faithful, and wilt bestow it on the faithful yet unborn! Hail to thee, whom the Jahi kills, and the ungodly Ashemaogha, and the wicked tyrant. 1. The primeval bull who was created by Ohrmazd and killed by Ahriman with the help of the Jahi. -- Clause 1 is to be recited when one meets an ox or any kind of cattle, Gr. Riv. 386. 2. Possibly, 'who dost kill the Jahi (by means of gomez). 3. His daily food. 4. The wicked kills animals, out of mere cruelty, beyond his needs (Yasna 29.1; Y32.12, 14; Y48.7). II. 2.
