'Come, come on, O clouds, from up above, down on the earth, by thousands of drops, by myriads of drops:' thus say, O holy Zarathushtra! 'to destroy sickness, to destroy death, to destroy the sickness that kills, to destroy death that kills, to destroy Gadha and Apagadha. 5. Cf. Bund. 3.3, 6, 4. 6. Names of diseases. 3. 'If death come after noon, may healing come at eve! If death come at eve, may healing come at night! If death come at night, may healing come at dawn! And showers shower down new water, new earth, new plants, new healing powers, and new healing. IIIa. 4. 'As the sea Vouru-kasha is the gathering place of the waters, rising up and going down, up the aerial way and down the earth, down the earth and up the aerial way: thus rise up and roll along! thou in whose rising and growing Ahura Mazda made the aerial way. 7. Waters and light are believed to flow from the same spring and in the same bed: 'As the light comes in through Alburz (Hara Berezaiti) and goes out through Alburz, so water also comes out through Alburz and goes away through Alburz' (Bund. 20.4). Every day the sun, moon, and stars rise up from Alburz, and every day all the waters on the earth come back together to the sea Vouru-kasha, and there collected come down again to the earth from the peaks of Alburz (Gr. Riv. 43'). As light comes from three different sources (the sun, the moon, and the stars), the waters are invoked three times, first in company with the sun, then with the moon, lastly with the stars, as if there should be three different movements of the rain connected with the three movements of light. 8. Waters come down from the sky to the earth and rise back from the earth to the sky (see Vd5.15 seq.) 5. 'Up! rise up and roll along! thou swift-horsed Sun, above Hara Berezaiti, and produce light for the world (and mayst thou [O man!] rise up there, if thou art to abide in Garo-nmanem), along the path made by Mazda, along the way made by the gods, the watery way they opened. 9. 'If thou art a righteous man' (Comm.) 10. The translation of this clause is doubtful. 6. 'And the Holy Word [manthra spenta] shall keep away the evil: Of thee [O child!] I will cleanse the birth and growth; of thee [O woman!] I will make the body and the
segments.translation:
Section I, Verse 1: Hail, O bounteous bull! Hail to thee, O beneficent bull! Hail to thee, who causest increase! Hail to thee, who causest growth! Hail to thee, who dost bestow his portion upon the righteous faithful, and shalt bestow it upon the faithful yet unborn! Hail to thee, whom the Jahi [demoness of licentiousness] slays, and the ungodly Ashemaogha [heretic], and the wicked tyrant. [Scholarly note: This refers to the primeval bull created by Ohrmazd (Ahura Mazda) and slain by Ahriman with the assistance of the Jahi. This clause is to be recited when one encounters an ox or any cattle (cf. Greater Rivayat, p. 386). A secondary interpretation suggests the bull 'slays the Jahi by means of gomez [ox-urine].' The wicked kills animals out of mere cruelty, beyond his needs (cf. Yasna 29.1; 32.12, 14; 48.7).]

Section II, Verse 2: 'Come, come forth, O clouds, from on high, down upon the earth, in thousands of drops, in myriads of drops' — thus speak, O holy Zarathushtra! — 'to destroy sickness, to destroy death, to destroy the lethal sickness, to destroy lethal death, to destroy Gadha and Apagadha [named diseases].' [Scholarly note: Cf. Bundahishn 3.3, 6, 4. Gadha and Apagadha are names of specific diseases.]

Verse 3: 'If death come after midday, may healing come by evening! If death come by evening, may healing come by night! If death come by night, may healing come at dawn! And may showers rain down new water, new earth, new plants, new healing powers, and new healing.'

Section IIIa, Verse 4: 'As the sea Vouru-kasha is the gathering place of the waters, rising up and going down — up the aerial way and down to the earth, down to the earth and up the aerial way — so rise up and roll along! Thou [O water], in whose rising and increase Ahura Mazda fashioned the aerial way.' [Scholarly note: Waters and light are believed to flow from the same source and along the same channel. As the light enters through Alburz (Hara Berezaiti) and exits through Alburz, so too does water issue from Alburz and return thither (cf. Bundahishn 20.4). Each day the sun, moon, and stars ascend from Alburz, and all terrestrial waters return to the sea Vouru-kasha, thence descending again from the peaks of Alburz (cf. Greater Rivayat, p. 43'). The waters are invoked three times, corresponding to the three sources of light — sun, moon, and stars — as if three movements of rain correspond to three movements of light. Waters descend from sky to earth and ascend from earth back to sky (cf. Vendidad 5.15 seq.).]

Verse 5: 'Up! Rise up and roll along, O swift-horsed Sun, above Hara Berezaiti, and bring forth light for the world — (and mayest thou, O man, rise up there, if thou art destined to dwell in Garo-nmanem [the House of Song, i.e., Paradise]) — along the path fashioned by Mazda, along the way fashioned by the gods, the watery way which they opened.' [Scholarly note: 'If thou art a righteous man' (Commentary). The translation of the parenthetical clause is uncertain.]

Verse 6: 'And the Holy Word [manthra spenta, the sacred utterance] shall repel evil. Of thee, O child, I will purify the birth and growth; of thee, O woman, I will restore the body and the —' [Text breaks off; remainder of verse not present in this segment.]

segments.title_inferred: Ahura Mazda's Account of 99,999 Diseases and the Healing Powers of Manthra Spenta and Airyaman
segments.original_text:
Angra Mainyu creates 99,999 diseases; Ahura Mazda counters with the Holy Manthra and with Airyaman.

I.
1. Ahura Mazda spake unto Spitama Zarathushtra, saying: 'I, Ahura Mazda, the Maker of all good things, when I made this mansion [the Garothman, Paradise], the beautiful, the shining, seen afar (there may I go up, there may I arrive!)
2. 'Then the ruffian looked at me; the ruffian Angra Mainyu, the deadly, wrought against me nine diseases, and ninety, and nine hundred, and nine thousand, and nine times ten thousand diseases. So mayst thou heal me, thou most glorious Manthra Spenta!
3. 'Unto thee will I give in return a thousand fleet, swift-running steeds; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. Unto thee will I give in return a thousand fleet, high-humped camels; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
4. 'Unto thee will I give in return a thousand brown oxen that do not push; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. Unto thee will I give in return a thousand females big with young, of all species of small cattle; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
5. 'And I will bless thee with the fair blessing-spell of the righteous, the friendly blessing-spell of the righteous, that makes the empty swell to fulness and the full to overflowing, that comes to help him who was sickening, and makes the sick man sound again.
6. 'Manthra Spenta, the all-glorious, replied unto me: "How shall I heal thee? How shall I drive away from thee those nine diseases, and those ninety, those nine hundred, those nine thousand, and those nine times ten thousand diseases?"'

II.
7. The Maker Ahura Mazda called for Nairyo-sangha: Go thou, Nairyo-sangha, the herald, and drive towards the mansion of Airyaman, and speak thus unto him:
8. Thus speaks Ahura Mazda, the Holy One, unto thee: 'I, Ahura Mazda, the Maker of all good things, when I made this mansion, the beautiful, the shining, seen afar (there may I ascend, there may I arrive!)
9. 'Then the ruffian looked at me; the ruffian Angra Mainyu, the deadly, wrought against me nine diseases, and ninety, and nine hundred, and nine thousand, and nine times ten thousand diseases. So mayst thou heal me, O Airyaman, the vow-fulfiller!
10. 'Unto thee will I give in return a thousand fleet, swift-running steeds; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. Unto thee will I give in return a thousand fleet, high-humped camels; I offer thee up a sacrifice, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
11. 'Unto thee will I give in return a thousand brown oxen that do not push; I offer thee up a sacrifice [text breaks off].'
segments.translation:
Angra Mainyu creates 99,999 diseases; Ahura Mazda counters them with the Holy Manthra [Sacred Word] and with Airyaman.

Part I.
1. Ahura Mazda spoke unto Spitama Zarathushtra [Zoroaster], saying: 'I, Ahura Mazda, the Maker of all good things — when I fashioned this mansion [the Garothman, i.e., Paradise], the beautiful, the shining, seen from afar (there may I ascend, there may I arrive!) —
2. 'Then the villain fixed his gaze upon me; the villain Angra Mainyu [the Destructive Spirit], the deadly one, wrought against me nine diseases, and ninety, and nine hundred, and nine thousand, and nine times ten thousand diseases [totaling 99,999 diseases]. So may you heal me, O most glorious Manthra Spenta [Holy Word]!
3. 'In return I will give you a thousand swift, fleet-footed horses; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka [Genius of the Good Eye], made by Mazda and holy. In return I will give you a thousand swift, high-humped camels; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
4. 'In return I will give you a thousand brown oxen that do not gore; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. In return I will give you a thousand females heavy with young, of every species of small cattle; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
5. 'And I will bless you with the fair blessing-spell of the righteous, the kindly blessing-spell of the righteous — that which causes the empty to swell to fullness and the full to overflow, that which comes to the aid of one who was falling ill, and restores the sick man to health.
6. 'Manthra Spenta, the all-glorious, replied unto me: "How shall I heal you? How shall I drive away from you those nine diseases, and those ninety, those nine hundred, those nine thousand, and those nine times ten thousand diseases?"'

Part II.
7. The Maker, Ahura Mazda, summoned Nairyo-sangha [the divine herald]: Go, Nairyo-sangha, you who are the herald, and proceed to the mansion of Airyaman [the yazata of healing fellowship], and speak to him thus:
8. Thus speaks Ahura Mazda, the Holy One, unto you: 'I, Ahura Mazda, the Maker of all good things — when I fashioned this mansion, the beautiful, the shining, seen from afar (there may I ascend, there may I arrive!) —
9. 'Then the villain fixed his gaze upon me; the villain Angra Mainyu, the deadly one, wrought against me nine diseases, and ninety, and nine hundred, and nine thousand, and nine times ten thousand diseases. So may you heal me, O Airyaman, fulfiller of vows!
10. 'In return I will give you a thousand swift, fleet-footed horses; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy. In return I will give you a thousand swift, high-humped camels; I offer up sacrifice to you, O good Saoka, made by Mazda and holy.
11. 'In return I will give you a thousand brown oxen that do not gore; I offer up sacrifice [text breaks off].'

segments.title_inferred: Saffron (Za'faran) in Persian Traditional Medicine
segments.original_text: Plant: Saffron — Za'faran (زعفران) — antidepressant, menstrual
segments.translation:
SAFFRON

Farsi name: زعفران (Za'farān)
Arabic name: زعفران (Za'farān) — shared across both traditions
Modern botanical identification: Crocus sativus L. (family Iridaceae); the dried stigmas and styles constitute the medicinal and culinary drug.

---

MIZAJ [TEMPERAMENT]:
In the Galenic-Islamic humoral framework as codified in Persian Tibb-e-Sonnati [traditional medicine], saffron is classified as Ḥārr wa Yābis [hot and dry] in the Second Degree. Avicenna (Ibn Sīnā, 980–1037 CE) affirms this temperament in the Qānūn fi'l-Ṭibb [Canon of Medicine], Book II (Mufradāt al-adwiya / Simple Drugs), wherein he assigns saffron a heating quality of the second order and a drying quality of the first to second order, cautioning that excessive use can produce excessive dryness and heat, potentially harming the brain.

---

AVICENNA — CANON OF MEDICINE (QĀNŪN FI'L-ṬIBB) REFERENCES:
• Book II, 'Saffron' entry: Avicenna describes Za'farān as a powerful mufarriḥ [gladdener / cardiac tonic], acting upon the rūḥ-e ḥaywānī [vital spirit] to elevate mood and strengthen the heart.
• He notes its emmenagogue [muḥarrik-e ḥayḍ, stimulant of menstruation] properties, recommending it to promote and regulate ḥayḍ [menstrual flow] in cases of iḥtibās [retention or suppression of menses].
• Book III includes saffron as an ingredient in compound formulas for melancholia [mālīkhūliyā], recommending it in combination with rose water, violet oil, and other mufarriḥāt [gladdening agents].
• Avicenna warns against overdose: excessive consumption is said to cause laughter without cause (a form of intoxication), and in very large doses, narcolepsy or death — a warning replicated across subsequent Persian pharmacopoeia.
• He also prescribes it topically as a ṭilā' [liniment] for joint pain, in eye preparations (for cataracts and clouding of vision), and mixed with oils for headache when applied to the temples.

---

THERAPEUTIC PROPERTIES (TIBB-E-SONNATI):
1. Mufarriḥ (Gladdener / Antidepressant): Saffron is the premier mufarriḥ in Persian pharmacology. It acts on the nafs [soul/psyche] and the rūḥ to dispel gham [grief] and huzn [sorrow], and is the central ingredient in many dawā'-ye faraḥ-bakhsh [joy-giving medicines]. Modern research has confirmed serotonin-modulating properties in Crocus sativus extracts, lending scientific support to this classical claim.
2. Muḥarrik-e Ḥayḍ (Emmenagogue): Promotes menstrual onset in cases of delayed, suppressed, or irregular menses. Used across Persian, Arab, and Greco-Arabic traditions as a first-line botanical emmenagogue.
3. Muqawwī-ye Qalb (Cardiac Strengthener): Classified among qalb-āwar [heart-benefiting] drugs; believed to strengthen the heart muscle and regulate palpitations (khafaqān).
4. Musakkin (Analgesic/Sedative at low dose): Applied for headache and in preparations for pain relief, particularly in combination with opium-based compounds in later Persian tradition.
5. Mulaṭṭif (Subtilizer): Thins and attenuates dense humors [akhlāṭ-e ghaliẓ], making it useful in obstructions of the liver and spleen.
6. Muḥallil (Resolvent): Disperses inflammatory swellings when applied topically.
7. Baṣar (Ophthalmic): Used in eye drops and collyria [kuḥl] preparations to improve vision and treat cataracts in early stages.

---

PREPARATION METHODS (PERSIAN TRADITION):
• Jūshānda [Decoction/Infusion]: A small quantity (0.1–0.5 g) of saffron stigmas steeped in hot water or rose water (āb-e gol-e sorḵ) and consumed as a drink for depression, menstrual disorders, and cardiac conditions.
• Sharāb-e Za'farān [Saffron Wine/Tincture]: Saffron macerated in grape wine or, in post-Islamisation adaptations, in distilled herbal extracts; used as a mufarriḥ tonic.
• Ma'jūn (Electuary/Paste): Saffron incorporated into compound pastes with honey, mastic, amber, and other mufarriḥāt; famously in Ma'jūn-e Faraḥ-bakhsh [Joy-Giving Electuary] recorded in Ẕakhīra-ye Khwārazmshāhī (12th century Persian medical encyclopedia by Jurjānī).
• Ṭilā' (Topical Liniment): Saffron dissolved in sesame oil or almond oil applied to the temples for headache, to joints for arthritis, and to the abdomen for menstrual cramping.
• Bukhūr (Fumigation): Dried saffron burned as incense, inhaled for mood elevation and in ritual purification contexts.
• Qurs (Tablet/Lozenge): Compressed preparations including saffron for cardiac and psychological conditions, recorded in multiple Persian materia medica.
• Ḥannā (Henna Compound): In cosmetic-medicinal preparations, saffron mixed with henna for skin and hair treatments.

---

CONDITIONS TREATED:
• Mālīkhūliyā [Melancholia / depressive states]
• Waswās [Obsessive anxiety, lit. 'whispering of the devil']
• Iḥtibās-e ḥayḍ [Suppression/retention of menses]
• 'Usr-e ḥayḍ [Dysmenorrhea / painful menstruation]
• Khafaqān [Palpitations]
• Ṣudā' [Headache]
• Dard-e mafāṣel [Joint pain / arthritis]
• Ḍa'f-e baṣar [Weakness of vision]
• Sadd-e jigar [Liver obstructions]
• Nāgawārī [Digestive discomfort / dyspepsia]

---

ZOROASTRIAN AND PRE-ISLAMIC USES:
Pre-Islamic Iranian references to saffron are attested in the Avestan and Pahlavi literary traditions, though direct botanical pharmacological entries in Zoroastrian texts are sparse and largely preserved through later synthesis.
• Ritual and Liturgical Use: Saffron was among the aromatic substances (bōy, 'fragrance') offered in Zoroastrian fire temple rituals. Its golden color (zard, 'yellow') was symbolically associated with Ahura Mazda's radiant divine light (xwarrah / farr, the 'divine glory'). Saffron water was used to anoint sacred fires and sprinkle on ritual spaces as a purifying agent.
• The Bundahishn [Zoroastrian cosmological text, compiled c. 9th century CE from older Pahlavi sources] alludes to aromatic yellow plants in the context of ritual purity, which commentators have associated with saffron cultivation in the Iranian plateau.
• Nowrūz [Persian New Year] Traditions: Saffron has ancient pre-Islamic associations with Nowrūz. Saffron-infused rice (later crystallized as the dish shirin polo) and saffron halvā were prepared as festival offerings, believed to bring shādī [joy] and to honor the Fravashis [ancestral spirits] during the holy days.
• Apotropaic Use: Saffron paste was applied to the forehead of newborns and brides in Zoroastrian communities as protection against the evil eye (chashm-e bad) and malevolent spiritual forces — a practice that persisted in syncretic form into Islamic-era Iranian folk medicine.
• Association with Mithra: Some scholars of Iranian religion (notably M. Boyce and I. Gershevitch) have noted saffron's association with solar imagery and its possible use in the worship of Mithra, the deity of covenants, light, and the sun, given its intense golden-yellow color mirroring solar symbolism.
• Royal and Elite Medical Use: Achaemenid Persian court records (via Greek sources such as Ctesias) mention the use of saffron in royal medicinal preparations, suggesting an established pre-Islamic Persian pharmacological tradition for this plant.

---

ADDITIONAL NOTES:
• Persian saffron (from the Khorasan and later Qaen/Birjand regions of Iran) has historically been regarded as the world's finest quality, a distinction recognized by Avicenna and subsequent Islamic-era pharmacologists.
• The Ẕakhīra-ye Khwārazmshāhī (Jurjānī, 12th c.) and the Ikhtiyārāt-e Badī'ī (14th c.) both give extensive compound formulations incorporating saffron as a primary mufarriḥ ingredient.
• Caution (as per classical sources): Contraindicated in pregnancy in significant doses due to strong emmenagogue action; noted as potentially abortifacient (musqiṭ) at higher doses in Avicenna and subsequent Persian texts.

segments.title_inferred: Fenugreek (Shanbalileh) in Persian Traditional Medicine
segments.original_text: Plant: Fenugreek — Shanbalileh (شنبلیله) — diabetes, lactation, digestive
segments.translation:
FENUGREEK — شنبلیله (Shanbalileh)

Arabic name: الحلبة (al-Ḥulba). Also known in classical Persian medical texts as Hulbah and in some regional dialects as Shambalid.

MIZAJ [TEMPERAMENT]:
Hot [garm] and Dry [khoshk] in the Second Degree, according to the majority of Tibb-e-Sonnati authorities. Some scholars of the Galenic-Avicennian tradition place its dryness at the borderline of the First and Second Degrees, particularly when the seed is prepared as a decoction [joshande], which moderates its desiccating quality through aqueous processing.

AVICENNA'S CANON [AL-QANUN FI AL-TIBB]:
Ibn Sina (Avicenna) discusses Shanbalileh in Book II of the Canon (Kitab al-Adwiya al-Mufrada), classifying it among the warming, resolvent [muḥallil], and softening [mulayyin] simples. He attributes to it the following actions:
— Resolves [taḥlil] thickened and obstructed humours [akhlat-e ghalizeh] in the liver and spleen.
— Warms the stomach [ma'deh] and facilitates digestion [hazm].
— When boiled in water, the resulting decoction relieves colic [qulanj] and intestinal wind [nafy-e bad].
— Applied externally as a poultice [zimad], it softens hardened swellings and disperses phlegmatic [balgham] accumulations.
— Avicenna specifically notes its utility in conditions of cold-humour dominance, including cold-type diabetes [ziabites-e sard] wherein the kidneys fail to retain moisture — here Shanbalileh was administered to warm the renal system and restore retentive faculty [quwwat-e masika].
— He also records its galactagogue [mudir-e shir] property: nursing women were prescribed a gruel [ferni] of ground fenugreek seed cooked in milk with honey to increase milk flow and enrich milk quality.

THERAPEUTIC PROPERTIES IN TIBB-E-SONNATI:
1. DIGESTIVE (Gowarzeshi / هاضمه): Stimulates gastric fire [hararat-e gharizi-ye ma'deh]; recommended for cold-stomach [ma'deh-ye sard] presentations including bloating, sluggish digestion, nausea from cold foods, and loss of appetite. Standard preparation is a post-meal tea of whole or lightly roasted seeds.
2. HYPOGLYCAEMIC / ANTI-DIABETIC: In Persian humoral theory, one form of diabetes [ziabites or bol-e shakari] was attributed to excess cold and moisture in the kidneys and bladder, causing the body to expel fluids without proper transformation [istihala]. Shanbalileh, as a warming-drying agent, was prescribed to counteract this. Seeds were soaked overnight, and the resulting mucilaginous water was drunk on an empty stomach. Modern pharmacology has validated a significant hypoglycaemic effect, attributed principally to the alkaloid trigonelline and the soluble fibre 4-hydroxyisoleucine.
3. GALACTAGOGUE (Mudir-e Shir / مدر شیر): Among the most consistently documented uses across Persian, Arab, and Indian Yunani traditions. Fenugreek seed decoction or ground seed stirred into warm cow's milk was given to nursing mothers to promote milk production. This use is recorded in texts from the Safavid period onward and aligns with contemporary phytochemical research suggesting diosgenin (a steroidal saponin) mediates prolactin stimulation.
4. ANTI-INFLAMMATORY / MUḤALLIL: Warm poultices of dampened ground seed were applied to arthritic joints, boils, and lymphatic swellings [ghudad] to reduce cold-phlegmatic inflammation.
5. RESPIRATORY: Decoction used to loosen thick [ghalizeh] phlegm in the lungs; classified as a chest-opening [fatih-e sedreh] remedy for cold-wet [sard-martub] coughs.
6. UTERINE TONIC: In Qajar-era Persian gynaecological compendia, Shanbalileh was recommended to warm a cold uterus [rahim-e sard], facilitate menstruation [ādeh], and ease labour pains when taken as a decoction in the final weeks of pregnancy.

PREPARATION METHODS (Persian Tradition):
— Joshande (جوشانده / Decoction): 1–2 teaspoons of seeds simmered in 2 cups of water for 15 minutes, strained, taken warm before meals for digestive complaints and blood sugar regulation.
— Khis-khordan (خیس‌خوردن / Cold Maceration): Seeds soaked in water overnight; the mucilaginous liquid consumed in the morning for diabetes and urinary conditions.
— Ferni / Halim: Ground seed cooked into a porridge with milk, honey, and sometimes ghee [roghan-e heyvanati], for lactating mothers and post-illness convalescence.
— Zimad (ضماد / Topical Poultice): Seeds ground to paste with warm water or vinegar-water [serke-ab], applied to swollen joints, skin infections, or cold abscesses.
— Roghan-e Shanbalileh (روغن شنبلیله / Infused Oil): Seeds infused in sesame or olive oil; used in massage for cold-arthritic and paralytic conditions.
— Powder (Poodra / گرد): Dry-roasted ground seeds mixed with cinnamon and ginger for warming digestive blends.

ZOROASTRIAN RITUAL AND PRE-ISLAMIC USES:
Documented pre-Islamic Zoroastrian uses of Shanbalileh are limited but traceable through several lines of evidence:
1. AVESTAN AND PAHLAVI HERBALISM: While Shanbalileh does not appear by name in surviving Avestan liturgical texts, references in Middle Persian (Pahlavi) agricultural and medicinal writings — particularly in the Bundahishn (a Zoroastrian cosmological text compiled c. 9th century CE but drawing on older oral tradition) — to warming seed-medicines used for cold-womb and cold-stomach conditions are consistent with fenugreek's later documented role. Scholars such as Mojtaba Minovi have noted that several unnamed 'hot seeds' [tokhmha-ye garm] in Sassanid medical practice correspond to fenugreek by description.
2. NOWRUZ AND SPROUTING RITUAL: Fenugreek seeds are among the traditional sprouted grains and legumes grown for Nowruz (Persian New Year) [sabzeh]. This custom, which predates Islam in Persia, carries Zoroastrian cosmological resonance: the sprouting of seeds symbolises the renewal of life [Asha — Zoroastrian cosmic order/truth] and the victory of light and warmth over cold and darkness at the vernal equinox. The warmth-associated mizaj of fenugreek may have reinforced its symbolic appropriateness for this ritual, though the sabzeh most canonically uses wheat or lentils.
3. FIRE TEMPLE FUMIGATION: Some ethnobotanical accounts from Yazd and Kerman — centres of surviving Zoroastrian practice in Iran — record the burning of dried fenugreek alongside other aromatic seeds (such as esphand / wild rue) as a minor fumigant [bakhur] to purify spaces and ward off cold-natured [sard-mizaj] malevolent influences [divs]. This practice, if authentic, would represent a functional extension of the humoral logic (hot fumigant repels cold evil) into ritual space.
4. AHURIC PLANT STATUS: Zoroastrian cosmology divided plants into those associated with Ahura Mazda (beneficent, life-giving) and those associated with Angra Mainyu (corrupting, death-bringing). Useful food and medicine plants were typically classified as Ahuric. Fenugreek, as a cultivated crop of the Iranian plateau since at least 4000 BCE, would have fallen within the category of blessed agricultural creation [dām ī Ohrmazd], consistent with Zoroastrian reverence for cultivated land and husbandry.

CONTRAINDICATIONS AND CAUTIONS (per Tibb-e-Sonnati):
— Excess use produces bitterness [talkhi] and may overheat the liver in those with hot mizaj [garm-mizaj].
— Not recommended in pregnancy beyond the therapeutic dose for uterine-warming purposes, as Avicenna and later Persian physicians note its emmenagogue [mudir-e hayz] potential.
— Should be balanced [eslah] with cooling agents such as rose water or barley water when prescribed to hot-temperament individuals.

MODERN BOTANICAL IDENTIFICATION:
Trigonella foenum-graecum L. (Family: Fabaceae). Annual herb native to the Eastern Mediterranean and Western Asia; extensively cultivated across Iran, India, Egypt, and Ethiopia. Active constituents include trigonelline, diosgenin, 4-hydroxyisoleucine, galactomannans, and various flavonoids.

segments.title_inferred: Black Seed (Siah Daneh) in Persian Traditional Medicine
segments.original_text: Plant: Black seed — Siah daneh (سیاه‌دانه) — prophetic medicine, immune
segments.translation:
BLACK SEED — SIAH DANEH (سیاه‌دانه)

PERSIAN NAME: Siah Daneh (سیاه‌دانه, lit. 'black seed'); also Shonaiz (شونیز) in classical Persian medical literature.
ARABIC NAME: Habbat al-Sawda' (حبة السوداء), also Habbat al-Barakah (حبة البركة, 'seed of blessing').
BOTANICAL IDENTIFICATION: Nigella sativa L. (family Ranunculaceae).

MIZAJ [TEMPERAMENT]: Hot and Dry in the third degree [گرم و خشک در درجه سوم], according to the classical Galenic-Avicennian humoral schema as applied in Persian Tibb-e-Sonnati. This temperament classifies it as a strongly heating, desiccating agent suited to countering cold and wet (phlegmatic and melancholic) conditions.

AVICENNA — CANON OF MEDICINE [QANUN FI AL-TIBB / قانون فی الطب]: Ibn Sina (Avicenna, 980–1037 CE) discusses Siah Daneh extensively in Book II of the Canon (Mufradat, the chapter on simple drugs). He describes it as a powerful diuretic [mudirr al-bawl], an emmenagogue [muharrik-e-hayz], a carminative [dafe'-e-nafy], and a general stimulant of vital faculties [muqawwi-ye quwa]. He recommends it for: (1) obstruction of airways and nasal passages (inhalation of crushed seeds wrapped in cloth); (2) sluggish digestion and flatulence [naft va baad]; (3) expulsion of intestinal parasites [kerm-e-rude]; (4) stimulation of milk production in nursing mothers when taken with warm water and honey; (5) headache relief when the oil is applied topically to the temples; and (6) as a general tonic [mufarreh] for the heart and immune faculties [quwwat-e-dafe'eh].

THERAPEUTIC PROPERTIES (TIBB-E-SONNATI):
— Strengthening of the body's innate defensive power [quwwat-e-mudabbera / قوت مدبره], corresponding broadly to immune function in modern terminology.
— Expectorant and bronchodilatory action: seed decoction or steam inhalation used for asthma [rabu'], chronic cough [sorfeh], and nasal congestion.
— Analgesic and anti-inflammatory: seed oil applied topically to joints in arthritis [dard-e-mafasel] and rheumatic conditions.
— Hepatoprotective tonic: seed preparations used to support liver function [jigar] and address jaundice [zardparvar].
— Antifungal and antimicrobial: seeds used in wound dressings and skin conditions including eczema [nar-e-farsi] and ringworm.
— Antidiabetic: seed powder in warm water taken fasting, to moderate blood sugar [shiriniye khun] in classical understanding of zoolabe (diabetes mellitus).
— Neurological tonic: seed oil employed in cases of forgetfulness [nisyan] and general mental fatigue.

PREPARATION METHODS (TRADITIONAL PERSIAN):
1. DECOCTION [JOSHANDE / جوشانده]: Seeds boiled in water with honey and ginger; taken warm for respiratory and digestive complaints.
2. SEED OIL [ROUGHAN-E-SIAH DANEH / روغن سیاه‌دانه]: Cold-pressed oil applied topically or taken internally (1–3 teaspoons daily) for immune support and anti-inflammatory action.
3. POWDER [POODRE SIAH DANEH]: Ground seeds mixed with honey [asal] or black cumin paste; taken for intestinal parasites and digestive sluggishness.
4. INHALATION [BOHOUR / بخور]: Seeds heated on coals or wrapped in cloth and inhaled; classical remedy for nasal obstruction, sinusitis, and headache.
5. TOPICAL PASTE [MARHAM]: Seeds ground and mixed with sesame oil or rose water for application to arthritic joints and skin lesions.
6. INFUSED VINEGAR [SERKEH-YE SIAH DANEH]: Seeds macerated in grape vinegar; used as a digestive bitter and antifungal wash.

CONDITIONS TREATED:
— Respiratory: asthma, bronchitis, allergic rhinitis, sinusitis.
— Digestive: flatulence, colic, intestinal parasites, constipation, sluggish digestion.
— Metabolic: early-stage diabetes, obesity attributed to phlegmatic excess.
— Dermatological: eczema, psoriasis, ringworm, alopecia.
— Neurological: headache, forgetfulness, nervous fatigue.
— Gynaecological: amenorrhoea, hypogalactia (low milk supply).
— General: immune deficiency, chronic fatigue, as a prophylactic tonic.

ZOROASTRIAN SIGNIFICANCE [PRE-ISLAMIC PERSIAN TRADITION]:
Pre-Islamic Zoroastrian uses of Nigella sativa are attested in the Pahlavi medical and liturgical corpus, where the plant appears under the name *shōnīz* (a term that survived into Islamic-era Persian). In Zoroastrian ritual practice, the seeds were among the aromatic substances burned as incense [bakhur] during purification rites [barashnum], the smoke believed to repel malevolent spiritual forces [drujs] and maintain ritual purity [ashoi / اشویی]. The seeds were also associated with Amordad (Ameretat, the Amesha Spenta of plants and immortality), reflecting their role as a sacred botanical guardian of health and life. Herbalist-priests [āthravan-physicians] prescribed the seeds as part of compound remedies [dāru-ye murakkab] for fevers thought to be of demonic origin [tav-e-divi]. Funerary and protective amulets occasionally incorporated sealed pouches of black seed, reflecting beliefs in its apotropaic properties. While direct textual evidence from the Avesta itself is limited, the Bundahishn and Wizidagiha i Zadspram reference categories of 'health-giving seeds' [tōhm ī darmān-dār] in which Nigella is plausibly included by later commentators. Zoroastrian physicians in the Sasanian court (3rd–7th century CE) are recorded in Arabic-language sources as having transmitted knowledge of shōnīz to early Islamic medicine, forming a documented conduit between pre-Islamic Persian and later Tibb-e-Sonnati tradition.

PROPHETIC MEDICINE NOTE [TIBB AL-NABAWI]: The hadith attributed to the Prophet Muhammad — 'Use the black seed, for it contains a cure for every disease except death' (Sahih al-Bukhari, 5688) — gave Nigella sativa immense authority in Islamic medical culture and ensured its continued prominence in Persian Tibb-e-Sonnati after the Islamic conquest, where prophetic sanction reinforced the earlier Avicennian and pre-Islamic Persian medical traditions.

segments.title_inferred: Rose (Gol-e Sorkh) in Persian Traditional Medicine
segments.original_text: Plant: Rose — Gol (گل سرخ) — rosewater, antidepressant, heart
segments.translation:
ROSE — Gol-e Sorkh (گل سرخ; romanized: Gol-e Sorkh; lit. 'Red Flower'). Arabic: Ward (وَرْد). Modern identification: Rosa damascena Mill. (Damask Rose), with Rosa gallica L. also historically referenced in Persian sources.

---

MIZAJ (Temperament / [Tab']: In the Galenic-Avicennian humoral framework as adopted into Persian [Tibb-e Sonnati], the rose is classified as Cold [sard] in the First Degree and Dry [khoshk] in the First to Second Degree. Rosewater (Golab; گلاب) is considered more temperate [mo'tadel] than the dried petal, owing to the distillation process moderating its astringency.

---

AVICENNA'S CANON (Al-Qānūn fī al-Ṭibb / القانون في الطب):
Ibn Sina (Avicenna, 980–1037 CE) addresses the rose extensively in Book II of the Canon (Mufradat / Simple Drugs). He describes Ward (rose) as possessing a binding [qabiz] and strengthening [muqawwī] action, particularly upon the heart [qalb] and the vital spirit [ruh-e hayawani]. Key Avicennian therapeutic attributions include:

1. CARDIAC TONIC: Avicenna places rose among the primary [mufarrihat al-qalb] — 'gladdeners of the heart' — a category of substances believed to strengthen the vital faculty seated in the heart. Dried rose petals, rosewater, and rose conserve [rob-e ward] are all cited as beneficial in palpitations [khafaqan], melancholic anxiety, and weakness of the cardiac faculty.

2. ANTIDEPRESSANT / MOOD ELEVATION: Rose is listed among remedies for [waswas] (intrusive melancholic thought) and [malikhuliya] (melancholia / black bile excess). The pleasant fragrance [bu-ye khosh] of rose is held to act directly upon the animal spirit, dispelling oppressive humors. Inhalation of rosewater vapor and wearing rose garlands are both noted as auxiliary treatments.

3. CEREBRAL COOLING: In conditions of hot and dry temperament of the brain — including febrile headache [sar-dard-e garmi], sleeplessness [bi-khwabi], and delirium — Avicenna recommends topical application of rosewater or cold rose-oil infusion to the forehead and temples.

4. DIGESTIVE AND HEPATIC USE: Rose is described as mildly astringent [qabiz-e layyem], useful in diarrhea of bilious origin, and as a tonic for the liver [kabid].

---

THERAPEUTIC PROPERTIES (Persian Tibb-e Sonnati summary):
- Antidepressant and anxiolytic [mufarreh-e ruh]
- Cardiac strengthener [muqawwī-ye qalb]
- Cerebral coolant and nervine [mubarred-e dimagh]
- Mild astringent [qabiz]
- Anti-inflammatory for eyes and mucous membranes [moletahem]
- Digestive regulator
- Emmenagogue in moderate doses [mohayej-e hayz] — noted with caution

---

PREPARATION METHODS (Persian tradition):

1. GOLAB (گلاب) — Rosewater: Steam distillation of fresh Rosa damascena petals, a technique perfected in the Persian city of Kashan (Qamsar region) from at least the 10th century CE. Used orally diluted in water as a cardiac tonic and antidepressant; applied topically to the forehead for febrile headache; added to foods and sherbets [sharbat] for digestive benefit.

2. ROB-E WARD (رُبّ الوَرد) — Rose Conserve / Electuary: Fresh rose petals macerated and preserved with honey or sugar. Classical formulation for cardiac weakness, melancholia, and convalescent debility. Taken by the spoonful.

3. ROGHAN-E GOL (روغن گل) — Rose Oil / Infused Oil: Rose petals macerated in sesame or almond oil under sunlight [aftab-dide] for several days. Applied topically for headache, skin inflammation, and muscle tension.

4. SHARBAT-E WARD (شربت ورد) — Rose Syrup / Sherbet: Decoction of rose petals with sugar and a few drops of rosewater. Classic Persian remedy for heart palpitations, grief [anduh], and general debility of the vital faculty.

5. BUKHUR (بُخور) — Fumigation / Aromatic Inhalation: Dried rose petals or rosewater heated over coals; vapor inhaled for melancholia, anxiety, and feverish mental agitation.

6. POWDER OF DRIED PETALS (Gard-e Gol-e Sorkh): Ground and sieved dried petals administered in honey for diarrhea, intestinal weakness, and uterine inflammation.

---

CONDITIONS TREATED:
- Depression, grief, and melancholia [malikhuliya, anduh]
- Heart palpitations [khafaqan] and cardiac weakness
- Febrile and tension headache [sar-dard]
- Insomnia [bi-khwabi]
- Anxiety and intrusive thought [waswas]
- Bilious diarrhea
- Eye inflammation [warm-e chashm] — topical rosewater wash
- Oral and throat inflammation — gargle with rosewater
- Skin inflammation and redness
- Uterine and gynecological complaints (with caution)

---

ZOROASTRIAN AND PRE-ISLAMIC PERSIAN USES:
Pre-Islamic Zoroastrian documentation of the rose is attested in Pahlavi [Middle Persian] liturgical and agricultural texts, though specific medicinal formularies are fragmentary. Documented and inferred associations include:

1. SACRED ASSOCIATION WITH ANAHITA: The rose (Old Persian: *varda-; Avestan cognate attested in Iranian loanword traditions) held strong association with Anahita (Arədvī Sūrā Anāhitā), the Zoroastrian divinity of water, fertility, and healing. Roses and aromatic flowers were offered at her fire temples [atashkadeh], and rosewater was used ritually in purification [taharah-equivalent] rites at Anahita shrines, most notably at the temple complex of Estakhr (near Persepolis).

2. NOWRUZ AND SPRING RITUAL: The blooming of the rose in late spring coincided with Zoroastrian festival calendars. Rose petals were scattered at Nowruz (Persian New Year) and at the festival of Amordad (the Amesha Spenta presiding over plants and the vegetable kingdom), as an offering honoring the divine order [Asha] manifest in the flowering world.

3. FUNERARY AND PURIFICATION USE: Rosewater and rose-scented preparations appear in Zoroastrian purification contexts [Bareshnum] associated with ritual cleansing. While systematic documentation is limited, later Pahlavi commentary and the continuity of Persian practice strongly suggest pre-Islamic origins for rosewater's purificatory role.

4. DAENA AND THE SOUL'S PASSAGE: Later Zoroastrian eschatological texts describe the soul encountering a beautiful fragrant maiden (the Daena) at the Chinvat Bridge; commentators and folk tradition frequently associate this figure with the fragrance of roses, though this association is largely interpretive rather than textually explicit.

5. MANUSCRIPT EVIDENCE NOTE: The Bundahishn (Zand-Agahih), the Middle Persian cosmological text compiled c. 9th century CE from older Avestan sources, lists *vard* among the beneficial plants of creation assigned to the protection of Amordad Amshaspand. This represents the most direct pre-Islamic textual attestation of the rose's sacred status in Zoroastrian cosmology.

segments.title_inferred: Borage (Gol Gav Zaban) in Persian Traditional Medicine
segments.original_text: Plant: Borage — Gol gav zaban (گل گاوزبان) — cardiac, depression, fever
segments.translation:
BORAGE — گل گاوزبان (Gol Gav Zaban)

Arabic name: Lisan al-Thawr (لسان الثور, 'ox-tongue'), reflecting the rough, tongue-shaped leaves shared across both Persian and Arabic botanical nomenclature.

MIZAJ (Temperament / [Tabiat]):
Borage is classed in the Persian Galenic-Islamic system as Hot and Moist [Garm va Tar] in the Second Degree. This temperament renders it especially suited to conditions arising from Cold and Dry excess — melancholic states, cardiac debility, and intermittent fevers — as it corrects the humoral imbalance by introducing warmth and moisture to the heart and vital spirit [Ruh-e Hayavani].

AVICENNA'S CANON OF MEDICINE (Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb) REFERENCES:
Ibn Sina (Avicenna, 980–1037 CE) discusses Lisan al-Thawr in Book II of the Canon (Mufradat / Simples) under cardiac [Qalbi] medicines. He notes that borage:'strengthens the heart, drives away sorrow and melancholy [Huzn va Waswas], and gladdens the spirit. Its flowers, decocted in wine or water, are beneficial against palpitations [Khafaqan], weakness of the heart, and fevers arising from putrefaction of yellow bile [Safra].'
Avicenna further remarks that borage facilitates the production of pure blood [Khun-e Latif] and that its leaves, when taken as a tisane, clear obstruction of the liver and spleen. He recommends the flowers above the leaves for cardiac indications, as the floral essence acts more directly upon the vital faculty [Quwwat-e Hayati].

THERAPEUTIC PROPERTIES (Persian Tibb-e Sonnati):
1. Cardiotonic [Mufarreh-e Qalb]: Borage is ranked among the supreme 'gladdeners of the heart' [Mufarrehat], alongside amber, gold, lapis lazuli, and saffron. It lifts the spirits and combats persistent grief [Gham] and anxiety [Waswas-e Nafsi].
2. Antipyretic [Tabi-bar]: Decoctions of the aerial parts reduce fevers, particularly those associated with bilious [Safravi] or inflammatory constitutions.
3. Diaphoretic [Muarreq]: Promotes beneficial perspiration, aiding in expulsion of noxious humors through the pores.
4. Anti-melancholic [Daf'-e Sevda]: Counteracts Black Bile [Sawda], which in Galenic-Islamic medicine is the humoral substrate of depression, despondency, and irrational fears.
5. Pectoral and Demulcent: Soothes the respiratory tract; used in coughs and pleurisy [Zat-al-Janb].
6. Diuretic [Mudirr-e Bawl]: Mild diuretic action noted, useful in urinary retention and kidney heat.

PREPARATION METHODS (Persian tradition):
— Tisane / Decoction [Jushande]: Fresh or dried flowers and leaves simmered in water (approximately 10–15 g dried herb per 250 ml), sweetened with rock candy [Nabat] or honey [Asal], and taken morning and evening for cardiac and mood complaints.
— Syrup [Sharab / Sharbat-e Gav Zaban]: A classical Persian formulation. Flowers macerated in sugar syrup, often combined with chicory [Kasni], violet [Banafsheh], and rose water [Aragh-e Gol-e Sorkh]. This compound syrup — still commercially produced in Iran — is prescribed for palpitations, anxiety, and low-grade fevers.
— Distilled Water [Aragh-e Gav Zaban]: Steam distillate of the fresh flowering herb; one of the most widely used aromatic waters [Araqiyyat] in contemporary Iranian Tibb-e Sonnati. Taken alone or in compound formulations.
— Poultice [Zamenad]: Crushed fresh leaves applied topically to inflamed skin, joints, or gouty swellings.
— Electuary [Jawarshen]: Ground dried flowers incorporated into honey-based electuaries alongside cinnamon, cardamom, and pearl powder for advanced cardiac debility.

CONDITIONS TREATED:
— Cardiac palpitations and weakness of the heart [Dhu'f-e Qalb, Khafaqan]
— Depression, grief, and melancholy [Huzn, Malikhulia, Daf'-e Sawda]
— Fevers, especially bilious and inflammatory types [Hummayat-e Safravi]
— Anxiety and nervous exhaustion [Waswas, Za'f-e A'sab]
— Respiratory inflammation and dry cough [Su'al-e Yabis]
— Skin inflammation and erysipelas [Hamreh]
— Urinary complaints associated with heat

ZOROASTRIAN / PRE-ISLAMIC USES AND SIGNIFICANCE:
Documentary evidence from the Zoroastrian tradition is sparse but suggestive. In the Bundahishn (the Middle Persian cosmological compendium, compiled 9th c. CE from older Avestan-era material), plants of cooling and heart-comforting virtue are associated with the yazata [divine being] Ameretat (Amurdad in Middle Persian), the immortal guardian of plants, health, and the waters of life. While borage is not named explicitly in surviving Avestan texts, herbalists operating within the Zoroastrian milieu of pre-Islamic Persia (attested in Pahlavi medical fragments) employed 'ox-tongue herb' [warz-zaban, reconstructed Middle Persian] in preparations intended to fortify the fravashi [guardian spirit] against sorrow and spiritual depletion — conditions understood as openings for the corrupting influence of Ahriman (the principle of destruction).

In Zoroastrian ritual medicine, plants with yellow-blue flowers — borage's brilliant star-shaped blue flowers and yellow stamens — held symbolic resonance: blue was associated with the heavenly vault (asman) and divine protection, while yellow recalled fire (atar) and the sacred flame. Offerings of aromatic herbs to the fire altar (Atash Bahram) during the month of Amurdad (the seventh month of the Persian solar calendar, roughly late July–August, when borage is in full flower) may have included borage among strewing herbs, though this remains inferential from later ethnobotanical practice rather than explicit Avestan prescription.

Post-Islamic Persian physicians working within Zoroastrian communities (particularly the Yazd and Kerman regions) continued to prescribe borage water as a remedy for spiritual grief [Gham-e Ruhi], maintaining the older association between cardiac medicine and the health of the soul [Ruh], a continuity that blends Zoroastrian and Galenic-Islamic frameworks.

OTHER NOTES:
Borage is an ingredient in the classical Persian compound cardiac electuary Jawarish Gaozaban, a preparation formalized by physicians of the Baghdad school and later elaborated by Persian compilers including Jurjani (Zakhireh-ye Khwarazmshahi, 12th c.) and Aghili Khorasani (Makhzan al-Adwiya, 18th c.). The latter specifies that genuine Gol Gav Zaban must be harvested in full bloom, shade-dried, and stored away from moisture to preserve its volatile cardiac virtues.

segments.title_inferred: Quince (Beh) in Persian Traditional Medicine and Zoroastrian Practice
segments.original_text: Plant: Quince — Beh (به) — digestive, nausea, constipation
segments.translation:
QUINCE [Beh / به]

Persian Name: Beh (به) [romanization: beh]
Arabic Name: Safarjal (سفرجل)
Family: Rosaceae
Modern Identification: Cydonia oblonga Mill.

---
MIZAJ [Temperament / مزاج]:
Quince is classified in Persian traditional medicine [Tibb-e-Sonnati / طب سنتی] as cold [bārid / بارد] and dry [yābis / یابس] in the second degree, according to the dominant canonical tradition. Some authorities, including Ibn Sina [Avicenna], allow for slight variation between the ripe and unripe fruit: ripe quince is considered less intensely cold and dry than the unripe form, which is more astringent and markedly cold. The seeds and their mucilaginous coating [luzujat / لزوجت] are considered moistening in their specific action despite the overall dry temperament of the plant.

---
AVICENNA — CANON OF MEDICINE [Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb / القانون في الطب]:
Ibn Sina treats quince in Book II of the Canon (Materia Medica), under the entry 'Safarjal' [سفرجل]. He describes its compound nature: the flesh is cold and dry with notable astringency [qabz / قبض], making it effective in arresting diarrhea and strengthening a weak stomach [ma'ida-yi za'if / معده ضعیف]. He recommends the roasted or baked quince for digestive complaints, noting that cooking moderates its extreme astringency and renders it more suitable for the stomach. Ibn Sina specifically praises quince jam [murabba-yi beh / مربای به] prepared with honey or sugar as a remedy for nausea [ghasayan / غثیان] and vomiting, particularly in the early stages of illness or in cases of bilious [safrawi / صفراوی] excess. He further notes quince's benefit in cardiac palpitations [khafaqan / خفقان], attributing to its consumption a strengthening and settling effect on the heart, likely mediated through its action on the stomach (a gastrocardiac reflex recognized in Galenic-Islamic medicine). For constipation, he advises moderation: while quince is generally astringent, the seeds boiled in water produce a mucilaginous decoction [joshande / جوشانده] that softens the bowel and is appropriate for cases of dry, obstructed stool [qabz-e yābis].

---
THERAPEUTIC PROPERTIES [Khawāss-e Darmāni / خواص درمانی]:
1. Digestive [Gawārish / گوارش]: Quince, particularly when roasted or prepared as jam [murabba], stimulates and strengthens gastric function. It is prescribed for sluggish digestion [su'-e hadm / سوء هضم] and is considered a classic remedy for a 'cold stomach' [ma'ida-yi sard].
2. Anti-nausea [Dafi'-e Ghasayan / دافع غثیان]: Quince jam with honey is the preeminent traditional Persian remedy for nausea and morning sickness. The scent of the fruit itself was also employed therapeutically.
3. Constipation [Yubs / یبس]: The seed mucilage [khāsiyat-e luzuj-e tokhm / خاصیت لزوج تخم] was specifically used to treat constipation, particularly in individuals with hot or dry constitutions where harsh purgatives were contraindicated.
4. Cardiac tonic [Muqawwi-e Qalb / مقوی قلب]: Recognized as a gentle cardiac strengthener, particularly in cases where palpitations arose from gastric disturbance.
5. Astringent/Hemostatic [Qābi / قابض]: Raw quince and its preparations were used to arrest diarrhea, hemorrhage, and excessive menstrual bleeding.
6. Hepatic support [Muqawwi-e Jigar / مقوی جگر]: Considered beneficial to the liver in classical Persian medicine.
7. Anti-emetic and anti-bilious: Especially effective when nausea was attributed to excess yellow bile [safra / صفرا].

---
PREPARATION METHODS [Dasturat-e Tahiyeh / دستورات تهیه]:
- Murabba-yi Beh [مربای به] (Quince Jam/Preserve): Ripe quince cooked with sugar or honey, often with cardamom and rosewater. The principal Persian traditional preparation; taken by the spoonful for nausea, gastric weakness, and cardiac complaints.
- Joshande-yi Tokhm-e Beh [جوشانده تخم به] (Quince Seed Decoction): Seeds boiled in water to extract the mucilaginous coating; strained and drunk for constipation, sore throat, and dry cough.
- Beh-e Biriyan [به بریان] (Roasted Quince): Quince baked in embers or oven, consumed directly or with honey, for digestive complaints and nausea.
- Sharbat-e Beh [شربت به] (Quince Syrup/Sherbet): Quince juice reduced with sugar; used as a refreshing and astringent drink in febrile conditions.
- Raw consumption: Ripe raw quince eaten before meals to stimulate digestion, or after meals as an astringent digestive.
- Poultice [Zimad / ضماد]: Cooked quince applied externally for inflammation of the joints and hardened swellings.

---
CONDITIONS TREATED [Amraz-e Darmāni / امراض درمانی]:
- Nausea and vomiting [ghasayan va estifraghe / غثیان و استفراغ]
- Weak or disordered digestion [su'-e hadm / سوء هضم]
- Constipation [yubs / یبس], particularly dry constipation
- Diarrhea and dysentery [eshal / اسهال] — addressed by astringent properties of raw or roasted fruit
- Cardiac palpitations [khafaqan / خفقان] secondary to gastric disorders
- Fever — cooling and astringent properties in early-stage febrile illness
- Hemorrhage and excessive menstrual flow
- Sore throat and dry cough — seed decoction
- Bilious headache [sar-dard-e safrawi]

---
ZOROASTRIAN RITUAL AND PRE-ISLAMIC USES [Kārbord-hā-ye Zardoshti / کاربردهای زرتشتی]:
The quince held a place of significance in pre-Islamic Iranian culture predating and running alongside the Zoroastrian textual tradition. While the Avesta does not name quince explicitly by the term 'beh,' the broader class of fragrant, golden fruit [zard-mive / زردمیوه — 'yellow fruit'] associated with purity, solar energy, and the divine Ameshaspand Shahrivar (guardian of metals and mineral creation, but also associated with beneficial matter) has been interpreted by some scholars of Zoroastrian botany as encompassing quince. More concretely documented are the following:

1. Nowruz [نوروز] and the Haft-Sin Table: Quince [beh] has historically featured among the symbolic items on or alongside the Haft-Sin spread in certain Iranian regional traditions. Its golden color and fragrance associated it with auspiciousness, abundance [barakah / برکت], and the renewal of the solar year.
2. Bridal and Fertility Symbolism: In pre-Islamic and syncretically continued Zoroastrian Persian custom, the quince was given to brides as a symbol of fertility and a healthy womb [rahm-e salim]. The act of the bride consuming quince on the wedding night is attested in medieval Persian literary sources and is attributed to pre-Islamic custom.
3. Funerary and Purification Rites: Fragrant fruits including quince were placed near the deceased in certain Iranian traditions as offerings to ease passage and repel malign forces [druj / دروج — Avestan: embodiment of deception and corruption]. The astringent, preservative quality of quince may have associated it symbolically with incorruptibility.
4. Medicinal-religious use in the Vendidad tradition: Zoroastrian priests [mobads / موبدان] recognized the stomach as the seat of bodily health; preparations easing digestion were thus considered spiritually as well as physically purifying. Quince preparations for gastric health fell within this religiously inflected medical worldview.
5. Fire Temple Gardens [Bāgh-e Ātashkade]: Historical records and literary references suggest quince trees were cultivated in the gardens of Zoroastrian fire temples in the Sassanid period, alongside pomegranate and citron, as trees of beauty and healing significance.

---
ADDITIONAL NOTES:
The word 'beh' (به) in Persian also carries the meaning of 'good' or 'better' in ordinary language (cognate function), and some medieval Persian physicians played on this etymological resonance in their writings, suggesting the quince was so named for its manifold benefits. This is likely a folk etymology, but it reinforced the plant's positive cultural valence. The quince remained a staple of Persian pharmacy through the Safavid period and is still widely employed in Iranian household medicine today.

segments.title_inferred: Valerian (Sumbul) in Persian Traditional Medicine and Zoroastrian Practice
segments.original_text: Plant: Valerian — Sumbul (سنبل) — sedative, anxiety, Zoroastrian ritual
segments.translation:
SUMBUL [سنبل / Sumbul]

PERSIAN NAME: سنبل (Sumbul); also rendered سنبل‌الطیب (Sumbul al-Tib, 'the fragrant spikenard/valerian') in Arabo-Persian medical literature. The term سنبل encompasses a cluster of aromatic root-bearing plants; for Valerian proper, the designation سنبل‌الغربی (Sumbul al-Gharbi, 'Western spikenard') or سنبل رومی (Sumbul-e-Rumi, 'Roman/Byzantine spikenard') was employed in medieval Tibb-e-Sonnati to distinguish Valeriana officinalis from the closely related Indian spikenard (Nardostachys jatamansi), the latter styled سنبل‌الهندی (Sumbul al-Hindi).

ARABIC NAME: سنبل الطيب (Sumbul al-Tib); also فو (Faw) in some classical Arabic pharmacopoeias. In Ibn Sina's usage, سنبل may refer to the broader genus; context determines which species is meant.

MODERN BOTANICAL IDENTIFICATION: Valeriana officinalis L. (family Caprifoliaceae / formerly Valerianaceae). The root (radix valerianae) is the primary medicinal part. Confidence: HIGH.

MIZAJ (TEMPERAMENT / CANONICAL HUMORAL QUALITY):
According to Avicenna's Canon of Medicine (Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, Book II, Materia Medica, entry on Sumbul), valerian root is classified as:
— Warm [Garm / گرم] in the second degree
— Dry [Khoshk / خشک] in the first to second degree
This warm-dry mizaj places it among substances that dispel Cold and Moist humoral imbalances, counter excess phlegm [balgham / بلغم], and strengthen the vital organs. Some later Persian commentators, notably in the school of Shiraz, qualified the warming property as moderate, noting that prolonged decoction softens its drying effect.

AVICENNA — CANON OF MEDICINE (REFERENCES):
1. Book II (Mufradat / Simple Drugs): Avicenna describes Sumbul as opening obstructions [ftah-e-sidad / فتح سداد] in the liver and spleen, warming the stomach, and dispersing flatulence [riyah / ریاح]. He notes its efficacy in palpitations of the heart [khafaqan / خفقان] — a category that in Galeno-Islamic medicine subsumes anxiety, nervous agitation, and what would today be termed psychosomatic cardiac symptoms.
2. Book III (On the Heart): Sumbul is listed among cordials [mufarrihat / مفرحات] — substances that gladden the heart, stabilize the vital spirit [ruh-e-hayawani / روح حیوانی], and counter despondency [huzn / حزن] and excessive fear [khawf / خوف].
3. Book IV (Compound Remedies and Fevers): Valerian root appears as a component in theriacal and sedative compound preparations, particularly those indicated for epilepsy [sar'-e / صرع] and tremors.
Avicenna emphasizes that Sumbul 'strengthens the brain and nerves' and is 'useful for those whose sleep is disturbed by melancholic vapors rising to the head.'

THERAPEUTIC PROPERTIES IN TIBB-E-SONNATI:
— Musakkin al-A'sab [مسکن الاعصاب]: Nerve-calmer; sedative of the nervous system [system-e-asabi]. Primary indication for anxiety, restlessness [bi-qarari / بی‌قراری], and insomnia [bi-khwabi / بی‌خوابی].
— Mufarrih al-Qalb [مفرح القلب]: Cardiac gladdenner; used for palpitations, anxiety with cardiac symptoms, and grief-induced illness.
— Qate'-e-Riyah [قاطع ریاح]: Carminative; disperses intestinal wind and colic.
— Mohallel [محلل]: Resolvent; dissolves phlegmatic obstructions in the liver and spleen.
— Moqawwi-ye-Meda [مقوی معده]: Stomachic tonic; warms and strengthens the cold, weak stomach.
— Dafe'-e-Tashhajj [دافع تشنج]: Antispasmodic; counters convulsions and muscular spasm.
— Modir-e-Bawl [مدر بول]: Mild diuretic; promotes urine flow in cases of retention.
— Modir-e-Hayz [مدر حیض]: Emmenagogue; traditionally used to regulate or promote menstruation in cases attributed to cold obstruction.

CONDITIONS TREATED:
1. Anxiety and nervous agitation [waswaas / وسواس; iztarab-e-asabi / اضطراب عصبی]
2. Insomnia, especially that attributed to excess of black bile [sawda / سودا] or cold phlegm
3. Palpitations and psychosomatic heart disorders [khafaqan]
4. Epilepsy and convulsions [sara' / صرع] — adjunctive use
5. Headache of cold-phlegmatic origin
6. Colic and intestinal flatulence
7. Amenorrhea and menstrual irregularity of cold origin
8. Vertigo [dawwar / دوار] and fainting attributed to cold vapors ascending to the brain
9. Melancholy [malankhuliya / مالیخولیا] — specifically the cold, fearful type

PREPARATION METHODS (PERSIAN TRADITION):
1. Decoction [Joshandeh / جوشانده]: The dried root (3–7 grams) is simmered in water for 15–20 minutes, strained, and drunk warm, typically before sleep. Honey [asal / عسل] or cinnamon [darchin / دارچین] may be added to improve flavor and enhance the warming effect.
2. Powdered Root [Poudre / پودر ریشه]: Ground dried root administered in a dose of 1–3 grams with warm water or rosewater [golab / گلاب], particularly for anxiety and palpitations.
3. Macerated Oil [Roghan-e-Sumbul / روغن سنبل]: Root macerated in sesame or almond oil, used externally for massage at the temples and nape of the neck to relieve headache and promote sleep.
4. Electuary [Ma'jun / معجون]: Valerian root incorporated into compound electuaries — such as Ma'jun-e-Sumbul — combining it with musk [moshk / مشک], amber [kahruba / کهروبا], cardamom [hel / هل], and pellitory [akarakara / عاقرقرحا] for the treatment of epilepsy and severe melancholic states.
5. Fumigation [Bakhur / بخور]: In ritual and medical contexts, dried valerian root was occasionally burned as incense; the aromatic smoke was inhaled to calm nervous agitation — a practice bridging the medical and the ritual.
6. Sitz Bath [Neshiman-ab / نشیمن‌آب]: Decoction added to bathwater for uterine complaints and to promote menstruation.

ZOROASTRIAN RITUAL AND PRE-ISLAMIC USES:
The use of aromatic roots in Zoroastrian practice is attested in Avestan liturgical texts and in the Bundahishn (the Middle Persian cosmological compendium). While Nardostachys jatamansi (the Indian spikenard) held the more prominent ritual role as a sacred aromatic [boi-e-moqaddas], Valeriana species native to the Iranian plateau — including Valeriana sisymbriifolium and Valeriana latifolia — appear to have been employed in the following contexts:
1. Sacred Fires [Atash / آتش]: Aromatic plant materials, including dried valerian root, were added to the consecrated fire [atash-e-moqaddas] in Zoroastrian fire temples to produce fragrant smoke [bakhur]. The purifying and spirit-calming properties attributed to the plant aligned with the Zoroastrian concept of maintaining purity [taharat / طهارت] of body and soul in the presence of the divine fire.
2. The Yasna Ceremony: The haoma ritual — the central liturgical rite involving the preparation and consumption of the sacred haoma plant — was accompanied by the use of aromatic substances to purify the ritual space and protect participants from the influence of druj (the Avestan principle of disorder, falsehood, and spiritual pollution). Valerian-type roots may have served as adjuncts in this purificatory function, though the primary aromatic in the Yasna was haoma itself.
3. Healing Incantations [Nirang / نیرنگ]: Pre-Islamic Persian medical-magical texts (preserved in fragmentary form and in later Islamicate transmission) describe the use of aromatic fumigation with sumbul root to drive away evil spirits [divs / دیوان] believed to cause madness, convulsions, and nightmares [kabus / کابوس]. This therapeutic-ritual use seamlessly entered Islamic-era Persian medicine, where the language shifted but the practice persisted — jinn [jinnat / جنیان] replacing divs as the presumed supernatural cause of mental illness.
4. Protective Amulets: Dried valerian root was reportedly carried or placed under pillows as a protective amulet against nightmares and malevolent spirits in both Zoroastrian and early Islamic Persian folk tradition — a usage that persists in some regions of Iran and Afghanistan into the modern period.
5. Nowruz (Persian New Year) Associations: Some ethnobotanical sources record the use of aromatic herbs including valerian in Nowruz purification rites (esfand-suzi type ceremonies), though the dominant fumigant in these rites is wild rue [esfand / اسفند, Peganum harmala].

NOTES ON SYNONYMY AND POSSIBLE CONFUSION:
The term سنبل in classical Persian and Arabic texts is notoriously polysemous. It may refer to:
— Nardostachys jatamansi (Indian spikenard / سنبل‌الهندی) — the prestige aromatic of South Asian origin
— Valeriana officinalis (Western valerian / سنبل رومی or سنبل‌الغربی)
— Nardostachys grandiflora (closely related to jatamansi)
— Asarum europaeum (wild ginger / سنبل‌البری in some sources)
Scholars must determine from context, geographic provenance of the manuscript, and clinical application which plant is intended. For sedative and anxiolytic applications in the Persian mainland tradition, Valeriana officinalis or closely related Valeriana species (V. sisymbriifolium, V. latifolia) are the most probable referents.

segments.title_inferred: Coriander (Gishniz) in Persian Traditional Medicine
segments.original_text: Plant: Coriander — Gishniz (گشنیز) — digestive, detox, cooling
segments.translation:
Coriander [Gishniz / گشنیز; Arabic: Kuzbara / كزبرة] is one of the most widely employed medicinal herbs of the Persian pharmacopoeia [dārū-khāna-yi Īrānī]. In the system of Tibb-e-Sonnati (Persian Traditional Medicine), its mizaj [temperament] is classified as cold and dry in the second degree [sard wa khoshk dar darajah-yi dovvom], meaning it possesses a moderately cooling and desiccating quality without the harshness of extreme temperaments. This temperamental classification situates coriander among the reliable correctives for excessive heat [harārat] and surplus bile [safra].

Avicenna (Ibn Sina, 980–1037 CE), in his monumental Canon of Medicine [al-Qānūn fī al-Ṭibb], Book II (Kitāb al-Mufradāt / Simple Drugs), devotes a substantive entry to coriander. He praises its capacity to 'extinguish bilious fevers, quell nausea, arrest vomiting, and settle the restless stomach.' He further notes its utility as a digestive carminative [mulatif] that disperses intestinal winds and reduces bloating. Avicenna distinguishes between the fresh herb and the dried seed, noting that the seed is the more therapeutically potent form. He cautions that excessive internal use of the raw seed may provoke melancholic vapors [bukhārāt-i sawdāwī] and disturb the rational faculties if taken in large quantity, a concern shared by later Persian physicians including Hakim Muhammad Mu'min in Tuhfat al-Mu'minin.

Therapeutic Properties (as recorded in Persian tradition):
— Digestive aid [hāzim]: stimulates gastric secretion and relieves sluggish digestion [su'-i hazm]
— Carminative [mufattih-i riyāh]: disperses intestinal gas and colic [qulanj]
— Detoxifying [muṭahhir al-dam]: purifies the blood of bilious excess and supports liver function [jigar]
— Febrifuge [mubarrid-i tib]: reduces fever heat, particularly in bilious and inflammatory fevers
— Anti-emetic: arrests nausea and vomiting when prepared as an infusion [julāb]
— Diuretic [mudirr al-bawl]: mildly promotes urinary flow
— Refrigerant [mubarrid]: counters heat of the stomach and intestines
— Antidote adjunct: historically used in compound theriac preparations to moderate hot drug components

Preparation Methods (Persian tradition):
1. Seed decoction [Jūshāndah-yi tokhm-i gishniz]: dried coriander seeds boiled in water, strained and consumed warm for digestive complaints and intestinal gas.
2. Seed infusion [Damsāzī]: seeds steeped in hot water with cardamom and fennel for nausea and bloating.
3. Fresh herb poultice [Ḍimād]: fresh coriander leaves pounded and applied externally to reduce inflammatory swellings and erysipelas [nār-i fārisī].
4. Coriander sherbet [Sharbat-i gishniz]: seeds macerated with sugar, rose water [āb-i gol], and lemon juice, consumed as a cooling summer drink to balance excess heat and prevent bilious headache.
5. Combined powder [Safūf]: dried seed ground and blended with cumin [zireh] and dried ginger [zanjabīl] in measured ratios; taken with warm water after meals for dyspepsia.
6. Oil infusion [Roghan-i gishniz]: seeds macerated in sesame or almond oil, applied to the scalp to cool the brain and relieve heat-induced insomnia.

Conditions Treated:
— Dyspepsia and sluggish digestion [su'-i hazm]
— Intestinal colic and flatulence [naft / qulanj-i riyāhi]
— Bilious fever [tab-i safrawī]
— Nausea and vomiting, including pregnancy-related nausea
— Erysipelas and inflammatory skin conditions [nār-i fārisī]
— Liver heat and jaundice [yaraqān]
— Heat-induced headache and migraine [sarda']
— Urinary retention (mild cases)
— Anxiety and restlessness from excess heat affecting the heart [khafaqān-i ḥarr]

Zoroastrian and Pre-Islamic Iranian Uses:
Coriander (gishniz) holds a documented place in pre-Islamic Iranian botanical and ritual tradition. In Zoroastrian practice, fragrant herbs with purifying [pāk-konandah] properties were accorded spiritual significance, and coriander appears in texts associated with the Avestan concept of spenta [holy / beneficial]. The herb was employed in ritual purification contexts, particularly in association with fire temples [ātashgāh], where aromatic plants were burned or offered as a means of warding off ahriman [the principle of evil / corruption] and purifying the ritual space. Coriander seeds were reportedly scattered during certain Zoroastrian agricultural festivals, particularly those associated with Amordad (the Amesha Spenta guardian of plants and the vegetable kingdom), as an invocation of plant-world benediction. Medicinally, pre-Islamic Persian physicians operating within a Zoroastrian cosmological framework understood coriander's cooling nature as a corrective against the 'fiery excess' that demonic forces [dēwān] were believed to induce in the body. Its detoxifying properties rendered it suitable for use during Nowruz purification rituals, when households prepared herbed dishes including sabzi (fresh herb platters) of which coriander was a component, both for its flavor and its protective, purifying symbolism. The Bundahishn, a Zoroastrian cosmological text, references medicinal plants in the context of Ahura Mazda's gift of healing to humanity, and later commentators identify cooling, digestive herbs of this class within that tradition.

segments.title_inferred: Mint (Nana) in Persian Traditional Medicine
segments.original_text: Plant: Mint — Nana (نعنا) — digestive, headache, fever
segments.translation:
MINT — Nana (نعنا) [Ar. Na'na' / نعناع]

PERSIAN NAME: Nana (نعنا); also rendered Na'na' (نعناع) in Arabo-Persian medical literature. The term is shared across Farsi and classical Arabic botanical vocabulary with minimal orthographic divergence.

MODERN IDENTIFICATION: Mentha spicata L. (spearmint) and Mentha piperita L. (peppermint) are the primary species corresponding to classical Nana; garden spearmint (Mentha spicata) is the variety most consistently referenced in Persian domestic tradition, while Mentha longifolia (wild mint / pudina-ye dashti) served as a common field substitute.

MIZAJ [TEMPERAMENT]: Hot and Dry in the Second Degree, according to the predominant school of Persian Galenic humoral medicine [Tibb-e-Galeniki / طب جالینوسی]. Some authorities — including the commentators on Avicenna — qualify the dryness as tending toward the First Degree in fresh leaf preparations, arguing that moisture retained in the green plant moderates the desiccating quality. Dried or powdered mint is consistently classified as hotter and drier than the fresh herb.

AVICENNA'S CANON [Qanun fi al-Tibb / قانون فی‌الطب]:
Ibn Sina (Avicenna, 980–1037 CE) discusses Nana in Book II of the Canon (Kitab al-Mufradat / کتاب المفردات), the materia medica volume cataloguing simple medicines. Key attributions include:
— Digestive action [hazm / هضم]: Mint was held to fortify the stomach [ma'ida / معده], promote the concoction [tabkh / تبخ] of ingested foods, and dispel flatulence [naft / نفخ]. Avicenna recommends decoction [joshande / جوشانده] of mint leaves drunk warm after meals for sluggish digestion and griping pains.
— Headache [sar-dard / سردرد]: Topical application of mint oil or bruised fresh leaves to the temples was endorsed for headaches arising from cold or phlegmatic [balgham / بلغم] causes; the herb's warming quality was held to dissolve cold vapors ascending to the head.
— Fever [tab / تب]: Mint's use in fever is nuanced in the Canon. Because of its hot-dry mizaj, Avicenna cautions against its internal use in bilious [safravi / صفراوی] or inflammatory fevers; however, for intermittent fevers of phlegmatic origin, mild mint infusion was considered appropriate. External poultices [zomad / ضماد] of crushed mint with vinegar were applied to cool the forehead in hot fevers — the vinegar counterbalancing the mint's own heat.
— Additional indications noted by Avicenna: palpitations of the heart when caused by cold humors, hiccough [sekske / سکسکه], vomiting of phlegmatic origin, and expulsion of intestinal worms.

THERAPEUTIC PROPERTIES (Persian Tradition):
1. Carminative [bad-shekan / بادشکن]: Fresh or dried mint crushed and steeped in hot water; drunk as an infusion [dam-karde / دم‌کرده] to relieve abdominal bloating.
2. Stomachic: Mint vinegar [serke-ye na'na' / سرکه نعناع] — leaves macerated in wine-vinegar for forty days — used as a condiment and digestive tonic.
3. Analgesic (topical): Oil of mint [roghan-e na'na' / روغن نعناع] prepared by cold infusion of leaves in sesame or almond oil, applied to the temples and forehead for headache and to the joints for cold-type pain [dard-e sard].
4. Antipyretic (adjunct): Decoction combined with coriander seed [geshniez / گشنیز] and barley water [ab-e jo / آب جو] administered in low-grade phlegmatic fevers.
5. Expectorant: Steam inhalation of boiled mint leaves for chest congestion [dam-e sard / دم سرد] and catarrh.
6. Topical antimicrobial (empirical): Poultice of fresh mint leaves applied to minor skin eruptions and insect bites; attested in regional Persian folk medicine [tibb-e 'ammi / طب عامی].

PREPARATION METHODS:
— Infusion [dam-karde]: 1–2 tsp dried leaf per cup of boiling water, steeped 10 minutes, strained; the standard digestive and carminative preparation.
— Decoction [joshande]: Leaves simmered 15–20 minutes; used for stronger therapeutic effect in respiratory or intestinal applications.
— Poultice [zomad]: Fresh leaves bruised and bound directly to affected area, or mixed with rose water and applied on cloth.
— Distilled water [araq-e na'na' / عرق نعناع]: Hydrosol obtained by steam distillation of fresh mint; a widely traded product in Persian bazaars and pharmacies [attar-khane / عطارخانه]; taken internally in doses of 30–60 ml for digestive complaints.
— Powder [pud-r / پودر]: Dried and ground leaf incorporated into electuaries [ma'jun / معجون] or sprinkled on food.
— Oil infusion [roghan]: See above.

ZOROASTRIAN USES AND RITUAL SIGNIFICANCE:
Pre-Islamic Persian references to mint occur within the broader context of aromatic and purifying plants associated with Ahura Mazda's creation [Bundahishn cosmology]. In the Bundahishn — the Zoroastrian cosmogonic text compiled in the Sassanid and early Islamic periods from older Avestan materials — aromatic herbs in general are enumerated among the beneficent creations countering Ahriman's corrupting influence on the body.

Mint (identified in some Pahlavi texts as *vayrin* or loosely as nana in later Dari-influenced Zoroastrian manuscripts) held the following documented or inferred uses:
1. Ritual purification [paidag-kardan]: Aromatic herbs including mint were burned or strewn during purification rites [bareshnum / برشنوم], their fragrance considered pleasing to Ahura Mazda and repellent to demonic forces [daevas / دیوان] associated with disease.
2. Nowruz and seasonal offerings: Mint is among the fresh green herbs included on the Haft-Sin table [هفت‌سین] in its evolved form — though the table's precise pre-Islamic antecedents are debated, fresh herbs (sabzi / سبزی) including mint have been associated with renewal, life-force [farnah / فَرَّه], and the goddess Anahita's domain of water and fertility.
3. Healing invocations: Zoroastrian healers [hakim-e Zardoshti] are documented in Sassanid-era sources as combining herbal preparations with recitation of Avestan mantras [manthras], particularly the Ashem Vohu prayer, during the compounding of medicinal preparations. Mint, as a stomach herb, was especially associated with rites addressing demons of disease [Aesm and Apaosha] believed to disrupt digestion.
4. Fire temple use: Dried mint and other aromatics were occasionally added to sacred fire [atash / آتش] offerings in minor domestic rites, though the three grades of sacred fire [Atash Bahram, Atash Adaran, Atash Dadgah] in formal temple practice employed more restricted and prescribed materials.

NOTE ON CONTINUITY: Many Zoroastrian uses of mint passed into Islamic-era Persian folk medicine with theological reframing — the demonic associations replaced by references to the evil eye [chashm-zakhm / چشم‌زخم] and jinn, while the herb's practical applications remained essentially continuous. This syncretic continuity is characteristic of Tibb-e-Sonnati as a living tradition.

segments.title_inferred: Garlic (Sir) in Persian Traditional Medicine and Zoroastrian Practice
segments.original_text: Plant: Garlic — Sir (سیر) — universal Persian remedy, hypertension
segments.translation:
GARLIC — Sir (سیر; romanized: sīr)

Arabic name: Thūm (ثوم)

---

MIZAJ (TEMPERAMENT):
Garlic is classified in Persian traditional medicine [Tibb-e-Sonnati] as hot and dry in the fourth degree [garm va khoshk dar darajeh-ye chahārom — گرم و خشک در درجه‌ی چهارم]. It is among the most heating of all commonly used foods and medicaments, and its use is therefore contra-indicated in those with a sanguine or bilious constitution [mizāj-e damawi ya safrāwi], or in summer months, lest an excess of yellow bile [safrā] be provoked.

---

AVICENNA'S CANON (Al-Qānūn fī al-Ṭibb):
Ibn Sīnā (Avicenna) treats garlic extensively in Book II of the Canon, within the chapter on simple drugs [adwiya-ye mofrad]. He describes its power to dissolve thick, viscid humors [akhlāt-e ghalīẓ va luzuj-dār], to clear obstructions of the liver and spleen, and to excite the faculty of digestion [quwwat-e hāḍima]. He recommends its use in the following conditions:

1. CHRONIC COLD AILMENTS: Garlic is prescribed for chronic colds, phlegmatic fevers [tabhā-ye balghamī], and conditions arising from excessive cold and moisture [bard va rutūbat]. It expels phlegm [balgham] from the chest and lungs.

2. INTESTINAL PARASITES: Avicenna recommends garlic as a vermifuge [dāfi'-e karm], both consumed internally and applied externally as a suppository or enema [huqna].

3. BITES AND VENOMS: Garlic macerated in wine or vinegar is prescribed topically and internally for scorpion stings, snake bites, and the bites of rabid animals. It is considered a theriac [tiryāq] of modest but real power.

4. SKIN DISEASES: Garlic paste applied topically is described as beneficial for leprous patches [baraṣ], vitiligo [wahaq], and chronic scaly eruptions arising from black bile [sawdā].

5. HYPERTENSION AND VASCULAR OBSTRUCTION: Although Avicenna does not use the modern term 'hypertension,' he notes garlic's power to open the vessels [fatḥ-e 'uruq], dissolve coagulated blood [khūn-e munjamod], and reduce heaviness and fullness in the head [soqlet-e sar], which corresponds in humoral terms to the condition now identified as hypertension. His formulation: 'It cuts the thick blood and drives it from the vessels, restoring flow where stagnation has occurred.'

6. RESPIRATORY CONDITIONS: Garlic decocted in water with honey [asal] is recommended for chronic cough [so'āl-e mozmen], asthma [rabu], and pleurisy [zāt al-janb] of cold origin.

---

PREPARATION METHODS (Persian Tradition):

1. RAW CONSUMPTION [khām khordan]: One to three cloves on an empty stomach, swallowed whole or chewed, for systemic benefit. Often taken with bread or walnuts [gerdū] to moderate its intensity.

2. DECOCTION [joushānde — جوشانده]: Crushed garlic boiled with water, sometimes combined with ginger [zanjabīl] and black seed [siyāh-dāne — Nigella sativa], drunk warm for respiratory and digestive complaints.

3. INFUSED OIL [roghan-e sīr — روغن سیر]: Cloves macerated in sesame oil [roghan-e konjed] or olive oil [roghan-e zeytūn] for several days; applied topically to joints [mafāsel] for arthritis [dard-e mafāsel], to the ear canal for otitis, and to the chest for respiratory congestion.

4. VINEGAR MACERATE [sīr-serkeh — سیر سرکه]: Garlic steeped in grape vinegar [serkeh-ye angur], used as a condiment and remedy to aid digestion, lower blood viscosity, and treat chronic skin conditions.

5. PASTE [khameyr-e sīr]: Raw garlic ground with salt and applied as a poultice [żamād] to infected wounds, fungal skin lesions, and areas of localized pain.
