Desmodium adscendens is considered a weed species across most of its range and is not under conservation pressure. It is freely harvested from roadsides and farm margins. Commercial dried herb and tea preparations are available in Ghanaian markets (Makola Market, Accra; Kumasi Central Market) and in West African diaspora grocery outlets in Europe and North America.

SOURCE SURVEYS CITED
Ethnobotanical surveys in Ghana (1978–2019), Nigeria (Yoruba and Igbo survey zones, 1985–2016), Senegal (Casamance and Dakar region surveys, 1990–2010), Mali (Bambara traditional healer interviews, 2003–2012), Côte d'Ivoire (ORSTOM/IRD surveys, 1980–2000), and Burkina Faso (IRSS ethnopharmacology programme, 1995–2015).
segments._label: West African Traditional Medicine — Desmodium adscendens

segments.title_inferred: Griffonia simplicifolia: West African Ethnobotanical Uses and Pharmacological Profile
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Griffonia simplicifolia — 5-HTP source — depression (Ghana)

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Griffonia simplicifolia (DC.) Baill.
Family: Fabaceae (subfamily Caesalpinioideae)
Primary Active Constituent: 5-hydroxytryptophan (5-HTP)
Primary Documented Use: Depression and related neuropsychiatric conditions (Ghana; also recorded across Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso)

---

LOCAL AND VERNACULAR NAMES

• Twi (Ghana): 'Kagya' or 'Kagya dua' (literally: 'hard-seed tree'); also recorded as 'Ntorowa'
• Yoruba (Nigeria): 'Eku-osho' or 'Asoro-jedi-jedi'; some survey records note 'Aidan-gogoro' in reference to its climbing habit, though nomenclature varies by subregion
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): 'Tafashiya'; alternatively transcribed 'Tafasiya' in Northern Nigerian survey records
• Wolof (Senegal): 'Nep-nep' (widely applied to multiple climbing legumes; identification context required); more specifically 'Xam-xam bu dëkk' in some Dakar-region records
• Bambara/Dioula (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): 'Séguéné' or 'Ségué'; also 'Toro-toro' in Burkina Faso survey records
• Fon (Benin/border regions): 'Ahozon'

---

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION

Griffonia simplicifolia is a woody climbing shrub or liana reaching 3–5 metres in height, native to the humid forest zones and forest-savanna transition belts of West and Central Africa. Leaves are simple, alternate, ovate to elliptic, glossy dark green, and coriaceous [leathery]. Flowers are greenish-white, borne in axillary or terminal racemes. Pods are distinctive: flat, oblong, purplish-black at maturity, indehiscent [non-splitting], containing 1–2 large seeds. Seeds are the principal commercial and medicinal part of the plant, being the primary natural source of 5-hydroxytryptophan (5-HTP), a direct precursor to serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) in vertebrate neurochemistry.

---

THERAPEUTIC USES IN TRADITIONAL PRACTICE

1. Neuropsychiatric and Mood Disorders
The most pharmacologically significant traditional use, extensively documented in Ghana (particularly among Akan-speaking communities) and corroborated in Nigerian and Ivorian surveys, is the treatment of states locally described as persistent sadness, loss of appetite, social withdrawal, sleeplessness, and fatigue — a symptom cluster broadly corresponding to depressive disorder in contemporary clinical classification. Healers [traditional practitioners; dibia, babalawo, or okomfo depending on regional tradition] administer preparations of the dried seed or seed powder to patients presenting with these complaints. The seeds are also employed for anxiety states, fear, and what several survey informants described as 'thinking too much' or 'the disease of worrying.' Insomnia is a secondary but frequently cited indication.

2. Pain Management and Headache
Decoctions of the root bark and stem are applied in several traditions for generalised body pain, headache, and migraines. Ghanaian and Nigerian survey records note use for 'head heaviness' and chronic pain syndromes. The serotonergic mechanism of 5-HTP is consistent with observed efficacy in migrainous conditions.

3. Appetite Regulation and Weight Management
In Ivorian and Malian records, seed preparations are documented for suppression of excessive appetite; modern pharmacological research has confirmed 5-HTP's role in serotonin-mediated satiety signalling, lending biochemical plausibility to this traditional application.

4. Fever and Malaria-Associated Symptoms
Root decoctions are recorded in Burkina Faso and northern Ghana for febrile illness, particularly in the context of malaria. The antipyretic use is likely attributable to secondary metabolites (alkaloids, saponins, flavonoids) rather than to 5-HTP directly.

5. Sexually Transmitted Infections and Urogenital Complaints
Bark and leaf preparations are documented in Nigeria (Yoruba practice) for urethral discharge, dysuria, and conditions locally attributed to 'venereal heat.' Antimicrobial properties of bark extracts have been partially characterised in vitro.

6. Skin Conditions and Wound Healing
Leaf poultices are applied topically for skin eruptions, infected wounds, and ulcerations in several survey regions (Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana). Leaf sap is sometimes applied directly to minor cuts.

7. Gastrointestinal Disorders
Bark decoctions are employed for diarrhoea, dysentery, and intestinal cramping. Root preparations are noted for constipation and 'sluggish digestion' in Senegalese records.

8. Antiparasitic Use
Several Nigerian and Ghanaian herbalists report use of seed or root preparations for intestinal worms, consistent with documented alkaloid content with antiparasitic activity.

---

PREPARATION METHODS

A. Infusion (Seed/Leaf)
Dried seeds are ground to a coarse powder and steeped in hot (not boiling) water for 15–30 minutes. The resulting infusion is strained and consumed in small quantities, typically one small calabash cup (approximately 100–150 ml) once or twice daily. This preparation is favoured for mood disorders, insomnia, and appetite regulation. Leaf infusions are prepared similarly and used for milder symptomatic complaints.

B. Decoction (Root Bark / Stem Bark)
Root bark or stem bark is cut into small pieces, boiled in water (ratio approximately 1 part bark to 10–15 parts water) for 20–40 minutes, and reduced to a concentrated liquid. Administered orally in small doses for fever, pain, gastrointestinal complaints, and urogenital infections. Dosage is typically one small cup (50–100 ml) two to three times daily. Some practitioners add other botanicals (e.g., Zingiber officinale, Cymbopogon citratus) to modulate taste and enhance efficacy.

C. Seed Powder (Direct Administration)
In some Ghanaian practices, ground seed powder is mixed with a small amount of water, honey, or palm wine [emu or pito] and consumed directly. This method delivers the highest concentration of 5-HTP. Practitioners advise small, carefully controlled doses due to awareness (expressed in traditional terms) of the potential for adverse effects at higher quantities — notably nausea and purging.

D. Poultice (Leaf)
Fresh leaves are bruised or macerated and applied directly to wounds, skin lesions, and painful joints. The poultice may be bound with a cloth and replaced once or twice daily.

E. Topical Bark Wash
Bark decoction (cooled) is used as a topical wash for infected skin areas and as a genital wash for urogenital complaints in Nigerian practice.

---

PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH AND CLINICAL RELEVANCE

5-Hydroxytryptophan (5-HTP) Content:
The seeds of Griffonia simplicifolia contain among the highest concentrations of 5-HTP recorded in any plant, typically ranging from 6–14% dry weight of seed, with some cultivar and seasonal variation reported. 5-HTP is the immediate biosynthetic precursor to serotonin (5-HT) and crosses the blood-brain barrier efficiently, unlike serotonin itself. Commercial 5-HTP supplements are derived almost exclusively from Griffonia seed extract.

Neuropsychiatric Evidence:
Multiple clinical and pre-clinical studies have examined 5-HTP for major depressive disorder (MDD), fibromyalgia, migraine prophylaxis, and obesity. A frequently cited Italian randomised controlled trial (Poldinger et al., 1991; Angst et al., 1977; van Praag, 1984 series) demonstrated equivalence or superiority of 5-HTP to fluvoxamine in mild-to-moderate depression, with a favourable side-effect profile. Mechanistically, 5-HTP increases central serotonergic neurotransmission, complementing the target pathway of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). The alignment between traditional use for depression/anxiety and established pharmacology is unusually direct and well-supported for a traditionally used plant.

Antimicrobial Activity:
Ethanolic and aqueous extracts of bark and leaves have demonstrated in vitro inhibitory activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhi, and Candida albicans in multiple West African phytochemical studies (University of Ghana, Legon; Obafemi Awolowo University, Ile-Ife; University of Ouagadougou). Minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) varied considerably by extraction method.

Antimalarial/Antiparasitic Activity:
Alkaloid fractions (including griffonine and related compounds) have shown activity against Plasmodium falciparum in vitro, though in vivo efficacy data remain limited. Antiparasitic effects against Caenorhabditis elegans (as a helminth model) have been reported in preliminary studies.

Toxicological Considerations:
High-dose 5-HTP administration can cause serotonin syndrome, characterised by hyperthermia, agitation, tachycardia, and neuromuscular abnormalities. Traditional practitioners in Ghana and Nigeria appear to have empirical awareness of dose-limiting toxicity, reflected in cautious dosing protocols and warnings against exceeding recommended quantities. Interaction with monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), SSRIs, and other serotonergic drugs represents a clinically significant pharmacological risk not typically incorporated into traditional safety frameworks.

---

ETHNOBOTANICAL AND CONSERVATION NOTES

Griffonia simplicifolia has become a commercially important plant due to global demand for 5-HTP supplements, with Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire as the principal export sources. This commercial pressure has raised conservation concerns, as wild populations in forest-margin habitats are being harvested at rates potentially exceeding natural regeneration. Ethnobotanical surveys in southern Ghana (2005–2015) document awareness among traditional healers of declining availability of wild specimens. Some community-based cultivation initiatives have been established in the Brong-Ahafo and Ashanti regions of Ghana.

The plant occupies an important position in local healing systems not only for its specific therapeutic applications but as a symbol of 'medicines for the mind' — a category of botanical treatment that several survey informants indicated was historically under-represented in formal ethnobotanical documentation due to the social stigma surrounding mental illness in many West African communities.

---

SURVEY SOURCES
Ethnobotanical surveys: Ghana (Akan/Twi-speaking communities, forest zone), Nigeria (Yoruba southwest; Hausa-Fulani north), Senegal (Wolof, Serer communities, Casamance region), Mali (Bambara, Dogon communities), Côte d'Ivoire (Dioula, Baoulé communities), Burkina Faso (Mossi, Bissa communities).
Survey period: c. 1975–2020 (composite record).
Documentation method: Semi-structured interviews with traditional healers, herbalists, and community health practitioners; voucher specimens deposited at national herbaria (Ghana Herbarium, Legon; Federal Herbarium, Lagos).
segments._label: West African Traditional Medicine — Griffonia simplicifolia

segments.title_inferred: Ocimum gratissimum (Fever Basil) in West African Traditional Medicine
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Ocimum gratissimum — fever basil — fever, malaria (Yoruba)

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant/Drug Name: Ocimum gratissimum L. (Lamiaceae)
Common English Name: Fever basil; African basil; clove basil

LOCAL NAMES
— Yoruba (Nigeria): Efinrin nla; Efinrin-ajẹ; Efinrin wewe (distinguishing large-leafed varieties)
— Hausa (Nigeria/Niger/Burkina Faso): Daidoya; Tazargade
— Twi (Ghana): Nunum (general aromatic basil term, applied broadly to this species)
— Wolof (Senegal): Odjouma; Aadama niar
— Fon (Benin/Togo): Akokó
— Bambara/Dioula (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): Ntuulu

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Ocimum gratissimum is an erect, aromatic, perennial herb or subshrub reaching 0.5–2 m in height. Stems are woody at the base, branching profusely, and covered with fine hairs [pubescent]. Leaves are ovate to elliptic, 4–10 cm long, with serrated [crenate-serrate] margins, strongly aromatic when crushed due to high concentrations of volatile essential oils, principally thymol and eugenol. Flowers are small, white to pale lilac, borne in elongated terminal racemes [verticillasters]. The plant is widely distributed across sub-Saharan Africa, growing in secondary forest margins, roadsides, farmland borders, and disturbed ground. It is commonly cultivated in household gardens for culinary and medicinal use throughout the survey region.

THERAPEUTIC USES — BY CONDITION AND SURVEY REGION

1. FEVER AND MALARIA
Primary use documented across all six survey countries. Fever basil is one of the most consistently cited antipyretic [fever-reducing] plants in West African ethnomedicine.
— Nigeria (Yoruba practitioners): Leaves are gathered fresh, typically in the morning before the dew has dried, as this is considered the time of peak potency. A decoction [strong tea prepared by boiling] is made by simmering a large handful of fresh leaves (approximately 50–80 g) in 500–750 mL of water for 15–20 minutes. The liquid is cooled, strained, and administered orally in doses of approximately 150–200 mL two to three times daily until fever subsides. In some documented cases, the decoction is combined with the leaves of Vernonia amygdalina [bitter leaf] and/or the bark of Morinda lucida to enhance antimalarial efficacy.
— Ghana (Akan/Twi-speaking communities): Crushed fresh leaves are rubbed directly onto the body of febrile [feverish] patients, particularly children, as an external febrifuge [fever-reducing] measure. A simultaneous oral infusion [preparation by steeping leaves in hot but not boiling water] is often administered. The infusion is prepared by placing fresh or dried leaves in hot water for 10–15 minutes, then straining and sweetening with honey if available.
— Senegal (Wolof practitioners): The plant is prepared as a steam inhalation to treat fever accompanied by headache and nasal congestion. Fresh leaves and stems are placed in a pot of boiling water; the patient leans over the pot covered with a cloth to inhale the vapour [herbal steam bath, known locally as thiouraye-style treatment]. This is considered particularly effective for fevers of a 'cold' or 'catarrhal' [mucous] character.
— Mali and Burkina Faso (Bambara and Mossi practitioners): Root decoctions are occasionally used in addition to leaf preparations when fever is severe or prolonged, with the root considered by some healers to be more potent than the aerial parts.

2. RESPIRATORY CONDITIONS
— Documented across Nigeria, Ghana, and Côte d'Ivoire. Leaf infusions and steam inhalations are used to treat cough, bronchitis [inflammation of the bronchial passages], and asthma. The volatile oils (particularly eugenol and thymol) are believed by practitioners to 'open the chest' and relieve bronchospasm.
— Preparation: Steam inhalation as described above, or oral infusion of leaves taken warm, sometimes with the addition of ginger (Zingiber officinale) and honey.

3. GASTROINTESTINAL DISORDERS
— Diarrhoea, dysentery [severe diarrhoea with blood or mucus], and abdominal colic [cramping pain] are treated with leaf decoctions across all survey countries.
— Preparation (Nigeria, Ghana): A decoction of leaves, sometimes combined with the dried inner bark of Psidium guajava [guava], is taken orally in 100–150 mL doses up to three times daily. Some practitioners add a pinch of potash [local potassium carbonate preparation] to the decoction.
— The antimicrobial properties of the essential oils are considered by practitioners to act against the 'worm' or 'bad organisms' [likely referring to bacterial or parasitic pathogens] causing the condition.

4. SKIN INFECTIONS AND WOUND HEALING
— Documented in Nigeria, Ghana, and Burkina Faso. Fresh leaves are used as a poultice [crushed or macerated plant material applied directly to the skin] for infected wounds, fungal skin infections [tinea, ringworm], boils, and insect bites.
— Preparation: Fresh leaves are bruised or lightly pounded in a mortar, optionally mixed with shea butter (Vitellaria paradoxa) or palm kernel oil as a carrier, and applied directly to the affected area. The preparation is renewed once or twice daily.
— In some documented cases (Côte d'Ivoire), the leaf juice [expressed liquid] alone is applied to fungal lesions without a carrier.

5. HEADACHE AND NEUROLOGICAL CONDITIONS
— Documented in Senegal and Mali. Leaf infusions or crushed fresh leaves applied to the temples are used to relieve headache. Steam inhalation is also employed.
— Some Malian practitioners document use in conditions described as 'convulsions in children' [possibly febrile convulsions secondary to high fever], where a combined leaf bath and oral decoction treatment is applied.

6. SEXUALLY TRANSMITTED INFECTIONS AND UROGENITAL CONDITIONS
— Documented in Nigeria (Yoruba and Igbo practitioners). Leaf decoctions are used as a urogenital wash or taken orally for conditions described as gonorrhoea [Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection] and vaginal infections.

7. ORAL HEALTH
— Documented across Ghana and Nigeria. Chewing fresh stems and leaves is reported to treat oral thrush [candidiasis], toothache, and bad breath. The antimicrobial essential oils are likely responsible for these effects.

PREPARATION METHODS SUMMARY
1. Decoction: Fresh or dried leaves (and occasionally roots) boiled in water for 15–30 minutes, strained, and taken orally. Most common preparation for fever, malaria, and gastrointestinal conditions.
2. Infusion: Leaves steeped in hot water for 10–15 minutes without sustained boiling. Used for respiratory conditions and milder fevers.
3. Poultice: Fresh leaves pounded or crushed and applied topically. Used for skin infections and wounds.
4. Steam inhalation: Leaves and stems placed in boiling water; vapour inhaled under a cloth covering. Used for fever with respiratory symptoms and headache.
5. Direct topical application: Leaf juice or bruised leaves applied to skin without carrier for fungal infections.
6. Oral mastication [chewing]: Fresh stems and leaves chewed in situ for oral health conditions.

PHARMACOLOGICAL AND SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH
A substantial body of peer-reviewed research supports several of the traditional uses attributed to Ocimum gratissimum:

— Antimicrobial activity: The essential oil of O. gratissimum, particularly the thymol and eugenol chemotypes prevalent in West Africa, has demonstrated broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity in multiple in vitro [laboratory] studies against bacteria including Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhi, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Candida albicans. These findings are consistent with traditional uses against infections and gastrointestinal pathogens. (Nakamura et al., 1999; Onajobi, 1986; Nakamura, 1993.)
— Antimalarial activity: Aqueous and ethanol leaf extracts have shown in vitro activity against Plasmodium falciparum [the primary causative organism of severe malaria in West Africa] in several studies, supporting the widespread use as an antimalarial. Synergistic activity when combined with Vernonia amygdalina extracts has been noted in some studies.
— Antipyretic [fever-reducing] activity: Animal model studies have confirmed significant antipyretic activity for aqueous leaf extracts, consistent with traditional use as a febrifuge.
— Anti-inflammatory activity: Several studies document anti-inflammatory effects, likely mediated through inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis [biochemical pathway central to inflammation and fever].
— Antifungal activity: Essential oil preparations have shown notable activity against dermatophytes [fungi causing skin infections such as ringworm], supporting traditional topical use for fungal conditions.
— Antioxidant properties: High phenolic content, including rosmarinic acid and flavonoids, contributes to antioxidant activity documented in multiple studies.
— Toxicity: At high doses in animal models, hepatotoxic [liver-damaging] effects of essential oil preparations have been noted, suggesting caution with excessive or prolonged oral use of concentrated preparations. Aqueous decoctions at traditional doses are generally considered safe.

CONTRAINDICATIONS AND CAUTIONS DOCUMENTED BY PRACTITIONERS
— Pregnancy: Several practitioners across Nigeria and Ghana advise against high-dose decoction use during pregnancy, citing potential uterotonic [stimulating uterine contraction] effects.
— Children under two years: Steam inhalation in proximity to very young children is cautioned against by some practitioners.
— Prolonged use: Some Yoruba herbalists advise against using strong decoctions for more than seven consecutive days without a rest period.

SOURCE DOCUMENTATION
Data compiled from ethnobotanical field surveys conducted in:
— Ghana (Ashanti, Eastern, and Volta Regions)
— Nigeria (Ogun, Oyo, Kano, and Enugu States)
— Senegal (Dakar, Thiès, and Kaolack Regions)
— Mali (Bamako District and Ségou Region)
— Côte d'Ivoire (Abidjan and Bouaké Regions)
— Burkina Faso (Ouagadougou and Bobo-Dioulasso Regions)
Informants included licensed traditional herbalists [herbalists recognised by national traditional medicine associations], market herb vendors, and community elder practitioners. Cross-referencing performed against regional ethnobotanical literature and pharmacognosy databases.
segments._label: West African Traditional Medicine — Ocimum gratissimum

segments.title_inferred: Paullinia pinnata: West African Ethnobotanical Profile — Malaria, Snakebite, and Related Therapeutic Uses
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Paullinia pinnata — akube — malaria, snake bite

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Paullinia pinnata Linn. (Family: Sapindaceae)

LOCAL NAMES
- Yoruba (Nigeria): akube, áàkùbè
- Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): ba'arin daji, tsarkiyar kurege
- Twi (Ghana): ahoma, danhomo
- Wolof (Senegal): mbep, mbeup
- Additional vernacular designations recorded across survey regions include: 'kpetekpete' (Ewe, Ghana), 'liane amère' [bitter liana] (Francophone West Africa, general), and 'saa' or 'sa-bii' (Mandinka/Mali variants).

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Paullinia pinnata is a robust, woody liana (climbing shrub) native to tropical West and Central Africa. It is characterised by pinnately compound leaves bearing 5–9 leaflets with serrate [toothed] margins, tendrils arising from the leaf axils, small white to cream-coloured zygomorphic [bilaterally symmetrical] flowers borne in axillary racemes [elongated flower clusters], and trilobed capsular fruits that turn red to orange upon maturity, enclosing black seeds partially covered by a white aril [seed coat]. The plant is commonly encountered in secondary forest margins, riverine galleries, and disturbed savanna-woodland ecotones [transitional ecological zones] across the survey region.

THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

1. Malaria and Febrile Illness [Fever-Associated Conditions]
The most widely documented therapeutic application of Paullinia pinnata across all six surveyed countries is the management of malaria (locally conceptualised variously as 'fever sickness,' 'iba' [Yoruba], 'zazzabi' [Hausa], or 'sumsum' [Twi]) and associated febrile states. Healers in Ghana, Nigeria, and Côte d'Ivoire preferentially employ the leaves and stem bark. A standard preparation involves a decoction [prolonged aqueous extraction by boiling] of fresh or dried leaves and stem bark segments, boiled in water for 20–45 minutes, cooled, and administered orally in doses of approximately 150–250 mL, two to three times daily until fever abates. In some Senegalese preparations documented by Wolof practitioners, the root bark is additionally incorporated to intensify antipyretic [fever-reducing] and antiparasitic action. Steam inhalation of the decoction vapour is recorded as an adjunct treatment for headache associated with malarial episodes.

2. Snakebite [Ophidian Envenomation]
The use of Paullinia pinnata in the management of snakebite is recorded in Nigeria (Yoruba- and Hausa-speaking communities), Ghana (Akan communities), and Mali. Traditional healers apply a poultice [a moist, locally applied preparation] of pounded fresh leaves directly to the site of the bite, with the aim of reducing local inflammation, inhibiting venom diffusion [spread of venom through tissue], and drawing out toxins. In some preparations, the mashed leaves are combined with the sap or latex of other locally recognised antivenom plants. Concurrent oral administration of a leaf decoction is also recorded, with the dual rationale of systemic [whole-body] detoxification and pain relief. Burkina Faso surveys note additional use of the root in snakebite management, administered as a cold-water infusion [maceration at ambient temperature] rather than a decoction.

3. Rheumatism [Joint and Musculoskeletal Pain]
In Côte d'Ivoire and Mali, the stem bark and leaves are prepared as a warm decoction used both for oral administration and as an external wash or bath for rheumatic pains, arthritis, and general body aches. The preparation is sometimes combined with shea butter (Vitellaria paradoxa) for topical application.

4. Sexually Transmitted Infections and Urogenital Complaints
Nigerian and Ghanaian ethnobotanical surveys document use of root and leaf decoctions for the treatment of gonorrhoea [Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection] and other urogenital complaints characterised by dysuria [painful urination] and urethral discharge. The decoction is administered orally over several days.

5. Wound Healing and Skin Infections
Fresh leaf poultices or dried leaf powder are applied topically to infected wounds, skin ulcers, and furuncles [boils] in multiple surveyed communities. The astringent [tissue-tightening] properties of the plant are invoked by practitioners as a rationale for this use.

6. Gastrointestinal Disorders
Bark infusions and leaf decoctions are used for the treatment of dysentery [severe infectious diarrhoea, often with blood], intestinal worms [helminthiasis], and general abdominal pain in Senegalese and Malian communities.

7. Neurological and Neuropsychiatric Uses
Limited survey data from Nigeria record use of Paullinia pinnata root preparations in the management of epilepsy and convulsive states in children, typically as a decoction administered orally or used in ritual baths.

PREPARATION METHODS — SUMMARY
- Decoction [aqueous extraction by boiling]: Most commonly prepared from leaves and/or stem bark; used for malaria, urogenital conditions, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal complaints. Boiling time typically 20–45 minutes; administered orally or as a topical wash.
- Infusion / Maceration [cold or warm-water steeping]: Occasionally recorded for root preparations, particularly in Burkina Faso (snakebite) and Senegal (general tonic use).
- Poultice [topical application of pounded or mashed plant material]: Fresh leaves pounded and applied directly to snakebite sites, wounds, and skin infections; sometimes mixed with other plant material or animal fat.
- Steam Inhalation: Decoction vapour inhaled as adjunct treatment for malarial headache.
- Powder: Dried and powdered leaf or bark applied topically to wounds.

PHYTOCHEMISTRY AND PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH
Phytochemical [plant-chemical] analyses of Paullinia pinnata have detected the presence of alkaloids, saponins, tannins, flavonoids, terpenoids, and cardiac glycosides [secondary metabolites with cardiac activity] in various plant parts. Key findings from published pharmacological literature include:

- Antimalarial Activity: In vitro [laboratory, cell-based] studies have demonstrated activity of Paullinia pinnata leaf and root extracts against Plasmodium falciparum [the principal causative agent of severe malaria in West Africa], with ethanolic [ethanol-extracted] leaf extracts showing dose-dependent inhibition of parasite growth. These findings are consistent with traditional antimalarial claims, though clinical trial data remain limited.
- Antivenom / Anti-inflammatory Properties: Studies have documented inhibition of phospholipase A2 [a key enzyme involved in venom-mediated tissue damage] by plant extracts, providing a plausible biochemical basis for the traditional use in snakebite management. Anti-inflammatory activity has also been demonstrated in rodent models using carrageenan-induced oedema [experimental inflammation] assays.
- Antimicrobial Activity: Extracts from leaves and stem bark have shown activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial strains in disc diffusion assays [standard laboratory antimicrobial testing], including Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, supporting traditional use in wound infection and gonorrhoea management.
- Analgesic [Pain-Relieving] Activity: Rodent model studies using acetic acid-induced writhing [a standard pain assay] and hot-plate tests have documented significant analgesic activity for aqueous and ethanolic leaf extracts.
- Anticonvulsant Activity: Preliminary rodent studies have reported dose-dependent anticonvulsant effects of Paullinia pinnata root extracts in pentylenetetrazol-induced seizure models, partially corroborating traditional neurological applications.
- Toxicological Note: High-dose administration of root extracts has been associated with hepatotoxic [liver-toxic] effects in rodent studies, and elevated doses in humans are cautioned against in several survey records. Practitioners in Ghana and Nigeria uniformly emphasise that root preparations require careful dosing and should not be administered to pregnant women.

CONSERVATION AND AVAILABILITY
Paullinia pinnata is widely distributed and not currently regarded as threatened across the survey region, though habitat loss due to agricultural expansion is noted as a concern in southern Ghana and southwestern Nigeria. The plant is commonly harvested from the wild; cultivation is not widely practised.

CROSS-REFERENCES
Related taxa utilised in overlapping therapeutic contexts in the survey region include Paullinia cupana [guaraná, not native but occasionally introduced], and other Sapindaceae liana species. Synergistic preparations combining Paullinia pinnata with Morinda lucida, Azadirachta indica [neem], and Khaya senegalensis are recorded in antimalarial compound preparations across multiple survey sites.
segments._label: West African Traditional Medicine — Paullinia pinnata

segments.title_inferred: Allium sativum (Garlic) in West African Traditional Medicine: Ethnobotanical Survey of Uses Across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso
segments.original_text:
Plant/Drug: Allium sativum — garlic (West African use) — infections

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY
Family: Amaryllidaceae (formerly Alliaceae)
Species: Allium sativum L.
Common name (English): Garlic

REGIONAL VERNACULAR NAMES
• Yoruba (Nigeria): Ajo, Ayu
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): Tafarnuwa, Tafarnawa
• Twi (Ghana): Gyeene, Abirigya
• Wolof (Senegal): Laya, Ail (loan from French)
• Bambara/Dioula (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): Tigan-daga, Tiganindaga
• Fula/Fulfulde (Sahel region): Laayo
• Mooré (Burkina Faso): Laayã

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Allium sativum is a perennial bulbous herb of the family Amaryllidaceae, widely cultivated throughout West Africa as both a food condiment and medicinal plant. The bulb (the principal medicinal part) is composed of multiple cloves enclosed within a papery white-to-purple tunic. Leaves are flat, linear, and glaucous. Although likely of Central Asian origin, garlic has been integrated into West African agricultural and healing traditions for several centuries, introduced through trans-Saharan trade routes and later reinforced by colonial-era commerce.

ETHNOBOTANICAL USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

1. Infectious and Microbial Conditions
Across all six surveyed countries, garlic is consistently and prominently employed against a broad spectrum of infectious conditions. Traditional healers [onisegun in Yoruba tradition; boka in Hausa; okomfo-duru in Akan/Twi contexts] prescribe raw or prepared garlic for:
• Respiratory tract infections: coughs, colds, bronchitis, and pulmonary complaints locally described as 'chest tightness' or 'heat in the chest.'
• Gastrointestinal infections: diarrhoea, dysentery, intestinal worms [helminthiasis], and general stomach ailments attributed to 'bad water' or 'spoiled food.'
• Skin and wound infections: infected cuts, boils [furuncles], abscesses, and fungal conditions of the skin and nails.
• Febrile illness: malaria and undifferentiated fevers, particularly in rural areas where Plasmodium falciparum malaria is endemic.
• Sexually transmitted infections: gonorrhoea and urethral discharge are treated in several Nigerian and Ghanaian communities with garlic-based preparations.
• Ear infections: instillation of diluted garlic juice into the ear canal for otitis.

2. Cardiovascular and Metabolic Conditions
In urban and semi-urban contexts across Ghana and Nigeria especially, garlic is widely used for:
• Hypertension [high blood pressure]: consumption of raw cloves or decoctions is prescribed daily to 'clean the blood' and 'reduce pressure.'
• Hypercholesterolaemia and atherosclerosis: described locally as 'fatty blood' or 'hardening of the vessels.'
• Diabetes mellitus: adjunct use for blood sugar regulation, particularly in Senegal and Mali.

3. Antiparasitic Uses
• Intestinal helminthiasis (roundworm, tapeworm, pinworm): oral decoctions and raw consumption.
• Malaria prophylaxis and treatment: widely reported in rural Burkina Faso, Mali, and northern Ghana.

4. Analgesic and Anti-inflammatory Uses
• Toothache: raw clove placed directly on the affected tooth or gum.
• Headache and rheumatic pain: topical poultice applied to temples or inflamed joints.

5. Reproductive and Gynaecological Uses
• Menstrual irregularities and post-partum care: garlic decoctions administered in several Senegalese and Malian communities.
• Aphrodisiac: attributed in Hausa and Yoruba traditions.

PREPARATION METHODS

(i) Raw Consumption
The simplest and most prevalent mode. One to three fresh cloves are peeled and consumed directly, often with honey or groundnut to mitigate pungency. Used chiefly for infections, hypertension, and as a prophylactic tonic.

(ii) Aqueous Decoction [omi-jise / ruwan tafarnuwa]
Fresh or dried cloves are crushed and boiled in water for 10–20 minutes. The resulting liquid is strained and consumed warm, two to three times daily. Used for malaria, intestinal infections, respiratory complaints, hypertension, and diabetes. The decoction is sometimes combined with Zingiber officinale (ginger), Citrus aurantifolia (lime), or Cymbopogon citratus (lemongrass) to enhance efficacy and palatability.

(iii) Cold Infusion / Maceration
Crushed cloves are steeped in cold or room-temperature water overnight. The strained liquid is consumed the following morning on an empty stomach. Preferred for gastrointestinal complaints and as a morning tonic for hypertension.

(iv) Poultice [lepa / miyar garlic]
Fresh cloves are crushed or ground into a paste, sometimes mixed with shea butter [Vitellaria paradoxa], palm oil, or clay, and applied topically to infected wounds, boils, arthritic joints, or the forehead for fever and headache. The poultice may be bound with a cloth and left overnight.

(v) Topical Oil / Expressed Juice
Garlic is pressed or mashed to express its juice, which is applied directly to skin infections, fungal lesions, or instilled into the ear canal for otitis. In some Ghanaian practices, garlic juice is combined with honey for wound care.

(vi) Vapour Inhalation
Crushed garlic is added to boiling water; the patient inhales the steam under a covering cloth. Used for nasal congestion, sinusitis, and upper respiratory infections.

(vii) Alcoholic Maceration / Tincture
In some urban Nigerian and Senegalese contexts influenced by herbalist commerce, garlic cloves are macerated in local spirits (e.g., afofo, sodabi) for one to two weeks. The resulting tincture is used in small doses as a systemic tonic and antimicrobial.

PHARMACOLOGICAL AND PHYTOCHEMICAL BASIS
The primary bioactive constituent of Allium sativum is allicin (diallyl thiosulphinate), formed enzymatically from alliin by the action of alliinase upon crushing or cutting of the clove. Allicin and its secondary degradation products (diallyl disulphide [DADS], diallyl trisulphide [DATS], ajoene, and vinyl dithiins) are responsible for much of the plant's documented biological activity:

• Antimicrobial activity: Multiple in vitro studies confirm broad-spectrum antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Salmonella spp., Streptococcus spp., and Helicobacter pylori. Antifungal activity against Candida albicans and dermatophytes has been demonstrated. These findings provide direct pharmacological support for traditional uses against infections and wound care.

• Antimalaria activity: In vitro studies demonstrate that ajoene and DATS inhibit Plasmodium falciparum growth, though clinical evidence in vivo remains limited. Epidemiological and ethnopharmacological reports from Burkina Faso (Nacoulma-Ouédraogo, 1996) and Mali document consistent traditional use for febrile illness.

• Cardiovascular effects: Multiple randomised controlled trials (meta-analyses by Ried et al., 2016; Stabler et al., 2012) confirm modest but statistically significant reductions in systolic and diastolic blood pressure, as well as reductions in total and LDL cholesterol, validating traditional Ghanaian and Nigerian hypertension use.

• Antidiabetic effects: Animal model studies and some human trials indicate that garlic supplementation reduces fasting blood glucose, possibly via enhanced insulin sensitivity and inhibition of hepatic gluconeogenesis.

• Antihelminthic activity: In vitro studies confirm efficacy against Ascaris lumbricoides and Strongyloides stercoralis, consistent with traditional antiworm applications.

• Anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects: Garlic extracts inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX) and lipoxygenase (LOX) pathways in vitro, providing mechanistic support for traditional use in pain and inflammatory conditions.

NOTED COMBINATIONS AND SYNERGISTIC FORMULATIONS
Traditional healers across the region frequently combine garlic with:
• Zingiber officinale (ginger) — for respiratory and febrile conditions
• Citrus aurantifolia / Citrus limon (lime/lemon) — for antimicrobial and cold remedies
• Honey — for wound poultices and soothing throat preparations
• Moringa oleifera leaves — for nutritional-tonic and antihypertensive formulations
• Azadirachta indica (neem) — for antimalarial compounding in Burkina Faso and Mali

CONTRAINDICATIONS AND SAFETY NOTES (AS DOCUMENTED IN SURVEYS)
Traditional practitioners in several communities advise against:
• High-dose use during pregnancy (abortifacient concern noted anecdotally in Senegal)
• Use prior to surgical procedures due to antiplatelet effects
• Concurrent use with anticoagulant medications
Gastrointestinal irritation (nausea, heartburn) is commonly noted as a side effect of raw consumption, particularly on an empty stomach.

STATUS IN SURVEYED COUNTRIES
• Ghana: Widely sold in markets and used by both herbalists and households; included in the Ghanaian Herbal Pharmacopoeia (2007).
• Nigeria: Commercially available; documented in ethnobotanical surveys in Ogun, Ondo, Kwara, Kano, and Sokoto States.
• Senegal: Common in Dakar market medicine; integrated into both Wolof and Lebu healing traditions.
• Mali: Used by Bambara and Dogon healers; documented by Diallo et al. (2006) and Togola et al. (2005).
• Côte d'Ivoire: Reported in savannah and forest-zone surveys; documented by Tra Bi et al. (2008).
• Burkina Faso: Prominent in Mossi and Bissa healing traditions; documented by Nacoulma-Ouédraogo (1996) and Ouédraogo et al. (2012).
segments._label: West African Traditional Medicine — Allium sativum

segments.title_inferred: Kava
segments.original_text: Rhizoma Piperis Methystici (WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2). Pharmaceutical definition: The dried rhizome and roots of Piper methysticum G. Forst. (Piperaceae). Major active constituents: kavalactones (kavapyrones) comprising 3–20% of dry weight of peeled, dried rhizome, principally kawain (kavain), dihydrokawain (dihydrokavain), methysticin, dihydromethysticin, yangonin, and desmethoxyyangonin. Also contains: chalcones (flavokavains A, B, C), pipermethystine (piperidine alkaloid, concentrated in leaves and stems), starch (~43%), proteins, minerals, and trace amounts of essential oil.
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: The drug consists of the dried rhizome and roots of Piper methysticum G. Forst. (family Piperaceae), peeled and dried. The commercial product may be whole, cut, or powdered. The principal active constituents are the kavalactones (also termed kavapyrones), a class of α-pyrone compounds present at 3–20% of the dry weight of peeled rhizome.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Piper methysticum is a robust, erect, glabrous perennial shrub reaching 1–3 m in height. Stems are woody, jointed, and swollen at nodes. Leaves are alternate, broadly cordate to ovate, 6–20 cm long, with 9–11 prominent veins and a waxy upper surface. Flowers are small, unisexual, borne on cylindrical spikes; the plant is largely sterile and propagated exclusively by vegetative means. The rhizome is large, knotty, and cream-to-brown in colour with a starchy, fibrous interior and a peppery, numbing taste.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to the western Pacific Islands (Melanesia, Micronesia, and Polynesia), including Fiji, Tonga, Samoa, Vanuatu, and Hawaii. Cultivation extends throughout the Pacific region; commercial cultivation also exists in Hawaii and parts of Australia. The plant requires tropical or subtropical conditions, moist fertile soils, and partial shade.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The kavalactone fraction (3–20% dry weight) includes six major compounds: (1) kawain [kavain] (~1.8%), (2) dihydrokawain [dihydrokavain] (~1.0%), (3) methysticin (~1.2%), (4) dihydromethysticin (~0.5%), (5) yangonin (~1.0%), and (6) desmethoxyyangonin (~0.5%). The relative proportion varies by cultivar ('chemotype'). Additional constituents include flavokavains A, B, and C (chalcones); pipermethystine, a piperidine alkaloid found primarily in leaf and stem tissue (not the rhizome); starch (approximately 43%); proteins; minerals; and trace essential oil.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: Kava rhizome extracts (standardised to kavalactone content) are used for the symptomatic relief of mild-to-moderate anxiety, nervous tension, and restlessness. Multiple randomised controlled trials and two published meta-analyses support efficacy over placebo. Extracts have also been investigated for insomnia associated with anxiety and for menopausal anxiety.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: In Pacific Island cultures, aqueous preparations of the fresh or dried rhizome (prepared by masticating or grinding and then straining in water) have been consumed ceremonially, socially, and medicinally for centuries. Traditional indications include: anxiety, insomnia, restlessness, urinary tract infections (as a diuretic and anti-inflammatory), headache, muscle tension, and as a general relaxant and social anxiolytic. Kava is listed in the pharmacopoeias of Germany (Commission E) and other European regulatory frameworks as a plant medicine for nervous anxiety, stress, and restlessness.

PHARMACOLOGY — MECHANISMS OF ACTION:
• CNS activity: Kavalactones modulate GABA-A receptor function, though not via the benzodiazepine binding site; they also reversibly inhibit voltage-gated sodium channels and reduce neuronal excitability. Kawain and dihydromethysticin have demonstrated anticonvulsant properties in animal models.
• Anxiolytic action: In vivo studies in rodents demonstrate anxiolytic effects comparable to diazepam in elevated plus-maze and other anxiety paradigms, without marked sedation at standard doses.
• Analgesic/antispasmodic effects: Kavalactones inhibit cyclo-oxygenase (COX) activity and demonstrate direct muscle-relaxant properties in isolated smooth and skeletal muscle preparations.
• Dopaminergic activity: Yangonin binds to CB1 cannabinoid receptors; kawain inhibits neuronal dopamine re-uptake, contributing to mood-elevating effects.
• Antimicrobial: In vitro studies demonstrate activity against Candida species, Aspergillus, and some Gram-positive bacteria.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE:
1. Volz & Kieser (1997): Double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled multicentre trial (n=101, 25 weeks). Standardised kava extract WS 1490 (70% kavalactones, 300 mg/day [equivalent to 210 mg kavalactones/day]) versus placebo in patients with non-psychotic anxiety disorders (ICD-10). Hamilton Anxiety Scale (HAM-A) scores declined significantly more in the kava group (mean reduction −15.0 vs −9.6; p<0.01). No clinically relevant adverse effects attributable to treatment.
2. Pittler & Ernst meta-analysis (2000, updated 2003): Systematic review and meta-analysis of seven randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials using standardised kava extract. Pooled weighted mean difference on HAM-A: −3.9 points (95% CI −5.7 to −2.2) favouring kava over placebo. Concluded that standardised kava extract is a relatively safe and effective symptomatic treatment for anxiety.
3. De Leo et al. (2000): Randomised controlled trial in peri-menopausal women (n=40) with anxiety but without severe vasomotor symptoms. Kava extract (100 mg three times daily) produced significant reduction in Kupperman Index and HAM-A scores compared to placebo, with no adverse hormonal effects.

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
• Common (at standard doses): Mild gastrointestinal complaints (nausea, stomach upset), allergic skin reactions.
• Dermatological: 'Kava dermopathy' (kawaism) — reversible ichthyosiform scaling of skin with chronic heavy use of traditional aqueous preparations; resolves upon cessation.
• Hepatotoxicity [IMPORTANT SAFETY SIGNAL]: Post-marketing surveillance identified rare but serious cases of hepatotoxicity (hepatitis, liver failure, including cases requiring transplantation) associated primarily with ethanolic or acetonic solvent-extracted kava products or with products manufactured from aerial plant parts (leaves, stems) containing elevated pipermethystine. Causality remains debated; some cases involved concomitant medications or pre-existing liver disease. Several national regulatory agencies (EU, Canada, Australia) issued warnings or market withdrawals (2001–2002).
• Neurological: High-dose or prolonged use may cause mild sedation, impaired reaction time, and potentiation of CNS depressants.
• Drug interactions: Potentiation of CNS depressants (alcohol, benzodiazepines, barbiturates); potential inhibition of cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, CYP3A4) — clinical significance not fully established but clinically relevant interactions possible.

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
• Known hypersensitivity to Piper methysticum or any Piperaceae.
• Pregnancy and lactation (insufficient safety data; kavalactones cross the placenta and may be excreted in breast milk).
• Pre-existing hepatic disease or elevated liver enzymes.
• Endogenous depression (kavalactones may mask symptoms and delay diagnosis).
• Concurrent use of hepatotoxic drugs or CNS depressants without medical supervision.
• Children under 12 years of age.

DOSAGE — WHO-RECOMMENDED ADULT DOSE:
• Oral: Standardised dry extract (kavalactones 70%): 100–200 mg kavalactones per dose, up to 3 times daily (total 210–240 mg kavalactones/day). Do not exceed 6 months' continuous use without medical supervision.
• Aqueous extract (traditional preparation): 60–120 ml of standardised aqueous root extract.
• Tincture (1:2, 60–70% ethanol): 3–6 ml up to three times daily.
• Liver function monitoring is recommended for prolonged use. Patients should be advised to report any signs of jaundice immediately.

segments.title_inferred: Senna (Sennosides, Anthraquinone Laxative)
segments.original_text: Folium Sennae — WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2. Pharmaceutical source: dried leaflets of Senna alexandrina Mill. (syn. Cassia senna L.; Cassia acutifolia Delile; Cassia angustifolia Vahl), family Leguminosae (Fabaceae). Principal active constituents: dianthrone glycosides (sennosides A, B, C, D; collectively 1.5–3.0% dry weight by pharmacopoeial standard, minimum 2.5% sennosides calculated as sennoside B per European Pharmacopoeia); also present: rhein, aloe-emodin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin, mucilage, and resin. Two commercial varieties recognized: Alexandrian (Khartoum) Senna, derived principally from C. acutifolia, and Tinnevelly (Indian) Senna, derived principally from C. angustifolia.
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: Folium Sennae consists of the dried leaflets of Senna alexandrina Mill. (Leguminosae/Fabaceae), encompassing both the Alexandrian and Tinnevelly commercial varieties. The drug must contain not less than 2.5% sennosides, calculated as sennoside B on a dry-weight basis, in accordance with pharmacopoeial standards.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Senna alexandrina is a small, erect, branching shrub reaching 60–90 cm in height. Leaves are alternate, pinnate, with 4–8 pairs of lanceolate-ovate leaflets, each 2–4 cm long and 0.5–1.5 cm wide, with an asymmetric base and an acute apex. The leaflet surface is pale green to greyish-green, glabrous (smooth) or finely pubescent (slightly hairy) on the lower surface. Flowers are bright yellow, borne in axillary racemes. Fruit is a flat, papery, broadly oblong pod, 4–7 cm long and 1.5–2.5 cm wide, containing 5–7 seeds. Seeds are obovate, compressed, olive-green to dark brown.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to semi-arid regions of northeastern Africa and the Arabian Peninsula. Alexandrian Senna is sourced primarily from wild and cultivated populations in Sudan and Egypt (the Nile valley). Tinnevelly Senna is commercially cultivated in southern India, particularly in Tamil Nadu (Tirunelveli and Ramanathapuram districts). Cultivation has extended to Somalia, Pakistan, and parts of China. The plant favours sandy or loamy well-drained soils in tropical and subtropical dry climates.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The principal pharmacologically active constituents are dianthrone [anthraquinone-derived] glycosides, specifically:
— Sennoside A (rhein dianthrone-8,8′-diglucoside): major active constituent
— Sennoside B (stereoisomer of sennoside A)
— Sennoside C (rhein-aloe-emodin heterodianthrone glucoside)
— Sennoside D (stereoisomer of sennoside C)
Total sennoside content: 1.5–3.0% dry weight; pharmacopoeial minimum is 2.5% calculated as sennoside B. Additional constituents include free anthraquinone aglycones (rhein, aloe-emodin, emodin), flavonoids (kaempferol, isorhamnetin, kaempferol-3-glucoside, kaempferol-3-rutinoside), naphthalene glycosides (tinnevellin glucoside), mucilage polysaccharides, and resins.

MEDICINAL USES — Clinical Evidence: Senna is used as a short-term treatment for constipation and for bowel cleansing prior to diagnostic procedures (e.g., colonoscopy, radiological examination). It is one of the most extensively studied herbal laxatives and is approved in numerous national pharmacopoeias and regulatory frameworks (including USP, BP, Ph. Eur.) as a stimulant [irritant] laxative. The primary clinically supported indications are: (1) occasional constipation in adults and children over 12 years; (2) pre-procedural bowel evacuation.

MEDICINAL USES — Traditional: In traditional Unani and Ayurvedic medicine, senna leaf has been used for centuries as a purgative for constipation, 'biliousness,' intestinal parasites, and skin disorders. Arabic medicinal texts (including those of Ibn Sina/Avicenna, 10th–11th century CE) reference senna as a gentle but reliable purge. In African traditional medicine it is also used topically for skin infections, and decoctions have been used for gonorrhoea and fever, though these uses lack clinical evidence.

PHARMACOLOGY — Mechanism of Action: Sennosides are pharmacologically inactive prodrugs that are not absorbed in the upper gastrointestinal tract. Upon reaching the colon, they are hydrolysed by bacterial beta-glucosidases to yield the active metabolite rhein-anthrone [rhein-9-anthrone]. Rhein-anthrone exerts two principal effects on colonic mucosa: (1) Secretory effect — stimulates active chloride secretion into the colonic lumen via inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase on colonocyte basolateral membranes, resulting in passive water and electrolyte secretion and increased luminal fluid; (2) Motility effect — inhibits stationary colonic contractions (haustration) while stimulating propulsive peristalsis [high-amplitude propagated contractions], thereby accelerating colonic transit. Net result: softer, more easily passed stools within 6–12 hours of oral administration. In vitro studies confirm rhein-anthrone stimulates PGE2 [prostaglandin E2] production, contributing to secretory and motility effects. Animal models (rat and guinea pig) demonstrate dose-dependent laxation with sennoside doses of 5–100 mg/kg.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE:
1. Constipation (general): Multiple randomised controlled trials have demonstrated that senna preparations (standardised to sennoside content) are significantly more effective than placebo in increasing stool frequency and reducing straining in adults with chronic or occasional constipation. In one double-blind trial (n=77), senna syrup (sennoside content 15 mg/5 mL) produced significantly more bowel movements than placebo over a one-week period (p<0.01), with a mean increase of 2.1 additional stools per week in the treatment group.
2. Opioid-induced constipation in palliative care: A randomised trial comparing senna with lactulose in cancer patients receiving opioid analgesics (n=91) found equivalent laxative efficacy (bowel movement frequency, stool consistency) but senna was associated with lower rates of abdominal cramping and flatulence and was preferred by patients; no statistically significant difference in adverse events between groups.
3. Pre-colonoscopy bowel preparation: Senna-based bowel preparation (senna plus bisacodyl or senna alone at high doses) has been evaluated in several RCTs against polyethylene glycol (PEG) electrolyte solutions. A systematic review (4 trials, n>500) found senna-based preparation to be non-inferior to PEG for overall bowel cleansing quality, with comparable adenoma detection rates and better patient tolerance/acceptability scores.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Most adverse effects are dose-related and reversible upon discontinuation. Common (dose-dependent): abdominal cramping and pain, diarrhoea, nausea. Prolonged or excessive use (months to years) may cause: electrolyte imbalance (particularly hypokalaemia), which can potentiate cardiac glycoside [digoxin] toxicity; 'melanosis coli' [pseudomelanosis coli] — brown-black pigmentation of the colonic mucosa, a benign and reversible phenomenon caused by lipofuscin accumulation in macrophages; laxative dependence and atonic colon ('cathartic colon') following years of misuse. Rare: allergic reactions (urticaria, contact dermatitis in occupationally exposed handlers). Drug interactions: hypokalaemia from prolonged senna use may potentiate the effects of cardiac glycosides (digoxin), antiarrhythmic drugs, and thiazide diuretics; concurrent use with other potassium-depleting drugs (corticosteroids, thiazides) is inadvisable.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Senna is contraindicated in: intestinal obstruction or stenosis; acute inflammatory bowel diseases (Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis during acute flares); appendicitis or undiagnosed acute abdominal symptoms; severe dehydration with electrolyte depletion; children under 12 years (unless under medical supervision); pregnancy (stimulant laxatives are generally avoided, particularly in the first trimester; small amounts of sennosides may pass into breast milk — use with caution during lactation). Not for use as a long-term treatment without medical supervision.

DOSAGE — WHO-Recommended Adult Doses: Oral dose should be standardised to sennoside content, taken at bedtime (onset of action: 6–12 hours). Dried leaf (as infusion/tea): 0.6–2.0 g per dose, once daily at night. Standardised preparations: equivalent to 15–30 mg sennosides (calculated as sennoside B) per day, in a single evening dose. Maximum duration of unsupervised use: 10 consecutive days. For bowel preparation prior to procedures: higher single doses (up to 150 mg sennosides) may be used under clinical supervision. Paediatric use (ages 6–12): half the adult dose, under medical advice only.

segments.title_inferred: Dandelion Root
segments.original_text: Radix Taraxaci — the dried or fresh root and rhizome of Taraxacum officinale Weber ex Wiggers (Asteraceae/Compositae). Key chemical constituents include: sesquiterpene lactones (taraxacin, taraxacerin; bitter index up to 1:5000); triterpenes (taraxol, taraxerol, taraxasterol, beta-sitosterol, stigmasterol); inulin (up to 40% of dry weight in autumn-harvested roots); phenolic acids (caffeic acid, chlorogenic acid, chicoric acid); flavonoids (luteolin, luteolin-7-glucoside); hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives; phytosterols; polysaccharides; minerals including potassium (up to 4.5% dry weight); vitamins A, B, C, D; and trace tannins. Sesquiterpene bitter principles (eudesmanolide and germacranolide types) are considered primary active constituents for digestive pharmacological activity.
segments.translation:
Dandelion root is derived from the dried or fresh root and rhizome of Taraxacum officinale, a member of the daisy family (Asteraceae). It has a long history of use in European, Chinese, and Native American traditional medicine, and appears in numerous national pharmacopoeias including those of Austria, Belgium, the Czech Republic, France, Hungary, Poland, Russia, and Switzerland.

**Definition and Plant Part Used:** The medicinal material (drug substance) consists of the whole, cut, or powdered dried root and rhizome, harvested either in spring (before flowering, lower inulin content) or autumn (after seed set, highest inulin content, up to 40% dry weight). The fresh root preparation is also officially recognized.

**Botanical Description:** Taraxacum officinale is a perennial herbaceous plant with a deep, fleshy taproot (typically 5–30 cm long, 5–20 mm diameter), brown to dark brown externally and white to yellowish internally, exuding a milky latex when cut. Leaves form a basal rosette and are oblanceolate to spatulate in outline, deeply pinnately lobed (runcinate), with lobes pointing backward toward the base. The hollow flowering scape is erect (5–40 cm), bearing a solitary bright yellow composite flower head composed entirely of ligulate (ray) florets. Achene fruits bear a persistent feathery white pappus (the familiar 'clock' or 'blowball'). Chromosome number 2n = 24 (diploid sexual) or polyploid in many apomictic microspecies.

**Geographical Distribution:** Native to Eurasia; now naturalized throughout temperate regions worldwide including North America, South America, Australia, and New Zealand. Cultivated commercially in Europe (notably France, Germany, Belgium) and harvested from wild populations across Eastern Europe and Russia. Grows in disturbed habitats, meadows, lawns, roadsides, and cultivated ground, from sea level to alpine zones.

**Chemical Constituents (detail):**
- *Sesquiterpene lactones (bitter principles):* Taraxacin and taraxacerin (eudesmanolide and germacranolide skeletons); responsible for characteristic bitter taste (bitter index up to 1:5,000) and considered primary drivers of choleretic and digestive activity.
- *Triterpenes and phytosterols:* Taraxol, taraxerol, taraxasterol, beta-sitosterol, stigmasterol, alpha-amyrin.
- *Inulin:* Fructooligosaccharide polymer; up to 40% dry weight in autumn root (2–3% in spring); prebiotic properties.
- *Phenolic acids and hydroxycinnamic derivatives:* Caffeic acid, chlorogenic acid, chicoric acid, monocaffeoyltartaric acid.
- *Flavonoids:* Luteolin, luteolin-7-O-glucoside, apigenin derivatives.
- *Polysaccharides:* Fructans, pectins.
- *Minerals:* Potassium (notably high, up to 4.5% dry weight), calcium, iron, manganese.
- *Vitamins:* Provitamin A (beta-carotene), ascorbic acid (vitamin C), tocopherols, B-vitamins.
- *Other:* Taraxinic acid beta-D-glucopyranosyl ester (bitter glucoside); scopoletin (coumarin); traces of tannins.

**Pharmacology and Mechanisms of Action:**
1. *Choleretic effect:* Animal studies (rat, dog) demonstrate that aqueous and hydroethanolic root extracts significantly increase bile flow and bile salt secretion. Sesquiterpene lactones and phenolic acids are implicated as primary active principles. The bitter principles are thought to act via reflex stimulation of the vagus nerve and direct stimulation of hepatocyte bile production.
2. *Cholagogue effect:* Root extracts have been shown to promote contraction of the gallbladder and relaxation of the sphincter of Oddi, facilitating bile release into the duodenum — supporting traditional use in biliary dyskinesia and sluggish bile flow.
3. *Diuretic activity:* Aqueous root (and especially leaf) extracts demonstrate diuretic effects in animal models. Notably, the high endogenous potassium content may offset urinary potassium loss, unlike many synthetic diuretics — a pharmacologically important distinction.
4. *Anti-inflammatory activity:* In vitro studies show inhibition of COX-2 and nitric oxide (NO) production by lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-stimulated macrophages; taraxasterol and luteolin implicated.
5. *Antioxidant activity:* Root extracts demonstrate free radical scavenging activity (DPPH assay) attributable to phenolic acids and flavonoids.
6. *Prebiotic/gut microbiome effects:* Inulin fractions support Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus growth in vitro.
7. *Hypoglycaemic effect:* Animal (streptozotocin-diabetic mouse/rat) studies suggest root extracts reduce fasting blood glucose, possibly via alpha-glucosidase inhibition and insulin sensitization.
8. *Anti-tumour activity (in vitro):* Taraxasterol and aqueous extracts show cytotoxic activity against human breast, prostate, and melanoma cell lines in vitro; clinical significance not established.

**Clinical Evidence (summarized):**
- *Study 1 — Choleretic/digestive use:* An open-label clinical study (Germany, n=24, early post-cholecystectomy patients) using a standardized combination herbal preparation containing Taraxacum root extract demonstrated improved digestive symptom scores (bloating, dyspepsia, nausea) over a 4-week treatment period vs. baseline. Limitations: no placebo arm, combination product confounds attribution.
- *Study 2 — Diuretic effect:* A small pilot human trial (n=17 healthy volunteers, University of Maryland, 2011, published in the *Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine*) evaluated fresh leaf extract rather than root, reporting statistically significant increases in urinary frequency and volume over 5-hour measurement windows following standardized doses. Root-specific human diuretic trials remain sparse; this study is frequently cited in monograph contexts as proof-of-concept for the genus.
- *Study 3 — Dyspepsia:* Clinical use within the approved German Commission E monograph context relies on aggregate evidence: dandelion root (alone and in combination with other choleretic herbs) is described as effective in the symptomatic treatment of dyspeptic complaints including fullness, flatulence, and temporary loss of appetite. The Commission E concluded sufficient evidence exists to approve the use of root and herb for restoration of disturbed bile flow and dyspeptic complaints. Formal placebo-controlled RCT data for root alone remain limited as of the WHO Vol. 2 publication date.

**Adverse Reactions:**
- Generally well tolerated at recommended doses.
- Contact dermatitis reported in sensitive individuals (sesquiterpene lactone sensitization; cross-reactivity with other Asteraceae members including chrysanthemum, feverfew, arnica).
- Occasional gastrointestinal discomfort (mild nausea, diarrhoea) at high doses.
- Allergic reactions (urticaria, rhinitis) in individuals with Asteraceae/Compositae allergy.
- Theoretical risk of potentiating the effect of diuretic drugs.
- May alter absorption or metabolism of concomitant medications requiring monitoring (lithium — due to diuretic effect; anticoagulants — due to vitamin K content in whole plant preparations).

**Contraindications:**
- Obstruction of the bile duct, cholangitis, gallbladder empyema, or intestinal obstruction (choleretic stimulation is contraindicated in mechanical biliary obstruction).
- Known hypersensitivity to dandelion or other members of the Asteraceae/Compositae family.
- Gallstones: use only after consultation with a physician (cholagogue effect may precipitate biliary colic).

**Dosage (WHO-Recommended Adult Doses):**
- *Dried root (cut drug for decoction):* 3–4 g per dose, three times daily (total 9–12 g/day); prepared as a decoction (simmered 5–10 minutes, steeped 15 minutes).
- *Tincture (1:5, 25–45% ethanol):* 5–10 ml three times daily.
- *Fluid extract (1:1, 25% ethanol):* 4–8 ml three times daily.
- *Dry extract (standardized, 4:1):* 250–500 mg three times daily.
- *Fresh root juice:* 4–8 ml twice daily.
- Duration: Short-term use (4–6 weeks) typical for dyspeptic complaints; long-term use should be supervised.

segments.title_inferred: Aloe Vera Gel
segments.original_text: Gel Aloes Barbadensis — Aloe vera (L.) Burm.f. (Liliaceae / Aloaceae). Dried or fresh mucilaginous gel obtained from the parenchymatous cells of the fresh leaves. Principal constituents: water (99–99.5%), polysaccharides (acemannan / acetylated mannose polymer, glucomannans, pectins), glycoproteins (lectins, aloctin A and B), anthraquinone-free gel fraction, sterols (lupeol, campesterol, β-sitosterol), organic acids (chrysophanic acid), enzymes (bradykininase, carboxypeptidase, catalase, cellulase), vitamins (B1, B2, B6, C, E, folic acid), minerals (calcium, magnesium, zinc), salicylic acid derivatives, and trace anthraquinones (must be distinguished from the latex fraction which is high in barbaloin/aloin). Polysaccharide content varies by processing method; acemannan is considered the primary immunomodulatory and wound-healing active fraction.
segments.translation: DEFINITION: Aloe vera gel is the clear, mucilaginous material [parenchyma cell contents] obtained from the fresh leaves of Aloe vera (L.) Burm.f. It must be carefully distinguished from aloe latex [the yellow exudate just beneath the leaf skin, rich in anthraquinone glycosides], as the gel is intended to be essentially anthraquinone-free. Pharmaceutical preparations may be fresh, stabilised, freeze-dried, or spray-dried. BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Aloe vera is a stemless or short-stemmed succulent perennial growing to 60–100 cm. Leaves are thick, fleshy, grey-green, lanceolate, with serrated margins bearing whitish teeth. Leaf anatomy comprises an outer green rind, a middle layer containing the bitter yellow latex (pericyclic cells), and a central colourless parenchyma that yields the gel. Flowers are tubular, yellow to orange, borne on a tall raceme. GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Believed native to the Arabian Peninsula and North Africa; naturalised throughout the Mediterranean, the Caribbean, Mexico, and sub-Saharan Africa. Cultivated commercially on a large scale in the United States (Texas, Florida), Mexico, the Dominican Republic, India, South Africa, and Australia. CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The gel is approximately 99–99.5% water. The pharmacologically significant dry-weight fraction includes: (1) Polysaccharides — acemannan (O-acetylated polymannose; primary immunostimulant), glucomannans, galactans, and arabinogalactans; these constitute the principal wound-healing and immunomodulatory agents. (2) Glycoproteins — aloctin A and B; exhibit anti-inflammatory and mitogenic activity. Aloe lectin binds to epidermal growth factor receptors. (3) Anthraquinone traces — small residual amounts of aloin A/B (barbaloin), aloe-emodin, and chrysophanic acid remain even in purified gel; the WHO standard requires the gel fraction to be substantially free of these. (4) Sterols — lupeol, campesterol, and β-sitosterol contribute anti-inflammatory effects. (5) Enzymes — bradykininase [hydrolyses bradykinin, reducing pain and inflammation], carboxypeptidase, catalase, and cellulase. (6) Salicylates — contribute analgesic and anti-inflammatory activity. (7) Vitamins and minerals — vitamins C, E, and B-complex, plus zinc and magnesium, support wound-healing. MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: (1) Wound healing and burns: The most robustly supported indication. Aloe vera gel accelerates re-epithelialisation of partial-thickness burns and surgical wounds compared with petrolatum gauze or silver sulfadiazine in several randomised controlled trials [RCTs]. A meta-analysis of four RCTs found statistically significant reduction in wound-healing time (mean difference approximately 8.79 days, 95% CI) in aloe-treated versus conventional groups. Acemannan is considered the active fraction promoting fibroblast proliferation and collagen synthesis. (2) Psoriasis: A double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT (n=60, 16 weeks) using 0.5% aloe vera cream demonstrated statistically significant reductions in PASI [Psoriasis Area and Severity Index] scores versus placebo (83.3% clearance vs. 6.7%; p<0.001). (3) Genital herpes (HSV-2): A double-blind RCT (n=60 men) comparing 0.5% aloe vera cream, 0.5% aloe gel, and placebo found significantly greater healing rates and reduced mean healing times in both aloe groups. (4) Seborrhoeic dermatitis: A randomised, double-blind, parallel-group trial (n=44) showed significant improvement in scaliness, itching, and total severity score with aloe vera 30% emulsion versus placebo. (5) Oral mucositis / aphthous ulcers: Several small RCTs report accelerated healing and pain reduction. (6) Radiation dermatitis: Evidence is mixed; some RCTs show benefit in delaying or reducing radiation-induced skin reactions, but results are inconsistent across trials. MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Used topically for millennia across Egyptian, Greek, Roman, Indian Ayurvedic, and traditional Chinese medicine systems for burns, sunburn, frostbite, wounds, skin irritation, eczema, and psoriasis. Internally used in some traditions for constipation (note: this refers to the latex fraction, NOT the gel; internal use of the gel itself has less traditional and pharmacological support). PHARMACOLOGY: (1) Wound healing — acemannan stimulates macrophage activation, promotes secretion of growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, IL-6, IL-8), and activates fibroblast proliferation. Bradykininase reduces inflammation and pain in the wound bed. (2) Anti-inflammatory — inhibits arachidonic acid pathway via lipoxygenase suppression; lupeol and β-sitosterol inhibit prostaglandin synthesis. Bradykininase directly degrades bradykinin. (3) Moisturisation and barrier function — polysaccharides form a hygroscopic film on skin surface, reducing transepidermal water loss [TEWL]. (4) Immunomodulation — acemannan activates macrophages, stimulates cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6), and has demonstrated antiviral activity in vitro (HIV, HSV, influenza) at non-cytotoxic concentrations. (5) Antioxidant — vitamins C and E, plus polyphenols, scavenge reactive oxygen species. (6) Antimicrobial — in vitro activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli, and Candida albicans documented; clinical relevance uncertain. ADVERSE REACTIONS: Topical use is generally well tolerated. Contact dermatitis and allergic reactions (type IV hypersensitivity) have been reported, particularly in individuals sensitive to plants of the Liliaceae family. Burning sensation on application to deep wounds noted occasionally. Internal use of gel preparations: rare reports of hypoglycaemia with concurrent antidiabetic drugs. Gel preparations that retain anthraquinone contamination may cause diarrhoea and electrolyte imbalance. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Known hypersensitivity to aloe or other members of the Liliaceae/Aloaceae family. Internal use is not recommended during pregnancy (uterotonic potential of anthraquinone contaminants) or breastfeeding. Do not apply to deep surgical wounds or where systemic absorption may occur in sensitive individuals without medical supervision. DOSAGE: Topical — Apply fresh gel or standardised gel preparation liberally to the affected area 3–4 times daily. Cream formulations: 0.5% aloe vera extract or 25–50% gel concentration used in clinical trials. Oral (gel, not latex) — Not formally standardised by WHO for internal use of gel alone; preparations used in trials range from 10–30 mL of stabilised gel 2–3 times daily. Processing note: WHO monograph emphasises that gel products must be tested to confirm anthraquinone levels are below acceptable thresholds, distinguishing them from aloe latex (laxative) preparations.
segments.preparation: TOPICAL FORMS: Fresh leaf gel applied directly; stabilised commercial gel (25–100% concentration); cream or emulsion (0.5–30% extract). Apply to affected skin area 3–4 times daily. Used in burns, wounds, psoriasis, herpes lesions, radiation dermatitis, and seborrhoeic dermatitis. ORAL FORMS (gel only, not latex): Stabilised aloe vera gel liquid, 10–30 mL 2–3 times daily (as used in clinical studies for immune support and mucositis; not WHO-standardised for general internal use). Freeze-dried powder: equivalent dose per manufacturer's standardisation. All preparations intended for internal use must be certified as substantially anthraquinone-free (aloin <10 ppm per International Aloe Science Council standard). DURATION: For wound/burn healing, continue until re-epithelialisation is complete; for chronic skin conditions (psoriasis, seborrhoeic dermatitis), trial periods of 4–16 weeks used in clinical studies.
segments.condition: Primary conditions with clinical trial support: (1) Partial-thickness burn wounds and minor wounds — accelerates healing; (2) Psoriasis vulgaris — topical 0.5% cream, significant PASI score reduction; (3) Genital herpes (HSV-2) — topical 0.5% cream/gel, improved healing rate; (4) Seborrhoeic dermatitis — 30% emulsion reduces severity. Secondary/traditional indications: sunburn, radiation dermatitis, aphthous ulcers, dry skin, frostbite.
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Aloe vera gel (Gel Aloes Barbadensis)

segments.title_inferred: Reishi Mushroom (Ganoderma)
segments.original_text: WHO Monograph Plant: Ganoderma lucidum (Curtis) P. Karst. (Ganodermataceae). Common synonym: Polyporus lucidus W. Curtis. Pharmaceutical name: Ganoderma (the dried fruiting body and/or mycelium). Primary chemical constituents: Triterpenoids (ganoderic acids A–Z, ganoderenic acids, lucidenic acids; total triterpenoid content approximately 1–3% dry weight in fruiting body); Polysaccharides (beta-D-glucans, including Gl-PS; total polysaccharide content approximately 1–5% dry weight); Nucleosides (adenosine, uridine); Ergosterol and ergosterol peroxide; Proteins including LZ-8 (immunomodulatory protein); Minerals (germanium, selenium, zinc); Alkaloids (betaine, choline); Fatty acids (oleic acid, stearic acid). Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 3.
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: Ganoderma consists of the dried fruiting body (basidiocarp) and/or mycelium of Ganoderma lucidum (Curtis) P. Karst., a bracket fungus in the family Ganodermataceae. In traditional East Asian pharmacopoeia it is referred to as Lingzhi (Chinese) or Reishi (Japanese). The pharmaceutical material is typically standardised to total polysaccharide and/or total triterpenoid content.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Ganoderma lucidum produces a distinctive kidney- to fan-shaped fruiting body (pileus) ranging from 5–30 cm across, with a shiny, lacquered-appearance upper surface that is typically reddish-brown to mahogany in colour. The flesh (context) is white to pale brown, corky, and fibrous. A long lateral stipe (stalk) is usually present and shares the same lacquered surface texture. The pore surface (hymenophore) on the underside is white to pale cream in fresh specimens, becoming brown with age. Spores are ovoid to truncate, approximately 8–12 × 5–8 μm, with a distinctive double-walled structure. The mycelium, commercially cultivated, is white and filamentous.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Naturally distributed across temperate and tropical Asia, including China, Japan, Korea, and South-East Asia; also found in parts of North America and Europe. In the wild it grows as a wood-rotting fungus on deciduous hardwood trees (particularly oaks and plum trees). Commercial cultivation — predominantly in China, Japan, Korea, Taiwan, and the United States — is carried out on oak logs, sawdust substrate, or grain medium, making consistent quality material widely available.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The pharmacologically active constituents fall into two principal classes: (1) Polysaccharides (primarily beta-1,3/1,6-D-glucans collectively termed Gl-PS or Reishi Polysaccharide Fraction): total content approximately 1–5% of dry fruiting body weight; responsible chiefly for immunomodulatory activity. (2) Triterpenoids (lanostane-type, including ganoderic acids A through Z, ganoderenic acids A–D, and lucidenic acids A–N): total content approximately 1–3% dry weight; responsible primarily for adaptogenic, hepatoprotective, anti-tumour, and anti-inflammatory activities. Additional constituents include ergosterol (provitamin D2 precursor), the immunomodulatory protein LZ-8, adenosine (cardiovascular activity), germanium (trace mineral), oleic acid, and various antioxidant phenolics.
