MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL/EVIDENCE-BASED: (1) Immunomodulation and adjunctive oncology support: Multiple randomised controlled trials have assessed Ganoderma polysaccharide extracts as adjuvants to chemotherapy and radiotherapy in cancer patients, demonstrating improvements in immune cell counts (natural killer cells, T-lymphocytes), quality-of-life scores, and reduced adverse event burden. (2) Cardiovascular risk factors: Clinical trials report modest reductions in total cholesterol, LDL-cholesterol, and blood pressure in hypertensive and hyperlipidaemic subjects following 4–12 weeks of standardised extract supplementation. (3) Glycaemic control: Pilot trials in Type 2 diabetes indicate modest hypoglycaemic effects of Ganoderma polysaccharide fractions. Evidence remains preliminary and confirmatory large-scale RCTs are lacking.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Described continuously in Chinese materia medica for over 2,000 years (first recorded in Shennong Bencao Jing, c. 100 CE). Indicated traditionally for: tonifying Qi [vital energy] and calming the mind (treatment of insomnia, anxiety, neurasthenia); supporting liver function; alleviating cough, asthma, and bronchitis; general immune tonic and adaptogen; anti-ageing and longevity promotion. Used in Korean, Japanese, and Tibetan traditional medicine systems under equivalent designations.

PHARMACOLOGY: (1) Immunomodulation: Beta-D-glucans activate macrophages, dendritic cells, natural killer cells, and T-lymphocytes via pattern recognition receptors (Dectin-1, TLR-2/4), upregulating cytokine production (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ). LZ-8 protein suppresses IgE-mediated allergic responses. (2) Antitumour activity: In vitro and animal studies demonstrate induction of apoptosis in cancer cell lines (via caspase-3/7 activation, Bcl-2 suppression), inhibition of angiogenesis (suppression of VEGF expression), and anti-metastatic effects. Mechanism is partly immunomodulatory (NK cell activation) and partly direct cytotoxic. (3) Hepatoprotection: Ganoderic acids inhibit hepatic lipid peroxidation, upregulate glutathione peroxidase, and reduce ALT/AST elevation in carbon tetrachloride-induced liver injury models. (4) Cardiovascular: Adenosine and ganoderic acids inhibit platelet aggregation; triterpenoids inhibit HMG-CoA reductase (cholesterol biosynthesis pathway), analogous to statins. (5) Adaptogenic/anti-stress: Animal studies indicate modulation of HPA-axis reactivity and reduction in corticosterone levels under chronic stress conditions.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — KEY TRIALS: (1) Chen et al. (2006) — A double-blind RCT (n=68) in patients with advanced colorectal cancer receiving chemotherapy compared Ganoderma polysaccharide extract (Gl-PS, 1,800 mg/day orally for 12 weeks) versus placebo. Outcomes: significant improvement in NK cell activity, CD4+/CD8+ T-cell ratio, and patient-reported quality of life scores (p<0.05); no significant difference in tumour response rate. (2) Jin et al. (2012) — Systematic review and meta-analysis (Cochrane-style; 5 RCTs, n=373) of Ganoderma as adjunct to standard cancer therapy. Finding: Ganoderma significantly increased tumour response rate (OR 1.75, 95% CI 1.16–2.64) and improved quality of life (OR 1.27, 95% CI 1.05–1.54) compared with chemotherapy/radiotherapy alone. Authors noted methodological limitations (small sample sizes, risk of bias). (3) Klupp et al. (2015, Cochrane review) — Reviewed 5 RCTs of Ganoderma in cancer patients; concluded there is insufficient evidence to justify use as a first-line cancer treatment but evidence supports a possible role as an adjunct to conventional therapy for immune support, with an acceptable safety profile.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include: mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, diarrhoea, stomach discomfort); dry mouth and throat; dizziness in some patients during initial use; rare skin rash. At high doses or with prolonged use (>6 months): elevated liver enzymes reported in isolated case reports (hepatotoxicity risk remains debated). Drug interactions: potential additive effect with anticoagulants (warfarin, aspirin) due to platelet inhibition by adenosine/ganoderic acids — monitor INR; theoretical interaction with immunosuppressants (cyclosporine) due to opposing immunostimulatory effect; possible additive hypoglycaemic effect with antidiabetic medications.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: (1) Known hypersensitivity to Ganoderma lucidum or other fungi; (2) Concurrent use of anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy without medical supervision; (3) Organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressive regimens; (4) Pregnancy and lactation (insufficient safety data); (5) Autoimmune diseases (systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis) — theoretical risk of immune stimulation exacerbating disease.

DOSAGE — WHO/STANDARD ADULT RECOMMENDATIONS: Dried fruiting body powder: 1.5–9 g/day orally in divided doses (traditional range); most clinical trials use 1.44–5.4 g equivalent/day. Standardised extract (polysaccharide fraction, typically 30% beta-glucan): 150–900 mg/day. Standardised extract (triterpenoid fraction): 6 mg/day (typical commercial standardisation). Decoction: 6–15 g dried material simmered in water for 1–2 hours; 1–2 cups daily. Tincture (1:5 in 40% ethanol): 2–4 mL three times daily. Duration: Most clinical trials of 4–12 weeks duration; long-term use beyond 6 months warrants periodic hepatic function monitoring.

segments.title_inferred: Noni (Morinda citrifolia fruit)
segments.original_text: Fructus Morindae Citrifoliae — The dried or fresh ripe fruit of Morinda citrifolia L. (Rubiaceae). Key chemical constituents include: iridoid glycosides (deacetylasperulosidic acid, asperulosidic acid, asperuloside); anthraquinones (damnacanthal, morindone, rubiadin, nordamnacanthal); polysaccharides (acetylated and non-acetylated glucurono-galactans, arabinogalactans); flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, rutin); organic acids (caproic acid, caprylic acid, hexanoic acid — responsible for characteristic pungent odour); scopoletin (coumarin derivative, approx. 0.0025–0.01% dry weight); terpenes (ursolic acid, beta-sitosterol); alkaloids (xeronine, proxeronine — disputed biosynthetic significance); vitamins (ascorbic acid ~33–155 mg/100g fresh weight); and potassium (~56 mg/30 mL juice).
segments.translation: Noni fruit (the ripe fruit of Morinda citrifolia, family Rubiaceae) is the medicinal plant part used. The fruit is chemically complex, containing several classes of bioactive compounds. The iridoid glycosides — particularly deacetylasperulosidic acid and asperulosidic acid — are considered primary pharmacologically active constituents and are used as chemical markers for quality standardisation. The anthraquinone damnacanthal has attracted research interest as a potential inhibitor of tyrosine kinase activity in RAS oncogene-transformed cell lines [in vitro significance only]. The coumarin derivative scopoletin has demonstrated serotonin-receptor binding activity in experimental models, providing a partial mechanistic basis for proposed effects on blood pressure and mood regulation. High-molecular-weight polysaccharides (principally glucurono-galactans) have shown immunostimulatory activity in murine models, stimulating peritoneal macrophage activity and increasing survival in Lewis lung carcinoma mouse models — though these findings have not been replicated in human clinical trials at the time of monograph publication. The characteristic pungent, fetid odour and flavour of noni fruit is attributable to short-chain fatty acids (hexanoic and octanoic acids). Traditional medicinal systems across Polynesia, Southeast Asia, and South Asia have employed noni fruit for a wide range of indications including hypertension, diabetes, pain, fever, skin infections, and general 'tonic' or adaptogenic purposes. At the time of WHO monograph publication (Volume 3), clinical evidence supporting specific therapeutic claims remained limited: most data derived from in vitro, animal, or uncontrolled observational studies. One notable open-label pilot study (N=29, cancer patients) suggested potential improvement in quality-of-life measures. No large-scale randomised controlled trials (RCTs) were available at monograph publication. Pharmacological mechanisms proposed include: (1) antioxidant activity via free-radical scavenging; (2) anti-inflammatory effects through COX inhibition [in vitro]; (3) mild analgesic activity comparable to approximately 75% efficacy of tramadol in rodent models (hot-plate and acetic acid writhing tests); (4) antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhi, and Bacillus subtilis in disk-diffusion assays; (5) immunomodulatory activity via polysaccharide-mediated macrophage activation. Adverse reactions documented include: hepatotoxicity (case reports of acute hepatitis associated with high-dose commercial noni juice consumption — causal attribution debated but flagged by European regulatory authorities); hyperkalaemia risk in patients with renal impairment due to significant potassium content (~56 mg/30 mL); potential drug interactions with warfarin (coumarin content); and gastrointestinal intolerance (nausea, diarrhoea) at high doses. Contraindications include: known hepatic disease; chronic renal failure or hyperkalemia; pregnancy and lactation (insufficient safety data); and concurrent use of potassium-sparing diuretics or ACE inhibitors. Recommended adult dosage per WHO guidance: commercial noni juice — 30 mL daily (commonly marketed dose; not formally standardised by WHO as evidence base was insufficient for a definitive therapeutic dose recommendation at time of publication); standardised extracts should be characterised by iridoid glycoside content. The WHO monograph concludes that while noni has a well-documented ethnomedicinal history and promising preliminary pharmacological data, the clinical evidence base at time of publication was insufficient to support definitive therapeutic recommendations beyond traditional use designation.

segments.title_inferred: Roselle / Hibiscus (Dried Calyces)
segments.original_text: Flos Hibisci Sabdariffae — the dried calyces and epicalyces of Hibiscus sabdariffa L. (Malvaceae). Principal chemical constituents include: anthocyanins [hibiscin / delphinidin-3-sambubioside and cyanidin-3-sambubioside, 1.5–2.5% dry weight], organic acids [citric acid, malic acid, tartaric acid, hibiscic acid], polysaccharides, flavonoids [quercetin, luteolin, gossypetin], phytosterols [beta-sitosterol], protocatechuic acid, hydroxycitric acid lactone [hibiscus acid], and mucilaginous polysaccharides. Anthocyanin content is the primary marker of pharmacological activity and commercial quality. Standardisation is typically against total anthocyanin content measured by pH differential spectrophotometry.
segments.translation:
Roselle (Hibiscus sabdariffa) calyces are the fleshy, deep-red, dried flower-cup structures harvested after the petals fall. The plant is an erect annual or perennial herb in the mallow family, growing 1–3 metres tall with toothed, palmate leaves and pale yellow flowers bearing a distinctive dark eye. It is native to tropical West Africa and South Asia and is now widely cultivated across tropical and subtropical regions globally — including Mexico, Egypt, Sudan, Thailand, China, and Jamaica — both as a beverage crop and a medicinal plant.

--- CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS ---
The dominant bioactive compounds are anthocyanins (principally delphinidin-3-sambubioside and cyanidin-3-sambubioside), which give the calyx its characteristic crimson colour and account for most antioxidant, antihypertensive, and antimicrobial activity. Organic acids — especially hibiscus acid (hydroxycitric acid lactone) and citric acid — contribute to the diuretic and lipid-modulating effects. Gossypetin and quercetin are the primary flavonoid aglycones. Protocatechuic acid (a phenolic metabolite of anthocyanin degradation) has demonstrated vasorelaxant and endothelium-protective activity in isolated vessel preparations.

--- PHARMACOLOGICAL MECHANISMS ---
• Antihypertensive: Inhibition of angiotensin-converting enzyme [ACE] activity; direct vasodilatory effect on vascular smooth muscle (calcium-channel antagonism); endothelium-dependent relaxation mediated by nitric oxide (NO) release. Animal studies (rat aorta ring assays) confirm protocatechuic acid and anthocyanins are the primary vasoactive agents.
• Diuretic: Organic acid load increases urinary volume and electrolyte excretion in rodent models; human studies show modest but significant natriuresis at standard tea doses.
• Hypolipidaemic: Inhibition of hepatic cholesterol biosynthesis and upregulation of LDL-receptor expression; hibiscus acid has structural similarity to hydroxycitric acid (the putative lipase inhibitor in Garcinia cambogia), but direct lipase inhibition in Hibiscus has not been robustly confirmed in humans.
• Antioxidant: High oxygen radical absorbance capacity [ORAC] values; free-radical scavenging by anthocyanins and protocatechuic acid reduces lipid peroxidation markers in vitro and in animal models.
• Antimicrobial: Aqueous and ethanolic calyx extracts show in vitro inhibitory activity against Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, and Candida albicans, consistent with organic acid-mediated pH reduction and membrane disruption.

--- CLINICAL EVIDENCE (Key Trials) ---
1. ANTIHYPERTENSIVE EFFECT (Herrera-Arellano et al., 2004 — Mexico): Randomised, double-blind, controlled trial (n=75) comparing Hibiscus sabdariffa aqueous extract [standardised to 9.6 mg anthocyanins per dose, three times daily] versus captopril 25 mg twice daily in patients with mild-to-moderate hypertension (Stage I–II). After 4 weeks, the Hibiscus group showed mean systolic blood pressure reduction of 11.58 mmHg and diastolic reduction of 3.11 mmHg, compared to 13.68 mmHg systolic and 6.43 mmHg diastolic in the captopril group. Difference was not statistically significant, suggesting comparable efficacy for mild hypertension. Tolerability was superior in the Hibiscus group.
2. ANTIHYPERTENSIVE EFFECT (McKay et al., 2010 — USA, Tufts University): Randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial (n=65) in pre-hypertensive and mildly hypertensive adults. Three cups per day of brewed Hibiscus tea (standardised preparation) for 6 weeks produced a mean systolic blood pressure reduction of 7.2 mmHg (p=0.030) versus 1.3 mmHg for placebo. Effect was most pronounced in participants with higher baseline systolic pressure. No significant adverse events reported.
3. LIPID-LOWERING EFFECT (Mozaffari-Khosravi et al., 2009 — Iran): Randomised controlled trial (n=60) in patients with Type 2 diabetes. Hibiscus sabdariffa tea (two cups daily for 30 days) significantly reduced total cholesterol (mean −3.8%), LDL cholesterol (−8.0%), and triglycerides vs. black tea comparator, while HDL cholesterol increased. Renal and liver function tests remained within normal limits.

--- MEDICINAL USES (Traditional / Pharmacopoeial) ---
Widely used in traditional medicine systems across Africa, the Caribbean, Mexico, and South Asia for: hypertension, fever reduction (antipyretic tisane), liver complaints, urinary tract infections [UTI], constipation, and as a topical antimicrobial agent for wounds. Listed in the pharmacopoeias of Mexico (Farmacopea Herbolaria), Egypt (Egyptian Pharmacopoeia), and is referenced in African traditional medicine compendiums. Consumed as the beverage 'agua de Jamaica' (Mexico), 'karkadé' (Egypt/Sudan), 'bissap' (West Africa), and 'sorrel' (Caribbean).

--- ADVERSE REACTIONS ---
• Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Mild gastrointestinal effects (nausea, flatulence) reported at high doses.
• Potential additive hypotensive effect when combined with antihypertensive drugs — blood pressure monitoring advised.
• High organic acid content may worsen gastro-oesophageal reflux.
• Case reports suggest possible hepatotoxicity at very high doses in animal models; human hepatotoxicity not established at standard doses but warrants caution.
• Drug interaction: May reduce bioavailability of chloroquine (antimalarial) — concurrent use not recommended. Possible interaction with diuretics (potentiated effect).
• Weak oestrogenic activity reported in some in vitro assays; clinical relevance uncertain.

--- CONTRAINDICATIONS ---
• Pregnancy: Uterotonic activity demonstrated in animal studies; use during pregnancy is contraindicated.
• Lactation: Insufficient safety data; avoid.
• Concurrent chloroquine therapy: pharmacokinetic interaction documented.
• Known hypersensitivity to Malvaceae family plants.

--- WHO-RECOMMENDED ADULT DOSAGE ---
• Infusion (tea): 1.5–3 g of dried calyces in 150–250 mL boiling water, steeped 10 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily.
• Standardised extract: Equivalent to approximately 9.6–14.4 mg total anthocyanins per dose, 2–3 times daily.
• Duration: Clinical trials support use for 4–6 weeks for antihypertensive indication; long-term safety beyond 6 weeks not fully established in controlled settings.
segments.preparation: Infusion (aqueous tea): 1.5–3 g dried calyces per 150–250 mL boiling water, steeped 10 minutes, 2–3 times daily. Standardised extract capsules: 9.6–14.4 mg total anthocyanins per dose, 2–3 times daily. Syrup or concentrated aqueous extract formulations also in use regionally. Duration of use: 4–6 weeks per clinical trial data.
segments.condition: Mild to moderate hypertension (Stage I); hyperlipidaemia / dyslipidaemia adjunct (particularly LDL and triglyceride reduction); diuresis; traditional: fever, urinary tract infections, liver complaints
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 4 — Flos Hibisci Sabdariffae
segments._label: WHO Monographs — Roselle / Hibiscus (Flos Hibisci Sabdariffae)
